Document 1 of 500

The Ford government is putting our safety and democracy at risk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-is-putting-our-safety-democracy/docview/3142182713/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ford+government+is+putting+our+safety+and+democracy+at+risk&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The provincial auditor's report was very damning yet again. Particularly on the development of Ontario Place as "not transparent or fair."
The report noted keeping the Ontario Science Centre where it is and making repairs was far less expensive than moving and building a new one. The auditor general's report comments "without proper planning" could also have included "eliminating public consultation."
The CEO of Infrastructure Ontario jumped to Ford's defence, claiming "the government's vision would have the best benefits to taxpayers." The AG's report shows that claim is complete bunk.
When Ford suggested a multibillion-dollar tunnel under the 401, he was roundly criticized for it. The board of trade quickly jumped to Ford's defence, calling it "bold leadership," likely because its members would profit handsomely (showing again Ford's cosy relationship with big business).
Since first being elected in 2018, Ford has repeatedly shown he lets nothing stand in his way to benefit private corporations. Certainly not democratic principles or norms. Ford has repeatedly shown his disdain for democracy in the same alarming pattern as the Mike Harris government.
Ford has implemented ministerial zoning orders 114 times, overriding local democracy. Giving "strong-mayor" powers is a prime example of subverting democracy, where mayors now have the power to override decisions by democratically elected councillors.
Ford replaced the Ontario Municipal Board with the Ontario Land Tribunal, where a lone provincial appointee has the power to decide on appeals overriding locally elected officials again.
Harris repeatedly subverted democracy by eliminating referendum voting rights any time a public asset was to be sold. It seems that a Progressive Conservative government's normal operating procedure is to bypass and eliminate democracy to the benefit of the private sector.
Conservatives love claiming they are "cutting red tape," making it sound good, but are actually eliminating laws and regulations that require public input and approval. Regulations that were put there to protect people and the environment, but get in the way of profit making. The prime example now is Ford's Building Highways Faster Act side-stepping environmental assessments.
The worst is whenever they pass new legislation, they give themselves and their ministers more power to rule by decree with no transparency and no public input whatsoever.
Harris did this repeatedly, particularly with his bills in 1998 on hydro deregulation. And now Ford has repeatedly done this. The prime example being the Ford government's Bill 165, Keeping Energy Costs Down Act, giving itself more power to rule by decree. Another example is Bill 218, Supporting Ontario's Recovery and Municipal Elections Act, which shielded private owners of long-term-care facilities from being sued for negligence, where so many died during the pandemic from neglect.
Now Ford has shielded his government from being sued for removing bike lanes where people could be injured or killed as a result of Bill 212. Ford has also closed supervised injection sites, which could result in more deaths.
The most serious threat to the public's health and safety is Ford's cancellation of the most effective low-cost way of detecting disease risk to the public: wastewater testing. Ford saved a few bucks there, while spending more than $100 million on advertising telling us what a great job he is doing.
Ford has campaigned on two slogans, "For the people" and "Getting it done." He has gone to great effort showing he's "getting it done" for wealthy people. If only Ford put this much effort and money into solving the health care, housing, education and affordability crises.
The big questions now are, will $200 persuade you to ignore the dangers to you and your family? And, will you ignore the damage to our environment and trampling of your democratic rights?
The price we'll pay for voting for Ford is far too high.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends question period Tuesday. Will a $200 "rebate" cheque persuade you to ignore the dangers to you and our environment that this government has brought, Paul Kahnert asks.
CREDIT: Paul Kahnert

Subject: Local elections; Democracy

Document 2 of 500

The GST/HST holiday tax break kicks in this week. Here is what is covered

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gst-hst-holiday-tax-break-kicks-this-week-here-is/docview/3142182613/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+GST%2FHST+holiday+tax+break+kicks+in+this+week.+Here+is+what+is+covered&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tax holiday for the holiday season will start this week, giving some Canadians relief on year-end shopping.
The temporary tax break will remove the federal and provincial tax from all groceries, restaurant meals, children's clothes, toys and diapers, as well as beer and wine, for a two-month period from Dec. 14 to Feb. 15, in a bid to ease the cost of living for Canadians.
In Ontario, for the products included in the tax break, that means the harmonized sales tax (HST), which is comprised of the federal GST portion (at five per cent) and the provincial portion (at eight per cent), won't apply.
The Liberals have also proposed sending $250 cheques to anyone who worked in 2023 and made less than $150,000, but have not yet introduced legislation on that measure. The New Democrats and Bloc - joined by some members of the Liberal caucus - want to see eligibility expanded to more Canadians.
What is covered under the GST/HST holiday break?Children's goods
Clothing, diapers, car seats and toys for kids are all covered under the new temporary tax break.
For clothing, that includes: baby clothes, including bibs and blankets; children's clothes up to girls' size 16 and boys' size 20; children's socks, hats, scarves and gloves; shoes smaller than 24.25 cm; and sports clothing like jerseys, leotards, ski jackets and recreational swimsuits.
What's not included: any specialized clothing or shoes designed exclusively for sports (like wetsuits, soccer cleats, skates, ski boots, etc.), adult clothing purchased for a child, costumes or makeup and jewelry.
Most diapers are included in the tax break, including cloth and disposable diapers, diaper inserts or training pants - however, adult diapers are not included (they are already exempt from GST/HST).
The same goes for children's car seats, which includes all boosters and seats that meet federal safety standards, but does not include car seats that are built as part of a stroller or carrier combination.
Children's toys covered under the tax break include most items intended for children under 14 years old (which mostly captures toys recommended for children aged 8 and up). That encompasses board games, card games, toys, dolls, plush toys and jigsaw puzzles (for all ages). Excluded are collectibles, like hockey cards or collectible dolls, and toys marketed for adults (like adult Lego sets).
Video games
Video game consoles, controllers and video games are included in the tax break as well, though only physical copies of games (like in CDs or cartridges) qualify.
Books
For the most part, published printed books (both hardcover and softcover editions), magazines and periodicals are covered under the tax break.
E-books and audio books are not included and magazines need to be supplied by a subscription or have less than five per cent of its printed space devoted to advertising in order to qualify. Agendas, calendars and notebooks do not qualify for the tax break.
Newspapers
Printed newspapers also qualify for the tax break, but excludes digital versions of those publications.
Christmas trees
Both natural and artificial Christmas trees, as well as "similar decorative trees," qualify for the tax break, according to the government.
Food and drinks
All restaurant meals - including dine-in, takeout and delivery - are covered by the tax holiday. That also covers prepared foods, pre-made meals and catering. The tax break also includes: snacks like chips, candy and baked goods; non-alcoholic drinks including coffee, tea, juices, soda and smoothies; beer; wine, cider and sake at 22.9 per cent ABV or less; and premixed alcoholic drinks at seven per cent ABV or less.
That exemption does not include food or drink from vending machines, dietary supplements and pet food.
Basic groceries - including fresh fruits, vegetables, meat, poultry and fish - are already excluded from both the provincial and federal portions of the HST in Ontario.
How about the provincial sales tax break?
The Ford government has pledged to match the federal government's two-month GST holiday, which overlaps with pre-existing tax breaks in the province.
Ontario already provides a "point-of-sale rebate" on the eight per cent portion of the HST on: books, children's clothes and shoes, children's car seats, diapers, some prepared food and beverages under $4 and some books and newspapers.
That will expand to cover the federal GST/HST tax holiday, meaning consumers will see a full 13 per cent tax break on the items above.
How much can I save?
The total savings you will see depends entirely on your spending habits. Stocking up on toys, books and processed foods? You could save a few dozen dollars. If you're sticking to the basic groceries and having a low-key holiday season, you'll be less likely to see any reduction in your expenses, as the HST already doesn't apply to basic groceries.
Still confused? Here are some examples.
Let's say you go all out and purchase a PlayStation 5 Slim, plus an extra controller and a physical copy of a game (for example, NHL 25). Using prices from Dec. 3 on BestBuy, your total would usually be $802.27. Remove the HST and that would bring your total down to $709.97, resulting in $92.30 in savings.
Maybe you stock up on diapers, toys and food at Costco. Assuming you get a box of 192 size 1 diapers (Kirkland brand), plus a kids Lego set and a box of assorted chips, your total would be around $214.67 pre-tax holiday. Between December and February, that comes down to $189.97, or about $24.70 in savings.
And maybe you're buying Christmas presents for a book worm? A box set of the first five Game of Thrones books at Indigo (through its online store) would usually cost $67.74, but with the tax break the price comes down to $59.95.
CAPTION: The federal government announced the tax holiday in November, in an attempt to ease Canadians' cost-of-living woes.

Hamilton-area Today: Families, patients 'panicked' over mental-health clinic closure + 'complex' outdoor drug market operated like a business

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-area-today-families-patients-panicked/docview/3142182589/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton-area+Today%3A+Families%2C+patients+%27panicked%27+over+mental-health+clinic+closure+%2B+%27complex%27+outdoor+drug+market+operated+like+a+business&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning! It's Dec. 9, here are the top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
Expect rain and some slightly warmer temperatures. Environment Canada is forecasting a high of 5 C at the airport weather station. There are no alerts.
Traffic and transit updates
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.
Top stories on The Spec"There are so many people in need"
Brian Smith's family is panicking about what will happen to him when the east Hamilton mental-health clinic that has treated him for more than 35 years shuts down in March. "We're shocked and we're panicked."
Mayor Collins?
It wasn't a "yes" but hardly a "no."
Scott Radley spoke to Chad Collins about his political ambitions following news about the Stoney Creek MPs public disagreement with the Liberal party over planned $250 rebates. Will he run for mayor?
Is Mouhamad Al Jalmoud criminally responsible?
The defence and Crown agree Mouhamad Al Jalmoud caused the deadly, split second collision. But was he criminally responsible for the crash? Susan Clairmont looks at trial closing arguments: What time frame puts context around the crash? Is it the entire span of then 20-year-old's life? When his PTSD was "triggered" by a brief encounter with police? Or the moment he chose to drive?
Police shut down drug market, arrest 57 people
Hamilton police say they have shut down an open-air drug market, operating at King Street East and East Avenue, that preyed on vulnerable people in the area. Over the five-week project, police arrested 57 people and laid 103 charges, including charging the alleged leader of the "sophisticated" operation.
Painful cuts at RBG
The Royal Botanical Gardens has axed 13 jobs - including those held by well-known senior staffers, horticulture managers and workers - in a restructuring aimed at bringing "financial sustainability" to the deficit-plagued charity.
Subscribe to our newsletters for the latest local content.
CAPTION: Downtown Hamilton from Hamilton city hall at centre and Bayfront Park to the north.

Document 4 of 500

Doug Ford's Tories leading as potential election looms, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-leading-as-potential-election/docview/3142072618/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+leading+as+potential+election+looms%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford mulls gambling on an early election, a new poll suggests that might be a safe bet.
The latest Abacus Data monthly tracking survey for the Star found Ford's Tories at 43 per cent - well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 25 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
"Now is as good a time as any to go (to voters). I don't see how their numbers get better," said Abacus president David Coletto.
"He's still by far the favourite to win the election," said Coletto, warning that federal circumstances beyond Ford's control could transform the electoral landscape.
"Tim Houston benefited from the fact that Justin Trudeau is still the Liberal leader," he said, referring to last week's landslide re-election victory by the Nova Scotia Tories in which Trudeau's unpopularity hindered the provincial Grits there.
"Trudeau is pulling down Bonnie Crombie's numbers here. But what happens when - and I think it's a 'when' - the federal Liberals get defeated?"
Coletto said if, as every recent public-opinion poll suggests, Trudeau's minority Liberal government is toppled by Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives, the political dynamic at Queen's Park could change.
"Trudeau is the glue. But once he's gone, what happens? That's why Ford wants to go before Trudeau is gone."
While a provincial election is not scheduled until June 2026, the premier and his campaign advisers have been ruminating over facing voters this spring just after 15 million Ontarians receive $200 "rebate" cheques.
Three senior Tory insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, told the Star that Ford shrugged off Houston's massive win on Nov. 26.
"Ontario is not Nova Scotia," the premier told several people in the days after that election when aides insisted the PC success there portended good things for the Ontario party.
Sources say Ford is preoccupied by the crisis triggered by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threat to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods unless both countries tighten their borders.
But that does not mean a snap election is necessarily on the backburner, they confide.
Mindful of the uncertainty that looms once Trump takes office Jan. 20, Ford's team is concerned Ontario could be plunged into a recession if tariffs spark a trade war.
Such economic upheaval could make re-election more challenging, they note.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from Nov. 28 through Wednesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
That means the poll was conducted in the wake of Trump's highly publicized tariff gambit.
In terms of opinions on the party leaders, 37 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 43 per cent had a negative view with 18 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say. That's a net impression rating for him of minus six per cent.
Crombie was 25 per cent positive, 32 per cent negative, 29 per cent neutral and 13 per cent unsure for a minus seven per cent net rating.
Stiles had a 28 per cent positive with a 25 per cent negative, a 30 per cent neutral and 18 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
Schreiner had a 22 per cent positive, 20 per cent negative, 37 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus two per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends a signing of a memorandum of understanding with Governor of Illinois J.B. Pritzker, at the US-Canada Summit in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Document 5 of 500

Failing to use tax breaks to help those struggling to get by is as Grinchy as it gets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/failing-use-tax-breaks-help-those-struggling-get/docview/3141897540/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Failing+to+use+tax+breaks+to+help+those+struggling+to+get+by+is+as+Grinchy+as+it+gets&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Retail politics came on strong as Ontarians entered the holiday season. First, Premier Doug Ford's $200 gift to every resident; second, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's "GST holiday" on certain items.
Some of these items are essential, such as children's clothing and diapers. But the rest of the list - beer, wine, chips, candies, cake, Christmas trees - are nice to have, not essentials.
And that misses the point.
Tax breaks and transfers in the name of affordability, which is the new retail politics in Canada, should be targeted to low-income people struggling to get by. They're not.
They're also a drop in the bucket. The GST holiday, for instance, would only amount to a five per cent tax break on those select holiday items. That won't pay the rent.
What's more, nothing is stopping big stores from jacking up prices or nixing holiday sales. After all, the federal government just took the wind out of GST-free marketing incentives.
That's part of the folly of retail politics. They drain government coffers and subsidize big businesses.
But wait, there's more.
In the new year, the federal government will also send a $250 cheque to all workers who make under $150,000. Guess what? Ninety-five per cent of working Canadians make under $150,000.
Retail politics might offer broad appeal, but they do nothing to alleviate the struggles of Canada's poorest. The cheques and GST holiday are not going to help low-income households that are increasingly using food banks and facing homelessness this holiday season.
The premier and prime minister's announcements come on the heels of the new report card on child and family poverty from the anti-poverty watchdog coalition, Campaign 2000.
The report found record increases in child poverty in Canada for two straight years in a row. By 2022, nearly one in five children, or 1.4 million, were below the poverty line. That's an increase of nearly five percentage points over two years - the largest rise in poverty on record in Canada.
The working poor are hurting, too.
Only three of Canada's 62 largest urban areas are affordable enough for a minimum-wage worker working full-time to afford to rent a two-bedroom apartment.
In cities like Toronto and Vancouver, working two full-time minimum-wage jobs means you still can't afford to rent a one-bedroom apartment.
News flash: $250 isn't going to help.
Instead of throwing $6.3 billion out the window on a one-time holiday gift that will do nothing to reduce poverty, the federal government should target supports to the lowest-income people in Canada.
The Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives has modelled a supplement to the Canada Child Benefit that targets families in deep poverty and provides them with a maximum of $8,500 per year for a family making $19,000 or less.
That's a cheaper bill for the federal government than the GST holiday.
And low-income families would benefit from $8,500 more a year in support. Canada would immediately cut child poverty rates in half with one single measure. Talk about getting bang for your buck.
It's one of Campaign 2000's priority recommendations to work toward eliminating child poverty.
The most meaningful thing the federal and provincial governments could do to reduce poverty is to target supports to the lowest-income households in Canada.
Failing to do that is about as Grinchy as it gets.
CAPTION: Instead of throwing $6.3 billion out the window on a one-time holiday gift that will do nothing to reduce poverty, the government should target supports to the lowest-income people, Leila Sarangi and David Macdonald write.
CREDIT: Leila Sarangi and David Macdonald

Subject: Federal government; Child poverty; Tax cuts; Politics; Prime ministers; Low income groups

Document 6 of 500

One of us thinks we need a federal election ASAP. The other thinks that's a terrible idea

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/one-us-thinks-we-need-federal-election-asap-other/docview/3141897517/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+of+us+thinks+we+need+a+federal+election+ASAP.+The+other+thinks+that%27s+a+terrible+idea&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
MG: We're into December now. Normally I'd say this is silly season but I think it's been silly season for months. I've been thinking about this a lot, and I've been saying some version of this for a while, but let me just say it bluntly: it's in everyone's best interests for there to be an election. Soon. As soon as we can arrange one after Christmas. Issue the writ just after New Years's, vote in early February?
I think the country needs this, because all the federal parties are clearly giving up. I think we'll be better able to handle Donald Trump with a government with a clear mandate. And, finally, I think the Liberals will be better off to just get this over with. Not only is there no Trump bump, but the Tories seem to be furthering their lead. The GST holiday and rebates are backfiring. This is humiliating enough and for the Liberals, worse is always possible. Enough. Throw it back to the people. It's time.
SD: I am keen to hear you game this out, but I do have to ask, are you trying to be the lump of coal in our Christmas stockings?
MG: I'm honestly not. I think the status quo we're stuck with until we have an election is the coal.
SD: So my obvious question is this one, and you know what I'm going to say. Picture this: it's Jan. 21 and Trump makes good on his threat to impose sweeping 25 per cent tariffs. Canada has a caretaker government. Do we just appoint Ontario Premier Doug Ford to speak for the federation while we are sorting out who's prime minister?
MG: No. We eat the pain for a few weeks, get a government that isn't a desiccated husk of its former self, and then that government can have a steady hand for years, instead of the constant high-wire act we're stuck with now. Because the scenario you just laid out isn't something that can only happen if we call an election deliberately. It can happen if a handful of Liberal MPs get a tummy ache.
SD: Those aren't going to be quiet weeks before or after the inauguration on Jan. 20. As we've already seen, Trump is acting like he's president already. Justin Trudeau and his government, I believe correctly, are mindful of this - hence the trip to Mar-a-Lago. At the very time that events in the U.S. are going into overdrive, an election would throw us into slow motion.
It would also mean Canada-U.S. relations would dominate an election in ways we haven't seen since the great free-trade election of 1988. I'm not saying that's a bad thing in itself. But I do believe Canada has to look united in the face of the Trump threat and an election creates divisions, not unity.
MG: Nothing about our current politics is channelling unity. We are, at best, aspiring to unity. And I'm not even sure that's consistently true. And that's not going to change without an election. Let me flip the question around here: consider the last six months of Canadian politics, or 12, or 18. Consider where we are right now, today. What's the case for this continuing?
SD: I will agree with you that nothing in the past year and a half is a good argument for more of the same. That's not just because we're dealing with a nine-year-old government. We're dealing with an opposition that appears to have zero interest in making Parliament or government work. Pierre Poilievre, if he does become prime minister, may turn out to be a competent one. But that won't happen in the first few weeks. I'm giving you a version here of what I've already said: to have Canada led by a caretaker government, then a rookie one precisely when Trump is dangling existential threats to our economy is in no one's interest.
MG: That's inevitable. It's going to happen, unless we're going to somehow not have an election again until Trump leaves office. It's just a matter of timing. And on that question, my answer is: as soon as possible.
SD: I'd have a bit more confidence in our ability to handle a transition if Poilievre was talking as a prime minister in waiting these days. Maybe he is behind the scenes. Maybe he has people making contact with the incoming Trump team. Let's hope so. But that's not what he's doing in public. He is standing behind placards saying essentially, "we agree with you, Trump, this country is a mess." He'll do more of that in an election. That's not a negotiating position and some of that will come back to haunt him, if and/or when he gets the chance at the big job.
MG: In the final analysis, the most honest answer someone can give me against having an election now is probably something like, "I don't like who'd win." And that's fair. I really mean that. That's a completely legitimate answer. What I'm saying is this: unless you (a royal you, meaning anyone) really think there's a chance Poilievre loses the next election, the only thing that should matter to you is timing. And I think sooner is much, much better than later.
SD: Maybe I've worked in Ottawa too long and absorbed some of the risk-averse culture around here. The danger to me here isn't the outcome of an election, but the risks to the country of plunging it into a campaign - a nasty one, we can safely predict - when our most important relationship is on the verge of a shock to the system. We know two things about Trump: personal relationships matter to him and he has a ruthless instinct when dealing with weakness. An election would be handing him all the ammunition he needs to make us weaker.
MG: There is no risk-free option here. I wish there was. We have to pick our poison. Trump has ammunition enough already. That can't guide us.
SD: You make some good points, but I'm still going to resist the prospect of a campaign in the first weeks of 2025. And I bet the government will, too.
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump being awarded the 'Patriot of the Year' award at the FOX Nation Patriot Awards on Dec. 5, 2024, in Greenvale, N.Y.

Subject: Political campaigns; Awards & honors; Politics; Prime ministers

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: One of us thinks we need a federal election ASAP. The other thinks that's a terrible idea

Document 7 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3141775866/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tackle the cause, not outcome
Re: Hamilton to experiment with new algae fighting tools, Dec. 4
Reading of this future work to clean up the algae in Hamilton Harbour reminds me of a dog owner fed up with cleaning up after his dog in the house and cursing the mess but never thinking of training the dog. You can't vacuum up the whole bay.
To fix the harbour, the whole community has to act to significantly reduce the nutrients flowing into the bay.
The city should put the $500,000 into a campaign to get property owners to stop using fertilizers on lawns, to reduce hard surfaces on their properties, install rain gardens on residential, commercial and public lands and encourage green swathes along drainage channels in agricultural areas. Ban drain pipes from going into storm sewers. These are the simple steps leading to a big correction. There are many more.
The science is there. People do not understand how each one of us adds to the problem and how each one of us can contribute to the solution. Until we all care about the water and learn how we are harming it, the bay will only suffer and ourselves along with it. Remember where our drinking water comes from.
Bruce Mackenzie, Grimsby
More destruction on the way
It appears that Metrolinx is once again on its path of destruction of wide swaths of Hamilton. Not content with creating a large area on King Street that looks like a war zone and displacing many people (many of whom now live in tents), the powers to be are now embarking on a new wave of destruction.
The properties to be demolished are mostly in West Hamilton but not exclusively - the list of properties to be expropriated fills multiple full pages in the newspaper. Metrolinx's latest effort will see many more people displaced while our city councillors sit idly by. I pray this folly will end, but I don't hold much hope.
Paul John Phillips, Dundas
Use buses for LRT experiment
There has been ongoing debate regarding Hamilton's LRT project. A simple low-cost test to learn honestly what people think it would be like to have the buses on the route stop only at the proposed LRT stops for a month or perhaps even a week and review the comments.
This or something like it should have been done when the project was first brought forward as then there would be comments from potential users.
Craig Thompson, Hamilton
Voting splitting needs to stop
Re: The Ford way of running Ontario, Dec. 5
Re: There goes democracy, Dec. 5
Columnists Edward Keenan and Joan Little effectively outlined staggering mismanagement by the headstrong Ford government. Alas, the Progressive Conservatives will be difficult to unseat in the next election given their strong majority, formidable fundraising and record spending on partisan advertising.
There's a real danger the three opposition parties, led by capable leaders, may well ensure another Tory majority if they split the anti-government vote. The 2022 election, when the Progressive Conservatives won 40.8 per cent of the popular vote while the NDP and Liberals had 23.7 and 23.8 per cent respectively, speaks volumes.
It's time the opposition leaders consider strategic co-operation as envisioned by Steve Paikin, host of TVO's Agenda. Paikin's "non-aggression pact," as he calls it, would see the parties divide ridings. They would choose candidates according to which person, party label aside, has the best chance of defeating a PC candidate in each constituency.
While opposition co-ordination wouldn't necessarily end Progressive Conservative rule, it would offer hope of holding the Ford government to a more accountable minority at least.
Gord McNulty, Hamilton
Ford is on the right track
Say what you want about Doug Ford, but his move to override the courts by giving cities and police the power to dismantle the camps in our parks is the smartest move he could have made and basically the only move.
You have to wonder why Andrea Horwath is not on the list of mayors that have appealed to Ford weeks ago about the deplorable conditions our parks are in. Does she plan to walk in the parks and pick up the dirty needles herself?
The situation has become out of hand and it will only become worse, especially if our courts continue to allow our public spaces to become dumping grounds for drug paraphernalia and escalating violence.
Something has to be done and this initiative Ford has put forth is one of the best solutions anyone has brought forward.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown

Document 8 of 500

Canadians want a tough approach to Donald Trump - but some like him better than Justin Trudeau, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-want-tough-approach-donald-trump-some/docview/3141775719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+want+a+tough+approach+to+Donald+Trump+-+but+some+like+him+better+than+Justin+Trudeau%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-As Canadian politicians jockey to position themselves as the best to deal with a mercurial incoming U.S. president Donald Trump, a wary Canadian public is cool to any compromises and supports a tough approach, according to a pair of new polls.
Many Canadians - 40 per cent - are prepared to cut Mexico loose in any trade talks Canada has with the United States, and most - 56 per cent - are not prepared to welcome any influx of migrants fleeing northward to escape Trump's threatened mass deportation orders, according to an Abacus Data poll over the last week shared with the Star.
An Angus Reid Institute poll similarly shows almost half of Canadians - 49 per cent - want the federal government to take a tough approach to Trump's tariff threat to slap a 25 per cent surcharge on Canadian imports. They agree with the suggestion Ottawa should "play hardball - even if the tariffs come, Canada shouldn't let itself be bullied." Another 33 per cent said the federal government should "try to negotiate for a lower tariff," while only 10 per cent said Canada should "concede and do whatever the U.S. demands to avoid the tariffs."
Economists say Trump's threatened 25 per cent across-the-board tariff on imports from Canada and Mexico, with an additional 10 per cent tariff on China (which Trump has previously threatened with 60 per cent tariffs) would slam Canada's economy and trigger a recession.
The Angus Reid poll found that pessimistic view is reflected in the expectations of most Canadians - about 75 per cent expect the Canadian dollar to suffer and the economy to worsen, and nearly half expect their own finances to take a hit. As a result, more than half - 54 per cent - believe Canada should increase border security and 55 per cent want Ottawa to increase its defence spending to the NATO target of two per cent of GDP or higher - and on a faster pace than is currently planned - reflecting a desire to see the federal government meet Trump's demands.
Taken together, the surveys suggest a high level of anxiety among Canadians vis-à-vis the incoming Trump administration that political leaders may seek to allay, amplify or aggravate.
In the past week, they've done all three.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has sought to assure Canadians that he had a productive face-to-face meeting with Trump last week at the Mar-a-Lago resort, saying his government will commit unspecified additional funding to securing the Canada-U.S. border with more technology and manpower to work with the U.S. to stem flows of illegal immigrants and fentanyl - all while arguing Canada is not the same headache for Washington on those two issues that Mexico is.
The Liberals are hopeful Canada can win a reprieve, but say they're not there yet.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has amplified Trump's concerns and blamed Trudeau for having "lost control of our border, lost control of immigration, lost control of our finances." In French, he said Trump is an "aggressive president," and said Canada needs tougher immigration and border policies to deal with "border disorder." Poilievre has echoed Trump's America first discourse, saying he'd bolster border security, increase police powers, cap the number of immigrants and "false refugees" Canada lets in, and slash taxes, calling his approach a "Canada first" plan.
The NDP has aligned with the effort to allay Trump's concerns, while talking tough. Leader Jagmeet Singh demands 1,100 more front-line border guards be hired (the border guards' union says up to 3,000 more are needed) and wants the prime minister to "stand up and fight like hell."
Curiously, despite Trump's threats, Coletto's latest survey also shows that some Canadians' impressions of Donald Trump have improved over the last two weeks, with his positive impressions slightly higher than Trudeau's.
Abacus found 26 per cent of Canadians held a positive view of Trump, up five points in a month; 14 per cent had a neutral impression, but most - 58 per cent - have a negative view (down seven points in a month).
Meanwhile, Trudeau's negatives outweigh his positives, with 23 per cent citing a positive impression, 15 per cent a neutral one, and 61 per cent a negative one, according to the same poll.
In fact, some Canadians' views of Trump have improved since November 2020, near the end of Trump's first term in office, while their view of Trudeau has worsened. Back then, 11 per cent of Canadians had a positive view of Trump, 11 per cent were neutral, while 77 per cent had a negative view. That was after Trump renegotiated NAFTA with Canada and Mexico, slapped steel and aluminum tariffs (later withdrawn) on Canada, and withheld exports of personal protective equipment to Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic. At the same time 42 per cent of Canadians had a positive impression of Trudeau, 22 per cent a neutral view, and 35 per cent a negative impression, says Coletto.
Trudeau, and his Liberal party, have since plunged in national polls, with Abacus' latest findings showing a 23-point lead for the Poilievre-led Conservatives over Trudeau's Liberals.
Coletto says it's too early to know who Canadians think is doing the best job in responding to Trump, but he doubts Poilievre is taking much of a risk in purposely not joining "Team Canada" - in part, he says, because "the prime minister is even less popular than Trump is in Canada. He is seen as so either disliked or ineffective that this is almost giving Poilievre a space to not have to be seen as collaborative and supportive of this effort."
Furthermore, many Canadians - 40 per cent according to Abacus - agree with two Conservative premiers who flatly said Canada should negotiate a separate free trade agreement with the United States that does not include Mexico, as Ontario's Doug Ford and Alberta's Danielle Smith stated.
Among those who identify as Liberal supporters, those who think it's a good idea to exclude Mexico is 42 per cent. And among Conservative supporters, it's higher still: 50 per cent agree that Mexico should be cut loose in future trade talks.
Trudeau has said his government is "leaving all doors open" on negotiating trade matters with Trump. "Ideally, we do that as a united North American market," he told reporters at press conference in Toronto two weeks ago. "But, pending decisions and choices that Mexico has made, we may have to look at other options."
Abacus Data surveyed 2,720 Canadian adults from Nov. 29 to Dec. 4, using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have a margin of error of plus or minus 1.9 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The Angus Reid Institute conducted an online survey from Nov. 29 to Dec. 2, using an online panel of 3,003 Canadian adults, and a random sample of this size would carry a margin of error of plus or minus 1.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
CAPTION: Then-U.S. President Donald Trump, left, and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau talk before to a NATO roundtable meeting in Watford, Hertfordshire, England on Dec. 4, 2019.

Subject: International trade; Canadians; Prime ministers; Immigration; Border security; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade Tariffs

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Dec. 7: Tackle the cause of harbour algae, vote splitting needs to stop and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-7-tackle-cause-harbour-algae-vote-splitting/docview/3141775428/se-2?accountid=15090

This or something like it should have been done when the project was first brought forward as then there would be comments from potential users.
Craig Thompson, Hamilton

Re: The Ford way of running Ontario, Dec. 5
Re: There goes democracy, Dec. 5

Ford is on the right track
Say what you want about Doug Ford, but his move to override the courts by giving cities and police the power to dismantle the camps in our parks is the smartest move he could have made and basically the only move. You have to wonder why Andrea Horwath is not on the list of mayors that have appealed to Ford weeks ago about the deplorable conditions our parks are in. Does she plan to walk in the parks and pick up the dirty needles herself? The situation has become out of hand and it will only become worse, especially if our courts continue to allow our public spaces to become dumping grounds for drug paraphernalia and escalating violence. Something has to be done and this initiative Ford has put forth is one of the best solutions anyone has brought forward.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Release police budget earlier
In a press release, the Hamilton Police Service Board has requested comments from the public regarding the 2025 budget which will be presented to the board at its meeting on Dec. 17. However, there is a deadline for submitting those comments: "Community members may submit their opinions .... up until 5 p.m. on Monday, Dec. 9, 2024." The 2025 budget itself will be made public on the following day, Dec. 10, when the agenda for the meeting is posted. It is impossible for the public to make an informed comment about the budget without being able to access it. The bad faith and contempt for the people of Hamilton inherent in this approach could not be more apparent.
Andrew Bell, Stoney Creek
Tax holiday will be a mess
This sounds too absurd to be true. I dad lunch at our local pub. Brought the machine and the receipt back to our server at the bar and jokingly said something like next week the HST wouldn't be on the bill. Asked how they were coping with getting their systems in line. She said it's a nightmare, but this is the incredible part. She said that if you have a beer or a wine it's tax exempt. But if you have a mixed drink, one that has a soft drink mixed with an alcoholic beverage, then it is taxed. I don't know if this is correct, but if so, what a mess. Then again, the whole thing is a mess. But we shouldn't be surprised. Desperate times (a.k.a. not getting re-elected) call for desperate measures. Monty Python couldn't top this.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Blame Trudeau for tariffs
Remember Justin Trudeau infamously stating "Canada has no core identity?" Certainly in the past 10 years he has done his best to make that a fact. But now, when faced with 25 per cent tariffs, the levy of which can be blamed on his incompetence of the immigration and fentanyl files, he asserts that Pierre Poilievre is "un-Canadian" for not wanting to be part of "Team Canada." A team representing a country without "core identity. A bit rich it seems to me.
Neville Newman, Hamilton

Document 10 of 500

The Ford government needs to declare femicide an epidemic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-needs-declare-femicide-epidemic/docview/3141775412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ford+government+needs+to+declare+femicide+an+epidemic&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Association of Interval and Transition Houses (OAITH) defines femicide as a gender-based killing of a woman, child, trans woman, two-spirited person, or gender nonconforming individual where a man has been charged in relation to the death in Ontario.
OAITH's team reviews and analyses hundreds of media articles every year to examine relationships between victims and those charged or deemed responsible, including a prior history of violence, criminal justice charges laid at the time the crime was committed, cause of death, race, geography, age and the location where the crimes occurred.
Unfortunately, OAITH cannot possibly capture all femicides, partially because some femicides are not reported by the media while others are wrongly classified. Systemic forms of racism and oppression also contribute to the undervaluing marginalized folks who are killed by men and that can impact reporting.
OAITH has determined that femicides are preventable and that has been borne out by Ontario's Domestic Violence Death Review Committee (DVDRC), which maintains there are at least 39 identifiable risk factors.
In 2021 Prabhu Rajan, chief counsel to Ontario's chief coroner and chief forensic pathologist, became chair of the Ontario DVDRC. That year, the DVDRC published its last annual report analyzing intimate partner violence-related deaths for 2019 and 2020.
The committee reviewed 35 cases, making 59 recommendations. Yet, that was the last year a report was issued, ending 18 years of important femicide data collection, synthesis, recommendations and public access.
And, that was on Doug Ford's watch.
OAITH continues to release an annual femicide list to ensure the lives lost are remembered while bringing attention to the violence women experience and to initiate action.
This year's list - Nov. 26, 2023 to Nov. 25, 2024 - names a total of 62 women and children ranging in age from 86 to two months old.
It lays bare the fact that the Ford government needs to declare femicide an epidemic.
As for Ford stating, "I have four daughters. And if anyone ever touched my daughters, that would be the worst day of their lives. I have zero tolerance for any sort of violence against women or anyone in that fact," that is pure patriarchal, misogynous rhetoric that is not only completely out of touch, but just not good enough.
That statement has overtones of Donald Trump declaring he will take care of "the" women whether they want him to or not. Women don't need Ford to take care of them.
Women need meaningful, enforceable policy along with better funding for women's shelters, transition housing, deeply affordable housing as well as robust rent controls, $10-a-day child care, a living wage and a universal standard of living - a.k.a. basic income - so they can take care of themselves and their children.
The real solution was crafted by Ontario New Democrat MPPs Peggy Sattler, Lisa Gretzky, Jill Andrew and Kristyn Wong-Tam in the form of Bill 173, Intimate Partner Violence Epidemic Act (2024).
These progressives recognize Ford beating up his daughters' intimate partners does absolutely nothing for women living with, leaving and recovering from intimate partner violence as well as the threat of femicide and familicide.
And, 95 Ontario municipalities agree with the progressive New Democrat MPPs because these communities declared intimate partner violence an epidemic.
Ford, stop stalling and pass Bill 173 because that is what you promised hundreds of survivors and advocates who showed up at Queen's Park last April. But since then, nothing.
I echo the words of NDP Leader Marit Stiles when she asked Ford during question period, "How many more people have to die before this government declares intimate partner violence an epidemic?"
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford needs to stop stalling and declare intimate partner violence an epidemic in Ontario, Doreen Nicoll writes.
CREDIT: Doreen Nicoll

Ford needs to declare epidemic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-needs-declare-epidemic/docview/3141775109/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+needs+to+declare+epidemic&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 12 of 500

This is about as Grinchy as it gets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-is-about-as-grinchy-gets/docview/3141774937/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+is+about+as+Grinchy+as+it+gets&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Retail politics came on strong as Ontarians entered the holiday season. First, Premier Doug Ford's $200 gift to every resident; second, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's "GST holiday" on certain items.
Some of these items are essential, such as children's clothing and diapers. But the rest of the list - beer, wine, chips, candies, cake, Christmas trees - are nice to have, not essentials.
And that misses the point.
Tax breaks and transfers in the name of affordability, which is the new retail politics in Canada, should be targeted to low-income people struggling to get by. They're not.
They're also a drop in the bucket. The GST holiday, for instance, would only amount to a five per cent tax break on those select holiday items. That won't pay the rent.
What's more, nothing is stopping big stores from jacking up prices or nixing holiday sales. After all, the federal government just took the wind out of GST-free marketing incentives.
That's part of the folly of retail politics. They drain government coffers and subsidize big businesses.
But wait, there's more.
In the new year, the federal government will also send a $250 cheque to all workers who make under $150,000. Guess what? Ninety-five per cent of working Canadians make under $150,000.
Retail politics might offer broad appeal, but they do nothing to alleviate the struggles of Canada's poorest. The cheques and GST holiday are not going to help low-income households that are increasingly using food banks and facing homelessness this holiday season.
The premier and prime minister's announcements come on the heels of the new report card on child and family poverty from the anti-poverty watchdog coalition, Campaign 2000.
The report found record increases in child poverty in Canada for two straight years in a row.
By 2022, nearly one in five children, or 1.4 million, were below the poverty line. That's an increase of nearly five percentage points over two years - the largest rise in poverty on record in Canada.
The working poor are hurting, too.
Only three of Canada's 62 largest urban areas are affordable enough for a minimum-wage worker working full-time to afford to rent a two-bedroom apartment.
In cities like Toronto and Vancouver, working two full-time minimum-wage jobs means you still can't afford to rent a one-bedroom apartment.
News flash: $250 isn't going to help.
Instead of throwing $6.3 billion out the window on a one-time holiday gift that will do nothing to reduce poverty, the federal government should target supports to the lowest-income people in Canada.
The Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives has modelled a supplement to the Canada Child Benefit that targets families in deep poverty and provides them with a maximum of $8,500 per year for a family making $19,000 or less.
That's a cheaper bill for the federal government than the GST holiday.
And low-income families would benefit from $8,500 more a year in support. Canada would immediately cut child poverty rates in half with one single measure. Talk about getting bang for your buck.
It's one of Campaign 2000's priority recommendations to work toward eliminating child poverty.
The most meaningful thing the federal and provincial governments could do to reduce poverty is to target supports to the lowest-income households in Canada.
Failing to do that is about as Grinchy as it gets.
Leila Sarangi is the national director of Campaign 2000. David Macdonald is a
senior economist with
the Canadian Centre
for Policy Alternatives.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Leila Sarangi and David Macdonald;

Document 13 of 500

Is more hallway health care the price of privatization?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-more-hallway-health-care-price-privatization/docview/3141358137/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+more+hallway+health+care+the+price+of+privatization%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has to be disappointed by the lack of progress on "hallway health care."
Compared to when they were elected in June 2018, "unconventional spaces" for patients have doubled to roughly 2,000 per day, on average.
At our city's largest employer, Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS), about 100 patients per day were bedding down in the hallway when the provincial Tories swept to power on a pledge to fix hallway health care. By the beginning of this year, the number ballooned another 12.5 per cent. Toronto's Unity Health saw a 68.3 per cent surge. Niagara Health System saw a 37.9 per cent increase.
Like the tip of an iceberg, the more serious challenge is merely hinted at by patients spilling into hospital hallways. Despite increasing health spending 31 per cent to more than $85 billion, the Ford government is now confronting a tidal wave of delayed surgeries impacted by a shortage of hospital staff.
Statistics Canada reports that the industry with the biggest job vacancy rate increase compared to before the pandemic is health and welfare, recording an explosive 56.8 per cent boost. That is a stark contrast to the general trend, which saw the job vacancy rate across all industries decline 6.8 per cent in the same period.
Looking at Ontario hospital job vacancies tells a similar story. According to the Ontario Hospital Association, before the Ford government was elected, the 2018 service vacancy rate was 3.9 per cent. By the fall of 2022, the vacancy rate soared 155 per cent.
At HHS, there are 1,502 vacancies, according to the Hamilton Spectator. That has contributed to a 6,600 surgery backlog.
"We've already noticed that the hospital is pulling job postings, leaving jobs vacant when we desperately need more front-line workers," says Canadian Union of Public Employees Local 7800 president Jillian Watt at HHS. "We know there is a deep health-care staffing crisis and it's terrifying to contemplate there may be more ... It means longer wait times in the ER, a longer list for surgeries and fewer staff to care for patients."
As a retired job evaluation specialist for Canada's largest union, I know that staff retention and recruitment difficulties can mean an unfair pay structure. Ontario operated with the lowest per capita hospital spending of any province even before Doug Ford took office.
His government pitched privatization as a cure-all for health care backlogs and emergency room closures. Ontario Council of Hospital Unions/Canadian Union of Public Employees' research indicates the Progressive Conservatives increased the 2023-24 "Independent Health Facilities" (private, for-profit, surgical and diagnostic procedures) budget estimate 212 per cent while restraining hospital funding at 0.5 per cent. The unfortunate truth, though, is that privatization does not fund more beds.
Look at the case of private, for-profit, agency nurses, highlighted by the Canadian Federation of Nurses Unions (CFNU). More money for private providers does not get to the root of the problem.
Across Canada, governments added more than $1.2 billion in spending on for-profit agency nurses with little effect over the last three years, according to the CFNU. In Ontario, the Ford government increased spending on for-profit agency nurses by more than 1,475 per cent between 2020 and 2024, with little impact on staff shortages.
More broadly, the CFNU study suggests the probable negligible impact of increased temporary staff ratios across the broader health-care system. The use of more private staffing solutions does not appear to have had an impact on staff shortages and backlogs.
The real answer to health-care staff shortages and medical backlogs lies elsewhere. Getting more bang for our health-care buck means redirecting private health-care profits to reducing health-care vacancies. Step 1 to getting medicare back on a more solid footing is the recognition that for-profit privatization does not work for public goods.
Medicare is not for sale. Health care should not be a racket.
CAPTION: Like the tip of an iceberg, the more serious challenge is merely hinted at by patients spilling into hospital hallways. Despite increasing health spending, the Ford government is confronting a tidal wave of delayed surgeries impacted by a shortage of hospital staff, Tom Baker writes.
CREDIT: Tom Baker

Subject: Hospitals; Workforce planning; Job openings; Privatization; Surgery; Health care policy; Nurses; Medicare; Profits; Labor shortages

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Workforce planning Job openings Privatization Medicare Profits Labor shortages

Location: Canada

Document 14 of 500

Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford have one eye on Donald Trump and another on their own futures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-pierre-poilievre-doug-ford-have/docview/3141358049/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%2C+Pierre+Poilievre+and+Doug+Ford+have+one+eye+on+Donald+Trump+and+another+on+their+own+futures&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a collective campaign to save Canada from Donald Trump's tariffs.
And then there's the competing political campaigns:
 Justin Trudeau is campaigning to be re-elected as prime minister, boasting that he's practiced at placating the president-elect.
 Pierre Poilievre is campaigning to displace him, promising to push back against America when empowered as a Conservative PM.
 Doug Ford is campaigning to be re-elected premier, while still aspiring to be prime minister one day.
May the most patriotic (and persuasive) politician win the day.
Canadians traditionally unite against a common adversary. Hence the homage, of late, to the Team Canada spirit of the past - when political rivals rallied to protect our patrimony and economy.
The father of that Team Canada playbook, former Liberal PM Jean Chrétien, was invited to meet Ontario's Progressive Conservative premier the other day for a brainstorming photo-op. First ministers also held a video-conference last week in that same spirit.
It's always comforting to watch premiers and prime ministers, past and present, publicly pat each other on the back lest they stab one another in the front. But beyond the bonhomie, beware the rivalry.
As federal opposition leader, Poilievre has a more rambunctious playbook. It's part of his job description to criticize the government of the day, but nowhere is it written that he must uncritically amplify the hyperbolic rhetoric of an incoming American government.
Trump's misapprehensions about smuggling (of both drugs and humans) have become fresh ammunition for the Conservatives to portray the border as a hot mess. It's not - at least not now.
Which is why the Trudeau government has publicly countered the exaggerations, while also promising to counteract any existing border problems before they get out of hand. It's undeniably complicated to control the world's longest undefended frontier, but Ottawa gets credit for reaching a deal with Washington in early 2023 to stop the flow of asylum-seekers at Roxham Road.
Beyond the modalities of border crossings, however, it's the political boundaries that are being transgressed in interesting ways.
Poilievre taunts Trudeau as powerless, because he is unpopular at home and unloved by America's president-elect. It is conventional wisdom among Conservatives that only a new government in Ottawa, empowered by a new electoral mandate, can give Canada the reset it requires in the bilateral relationship.
Equally at Queen's Park, the presumption - or pretext, perhaps - is that a federal government on its last legs in Ottawa must be shored up by a more popular Ontario government. Hence the premier is auditioning for the role of Captain Canada by talking up bilateral issues.
But the posturing betrays a misunderstanding of how unpredictable power and popularity can be. Trump was toast just a few years ago and now he's a political titan again.
Relationships are no less ephemeral and situational. Trudeau proved that point by pre-empting much of the world with his impromptu flight to Florida where he dined at Mar-a-Lago, with both sides beaming.
Why would an ascendant Trump hobnob with Trudeau at his lowest moment? Didn't he disdain the prime minister in the past, accusing him of dishonesty and disrespect?
We can't read Trump's mind. But we can watch his body language and read his social media musings.
He cares about crowd size. But he also relishes star power.
Say what you will about Trudeau's declining aura at home, he is still a celebrity abroad. Trump was clearly chuffed that the world-famous prime minister came at a moment's notice to give him face.
The notion that Trump still holds a grudge against him, after a tiff at a summit years ago, is a misreading of his mercurial ways. The president-elect loves nothing more than supplicants who make amends for past slights.
Trump famously mocked Marco Rubio mercilessly when the Florida senator ran against him for the Republican nomination a decade ago. Now Rubio is his nominee for Secretary of State.
Trump evidently derives greater satisfaction from former adversaries who pay homage than from fellow travellers seeking favours. The notion that a global unknown like Poilievre, eager to prove his bona fides as a fellow right-winger, would win Trump's affection or bend to his will remains untested.
Ford is certainly well placed to advocate for Canada's industrial heartland - his folksy style is a good fit for Americans. Ontario has produced a series of earnest ads for broadcast on the Fox TV network extolling our indispensability and amity for America, and the premier made his own appearance this week.
In fact, Ford is the country's most powerful Conservative politician, but like the federal opposition leader - who styles himself our prime minister in waiting - the premier remains a relative unknown in America. Unlike Poilievre, however, Ford is at least singing from the same Team Canada songbook as Trudeau.
Which leaves us with two different playbooks from our political leaders for saving Canada from Trump. And three distinct campaigns to save their own political skins in the elections to come.
CAPTION: Donald Trump speaks at a campaign rally at PPG Paints Arena on Nov. 4, 2024, in Pittsburgh, Pa.

Subject: Conservatism; Political campaigns; Boundaries; Decades; Politics; Prime ministers

Location: Florida; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford have one eye on Donald Trump and another on their own futures

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 6, 2024

Section: politics/political-opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3141358049

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-pierre-poilievre-doug-ford-have/docview/3141358049/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 15 of 500

Is more hallway health care the price of privatization?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-more-hallway-health-care-price-privatization/docview/3141356167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+more+hallway+health+care+the+price+of+privatization%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has to be disappointed by the lack of progress on "hallway health care."
Compared to when they were elected in June 2018, "unconventional spaces" for patients have doubled to roughly 2,000 per day, on average.
At our city's largest employer, Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS), about 100 patients per day were bedding down in the hallway when the provincial Tories swept to power on a pledge to fix hallway health care. By the beginning of this year, the number ballooned another 12.5 per cent. Toronto's Unity Health saw a 68.3 per cent surge. Niagara Health System saw a 37.9 per cent increase.
Like the tip of an iceberg, the more serious challenge is merely hinted at by patients spilling into hospital hallways. Despite increasing health spending 31 per cent to more than $85 billion, the Ford government is now confronting a tidal wave of delayed surgeries impacted by a shortage of hospital staff.
Statistics Canada reports that the industry with the biggest job vacancy rate increase compared to before the pandemic is health and welfare, recording an explosive 56.8 per cent boost. That is a stark contrast to the general trend, which saw the job vacancy rate across all industries decline 6.8 per cent in the same period.
At HHS, there are 1,502 vacancies, according to the Hamilton Spectator. That has contributed to a 6,600 surgery backlog.
"We've already noticed that the hospital is pulling job postings, leaving jobs vacant when we desperately need more front-line workers," says Canadian Union of Public Employees Local 7800 president Jillian Watt at HHS. "We know there is a deep health-care staffing crisis and it's terrifying to contemplate there may be more ... It means longer wait times in the ER, a longer list for surgeries and fewer staff to care for patients."
As a retired job evaluation specialist for Canada's largest union, I know that staff retention and recruitment difficulties can mean an unfair pay structure. Ontario operated with the lowest per capita hospital spending of any province even before Doug Ford took office.
His government pitched privatization as a cure-all for health care backlogs and emergency room closures. Ontario Council of Hospital Unions/Canadian Union of Public Employees' research indicates the Progressive Conservatives increased the 2023-24 "Independent Health Facilities" (private, for-profit, surgical and diagnostic procedures) budget estimate 212 per cent while restraining hospital funding at 0.5 per cent. The unfortunate truth, though, is that privatization does not fund more beds.
Look at the case of private, for-profit, agency nurses, highlighted by the Canadian Federation of Nurses Unions (CFNU). More money for private providers does not get to the root of the problem.
Across Canada, governments added more than $1.2 billion in spending on for-profit agency nurses with little effect over the last three years, according to the CFNU. In Ontario, the Ford government increased spending on for-profit agency nurses by more than 1,475 per cent between 2020 and 2024, with little impact on staff shortages.
More broadly, the CFNU study suggests the probable negligible impact of increased temporary staff ratios across the broader health-care system.
The use of more private staffing solutions does not appear to have had an impact on staff shortages and backlogs.
The real answer to health-care staff shortages and medical backlogs lies elsewhere. Getting more bang for our health-care buck means redirecting private health-care profits to reducing health-care vacancies. Step 1 to getting medicare back on a more solid footing is the recognition that for-profit privatization does not work for public goods.
Medicare is not for sale. Health care should not be a racket.
Tom Baker is a retired union job evaluation specialist. He lives in Burlington.
CAPTION:
