Ford ready to override courts and 'dismantle' homeless encampments: Could give 'enhanced powers' to municipalities and police

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-ready-override-courts-dismantle-homeless/docview/3141356132/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+ready+to+override+courts+and+%27dismantle%27+homeless+encampments%3A+Could+give+%27enhanced+powers%27+to+municipalities+and+police&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is prepared to override the courts by giving municipalities and police "enhanced legislative powers" to "dismantle" homeless encampments across Ontario.
"We are acting to put an end to the public disorder, drug use and trafficking and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth in encampments," Ford said in a letter to 12 Ontario mayors.
His comments came in a response mayors who appealed to Ford to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts aren't overturned by the courts.
"I am confident that the legislative authorities the province is preparing are common sense, practical and entirely aligned with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, especially for the law-abiding residents who simply want to enjoy the benefits of their local public spaces," the premier told them.
"For that reason, I do not expect the legislation will need to proactively invoke Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms," he said.
"However, should the courts interfere with our shared goal of effectively addressing and clearing out encampments using these enhanced tools, with your support, our government is fully prepared to use the notwithstanding clause."
His letter came after the mayors of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury and Windsor appealed to him two weeks ago for help.
They urged his government to "become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments" and "strengthen the existing system of mandatory community-based and residential mental health care and to expand service to treat those who have severe and debilitating addictions."
While there are more than 1,000 people living in tents in Toronto parks throughout the city, Mayor Olivia Chow did not sign the letter.
The premier agreed there is "an urgent need to do more and I am thrilled that you have shown considerable leadership in putting forward concrete and actionable solutions."
"Ontario is finalizing the details of new initiatives, including enhanced legislative powers, that will be responsive to your requests," he wrote.
"The new legislation and supporting initiatives will achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Legislation, which is expected before the house rises for the holiday break next Thursday, will include a funding boost "to create more capacity in our shelter systems, supported by the new accountability measures to ensure these funds support dismantling encampments."
As well, it will gives police "new tools and authorities" to further curb the use of illicit drugs in public.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he was "very pleased with the response from the premier."
"We don't permit illicit drugs in our Peel shelters and it's why we have seen encampments grow among those struggling with addictions," said Brown.
"We simply can't have dangerous drug use in our parks and public spaces. The commitment to clearly prohibit it and back it up legally is helpful," he said.
"I just hope the next shoe to fall is compassionate intervention for addictions."
In Waterloo last year, a judge ruled that evicting those living in homeless encampments would be a violation of their Charter rights if no shelter spaces are available. A similar judicial ruling was also handed down in Kingston.
Use of the notwithstanding clause - which Ford has invoked before, being the first Ontario premier to use the Constitution's nuclear option - allows a government to pass legislation overriding certain rights.
On Tuesday, the auditor general criticized him for his decision to close 10 of 17 Ontario's supervised consumption sites in favour of 19 Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment hubs.
Auditor general Shelley Spence said shuttering the facilities near schools and daycares was made "without proper planning."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says the government will put an end to the loss of public space regarding homeless encampments in Ontario.Tents are shown at an encampment in Toronto's Alexandra Park. Ontario Premier Doug Ford says new legislation is coming that will target homeless encampments across the province.Chris Young The Canadian PressChris Young The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Toronto Star

Dec. 6: Police budget process is senseless, ease up on road salt and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-6-police-budget-process-is-senseless-ease-up/docview/3141355929/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+6%3A+Police+budget+process+is+senseless%2C+ease+up+on+road+salt+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This process makes no sense
In the Dec. 2 edition of the Spectator there was an article about how Hamilton police want public input regarding another budget increase for 2025 of 5.7 per cent to be submitted by Dec. 9. I commented on this after a social media post from a councillor questioning how we can give input if we have no details.
I was surprised to see a direct response to me from Hamilton Police Service on my social media. It read as follows:
"Don - The Hamilton Police Service Board requested communication from the public on the draft budget increase of 5.7 per cent. The Hamilton Police Service will present its budget to the Hamilton Police Service Board on Dec. 17. The Budget report will be available to the public on Dec. 10 on the Board's website. If you have any questions or comments, please reach out to the Hamilton Police Service Board at kstevenson@hamilton.ca."
So it would appear we are supposed to supply input on the increase by Dec. 9, but can't see any details until Dec. 10. Does this make any sense to anyone?
For perspective, if they are successful and are granted this increase it would amount to a 20.82 per cent increase total over the last three years while crime in the city is at an all-time high. That alone tells me we need to take a very close look at the budget to ensure dollars are being spent where needed.
Don Holland, Hamilton
Put vacant lot to good use
Re: Collapsed downtown buildings once burnt to the ground, Dec. 2
Surely it will take many years before we see any development at this location given the city's past record. I propose we ask the owners to put up some wire fencing and lay down some artificial turf and let the kids safely kick a ball round and make good, healthy use of the space until then.
Joe Benedetti, Hamilton
Ease up on the road salt use
Earlier this week, an HSR bus stop area was buried under "more than necessary" rock salt. Surely people do not want to slip on icy surfaces, but do we really need to apply so much salt that would kill the Dead Sea? Not to mention the surrounding environment. A little more care should be exercised when applying something as destructive as salt to our world.
Marilyn Miller, Hamilton
What happened to stewardship?
The concept of stewardship - caring for resources, communities, and future generations - has all but disappeared from modern governance. Once a cornerstone of leadership, stewardship has been overshadowed by profit-driven priorities, short-term expediency, and political self-interest.
Consider Ontario's Highway 413, a project that will devastate farmland, wetlands and wildlife habitats while saddling taxpayers with enormous costs - all for the dubious benefit of shaving a few minutes off commute times. The push to get beer and wine into convenience stores ahead of schedule cost Ontario taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars. And the sell-off and redevelopment of Ontario Place risks transforming a beloved public green space into a privatized playground. Meanwhile, critical needs remain unmet. Hospitals are chronically underfunded. Homelessness and the shortage of affordable housing continue to escalate.
These symptoms of political neglect reflect an abandonment of true stewardship. Leadership requires foresight, compassion, and a commitment to the common good. Ontarians must demand accountability and insist on stewardship as a guiding principle. It is our money, our communities and our future at stake.
James Newman, Hamilton
Loyalty without question
Re: America has spoken, Dec. 3
On Dec. 3, one contributor states in a letter "absolutely nothing" would alter his support for Donald Trump. Absolutely nothing. There you have it folks. There is nothing Trump could do to alienate these people. There is no behaviour he could engage in, or policy he could endorse, that would turn them off. No sin or crime he could commit. No standard of ethics or morality he could possibly violate. No higher principle he will be held to (except maybe "toughness"). No situation or scenario that would turn them against Dear Leader. Loyalty without condition, obedience without question. This is the world we're entering into. Brace yourselves.
Steve Dylag, Burlington
Where are Tory MPPs?
Re: Place the blame for homelessness where it belongs, Dec. 3
Thank you for publishing Margaret Skimba's missive on putting the blame squarely on Doug Ford's government, and the two MPPs from his cabinet that are supposed to be working for Hamiltonians. Neil Lumsden rarely responds to emails and I couldn't tell you anything he has done for Hamilton except flip burgers at a Stoney Creek event. Useless. I don't even think he attends Ticats games, even though he is the minister of sport. The Progressive Conservatives are making a war on the poor, not to mention pushing the middle class off of the affordability cliff.
Natasha Huyer, Hamilton
Housing should be replaced
I was not to surprised to see this application in Thursday's Spec about expropriation buildings along the LRT plan. I hope the city will take a good look at how many of these buildings house affordable rentals and will displace renters to further strain on our homeless situation. I feel if they displace they should be accountable to replace.
Wayne Battaglio, Hamilton
Resignation a telling sign
Re: Phil Verster quits top job at Ontario's Metrolinx, Dec. 2
This 2 1/2-inch story buried on Page A9 of the Dec. 3 Spectator should have been front page news given the disaster that is Hamilton's own Metrolinx experience.
We reflect back to a Nov. 7 photo of a smiling Mayor Andrea Horwath and a stoic-looking Phil Verster, announcing that "Hamilton's off-and-on LRT project is finally going to tender-again."
As Metrolinx CEO, Verster has overseen the debacle that is the Toronto Eglinton Crosstown LRT which was supposed to be completed in 2020 and there is still no firm timeline as to when this project, billions of dollars over budget, will be completed.
Look for a similar fate to befall the Hamilton LRT. Provincial and federal governments have kicked in $3.4 billion for the 14-kilometre route from Eastgate Square to McMaster University but by the province's own reckoning, construction costs have already risen 30 per cent. Who pays for overruns? Who will operate the system? These and dozens of other questions remained unanswered. They will come, we are told, sometime in the future. Of course. No timelines, no responsibility and information secrecy that further muddies the local LRT project.
And so we bid farewell to Mr. Vester and his more that $830,000 a year salary. He leaves a legacy of ineptitude and countless lawsuits swirling around the Toronto LRT projects. Reports say he is leaving the country for another job. Good luck to the person who tries to right the Metrolinx ship.
Norm Alexander, Dundas

Document 18 of 500

The Ford Way of running Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-way-running-ontario/docview/3140792358/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ford+Way+of+running+Ontario&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a phrase in provincial auditor general Shelley Spence's report that could, by now, serve as a slogan for Doug Ford's government: "Without proper planning."
That was her description, in Tuesday's annual report, of how the decision to close supervised-consumption drug sites was made.
But six years into the life of a government that has always shown an eagerness to fire before it aims, it seems more like an all-purpose description of The Doug Ford Way.
The report contains plenty of other language that might seem jolting when applied to government actions, but by now seems overly familiar. The decisions to issue minister's zoning orders (or MZOs) were "not fair, transparent or accountable." The assessment process for the Ontario Place redevelopment was "irregular" and "subjective," and "rules and guidelines ... were not followed."
Another description of MZOs that may as well be a shorthand summary of this government's standard operating procedure: "no protocol and no apparent rationale."
Those descriptions could easily describe any number of the Ford government's other moves: slashing the size of Toronto's city government and reorganizing other GTA governments in the middle of an election campaign; the whole Greenbelt hokey-pokey; trying to slash funding to Toronto Public Health a year before a global pandemic; the eventually reversed decision to change municipal boundaries; the eventually reversed decision to dissolve Peel region; the recent move to rip out bike lanes.
The list is not exhaustive.
There are a few themes there that are at the forefront of Spence's report. Decisions seem to be made quickly and on impulse, according to either the political whims and vendettas of the premier or the backroom desires of developers and corporate interests. Traditional accountability checks or analysis of impacts are discarded. Rinse, repeat.
Sometimes, Ford and his government reverse course when it becomes clear what they are doing is phenomenally stupid and massively unpopular. But a willingness to admit a mistake has never caused them to pause and think before steamrolling forward into the next big blunder.
If it wasn't for hindsight, they wouldn't have any kind of sight at all. Ford may be willing to change his mind, but he has never changed his ways.
So where does that leave us?
Well, in the case of Ontario Place, we see that some bidders had closed-door meetings with the premier's staff and other government officials (despite this being explicitly prohibited) and that they "had direct access to an Infrastructure Ontario executive." Rules for assessing the bids were not clear and were not followed.
And the redevelopment costs to be borne by the province have more than quadrupled to $2.237 billion.
Oh, and the cost of moving the Ontario Science Centre to Ontario Place has ballooned such that it is now more expensive than the estimates for rehabbing the old building.
On supervised injection sites, the auditor notes harm reduction strategies that prevented 1,500 deaths from overdoses are being discontinued without proper planning or impact analysis.
Which sounds like a roundabout way to say people are likely to die.
If there's another thing that has defined Ford's government it's boastfulness - and that's in the auditor's report, too. The government tripled its advertising spending in one year (to $103.5 million), setting an Ontario government record. The report concludes that about 62 per cent of these dollars were essentially partisan: their primary objective was "to promote the governing party."
If the ad bonanza continues into what is expected to be an election year next year, the report does offer some fantastic, auditor-approved tag lines. "Ontario: no protocol and no apparent rationale" has a certain ring to it. The ring of truth.
Edward Keenan is a Torstar columnist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Edward Keenan Opinion;

Subject: Redevelopment

Document 19 of 500

There goes our democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/there-goes-our-democracy/docview/3140792276/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+goes+our+democracy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Spectator story last week caught my eye about Hamilton councillors being aghast at strong-mayor powers.
Those make councillors mere onlookers on budgets they should be setting, because the mayor can veto their decision. Welcome to Doug Ford's version of democracy, a.k.a. autocracy!
For those unaware of his new system - Burlington has it too - a strong mayor has absolute autonomy on some issues, including operating and capital budgets, and must produce them within a 30-day time frame.
This means public involvement is limited. Why even elect councils? Just people to clutter up a mayor's agenda. But aren't they there as voices for people they represent?
Councils have been emasculated. All it would take is an egotistical unthinking mayor to create chaos. But a Progressive Conservative one would be even more likely to follow his bidding.
And is Ford the dynamic visionary leader you'd hitch your wagon to? PC governments traditionally watched spending, but Ford's Ontario is projecting about a $6-billion deficit!
I was delighted when he first ran that a key platform plank was to open thousands of new long-term-care beds. How's that working for caregivers burning out and four-year waiting lists?
Now he's handing out rebates in an early pre-election gambit. Some staunch PC friends are jittery about some of his judgment calls: the Ontario Science Centre replacement, a spa at Ontario Place, with its tree loss, and the early unpriced contract buyout to put beer in corner stores.
Worst of all, the forfeiture of our enshrined Greenbelt until public fury forced his "sorry about that" 180. And he's determined to ram a highway through sensitive wetland without an environmental assessment. He's even talking about duplicating a highway by tunnelling under one. At what cost? Further, the RCMP is checking into favouritism to developer friends.
Councils and the public know his changes to the Planning Act have all but eliminated meaningful public input - the heart of what democracy's about.
It's almost impossible because his new deadlines are too short to gather needed information and provide decisions on applications. He actually passed, then later rescinded, legislation making councils rebate developers' fees if deadlines weren't met. Whether a proposal is minor or a huge one involving complex issues, the clock ticks out the same time frames.
Ford's Ontario Land Tribunal replaced the Ontario Municipal Board that had existed for generations. Cities have long called for the demise of such a tribunal, which Ontario alone in Canada has. There needs to be a forum for developers to dispute inaction or unreasonable treatment by councils, but appeals should be limited to only these situations.
Reaching a conclusion a developer didn't want should not allow an appeal, providing councils follow Planning Act rules. OLT appeals are usually heard by a lone provincial appointee with power to overturn an elected council's decision. The OLT has to go, or have its powers limited to address only malfeasance.
Why should a mayor be empowered to veto a council on housing? The housing starts Ford had projected haven't materialized. As the saying goes, "It's the economy, stupid!" It's not because of biased councils. Not blasé staff. Not overzealous citizens. In fact, Ford changed the act to disallow "third party" (citizen) appeals.
Provinces have jurisdictions and so do municipalities. Cities decide where their bike lanes belong. Suddenly Big Brother, in Toronto, will tell them where they should go?
While we're at it, do we even need councils? Think of the money we'd save with dictatorships! Premiers and strong mayors could do it all. Mayors could even be appointed, saving election costs.
There goes democracy! But it was kinda nice while it lasted. We even got to choose candidates we admired. Vote against those we didn't. Those were the good old days!
Freelance columnist
Joan Little is a former
Burlington alderperson
and Halton councillor. Reach her at specjoan@cogeco.ca.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Joan Little OPINION;

Document 20 of 500

There goes democracy. It was nice while it lasted

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-goes-democracy-was-nice-while-lasted/docview/3140792223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+goes+democracy.+It+was+nice+while+it+lasted&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Spectator story last week caught my eye about Hamilton councillors being aghast at strong-mayor powers.
Those make councillors mere onlookers on budgets they should be setting, because the mayor can veto their decision. Welcome to Doug Ford's version of democracy, a.k.a. autocracy!
For those unaware of his new system - Burlington has it too - a strong mayor has absolute autonomy on some issues, including operating and capital budgets, and must produce them within a 30-day time frame.
This means public involvement is limited. Why even elect councils? Just people to clutter up a mayor's agenda. But aren't they there as voices for people they represent?
Councils have been emasculated. All it would take is an egotistical unthinking mayor to create chaos. But a Progressive Conservative one would be even more likely to follow his bidding. And is Ford the dynamic visionary leader you'd hitch your wagon to? PC governments traditionally watched spending, but Ford's Ontario is projecting about a $6-billion deficit!
I was delighted when he first ran that a key platform plank was to open thousands of new long-term-care beds. How's that working for caregivers burning out and four-year waiting lists?
Now he's handing out rebates in an early pre-election gambit. Some staunch PC friends are jittery about some of his judgment calls: the Ontario Science Centre replacement, a spa at Ontario Place, with its tree loss, and the early unpriced contract buyout to put beer in corner stores.
Worst of all, the forfeiture of our enshrined Greenbelt until public fury forced his "sorry about that" 180. And he's determined to ram a highway through sensitive wetland without an environmental assessment. He's even talking about duplicating a highway by tunnelling under one. At what cost? Further, the RCMP is checking into favouritism to developer friends.
Councils and the public know his changes to the Planning Act have all but eliminated meaningful public input - the heart of what democracy's about.
It's almost impossible because his new deadlines are too short to gather needed information and provide decisions on applications. He actually passed, then later rescinded, legislation making councils rebate developers' fees if deadlines weren't met. Whether a proposal is minor or a huge one involving complex issues, the clock ticks out the same time frames.
Ford's Ontario Land Tribunal replaced the Ontario Municipal Board that had existed for generations. Cities have long called for the demise of such a tribunal, which Ontario alone in Canada has. There needs to be a forum for developers to dispute inaction or unreasonable treatment by councils, but appeals should be limited to only these situations.
Reaching a conclusion a developer didn't want should not allow an appeal, providing councils follow Planning Act rules. OLT appeals are usually heard by a lone provincial appointee with power to overturn an elected council's decision. The OLT has to go, or have its powers limited to address only malfeasance.
Why should a mayor be empowered to veto a council on housing? The housing starts Ford had projected haven't materialized. As the saying goes, "It's the economy, stupid!" It's not because of biased councils. Not blasé staff. Not overzealous citizens. In fact, Ford changed the act to disallow "third party" (citizen) appeals.
Provinces have jurisdictions and so do municipalities. Cities decide where their bike lanes belong. Suddenly Big Brother, in Toronto, will tell them where they should go?
While we're at it, do we even need councils? Think of the money we'd save with dictatorships! Premiers and strong mayors could do it all. Mayors could even be appointed, saving election costs.
There goes democracy! But it was kinda nice while it lasted. We even got to choose candidates we admired. Vote against those we didn't. Those were the good old days!
CAPTION: Under Doug Ford, municipal councils have been emasculated, Joan Little writes.

Subject: Mayors; Councils; Democracy; Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing starts

Trudeau's environment minister says he understands why Ontario Liberal leader is distancing herself from 'carbon tax'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeaus-environment-minister-says-he-understands/docview/3141158267/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+environment+minister+says+he+understands+why+Ontario+Liberal+leader+is+distancing+herself+from+%27carbon+tax%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said fighting climate change is "politically tough," which is why he understands that Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie is distancing herself from the carbon levy.
Crombie criticized the federal tax on Tuesday night during a party fundraising dinner. She said the federal carbon levy is "wrong."
"I'm not here to tell the prime minister how to do his job, but, I promise you, I will tell him when he's wrong. Like on the carbon tax," Crombie said.
Guilbeault said he wasn't surprised by Crombie's remarks, but he stands behind the levy.
"It's not the first time that she's said that, so there's nothing surprising there," he said. "Economists, including Nobel laureates, said that it's the best way to fight climate change."
Crombie is not alone among provincial Liberals in distancing herself from the federal tax.
Liberal Premier of Newfoundland Andrew Furey wrote an open letter to Trudeau in the spring calling on Trudeau to pause the annual increase in the tax. Susan Holt, the newly elected Liberal premier of New Brunswick has said she intends to draft her own carbon pricing plan, so her province can be exempt from the federal plan.
The federal carbon tax only applies in provinces that don't impose their own climate change plans that meet federal standards for emissions reductions. Only British Columbia and Quebec have provincially made plans, with all the other provinces taking the federal program.
The Liberal plan includes a levy on gasoline and heating fuels and rises every April. It also includes regular rebates paid to consumers.
Guilbeault said he understands the tax is unpopular, but it is the right thing to do.
"It's tough politically and fighting climate change. It is difficult and that's where you see leadership."
The minister was asked if he was calling Crombie a weak leader. He said simply that he is acknowledging the politics of climate change are hard.
"Being a leader is doing difficult things sometimes, even if they're unpopular, but they're the right thing to do."
At Queen's Park, Crombie emphasized that she wasn't presuming to tell Trudeau's government what to do.
"But our priorities are different. I have never supported the carbon tax," she said.
Asked by the Star if she was getting any blowback for her stance from federal Liberals, Crombie confided that unnamed MPs approached her at Tuesday's provincial Grit fundraiser to praise her for speaking out against a controversial measure that is causing them a political headache.
With files from Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault rises during Question Period on Parliament Hill in Ottawa in November.

Subject: Leadership; Carbon; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Environmental tax

Document 22 of 500

Justin Trudeau's 'carbon tax' is 'wrong,' Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-carbon-tax-is-wrong-ontario/docview/3140614523/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+%27carbon+tax%27+is+%27wrong%2C%27+Ontario+Liberal+Leader+Bonnie+Crombie+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Taking aim at Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon price as "wrong," Bonnie Crombie says her Ontario Liberals need to go their own way with an early provincial election looming.
"We have to get our elbows up," she said in a Tuesday night speech prepared for the first Liberal leader's fundraising dinner in eight years, putting $1.65 million into the party's campaign war chest.
It marked her strongest attempt yet to distance Ontario Grits from their federal cousins, who are struggling in the polls and have been blamed by pollsters for dragging provincial Liberal parties down.
"I'm not here to tell the prime minister how to do his job. But, I promise you, I will tell him when he's wrong. Like on the carbon tax," Crombie said in the speech at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre.
"And, I'd rather cut your income taxes permanently than cut you a rebate cheque."
The rebate remark was a dig at both Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, who have promised cheques of $250 and $200 respectively to defray the high cost of living.
Crombie said her promised middle class income tax cut proves she's moving the party - which has just nine MPPs for a distant third place in the legislature behind the official opposition New Democrats - toward the centre of the political spectrum after it veered left under former premier Kathleen Wynne.
Most voters are concerned about politicians "getting the basics right" such as fixing the doctor shortage and improving schools, Crombie added.
"They certainly don't need radical, far-left politicians who want them to feel bad about what they have, or how they live their lives," she said, echoing an NDP attack line from Ford.
While the premier has been focused on getting beer and wine in corner stores, removing bike lanes and building a "mega spa" at Ontario Place, problems like gridlock and homeless encampments in parks have grown worse, charged Crombie.
"I am not going to build a tunnel under the 401. But - I promise - I will get you a family doctor," she said in reference to her $3.1-billion plan to do so within four years. About 2.5 million Ontarians lack a family physician.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at the provincial party's convention in London, Ont. in September.

Subject: Carbon; Prime ministers; Environmental tax

Document 23 of 500

Ontarians shouldn't have to wait for an election to see a reduction in personal income taxes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-shouldnt-have-wait-election-see/docview/3139099360/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+shouldn%27t+have+to+wait+for+an+election+to+see+a+reduction+in+personal+income+taxes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie announced that, if elected, she would reduce taxes on personal income between $51,446 and $75,000.
This promise is strikingly similar to the tax relief plan Doug Ford proposed during the 2018 election but ultimately failed to deliver as premier.
To be fair, it's a good thing Crombie has brought tax relief into the policy conversation in Ontario. It's been 14 years since the last personal income tax reduction, and that was a modest one-percentage point reduction to the province's lowest tax bracket. However, her plan does not go nearly far enough to make Ontario's income tax system competitive within North America. It would still leave Ontario with some of the highest marginal tax rates at many levels of income.
Of course, tax rates influence the economic decisions people make. An individual decides whether it's worth working an extra hour of overtime, for instance, based in part on how much of the extra money they earn they will keep rather than hand over to the government. Ontario's personal income tax rates are among the highest in Canada and the United States across a large portion of the income distribution.
First, at 53.53 per cent, Ontario has the third-highest combined (federal and provincial/state) personal income tax rate in Canada and the U.S. for individuals earning $300,000. This makes it harder for Ontario to attract high-skilled mobile workers that fuel economic growth. Many politicians and business leaders have spoken of a desire to make Ontario a tech hub in North America, but such a high tax rate makes it hard to attract the people who can make that vision a reality.
Down the income scale, at $150,000, Ontario has the third-highest combined tax rate (again, in Canada and the U.S.) behind only Quebec and Nova Scotia. At $50,000, Ontario's has the ninth-highest combined income tax rate, which is significantly higher than rates in many of the nearby Great Lakes states with whom we compete economically. For example, our combined personal income tax rate at this income level is nearly eight percentage points higher than neighbouring Michigan.
In light of Ontario's poor position relative to its peers, Crombie's proposal for a two-point reduction to a middle-income bracket is a good idea, but only a small step in the right direction. Premier Ford and other provincial party leaders should consider a much more ambitious plan to reduce income tax rates for a much larger range of workers. If the government wants to promote economic growth, it should reduce the top rate to be more competitive with nearby jurisdictions. There's a great deal of evidence showing high personal income tax rates at higher levels of income hurt the economy by reducing incentives to work and invest, and thereby stifling economic activity, growth, job creation and prosperity across income levels.
It's long past time for personal income tax reform in Ontario. It's been 14 years since the provincial government cut personal income taxes, and the reduction was modest. Since then, the government has raised taxes on higher earners. Ontarians shouldn't have to wait for the next election; the Ford government should reduce personal income taxes now to encourage sorely needed economic growth in the province.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie wants a personal income tax reduction, but her plan doesn't go nearly far enough, the Fraser Institute's Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss write.
CREDIT: Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss

Subject: Income distribution; Tax rates; Relief provisions; Personal income; Economic growth; Income taxes

Document 24 of 500

Nov. 4: Downtown traffic will slow to a stop, Ford spoke for many of us and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-4-downtown-traffic-will-slow-stop-ford-spoke/docview/3139098127/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+4%3A+Downtown+traffic+will+slow+to+a+stop%2C+Ford+spoke+for+many+of+us+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Traffic will slow to a stop
Re: Main Street slowdowns loom amid week of lane closures, Dec. 1
The Spectator reported upcoming Main Street lane reductions, starting preparation for conversion to two-way traffic. Council has deemed this necessary to slow downtown Hamilton traffic in the interests of safety. Downtown Hamilton traffic is already unbearably slow, so it seems likely that the two-way Main Street with its ultimate complete street design will bring traffic to a near-standstill (one councillor said she wasn't interested in traffic flow).
It is already faster and easier for folks in West Hamilton and Dundas to drive to Burlington rather than suffer the delays of downtown traffic while getting to restaurants and businesses in central and east Hamilton. It is likely that the traffic standstill associated with a two-way Main Street will make Burlington even more attractive than Hamilton for these folks to dine and shop. The area of Hamilton below the mountain may essentially become two separate cities.
This may make it safer for the many pedestrians staring at their cellphones as they walk across busy intersections; the folks who bang on the windows of stopped cars, demanding money; the few cyclists seen using bike lanes crossing Main Street; and those anti-car Hamilton councillors who presumably are not driving to meetings at city hall. This may also create opportunities for restaurants and businesses to open new West Hamilton and Dundas outlets to recoup lost patronage of folks who may otherwise go to Burlington.
Fred Leech, Dundas
Wait for seniors intolerable
Re: Need long-term care in Hamilton? Here's how long will you have to wait, Dec. 2
It's absolutely horrible that our seniors have to wait so long for a long-term-care bed. Seems like our government is just waiting for them to die to solve the problem. The hospitals now put them in their satellite health facility and charge them $65 a day to live in poor conditions. They will likely make more of these so they can make money off the very people who not only can't afford this, but who built the system that is now charging them. The ineptitude of this government never fails to amaze. I find it ironic that on the very next page of the paper is the police once again asking for more money.
This city is in big trouble.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Protect our heritage links
City hall is attempting to protect and preserve structures of historical significance. That is a noble cause. Hamilton has an historically meaningful golf course that was designed by Stanley Thompson, a world-renowned designer. The courses are more than 100 years old. Let's protect those treasures for future generations that are using them right now.
Pat and Lynda Lynch, Hamilton
We need 'guaranteed income'
Re: Housing those in need through a guaranteed basic income, Nov. 30
Mark Pancer's piece on the value of basic income as a remedy for homelessness got my attention. I could not agree more. We have tried the opposite, in the 1990s, by reducing welfare rates to below the survival level. Those who lived through it or were front-line workers would agree with my friend who raised children as a single mom. She was staggered at the logic that starvation rates were good social policy. She said, "The welfare rates went down by 22 per cent and, almost overnight, the signs in my neighbourhood were significant: increases in crime, violence, drug use and homelessness. Young people lost hope in ever succeeding. Children went hungry."
Social policies which deepen poverty instead of relieving it have the effect of holding people down, not raising them to something better. A guaranteed, livable income has been shown to lift people up, offer a chance to finish their education or simply travel across town to seek work, instead of walking to food banks or standing in lines at soup kitchens.
We may believe that some of the poor are just lazy. Is that a reason for punishing the rest? The unemployed, mothers, seniors and newcomers? How does this sort of vindictiveness help? This is not Canadian. We can and should have a guaranteed income in Canada. Social assistance is basic, but it is not guaranteed, and definitely not livable. Don't believe me? Ask someone living in a tent.
Ann McRae, Dundas
Ford spoke for many of us
Re: Ford decries Trump tariff threat, Nov. 26
I never ever thought I would feel anything close to resembling admiration for Ontario's Premier Doug Ford, but I do.
When Ford recently responded calmly but in a state of shock after learning that president-elect Donald Trump was threatening to put a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods, Ford said of our U.S. neighbours that it is like being stabbed in the heart by a family member. That one sentence won my admiration.
It's true. It does feel like that. It hurts. And as Premier Ford spoke, he looked exactly like he had received a stunning shock.
I certainly hope that Ford plays a strong role in the communications with the Trump administration. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has his role, but as far as personality and strong messaging, Ford won hands down in this tariff matter response.
Ana Long, Hamilton
Colleges presidents lacked foresight
The CEOs of Stellantis, Intel and Metrolinx have resigned. The reason: poor results.
Contrast this with the presidents of Ontario's colleges and universities. While they do everything they can to lay the blame for their actual and projected shortfalls on a lack of government funding, a phrase that keeps cropping up is a search for "efficiencies," leaving the distinct impression that inefficiency in departments is also to blame. I hasten to add that I exclude the current president of Mohawk, who has only just recently taken the reins there.
A lack of foresight leading to a failure to provide for the inevitable "rainy day" is at the heart of post-secondary education's woes, and the responsibility for that lies with the presidents of these institutions.
While the aforementioned presidents have fallen on their swords in an act of public contrition, Ontario college and university presidents remain in their superannuated comfort. Does anyone else see something woeful in this situation?
Neville Newman, Hamilton
Family put in terrible position
Re: Man's cremated remains in limbo due to postal strike, Nov. 29
I am writing in regards to this despicable loss of compassion and responsibility for this family that would like to lay their father's ashes to rest.
Having worked many years in a medical laboratory in Hamilton hospitals (now retired), and currently working for a transportation service that moves human specimens between hospitals, specimens can be tracked at any time by bar code and location scans.
I am extremely baffled as to why Canada Post cannot track precisely where a package that has a bar code is at all times! If the package was scanned onto a truck that also is bar coded, that truck could be located. If they can't track exactly which truck it is on, or what warehouse it is located at, it is high time they improve their tracking abilities and responsibilities.
If they had this capability, I would surely hope someone (management or a union employee) would put their differences aside and show some compassion to deliver to the family their loved one's ashes. Have some respect, please!
Rob Hancock, Binbrook
Calling it like I see it
Re: Your blame is misplaced, Nov. 30
Rubes and ignoramuses? OK, grumpy Grandpa. If the citizens of Canada perceive politicians as corrupt and greedy, it's because they are often guilty of those charges. It seems there isn't a week that goes by that Justin Trudeau isn't mired in a new scandal. But the same could be said of past prime ministers like Stephen Harper and Brian Mulroney. Ontario has had more than four years of Doug Ford trying to line the pockets of wealthy investors who helped back his election run. While politicians blame illegal immigrants for stealing jobs, it's actually multinational corporations who bust unions using free trade agreements to send jobs to offshore sweatshops and layoff local labour for greater profits. The Bank of Canada destroys the real estate market, blaming the average Canadian for inflation, while Canada's banks turn a blind eye to money laundering and drug money. At the same time, oil companies, grocery chains and other businesses use COVID-19 to hike prices and make record profits selling half the product at twice the price. If citizens think of politicians and bureaucrats as corrupt and greedy, it is because they have yet to be proven wrong.
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
Put rebate funds into health care
Lord Doug wants give all his subjects a "gift" to purchase their loyalty. King Justin just got on the same train.
I recently spent four days in the ER at a local hospital. The people were great, the conditions much less so. So many stretchers packed against every inch of wall space. It felt like a stockyard for sick subjects. And far too few resources to bring about timely outcomes.
Gentlemen, give your heads a shake. Put the money where it's needed. A kingdom dies when its subjects wither away.
Peter Demik, Hannon
Enough with the government ads
Am I the only one that is totally annoyed by Doug Ford's advertisement about how great we have it in Ontario? Then at the end of it, he has the nerve to say that the ad has been paid for by the Government of Ontario. As a taxpayer, I find the ad offensive.
Henk ten Oever, Smithville
There are no simple solutions
A letter and a substantial article in Saturday's paper spoke about the huge disparities in public health between neighbourhoods. The hard part is figuring out how much is caused by the places in which people live. Wherever they live, people with lower incomes and mental health challenges tend to have greater health problems and die younger. They are also concentrated in certain neighbourhoods. Sometimes it is clear that those neighbourhoods raise physical risks from air or soil pollution, as the letter-writer suggests. The social causes, including crime, street drugs and poorer schools, are harder to quantify, but may be just as important. That is why we need public interventions that are directed at both neighbourhoods and at vulnerable individuals. As with so many of the issues we face, there is no simple solution. It's a lesson that I wish more politicians would learn.
Richard Harris, Hamilton
It's not easy to make healthy choices
Cigarette smoking is probably right up there with obesity as one of the top causes of health problems. This being the case, why are anti-smoking devices and medications and patches, etc., so darn expensive and why do insurance companies only partially cover these therapies, if at all? They usually only give you one shot to quit. Maybe, just maybe, if these therapies were less expensive or better coverage was available, a few less people would get sick and insurance companies might have to pay out less.
Same with obesity. Why are healthy foods so expensive? Why are supplements like protein drinks, fresh vegetables, etc., costing so much? Why are unhealthy alternatives so convenient? With all our health-care shortages and issues, maybe more time and money should be invested in "Making Canada Healthy Again." Do we really need a tunnel under a highway or another highway (413)?
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Liberals not proactive enough
The Trumpster has threatened to impose a 25 per cent tariff on all products from Canada if we do not get our house in order.
We have a Liberal government that for nine years has been reactive to issues, as opposed to taking a proactive approach. I call it the "do-nothing attitude." They are now reacting to a crisis that could been circumvented long ago.
The warnings about our national defence and the request to bump up our payments for NATO date back to 2016.
Unfortunately, the Liberal party has set the bar so low in this country that we are at subterranean levels. We have had the lowest productivity since 2015 and continue to spiral down.
We have suffered a government that has been irresponsible, reckless with taxpayers' money. It is embarrassing and disgraceful to see the Liberal party involved in numerous scandals. Canada is suffering from an absence of leadership and this is the reason we are caught up in the current situation with Trump.
It is a self-serving government that is tone-deaf to Canadians.
Our politicians have been put on notice and I hope that they will for once come through for Canadians. Make Canada great again.
Joanne Venzon, Hamilton
Use tariff threats to our advantage
Donald Trump's threat of a punitive 25 per cent tariff on all imports from Canada is reported to remain in place until Canada significantly reduces illegal drugs and migrants crossing the border to the U.S. Obviously, Canada must achieve that reduction.
This unfortunate situation gives Canada the opportunity to respond by threatening a 25 per cent tariff on all goods from the U.S. until it significantly reduces the large number of illegal firearms crossing the border into Canada.
Canada can take the position that this is not a retaliatory tax, but instead is simply following the U.S. precedent in using tariffs to achieve policy objectives. Trump would have little credibility if he were to claim this is unreasonable.
Fred Leech, Dundas
CAPTION: Emily Walstrom isn't sure where the cremated remains of her father, Dennis Walstrom, are due to the Canada Post strike. Both sides should show some compassion and help this family, a reader says.It is already faster and easier for folks in West Hamilton and Dundas to drive to Burlington rather than suffer the delays of downtown traffic while getting to restaurants and businesses in central and east Hamilton, a reader writes.

Document 25 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3139098102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wait for seniors
is intolerable
Re: Need long-term care in
Hamilton? Here's how long
will you have to wait, Dec. 2
It's absolutely horrible that our seniors have to wait so long for a long-term-care bed. Seems like our government is just waiting for them to die to solve the problem. The hospitals now put them in their satellite health facility and charge them $65 a day to live in poor conditions. They will likely make more of these so they can make money off the very people who not only can't afford this, but who built the system that is now charging them. The ineptitude of this government never fails to amaze. I find it ironic that on the very next page of the paper is the police once again asking for more money.
This city is in big trouble.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
'Guaranteed
income' needed
Re: Housing those in need through a guaranteed basic
income, Nov. 30
Mark Pancer's piece on the value of basic income as a remedy for homelessness got my attention. I could not agree more. We have tried the opposite, in the 1990s, by reducing welfare rates to below the survival level. Those who lived through it or were front-line workers would agree with my friend who raised children as a single mom. She was staggered at the logic that starvation rates were good social policy. She said, "The welfare rates went down by 22 per cent and, almost overnight, the signs in my neighbourhood were significant: increases in crime, violence, drug use and homelessness. Young people lost hope in ever succeeding. Children went hungry."
Social policies which deepen poverty instead of relieving it have the effect of holding people down, not raising them to something better. A guaranteed, livable income has been shown to lift people up, offer a chance to finish their education or simply travel across town to seek work, instead of walking to food banks or standing in lines at soup kitchens.
We may believe that some of the poor are just lazy. Is that a reason for punishing the rest? The unemployed, mothers, seniors and newcomers? How does this sort of vindictiveness help? This is not Canadian. We can and should have a guaranteed income in Canada. Social assistance is basic, but it is not guaranteed, and definitely not livable. Don't believe me? Ask someone living in a tent.
Ann McRae, Dundas
Ford spoke
for many of us
Re: Ford decries Trump tariff threat, Nov. 26
I never ever thought I would feel anything close to resembling admiration for Ontario's Premier Doug Ford, but I do.
When Ford recently responded calmly but in a state of shock after learning that president-elect Donald Trump was threatening to put a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods, Ford said of our U.S. neighbours that it is like being stabbed in the heart by a family member. That one sentence won my admiration.
It's true. It does feel like that. It hurts. And as Premier Ford spoke, he looked exactly like he had received a stunning shock.
I certainly hope that Ford plays a strong role in the communications with the Trump administration. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has his role, but as far as personality and strong messaging, Ford won hands down in this tariff matter response.
Ana Long, Hamilton
Traffic will slow
to a complete stop
Re: Main Street slowdowns loom amid week of lane
closures, Dec. 1
The Spectator reported upcoming Main Street lane reductions, starting preparation for conversion to two-way traffic. Council has deemed this necessary to slow downtown Hamilton traffic in the interests of safety. Downtown Hamilton traffic is already unbearably slow, so it seems likely that the two-way Main Street with its ultimate complete street design will bring traffic to a near-standstill (one councillor said she wasn't interested in traffic flow).
It is already faster and easier for folks in West Hamilton and Dundas to drive to Burlington rather than suffer the delays of downtown traffic while getting to restaurants and businesses in central and east Hamilton. It is likely that the traffic standstill associated with a two-way Main Street will make Burlington even more attractive than Hamilton for these folks to dine and shop. The area of Hamilton below the mountain may essentially become two separate cities.
This may make it safer for the many pedestrians staring at their cellphones as they walk across busy intersections; the folks who bang on the windows of stopped cars, demanding money; the few cyclists seen using bike lanes crossing Main Street; and those anti-car Hamilton councillors who presumably are not driving to meetings at city hall.
This may also create opportunities for restaurants and businesses to open new West Hamilton and Dundas outlets to recoup lost patronage of folks who may otherwise go to Burlington.
Fred Leech, Dundas
Colleges clearly lacked foresight
The CEOs of Stellantis, Intel and Metrolinx have resigned. The reason: poor results.
Contrast this with the presidents of Ontario's colleges and universities. While they do everything they can to lay the blame for their actual and projected shortfalls on a lack of government funding, a phrase that keeps cropping up is a search for "efficiencies," leaving the distinct impression that inefficiency in departments is also to blame. I hasten to add that I exclude the current president of Mohawk, who has only just recently taken the reins there.
A lack of foresight leading to a failure to provide for the inevitable "rainy day" is at the heart of post-secondary education's woes, and the responsibility for that lies with the presidents of these institutions.
While the aforementioned presidents have fallen on their swords in an act of public contrition, Ontario college and university presidents remain in their superannuated comfort. Does anyone else see something woeful in this situation?
Neville Newman, Hamilton
Family put in
terrible position
Re: Man's cremated remains
in limbo due to postal strike, Nov. 29
I am writing in regards to this despicable loss of compassion and responsibility for this family that would like to lay their father's ashes to rest.
Having worked many years in a medical laboratory in Hamilton hospitals (now retired), and currently working for a transportation service that moves human specimens between hospitals, specimens can be tracked at any time by bar code and location scans.
I am extremely baffled as to why Canada Post cannot track precisely where a package that has a bar code is at all times! If the package was scanned onto a truck that also is bar coded, that truck could be located. If they can't track exactly which truck it is on, or what warehouse it is located at, it is high time they improve their tracking abilities and responsibilities.
If they had this capability, I would surely hope someone (management or a union employee) would put their differences aside and show some compassion to deliver to the family their loved one's ashes. Have some respect, please!
Rob Hancock, Binbrook
Put rebate funds into health care
Lord Doug wants give all his subjects a "gift" to purchase their loyalty. King Justin just got on the same train.
I recently spent four days in the ER at a local hospital. The people were great, the conditions much less so. So many stretchers packed against every inch of wall space. It felt like a stockyard for sick subjects. And far too few resources to bring about timely outcomes.
Gentlemen, give your heads a shake. Put the money where it's needed. A kingdom dies when its subjects wither away.
Peter Demik, Hannon
Who's the real 'rube' here?
Re: Your blame is misplaced, Nov. 30
Rubes and ignoramuses? OK, grumpy grandpa. If the citizens of Canada perceive politicians as corrupt and greedy, it's because they are often guilty of those charges. It seems there isn't a week that goes by that Justin Trudeau isn't mired in a new scandal. But the same could be said of past prime ministers like Stephen Harper and Brian Mulroney. Ontario has had more than four years of Doug Ford trying to line the pockets of wealthy investors who helped back his election run.
While politicians blame illegal immigrants for stealing jobs, it's actually multinational corporations who bust unions using free trade agreements to send jobs to offshore sweatshops and layoff local labour for greater profits. The Bank of Canada destroys the real estate market, blaming the average Canadian for inflation, while Canada's banks turn a blind eye to money laundering and drug money.
At the same time, oil companies, grocery chains and other businesses use COVID-19 to hike prices and make record profits selling half the product at twice the price.
If citizens think of politicians and bureaucrats as corrupt and greedy, it is because they have yet to be proven wrong.
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
Enough with the government ads
Am I the only one that is totally annoyed by Doug Ford's advertisement about how great we have it in Ontario? Then at the end of it, he has the nerve to say that the ad has been paid for by the Government of Ontario. As a taxpayer, I find the ad offensive.
Henk ten Oever, Smithville
Protect our
heritage links
City hall is attempting to protect and preserve structures of historical significance. That is a noble cause. Hamilton has an historically meaningful golf course that was designed by Stanley Thompson, a world-renowned designer.
The courses are more than 100 years old. Let's protect those treasures for future generations that are using them right now.
Pat and Lynda Lynch,
Hamilton
There are no
simple solutions
A letter and a substantial article in Saturday's paper spoke about the huge disparities in public health between neighbourhoods.
The hard part is figuring out how much is caused by the places in which people live. Wherever they live, people with lower incomes and mental health challenges tend to have greater health problems and die younger. They are also concentrated in certain neighbourhoods.
Sometimes it is clear that those neighbourhoods raise physical risks from air or soil pollution, as the letter-writer suggests. The social causes, including crime, street drugs and poorer schools, are harder to quantify, but may be just as important.
That is why we need public interventions that are directed at both neighbourhoods and at vulnerable individuals.
As with so many of the issues we face, there is no simple solution. It's a lesson that I wish more politicians would learn.
Richard Harris, Hamilton
Not easy to make healthy choices
Cigarette smoking is probably right up there with obesity as one of the top causes of health problems. This being the case, why are anti-smoking devices and medications and patches, etc., so darn expensive and why do insurance companies only partially cover these therapies, if at all? They usually only give you one shot to quit. Maybe, just maybe, if these therapies were less expensive or better coverage was available, a few less people would get sick and insurance companies might have to pay out less.
Same with obesity. Why are healthy foods so expensive? Why are supplements like protein drinks, fresh vegetables, etc., costing so much? Why are unhealthy alternatives so convenient?
With all our health-care shortages and issues, maybe more time and money should be invested in "Making Canada Healthy Again."
Do we really need a tunnel under a highway or another highway (413)?
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Liberals not proactive enough
The Trumpster has threatened to impose a 25 per cent tariff on all products from Canada if we do not get our house in order.
We have a Liberal government that for nine years has been reactive to issues, as opposed to taking a proactive approach. I call it the "do-nothing attitude." They are now reacting to a crisis that could been circumvented long ago.
The warnings about our national defence and the request to bump up our payments for NATO date back to 2016.
Unfortunately, the Liberal party has set the bar so low in this country that we are at subterranean levels. We have had the lowest productivity since 2015 and continue to spiral down.
We have suffered a government that has been irresponsible, reckless with taxpayers' money. It is embarrassing and disgraceful to see the Liberal party involved in numerous scandals. Canada is suffering from an absence of leadership and this is the reason we are caught up in the current situation with Trump.
It is a self-serving government that is tone-deaf to Canadians.
Our politicians have been put on notice and I hope that they will for once come through for Canadians. Make Canada great again.
Joanne Venzon, Hamilton

Document 26 of 500

Ford should finally reduce personal income tax: Bonnie Crombie's proposal is a good idea, but it is only a small step in the right direction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-should-finally-reduce-personal-income-tax/docview/3139089986/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+should+finally+reduce+personal+income+tax%3A+Bonnie+Crombie%27s+proposal+is+a+good+idea%2C+but+it+is+only+a+small+step+in+the+right+direction&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie announced that, if elected, she would reduce taxes on personal income between $51,446 and $75,000.
This promise is strikingly similar to the tax relief plan Doug Ford proposed during the 2018 election but failed to deliver.
To be fair, it's a good thing Crombie has brought tax relief into the policy conversation in Ontario. It's been 14 years since the last personal income tax reduction, and that was a one-percentage point reduction to the province's lowest tax bracket. However, her plan does not go nearly far enough to make Ontario's income tax system competitive within North America.
Of course, tax rates influence the economic decisions people make. An individual decides whether it's worth working an extra hour of overtime, for instance, based in part on how much of the extra money they earn they will keep rather than hand over to the government. Ontario's personal income tax rates are among the highest in Canada and the United States across a large portion of the income distribution.
First, at 53.53 per cent, Ontario has the third-highest combined (federal and provincial/state) personal income tax rate in Canada and the U.S. for individuals earning $300,000. This makes it harder for Ontario to attract high-skilled mobile workers that fuel economic growth. Many politicians and business leaders have spoken of a desire to make Ontario a tech hub in North America, but such a high tax rate makes it hard to attract the people who can make that vision a reality.
Down the income scale, at $150,000, Ontario has the third-highest combined tax rate (again, in Canada and the U.S.) behind only Quebec and Nova Scotia. At $50,000, Ontario's has the ninth-highest combined income tax rate, which is significantly higher than rates in many of the nearby Great Lakes states with whom we compete economically. For example, our combined personal income tax rate at this income level is nearly eight percentage points higher than neighbouring Michigan.
In light of Ontario's poor position relative to its peers, Crombie's proposal for a two-point reduction to a middle-income bracket is a good idea, but only a small step in the right direction. Premier Ford and other provincial party leaders should consider a much more ambitious plan to reduce income tax rates for a much larger range of workers. If the government wants to promote economic growth, it should reduce the top rate to be more competitive with nearby jurisdictions. There's a great deal of evidence showing high personal income tax rates at higher levels of income hurt the economy by reducing incentives to work and invest, and thereby stifling economic activity, growth, job creation and prosperity across income levels.
It's long past time for personal income tax reform in Ontario. It's been 14 years since the provincial government cut personal income taxes, and the reduction was modest. Since then, the government has raised taxes on higher earners. Ontarians shouldn't have to wait for the next election; the Ford government should reduce personal income taxes now to encourage sorely needed economic growth in the province.
Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss
are analysts at the
Fraser Institute.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss;

Document 27 of 500

10 highlights from the Ontario auditor general's searing report on the Ford government's performance

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/10-highlights-ontario-auditor-generals-searing/docview/3140614528/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=10+highlights+from+the+Ontario+auditor+general%27s+searing+report+on+the+Ford+government%27s+performance&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Serious problems stemming from policy decisions by Premier Doug Ford's government have been exposed in a searing 941-page report released Tuesday by auditor general Shelley Spence.
Here's what it found:
Ford government's Ontario Place redevelopment was 'not fair, transparent or accountable,' auditor general finds in scathing report
The process around redeveloping Ontario Place - to include a spa, waterpark, the Ontario Science Centre and a larger concert venue - was "irregular" and "subjective" and did not follow best practices. Costs have ballooned by $1.8 billion to $2.237 billion.The use of Minister's Zoning Orders, to override local planning decisions, appears random. A look at the 114 issued between 2019 and 2023 found "none contained an assessment as to whether the MZO was necessary." Shuttering 10 safe consumption sites and plans for opening 19 HART Hubs (Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment) will provide supportive housing and addiction recovery beds, but fewer places to access a safe narcotics supply or needle exchange. Government advertising has reached an all-time high, tripling to $103.5 million in 2023-24 from $33.7 million in 2022-23. The amount includes commercials promoting the province that aired during the Super Bowl and NHL games.Investigating potential application fraud under the Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program (OINP) takes so long that by the time it was uncovered, about one in five flagged candidates had already received permanent residency from the federal government.The province needs to better match job openings and newcomer skills. Despite thousands of nursing vacancies, they comprised less than one per cent of nominees under OINP.The Toronto District School Board has seen a sharp increase in violent incidents, now reported to be 17 incidents per 10,000 students. However, that is lower than the provincial average of 22. Spence urged the board to look at what's going on and boost mental health staff and supports.Toronto public schools are in the worst condition across the province, with a repair backlog estimated at $4.1 billion.The planned 950-bed Trillium Health Partners hospital in Mississauga is shaping up to be $4 billion over budget and the final price is "subject to change" as competition among bidders to build major infrastructure projects declines under the public-private partnership (P3) model.Despite spending $100 million over the past decade to digitize ServiceOntario services - such as renewing health cards and driver's licences - more than half of offerings still aren't available online. And for those that are, customer satisfaction rates sit at 48 per cent, well below the target of 75 per cent.
CAPTION: Shelley Spence, Ontario's Auditor General, has released a scathing report.

Title: 10 highlights from the Ontario auditor general's searing report on the Ford government's performance

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Document 28 of 500

Place the blame for homelessness where it belongs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/place-blame-homelessness-where-belongs/docview/3138416764/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Place+the+blame+for+homelessness+where+it+belongs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's lot of acrimony and finger-pointing going on regarding the continuing failure of city hall to deal with encampments, homelessness and overall general poverty issues in the city.
I don't believe anyone at city hall wants the status quo to continue. They certainly don't want to bear the brunt of community anger and animosity. Would you? I believe they're looking for solutions, but I also believe it's a difficult and complex issue for municipalities with limited resources and restricted powers.
It would be nice to see our "scrappy" mayor take on a more public champion role, but I don't think that's really her shtick. I thought it was, but it's easy to be "scrappy" in opposition. We can see that watching the antics of PM wannabe Pierre Poilievre; all critic, no creative. That is to say, it's easy to criticize when not in power and with no alternatives on offer.
But Andrea Horwath's silence isn't nowhere near as complete as the two Conservative MPPs who represent Hamilton: Donna Skelly, Flamborough-Glanbrook, and Neil Lumsden, Hamilton East-Stoney Creek.
Every time the mayor and city councillors are criticized for their lack of response, I bet Skelly and Lumsden breathe yet another sigh of relief, wipe their sweaty brow and chuckle to themselves about how dumb we are to not know they're the ones to blame. Skelly and Lumsden and the Conservative government, which they are part of and support, are the cause of the encampment issues, the lack of mental health supports, the legislated poverty of OW-ODSP and the broken rental market, both affordable and market rate.
The Liberals before them hold their share of the blame, particularly around social assistance rates, but COVID-19 changed everything and the Conservatives have not kept up with the many social gaps COVID exposed. In fact, they've exacerbated them by removing rent controls and putting their very heavy finger on the scale in favour of landlords, pauperizing those in need and punishing the sick and suffering.
Conservative policies are killing people in Ontario, and we should put the blame where it belongs: at the foot of the Ford government.
Meanwhile, sellout city councils across the province have signed up to urge Doug Ford to use the notwithstanding clause to clear their parks of encampments. Let's be clear. No one wants encampments, anywhere. Not in the parks, not on the boulevards, not on the grassy sections of merge ramps. People need real, solid homes.
Where are they? Who's building them?
Where are these same councillors urging Ford to address the paltry and completely inadequate social assistance rates of which the province holds the purse strings? People on social assistance are legislated into a poverty most of us can't even imagine. It's on Skelly and Lumsden, as Hamilton representatives, to represent Hamilton in caucus. People are suffering in their ridings, too. Who cares for them?
Where are these councillors petitioning the government to step in and do something about rapacious landlords increasing rents above guidelines year over year? Where are these councillors demanding the province do something about deeply affordable housing and timely and consistent access to mental health care?
Ontario Big City Mayors came up with their Solve the Crisis campaign earlier this year, consolidating their efforts to direct the blame where it belongs: on the provincial government.
Now it seems some mayors and councillors are capitulating to members of the public, who don't seem to care who has the power to solve the crisis - they just want their parks back. Not the boulevards or grassy spaces, just the parks. They don't want homelessness solved, they just want their parks back.
The rubber hits the road at the municipal level, no doubt. The effects of provincial inaction and antipathy are the root causes of our problems and are within the control of the provincial government. They could fix them if it was in their interest to. Why aren't we making it in their interest to?
CAPTION: City hall could do more on files like homelessness, but our outrage should be directed most at Queen's Park, Margaret Shkimba writes.The Conservative government is the cause of the encampment issues, the lack of mental health supports, the legislated poverty of OW-ODSP and the broken rental market, both affordable and market rate, Margaret Shkimba writes.
