Document 29 of 500

National briefs: Phil Verster quits top jobat Metrolinx

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3135043802/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Phil+Verster+quits+top+jobat+Metrolinx&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Clean electricity rules would add $35B cost: study
Ontario is urging the federal government to amend proposed electricity regulations after an analysis by the province's system operator concluded the rules would mean $35 billion in additional costs by 2050. A new analysis by the Independent Electricity System Operator looking at upcoming regulations around restrictions on emissions from electricity generation found that Ontario would have to add twice as much new generation as it is already planning, which is "not feasible" in that time frame. But if the province were to somehow do so, building enough new electricity generation to make up for restrictions on natural gas would add $35 billion in costs by 2050, increasing residential bills by $132 to $168 per year starting in 2033, the IESO said.
Phil Verster quits top job
at Metrolinx
The head of provincial transit agency Metrolinx is resigning after seven years on the job, having become the target of frequent criticism for the failure to open the long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT. Phil Verster has accepted a new position and will be moving on as soon as Dec. 16, Premier Doug Ford announced Monday. The agency - and Verster specifically - have come under frequent fire for the long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown project, which was supposed to open in 2020. There is still no projected opening date for the rail line under construction since 2011.

Canada
Report released into Chinook helicopter crash
An investigation report into a Chinook helicopter crash that killed two Armed Forces members last year is recommending the Air Force use an automatic flight system when flying low over water at night. Capt. Marc Larouche and Capt. David Domagala were killed in June 2023 when the helicopter crashed into the Ottawa River during a training exercise near Canadian Forces Base Petawawa. The crash happened when a student pilot was trying to turn the helicopter, but started to descend toward the water. Investigators say the crew was disoriented by environmental conditions and did not notice the descent before the helicopter crashed into the river at a high speed.
News services

Document 30 of 500

Nurses and other health-care professionals are first responders - except in the Ford government's view

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nurses-other-health-care-professionals-are-first/docview/3135043646/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nurses+and+other+health-care+professionals+are+first+responders+-+except+in+the+Ford+government%27s+view&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has announced a new mental health program for first responders to provide them with access to a 24-hour call line and web portal for therapy and peer support. A laudable, much-needed program indeed.
There's just one problem.
In Ford's world, first responders include police officers, correctional workers, paramedics and firefighters - notably professions that are still predominantly men. For health-care workers, such as nurses and other health-care professionals, the announcement was akin to "déjà vu all over again."
This is not the first time they have had to fight to access the supports they need in their roles as first responders.
Nurses were declared to be first responders back in 2013, under Bill 15, legislation that declared First Responders Day in Ontario. Yet Bill 163 - Supporting Ontario's First Responders Act (Posttraumatic Stress Disorder) - introduced in 2016, omitted them.
It took time, but the Ontario Nurses' Association (ONA) successfully persuaded the Liberal government to include nurses. While the legislation was never changed, the government did amend the Workplace Safety and Insurance Act, 1997, making it easier for nurses and health-care workers to access work-related PTSD benefits.
Fast forward to 2024, and here we are again.
Why is the Ford government now omitting nurses and health-care professionals from this new program?
Looking at who this government exempted from Bill 124, their now-repealed public-sector wage-suppression legislation, provides the answer. A familiar list of professions was exempted from having their wages unconstitutionally suppressed by Ford: police, firefighters and EMS personnel - all professions dominated by men.
In the view of this government, nurses and health-care professionals aren't truly first responders, despite having proved the contrary. Once again, they are facing disrespect and disregard by another government program.
Ontario's nurses and health-care professionals have been subjected to an enormous amount of trauma and what is called "moral injury" in their workplaces over the past five years. In addition to witnessing the horrors of the COVID-19 pandemic in its early stages, trying to cope with heavier and heavier workloads as their coworkers burned out and left, and providing the best patient care possible in extraordinary circumstances, those in these women-dominated professions have also faced more incidents of workplace violence.
Yet, the Ford government has not continued temporary funding for mental health supports for them, as it did during the first part of the pandemic.
Like police and other first responders, nurses and health-care professionals regularly face violence at work. They lack the necessary supports when frustrated, ill patients, or their friends and families take out their aggression on the very people trying to care for them.
There are a staggering number of incidents happening in health care, something confirmed by Workplace Safety and Insurance Board data. Their statistics show that the number of lost-time claims for those in health care increased from 13.7 per cent in 2020 to 17.4 per cent in 2021. Workplace violence accounted for 16.9 per cent of all lost-time claims in 2016, rising to 31 per cent of claims by 2022.
Furthermore, a survey of nurses conducted by the Canadian Federation of Nurses Unions found more than 82 per cent of Ontario respondents had experienced bullying or aggression in the past year, 63 per cent had experienced physical abuse, and 48 per cent had experienced unwanted sexual attention. More than 48 per cent of those nurses say they did not report the incidents, due to fear of reprisal.
Rather than allow nurses and health-care professionals to go unrecognized and unsupported again, we will lobby this government to recognize and include our dedicated nurses and health-care professionals in this new mental health program.
If Ford values them as much as he says he does, he will not repeat the mistakes he made with Bill 124 and instead, quickly correct his error omitting these highly skilled and vital workers.
At a time when Ontario needs every nurse and health-care professional it can find, supporting them with this program like every other first responder is what is needed.
CAPTION: A nurse and doctor work in a Toronto hospital's emergency department. In the view of this government, nurses and health-care professionals aren't truly first responders, despite having proved the contrary, Erin Ariss writes.
CREDIT: Erin Ariss

Subject: Occupational safety; Legislation; Professionals; Mental health; Health care; Nurses; Pandemics; Workplace violence; Professions; Post traumatic stress disorder

Business indexing term: Subject: Occupational safety Professionals Workplace violence

Document 31 of 500

Place the blame where it belongs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/place-blame-where-belongs/docview/3135043557/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Place+the+blame+where+it+belongs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's lot of acrimony and finger-pointing going on regarding the continuing failure of city hall to deal with encampments, homelessness and overall general poverty issues in the city.
I don't believe anyone at city hall wants the status quo to continue. They certainly don't want to bear the brunt of community anger and animosity.
Would you?
I believe they're looking for solutions, but I also believe it's a difficult and complex issue for municipalities with limited resources and restricted powers.
It would be nice to see our "scrappy" mayor take on a more public champion role, but I don't think that's really her shtick. I thought it was, but it's easy to be "scrappy" in opposition.
We can see that watching the antics of PM wannabe Pierre Poilievre; all critic, no creative. That is to say, it's easy to criticize when not in power and with no alternatives on offer.
But Andrea Horwath's silence isn't nowhere near as complete as the two Progressive Conservative MPPs who represent Hamilton: Donna Skelly, Flamborough-Glanbrook, and Neil Lumsden, Hamilton East-Stoney Creek.
Every time the mayor and city councillors are criticized for their lack of response, I bet Skelly and Lumsden breathe yet another sigh of relief, wipe their sweaty brow and chuckle to themselves about how dumb we are to not know they're the ones to blame.
Skelly and Lumsden and the Progressive Conservative government, which they are part of and support, are the cause of the encampment issues, the lack of mental health supports, the legislated poverty of OW-ODSP and the broken rental market, both affordable and market rate.
The Liberals before them hold their share of the blame, particularly around social assistance rates, but COVID-19 changed everything and the Conservatives have not kept up with the many social gaps COVID exposed. In fact, they've exacerbated them by removing rent controls and putting their very heavy finger on the scale in favour of landlords, pauperizing those in need and punishing the sick and suffering.
Progressive Conservative policies are killing people in Ontario, and we should put the blame where it belongs: at the foot of the Ford government.
Meanwhile, sellout city councils across the province have signed up to urge Doug Ford to use the notwithstanding clause to clear their parks of encampments. Let's be clear. No one wants encampments, anywhere. Not in the parks, not on the boulevards, not on the grassy sections of merge ramps. People need real, solid homes.
Where are they? Who's building them?
Where are these same councillors urging Ford to address the paltry and completely inadequate social assistance rates of which the province holds the purse strings? People on social assistance are legislated into a poverty most of us can't even imagine. It's on Skelly and Lumsden, as Hamilton representatives, to represent Hamilton in caucus. People are suffering in their ridings, too. Who cares for them?
Where are these councillors petitioning the government to step in and do something about rapacious landlords increasing rents above guidelines year over year? Where are these councillors demanding the province do something about deeply affordable housing and timely and consistent access to mental health care?
Ontario Big City Mayors came up with their Solve the Crisis campaign earlier this year, consolidating their efforts to direct the blame where it belongs: on the provincial government.
Now it seems some mayors and councillors are capitulating to members of the public, who don't seem to care who has the power to solve the crisis - they just want their parks back. Not the boulevards or grassy spaces, just the parks. They don't want homelessness solved, they just want their parks back.
The rubber hits the road at the municipal level, no doubt. The effects of provincial inaction and antipathy are the root causes of our problems and are within the control of the provincial government. They could fix them if it was in their interest to. Why aren't we making it in their interest to?
Margaret Shkimba
is a Hamilton writer.
For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) and friend her on
Facebook.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Margaret Shkimba OPINION Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) and friend her on Facebook.;

Health-care workers deserve the same supports

Document 33 of 500

'Wait times have exploded': Bonnie Crombie takes aim at Ontario doctor shortage in campaign promise

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/wait-times-have-exploded-bonnie-crombie-takes-aim/docview/3134880561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Wait+times+have+exploded%27%3A+Bonnie+Crombie+takes+aim+at+Ontario+doctor+shortage+in+campaign+promise&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is offering a "guarantee" of family doctors for all in a province where 2.5 million people don't have one.
As Ontario inches toward an expected early election next spring, Crombie unveiled a key component of her health care platform Monday in Brampton.
"You can't find a family doctor. Wait times have exploded. And hospitals are actually shutting their doors to people who need help," she said in a statement, referring to temporary emergency room closures around the province.
Crombie accused Premier Doug Ford, who was first elected in 2018 and has been hinting Ontarians will be heading to the polls soon instead of waiting until the next scheduled election in June 2026, of "deliberately destroying" the health care system.
While Ford's Progressive Conservative government has taken steps to improve the situation, such as creating more medical school spots, it takes years to train new doctors. The Ontario Medical Association estimates Ontario needs at least 2,500 more primary care physicians.
Ford has also tapped former federal Liberal health minister Jane Philpott, director of the medical school at Queen's University, to head a "primary care action team" aimed at connecting every Ontarian to a family doctor within five years. She officially begins that work this week.
The Liberal promises include attracting "thousands" of doctors trained in other countries, a "massive" expansion of Ontario health teams with physicians and a host of other medical professionals to provide comprehensive care, more evening and weekend medical appointments, and modernizing the system to phase out fax machines.
Crombie's statement did not detail exactly how a Liberal government would lure doctors to Ontario or how much it would spend to make sure everyone in Ontario could have a family doctor by the end of her first term if she's elected premier.
The government has been pouring millions more into health teams across the province, but opposition parties have repeatedly raised concerns that the investments are inadequate as more family doctors are reaching retirement age - which could result in at least four million Ontarians not having a family physician in a few years.
"You deserve to have a trusted relationship with your doctor who is available and invested in the best interest of your health no matter your age, health conditions, or where you live," Crombie said.
CAPTION: Bonnie Crombie (left) has accused Premier Doug Fordof "deliberately destroying" Ontario's health care system.

Document 34 of 500

Premier Doug Ford 'in conversation' for Highway 407 buyback from private owners

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/premier-doug-ford-conversation-highway-407/docview/3134880234/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Doug+Ford+%27in+conversation%27+for+Highway+407+buyback+from+private+owners&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is further exploring the possibility of buying back Highway 407.
Back in October, Ford brought up the idea of buying the toll highway back, as a way to ease congestion in the GTA.
A Ministry of Transportation spokesperson confirmed the government is exploring the idea.
"We have been in conversation with the 407 and will continue to work with our partners as we find new ways to keep our roads and economy moving," said media relations director Dakota Brasier.
The owner of Hwy. 407 is a company called 407 International Inc., which is a combination of the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, AtkinsRéalis Canada and Spanish company Ferrovial.
"We will continue to find ways to make life more affordable for Ontarians and ensure they can get where they need to go every day," Brasier said. "This includes looking at all options and building the critical infrastructure we need to keep up with our growing population."
Ford has also spoken about building a new highway with a tunnel through most of the congested areas of the GTA at an earlier news conference.
"Wouldn't it be great if we could even get over 50 kilometres an hour on the 401 in rush hour through the city?" he asked reporters during an Oct. 2 news conference.
Latest statistics from the 407 show there were 240,738,025 kilometres documented in September 2024. That's up from the same time last year, with 235,195,122 kilometres logged.
A long history
For those who don't remember, Ontario built the 151-kilometre Hwy. 407 in sections.
It was initially planned back in the 1950s as a way to bypass Highway 401 in Toronto.
Construction on the 407 highway started in 1987, with plans to have it stretch from Burlington to Pickering. It initially cost taxpayers $1.5 billion.
Although it was originally going to be a free highway, the idea of making it a tolled highway came in the 1990s, to make up for a funding shortfall.
Called the Highway 407 Express Toll Route (ETR), sections of the road were opened in 1997 to 1999.
There are no toll booths along the road, but instead, cameras use automatic licence plate recognition, or transponders, to collect tolls.
Fees are set by Hwy. 407 ETR. The company just announced that fees will be increasing as of Jan. 1, 2025.
A lease for profit
Back on May 5, 1999, the Mike Harris Conservative government sold the highway to the private consortium for $3.1 billion, with a 99-year-lease, for a profit of $1.5 billion.
Since then, the company says it has invested more than $1.6 billion in extensions and expansions, to double lane capacity.
It also gives back with various programs that include tree planting, a kids campaign called Keep Moving, and it just gave one Oshawa woman a 2025 Honda CR-V Hybrid with a year of free travel on Highway 407 ETR to celebrate the company's 25th anniversary.
On Oct. 24, 407 International Inc. announced financial results for the third quarter of 2024. Reported revenues were $487.1 million, up 16 per cent from 2023. Net income was $212.3 million, also up 16 per cent from the previous year.
Adding on
A new phase of Highway 407 was planned east from Pickering to Clarington in 2009 by the Dalton McGuinty Liberal government.
The first phase opened in 2016, starting at Brock Road in Pickering. Phases to join Whitby and Clarington opened in 2018 and 2019. It now ends at Highway 35/115 in Clarington and is still provincially owned.
While the two sections connect seamlessly, one is named Highway 407 ETR with set tolls, and the other is Highway 407, with the province setting the fees.
To learn more about the toll highway, visit 407etr.com or on407.ca.
CAPTION: The world's first all-electronic open access toll highway, 407 ETR, is celebrating 25 years of fast, safe and reliable travel in Ontario.Visit 407etr.com to learn more.Ontario Premier Doug Ford says buying back Highway 407 could help alleviate traffic congestion in the GTA.

Document 35 of 500

Hamilton seniors wait up to five years for long-term care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-seniors-wait-up-five-years-long-term/docview/3134879860/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+seniors+wait+up+to+five+years+for+long-term+care&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton has 1,938 seniors languishing on waiting lists for long-term care that have reached as high as two to five years.
New beds are years away from opening and far from what is needed to meet the demand, detailed a report by the city's healthy and safe communities department. In fact, the beds opening in the next three years barely replace what has already been lost.
"This is totally unacceptable," Coun. Tom Jackson said when the report on the state of long-term care in Hamilton came before the city's emergency and community services committee on Nov. 21.
Hamilton's wait list includes 368 seniors in crisis, meaning they are at "significant safety risk" in their current environment and all other options in the community have been exhausted.
Even those in crisis can wait six months or more to get into a Hamilton long-term care home.
"There is a desperate need for additional long-term care beds in our community," Coun. Brad Clark said. "We need the beds quickly."
Instead, the city has lost 321 long-term care beds since the pandemic, concluded the report.
Most of the beds were removed during COVID when three- and four-bed wards were eliminated, resulting in 231 fewer spaces at local long-term care homes.
The other 90 beds were at Pine Villa Care Centre in Stoney Creek and Blackadar Continuing Care Centre in Dundas, which have both stopped taking new admissions because they are shutting down.
The report does not mention that the loss of Blackadar is far more than the 60 beds that were in operation before it started closing. The long-term care home was supposed to be rebuilt into a modern facility with 192 beds but the private for-profit owners decided to close it instead and build residential housing at 101 Creighton Rd.
In addition, the report does not address unanswered questions about how many more homes in Hamilton and the surrounding area will shut down instead of rebuilding.
The Ontario Long Term Care Association reports that nearly half of Ontario's homes are old facilities that need renovation. Many of them have licences expiring in 2025. The Spectator has repeatedly asked the Ministry of Long-Term Care for more than 19 months for a list of homes closing instead of renovating. The ministry has refused to provide the information, citing "economic and other interests of Ontario."
The province has committed $6.4 billion to build 30,000 new long-term care beds in Ontario by 2028 and upgrade 28,000 existing beds. However, there is no guarantee of how many will be built in Hamilton and more than half of the city's planned new beds aren't expected to be built until well after 2028.
So far, the city knows of just 432 beds expected to open by 2028, which is only 111 more than Hamilton has already lost.
The new beds include a home being built by the Reformed Christian Dutch-Canadian community at 1415 Upper Wellington St. It will have 188 beds and is expected to open in late 2026.
However, Shalom Manor Hamilton will also serve Burlington after the Christian community closed its long-standing 60-bed home at 4486 Guelph Line on Nov. 7 because installing a required fire sprinkler system at Mount Nemo Christian Nursing Home was not feasible. The community's existing Christian long-term care home in Grimsby has a five- to eight-year waiting list.
Also expected in 2026 are 20 new beds at Macassa Lodge at 701 Upper Sherman Ave. In addition, the renovation will upgrade 44 existing beds.
In 2027, a new 224-bed facility is slated to open where Pine Villa was previously at 490 Hwy 8.
The biggest influx of long-term care beds to Hamilton have not been announced by the province yet and aren't expected to be built for eight to nine years from now.
As reported by The Spectator, Schlegel Villages plans to build new two long-term care homes with a combined total of 512 beds as part of a sweeping deal that will transform former provincial hospital lands at 290 Fennell Ave. W. The deal includes turning Hamilton Continuing Care into affordable housing because Schlegel says it's not possible to maintain the building as a long-term care home in the future. The downtown home's 64 beds will move to the brow lands once the new homes are ready to open and become part of the 512.
No exact dates have been set yet, but the deal requires residents moving into the completed long-term care homes within eight and a half years.
"Holy smokes, waiting almost 10 years for these beds," said Jackson. "I'm just so saddened and angered that I thought the ... (Premier Doug) Ford government was going to ... put multi-millions (of dollars) aside to build thousands upon thousands of long-term care beds and hopefully Hamilton would get its share to address the immediate need - not six, eight, 10 years from now."
In the meantime, there is significant pressure on Hamilton's 25 long-term care homes with a total of 3,773 beds. Occupancy rates are 98 per cent or more across the city and surrounding areas. The wait list is 1,500 to 2,000 people at any one time.
"I think the City of Hamilton needs to work with the ministry to somehow find a way to accelerate and make the entire process more efficient," said Clark.
Adding to the urgency of the problem is that 161 of the seniors waiting for long-term care as of Nov. 4 are in Hamilton's hospitals, occupying acute care beds they don't need but can't leave because no home can take them yet.
"The status of long-term care beds in Hamilton is critical to the well-being of our aging population," stated the report. "It is essential for the province to prioritize actions that enhance our long-term care capacity and address the systemic challenges faced by our healthcare system."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Coun. Tom Jackson called the years-long wait for new long-term care beds "unacceptable."Coun. Brad Clark said:"There is a desperate need for additional long-term care beds in our community."Mount Nemo Christian Nursing Home in Burlington closed Nov. 7.Blackadar Continuing Care Centre in Dundas closed instead of rebuilding.

Document 36 of 500

'Ontario has failed people with disabilities': People with disabilities in Oshawa, Whitby and Clarington speak bluntly about what accessibility act means to their lives: 'As a disabled person since 2010, the improvements I'm seeming in my community are absolutely minimal': Jim McEwen

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-has-failed-people-with-disabilities/docview/3134626057/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Ontario+has+failed+people+with+disabilities%27%3A+People+with+disabilities+in+Oshawa%2C+Whitby+and+Clarington+speak+bluntly+about+what+accessibility+act+means+to+their+lives%3A+%27As+a+disabled+person+since+2010%2C+the+improvements+I%27m+seeming+in+my+community+are+absolutely+minimal%27%3A+Jim+McEwen&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A barrier-free Ontario was the purpose of the Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA), unanimously passed by MPPs in 2005. With the province's 20-year deadline for the implementation of accessibility standards looming on Jan. 1, 2025, Restricted Access: Will Ontario meet its barrier-free goals? explores this quality-of-life issue for the millions of people living with disabilities - a demographic that's expected to skyrocket as the population ages.
When passed nearly 20 years ago, the AODA, as its most commonly known, was created to "establish, develop, implement and enforce standards related to goods, services, accommodation, employment and buildings before Jan. 1, 2025.
The legislation applies to every person in both the public and private sector. Its ultimate aim is to work toward a barrier-free Ontario.
Has it met that goal? Has it come close? Has much even changed?
DurhamRegion.com asked four people who live with disabilities and who have been members of local accessibility advisory committees in various municipalities whether the legislation has been effective.
Here are their stories, through their own lived experiences and in their own words:
Dot McFarlane, 58, of Oshawa
Dot McFarlane is the current chair of Oshawa's accessibility advisory committee.
She has "back issues that when I walk certain distances, my legs start to go numb. I also have a genetic endocrine issue and vertigo. I was diagnosed with my endocrine issue when I was in my teens. It causes problems with my face, my neck is very bulgy. It creates hypothyroidism, but my hypothyroidism manifests itself in my neck and it makes my neck look a lot bigger than most people."
The City of Oshawa insisted on this disclaimer about McFarlane's comments: "The opinions reflected by the member are their own personal comments and are not endorsed nor representative of the advisory committee, the City of Oshawa or Oshawa city council."
"Because of my hypothyroidism I get a lot of comments on my face, that I'm fat, that I should lose weight, all that kind of stuff; I get negative comments in regard to that. I started needing to use a scooter six or seven years ago ... At first, I wasn't approved for a scooter, so I ended up getting my own. The doctor had prescribed it. I faced comments in regard to that too. Even people that sort of know me, here in my building, they look at me and they say, you're too lazy, you shouldn't need that scooter. So it's a prime example of attitudinal disability. They think that just because they can see me walk that I shouldn't need that scooter at all, but they can't see what's going on, on the inside," she said.
How do you feel now as far as accessibility goes in 2024, compared to about a decade ago?
"We have made progress, but there is still a long way to go ... In my own life, it's been in transportation. I don't drive, my scooter is my car. When I'm going on public transit, with the buses, I'll go back 20 years ago when we were riding buses - the buses were not accessible at all. I used to have a roommate that used a wheelchair and I would always have to fold her wheelchair up to carry it on the bus and she managed to almost crawl on to the bus. Nowadays, it's much better."
Kathryn Bremner, 73, of Oshawa
Bremner's adult son Scott lives with a disability. She is a longtime disability rights advocate and former member of the City of Oshawa accessibility advisory committee.
"We are six weeks away from the AODA deadline for an accessible Ontario and two decades removed from when it became law and it's clear that Ontario has failed people with disabilities, miserably. This will come as no surprise to Premier Doug Ford. Four reviews raised alarms about Ontario's lack of progress. Just north of me in Oshawa, thousands of homes are being built with no thought to accessibility. Homes that will last for decades. The construction industry and the politicians who make the laws need to wake up to the reality that roughly 2.6 million Ontarians are disabled and the number is growing, as the population ages."
Mike Moraites, 63, of Whitby
Moraites lives with osteoarthritis diabetes and neuropathy and retired at age 53 from his job in Toronto, when he moved to Whitby. He is a former member of the Whitby accessibility advisory committee.
"One of the things I find in the Town of Whitby is the lack of enforcement for disabled parking, an inability to find a space, and people violating the laws by parking in the space, or parking in the space with a permit and waiting while they have their friend run into the store. There are all kinds of enforcement issues. That's the town we live in," he said.
"There are also two examples of how it's not working. When I went to visit my doctor's office, and I went to go into the accessible washroom, the door was shut. I pushed the door open, went to go in, and it was a storage room. I still went in, use the bars to get up and down. I waited and spoke to my doctor; she agreed it shouldn't be like that. On my next visit, I went to use the accessible washroom and the door was locked. I spoke to the doctor, but they had put a bar in the other bathroom, so it was not adequately equipped in a doctor's office. What it comes down to, there's nobody to complain to, nobody to call. What do you use if you come in with a walker or a wheelchair?" he said.
"My biggest worries and concerns are the AODA is not going to be fulfilled by the Jan. 1, 2025 date. I mean, I'm 100 per cent sure they are not going to be able to do that. I understand a lot of people fought for this, but maybe the province should have another look at the act and see what's doable and reset some achievement dates and try and figure out where the general public is going to come up with the funds to do what they have to do with regards to accessibility. Enforcement (of standards) is an important part for the province. In the town, we pay enough tax. We don't have money to hire more bylaw enforcement, and it should be a provincial responsibility," Moraites said.
Jim McEwen, 69, of Clarington
McEwen, a retired civil engineer, has lived with a disability since 2010 after suffering a stroke. He is a member of the Region of Durham accessibility advisory committee.
"As a disabled person since 2010, the improvements I'm seeing in my community are absolutely minimal. I'm not seeing the improvements I would like to see in restaurants. In most restaurants, I have to go to the women's restroom with my wife. Twenty years ago, there was no standard to have proper bars in handicapped stalls. Twenty years later, I've been in so many washrooms where they don't have enough bars and I'm trying to grab on the back of the toilet because they don't have accessibility," he said.
He said he's frustrated that the need for accessible washrooms seems to be an Ontario Building Code requirement rather than an AODA standard.
"There seems to be a disconnect between the AODA and Ontario Building Code with respect to providing a barrier-free Ontario."
"I like it when places have a washroom that is only for the disabled. I can go into it, use it and I'm totally fine with it. I have no issues with it and come out on my own. I know how to deal with them. They have a push-button door opener, and they've got proper bars in them. I'm totally safe in them," he said.
With the advisory committee, he said "we've had an impact. Durham Region was proposing a new long-term-care home in north Pickering and our committee had the opportunity to review the proposed drawings for accessibility, which was good."
CAPTION: Mike Moraites, who lives with a disability, is a former member of the Whitby accessibility advisory committee. He said he was frustrated with the committee and stepped down a few months ago.Jim McEwen of Clarington, a member of the Region of Durham's accessibility advisory committee, has lived with a disability since he suffered a stroke in 2010.Dot McFarlane, who has lived with a disability for a number of years and often uses a scooter to get around Oshawa, cuddles with two of her pet dogs, Morgan (on top), a schnauzer mix, and Riot, a poodle mix.

Subject: Committees; Stroke; Wheelchairs; Hypothyroidism; Medical practices; Building codes; Handicapped accessibility; People with disabilities; Enforcement; Disability; Ontarians with Disabilities Act-Ontario

Title: 'Ontario has failed people with disabilities': People with disabilities in Oshawa, Whitby and Clarington speak bluntly about what accessibility act means to their lives:   'As a disabled person since 2010, the improvements I'm seeming in my community are absolutely minimal': Jim McEwen

Document 37 of 500

'I could not live with myself if I didn't': Disabilities advocate David Lepofsky won't ever stop fighting to bust every barrier to make AODA work for accessible Ontario: 'I believe grassroots pressure on MPP after MPP, city councillor after city councillor, school board trustee after school board trustee can have a huge impact,' says David Lepofsky

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-could-not-live-with-myself-if-didnt/docview/3134625957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+could+not+live+with+myself+if+I+didn%27t%27%3A+Disabilities+advocate+David+Lepofsky+won%27t+ever+stop+fighting+to+bust+every+barrier+to+make+AODA+work+for+accessible+Ontario%3A+%27I+believe+grassroots+pressure+on+MPP+after+MPP%2C+city+councillor+after+city+councillor%2C+school+board+trustee+after+school+board+trustee+can+have+a+huge+impact%2C%27+says+David+Lepofsky&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A barrier-free Ontario was the purpose of the Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA), unanimously passed by MPPs in 2005. With the province's 20-year deadline for the implementation of accessibility standards looming on Jan. 1, 2025, Restricted Access: Will Ontario meet its barrier-free goals? explores this quality-of-life issue for the millions of people living with disabilities - a demographic that's expected to skyrocket as the population ages.
Renowned accessibility advocate David Lepofsky is just like the Terminator: he won't ever stop in his drive to eliminate barriers that prevent those with disabilities from having equality opportunity in Ontario.
Ask him why he's been fighting for disability rights for over 45 years and the 67-year-old lawyer has a straight-to-the-point answer.
"I could not live with myself if I didn't. If I sat back, I'd feel I can't complain if I didn't do something to try to make it better."
Lepofsky was at the heart of the fight to create the Accessibility for Ontarians Act that passed unanimously in the Ontario legislature in May 2005. He and others had worked for more than 10 years to get the act through the committee process and into law.
And he has spent the past 20 years working hard to keep governments of various stripes accountable for following the terms of the act, for ensuring the employment, transportation, customer service, information and communications, and design of public spaces standards are adhered to and followed.
As well, Lepofsky has worked for years to try to help develop an education standard that would help bring down barriers for children with disabilities in Ontario.
"I get my enthusiasm and energy from other people I meet who are similarly battling for this. I recently chaired a town-hall meeting for parents and students with disabilities and parent after parent was telling horror stories of what they're up against in education for their child. I listened to the pain in their voices and the determination in their voices and I say, 'I'm with them, and they're with me, we're all together in this.' We don't know each other, we may never meet, but we're all together in this," he said.
Lepofsky is head of the AODA Alliance, which has a treasure trove of information about accessibility in Ontario and about what's happening with the AODA itself. He has watched as four separate independent reports have been presented to the government since 2009, the latest in 2023.
Each report in turn was damning in its assessment of how the government was progressing on making Ontario barrier free and enforcing accessibility standards.
The latest report, by Rich Donovan, spoke of a "crisis" with accessibility in Ontario.
"This crisis (with accessibility) extends beyond immediate emergency response," Donovan wrote. "More than 75 per cent of surveyed Ontario residents in 2021 with a disability reported negative experiences; 2.9 million people in Ontario currently have a disability. Despite this, there has been minimal change in accessibility. This in turn means Ontario is forgoing a material, and growing, economic opportunity."
Donovan's response is in stark contrast to a reply DurhamRegion.com received from the Ministry of Seniors and Accessibility when we asked straightforward questions about the progress of the AODA in the march toward the Jan. 1 deadline.
The goal by the deadline is to establish, develop, implement and enforce standards related to goods and services in various standards like employment, transportation, customer service, buildings and accommodation. The legislation applies to every person in both the public and private sector. Its ultimate aim is to work toward a barrier-free Ontario for all Ontarians.
The ministry said that "project by project, community by community, Ontario is meeting, achieving or exceeding the (AODA) across the province. This includes historic spending in infrastructure, school upgrades, new and retrofitted hospitals and long-term-care facilities, as well as public transit investments, including over 2,200 new accessible buses being delivered provincewide."
The ministry's response does not match what disability advocates and those living with a disability have said to Metroland.
Kathryn Bremner of Oshawa, whose son, Scott, lives with a disability, was scathing in her remarks about government inaction.
"We are six weeks away from the AODA deadline for an accessible Ontario and two decades removed from when it became law and it's clear that Ontario has failed people with disabilities, miserably," she said.
"This will come as no surprise to Premier Doug Ford. Four reviews raised alarms about Ontario's lack of progress. Just north of me in Oshawa, thousands of homes are being built with no thought to accessibility. Homes that will last for decades. The construction industry and the politicians who make the laws need to wake up to the reality that roughly 2.6 million Ontarians are disabled and the number is growing as the population ages."
Jim McEwen, a 69-year-old retired engineer from Clarington, has lived with a disability since 2010 after suffering from a stroke. He said he has not noticed much of a difference in his community when it comes to accessibility.
"As a disabled person since 2010, the improvements I'm seeing in my community are absolutely minimal. I'm not seeing the improvements I would like to see," McEwen said.
Lepofsky could see much of this coming over the years, and he has pointed out some of the biggest and most obvious flaws, often in shocking videos that have attracted widespread attention. One focuses on the many failures of the $1-billion Toronto provincial courthouse when it was built a few years ago.
He has also filed Freedom of Information (FOI) requests to call the government to account over inaction on the AODA, including in 2015 when he was upset with the Liberal government.
The FOI documents revealed, among other things:
Rampant private sector AODA violations persisted, known to the Kathleen Wynne government, almost three years after the AODA Alliance revealed this troubling trend.In 2015, the Wynne government continued to only have scant staffing for AODA enforcement. To enforce the AODA across Ontario, the government had only appointed three directors and one inspector.Private sector organizations with under 20 employees were getting a virtual free pass from AODA enforcement. Of the hundreds of thousands of those organizations, only nine were audited for AODA compliance in 2014 and the first half of 2015.
Still, Lepofsky and the AODA Alliance continue to battle to make the government accountable and won't stop fighting - and they're hoping more people will join in.
He believes real change won't happen until plenty of political pressure - and a bit of media pressure too - is brought to bear on the government and provincial parties.
He encourages people to visit www.aodaalliance.org to sign up for email updates and read those he has already provided. The site also offers action tips on how to help.
"I believe grassroots pressure on MPP after MPP, city councillor after city councillor, school trustee after school trustee, can have a huge impact," Lepofsky said. "The four political parties - we may very well be going into an election next year or the year after - they each need to come up with a detailed plan about what they're going to do about this.
"For the Ford government, they're in power, they could be taking action today," he said. "They need to announce that action plan right now."
CAPTION: David Lepofsky receives the Order of Ontario for his work in 2008 to help break down barriers for people with disabilities. He received the honour from David Onley, himself an advocate for people with disabilities.David Lepofsky, who is blind, outside the Ontario Court of Justice at 10 Armoury St. in Toronto. Lepofsky has fought for more than 45 years to break down barriers for people with disabilities and won't stop anytime soon, he says.

Subject: Customer services; Handicapped accessibility; People with disabilities; Disability; Ontarians with Disabilities Act-Ontario

Business indexing term: Subject: Customer services

Title: 'I could not live with myself if I didn't': Disabilities advocate David Lepofsky won't ever stop fighting to bust every barrier to make AODA work for accessible Ontario:   'I believe grassroots pressure on MPP after MPP, city councillor after city councillor, school board trustee after school board trustee can have a huge impact,' says David Lepofsky

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 2, 2024

Section: news

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 38 of 500

Seniors wait up to five years for spot in LTC: At time of desperate need, city lost 321 spaces while new beds are years away

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/seniors-wait-up-five-years-spot-ltc/docview/3134625932/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Seniors+wait+up+to+five+years+for+spot+in+LTC%3A+At+time+of+desperate+need%2C+city+lost+321+spaces+while+new+beds+are+years+away&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton has 1,938 seniors languishing on waiting lists for long-term care that have reached as high as two to five years.
New beds are years away from opening and far from what is needed to meet the demand, detailed a report by the city's healthy and safe communities department. In fact, the beds opening in the next three years barely replace what has already been lost.
"This is totally unacceptable," Coun. Tom Jackson said when the report on the state of long-term care in Hamilton came before the city's emergency and community services committee on Nov. 21.
Hamilton's wait list includes 368 seniors in crisis, meaning they are at "significant safety risk" in their current environment and all other options in the community have been exhausted.
Even those in crisis can wait six months or more to get into a Hamilton long-term-care home.
"There is a desperate need for additional long-term-care beds in our community," Coun. Brad Clark said. "We need the beds quickly."
Instead, the city has lost 321 long-term-care beds since the pandemic, concluded the report. Most of the beds were removed during COVID-19 when three- and four-bed wards were eliminated, resulting in 231 fewer spaces at local long-term-care homes.
The other 90 beds were at Pine Villa Care Centre in Stoney Creek and Blackadar Continuing Care Centre in Dundas, which have both stopped taking new admissions because they are shutting down.
The report does not mention that the loss of Blackadar is far more than the 60 beds that were in operation before it started closing. The long-term-care home was supposed to be rebuilt into a modern facility with 192 beds but the private for-profit owners decided to close it instead and build residential housing at 101 Creighton Rd.
In addition, the report does not address unanswered questions about how many more homes in Hamilton and the surrounding area will shut down instead of rebuilding.
The Ontario Long Term Care Association reports that nearly half of Ontario's homes are old facilities that need renovation. Many of them have licences expiring in 2025. The Spectator has repeatedly asked the Ministry of Long-Term Care for more than 19 months for a list of homes closing instead of renovating. The ministry has refused to provide the information, citing "economic and other interests of Ontario."
The province has committed $6.4 billion to build 30,000 new long-term-care beds in Ontario by 2028 and upgrade 28,000 existing beds. However, there is no guarantee of how many will be built in Hamilton and more than half of the city's planned new beds aren't expected to be built until well after 2028.
So far, the city knows of just 432 beds expected to open by 2028, which is only 111 more than Hamilton has already lost.
The new beds include a home being built by the Reformed Christian Dutch-Canadian community at 1415 Upper Wellington St. It will have 188 beds and is expected to open in late 2026.
However, Shalom Manor Hamilton will also serve Burlington after the Christian community closed its long-standing 60-bed home at 4486 Guelph Line on Nov. 7 because installing a required fire sprinkler system at Mount Nemo Christian Nursing Home was not feasible. The community's existing Christian long-term-care home in Grimsby has a five- to eight-year waiting list.
Also expected in 2026 are 20 new beds at Macassa Lodge at 701 Upper Sherman Ave. In addition, the renovation will upgrade 44 existing beds.
In 2027, a new 224-bed facility is slated to open where Pine Villa was previously at 490 Highway 8.
The biggest influx of long-term-care beds to Hamilton have not been announced by the province yet and aren't expected to be built for eight to nine years from now.
As reported by The Spectator, Schlegel Villages plans to build new two long-term-care homes with a combined total of 512 beds as part of a sweeping deal that will transform former provincial hospital lands at 290 Fennell Ave. W. The deal includes turning Hamilton Continuing Care into affordable housing because Schlegel says it's not possible to maintain the building as a long-term-care home in the future. The downtown home's 64 beds will move to the brow lands once the new homes are ready to open and become part of the 512.
No exact dates have been set yet, but the deal requires residents moving into the completed long-term-care homes within eight and a half years.
"Holy smokes, waiting almost 10 years for these beds," said Jackson. "I'm just so saddened and angered that I thought the ... (Premier Doug) Ford government was going to ... put multi-millions (of dollars) aside to build thousands upon thousands of long-term-care beds and hopefully Hamilton would get its share to address the immediate need - not six, eight, 10 years from now."
In the meantime, there is significant pressure on Hamilton's 25 long-term-care homes with a total of 3,773 beds. Occupancy rates are 98 per cent or more across the city and surrounding areas. The wait list is 1,500 to 2,000 people at any one time.
"I think the City of Hamilton needs to work with the ministry to somehow find a way to accelerate and make the entire process more efficient," said Clark.
Adding to the urgency of the problem is that 161 of the seniors waiting for long-term care as of Nov. 4 are in Hamilton's hospitals, occupying acute care beds they don't need but can't leave because no home can take them yet.
"The status of long-term-care beds in Hamilton is critical to the well-being of our aging population," stated the report. "It is essential for the province to prioritize actions that enhance our long-term-care capacity and address the systemic challenges faced by our health-care system."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Coun. Brad Clark said: "There isa desperate need for additional long-term-care beds in our community."Blackadar Continuing Care Centre in Dundas closed instead of rebuilding.Mount Nemo Christian Nursing Home in Burlington closed Nov. 7. Coun. Tom Jackson called the years-long wait for new long-term-care beds "unacceptable."Hamilton Spectator file photoCathie Coward Hamilton Spectator file photoSpectator file photoSpectator file photo

Document 39 of 500

Support for mental-health and addiction unit at Joseph Brant Hospital announced

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/support-mental-health-addiction-unit-at-joseph/docview/3134625806/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Support+for+mental-health+and+addiction+unit+at+Joseph+Brant+Hospital+announced&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A plan for 'transformational' change in local mental health and addictions care announced by Burlington's Joseph Brant Hospital nearly a year ago formally received provincial support last week.
While government officials did not release a specific financial contribution to the previously estimated $60-million project, Premier Doug Ford, with Minister of Health Sylvia Jones and associate minister of mental health Michael Tibollo, visited Joseph Brant on Nov. 25 to confirm the two-phase project is moving ahead.
The hospital hopes to start construction in early 2026.
"Our government will be building a new mental health unit at Joseph Brant Hospital," Ford said, adding the province will help ensure "Joseph Brant's hardworking staff have the tools and resources they need" to provide safe and comfortable mental-health care.
Jones said total anticipated funding for the project is not being publicized because construction has not been tendered yet.
The project includes:
Expansion of the inpatient unit's 21 beds to 26, including a new six-bed psychiatric intensive care unit;
Relocation of some outpatient services from a separate building to an area next to inpatient care;
And creation of a new child and youth mental-health day-hospital
Eric Vandewall, president and CEO of the hospital, said in January this year Joseph Brant was seeking $40 million in provincial funding, with the goal of having that money approved in the spring, starting construction as early as September 2024, and completing renovations and expansion by October 2026.
But provincial funding was not included in the spring budget.
The hospital's foundation kicked off a "For All Minds" campaign to raise an additional $20 million in January. Joseph Brant staff said earlier this month the campaign has raised nearly $10 million.
Last week, Vandewall said a lot of people at Joseph Brant and other local agencies have been working hard to secure provincial support.
He said the province has a planning process that the hospital is following, which is currently in the design stage.
Vandewall said construction of the new inpatient unit is anticipated to start in January 2026 and be completed nine to 10 months later, with work starting after that on the child and adolescent outpatient expansion.
Vandewall said the entire project should take "almost two and a half years, when it's all said and done."
He said there won't be any time lost between now and the start of construction, while planning and design, then tendering, takes place in partnership with the province.
"We are starting early work in April 2025 to prepare the area ... basically gutting down to the studs, so when a contractor comes in to bid there are no surprises, a blank canvas," Vandewall said.
In January, he said demand for inpatient mental-health care was increasing - with occupancy rates at the hospital anywhere from 100 to 140 per cent. The project would increase the unit's patient capacity by 23 per cent.
"The space is 1960s vintage. It has limitations, no private rooms ... lack of recreation space. The current space is not conducive to a higher level of care," Vandewall said.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford was at Joseph Brant Hospital on Nov. 25, announcing provincial support for expansion of the hospital's mental-health and addictions unit.Craig Campbell Metroland

Document 40 of 500

911's evolution in Canada from its history in London, Ont. to Next Generation technology and what happens when you dial by mistake

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/911s-evolution-canada-history-london-ont-next/docview/3134625794/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=911%27s+evolution+in+Canada+from+its+history+in+London%2C+Ont.+to+Next+Generation+technology+and+what+happens+when+you+dial+by+mistake&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Peel region celebrating a significant decrease in the number of non-emergency calls coming in to 911, amid the news it has switched to a new Next Generation 911 system, it's time to reflect on the history of Ontario's emergency system.
A history of 911
According to Service de Police de la Ville de Montreal spvm.qc.ca, in 1967, it was the AT&T company in the United States which suggested using the numbers 911 as a single phone number for reporting emergencies.
They picked that number because it was easy to remember, and it wasn't a North American area code.
Canada adopted the 911 call system in 1972 and the first city to implement it was London, Ont. in 1974.
In most other Ontario areas, a local seven-digit number was still required when calling for an ambulance, until the 911 emergency telephone number began use in the GTA around 1982, the Ontario Paramedic Association said on its website.
Ottawa's first 911 call came in June 22, 1988.
A slow roll out
Not all communities in Ontario today are connected with 911, with some northern areas still using a 10-digit number, or a 1-800 number to contact emergency personnel.
Last year, NDP MPP France Gélinas and other MPPs Guy Bourgouin, Sol Mamakwa, John Vanthof and Lise Vaugeois reintroduced a 911 Everywhere in Ontario bill.
"Ontario is the only province that still needs a 911 emergency response throughout the province," Vanthof said in a press release. "This has to change. The technology exists. We are in 2023; nobody else should suffer or die because they did not know what number to call for help."
Communities such as Attawapiskat and Pickle Lake still do not have 911 access, and residents in crisis must call either a 1-800 number or a 10-digit number.
Next generation
The Next Generation 911, nicknamed NG 911, came about in June of 2017, when the Canadian Radio-Television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC) required all Public Safety Answering Points to upgrade networks and technology.
According to the Ontario Association of Fire Chiefs, the system replaces decades-old analog technology in emergency communication centres with a digital network, allowing dispatchers to get to urgent calls faster and laying the groundwork for receiving texts, photos, and videos from callers in the future.
It also enables accurate location information through the Geographic Information System, which means operators can pinpoint the caller's location.
All telecommunications providers were expected to update their networks for NG 911 voice services as of March 1, 2022.
However, the global pandemic slowed some of that progress, and at least two separate companies let the CRTC know it would take longer to meet that time frame.
One company is Northwestel Inc., which operates on the traditional territories of First Nations, Métis, and Inuit peoples.
Northwestel wrote in 2021 that converting to NG 911 comes with challenges of "balancing the need to provide affordable and reliable services in the Far North that are comparable to those found in southern Canada, while still ensuring that we are appropriately compensated for the very high cost of providing services to remote communities in some of the most remote and challenging geographies in Canada."
CityWest Cable (North) Corp. echoed that it would have issues converting from basic 911 service in parts of northern British Columbia.
"In areas with basic 911 service, it is not feasible to implement NG 911 in the same way, or within the same time frames, as in areas that have enhanced 911 service," the CRTC noted on its website. "The technological solution to that issue is currently expected to be available in May 2027."
This March, the CRTC set a new deadline of March 4, 2025 for the completion of the NG 911 transition across Canada, making an exemption for Northwestel and CityWest Cable until a followup report is filed in June 2026.
A win for technology
Other communities have already made the switch, with London announcing it was the first single-tier municipality to transition on Tuesday, June 18.
Peel region converted to the new system back on Feb. 21, and by April, said it had already seen a dramatic decrease in call wait times.
Pocket dial fines
Of course, the introduction of 911 came long before most people owned a smartphone. Back then, if you were lucky enough to own a cellphone, you would have to press the numbers to connect.
However, today, cellphones often come equipped with an emergency call button that can dial 911 unintentionally.
"If you call 911 by mistake, please stay on the phone and advise us that it is a mistake," Toronto Police Services Communication Services Acting Manager Tamara Nalon said in a 2023 news release. "Otherwise, we are reaching out to call you or text you to confirm that you are safe and that can be time consuming."
When it takes operators a minimum of 90 seconds to call each person back, that is time that could be better spent responding to actual emergency 911 calls, when every second counts, Nalon said.
At the time, she said Toronto police averaged 5,200 emergency calls a day.
Peel region had been considering a fine for 911 misdials back in January, but that motion was not approved.
"Currently, there is no charge in Ontario for accidental calls to 911," the office of the solicitor general said.
CAPTION: In a Toronto Star file photo, emergency response 911 hotline operator Chris Bortkiewicz was photographed sending vital information over police radio after receiving a call through the Metro emergency response system, set up in 1982.A Toronto Star story from 1988 shows the lack of coverage of the 911 emergency number throughout the province.Looking back at the history of the 911 emergency service.

Document 41 of 500

Intense snow strands vehicles, knocks out power as Ontario town calls emergency

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/intense-snow-strands-vehicles-knocks-out-power-as/docview/3134625772/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Intense+snow+strands+vehicles%2C+knocks+out+power+as+Ontario+town+calls+emergency&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Intense snow squalls battering Ontario moved south Sunday after burying some parts of the province under more than a metre of snow, stranding people on roadways and prompting one town to declare a state of emergency.
Gravenhurst, a town in Ontario's Muskoka region hit with around 140 centimetres of snowfall, declared a state of emergency early Sunday.
"This is the most snow I've seen in the 27 years that I've lived here, in such a short period of time," Gravenhurst Mayor Heidi Lorenz said in an interview.
An impassable, snow-covered stretch of Highway 11 inundated by collisions had separated parts of the town from plows and emergency services on Saturday, prompting the state of emergency declaration, she said. "It was a perfect disaster."
Plow reinforcements were called in and skies started clear later Sunday after more than two days of nearly relentless snowfall.
The shifting winds offered relief to hard-hit communities but threatened to deliver snow squalls to areas further south, toward the Bruce Peninsula and London areas.
Premier Doug Ford said the province was working closely with local authorities to help them respond to the storm. Ford said he was relieved no injuries or deaths had been reported. "As cleanup crews continue their work, the best thing people in the area can do is stay home and stay safe until power is restored and roads and highways have been safely opened again," he said in a statement posted to social media.
Snowmobile-riding first responders took to otherwise impassable roads to help rescue people from stranded vehicles around Muskoka, police said. It was unclear how many people were still stranded on Sunday.

Hydro One, the provincial utility, reported that the number of customers without power was around 35,000 by Sunday afternoon, down from more than 60,000 earlier in the day.

A winter weather travel advisory was in effect for a band stretching from around Kitchener to Newmarket, where the forecast suggested between five and 10 centimetres of snow could fall Sunday.
CAPTION: Intense snow squalls battering Ontario are moving south after burying some central and northern parts of the province under more than a metre of snow and stranding vehicles on a highway.Spencer Colby The Canadian Press File Photo
