Document 85 of 500

'Good example of what's possible': Downtown Hamilton supportive housing welcomes residents

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/good-example-whats-possible-downtown-hamilton/docview/3132134449/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Good+example+of+what%27s+possible%27%3A+Downtown+Hamilton+supportive+housing+welcomes+residents&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Take a look at 257 King William St. - a new 24-unit supportive-housing building in downtown Hamilton that has started to welcome residents - and you'll find what the city needs to turn the tide on unsheltered homelessness.
So says Jeff Neven, CEO of Indwell, which is providing on-site support services for the three-storey CityHousing modular building near Wellington Street North - part of the non-profit's portfolio that provides supportive housing for more than 550 people in Hamilton.
"This project is a good example of what's possible, but unfortunately, 24 units is just not enough," Neven told city officials this week.
"The only way we're going to get out of the encampment and homelessness situation is if we create supportive housing," he said, urging more emphasis be placed on the mammoth task in Hamilton's upcoming budget.
More is on the way, the city's housing secretariat said in an update this week that touched on the new office's co-ordinated efforts on the sprawling file through various municipal divisions.
The secretariat - which operates under the auspices of a new housing strategic plan known as a "road map" - serves as a city division that evaluates housing applications vying for municipal support.
So far, the secretariat has received 42 eligible submissions representing 2,576 "potential" units of affordable and supportive housing through an online "portal" launched last December.
And of those, municipal contribution toward the construction of roughly 885 units have been approved, "pending the finalization of agreements," noted a report before council Wednesday.
"We're hopeful to have those agreements in place in the month of December," housing secretariat director Justin Lewis told The Spectator after an upbeat presentation that celebrated the city's achievements toward the road map.
The strategy stands on four "pillars": new construction, acquiring "at risk" rentals, preserving existing affordable stock, increasing supply through supports.
The road map draws on various funding envelopes, including the federal $93.5-million, four-year federal Housing Accelerator Fund, which is expected to help build 2,675 units "across the housing continuum" through incentives.
Council has also set aside $31 million over three years with the specific goal of supporting the construction of 200 supportive housing units through the city's non-profit partners, such as Indwell, YWCA, Sacajawea and Good Shepherd.
It has also committed $4 million a year that's to be allocated to projects via the secretariat's evaluation process with municipal contributions in various forms, including grants, waived fees or provision of land.
In parallel, the city's real-estate division is reviewing municipal land that could be made available for "nominal" amounts to non-profit to build affordable housing, including six initial properties identified last year.
On deck, is the development of a two-year pilot project that will help non-profits purchase affordable rental housing that's "at risk" of escalating rates through property sales, for instance.
St. Matthew's House's is finishing up a six-storey, 15-unit building at 412 Barton St. E., another modular build that's expected to open near Wentworth Street North in March.
The social-service agency decided to build the deeply affordable residence for Black and Indigenous seniors in a lot left vacant with the demolition of its former food bank building next door.
So far, St. Matthew's infill development, or "pocket housing," has received $7.6 million in municipal fee waivers, federal rapid housing dollars and provincial funding, executive director Renée Wetselaar noted.
The project sprung from talks with the African Canadian Caribbean Association and Hamilton Regional Indian Centre, Wetselaar told council ahead of Lewis's presentation.
"And we ran to the finish line because of the road map."
A key consideration for non-profits vying for upper-level government funding is municipal commitment to projects, a crucial ingredient that has been haphazard or missing, spurring complaints from agencies in recent years.
Lewis noted "letters of intent" to eligible applicants show the "city's at the table" for projects.
But the secretariat has listened to feedback that "we need to do better" through a new process, he said.
"We are looking to ensure that those agreements for those not-for-profit operators to have housing come to fruition."
Federal dollars are flowing, Lewis said, but noted funding from the Ontario government is lacking, specifically for mental-health and addictions services needed for supportive units.
During a stop in Hamilton for an unrelated announcement Friday, Premier Doug Ford said his government has committed more than $700 million to homelessness across the province, including $28 million in Hamilton.
"I want to give them shelter. I want to give them help. I want to give them a hand up," Ford said of those who are homeless. "Most importantly, I want to get them a good paying job to get them out of the situation they're in. That's what I want to do."
But Mayor Andrea Horwath there's "no doubt" provincial dollars are needed to create more supportive housing.
"And there's no doubt the city well utilizes the $28 million that the premier referenced, but that number is reflective of a community (from) a couple of years ago. Our community has changed significantly in terms of the need."
Hamilton expects 2024's overall spending on homelessness and housing across various divisions, and including in-year council approvals, to be $186 million, which is $39 million more than in 2023.
Of that, $41 million is from the provincial government and $20 million from Ottawa, with the city covering about $125 million, or 67 per cent.
Neven, meanwhile, during a budget-feedback session earlier this week, expressed the frustration of trying to align various funding envelopes.
Projects can face the hurdle of co-ordinating capital funding with provincial health dollars, the latter an application process that comes late in the development process.
"You essentially have to build them on spec, and then go after the province after that," said Neven, describing how the late-stage gamble of securing funding can put projects at risk.
For 257 King William St., a more than $14-million capital project, Indwell is committed to working with the city to have the residence's health-support costs "rightfully transferred" to the province, he said.
In September 2023, council approved $1.2 million in annual funding for support services at the building "until a provincial commitment for these costs can be obtained," CityHousing CEO Adam Sweedland noted via email.
Through his budget address, Neven said he also aimed to dispel the notion that supportive housing costs too much money, noting Indwell received $1.4 million last year for operations.
"Or put it another way, that works out to about $7 per person per day for the folks that we provide supportive housing to."
Moreover, most of Indwell's funding comes in the form of rental subsidies that flow through the municipality from higher levels of government, Neven said.
"By my count, Indwell received approximately less than $100,000 that's directly from the Hamilton levy last year. That works out to about 40 cents per person per day to provide housing and support services at Indwell."
Meanwhile, only 15 supportive units "by our entire sector," are under construction in Hamilton, said Neven, referring to the St. Matthew's House project on Barton.
"It is time for the budget to reflect the critical need and the shortage of supportive permanent housing as basic infrastructure."
Horwath said she wished the city had a "pot a gold" to dish out funding to non-profits "to get that housing built as quickly as possible."
But the secretariat's work is finding "various pathways to make improvements," she said, noting applicants in the past worked to "line up" councillor votes to land funding without proper analysis of proposals.
-With files from Matthew Van Dongen
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: St. Matthew's House is developing a six-storey, 15-unit affordable-housing building at 412 Barton St. E. in a vacant lot where its old food bank once stood next to its headquarters.St. Matthew's House is developing a six-storey, 15-unit affordable-housing building at 412 Barton St. E. in a vacant lot where its old food bank once stood next to its headquarters.The 24-unit modular build on King William Street is a "good example of what's possible" when it comes to supportive housing, Indwell CEO Jeff Neven says.

Document 86 of 500

Ontario, Canada recalls: Wall beds with crush risk, frozen chicken, soy sauce dish with high lead levels, carrots linked to deadly E. coli outbreak in U.S., and more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-canada-recalls-wall-beds-with-crush-risk/docview/3132134448/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%2C+Canada+recalls%3A+Wall+beds+with+crush+risk%2C+frozen+chicken%2C+soy+sauce+dish+with+high+lead+levels%2C+carrots+linked+to+deadly+E.+coli+outbreak+in+U.S.%2C+and+more&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Numerous food and consumer products have been recalled by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) and Health Canada recently.
Recalled products include wall beds, Harley-Davidson motorcycle helmets, vape liquid, a soy sauce dish with high lead levels, toys that present a choking risk, nuts with salmonella contamination risk, potstickers sold at Walmart, carrots with possible E. coli contamination and more.
Dorel wall beds recalled due to serious impact and crush hazard
Thousands of Dorel wall beds sold in Canada have been recalled due to "serious impact and crush hazard." The Health Canada recall notice dated Nov. 21 stated: "the beds can become detached from the wall and fall, posing a tip-over and crush hazard that can result in serious injury or death."
As of Nov. 12, the recall notice said there has been a report of a bed falling in Canada, but no report of concussions, contusions, cuts and bruises.
The company reported 2,614 units of the affected products were sold in Canada and 69,344 were sold in the United States between May 2021 to April 2024.
This recall involves Dorel full- and queen-sized wall beds sold online under the names Pinnacle, Paramount, Impressions, Holly Hills and Her Majesty wall beds. They were also sold as part of the brand names Signature Sleep or Novogratz. The beds were sold in white, light oak, grey oak, black oak, espresso, walnut and natural shades. They were sold with or without cabinets and lighting attached to the bedframe.
See the full recall notice for more information.
Winkler's breakfast sausage and smoked turkey thighs recalled due to pieces of metal
The CFIA has issued a recall for Winkler's brand breakfast sausage and smoked turkey thighs due to pieces of metal.
The recall notice dated Nov. 21 said the products were distributed in Alberta, British Columbia, Manitoba and Ontario.
What products were impacted?
CFIA said this recall is linked to the Sifto brand Hy·Grade salt, which was also recalled due to pieces of metal. The affected salt was used to make these recalled products. For more information on the food safety investigation, visit the CFIA's website on that specific investigation.
Stokke YOYO³ stroller recalled due to injury hazard
Health Canada has issued a recall notice for the Stokke YOYO³ stroller due to what is a being described as a potential "injury hazard." The recall notice dated Nov. 15 stated, "the parking brakes on strollers with '142AA' in the serial number may disengage, posing an injury hazard."
Nutworks brand raw whole pistachios recalled due to salmonella
Nutworks brand raw whole pistachios have been recalled due to possible salmonella contamination.
The CFIA issued a recall notice about the impacted pistachios on Nov. 17 and indicated the nuts were distributed in Ontario.
Scorpion and Harley-Davison motorcycle helmets recalled
Thousands of Harley-Davidson and Scorpion motorcycle helmets have been recalled due to a "physical hazard."
Two separate recall notices were posted this week and involve the Scorpion EXO Covert X and Harley-Davidson HD-X07 motorcycle helmets and the Scorpion EXO-C90 and Harley-Davidson HD-X06 motorcycle helmets.
Omni brand potstickers sold at Walmart recalled
Omni brand potstickers, which were sold at Walmart Canada, have been recalled due to undeclared shrimp, which is identified as an allergen risk.
The CFIA recall notice dated Nov. 19 said do not consume recalled products, which you are allergic or sensitive to, as they may cause a serious or a life-threatening reaction.
Red-white-and-blue ceramic soy sauce dish recalled due to lead
Health Canada's recall notice dated Nov. 21 said that agency's sampling and evaluation program has determined the recalled glazed ceramic plate contains lead in excess of allowable limits.
"Lead is highly toxic, especially to children. A range of serious health effects have been associated with exposure to lead and cadmium, including anemia, vomiting, diarrhea, heart and immune system," the recall notice read. "In extreme cases, there have been deaths."
This recall involves a red-white-and-blue ceramic soy sauce dish. The product UPC code is 4 573307 039234 and can be found in the sticker on the bottom of the dish. The product has a flower shape with 12 petals, consisting of six white petals, three red petals with a yellow-gold flower design and three blue petals with wave design.
The company reported 312 units of the affected products were sold in Canada between October 2023 to November 2024.
For more information, consumers can contact Oomomo Canada by telephone at 604-295-4037, by email at info@oomomostore.com or visit the company website.
For more information about the recall, visit the full recall notice.
Various brands of frozen breaded chicken products recalled due to pieces of metal
Various brands of frozen breaded chicken products have been recalled across Canada due to pieces of metal. The CFIA posted a recall notice about the frozen breaded chicken dated Nov. 14.
Compliments, Great Value, Janes, no name and Sysco are brands impacted by the national recall.
Various brands of organic carrots recalled due to E. coli O121
Various brands of organic baby carrots, linked to a deadly E. coli outbreak in the U.S., have been recalled in the U.S. and Canada.
Brands included in the national recall are Cal-Organic Farms, PC Organics, Compliments Organic and Bunny-Luv. The recalling firm is Grimmway Farms. Walmart Canada and Sobeys have confirmed the products were sold in their stores. Costco Canada and Amazon posted recall notices to customers.
For more information, visit CFIA's full recall notice dated Nov. 18.
Toys recalled due to choking hazard
Three toys, recalled due to choking hazards, were posted this week: The Souvenir du Canada brown bear plush toy along with Snowcap moose plush coin bag and the Snappy Plush cat model were recalled due to choking hazards.
Souvenir du Canada brown bear plush toy
Souvenir du Canada brown bear plush toy recall notice dated Nov. 19 said the bear's hard plastic eyes can detach and pose a choking risk to young children.
The brown bear plush toy has a red-and-white hat and sweater with Canada written on the front with a maple leaf symbol. The item number 54110 is located on the tag with UPC code 772548541108.
For more information on that recall, visit the full recall notice.
Snowcap moose plush coin bag recalled due to choking hazard
This recall involves the Snowcap brand moose plush coin bag. The plush toy has the appearance of a brown-and-yellow moose head with the UPC code 83797402711.
Health Canada's sampling and evaluation program has determined this plush toy does not meet the Toys Regulations under the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act. The hard plastic eyes on these plush toys can detach and pose a choking hazard to young children. For more information, visit the full recall notice.
Snappy Plush cat model recalled due to choking hazard
This recall involves the cat model of Snappy Plush - soft toys forming a bracelet that attaches to the wrist or to any object such as a backpack. They measure about 10-centimeters high and are made in three colours: dark pink, pale pink and rainbow colour. A label attached to the plush toy indicates the production batch at risk, which is E2402281039 distributed by the company Groupe Ricochet.
Health Canada determined the product does not comply with the Toys Regulations under the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act. Tests have allowed Health Canada to note the nose of stuffed animals can detach from the toy and pose a choking risk for young children.
For more information, visit the full recall notice.
Castelbel liquid soap recalled due to microbial contamination
Castelbel liquid soap, which has been recalled due to microbial contamination, can be returned to a Winners, Marshalls or HomeSense location for a refund, according to a Health Canada recall notice dated Nov. 20.
The Health Canada recall notice said customers should "immediately stop using" the recalled products.
ePuffer vaping substances recalled due to lack of proper labelling
This recall involves various flavours and nicotine concentrations of ePuffer 50-mililiter (mL) vaping substance bottles and ePuffer 30-mL vaping substance bottles.
The Health Canada recall notice dated Nov. 22 stated the recalled products do not meet the labelling requirements for vaping products as required by the Vaping Products Labelling and Packaging Regulations under the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act.
The company reported 883 units of the affected products were sold in Canada between July 2022 to November 2024.
The Health Canada recall notice said vaping substances containing nicotine present a poisoning risk if they are ingested. "Inappropriate labelling can lead to unintentional exposure to the product and cause serious illness or injuries, including death," the recall notice said.
As of Nov. 22, the company has received no reports of incidents or injuries in Canada.
For a the full list of recalled products and more information, visit the recall notice.
Jack's Magic Power Blue Instant Cartridge & DE Filter Cleaner recalled due to corrosivity hazard
This recall involves Jack's Magic Power Blue Instant Cartridge & DE Filter Cleaner (250 mL, 1 L and 20 L) for pools and spas. The recalled products can be identified by the UPC located on the back of the product label.
The Health Canada recall notice tells customers to immediately stop using the recalled product and contact Backyard Brands for a replacement product or refund.
The recalled product does not meet the labelling requirements of the Consumer Chemicals and Containers Regulations, 2001 under the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act.
The lack of appropriate labelling information could result in unintentional exposure to the products and lead to serious illness or injury.
As of Nov. 11, the company has received no reports of incidents or injuries in Canada.
For the full list of recalled products and more information, visit the recall notice.
For more recall notice posted by Health Canada and the Canadian Food Inspection Agency, visit the government's recall and safety alerts webpage.

Document 87 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3132045115/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bring more flights to airport
Re: Passenger numbers nosedive at Hamilton's
airport amid discount airline woes, Nov. 19
There's a lot of moaning and groaning about declining numbers of air travellers at John C. Munro Hamilton International Airport. Gee, go figure. Maybe if the powers that be up there would get some of the big airlines to actually fly out of the place the numbers would rise. Every time we travel somewhere, we have to drive to that nightmare of an airport in Toronto. Try offering the desired services and stop crying the blues. And it might even make Doug Ford happy by easing the gridlock on roads bound for Toronto.
Paul E. Casey, Ancaster
Families victimized once again
Re: Families of Paul Bernardo victims have
been barred from attending in-person parole
hearing, lawyer says, Nov. 20
I am incensed that the Parole Board of Canada initially told the families of Leslie Mahaffy and Kristen French that they were "unable to ensure safety and security of all hearing attendees."
My understanding is that prisons in Canada have both the capacity and wherewithal to ensure both the safety of the victims' families and the criminals.
It appears Leslie and Kristen's families are once again being victimized by both Canada's justice system and one of Canada's most dangerous offenders.
Mary Elizabeth Toth, Ancaster
Taxes better the community
Re: Don't spend tax dollars on golf, Nov. 21
With respect to Peter Mountford's suggestion to not spend his tax dollars on Chedoke Golf Course, the same could very well be said for public pools, pickleball/tennis courts, recreation centres, libraries, etc.
We all must get along and contribute to all these activities, even if we don't necessarily participate in them. If not, what kind of quality of life do we have? Not everyone has enough money to join a private club. Careful what you wish for.
Mary Hickey, Burlington
Altering course a slippery slope
Some people see golf courses, even municipal ones, as "elite." However, changing the status of the Chedoke land could open it to immense pressure from developers to build on it, and not affordable housing.
I just read an interesting report about how golf courses, which are quiet and dark at night, become more biodiverse than parks. Surely something to consider, if the goal is to keep the city healthy.
M.M. Bédard, Hamilton
Trudeau gets in on the action
Re: Liberals plan to give $250 cheques to millions
of Canadians, cut GST over holidays, Nov. 21
Well, it seems that our prime minister is taking a page from Premier Doug Ford's vote-buying spree. The PM's temporary GST tax break and Ford's $200 gift to all of Ontario's residents is nothing more than buying votes for anticipated forthcoming elections.
These millions of dollars would be more appropriately directed to our underfunded health-care programs and the current homeless situation. I would like to think that most Canadians sense the objectives of these power-hungry leaders.
Allan Avery, Binbrook
Send a message with 'rebate'
Regarding Premier Doug Ford's $250 rebate cheques: My first inclination was to support our local food bank and hospital. But this only provides this government fodder to not increase funding. Please consider these options:
Donate to one of the other political parties. You will get a tax receipt. You will "tick off" this present government. You will be sending a strong message about your disdain for this obvious veiled "bribe."
Find a Ukrainian or other refugee family. Give the funds directly to them.
Support a local charity such as Flamborough Connects or Flamborough Food Bank in their quest to provide Christmas gifts to needy seniors and children.
Inform the premier, your MPP and the health minister about your direction of funds.
We must send a strong message to this government that its wasteful spending and inadequate funding for health, education and housing will not be tolerated or ignored.
Annette Webber, Freelton
EV rebates and tariffs
I can't afford to buy an EV car, but I could if China's EV cars were allowed into Canada without a 100 per cent duty imposed on them.
This doesn't seem like a good way for Canada to fight climate change. At least the government of Canada offers a $5,000 rebate. Ontario Premier Doug Ford and his government offer nothing.
Herbert Hyman, Oakville

Document 88 of 500

Ontario signs $108M school food program deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-signs-108m-school-food-program-deal/docview/3132035840/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+signs+%24108M+school+food+program+deal&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has signed an agreement with the federal government for $108 million in funding for school nutrition programs, which officials say will allow the province's program to serve more children.
The federal Liberal government set aside $1 billion over five years to expand access to such programs across the country after pressure from federal New Democrats.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced Friday that Ontario is the third province to sign on to access the money, and it will receive $108.5 million over three years.
"When kids don't have full bellies, when they can't hear the teacher because of the grumbling in their tummy, when they're worried and low energy and not able to focus in class because they're hungry, we all suffer - not just them, but the future they're building, and (their) future contributions to Canada," he said at a school in Brampton.
Trudeau said the funding means healthy meals for 160,000 more kids in Ontario this school year.
Ontario's provincial program currently serves more than 600,000 children and the federal Conservatives note that there are more than two million kids in Ontario schools.
Premier Doug Ford welcomed the funding. "I've been a big fan of it from day one, and we're going to work hand in hand with the federal government to make sure the kids don't go to school hungry," he said in Hamilton.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Subject: Funding

Title: Ontario signs $108M school food program deal

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.15

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 23, 2024

Document 89 of 500

Firefighter cancer coverage to get boost: Ontario is improving benefits in legislation born out of infamously toxic, four-day Plastimet fire

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/firefighter-cancer-coverage-get-boost/docview/3132035810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Firefighter+cancer+coverage+to+get+boost%3A+Ontario+is+improving+benefits+in+legislation+born+out+of+infamously+toxic%2C+four-day+Plastimet+fire&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario plans to expand presumptive coverage for certain cancers in firefighters - a move that builds on legislation born in Hamilton following the worst toxic blaze in city history.
Premier Doug Ford came to Hamilton on Friday to announce legislation to make it easier for firefighters to access Workplace Safety and Insurance Board (WSIB) benefits for kidney and colorectal cancers.
Specifically, that would allow "presumptive coverage" for primary-site kidney cancer after 10 years of service by a firefighter, rather than the current 20 years. "Presumptive" means firefighters do not have to prove their illness was caused by their work.
The province will also remove the requirement for colorectal cancer to be diagnosed before age 61.
Ford noted the province has expanded or added presumptive coverage for various types of cancers in recent years to help address "alarming rates" of the disease among firefighters.
He also gave a shout-out to Mayor Andrea Horwath, who as a New Democrat MPP in 2006 teamed up with the son of local firefighter Bob Shaw to successfully push through the first presumptive coverage legislation the following year.
Shaw died in 2004 of esophageal cancer linked to his effort to extinguish the infamously toxic, four-day fire that burned through 400 tonnes of plastic at north-end recycler Plastimet.
His son, Nathan, has lobbied over two decades for further upgrades to the law and praised the current Tory government for the latest improvements.
"When we first advocated for these changes in 2006, (Ontario was) last in country for illness recognition for firefighters," he said in an interview Friday.
"We've gone from worst to first since then ... hopefully as the science evolves we will continue to build on (available coverage.)"
Shaw said he appreciated the "non-partisan support" for the firefighter health issue, noting the original private member's bill was pitched by then-NDP politician Horwath, enacted by a Liberal government and now improved by a Tory province.
"It's not about politics; it's about firefighters, their families and communities."
Horwath, who attended the announcement at a fire training facility on the Mountain on Friday, said she was proud of the original community-wide push to get the first legislation passed.
"But here we are, all these years later, improving it. Why? Because the sad part of the story is that firefighters are still dying of cancers and other illnesses they contract because of the work they do, to save people and be the heroes they are in our communities."
The current legislation recognizes 19 variations of cancer in presumptive coverage for firefighters.
Firefighter advocates are continually pushing for improvements to the law - both to add cancers to the list and cut the amount of service time required to allow coverage, said Rob D'Amico, a longtime firefighter and president of the Hamilton Professional Firefighters Association union.
"The science tells us firefighters are contracting these cancers at a rate four times higher than the general population," he said.
New research and statistics also justify reductions in the required service time to qualify for benefits coverage after becoming ill, D'Amico added.
He noted the province has made several improvements to available coverage in recent years.
In the last few years, the government also cut required service time to qualify for coverage of skin and esophageal cancer. Last year, it added presumptive coverage for thyroid and pancreatic cancers.
Matthew Van Dongen is
a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Local firefighter association president Rob D'Amico speaks ahead of an announcement by Premier Doug Fordin Hamilton. D'Amico said firefighter advocates are continually pushing for improvements to the law - both to add cancers to the list and cut the amount of service time required to allow coverage.Matthew Van Dongen The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter

Document 90 of 500

'Good example of what's possible' Downtown supportive housing welcomes residents: But more needed, non-profit Indwell says of collaboration with CityHousing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/good-example-whats-possible-downtown-supportive/docview/3132033845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Good+example+of+what%27s+possible%27+Downtown+supportive+housing+welcomes+residents%3A+But+more+needed%2C+non-profit+Indwell+says+of+collaboration+with+CityHousing&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Take a look at 257 King William St. - a new 24-unit supportive-housing building in downtown Hamilton that has started to welcome residents - and you'll find what the city needs to turn the tide on unsheltered homelessness.
So says Jeff Neven, CEO of Indwell, which is providing on-site support services for the three-storey CityHousing modular building near Wellington Street North - part of the non-profit's portfolio that provides supportive housing for more than 550 people in Hamilton.
"This project is a good example of what's possible, but unfortunately, 24 units is just not enough," Neven told city officials this week.
"The only way we're going to get out of the encampment and homelessness situation is if we create supportive housing," he said, urging more emphasis be placed on the mammoth task in Hamilton's upcoming budget.
More is on the way, the city's housing secretariat said in an update this week that touched on the new office's co-ordinated efforts on the sprawling file through various municipal divisions.
The secretariat - which operates under the auspices of a new housing strategic plan known as a "road map" - serves as a city division that evaluates housing applications vying for municipal support.
So far, the secretariat has received 42 eligible submissions representing 2,576 "potential" units of affordable and supportive housing through an online "portal" launched last December.
And of those, municipal contribution toward the construction of roughly 885 units have been approved, "pending the finalization of agreements," noted a report before council Wednesday.
"We're hopeful to have those agreements in place in the month of December," housing secretariat director Justin Lewis told The Spectator after an upbeat presentation that celebrated the city's achievements toward the road map.
The strategy stands on four "pillars": new construction, acquiring "at risk" rentals, preserving existing affordable stock, increasing supply through supports.
The road map draws on various funding envelopes, including the federal $93.5-million, four-year federal Housing Accelerator Fund, which is expected to help build 2,675 units "across the housing continuum" through incentives.
Council has also set aside $31 million over three years with the specific goal of supporting the construction of 200 supportive housing units through the city's non-profit partners, such as Indwell, YWCA, Sacajawea and Good Shepherd.
It has also committed $4 million a year that's to be allocated to projects via the secretariat's evaluation process with municipal contributions in various forms, including grants, waived fees or provision of land.
In parallel, the city's real-estate division is reviewing municipal land that could be made available for "nominal" amounts to non-profit to build affordable housing, including six initial properties identified last year.
On deck is the development of a two-year pilot project that will help non-profits purchase affordable rental housing that's "at risk" of escalating rates through property sales, for instance.
St. Matthew's House is finishing up a six-storey, 15-unit building at 412 Barton St. E., another modular build that's expected to open near Wentworth Street North in March.
Federal dollars are flowing, Lewis said, but noted funding from the Ontario government is lacking, specifically for mental-health and addictions services needed for supportive units.
During a stop in Hamilton for an unrelated announcement Friday, Premier Doug Ford said his government has committed more than $700 million to homelessness across the province, including $28 million in Hamilton.
"I want to give them shelter. I want to give them help. I want to give them a hand up," Ford said of those who are homeless. "Most importantly, I want to get them a good paying job to get them out of the situation they're in. That's what I want to do."
But Mayor Andrea Horwath there's "no doubt" provincial dollars are needed to create more supportive housing.
"And there's no doubt the city well utilizes the $28 million that the premier referenced, but that number is reflective of a community (from) a couple of years ago. Our community has changed significantly in terms of the need."
Hamilton expects 2024's overall spending on homelessness and housing across various divisions, and including in-year council approvals, to be $186 million, which is $39 million more than in 2023.
Of that, $41 million is from the provincial government and $20 million from Ottawa, with the city covering about $125 million, or 67 per cent.
Neven, meanwhile, during a budget-feedback session earlier this week, expressed the frustration of trying to align various funding envelopes.
Projects can face the hurdle of co-ordinating capital funding with provincial health dollars, the latter an application process that comes late in the development process.
"You essentially have to build them on spec, and then go after the province after that," said Neven, describing how the late-stage gamble of securing funding can put projects at risk.
For 257 King William St., a more than $14-million capital project, Indwell is committed to working with the city to have the residence's health-support costs "rightfully transferred" to the province, he said.
In September 2023, council approved $1.2 million in annual funding for support services at the building "until a provincial commitment for these costs can be obtained," CityHousing CEO Adam Sweedland noted via email.
Through his budget address, Neven said he also aimed to dispel the notion that supportive housing costs too much money, noting Indwell received $1.4 million last year for operations.
"Or put it another way, that works out to about $7 per person per day for the folks that we provide supportive housing to."
Meanwhile, only 15 supportive units "by our entire sector," are under construction in Hamilton, said Neven, referring to the St. Matthew's House project on Barton.
"It is time for the budget to reflect the critical need and the shortage of supportive permanent housing as basic infrastructure."
Horwath said she wished the city had a "pot a gold" to dish out funding to non-profits "to get that housing built as quickly as possible."
But the secretariat's work is finding "various pathways to make improvements," she said, noting applicants in the past worked to "line up" councillor votes to land funding without proper analysis of proposals.
With files from
Matthew Van Dongen
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: The 24-unit modular build on King William Street is a "good example of what's possible" when it comes to supportive housing, Indwell CEO Jeff Neven says.St. Matthew's House is developing a six-storey, 15-unit affordable-housing building at 412 Barton St. E. in a vacant lot where its old food bank once stood next to its headquarters.Cathie Coward photos The Hamilton SpectatorThe Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Teviah Moro Reporter With files from Matthew Van Dongen

Ontario expanding firefighter cancer coverage born out of Hamilton Plastimet fire

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-expanding-firefighter-cancer-coverage/docview/3132034665/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 92 of 500

Bonnie Crombie launches first campaign ad, blames Doug Ford for doctors' shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-launches-first-campaign-ad-blames/docview/3131838318/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+launches+first+campaign+ad%2C+blames+Doug+Ford+for+doctors%27+shortage&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie is fighting back.
The Liberal leader finally launched her first campaign commercial Friday after enduring millions of dollars in attack ads from Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
Taking aim at Ontario's chronic doctor shortage at a time when some 2.5 million people don't have a family physician, Crombie's advertisement says people deserve "better."
"Every day I hear that you can't find a doctor," she intones over footage of a diverse group of Ontario families going about their daily lives.
"Your kid's school is overcrowded and you can't afford the essentials. You feel stuck," says Crombie before a veiled reference to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now being investigated by the RCMP.
"And every day, you hear about another one of Doug Ford's friends getting richer while you fall behind. Enough is enough," she says.
"Your government should be working for you. As your premier, I'll make sure we're getting the basics right: your health care, your kids' education, a home and a life you can actually afford, and a strong economy that brings opportunity back to Ontario so you can get ahead."
The one-minute spot will initially be online only, but the party has plans to air it on more costly broadcast television in the weeks ahead.
Crombie's salvo comes after an 11-month blitz of Tory ads attacking her, which polls suggest have been effective for the governing party.
It's believed the Tories have spent about $10 million of party donors' money on their ads targeting the rookie Grit leader, who won the leadership last Dec. 2.
Mindful that polls show Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals are not popular, Crombie's commercial emphasizes her personal brand more than her party's name.
"I'm Bonnie Crombie and I'm here for Ontario," it concludes.
The ad comes as Ford is considering a snap spring election, one year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote.
CAPTION: The people of Ontario are struggling while Doug Ford's friends keep getting richer. As Premier, I'll focus on getting the basics right: better healthcare, quality education for your kids, and a strong economy - because your government should work for you.Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Premier Doug Ford.Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Premier Doug Ford.

Document 93 of 500

Nov. 22: Where's the scrappy mayor we voted for, don't feel sorry for Swifties and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-22-wheres-scrappy-mayor-we-voted-dont-feel/docview/3131838199/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+22%3A+Where%27s+the+scrappy+mayor+we+voted+for%2C+don%27t+feel+sorry+for+Swifties+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Where's our scrappy mayor?
Re: Two years into council's term: Our civic pride is collapsing around us, Nov. 20
Boy, Laura Babcock was right on and saying what so many Hamiltonians are asking: Where is Andrea? Where is the woman elected to fight for our city as most thought she would? I have seen her twice on the news since she took office and one was when she OK'd the closing of the parking lot in Stoney Creek by using her special mayoral powers, which was awful.
She's not standing up for Hamilton nor does she seem to be protecting it. We are falling apart as a city to the point it's not only embarrassing to say we live in Hamilton, but people are choosing to leave. Where is the Andrea you constantly saw on the news when she was in Queen's Park, always fighting for something, always complaining about the others and never backing down? Where on earth did she go?
Hamilton is dying as a city with the ones that count not fighting for it.
Debbie Escott, Hamilton
City moving in wrong direction
I totally support Laura Babcock's comments in Wednesday's Spec. Our city is out of control and has no direction or leadership. Workers are fixing curbs on Upper Wentworth across from Limeridge Mall. Curbs! Really? We have one of the worst roads in Ontario for the last three years, namely Aberdeen, and they decide to fix curbs somewhere else. No priority here!
At the West Harbour, crews worked feverishly installing curbs and roadways over the last few years and now it stands as the badlands with ugly weeds and roads to nowhere. What happened to the plan? We had a house being built on Ferrie Street which was falling over and it had to be demolished. That mess was found by an off-duty inspector. There are buildings in disrepair, collapsing and falling down on major downtown streets. They can't rebuild a housing development in our north end because the railway says no. None of this makes sense.
John Day, Hamilton
Situation gets even more frustrating
Re: New petition, two unions call on travelling Catholic trustees to resign, Nov. 20
These trustees have been caught with their hands in the cookie jar and they think they can repay the money and then go back to their jobs. They should resign or be fired. As far as board chair Rick Petrella saying he would give a further interview and then declined to do so, it makes you wonder, what is he hiding? As for the promised provincial government review, it has not even started; put on the back burner like so many other issues. It is like, "tell the people what they want to hear and give them nothing."
Now, we find out the education minister has not even appointed an investigator. What a joke!
Sally Carey, Hamilton
Maybe we can share the land?
Re: Should Hamilton shrink its golf footprint for new parks? Nov. 14
Here's an idea I would like to share with Hamilton city council. I can also charge a multi-thousand-dollar consultant fee to legitimize the current process. Let the golfers enjoy the properties during spring and summer months and let the general public enjoy the properties during the off-season. We can add some more rules and regulations to inflate, delay and complicate the idea, but I think it's a good start.
Pete Simpson, Cayuga
Keep criminals locked up longer
The cost of prisons is cheap compared to the cost of crime. Ten per cent of the criminals do 90 per cent of the crime. If we can keep that 10 per cent behind bars for a longer period of time, the crime rate will definitely fall. Compare the cost of prisons to what criminals actually cost society: police, courts, victims, lost work, etc., not to mention the cost of victims' mental distress. It's easy to see the cost to keep that 10 per cent incarcerated is very cheap compared to the cost of their crimes.
Bill Morgan, Hamilton
Hard to feel sorry for Swifties
Re: Halton police urge 'extreme caution' for Taylor Swift tickets as scam reports grow, Nov. 19
Burton Cummings had a great hit song called "Fine state of affairs." It really is appropriate for today. Good people who have had less than fortunate lives are living in encampments, yet others are crying and complaining of being scammed out of $70,000 or more on Taylor Swift tickets. "There by the grace of God go I."
Al Dionisi, Stoney Creek
Why didn't Democrats steal 2024 election?
It is beyond comprehension how the American people, watching any Donald Trump supporter on television answering questions with a long string of rationalizations and with the total absence of any proof, contend that the 2020 presidential election was stolen. Ask any conspiracy theorist why the 2016 and 2024 elections weren't also stolen. Of course, anyone who believes in universal conspiracy needs to consult a psychotherapist.
Dan Riga, Burlington
CAPTION: Where is the Andrea Horwath we so often saw in Queen's Park, always fighting for something and never backing down? a reader asks.

Subject: Crime; Local elections; Mayors; Conspiracy

People: Swift, Taylor

Title: Nov. 22: Where's the scrappy mayor we voted for, don't feel sorry for Swifties and other letters to the editor

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 22, 2024

Section: opinion/letters-to-the-editor

Document 94 of 500

160,000 Ontario kids will get a chance to eat for free under new school food program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/160-000-ontario-kids-will-get-chance-eat-free/docview/3131837914/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=160%2C000+Ontario+kids+will+get+a+chance+to+eat+for+free+under+new+school+food+program&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Ontario is set to get more than $108 million from the federal government to expand existing school food programs and start feeding an extra 160,000 kids per day before the end of the school year, the Star has learned.
The new funding is slated to be unveiled Friday, when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is expected to announce that Ontario is the third province to sign on to its emerging National School Food Program, after Newfoundland and Manitoba inked deals this fall.
Federal Families, Children and Social Development Minister Jenna Sudds told the Star ahead of the announcement that the funding - $108.5 million over the next three years - will go to Ontario's existing Student Nutrition Program and First Nations Student Nutrition Program, which provide meals through more than 13 agencies to schools across the province. That means "close to a million Ontario kids" will have access to school food before summer break, representing almost 75 per cent of the province's schools, she said.
Sudds added that the goal is ultimately to have a program that is available to any student that wants food, when asked about how Toronto's municipal government is looking into delivering a "universal" school food program across Canada's biggest city.
According to the Daily Bread Food Bank, more than 100,000 children across 220 schools in the city "do not have access to good food," while more than one in 10 people in the city rely on food banks to eat.
"Obviously we have ambitions of seeing an inclusive program with no barriers, that any child that needs access can access," Sudds said.
"Having said that, it's going to take a bit of time as we work with partners on the ground, and the capacity to be able to reach that.
"This is the incremental work that we are spearheading, along with our partners, to strive to get to that universality."
The funding comes from a national program the Trudeau government first announced in April ahead of this year's federal budget, though it had been building a broader policy since at least 2022 to guide such a program to ensure any food provided is nutritional, among other things.
In the budget, the Liberals earmarked $1 billion over the next five years, promising the money would feed an additional 400,000 students across Canada.
Newfoundland and Labrador was the first province to sign on, when it inked a deal in September to get $9.1 million over three years from the federal government to provide meals to another 4,100 kids. Manitoba then signed on in October, and is set to receive $17.2 million over the next three years to expand the province's existing food programs to another 19,080 kids.
Michael Parsa, Ontario's minister of children, community and social services, said in a statement to the Star that the province has a "long and proud history of supporting both student nutrition programs, and we are pleased to welcome the Government of Canada as a new partner in this important work. With this partnership, we can help give more children and youth the healthy start they need to learn, grow, and succeed."
Ontario's Education Minister Jill Dunlop noted that studies have shown that "students struggle to fully engage in learning when they're hungry. It's simple: when children eat well, they can concentrate on learning and developing important skills, like reading, writing and STEM disciplines." She called the agreement "a historic investment in the future of our children and youth and will support not only their academic success, but also their overall well-being."
Last year, the province spent almost $40 million on its student nutrition program, which provides free snacks, breakfasts and lunches to kids in thousands of schools across Ontario.
"I think it's so helpful for kids to have a little meal in them," Ontario Premier Doug Ford told reporters last month as negotiations with Ottawa were ongoing. "It makes them think better during the day."
But at the annual summer meeting of Canada's premiers in July, Ford and other leaders had stressed that they didn't want federal programs like school nutrition to overlap with ones that already exist in their provinces, calling that a duplication and a waste of taxpayer dollars. Ford argued it is best for federal funds to support provincial programs already in place.
And if not, "you know what happens?" Ford said, "the premiers get pissed. It's as simple as that. They just jump in there and think they know better than what we know, (what) we deal with on a daily basis."
Sudds said the deals ensure that new programs are in line with the federal policy on nutrition for school food, and the federal money is provided on condition that the provinces don't cut back on their own funding to feed schoolchildren.
In Ontario's case, Sudds said the new money could be used to provide breakfast, snacks or lunches, depending on what independent agencies decide is best.
"We don't profess to have that on-the-ground knowledge, but these lead agencies who have been doing the work over a number of years - many of whom are volunteers - certainly have that knowledge and the passion and the energy ... to be able to make those informed decisions about how best to support these kids," she said.
Sudds also said the $108.5 million for Ontario was allocated according to the number of students in the province, along with a "top-up" for students in northern and remote areas. After three years, the program will be reassessed with a planned new tranche of funding from the money Ottawa has budgeted.
With a federal election guaranteed within the next year, and a Liberal minority government that could fall sooner to a confidence vote, Sudds alleged the oppositions Conservatives - who are riding high in the polls - would cut the school food program.
Though Poilievre has never explicitly said he would do so, he has repeatedly attacked the program over how it hasn't yet started feeding kids. At times he has claimed "there is no food program," and at others alleged it is only fuelling the size of the government bureaucracy.
"(Trudeau's) multimillion dollar school food program has not served a single ham sandwich or a single little bowl of Kraft Dinner, not one meal to one child. It exists to feed bureaucracy in Ottawa, not kids in schools," Poilievre said Sept. 25 in the House of Commons.
Sudds said it's clear the program "is at jeopardy" if the Conservatives take power.
"It's a really scary thought to me," Sudds said.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau cuts fruit next to chef Jason Simpson at photo opportunity as they prepare food for a lunch program at the Boys and Girls Club East Scarborough, in Toronto, before an announcement to launch a National School Food Program on April 1, 2024.

Document 95 of 500

Liberals planning to give $250 to millions of Canadians: Trudeau also wants to offer GST tax break over the next two months

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-planning-give-250-millions-canadians/docview/3131758690/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+planning+to+give+%24250+to+millions+of+Canadians%3A+Trudeau+also+wants+to+offer+GST+tax+break+over+the+next+two+months&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal government is planning to give Canadians a tax break over the holidays and hand out billions of dollars in a move that two opposition leaders characterized as a trick to buy votes from frustrated people struggling with the high cost of living.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau made the announcement on Thursday, proposing to cut the federal goods and services tax on a slew of items over a two-month period.
"Our government can't set prices at the checkout, but we can put more money in people's pockets," Trudeau said at a news conference in Toronto alongside Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland.
"The working Canadians rebate of $250 which will be sent to people in April, is going to give people that relief they need, and the tax break over the next two months is going to help on the costs of everything as we approach the holidays, as we get into the new year."
The proposed GST break would begin Dec. 14 and end Feb. 15. The Liberals say it will apply to a number of items including children's clothing and shoes, toys, diapers, restaurant meals and beer and wine.
It also applies to Christmas trees - both natural and artificial - along with a variety of snack foods and beverages, and video game consoles.
Canadians who worked in 2023 and earned less than $150,000 would also receive a $250 cheque in the spring.
That means the money would go to about 18.7 million people, costing the government about $4.7 billion. The GST break is expected to cost another $1.6 billion.
The measures come as an inflation-driven affordability crunch has left voters unhappy with the Trudeau government. A federal election is slated to take place before next October, but could come sooner if the minority government falls before then.
The Conservatives have maintained a double-digit lead over the Liberals in public opinion polls for months, as Leader Pierre Poilievre promises to slash taxes and government spending to restore affordability.
High inflation has also put pressure on the Liberals to avoid introducing measures that would stimulate spending and fuel price growth.
However, the prime minister dismissed the idea that this move could raise inflation again, noting that price growth and interest rates are down.
"It allows us to make sure that we are putting money in people's pockets in a way that is not going to stimulate inflation, but is going to help them make ends meet and continue our economic growth," Trudeau said.
Poilievre blasted the NDP and Liberals alike over the announcement, saying his party had a better idea: ending the carbon price permanently.
"Today what we have is a two-month temporary tax trick that will not make up for the permanent quadrupling of the carbon tax on heat, housing, food and fuel," Poilievre said.
The Conservatives have pledged to scrap the federal fuel charge, which is applied on the purchases of 21 different fuels. Proceeds from the federal consumer carbon price are returned to Canadians and small businesses through rebates.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy accused the federal government of taking the idea from his playbook. Last month, the province announced it would send $200 cheques to all Ontario taxpayers and their children as part of its own suite of measures designed to ease the effects of the affordability crisis.
"We came up with the $200 rebate, but you know what the federal government can do is scrap the carbon tax," he said. "I mean, that is taking money out of people's pockets."
In order to get the measures passed through Parliament, the Liberals will need the support of an opposition party. The New Democrats say they are poised to be a willing partner..
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The Liberal government on Thursday announced a sweeping promise to make goods like groceries, children's clothing, Christmas trees and restaurant meals free from GST/HST between Dec. 14 and Feb. 15.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Nojoud Al Mallees The Canadian Press

Eligible businesses to get slice of WSIB's surplus: Premier says some refunds could be as much as $70,000

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/eligible-businesses-get-slice-wsibs-surplus/docview/3131758674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Eligible+businesses+to+get+slice+of+WSIB%27s+surplus%3A+Premier+says+some+refunds+could+be+as+much+as+%2470%2C000&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Businesses will get a financial boost - as well as a break on fees - after the Workplace Safety and Insurance Board posted a $2.5-billion surplus for its 2024 fiscal year.
"This includes delivering $2 billion in surplus funds back to Ontario businesses through the WSIB, which is a direct result of the agency's new approach to strong financial management," Premier Doug Ford announced Thursday during his speech at the Ontario Chamber of Commerce's annual economic summit.
"For a small business, like a bakery with fewer than five employees, this could mean a refund of up to $2,300," Ford said. "For a small construction company with 50 employees, the refund could climb to $46,000. And for a growing company with 100 employees, the refund could reach $70,000. That's money these businesses can reinvest back into their workers and into our economy, creating more jobs and opportunity."
The premier also announced that the average premium rate for businesses will drop to $1.25 from $1.30 per $100 of insurable payroll, saving them a total of $150 million a year, "lowering premium rates to the lowest level in half a century, all without reducing workers' benefits."
Since taking office in 2018, the government has reduced these rates by more than 50 per cent in total - saving businesses a total of $18.6 billion.
While businesses welcomed the move, New Democrat MPP Lise Vaugeois (Thunder Bay-Superior North), her party's WSIB critic, said the announcement does nothing for workers and called it "disgusting."
"Injured workers have been pushed into poverty over many, many years by this government" and previous ones, she said. "Are they putting any money into workers' pockets? Zero."
The latest news, she added, also does nothing to address "deeming," which can drastically reduce a worker's WSIB payments.
Deeming is when the WSIB determines an injured worker is able to do a job and then cuts their compensation based on those potential wages - even if they have not found employment.
"They just take it off - it's entirely arbitrary," Vaugeois said. "They deemed that that is a job you could have, so you are just being lazy by not getting it. It doesn't matter if your back is broken."
Among the other measures Ford announced included $400 million for workers' mental health, as well as injury prevention, care and recovery, and a $1,000 bonus for employers who have WSIB-approved health and safety action plans in place. The mental-health care is provided in partnership with hospitals including those in the Greater Toronto Area, Hamilton, Barrie and Ottawa.
The one-time rebate is available to employers who have had a clean record since 2020, and the government estimates more than 280,000 could be eligible. The funds are to be sent out next February.
In 2022, the province provided similar rebates totalling $1.5 billion.
"I'm proud that our strong financial position is allowing us to help businesses and contribute to the economic prosperity of Ontario while also investing $2.5 billion every year to help people recover and return to work by covering health-care costs and income replacement," said Jeff Lang, CEO of the WSIB, in a written statement.
The WSIB provides support and services to injured workers, as well as replacement income as they recover.
"The Ontario government is doing the right thing, putting more surplus funds back in the hands of businesses where they belong," said Julie Kwiecinski, the Canadian Federation of Independent Business's director of provincial affairs in Ontario.
The Ford government is also doing away with the $150 fee for apprentices taking their first qualification exam.
CAPTION: The province estimates more than 280,000 businesses could be eligible for the Workplace Safety and Insurance Board refund.

CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Toronto Star

City allocates $5.2M in federal encampment-response funds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-allocates-5-2m-federal-encampment-response/docview/3131758656/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+allocates+%245.2M+in+federal+encampment-response+funds&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton plans to put $5.2 million in federal encampment-response funding toward its effort to open more shelter spaces this winter.
Those dollars flow directly to the city without matching funds from the Ontario government, with the two senior levels of government unable to reach a deal.
That's "really unfortunate," Coun. Nrinder Nann said Wednesday, noting support from both governments would have "gone so much further" for Hamilton's encampment response while reducing the local tax impact.
The city plans to use the direct federal funding over two years to "reimburse" municipal spending on indoor and outdoor shelter spaces and allocate dollars to the Coalition of Hamilton Indigenous Leadership for its initiatives.
In September, council approved an $18.3-million plan to temporarily boost the shelter system with 192 indoor spaces and 40 micro shelters for up to 80 people with services on a city-owned property off Barton Street West.
The increase in shelter beds, some of which housing staff noted have opened up, is expected to increase capacity to 533 spaces as the city explores permanent increases to the system.
The federal Liberals announced the $250-million encampment-response initiative in their budget and worked to ink agreements with provinces to match dollars for municipalities but weren't able to firm one up with Ontario.
On Wednesday, council agreed to formally ask the provincial Tories to "forward their share" to Hamilton, nonetheless.
"I think we need to be clear with the provincial government and with residents of exactly who's come to the table and who hasn't," Coun. John-Paul Danko said.
A spokesperson for Ontario's Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra previously told The Spectator it's providing Hamilton with more than $28 million in annual homelessness-prevention funding.
City officials, however, point out the housing services division budget alone in 2024 is $89.2 million and forecast to rise to $103.4 million in 2025 as spending-plan talks get underway.
By another measure, the city expects 2024's spending on homelessness and housing across various divisions, and including in-year council approvals, to be $186 million, which is $39 million more than in 2023.
Of that, $41 million is from the provincial government and $20 million from Ottawa, with the city covering about $125 million, or 67 per cent.
Warming bus parked
The city won't put a nighttime warming bus back on the road this winter.
The decision comes after a review showed there wasn't enough room on the HRS bus or adequate services for riders looking to rest, eat and warm up.
"We didn't have enough space on the bus, first of all, for the people that wanted to use it," Michelle Baird, housing services director, told council.
Likewise, the city will discontinue extended hours at three recreation centres and the downtown library branch as part of its upcoming winter response.
Staff at those locations weren't necessarily able to meet the needs of those seeking respite from the cold, Baird said.
Instead, $1.2 million a year will be put toward making 45 existing, but temporary, shelter beds permanent at Salvation Army and Mission Services.
Mission Services will also operate another 25 evening drop-in spaces with on-site staff and services for people to rest, warm up and get food.
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: A man with his belongings in a cart boards the warming bus at Eastgate Square last winter. The city has decided to discontinue the service.Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 98 of 500

Too many people in jail under remand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/too-many-people-jail-under-remand/docview/3131758640/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Too+many+people+in+jail+under+remand&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's an uncomfortable statistic: of the almost 9,000 inmates incarcerated in Ontario's jails and penitentiaries last year, more than 80 per cent - about 7,200 people - were on remand, i.e., charged and awaiting trial. These individuals, none of whom have been found guilty, are housed in facilities across Ontario.
Many aren't there for violent crimes offences. The ranks of remand inmates - all of whom have been denied bail or are being held for bail violations - has surged in the past decade, despite recent Supreme Court rulings intended to restrict the deal-making between Crowns and judges that has resulted in overly restrictive bail conditions, which drive up custody levels.
A brief exchange late last month of rhetorical blows between the provincial Tories and the federal Liberals suggests bail could become a political football, again, with outcomes that may make the current situation worse.
The Ford government went first, calling on Ottawa to legislate mandatory minimum sentences and toughen up bail conditions for a wide range of crimes; mandate a three-strikes rule for accused who breach bail terms; and eliminate pre-trial time-served "credits" in sentencing. Federal Liberal Justice Minister Arif Varani shot back, saying those proposals ran afoul of Charter protections. These ideas, he said, underscore the Ontario government's failure to manage the bail system in ways that reduce backlogs instead of adding to the ranks of those serving pre-trial time.
Everyone involved in Ontario's criminal justice system knows the courts are clogged due to a judge shortage - a situation that has led appeals courts to throw out cases involving violent offences due to untenable delays.
The Ford government's gambit smells of politics, staking out a tough-on-crime stance and signalling to its federal counterparts that it would support a return to the hard-nosed criminal justice laws passed by Stephen Harper, such as mandatory minimum sentences. (Most were overturned by the Supreme Court.)
The Charter and the Criminal Code articulate the right to bail and set out a "ladder" approach that aims to match bail conditions to the severity of the allegations, as well as factors like public safety. Canadian law puts the onus on the Crown to demonstrate the need to attach conditions to a bail application. Yet Crowns and courts have taken to layering on ever more complex bail conditions, e.g., abstinence from drug use, that may be difficult or impossible for the accused to obey. The upshot: an escalating spiral of bail-arrest-detention that has filled prisons with people who were charged with a minor crime but are now behind bars for multiple bail violations.
Earlier this year, the Canadian Civil Liberties Association released a report concluding accused individuals continue to be released on bail with "extraneous or unnecessary conditions." To add salt to the wound, the CCLA also found the "constitutional right to a timely bail hearing" is being regularly violated due to backups in the court system.
The Supreme Court in 2017 issued a landmark ruling, R. vs. Antic, that reminded provincial courts and Crowns to obey the law and the Charter instead of indulging in the horse-trading that mars bail hearings. The federal Liberals responded with legislation designed to mitigate the negative impact of minor bail violations and thus slow or reverse the Catch-22 that entraps accused people who may have a lot of difficulty adhering to overly strict bail conditions but otherwise haven't caused any harm to a victim or property.
A 2022 study in the Canadian Journal of Law and Society found that since the Anticruling, bail courts have been more attentive to ensuring release conditions reflect the severity of the alleged crime. "(D)espite the Supreme Court's judgment that the 'least onerous conditions' be imposed," the authors added, "the impact of this high-level call to 'follow the law' is ... unclear."
Instead of investing in a solution to that problem and confronting the associated mess in the bail courts, Ford's Tories have opted instead to send $200 cheques, even as they advocate for criminal justice "reforms" that will drive up the public burden of locking up individuals who are, after all, presumed innocent.
John Lorinc is a Toronto journalist and editor.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: John Lorinc;

Subject: Bail; Violations; Crime; Trials

Title: Too many people in jail under remand

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.12

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 22, 2024

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 11899417

Source type: Newspaper

Ontario NDP critical of fundraiser

The Ontario Trucking Association has put out a "call to action" urging members to buy tickets and deliver a message to Sarkaria about cracking down on illicit operations.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles says cabinet ministers should not be fundraising off of people who have business in their ministry.
