Document 100 of 500

Ford keeps Wynne's fiscal legacy intact

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-keeps-wynnes-fiscal-legacy-intact/docview/3131758358/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+keeps+Wynne%27s+fiscal+legacy+intact&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When we were kids, we were wisely told not to judge a book by its cover.
Similarly, it's a mistake to assume you can predict how any government will behave in office based on its political party or campaign promises. In recent Canadian history, there are many examples of free-spending Conservative governments and many examples of fiscally disciplined and tax-cutting Liberal and NDP governments.
That said, the Ford government tabled its fall fiscal update Oct. 30, which some have branded a "mini-budget" because it contains several new initiatives. And once again, when it comes to fiscal policy, the Ford government has mostly continued the policies of its predecessor, the Liberal government led by Kathleen Wynne.
To be fair, Premier Doug Ford's mini-budget does contain one echo of Ontario's last long-serving Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris - namely, a $200 one-time "rebate" cheque to all Ontarians, which is something the Harris government did before its re-election campaign in 2000.
But wherever the era, the rebates are ill-advised - they don't change economic behaviour and boost economic growth the way permanent tax cuts can. Although it's important to note that the Harris rebate was part of a much more ambitious tax reduction and reform plan that spurred growth and left more "money in people's pockets" during a time when the province was balancing its budget after a long string of deficits.
Specifically, the Harris government significantly reduced personal income and business income tax rates, setting the stage for a period of prosperity in the province.
By contrast, the Ford government has broken its promises to meaningfully reduce taxes and instead has kept both personal income tax rates and business income tax rates at the same level Wynne left behind.
Instead of actually cutting taxes and improving the environment for economic growth and opportunity across all income levels, Ford is relying on his $200 one-time rebate to soften the blow of Ontario's high taxes.
On spending, Ford has also been much more Wynne-like than Harris-like. According to Wednesday's mini-budget, per-person spending (adjusted for inflation) is higher today than when Wynne left office. Harris, by contrast, sharply reduced spending.
Moreover, Harris's spending reductions allowed his government to quickly eliminate the deficit (left by his predecessor Bob Rae) and enact tax cuts - at a time, incidentally, when transfers from the federal government were shrinking.
Fast-forward to the Ford government. Despite maintaining spending at Wynne levels and governing during an era of increasing federal transfers, the Ford government still won't balance the books this year. The mini-budget pegs this year's deficit at $6.6 billion. And the Ford government has added a projected $106 billion in debt since taking office in 2018.
Ford acknowledged Harris's presence at a recent speech to the Empire Club of Canada in Toronto, and his $200 pre-election rebate is a political play drawn directly from the Harris playbook.
However, just as it's a mistake to judge a book by its cover, it's also unwise to judge a government's approach to policy by its political stripe or pre-election gimmicks.
In reality, Ford is not the heir to the Harris legacy of tax reform, balanced budgets and smaller government.
Ford's approach simply represents a continuation of the Wynne government's high-tax and deficit-fuelled approach to fiscal management that Ontarians have endured for years.
Ben Eisen is a senior fellow and Jake Fuss is director
of fiscal studies at the
Fraser Institute.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss;

Subject: Tax rates; Personal income; Budgets; Tax cuts; Economic growth; Business income; Tax reform; Politics; Income taxes

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Personal income Budgets Tax cuts Economic growth Business income Tax reform Income taxes

Document 101 of 500

Hamilton allocated $5.2 million in federal encampment-response funds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-allocated-5-2-million-federal-encampment/docview/3131918417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+allocated+%245.2+million+in+federal+encampment-response+funds&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton plans to put $5.2 million in federal encampment-response funding toward its effort to open more shelter spaces this winter.
Those dollars flow directly to the city without matching funds from the Ontario government with the two senior levels of government unable to reach a deal.
That's "really unfortunate," Coun. Nrinder Nann said Wednesday, noting support from both governments would have "gone so much further" for Hamilton's encampment response while reducing the local tax impact.
The city plans to use the direct federal funding over two years to "reimburse" municipal spending on indoor and outdoor shelter spaces and allocate dollars to the Coalition of Hamilton Indigenous Leadership for its initiatives.
In September, council approved an $18.3-million plan to temporarily boost the shelter system with 192 indoor spaces and 40 micro shelters for up to 80 people with services on a city-owned property off Barton Street West.
The increase in shelter beds, some of which housing staff noted have opened up, is expected to increase capacity to 533 spaces as the city explores permanent increases to the system.
Meanwhile, city officials have emphasized Hamilton is working with non-profits to build more affordable and supportive housing but noted the municipality needs more help from senior levels of government.
The federal Liberals announced the $250-million encampment-response initiative in their budget and worked to ink agreements with provinces to match dollars for municipalities but weren't able to firm one up with Ontario.
On Wednesday, council agreed to formally ask the provincial Tories to "forward their share" to Hamilton, nonetheless.
"I think we need to be clear with the provincial government and with residents of exactly who's come to the table and who hasn't," Coun. John-Paul Danko said.
A spokesperson for Ontario's Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra previously told The Spectator it's providing Hamilton with more than $28 million in annual homelessness-prevention funding.
City officials, however, point out the housing services division budget alone in 2024 is $89.2 million and forecast to rise to $103.4 million in 2025 as spending-plan talks get underway.
By another measure, the city expects 2024's spending on homelessness and housing across various divisions, and including in-year council approvals, to be $186 million, which is $39 million more than in 2023.
Of that, $41 million is from the provincial government and $20 million from Ottawa, with the city covering about $125 million, or 67 per cent.
Warming bus parked
The city won't put a nighttime warming bus back on the road this winter.
The decision comes after a review showed there wasn't enough room on the HRS bus or adequate services for riders looking to rest, eat and warm up.
"We didn't have enough space on the bus, first of all, for the people that wanted to use it," Michelle Baird, housing services director, told council.
The time it took the bus to run its route also left a considerable gap for those waiting to hop on at stops, Baird added.
Likewise, the city will discontinue extended hours at three recreation centres and the downtown library branch as part of its upcoming winter response.
Staff at those locations weren't necessarily able to meet the needs of those seeking respite from the cold, Baird said.
Instead, $1.2 million a year will be put toward making 45 existing, but temporary, shelter beds permanent at Salvation Army and Mission Services.
Mission Services will also operate another 25 evening drop-in spaces with on-site staff and services for people to rest, warm up and get food.
"We're hopeful that those investments are more meaningful investments and meet the needs, rather than these temporary solutions that we put in place," Baird said.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: This graphic shows what the City of Hamilton is spending on housing and homelessness.MicroShelters Inc. is supplying the city with 40 small steel cabins for a plan to create a temporary managed outdoor shelter site for homeless people off Barton Street West. The "micro shelters" are the same ones used in the United States project pictured.A man, with his belongings in a cart, boards the warming bus at Eastgate Square last winter. The city has decided to discontinue the service.City workers clean up garbage in the encampment at the Delta Parkette this past August.

Document 102 of 500

Ford government to ban lawsuits against province if cyclists are hurt or killed on streets where bike lanes were removed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-ban-lawsuits-against-province-if/docview/3131758929/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+ban+lawsuits+against+province+if+cyclists+are+hurt+or+killed+on+streets+where+bike+lanes+were+removed&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria has a message for cyclists: for your own safety, stay off Bloor, Yonge and University when bike lanes are removed.
While Sarkaria's proposed legislation, Bill 212, doesn't make it illegal to ride on those roads, a surprise amendment Thursday protects the government from lawsuits if cyclists are injured or killed on streets that used to have bike lanes.
Critics called that indemnification provision a troubling development that suggests Premier Doug Ford's government know its controversial changes will make roads more hazardous for riders.
"The safer thing for a cyclist to do would be to make a decision to go on streets that are safer," Sarkaria said after the Progressive Conservatives used their majority to pass the amendment in a legislative committee hearing.
"That's what we believe is the right way to do it, is on our secondary streets where there is much less traffic volume and ultimately safer for cyclists and everyone else."
New Democrat and Liberal MPPs on the committee condemned the change to the bill, which Sarkaria confirmed would give Ford's government power to remove all bike lanes on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue should it so decide.
"These new amendments turn a bad anti-bike bill into a terrible anti-bike bill," said Jessica Bell (University-Rosedale), who often cycles to and from the legislature.
The amendment states "claims arising from collisions between motor vehicles, cyclists or pedestrians ... are not claims of negligence."
Blocking lawsuits is "above-the-law stuff," said New Democrat MPP Jennifer French (Oshawa). "No one can bring anything against the Crown."
Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon (Beaches-East York) said cyclists have the right to be safe on city streets.
The government maintains bike lanes on primary routes are contributing to gridlock and last month introduced the legislation that allows the province to override municipal decisions on where bike lanes should go.
Municipalities will be required to get provincial approval for bike lanes if they take out a lane of traffic for cars and trucks, which critics fear could effectively be a ban on new lanes.
Opponents of the legislation repeatedly warned fewer bike lanes will lead to more deaths and injuries involving cyclists. Six riders have been killed in Toronto this year.
"It feels a lot safer as a car driver to know cyclists can zip past me in their lane and not come in front of my car," said French.
Ford said this week it's "a bunch of hogwash" that the City of Toronto's estimates it will cost $48 million to remove 22 km of bike lanes that cost $27 million to install.
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," said the premier.
Brian Rosborough, executive director of the Association of Municipalities, said gridlock in Toronto is "complex" and can't be solved by simply getting rid of bike lanes.
The legislation, for example, does not take aim at curb lane dining under the CaféTO program in warmer months or at lanes closed for construction of condominiums and other large buildings.
With an early election looming in the spring, Ford's Tories believe bike lanes will be a useful wedge issue against the NDP and Liberals that will help the PCs hold some of their dozen Toronto seats.
CAPTION: Hundreds of cyclists bike eastward along Bloor Street to show support for bike lanes on Nov. 9, 2024.

Document 103 of 500

Who is Pete Hoekstra, Donald Trump's pick for U.S. ambassador to Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-is-pete-hoekstra-donald-trumps-pick-u-s/docview/3131758915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+is+Pete+Hoekstra%2C+Donald+Trump%27s+pick+for+U.S.+ambassador+to+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President-elect Donald Trump has tapped a veteran politician from Michigan - Ontario's largest single trading partner - to be the next U.S. ambassador to Canada.
Pending congressional approval, Pete Hoekstra will succeed David Cohen, President Joe Biden's emissary to Ottawa, after Trump is sworn in on Jan. 20.
"Pete is well-respected in the Great State of Michigan - A state we won sizably," the U.S. president-elect wrote on social media.
"I overhauled the disastrous NAFTA Agreement, the worst Trade Deal in the History of the United States, which was switched to the USMCA (Mexico/Canada), which no one thought could be done," Trump's statement continued.
"We brought Trade with Mexico and Canada to a level playing field for our wonderful Farmers and Working Families," he said, signalling more trade tumult may loom.
"In my Second Term, Pete will help me once again put AMERICA FIRST. He did an outstanding job as United States Ambassador to the Netherlands during our first four years, and I am confident that he will continue to represent our Country well in this new role. Thank you, Pete!"
At Queen's Park, there was relief Trump had selected an ambassador from a border state that did more than $80 billion in two-way trade with Ontario in 2022 - eight per cent of the total trade between the Canada and the U.S.
Premier Doug Ford has worked closely with Michigan's Democratic Gov. Gretchen Whitmer to strengthen those ties, since the auto industry is hugely important to the economies of both the province and the state.
"There is no partnership more important to the economic success of Ontario than our close ties with the United States," Ford said Thursday at the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.
"As we head into the review of the USMCA, our government is doing everything we can to strengthen and grow our historic partnership. We're continuing to build relationships with our U.S. counterparts at all levels of government and all political stripes, driving home the message that we need to reject protectionism and promote a 'Buy Can-Am' approach," said the premier.
His comments came one day after he announced the Council of the Federation of provincial and territorial leaders is urging Ottawa to do a separate trade deal with the U.S., excluding Mexico for the transshipment of Chinese-made electric vehicles to evade American and Canadian tariffs.
In a congratulatory social media message to Hoekstra, Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to Washington, noted "Canada and the United States share the strongest bilateral relationship in the world, and I look forward to working with you to make it even stronger."
Bruce Heyman, who was Democratic president Barack Obama's ambassador to Canada, said Hoekstra brings some strengths to the job.
Heyman said he's an "experienced ambassador ... (who) obviously knows Trump well and has his trust (and is) experienced with Congress."
Obama's ambassador added Hoekstra is "clearly an insider and this is who Canada will need to get (the) straight scoop and real lay of the land and work with D.C." and "comes from a border state that will have direct knowledge and understanding of Canada (and Trump's) early naming shows importance of the relationship."
That means it "should be good news for the Canada-U.S. relationship as they will have someone to work with to navigate the changes that are coming."
The Dutch-born Hoekstra, 71, served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1993 to 2011 and was chair of the House intelligence committee.
As chair of the Michigan Republican Party, he was instrumental in Trump winning that key state over Democratic Vice-President Kamala Harris on Nov. 5.
The Detroit Free Press said Hoekstra professionalized a state party that was riven by factional infighting.
CAPTION: Michigan Republican Party chair Pete Hoekstra watches the results of the U.S. presidential election in Novi, Michigan on Nov. 5, 2024.

Document 104 of 500

Nov. 21: Bring the science centre here, leave the golf courses alone and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-21-bring-science-centre-here-leave-golf/docview/3131474314/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+21%3A+Bring+the+science+centre+here%2C+leave+the+golf+courses+alone+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bring the science centre here
Re: Workers say goodbye to an almost-empty Ontario Science Centre as repairs get underway, Nov. 14
The province is still looking for an interim location for the Ontario Science Centre until its new permanent location opens in 2028.
We have an almost-empty beautiful building at the Hamilton Waterfront which was once called The Marine Discovery Centre. It was a wonderful interactive marine museum. I used to take my grandchildren there until it, sadly, was closed down because of cuts to Parks Canada.
Since then, it has had various offices and a restaurant that came and went, but mostly it has been empty. I feel sad when I walk by and see that beautiful patio unused and the building seems to be almost empty.
Wouldn't it be wonderful for Hamilton if it became the temporary home of the Ontario Science Centre until the new one is built? It is a newer type building, in a fabulous location with space for displays and a restaurant and buses could shuttle children from Toronto. It is, after all, the Ontario Science Centre, not Toronto's Science Centre. I think it would be a win-win and good for Hamilton.
I know there are future plans for the building at the waterfront, but this would be only a temporary location for a science centre. It could be done if the will is there.
Janet Fraser, Mount Hope
Mohawk's leaders should share the pain
Re: Mohawk College set to axe hundreds of employees starting in early December, Nov. 20
Mohawk's president has announced upcoming layoffs of between eight per cent and 16 per cent of the college's staff. I wonder if senior management will reduce their own pay packets by that amount. Or, more appropriately, reduce the senior management staff complement by that amount. That would demonstrate real leadership, a trait that appears to be sadly lacking among today's public sector "leaders." But we live in hope.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Leave the golf courses alone
Re: Should Hamilton shrink its golf footprint for new parks? Nov. 14
Almost 60 years ago our city politicians of the day decided the future of our great city was a core shopping mall. They allowed the city centre from James to Bay, King to York to be demolished. The developers built a massive 390,000-square-foot retail mall. To ensure this mall would not be successful, city politicians allowed two four-lane thoroughfares in the core with timed traffic lights so people wouldn't even have to apply the brakes as they whizzed from one end of the city to the other. And they approved massive suburban shopping malls on the east, south and west boundaries of the city with ample free parking. Has our city suffered enough? No. Now we have developers holding on to our remaining heritage buildings waiting for them to self-demolish. A plan that is working. My point is, leave the golf courses alone. Let us for once not bow to the fads of the day. I propose we table the discussion for no less than 20 years and if it's still an idea, debate it in 2044.
Gary Ellis, Hamilton
Don't spend tax dollars on golf
Two letter writers expressed their support for maintaining the Chedoke golf course. One stated that the courses have always been "revenue positive" (which is the only way revenue can be in a functioning business; it doesn't mean they are profitable), while the other cited the fiscal irresponsibility of transforming the courses into other uses. However, neither writer cites the $17 million in capital repairs the three city courses require and the other unknown costs under the ground. I'm sure those letter writers are sincere in their concern for the pocketbooks of the hard-working taxpayers of Hamilton, however, they seem to be willing to make a massive exception for golf at Chedoke, even though they can't possibly know the real costs of maintaining the courses. I don't want any of my tax dollars being spent on golf. If the letter writers want to spend their discretionary dollars on golf, there is a whole industry out there to take their money.
Peter Mountford, Hamilton
Postal services are essential
Re: A 'lot of ground' remains between Canada Post, workers as strike talks progress, Nov. 19
I firmly believe the postal workers should be classified as essential services and not be allowed to strike. There are many seniors, disabled people and others who do not have direct deposits for their cheques so they will not receive their monthly income. What happens to them? Also, for those that receive their utility bills via paper, that, too, becomes an issue. And then we have those who do not have computers; how do they find out the cost of the utilities and make online payments? Government service workers should not be allowed to strike. It's no different than police, fire, ambulance workers and more. Pay increases should be based on the cost of living each year. Let's get with the program, people, and not make everyone suffer with frustration and anger.
Teresa Misale, Hamilton
Argos have earned my respect
Re: Is it time to ditch the 'Argos suck' chant? Toronto has hoisted five Grey Cups since Ticats last won one, Nov. 18
For a Tiger-Cat fan to praise the Argos is a very hard pill to swallow. However, after just winning their 19th Grey Cup, and their eighth cup in eight tries, it is impossible to ignore the winningest franchise in CFL history. All Hamilton teams combined, Tigers, Ticats, Wildcats and Alerts, have won 15 Grey Cups. I am disappointed with some in the Toronto sports media who barely mentioned this terrific win, after the Argos were a nine-point underdog. You would think the winningest sports team in Toronto's history would be celebrated by the Toronto media, but on a major sports station today, they stated that we could talk about the Argos Grey Cup victory, but instead they chose to discuss the Florida Gators and the Leafs and the Raptors. I congratulate the Toronto ownership, GM Pinball Clemons and the coach Ryan Dinwiddie for overcoming the odds. There is something to be learned from organizations that win championships on a consistent basis.
David Gallo, Ancaster
Not giving up the chant
Scott Radley wants to ditch the chant of "Argos suck" because they've won five Grey cups since Hamilton won their last in 1999. Don't forget that the Tiger-Cats were undefeated against the Argos this year. Argos will always suck in my opinion.
Mark Wingrove, Hamilton
Sammy will be missed
Re: Sammy Jo's founder Sammy Vrantsis one of first restaurateurs to ban smoking in Hamilton area, Nov. 18
I was sorry to hear of Sammy's passing. My husband and I have wonderful memories of meals there, always greeted by a smiling Sammy. His breakfasts and Greek salads were the best anywhere. RIP Sammy, you will be missed.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Wondering about LRT's arrival
The Sunday Star last weekend noted Toronto's Eglinton Crosstown LRT is entering its 14th year of construction. Columnist Shawn Micallef compared this with other projects, such as the Paris to London Chunnel, which took six years to complete.
It will be interesting to see what approach Hamilton takes.
The construction of the city's main stadium and the first pool to hold an indoor international swim competition - now the Jimmy Thompson Pool - took less than two years, from Burlington's Bobby Robinson's 1928 conception to the opening of the first British Empire (now Commonwealth), Games in 1930. And I'm pretty sure the track for the original Hamilton Street Railway was laid in a matter of months.
Deadlines help get things done.
David Collier, Hamilton
It's Ukraine that should be furious
Just so I have this straight; Vladimir Putin is furious that Joe Biden (and now likely Britain and France) has allowed Ukraine to strike military targets inside Russia using western-supplied ordnance, particularly those launch sites from which Ukraine is relentlessly attacked. Putin has no problem importing Iranian drones, munitions from China, North Korea and likely others and is happy to have 12,000-plus North Korean troops fight alongside his soldiers against the undermanned Ukrainian military.
The fury should be Ukraine's for having to wait this long for "permission" to fight back in kind. The West made the mistake of not challenging Putin 10 years ago when he invaded Crimea. By many accounts, the Kremlin is on thin ice in this war. Estimates of north of 700,000 dead Russian soldiers and how many injured? Mothers, fathers, brothers and sisters won't stand for this forever. Putin has no right to his fury.
Chris Carroll, Hamilton
A time and place for bikes
Re: Councillors want Doug Ford out of city's bike lanes, Nov. 17
I have nothing against cyclists and I own and ride a bicycle. Bicycles certainly are a great mode of transportation in the right environment.
But councillors forget bike lanes are seasonal and most businesses are not. One of the most bizarre statements I have ever heard came from Ancaster's Coun. Craig Cassar: "Cars don't support businesses. People do." Yes they do and 98 per cent of them drive to that business.
I don't ride my bike when I go to Theatre Aquarius. I don't ride my bike when I go to Rapscallion. I don't ride my bike when I have a lunch meeting with my banker in the city.
There was a time where the city of Hamilton had superior traffic flow. With the one-way streets and co-ordinated traffic lights. It was a busy, thriving city. That is certainly not the case now. Take a good look at the city core.
Then to foolishly challenge the province when you are trying to put in an LRT and need their support? I predict there will be no businesses to go to by car, by bike or by foot. Only time will tell.
Bruce Eccles, Hamilton
CAPTION: Turning the former Discovery Centre on the waterfront into a temporary science centre would be wonderful for Hamilton, a reader says.

Document 105 of 500

Doug Ford and Olivia Chow have the means to end encampments. One thing is stopping them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-olivia-chow-have-means-end-encampments/docview/3131474269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+and+Olivia+Chow+have+the+means+to+end+encampments.+One+thing+is+stopping+them&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A chill wind is blowing across Ontario.
As winter looms, homeless people are hunkering down in tent encampments. With the ground hardening under them in public parks, rival political camps are digging in for a fight.
So politicized is this issue that we cannot even agree on how to describe the people or decide on a policy. Activists insist that people in need are not the homeless but the unhoused, or "people experiencing homelessness" - a phoney war preoccupied with terminology over policy.
Now the ground is shifting.
On one side, a new alliance has formed between Doug Ford and a group of exasperated mayors across the province demanding action and eviction - with the premier leading the way. At Ford's behest, almost on a bluff, a dozen municipal leaders signed a letter urging him to clear out encampments by any means necessary.
If that necessitates suspending Charter rights and overruling the courts - which have blocked previous removals - these mayors are onside with the premier. But not all of them.
On the other side is Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who won power last year on a promise to tackle the housing problem. Eighteen months later, she has invested more political and fiscal capital in long-term affordability than in short-term accessibility.
The problem with her strategy is that in the long run we are all dead, as any economist will tell you. In the short run, homeless people need help, as any humanitarian will remind you.
Future affordable housing won't help people who can't afford their next meal, let alone their next rent cheque. Today's problems can't wait.
Yet the city now admits that hundreds if not thousands of homeless people will be frozen out, night after night, because of a shortage of shelter space this winter. Meanwhile, the courts have ruled that police cannot evict homeless people from public parks as long as they have nowhere else to go.
There's a certain logic to the verdict - why evict people from encampments if they will simply pop up at another park? But that lets everyone off the hook while politicians point accusing fingers.
As long as municipal politicians underfund emergency shelters and bylaws go unenforced, people will camp out in public parks. If you hate shelters, with all the rules and hassles, you can live on your own, at your own risk, in propane-heated tents that are fire hazards and death traps for others.
Which is precisely what's happening in Toronto and many other cities.
Encampments inevitably encroach on public parks that were designed for public use. If you believe in urban planning and, say, public transit, do you simply stop believing when people sleep in subways and streetcars or tents move in?
This occupation - or privatization - of public space is not only unhealthy and unsustainable for the most vulnerable in our cities, it also makes our parks unusable and our transit unrideable for everyone else.
All that said, few people are talking seriously about homelessness today. A battle between Ford and Chow over bike lanes, which seems like a side issue, gets all the bandwidth.
By comparison to the housing problem, bike lanes are a wedge issue wrapped in a rounding error. Homelessness, on the other hand, is an endlessly human story, a morality tale about political failure.
Every winter, as most cyclists (like me) retreat to the warmth of public transit, homeless people brave the cold and snow. Their allies rally to the cause of encampments, defending their right to freeze to death while the fight for emergency shelters is deferred to another day, another winter, or a long-term build.
The problem is not so much a lack of fiscal wealth but political will at every level of government.
Cities could easily divert funding from lesser priorities to meet the modest needs of emergency shelters, even if that means erecting pop-up tent cities in protected and secure areas of every municipality.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government not only has the money to rip out bike lanes, it keeps doling out billions of dollars to taxpayers as pre-election giveaways - $200 ahead of a possible 2025 election, and hundreds of dollars in gas tax and licence plate rebates before the 2022 vote. The premier could easily redirect some of the $7 billion he spends every year on hidden hydro subsidies - dreamed up by the last Liberal government on the dubious grounds that ratepayers had to choose between "heating and eating."
And the federal government should surely pay its fair share for the large number of refugees who are crowding Toronto's shelters.
The problem is that many of the same people who complain about encampments in neighbouring parks wouldn't countenance an emergency shelter in their neighbourhood. In NDP strongholds across the city, I've seen earnest lawn signs on pricey homes proclaiming, "I support my neighbours in tents" - only to see them quietly removed when encampments encroached.
When it comes to tents, good intent only goes so far. What you call them - the unhoused or homeless people - matters less than what you do for them.
It's not the money, it's the morality.
CAPTION: A tent encampment is seen in St. James Park in the King and Jarvis area on Nov. 12, 2024.

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Watch+now+to+see+if+Ontario+Premier+Doug+Ford+responds+to+Prime+Minister+Justin+Trudeau%27s+plan+to+give+Canadians+a+break+on+the+GST&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Premiers team up pushing bilateral trade deal with U.S.: Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-team-up-pushing-bilateral-trade-deal/docview/3131100719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+team+up+pushing+bilateral+trade+deal+with+U.S.%3A+Ford&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All 13 provincial and territorial premiers are aligned on a push for the federal government to negotiate a bilateral trade deal with the United States, Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Wednesday.
Ford, who is the current chair of the Council of the Federation, the group of Canada's 13 premiers, said they had a call and there is a clear consensus that the country needs separate agreements with the U.S. and Mexico.
"All the premiers, we know Mexico is bringing in cheap Chinese parts, slapping made-in-Mexico stickers on, shipping it up through the U.S. and Canada, causing American jobs to be lost, and Canadian jobs," he said after the call wrapped up. "We want fair trade."
Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland has said members of the outgoing administration of President Joe Biden and advisers of incoming president-elect Donald Trump have expressed "very grave" concerns to her about the issue of Mexico becoming a "back door" to Chinese goods.
Freeland has sought to reassure nervous Canadians that the country is in a good position with the incoming Trump administration, even as it threatens new tariffs, because Ottawa is moving in lock-step with the U.S. on Chinese trade irritants.
The Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement is up for review in 2026.
The premiers are calling for a meeting with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his officials to discuss the idea of bilateral negotiations.
The Canadian Press

Document 108 of 500

Canada has no reason to gloat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-has-no-reason-gloat/docview/3131100649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+has+no+reason+to+gloat&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After the U.S. election it's clear that women need no longer apply to be president. Two well-qualified female candidates, Hillary Clinton and Kamala Harris, ran against the worst man ever. They both lost.
The Democrats didn't see it coming this time. But the most unmanly of men gathered his bros and ran a hypermasculine campaign designed to pull in the male vote. Young men, old men and plenty of white women voted for Donald Trump. Evangelicals and those who don't give a damn about religion voted for him too. Latino and Black men increased their share of his vote. In total about half of U.S. voters decided that a convicted felon, sexual assaulter, an insurrectionist, and a liar was a better choice than either of those two women.
Since what happens in U.S. politics soon arrives in Canada, what does this mean for our next election? What does it mean for women in politics? Will we backslide just like the U.S?
Not that we have made great gains. Kim Campbell was our one and only one female prime minister and she was in office for only six months, when she was elected leader of the Progressive Conservatives in 1993. The PCs at the time were barely breathing - always a good time to elect a woman leader. She went down to ignominious defeat - only two PCs were elected in total - at the hands of Jean Chrétien and resigned shortly after.
We've done somewhat better with female premiers but not much. Since 1992 there have been 11 female premiers. Rita Johnson of British Columbia was the first, who managed to stay in office for six months before being turfed by Gordon Campbell. Christy Clark, another west coaster, held on for the longest. She was in office for six years and then resigned in 2017 shortly after an election due to a vote of non-confidence. Kathleen Wynne of Ontario held office for five years and then went down to defeat in 2018 to Doug Ford in the biggest loss for the Liberals since Confederation.
There were also four women premiers of the territories, among them Nellie Cournoyer of the Northwest Territories.
Currently there are two female premiers: Danielle Smith in Alberta and the recently elected Susan Holt in New Brunswick.
But so far none of these premiers have been in office for more than one term. Their predecessors were all defeated by male candidates, most of whom stayed in office much longer.
Unless Justin Trudeau has a change of heart it will be an all-male slate for the next federal election, too - except for Elizabeth May of the Green Party who must be getting tired of running by now.
It would seem that here in Canada we have gotten used to things the way they are. Men run politics and women stay in the background. Trudeau has certainly elevated many women to cabinet posts and important civil service and staff positions. But he can't seem to get out of the way so one of them could run for the leadership of the Liberals.
It is said that women don't necessarily vote for other women; they are more driven by policy or something more intangible such as values or appeal.
In the case of Trump it seems most Americans were swayed by his ugly words about immigrants and illegal migrants coming across the southern border.
Given our current situation - three male federal political leaders - it looks like it will be a long time before we have a female prime minister.
On that front, Canada is significantly behind its peers. England has already had three. So has Finland. Germany had the second longest-serving chancellor in German history in Angela Merkel.
Meanwhile, the United States has had zero women presidents, Canada is barely better. It speaks volumes. America should feel ashamed for electing Trump, but Canada has more than enough reason for its own shame.
Gillian Steward is a Calgary-based
writer and freelance contributing
columnist for Torstar. Follow her
on Bluesky at gillian2.blsk.social.
CAPTION:

Document 109 of 500

Walmart Canada, Amazon, Costco, Sobeys and more grocers inform customers of carrot recall linked to deadly E.coli outbreak in the U.S., products pulled from shelves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/walmart-canada-amazon-costco-sobeys-more-grocers/docview/3131563448/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Walmart+Canada%2C+Amazon%2C+Costco%2C+Sobeys+and+more+grocers+inform+customers+of+carrot+recall+linked+to+deadly+E.coli+outbreak+in+the+U.S.%2C+products+pulled+from+shelves&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Various brands of organic baby carrots, linked to a deadly E. coli outbreak in the U.S., have been recalled in the U.S. and Canada.
If you've got some baby carrots in your fridge to nibble on, you might want to check out the packaging to see if it's part of a new recall by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA).
The CFIA has issued a recall for various brands of organic carrots due to concerns with possible E. coli contamination.
Brands included in the recall are Cal-Organic, PC Organics, Compliments Organic and Bunny-Luv. The recalling firm is Grimmway Farms.
"Grimmway Farms has issued a recall of select organic whole carrots and organic baby carrots that should no longer be in grocery stores but may be in consumers' refrigerators or freezers," according to a statement on Grimmway Farms' website.
Grimmway Farms says the products may have been contaminated with Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli (E. coli) O121:H19.
E. coli O121:H19 is a bacterium that can cause serious and sometimes fatal infections in young children, elderly people and those with a weakened immune system.
"Some infections can cause severe bloody diarrhea conditions, such as a hemolytic uremic syndrome, or the development of high blood pressure, chronic kidney disease, and neurologic problems," the company wrote in a post on their website. "Symptoms include severe stomach cramps, diarrhea, fever, nausea, and/or vomiting. The incubation period for E. coli O121:H19 in humans can range from 24 hours to as much as ten days. The average incubation period is 3 to 4 days."
Grimmway Farms said recalled products were shipped directly to retail distribution centers nationwide in the United States, Puerto Rico and Canada.
"Do not consume, use, sell, serve or distribute recalled products," the CFIA said in the recall notice dated Nov. 18.
Walmart Canada and Sobeys have confirmed the product had been for sale in their stories.
"At Walmart Canada, food safety is a top priority," said Walmart Canada in a statement. "I can confirm this product was offered for sale by Walmart Canada in-store and online. Given the recall, it is being removed from sale."
A Sobeys spokesperson confirmed the impacted products are no longer in their supply chain or in any of their stores.
"Food safety is our number one priority, and we follow all guidance issued by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency regarding investigations and recalls," said read the statement from Sobeys.
Amazon posted a recall notice for the impacted carrots sold on their platform. Costco also posted a recall notice for customers on their website.
The concern is potential exposure to the E. coli 0121 bacteria, which may not look or smell spoiled, but can still make you sick.
"This recall was triggered by a recall in another country. There have been no reported illnesses associated with the consumption of these products in Canada."
According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 18 states in the United States have reported illness believed to be caused by the carrots, sold by Grimmway Farms in California.
The recall was made Nov. 16 after 39 cases of illness were reported, and 15 people hospitalized. One person has also died in connection with the E. coli 0121 infection, according to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
The carrots include organic juice carrots, fresh carrots, organic baby-cut carrots, baby-cut carrots, organic petite carrots, and carrots sold with the brands Bunny-Luv, PC Organics, Compliments Organic and Cal-Organic.
The recall includes both single and double packages, ranging in size from 340 grams, 454 grams, 907 grams, 2.27 kilograms, 11.34 kilograms to 50 pounds.
The Cal-Organic brands have best before dates from Sept. 11 to Nov. 2, and Aug. 14 to Oct. 23.
The Bunny-Luv brands had best before dates from Aug. 14 to Oct. 23.
Compliments Organic brands had best before dates ranging from Aug. 14 or Sept. 14 to Oct. 23 and 24.
The PC Organics brands had best before dates from Aug. 14 to Oct. 23, and Sept. 11 to Nov. 2.
These products should no longer be on store shelves, but if you still have these carrot packages at your home, throw them out or return to the location where they were purchased, the Canadian Food Inspection Agency said.
E. coli symptoms can include nausea, vomiting, mild to severe abdominal cramps and watery to bloody diarrhea.
In severe cases, some may have seizures or strokes, need blood transfusions and kidney dialysis or live with permanent kidney damage. Death may also occur in extreme cases.
Here's the list of products with UPC codes:

To learn more about the recall, visit recalls-rappels.canada.ca. Consumers with questions or products in their possession may contact Grimmway Farms' customer services desk at 1-800-301-3101.

Accusing Doug Ford of 'meddling,' NDP wants former Toronto mayor David Miller to draft a new deal for municipalities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/accusing-doug-ford-meddling-ndp-wants-former/docview/3131563346/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Accusing+Doug+Ford+of+%27meddling%2C%27+NDP+wants+former+Toronto+mayor+David+Miller+to+draft+a+new+deal+for+municipalities&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles has turned to former Toronto mayor David Miller for help in drafting a "new deal" for Ontario municipalities.
With an early election possible next year, Stiles said "meddling" by Premier Doug Ford's government - now planning to remove some Toronto bike lanes - in addition to a lack of provincial funding for transit, shelters and affordable housing makes an improved relationship crucial.
"We need the province to actually be a partner," she told a news conference with Miller on Wednesday, noting municipalities are having to raise property taxes because of rising costs, such as dealing with tent encampments.
Miller was mayor from 2003 to 2010, when he was succeeded by the premier's late brother, Rob Ford.
He told reporters he will use his experience in provincial-municipal relations to consult with local leaders and hold open consultations on a new way forward that restores "mutual respect" between the two levels of government.
"The relationship has to be fixed," he added, citing consistent long-term funding as a key component of recommendations he will make to Stiles.
"If and when they become the government, they will implement the right kind of change," Miller said of the New Democrats.
The premier, whose Progressive Conservatives enjoy a strong lead over the NDP and Liberals in public opinion polls, has been hinting at an early election next year instead of waiting until the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
In the latest Abacus Data poll for the Star, Ford's PCs were at 42 per cent support, ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, the NDP at 22 per cent and the Greens under Mike Schreiner at seven per cent.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy pushed back at NDP attacks over municipal funding this week, saying the province has increased it by 45 per cent in the last five years in addition to more money for infrastructure such as water and sewer lines to lay the groundwork for more housing.
CAPTION: Former Toronto mayor David Miller in Vancouver's Gastown neighbourhood in 2017.

Document 111 of 500

Canada's premiers want Justin Trudeau to do a trade deal with the U.S. that excludes Mexico, Doug Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-premiers-want-justin-trudeau-do-trade/docview/3131563342/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+premiers+want+Justin+Trudeau+to+do+a+trade+deal+with+the+U.S.+that+excludes+Mexico%2C+Doug+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's provincial and territorial leaders want Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to strike a separate trade deal with the United States, excluding Mexico, says Premier Doug Ford.
Ford, chair of the Council of Federation of premiers, said "there's a clear consensus that everyone agrees that we need a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. and a separate bilateral trade deal with Mexico."
With the spectre of a more protectionist American administration under President-elect Donald Trump, the Ontario premier said Wednesday it is key that Canada insulates itself from any trade tariffs by forging closer ties to the U.S.
"That's our number one trading partner, our closest ally, our closest friend," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park after a conference call with the other premiers.
"What our problem is - all the premiers - we know Mexico is bringing in cheap Chinese parts, slapping 'made in Mexico' stickers on, shipping (them) up through the U.S. and Canada, causing American jobs to be lost and Canadian jobs," he said.
The transshipment of artificially inexpensive Chinese electric vehicle parts, subsidized by Beijing, through Mexico is a sore point in Ottawa and Washington.
An expert with Canada's chamber of commerce cautioned that tossing Mexico out of the deal before talks even begin would be rash.
"We very much think that we should be working with all three countries and that we're better together. CUSMA has been good for Canadians. It's been good for Canadian businesses," said Catherine Fortin LeFaivre, the chamber's vice-president of strategic policy and global partnerships.
Ford said the premiers will meet in person in Toronto on Dec. 15-16 to further discuss the issue, which he has also raised on the phone with Trudeau.
A Team Canada trade mission to Washington is planned for February or March after Trump's inauguration.
"I had a call with the prime minister right after I sent my statement out (last week on concerns about Mexico being a back door to EVs in North America)," said the premier.
"He's very supportive and I had an opportunity to meet (Industry) Minister (François-Philippe) Champagne as well. And we'll reach out to Chrystia, I'm sorry, Minister Freeland and the rest of the team," he said, referring to Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland.
"So we have a good relationship all of us. That's the most important thing with all the premiers, we put all political stripes aside. We're speaking as Team Canada, and we feel the same way with the federal government. We're going to be there and take that Team Canada approach."
Ford noted Ontario does two-way trade with the U.S. worth $493 billion annually compared with $40 billion of trade with Mexico.
"I just want them to play fair," he said of the Mexican government.
"Right now, Mexico is not playing fair. They're not playing nice in the sandbox, per se, when they're importing Chinese-made products at a much lower cost, costing U.S. and Canadian jobs. That's not fair trade."
Led by Manitoba's Wab Kinew, the premiers also want Ottawa to hit its target of two per cent of gross domestic product spending on defence in order for Canada to meet its NATO commitments.
"I want to thank ... Premier Kinew for his idea and that came at our last meeting," said Ford.
Trump has often railed against other NATO allies for not spending enough on defence and instead relying on the American military to keep them safe.
Freeland said she has heard concerns about Mexico becoming a backdoor for Chinese products and she highlighted that Canada is the only country in the world to fully match the steel, aluminum and EV tariffs the U.S. has in place.
"I think those are legitimate concerns for our American partners and neighbours to have. Those are concerns that I share."
Trade Minister Mary Ng said China is producing an excess supply of EVs in particular and Mexico cannot be a conduit for dumping those vehicles on the North American market.
"We cannot have a back door for excess capacity into North America. We're concerned about it. We share those concerns," she said ahead of the Liberal caucus meeting Wednesday.
Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, who is currently on the campaign trail, said in a statement that he plans to focus on the U.S. trade relationship.
"The most important thing for Canadians is that our trade is protected. We have strong family and business ties to the U.S.," he said. "I will work with other premiers and the federal government to ensure that jobs and businesses in Nova Scotia that rely on U.S. trade are protected."
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has also called for a bilateral deal with the U.S.
In a statement to the Star, newly elected New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt said it was important to have a strong relationship with both countries.
"It's important to our province and economy to have strong trade agreements and create mutually beneficial agreements with both the U.S. and Mexico," she said. "We're focused on what's in our province's and New Brunswickers' best interest."
Canada and Mexico's trading relationship has been growing since the passage of the Canada-United States-Mexico agreement (CUSMA). In 2023, Canada shipped $8.3 billion worth of goods to Mexico, while Mexico shipped more than $46 billion in goods to Canada.
Fortin LeFaivre from the Canadian Chamber of Commerce said it is a good time for Canada to focus on the trade irritants it has with the U.S. and also look for ways to solve those issues. She said Mexico should look at its rules for Chinese imports, but the agreement can still hold.
"The situation geopolitically has changed very much since the initial CUSMA negotiations. And factors such as China are major and we should be aligned," she said. "Mexico needs to have a position that is stronger and more aligned with the position that's been taken by the U.S. and Canada."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford attend an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Trade agreements; Prime ministers

Nov. 20: Tighten rules around police shootings, don't bow to bullies and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-20-tighten-rules-around-police-shootings-dont/docview/3130783625/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+20%3A+Tighten+rules+around+police+shootings%2C+don%27t+bow+to+bullies+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tighten rules around police shootings
Re: Father of three identified as man killed in Hamilton police shooting, Nov. 11
The family and friends of Erixon Kabera are right to demand and (hopefully) receive answers from the province's Special Investigations Unit about why their loved one was shot dead by Hamilton police. And it's nice for the rest of us to think they will get those answers. Nice, but naive.
That's because the SIU has no power to compel the "subject officers" - those directly involved in the death - to sit for an interview, provide a statement or turn over their notes. And even if they do sit for an interview, there doesn't appear to be any wording in the legislation to indicate the statements made by the officers are under oath.
So in a case like this one, where there doesn't appear to be any nonpolice witnesses and no video footage (and, of course, no bodycam footage), what hope should any of us have that the SIU will get at the truth of what happened?
Unless an officer is charged criminally in this case, the only way the family of Mr. Kabera might ever get police to provide information under oath about the shooting is if they one day launch a civil lawsuit and the case eventually makes it to trial.
Carrying a gun to do your job comes with great responsibility. That responsibility should also include the requirement to answer questions truthfully about how that gun used in the line of duty led to a fatality.
Steve Buist, Burlington
Don't give them the attention
Re: A strong, unified voice against hate is needed in Hamilton, Nov. 19
I usually agree with Margaret Shkimba but I think a strong social media response to the morons who raised the racist banner at Jackson Square just gives them the publicity they want.
I'd like to see all our public figures join the exodus from X anyway, given it has degenerated into an extreme right-wing, conspiracy-theory-propagating cess pit under Elon Musk's management. Threads is a perfectly good and well run alternative.
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Not easy to fight auto theft
Re: The garage is for your car, not junk, Nov. 18
I agree with the letter writer and insurers actually ask if you park your vehicle in the garage. However, if you drive a high-end car and park it in the garage, the thieves will break into your home and demand the keys. Hard to win. Car theft is such a big, organized business.
Pat Stevens, Grimsby
Don't bow down to bullies
Re: Councillors want Doug Ford out of city's bike lanes, Nov. 16
In the final run-up to the U.S. election, much was made of the decision by many prominent newspapers not to endorse a presidential candidate. The Los Angeles Times and The Washington Post released contorted explanations for why they were choosing not to take a political stand. It was a move widely regarded as an example of what Timothy Snyder calls "anticipatory obedience," something that spineless, cowardly people and organizations do when confronted with a bully or tyrant.
I was shocked to see Coun. Brad Clark suggest that Hamilton perform its own anticipatory obedience by not "stepping on Superman's cape" regarding the bike lane debate, assuming "Superman" is Premier Doug Ford. Didn't Hamilton come up with a plan that was the product of extensive consultation and conscientious planning? Why would Hamilton offer Premier Ford its anticipatory obedience for not only the bike lanes issue but any other issue affecting the city and its operation? Hamilton is proud of being a scrappy town. If Premier Ford wants to scrap the bike lanes, or anything else the city has proposed, Hamilton should respond by telling him, "Any time, anywhere if you meddle in city business."
Peter Mountford, Hamilton
Bike lanes are for the few
This city has gone overboard with bike lanes. Upper Paradise onto Mohawk Road, there are no right turns because of bike-lane barriers which has increased cut-though traffic on Sandringham and Buckingham drives. Herkimer and Charlton from James to Queen is one lane due to parking and bike lanes. The traffic is frequently backed up from Bay to James due to the restriction. We are a city of mostly seniors and we are not going to start riding bikes, no matter how many lanes there are. The fact is the city is spending money to please the few is not acceptable.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Keep stories of survival alive
Re: The Nazis murdered her family but they couldn't take the love in her heart, Nov. 16
I was moved to tears by Jon Wells' poignant tribute to Vera Barany, a 94-year-old Auschwitz-Birkenau survivor, with whom I share a common heritage.
My mother was a Jewish-Hungarian, who, unbeknownst to me, was taken to Bergen-Belsen at the age of 14. As she was very private, I did not learn of this until after her passing in 2018.
Due to the ongoing Israel-Hamas war, bomb threats and gunfire targeting Jewish institutions have increased worldwide as well as in our local communities. As a longtime Hamiltonian, I have recently heard antisemitic remarks from certain groups, including former MPPs and school board trustees.
Kudos to The Spec for recounting the survival stories of Jewish Hamiltonians. It is only by listening to these first-hand accounts for as long as we are able, and educating our youth about the horrors of the Holocaust, that the mistakes of the past can be prevented from being repeated in the future.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Construct housing in place of derelict buildings
The entire downtown core is looking ready to collapse! It is most important, if at all possible, to preserve and protect historical buildings. They are a link to how our city grew and expanded over the years. Unfortunately, such preservation takes planning, co-operation among many groups, and money. The present state of Hamilton's downtown is unfortunately showing many signs of blight and boarded up buildings. We see new condominium towers rising over a very tired Hamilton downtown core. This is a most positive sign, however, with that we see homelessness ramped up in our downtown.
Hamilton once had a very beautiful downtown. Gore Park had beautiful trees and statues that linked this city to our past development. Both largely gone now, unfortunately.
We should focus first on the homelessness in our downtown core. Get those people off the streets. Next, if buildings are beyond help, they need to come down. In their place, affordable housing should be built. Once our downtown core starts looking more presentable, we should encourage positive development and expedite construction with full co-operation from city hall with minimal red tape. This is one way to bring people back to downtown Hamilton to shop and stroll.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
CAPTION: Attendees march from city hall to the central police station for a vigil for Erixon Kabera who was shot dead by police. Officers involved in a shooting should be required to answer any and all questions about the incident, a reader says.

Subject: Nazi era; Police shootings; Criminal investigations; Deadly force

Title: Nov. 20: Tighten rules around police shootings, don't bow to bullies and other letters to the editor

Document 113 of 500

Justin Trudeau's digital tax is hated by America. Here's why Donald Trump may use it against us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-digital-tax-is-hated-america/docview/3130783288/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+digital+tax+is+hated+by+America.+Here%27s+why+Donald+Trump+may+use+it+against+us&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-It's set to bring in billions of dollars the federal government is keen to have. But Ottawa must first fend off a resistant United States before it can collect.
The Trudeau government's digital services tax - to be paid by the world's digital giants operating in Canada - kicks in this year. However, a trade challenge from the U.S. and the re-election of Donald Trump has business groups and even Premier Doug Ford calling on Ottawa to back down out of fear of retaliation.
"We cannot put millions of Canadian jobs and our historic economic partnership with the U.S. at risk because of a stubborn refusal to listen to the concerns of our American friends," Ford said last month.
Now, the stakes could rise with the return of a Trump administration threatening tough tariffs with trade deal negotiations on the horizon.
Here's what you need to know about the digital services tax.
What's the digital services tax?
For several years, there have been global negotiations on how to tax the revenues from economic activity generated online, by large companies operating in other countries.
Supporters in Canada have argued the tax is necessary because global digital giants aren't paying their fair share of taxes. The Liberals first promised a tax in 2019. But Canada has always said its priority is a global agreement.
Those negotiations, however, have stalled.
Impatient with that process after several delays, Canada and other countries decided to impose their own taxes until there is progress on the global efforts. The U.K., France, Italy and India are among countries which have already put a digital services tax in place. Others have agreed to hold off until at least 2025.
Foreign digital giants that make more than $20 million in Canada and more than 750 million euros globally in annual revenues will now have to pay a three per cent tax on revenues earned within Canada.
It is expected to generate $900 million per year, but Ottawa will get $2.3 billion in its first year because it would apply retroactively to revenues generated since 2022.
Katrina Miller, the executive director for Canadians for Tax Fairness, told the Star that having our own digital services tax puts pressure on other countries to get a global deal done and could foster increased competition in Canada.
But the U.S. sees things differently.
Why is it a sensitive issue for the U.S.?
The U.S. has argued that unilateral digital service taxes "discriminate against U.S. companies," because it's mostly American companies that meet the revenue threshold.
The previous Trump administration launched investigations against countries which made the move, and even imposed retaliatory measures in some cases. But President Joe Biden's White House was more open to putting retaliatory measures on hold in an effort to reach a global deal.
When Canada announced its own tax would proceed this summer, arguing it was unfair for Canada to wait as negotiations stalled, the U.S. challenged it under the two countries' trade agreement, launching a period of dispute settlement consultations - a less heavy-handed approach in which the two countries hold official conversations in hopes of resolving the issue.
The deadline for those consultations quietly passed last week without a resolution - Canada remains committed to the tax in the absence of an international agreement - giving the U.S. the option to proceed further by requesting a dispute settlement panel.
But given the Biden administration's lame-duck position, some observers and a government official who spoke to the Star suggested it could be left to the incoming Trump administration to deal with Canada's tax. Even if the Biden administration proceeds with its complaint, that process is unlikely to be complete before Trump takes power in January - and the U.S.' argument is not "their strongest against Canada," said William Pellerin, an international trade lawyer with McMillan LLP.
"Ultimately, it's one that's going to be ... probably best dealt with politically rather than through the litigation process," Pellerin told the Star.
A spokesperson for United States Trade Representative Katherine Tai suggested the U.S. has no immediate plans to move further in an emailed statement to the Star.
"We continue to consult with Canada to address our concerns, and we reserve our rights to further proceedings, including under the USMCA, (United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement) should those concerns not be resolved," the spokesperson said.
What's at stake for Canadians?
Since the Canadian government announced the tax, fears of devastating retaliatory measures has had business groups here calling for the Trudeau government to back off.
And those warnings are taking on new urgency as Canada prepares for a Trump administration that has resorted to strong-armed political responses in the past.
Canada will have to renegotiate the U.S. and Mexico agreement with a Trump administration that has slapped tariffs on Canadian goods before and promised during the last election to implement a 10 per cent tariff on all goods coming into America.
"Trump is ready to use what we would call in the trade world the 'nuclear option,'" Pellerin said.
Jessica Brandon-Jepp, the senior director of fiscal and financial services policy at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, told the Star she worries Canada's new tax could threaten the nearly $3.6 billion (2023 figures) of goods and services that cross the border each day.
"This particular tax is not going to bring in nearly as much as the damage that it has the potential to do for the Canadian economy and our trading relationship," she said in an interview.
Pellerin said the federal government could be open to negotiating the terms of its tax during trade talks with the U.S., but the large revenues may be too difficult to give up.
The Trump transition team did not return a request for comment about how it plans to handle this issue.
Asked several times in the last few weeks about how she will deal with a Trump administration on the issue, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland would only point to the countries with their own digital services taxes, including France and Italy which have not faced retaliatory measures from the U.S. On Tuesday, she suggested it would be unfair to Canada if we faced retaliatory measures for doing the same as other countries.
"We simply believe that Canada should be on a level playing field, on an even footing, with our G7 partners," Freeland told reporters in Ottawa. "We think that Canada, with our very strong relationship with the United States, with our free trade agreement with the United States, should not be discriminated against relative to the U.K, to France, to Italy. That's our position."
CAPTION: Minister of Finance and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland speaks to reporters after a meeting of the Cabinet Committee on Canada-U.S relations following the election of President-elect Donald Trump on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Nov. 8, 2024.Freeland isn't flinching now that the U.S. can move forward with a trade challenge against the Liberal government's digital services tax.

Subject: Taxes; Trade agreements; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Trade agreements Tariffs

Location: Italy; Mexico; United States--US; Canada; France

People: Trump, Donald J; Freeland, Chrystia
