Document 114 of 500

Ford government announces tough new rules for 'bad actor' immigration consultants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-announces-tough-new-rules-bad/docview/3130782978/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+announces+tough+new+rules+for+%27bad+actor%27+immigration+consultants&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New provincial legislation will target "bad actor" immigration consultants who prey on newcomers - with a "name and shame" rule for offenders as well as a possible lifetime ban from the profession.
Minister of Labour and Immigration David Piccini said Wednesday he's setting out new, tougher rules for immigration representatives, saying many are "diligent and honest, but we know there are bad actors who exploit vulnerable newcomers, scamming them out of their life savings, lying to them with a fake job acceptance letter, coercing them into forging documents, jeopardizing their immigration status, and some immigrants are forced into horrendous and even more exploitative situations."
Speaking in Brampton, where he made the announcement with local Mayor Patrick Brown, among others, Piccini said the new legislation "will crack down on immigration scams by creating new standards and enforcement tools ... these new standards will require representatives to be accountable and to be honest with immigrants they're working for."
Under the changes, consultants will have to show proof they are licensed, draw up written contracts with clients and allow them to see their files.
Minimum penalties for fraud will be a three- to 10-year ban from the profession, and a lifetime ban for anyone convicted of human trafficking or withholding an applicant's passport.
Fines for representatives or employers who lie during the application process - or urge their client to be dishonest - rise to $10,000 from $2,000.
There are about 12,300 licensed immigration consultants in Canada, and almost 5,600 are in Ontario. Over the past five years, 153 people across the country have been charged with fraudulent immigration consultant offences.
"It is unfair for new immigrants to be taken advantage of," MPP Patrice Barnes, Piccini's parliamentary assistant, said Wednesday in the legislature.
"This is about levelling the playing field, protecting our workers and ensuring Ontario remains a place workers are treated unfairly and honestly."
Stef Lach, spokesperson for the College of Immigration and Citizenship Consultants, called the proposed changes a "positive step to protect prospective immigrants to Ontario. Licensees of the college are required to comply with all applicable federal and provincial laws. If Ontario brings in legislative requirements, those would automatically become a college regulatory requirement."
The college itself "can impose disciplinary measures up to and including permanent licence revocation, restitution and fines," he added.
In an editorial board meeting with the Toronto Star on Wednesday, federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller said "I've always wanted (the Ford government) to assume some level of responsibility, so I'll take anything at this point, but I need them to do more," reports the Star's Ryan Tumilty.
Syed Hussan, executive director of the Migrant Workers Alliance for Change, said the problem is that most of the offenders are "fly-by-night operators" and that newcomers need both the provincial and federal governments to step up.
"Once a person has been misled, what recourse do they have? How does the person get any money back?" he said. "...There's nothing in this regulatory process that allows migrants to have any reparations, or justice."
CAPTION: Labour minister David Piccini is shown with Premier Doug Ford at a funding announcement in 2023.Ontario Labour Minister David Piccini attends Question Period at the Ontario legislature in Toronto, Tuesday, Nov. 28, 2023. Piccini says his government will introduce legislation next week that will see fines increased for violations of the Employment Standards Act.THE CANADIAN PRESS/Chris Young

Subject: Fines & penalties; Immigration policy; Legislation; Fraud

Document 115 of 500

Two years into council's term: Our civic pride is collapsing around us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/two-years-into-councils-term-our-civic-pride-is/docview/3130782964/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+years+into+council%27s+term%3A+Our+civic+pride+is+collapsing+around+us&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There has been a lot written about the dire state of our downtown, awash in drugs, violent crime and scores of people suffering from homelessness.
But this past week, it went from a depressing, dilapidated dystopia to a crumbling of our civic pride when one of our most iconic buildings in Gore Park collapsed from neglect.
Every city in Ontario is dealing with post-pandemic inflation, a housing affordability crisis due to poor planning, a lack of housing stock, renovictions and increased pressure on municipal social services.
But not every city watches protected heritage buildings in its town square collapse from neglect, literally the day after crowds gathered in front of them during a Remembrance Day event. If that's not bad enough, we also learned that the heritage-protected Tivoli theatre has suffered from neglect to the point where the owner claims that it, too, cannot be saved.
I'm no architect or historian, but I know poor leadership when I see it. I know that being fully aware of a problem but seemingly turning away from what we know to be the truth results in failure. Buildings don't fall by themselves any more than a homelessness crisis solves itself.
The issues leading to the collapse of our heritage buildings were evident during the term of the last council and the one before that. But that doesn't excuse current leadership, especially the mayor, from taking responsibility for addressing lingering issues we continue to face as a city and reaching crisis levels on her watch.
Tackling the poly-crisis of homelessness, drug poisoning and violent crime isn't easy, but it gets a lot harder when you look away. It reminds me of the lyric in David Bowie and Queen's song "Under Pressure": "Turned away from it all like a blind man. Sat on the fence but it don't work."
We need a mayor who not only says she cares about the people of our city, but gets off the fence and fights for them. Two years into her term, I'm still waiting for Andrea Horwath to be the Hamilton scrapper we all heard about. I'm still waiting to see her words about how much she loves Hamilton translate into actions that help the people who call our city home.
When will she get in the arena and start fighting for what Hamiltonians need at Queen's Park, where she spent so many years as the leader of the official Opposition? Voters believed she, of all people, could take on Premier Doug Ford and get our city what it needs from the provincial government.
When will she take a hard stand against absentee developers who promise one thing and then let our most precious properties dangerously self-destruct while we watch in horror and helplessness?
When will she share a vision for Hamilton's future prosperity that inspires council to work together, restore confidence and attract people to the slew of optimistic new condo towers filling our skyline?
It's easy to run on name recognition and platitudes about how Hamilton's best days are ahead. It's another thing to lead our city with passion and vision to reach its potential.
Our city is literally and figuratively falling apart in front of our eyes. Does the mayor care? Does she even see it? Or will we get more happy photo ops on her social media and mediocre missives from her communication team?
It's time for Andrea Horwath to get in the fight or get out of the way before our civic pride collapses around us, like our buildings.
CAPTION: A demolition crew removes the facade of an 1840s building at Gore Park on Friday after two others dating to the 1870s collapsed. I'm no architect or historian, but I know poor leadership when I see it, Laura Babcock writes.
CREDIT: Laura Babcock

Subject: Leadership; Councils; Violent crime

People: Horwath, Andrea

Document 116 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3130583504/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't give them
the attention
Re: A strong, unified voice against hate is needed in Hamilton, Nov. 19
I usually agree with Margaret Shkimba but I think a strong social media response to the morons who raised the racist banner at Jackson Square just gives them the publicity they want.
I'd like to see all our public figures join the exodus from X anyway, given it has degenerated into an extreme right-wing, conspiracy-theory-propagating cess pit under Elon Musk's management. Threads is a perfectly good and well-run alternative.
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Tighten police shooting rules
Re: Father of three identified as man killed in Hamilton police shooting, Nov. 11
The family and friends of Erixon Kabera are right to demand and (hopefully) receive answers from the province's Special Investigations Unit about why their loved one was shot dead by Hamilton police. And it's nice for the rest of us to think they will get those answers. Nice, but naive.
That's because the SIU has no power to compel the "subject officers" - those directly involved in the death - to sit for an interview, provide a statement or turn over their notes. And even if they do sit for an interview, there doesn't appear to be any wording in the legislation to indicate the statements made by the officers are under oath. So in a case like this one, where there doesn't appear to be any nonpolice witnesses and no video footage (and, of course, no bodycam footage), what hope should any of us have that the SIU will get at the truth of what happened?
Unless an officer is charged criminally in this case, the only way the family of Mr. Kabera might ever get police to provide information under oath about the shooting is if they one day launch a civil lawsuit and the case eventually makes it to trial.
Carrying a gun to do your job comes with great responsibility. That responsibility should also include the requirement to answer questions truthfully about how that gun used in the line of duty led to a fatality.
Steve Buist, Burlington
Don't bow down
to bullies so easily
Re: Councillors want Doug Ford out of city's bike lanes, Nov. 16
In the final run-up to the U.S. election, much was made of the decision by many prominent newspapers not to endorse a presidential candidate. The Los Angeles Times and The Washington Post released contorted explanations for why they were choosing not to take a political stand. It was a move widely regarded as an example of what Timothy Snyder calls "anticipatory obedience," something that spineless, cowardly people and organizations do when confronted with a bully or tyrant.
I was shocked to see Coun. Brad Clark suggest that Hamilton perform its own anticipatory obedience by not "stepping on Superman's cape" regarding the bike lane debate, assuming "Superman" is Premier Doug Ford. Didn't Hamilton come up with a plan that was the product of extensive consultation and conscientious planning? Why would Hamilton offer Premier Ford its anticipatory obedience for not only the bike lanes issue but any other issue affecting the city and its operation? Hamilton is proud of being a scrappy town. If Premier Ford wants to scrap the bike lanes, or anything else the city has proposed, Hamilton should respond by telling him, "Any time, anywhere if you meddle in city business."
Peter Mountford, Hamilton
Bike lanes
are for the few
This city has gone overboard with bike lanes. Upper Paradise onto Mohawk Road, there are no right turns because of bike-lane barriers which has increased cut-though traffic on Sandringham and Buckingham drives. Herkimer and Charlton from James to Queen is one lane due to parking and bike lanes. The traffic is frequently backed up from Bay to James due to the restriction. We are a city of mostly seniors and we are not going to start riding bikes, no matter how many lanes there are. The fact is the city is spending money to please the few is not acceptable.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton

Document 117 of 500

Canada Briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-briefs/docview/3130580796/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Briefs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
New health program for
first responders
Ontario is launching a new mental-health program for first responders. The province says the Mental Health Supports for Public Safety Personnel program will provide help for police officers, correctional workers, paramedics and firefighters. The $32-million program will include a 24-hour call line and a web portal for services such as internet-based cognitive behavioural therapy and peer support. The province is also creating an anti-stigma strategy to make it easier for public safety workers to ask for help. The program will include leadership support and training and recommended mental-health practices. Solicitor General Michael Kerzner says first responders will receive targeted mental-health support to address the challenges they face.
Nova Scotia
Early voter turnout is down
With one week to go in the Nova Scotia election campaign, early voter turnout is down sharply compared to the 2021 election. Elections Nova Scotia says that as of Monday, a total of 64,000 early votes had been cast, which is 11,367 fewer than the 75,367 votes cast at the same point in 2021's summer campaign. Early votes include write-in ballots, early votes cast at the returning office and advance polls. Liberal party Leader Zach Churchill said during a news conference the figures may be due to "election fatigue" as the date comes on the heels of municipal and U.S. elections.
News services

Document 118 of 500

Our civic pride is collapsing around us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/our-civic-pride-is-collapsing-around-us/docview/3130580582/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+civic+pride+is+collapsing+around+us&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 119 of 500

Doug Ford says $48M estimate to remove Toronto bike lanes is 'hogwash'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-48m-estimate-remove-toronto-bike/docview/3130931637/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+%2448M+estimate+to+remove+Toronto+bike+lanes+is+%27hogwash%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Hogwash."
That's Premier Doug Ford's blunt assessment of city bureaucrats' $48-million estimate for removing bike lanes that cost $27 million to install on Yonge Street, Bloor Street and University Avenue.
Asked by reporters Tuesday for his thoughts on the City of Toronto's tally to dismantle up to 22 kilometres of cycling infrastructure, Ford did not mince words.
"You know it doesn't cost $50 million. That's a bunch of hogwash," the premier said.
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," he said, arguing the lanes contribute to traffic congestion.
Ford's comments came the morning after a legislative committee studying his controversial plan to scrap three bike lanes heard repeated pleas for them to be retained.
Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation planning, design and management at the City of Toronto, told the all-party panel Monday the scheme would mean "multiple years of successive construction that would disrupt other commuters, harm businesses and incur costs for years."
"While the intent of removing these lanes may be to improve traffic, the construction process to dismantle these lanes would mean considerable delays and disruption for drivers," she warned.
Hayward estimated it would cost $20 million to remove the lanes along 3.5 kilometres of University Avenue and Queen's Park Crescent as well as part of Bloor Street downtown.
The remaining $28 million would be for removing curbs, lane markings and signs and repaving the other 18.5 kilometres of lanes on Bloor Street West and Yonge Street.
But Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria noted the city spent $300,000 to remove about three kilometres of bike lanes on Jarvis Street in 2011 and $80,000 to dismantle them on a four-kilometre stretch of Brimley Road in 2020.
The Jarvis lanes cost $86,000 to build, while those on Brimley were $160,000 to install.
Sarkaria, who has promised Queen's Park would foot the tab for taking out bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University, acknowledged Monday the province has not completed any costing for the removal.
With a potential spring election in the offing, Ford's Progressive Conservatives have pounced on bike lanes as a potential wedge issue against the opposition New Democrats and Liberals that could help them hold some of their dozen Toronto seats.
The Tories' internal polling apparently reflects publicly available research - included a recent Abacus Data survey for the Star - that shows support for the plan.
Abacus found 55 per cent approved of Ford's edict that municipalities must get provincial approval for bike lanes that would interfere with vehicular traffic. That's about twice the 28 per cent that disagreed with the scheme with 17 per cent unsure.
The company polled 998 Ontarians from Oct. 31 through Nov. 5 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
But at Monday's legislative hearings, there was overwhelming support for keeping the lanes intact.
Presenters to the committee repeatedly urged Tory MPPs to rethink their plans.
Association of Ontario Municipalities executive director Brian Rosborough, expressing concern about Queen's Park infringing upon city councils' turf, said gridlock in Toronto is "complex" and can't be solved by getting rid of a few bike lanes.
Scott Butler, executive director of Good Roads, the 130-year-old municipal association that focused on the quality and design of Ontario roads, likened bike lanes to the forward-looking policy-making that led to a provincial building code and other universally accepted safety standards.
Butler pointed out some European cities were once as focused on cars and trucks as Toronto, but have since redesigned their streets to accommodate safe cycling, which has led to cleaner, greener communities.
CAPTION: Hundreds of cyclists gather on Saturday afternoon at Christie Pits on Nov. 9, 2024 to protest the Ford government's move to remove bikes lanes in Toronto.

Subject: Traffic

Document 120 of 500

'Triggered by a recall in another country': Organic baby carrots recalled in Canada and U.S. over E. coli concerns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/triggered-recall-another-country-organic-baby/docview/3130179037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Triggered+by+a+recall+in+another+country%27%3A+Organic+baby+carrots+recalled+in+Canada+and+U.S.+over+E.+coli+concerns&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you've got some baby carrots in your fridge to nibble on, you might want to check out the packaging to see if it's part of a new recall by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency.
The CFIA has issued a recall for various brands of organic carrots due to concerns with possible E. coli contamination.
"Do not consume, use, sell, serve or distribute recalled products," the health agency said on its website.
The concern is potential exposure to the E. coli 0121 bacteria, which may not look or smell spoiled, but can still make you sick.
"This recall was triggered by a recall in another country. There have been no reported illnesses associated with the consumption of these products in Canada."
According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 18 states in the United States have reported illness believed to be caused by the carrots, sold by Grimmway Farms in California.
The recall was made Nov. 16 after 39 cases of illness were reported, and 15 people hospitalized. One person has also died in connection with the E. coli 0121 infection.
The carrots include organic juice carrots, fresh carrots, organic baby-cut carrots, baby-cut carrots, organic petite carrots, and carrots sold with the brands Bunny-Luv, PC Organics, Compliments Organic and Cal-Organic.
The recall includes both single and double packages, ranging in size from 340 grams, 454 grams, 907 grams, 2.27 kilograms, 11.34 kilograms to 50 pounds.
The Cal-Organic brands have best before dates from Sept. 11 to Nov. 2, and Aug. 14 to Oct. 23.
The Bunny-Luv brands had best before dates from Aug. 14 to Oct. 23.
Compliments Organic brands had best before dates ranging from Aug. 14 or Sept. 14 to Oct. 23 and 24.
The PC Organics brands had best before dates from Aug. 14 to Oct. 23, and Sept. 11 to Nov. 2.
These products should no longer be on store shelves, but if you still have these carrot packages at your home, throw them out or return to the location where they were purchased, the Canadian Food Inspection Agency said.
E. coli symptoms can include nausea, vomiting, mild to severe abdominal cramps and watery to bloody diarrhea.
In severe cases, some may have seizures or strokes, need blood transfusions and kidney dialysis or live with permanent kidney damage. Death may also occur in extreme cases.
Here's the list of products with UPC codes:
Bunny-Luv organic juice carrots, 11.34g, 0 7878390227 9Bunny-Luv fresh carrots, 907g, 03338366001 1Cal-Organic organic baby-cut carrots 454g, 0 3338390205 0Cal-Organic organic baby cut carrots, two-pack 907g, 0 7878390207 1Cal-Organic organic baby cut carrots, 907g, 0 7878390206 4Cal-Organic organic carrots, 907g, 0 3338390202 9Cal-Organic organic carrots, 2.27 kg, 0 3338390203 6Cal-Organic organic petite carrots, 340g, 0 7878390810 3Cal-Organic carrots, 50 pound, no UPC codeCompliments Organic baby cut carrots, 454g, 0 5574250267 1Compliments Organic carrots, 907g, 0 5574250268 8PC Organics baby-cut carrots, 454g, 0 6038369288 9PC Organics baby-cut carrots, 907g, 0 6038373643 9PC Organics carrots, 907g, 0 6038369593 4
To learn more about the recall, visit recalls-rappels.canada.ca. Consumers with questions or products in their possession may contact Grimmway Farms' customer services desk at 1-800-301-3101.
CAPTION: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency has recalled certain brands of organic baby carrots after a potential E. coli contamination was discoveredThe Canadian Food Inspection Agency has recalled certain brands of organic baby carrots after a potential E. coli contamination was discovered

Nov. 19: Don't destroy our golf course, don't believe the polls and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-19-dont-destroy-our-golf-course-believe-polls/docview/3130178985/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+19%3A+Don%27t+destroy+our+golf+course%2C+don%27t+believe+the+polls+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't destroy our golf course
Re: Should Hamilton shrink its golf footprint for new parks? Nov. 14
Why is city hall attempting to destroy our historic golf courses and replace them with pickleball courts and the inevitable tent encampments that will follow in the "natural areas?" House values nearby will drop. The courses are not broken, but Aberdeen Avenue and Charlton Avenue in the neighbourhood certainly are. City hall needs to solve problems not create them.
Golf has been played at these courses since 1896. Golf Canada and Golf Ontario have helped establish a wonderful youth golf program at Chedoke. Seeing the excitement and enthusiasm of the kids is amazing. We can say goodbye to that program if some politicians have their way.
The courses have always been revenue positive. If the courses are destroyed there will be many good people losing jobs, including summer student jobs. Please contact your representatives at city hall and ask them to reconsider this flawed plan.
Pat Lynch, Stoney Creek
Bigger issues need attention
Coun. Maureen Wilson cites "a lack of park space" behind her motion to reconfigure Chedoke golf courses. Ward 1 is home to Dundurn Park, Victoria Park, Churchill Park, the rail trail and the Princess Point trail system!
I presume such a transformation would cost tens of thousands of dollars. Given another huge tax increase on the horizon, where is council's sense of fiscal responsibility? It is unethical for any councillor to defer dealing with the city's most pressing issues (crime, encampments, affordability, the state of our roads and the deterioration of our downtown) while actively pursuing their personal agendas.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Trust in polls has been lost
"Too close to call," "razor thin" and "within the margin of error" were phrases used to describe how close the U.S. election was going to be. Donald Trump and the GOP won by a landslide, taking the presidency, senate and congress.
This was one huge "margin of error."
This was so wrong, you have to wonder why anyone would pay attention in the future to polls and why the media would report them.
Canada will probably have an election next year. The polls are predicting a Conservative majority. These are the same pollsters that predicted a close vote south of the border. Based on this, don't be surprised if the Liberals are returned to power.
Robert Wood, Simcoe
Praise for police parade performance
Chief Frank Bergen and the senior officers of the Hamilton Police Service did such a beautiful job of engaging with the children of our community in their "search" for The Grinch at the Santa Claus Parade. Along with the kids of Hamilton, I think even The Grinch's heart grew three sizes that day thanks to you.
Tara Grisdale, Ancaster
This has gone on long enough
The Canadian Union of Postal Workers and Canada Post have been negotiating a new contract since Nov. 15, 2023. Talks have been going on for a full year and still no settlement has been reached. This is totally ridiculous and unacceptable.
The Bible tells us God created the world in seven days and yet CUPW and Canada Post can't get their act together in over a year.
Canada Post is losing money partially because of a competitive parcel delivery market that gained strength during COVID-19 and because people are using email and texting to communicate, resulting in a decline in letter mail volume. Canada Post reported a loss of $748 million in 2023 and has reportedly lost $490 million in the first half of 2024.
Obviously losses of that magnitude cannot be sustained. Postal workers' salaries, like the salaries of many others as well as the income of seniors, have not kept up with inflation.
Both the postal workers and Canada Post are in a tough spot, but the situation has to be resolved. They have had a year to find a solution. To negotiate for a year without coming up with an agreement is totally unbelievable and irresponsible.
Get it done now.
Al Knapp, Hamilton
CAPTION: The city will study possible alternative public uses for part of the Chedoke Golf Club's 278-acre property.

Document 122 of 500

Doug Ford and Justin Trudeau are talking to the wrong audience about Donald Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-justin-trudeau-are-talking-wrong/docview/3130178901/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+and+Justin+Trudeau+are+talking+to+the+wrong+audience+about+Donald+Trump&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Donald Trump takes power - and overpowers Canada - Doug Ford has a rescue plan:
Re-elect me. The sooner the better, for better or for worse.
It's a variation on the survival plan hatched by Justin Trudeau:
Re-elect me, too. The later the better, for the alternative is far worse.
That puts the premier and the prime minister seemingly on the same wavelength, albeit separate timelines, in search of renewed mandates. We need stability, they say, to right the ship of state in turbulent Trumpian times.
No longer a tag team, they are riding decidedly different waves. Ford hopes to surf an Ontario wave toward re-election, if he can get out ahead of a Greenbelt police probe; Trudeau is trying to keep his head above water before a national electoral deluge overwhelms him.
Facing headwinds from the south, both politicians are promising to serve and protect. They speak of trade protectionism (via tariffs) and border protection (from immigrant incursions); they're also talking up defence spending - not so much to protect us from overseas invasions as from American reverberations over NATO.
But will either of these federal or provincial playbooks protect us from the president-elect's wrath? Or are they merely self-serving re-election strategies, based on outdated bilateral tactics with little traction in Trump's America?
Ford cheerfully threw Mexico under the bus when he accused it of unfair trade practices by Chinese-owned EV carmakers in that country. He suggested we forget Fortress North America in favour of "Buy Can-Am" - replacing the current trilateral trade pact once known as NAFTA with a bilateral free trade treaty:
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them."
He boasts of his past success in pushing Ottawa for retaliation after Trump imposed tariffs on Ontario steel, claiming credit for the U.S. ultimately backing down. And he peppers most speeches with references to the nine million American workers whose jobs depend on exports to Ontario.
The premier opined boldly on federal tax policy, suggesting that Canada should cancel the controversial digital sales tax to calm bilateral waters. Like other premiers, Ford has also admonished the federal government to control the flow of irregular or illegal migrants across the border into Canada.
Trudeau, too, has cast himself as well-placed to navigate the next Trump administration, based on his handling of the last one. He has reactivated a cabinet committee on bilateral relations, reprised Deputy PM Chrystia Freeland in a leadership role and reassured Canadians on border questions.
The problem with all of the above is that the premier and the PM remain largely invisible to an incoming U.S. president with his eye on the world. Ford runs a province, not a country, and unless he becomes a regular on Fox News, he won't get on Trump's radar.
Much of the lobbying and persuading from Ford and Trudeau is also premised on the old playbook that Canadians need only remind Americans of their economic interests at the local level and word will get through to Washington.
But this notion of triangulation from the state and local level to the White House has limits. For one thing, many Southern U.S. states look to Mexico for their economic interests more than any Canadian connection.
Given those political realities, much of the posturing from the premier and prime minister seems intended as much for their domestic constituencies as any American audiences. That's especially true of the rarefied immigration rhetoric in Canada today.
Much of the alarmism about migrants is inward-looking, rather than looking at it from the American point of view. With Trump threatening to deport one million illegal migrants a year, the premiers of Quebec, Ontario and Alberta are clamouring for protection against waves of people trying to sneak into Canada, while worrying whether the Trump administration will take them back (as required by our latest bilateral agreement).
Rather than focusing solely on the northward flow of migrants into Canada, however, the federal government should be thinking hard about how to stop the southward flow into America which is much higher. After all, that's what has grabbed the attention of the incoming U.S. border czar, Tom Homan, who happens to be from upstate New York, near the Ontario border.
He called the Canada-U.S. border an "extreme national security vulnerability," predicting "tough conversations" with Ottawa. "There has to be an understanding from Canada that they can't be a gateway to terrorists coming into the United States."
While Canadians obsess about the notorious Roxham Road path into Canada, it turns out that borders are in the eye of the beholder - and very much a two-way street.
Ford and Trudeau are right to turn their minds to Trump. But the test of their tactics isn't how persuasive-sounding they seem when speaking to Canadians, but how effective they are in winning over the Americans in difficult negotiations ahead.
So far, both are following their re-election playbooks. But both will be judged by Canadian voters on how they play their hands with the Americans, not how they sound on the domestic campaign trail.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queens Park as the Ontario legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Trade agreements; Prime ministers; Protectionism; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade agreements Protectionism Tariffs

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Doug Ford and Justin Trudeau are talking to the wrong audience about Donald Trump

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 19, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 124 of 500

NDP MP cautioned for wearing pin supporting Palestinians in the House of Commons

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-mp-cautioned-wearing-pin-supporting/docview/3129924170/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+MP+cautioned+for+wearing+pin+supporting+Palestinians+in+the+House+of+Commons&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A New Democrat MP was warned Monday that her decision to don a watermelon pin - a symbol of the Palestinian cause - could be construed as a political "prop" that has no place in the House of Commons. During question period, Edmonton-Strathcona MP Heather McPherson took to the floor of the lower chamber to castigate Justin Trudeau's Liberal government for its response to the ongoing humanitarian crisis in Gaza."Entire families have been decimated. Children are starving to death. When will the Liberals live up to their obligations?" McPherson said, calling for sanctions on the Israeli government and the implementation of an "actual arms embargo."But before Rob Oliphant, the parliamentary secretary to the foreign affairs minister, could respond, House of Commons Speaker Greg Fergus interjected."I just want to encourage all members, to remind members, to be very careful about what they wear in this House to ensure that it's not considered a prop," Fergus said.McPherson was wearing a beaded watermelon pin when she delivered her remarks. Watermelon imagery has long been used as a stand-in symbol for the Palestinian flag, which shares the same colours as the fruit. Since Hamas's deadly Oct. 7 attacks on Israel and the resulting loss of lives in Gaza from Israel's retaliatory strikes, the watermelon has become a mainstream symbol to signal support for Palestinian rights. It has drawn less attention than the kaffiyeh, a Middle Eastern scarf also emblematic of the Palestinian cause, which was banned from being worn in the Ontario legislature earlier this year.In a heated exchange over what is and is not permitted to be worn in the Commons, McPherson rose on a point of order to question Fergus's suggestion."I have to tell you that I stand here proudly wearing the pin that stands in solidarity to Palestinian people, but people within this place are wearing pins for a various number of reasons," McPherson said.She referred to a moose hide pin that a number of MPs wear in the Commons, which was born from an Indigenous-led movement to end violence toward women and children. The NDP MP's reference to poppies also being worn in the chamber for Remembrance Day, however, was met with outrage from the opposition benches, with Conservative MPs expressing disbelief on social media over the comparison. According to the House of Commons' rules on order and decorum, "props of any kind, used as a way of making a silent comment on issues" are barred from the chamber. Political buttons and lapel pins usually fall outside those restrictions, but can similarly be prohibited if they create disorder or express specific positions. Speakers have, in the past, requested that MPs remove the so-called props from their lapels.Fergus stopped short of requesting McPherson do the same.MPs can frequently be seen sporting pins emblematic of causes in the Commons, such as green felt squares affixed to their lapels to protest Islamophobia and ribbons raising awareness about health conditions and diseases. "Mr. Speaker, I have proudly worn many pins in this House. For example, tomorrow, to mark a thousand days since Putin invaded Ukraine, I will wear the Ukrainian pin," McPherson said, referring to another pin commonly worn in the chamber. "I need to know whether the Conservatives will call this out as a prop."Fergus said he would take the matter under consideration and return to the House with further comments if necessary.
CAPTION: NDP MP Heather McPherson speaks in the House of Commons on Nov. 18, 2024.

Location: Gaza Strip; Israel

Title: NDP MP cautioned for wearing pin supporting Palestinians in the House of Commons

Ford government disputes Toronto's $48M bike lane removal cost: 'It can't cost double'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-disputes-torontos-48m-bike-lane/docview/3129922823/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+disputes+Toronto%27s+%2448M+bike+lane+removal+cost%3A+%27It+can%27t+cost+double%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Toronto's estimate that it could cost $48 million to remove $27 million of bike lanes "doesn't add up," charges Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
"I just don't think that can be accurate. It can't cost double the amount to remove them as it does to put them in place," Sarkaria told reporters Monday.
But the minister, who has promised Queen's Park would foot the tab for taking out bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University in order to alleviate traffic gridlock, acknowledged the province has not completed any costing for the removal.
"We've seen similar projects across the city here - and other places across Canada - be removed for way less than that. Jarvis and Brimley are two examples that come to mind," he said, referring to city bike lanes previously removed at a cost of $300,000 and $80,000 respectively.
Sarkaria's comments came as legislative hearings began into his controversial bill with opposition parties and others criticizing Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government for wasting taxpayers' money to scrap existing infrastructure.
Jacquelyn Hayward, director of transportation planning, design and management at the City of Toronto, warned Ford's scheme would mean "years" of disruption and reduce safety for cyclists.
"While the intent of removing these lanes may be to improve traffic, the construction process to dismantle these lanes would mean considerable delays and disruption for drivers," she said.
Hayward estimated it would cost $20 million to reconstruct the 3.5 km of University Avenue and Queen's Park Crescent as well as part of Bloor Street.
The remaining $28 million would be spent on removing lane markings, signs and curbs and repaving the other 18.5 km of lanes on Bloor and Yonge.
"Removing the bike lanes on Bloor, Yonge and University would not be a short-term inconvenience - we'd likely be facing multiple years of successive construction that would disrupt other commuters, harm businesses and incur costs for years," she told the legislative committee.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said it's "outrageous" that public money was being spent to reduce the number of bike lanes in the provincial capital.
"This is ... the people of Ontario being forced to pay the price for this premier's personal grievances, his vanity projects," said Stiles, suggesting Ford is fighting "battles that he already lost at Toronto city council when he was a councillor" from 2010 to 2014.
Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood) pointed out that six cyclists have been killed on Toronto streets this year.
"What is it they're not seeing? What is it they're not hearing? This is unbelievable when you have fatalities and we are putting (a price) on people's lives," said Hazell.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Sarkaria's legislation is "completely fiscally irresponsible."
"Why should people in Bracebridge, Owen Sound and Sudbury be paying for the cost of ripping out bike lanes in Toronto?" said Schreiner.
At the legislative committee, speaker after speaker implored the Tories to reconsider.
Association of Ontario Municipalities' executive director Brian Rosborough said it would be "misguided and ineffective" to get rid of existing bike lanes.
Brian Burchell, general manager of the Bloor Annex Business Improvement Area, noted the lanes have been positive for the 207 small businesses along the stretch of Bloor he represents.
"It's been almost 10 years. It's helped transform our area into a vibrant, green, welcoming business district," said Burchell.
Scott Butler, executive director of Good Roads, a 130-year-old municipal association concerned with the quality and design of roads in Ontario, said the government has yet to explain how its proposal would make commuting safer.
"Rather than thinking of these as cycling facilities or erroneously thinking of them as causes of congestion, we're better served by acknowledging" that the lanes contribute to road safety, said Butler, urging a "common sense, evidence-based approach to bike policy."
CAPTION: Ontario's Minister of Transportation Prabmeet Sarkaria during question period at the Ontario Legislature in Toronto on Nov. 28, 2023.

Subject: Commuting; Bicycling

Ford government moves to shut 'loophole' for supervised drug consumption sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-moves-shut-loophole-supervised/docview/3129922446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+moves+to+shut+%27loophole%27+for+supervised+drug+consumption+sites&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is closing a "loophole" it fears could lead to more safe drug consumption sites in Ontario.
As the province works to ban sites within 200 metres of schools or daycares, new legislation introduced Monday would prevent municipalities or local health boards from applying for federal approval or funding, Health Minister Sylvia Jones said Monday.
The proposed bill, if passed, will require approval from Ontario's health minister before that could be done, effectively nixing the process under Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"We are shutting down that loophole," Jones said. "We don't want them bypassing and getting federal approval for something that we vehemently disagree with."
"I want to be clear - there will be no further safe injection sites in the province," she added after the Safer Streets, Stronger Communities Act was tabled in the legislature by Solicitor General Michael Kerzner.
The legislation also introduces new penalties for fraudulent changing of vehicle identification numbers in a crackdown on auto thefts and prohibits convicted sex offenders on Ontario's sex offender registry from legally changing their names while they are required to check in with police.
The push to prevent more safe consumption sites from opening was condemned by harm reduction proponents.
"That is an overreach of power," Zoe Dodd of the Toronto Overdose Prevention Society told a news conference with fellow activists at Queen's Park.
The government's closure of 10 safe consumption sites by March 31 - including five in Toronto - under the 200-metre rule means ambulance services will be swamped with overdose calls, leading to delays for anyone needing a paramedic, Dodd predicted.
"Every municipality in this province had better get an emergency plan in place because people are going to be overdosing and dying."
Ford has called safe injection sites a "failed policy" experiment that has led to dangerous conditions near homes and schools.
Instead, his government has set aside $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs, with 375 supportive housing units and addiction recovery and treatment beds.
But proponents of safe consumption sites said waiting lists for addiction treatment and rehab spots are months long, which is why they are needed to reverse overdoses and save lives.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford chats with Health Minister Sylvia Jones at Queen's Park on Feb. 26 2024.

Subject: Drug overdose; Addictions; Sex crimes

Ford government wants to work with social media giants to keep children safe, minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-wants-work-with-social-media/docview/3129922425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+wants+to+work+with+social+media+giants+to+keep+children+safe%2C+minister+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Jill Dunlop has met with representatives from YouTube and is hoping to soon speak to executives from other social media companies about what they are doing to help students' mental health and well-being.
"I talked to the organization about what they do to protect children and young people online," Dunlop said Monday, the first day of Bullying Awareness and Prevention Week, after announcing $4.6 million for resources and supports for kids.
Talks were held with YouTube on Oct. 23.
Dunlop said when the provincial government enacted its cellphone ban this fall, "we also blocked social media in the classrooms and schools to ensure that students were focused on the learning at hand ... so I want to meet with the companies to look at what they are doing to protect our students."
Across the province, a dozen school boards and two private schools are suing social media giants TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and Instagram, alleging the popular platforms have harmed kids' mental health as well as their academic achievement. They are seeking a total of more than $8 billion.
YouTube is not named in any of the cases.
Premier Doug Ford has been critical of the court action. Last May, then-education minister Stephen Lecce said that "instead of talking about it and litigating about, we opt to act decisively with a comprehensive plan" to not only curb cellphone and social media use in schools but also to work with companies to bring about change.
Boards that have launched lawsuits include Toronto public, Toronto Catholic, Peel public, Dufferin-Peel Catholic, and York Catholic. Two private religious schools - Holy Name of Mary College School in Mississauga and Eitz Chaim day school in Toronto - have also signed on.
They have filed separate but similar cases in Ontario's Superior Court of Justice, alleging the companies' platforms have "rewired the way children think, behave and learn" that has cost them billions in supports for staff and students. They are also calling on the companies to make improvements to their platforms, say their statements of claim.
Their allegations have not been tested in court, and the social media companies have said they will defend themselves against the claims.
A spokesperson for Snapchat has previously told the Star that its platform "was intentionally designed to be different from traditional social media, with a focus on helping Snapchatters communicate with their close friends ... While we will always have more work to do, we feel good about the role Snapchat plays in helping close friends feel connected, happy and prepared as they face the many challenges of adolescence."
A Meta spokesperson, representing Facebook and Instagram, has said "we want to reassure every parent that we have their interests at heart in the work we're doing to provide teens with safe, supportive experiences" on both platforms.
The boards and two schools are not paying legal costs, but have instead agreed that the law firm Neinstein LLP will take a contingency fee from any court award.
Of the $4.6 million Dunlop announced for resources and support, almost $1.5 million will go to Big Brothers and Big Sisters for mentoring programs in elementary and secondary schools, while $800,000 is earmarked for Kids Help Phone for its around-the-clock counselling services. Another $436,500 will help Egale Canada support anti-cyberbullying efforts for LGTBQ students.
About $150,000 will go to Victim Services Toronto to help youth with issues around online dating and healthy relationships.
CAPTION: Education Minister Jill Dunlop attends question period in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Document 129 of 500

Ontario must declare intimate partner violence an epidemic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-must-declare-intimate-partner-violence/docview/3129646521/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+must+declare+intimate+partner+violence+an+epidemic&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In Canada, intimate partner violence (IPV) is an epidemic.
Approximately 44 per cent of women who have been in an intimate relationship - or about 6.2 million women aged 15 and older - have reported experiencing some form of psychological, physical or sexual abuse in a relationship at some point in their lives.
IPV victimization has severe ramifications, including negative effects on health, economic consequences for the victim as well as on societal institutions such as the health-care and legal systems. It also has an impact on children who are exposed to the violence.
The explicit understanding that IPV must be deemed an epidemic follows in part from a 2022 inquest into a triple femicide near Ottawa in 2015 that left Carol Culleton, Anastasia Kuzyk and Nathalie Warmerdam dead at the hands of a man with a long history of intimate partner violence.
One of the inquest's 86 recommendations called for "all levels of government in Canada declare gender-based, intimate partner and family violence to be an epidemic that warrants a meaningful and sustained society-wide response."
In some municipalities in Ontario, this recommendation is being taken seriously. To date, 95 municipalities in the province have recognized intimate partner violence as an epidemic. Other provinces in Canada are taking similar steps - Nova Scotia, in fact, recently adopted a bill to declare IPV an epidemic.
Yet the Ontario government seems resistant to officially do so.
In October 2024, Conservative members of Ontario's legislature opposed a proposal to fast-track Bill 173 - the Intimate Partner Violence Epidemic Act, 2024 - to the third-reading stage.
Bill 173 seeks to recognize IPV as an epidemic, increase public awareness about IPV, boost prevention efforts and provide support to survivors. The decision not to fast-track the bill has been described by Lisa Gretzky, Bill 173 co-sponsor and an NDP member of the provincial legislature, as "shameful."
There are serious potential consequences stemming from the Ontario government's refusal to declare IPV an epidemic. Here are three:
Femicide - Gender-based murders of women and girls often represent a lethal culmination of gender-based violence, usually following prior experiences of physical, sexual or emotional IPV. According to the Canadian Observatory for Justice and Accountability, 155 girls and women have been killed so far in 2024, four more than at this point in 2023. If IPV is not declared an epidemic in Ontario, the factors sustaining IPV and femicide - including a lack of urgency in addressing IPV and underfunded, fragmented responses among social services - will persist, likely leading to continued or increased rates of femicide.
Stigma and misconceptions about IPV survivors - IPV survivors may feel isolated in their experiences and encounter judgment and blame from others who might hold them responsible for "choosing" to remain in abusive relationships, or be asked why they don't "just leave," resulting in feelings of shame and self-blame. If IPV is not framed as an epidemic in Ontario, it could perpetuate perceptions of IPV as a "private matter" occurring between individuals, resulting in a lack of public awareness about the reality and complexities of the issue. That could dissuade IPV survivors from speaking out about the violence they are experiencing and seeking help.
Funding for social services and supports - If IPV is designated as an epidemic and the need to address IPV urgently is acknowledged, it could result in more funding and resources being directed toward multi-agency services to support IPV survivors. Without this designation, however, existing, insufficient funding - currently directed toward social supports such as women's shelters, crisis intervention, legal services and counselling services - will persist, ultimately failing to adequately support survivors. Women's shelters in Canada are already severely underfunded and often over capacity. They often cannot accommodate the number of women who require their services or support, and women and children are being turned away.
If IPV is not officially recognized as an epidemic in Ontario, it will be more difficult to break the barriers that prevent IPV from being effectively and comprehensively addressed.
Ultimately, failing to deem IPV an epidemic will permit the conditions that sustain IPV and femicide to persist - and likely worsen.
Ontario must follow Nova Scotia's lead in declaring IPV an epidemic and pass Bill 173 as soon as possible. The health, safety and lives of women and children are at stake.
CAPTION: A picture of murder victim Nathalie Warmerdam is displayed during a vigil at the Women's Monument in Petawawa, Ont., following the release of jury recommendations at an inquest in Pembroke, Ont., in June 2022. She was once involved with her killer.Mourners hold a candlelit vigil in remembrance of Carol Culleton, Anastasia Kuzyk and Nathalie Warmerdam in Wilno, Ont., in 2015. An inquest into their killings called on governments to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic.
CREDIT: Eden Hoffer

Subject: Domestic violence; Perceptions; Women; Shelters; Murders & murder attempts; Funding; Gender; Femicide

Location: Nova Scotia Canada; Canada

Document 130 of 500

Council to Ford: Stay out of city's bike lanes: 'War on cars' and 'wedge politics' raised during debate about opposing proposed legislation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/council-ford-stay-out-citys-bike-lanes/docview/3129466381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Council+to+Ford%3A+Stay+out+of+city%27s+bike+lanes%3A+%27War+on+cars%27+and+%27wedge+politics%27+raised+during+debate+about+opposing+proposed+legislation&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton will tell Premier Doug Ford to stay out of its bike lanes, but that doesn't mean councillors are pedalling in the same direction when it comes to the city's cycling network.
This week, in a 10-6 vote, council backed a resolution to formally oppose an Ontario government bill that would allow the province to ban new bike lanes that displace existing lanes of automobile traffic.
The recently proposed anti-gridlock legislation would also require cities to secure provincial approval for new cycling infrastructure and give the government power to review and potentially remove existing bike lanes.
Hamilton's expression of opposition is about bike lanes, but also throwing the brakes on an attempt to curb "municipal decision-making and autonomy," said Coun. Craig Cassar, who seconded Coun. Cameron Kroetsch's motion.
Helping people get around the city using modes of transportation other than cars can help them save money by avoiding expenses like repairs and insurance, said Cassar, rhyming off some of the benefits of bike lanes.
"More people on foot and on bike help businesses," added the Ancaster councillor, who regularly rides to city hall. "Cars don't support businesses. People do."
In an op-ed last month, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Ontario needs a "common sense, evidence-based bike lane policy - one that takes all the very real concerns about bike lanes on main streets into account."
"Instead of crowding everyone onto the same streets, a more reasonable bike lane policy would redirect cyclists to quieter streets and neighbourhoods, where they can get around quickly and safely without being jammed in next to cars and trucks."
But the Tory government's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, isn't about bike lanes, Coun. John-Paul Danko said during Wednesday's debate at city hall. In fact, it's about using them "as a distraction from the failures of their government," argued Danko, pointing to a litany of problems ranging from homelessness to health care.
And the Ministry of Transportation (MTO) wouldn't have the capacity or interest to review local cycling master plans in the first place, he said.
"The idea that the MTO would take on this work frankly is ludicrous."
Likewise, Coun. Maureen Wilson argued the province boils down to "wedge politics."
"It's the worst kind of politics with the worst kind of outcomes, and that's death and that's injury and that's also equity."
But expressing official resistance to the Ford's government's proposed legislation could lead to an unintended political collision, warned Coun. Brad Clark.
"Toronto is being targeted and by doing what you're doing you are saying, 'Here's the bull's-eye. Come and get us,'" he said. "So there are times when you step on Superman's cape and there are times when you don't."
Moreover, Clark suggested, councillors were "talking quite openly about a war on automobiles" in a province and city that relies on the interrelated car and steel industries. On a similar note, Coun. Tom Jackson said he was "supportive of union workers who are building vehicles in this province."
Jackson also said the motion "tied in" with council's decision to take what had been a "prudent" plan to expand Hamilton's cycling network and turn it into a $60-million fast-tracked development strategy that he opposed.
Constituents on the east Mountain, meanwhile, "overwhelmingly" appreciate expanded "recreational" bike lanes like along the brow and waterfront, but "not necessarily commuter lanes," he said.
Mayor Andrea Horwath, suggested the "politicization of this issue is happening" in the city hall chambers (and not exclusively at Queen's Park.)
But the fact is council has supported Vision Zero, the city's road-safety action plan, a blueprint that includes bike lanes, she said.
"This is something that affects all municipalities; it's not just the city of Toronto," the mayor said of the Ford government's proposed legislation.
Municipalities "shouldn't have to get permission" to plan their infrastructure, an exercise that works best based on local knowledge and expertise, said Horwath, calling the bill a "slippery slope."
It's important to maintain that autonomy, Kroetsch said, who took exception with Coun. Jeff Beattie's suggestion that the motion was "rather divisive."
Moreover, there's "nothing" in the formal call that wages "a war on cars," he fired back.
Environment Hamilton has joined counterpart organizations in Ottawa, Toronto and Windsor in expressing concern over the proposed legislation, saying it would "severely limit municipalities' ability to protect all road users, fight climate change and ease congestion."
In Hamilton, the Keddy Trail, a "vital" escarpment-climbing connection on the Claremont Access for pedestrians and cyclists, "would potentially not be possible under the proposed legislation, as it required the removal a vehicle lane."
City staff are expected to review Bill 212 and report to council on its potential implications.
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Hamilton council has supported Vision Zero, the city's road-safety action plan, a blueprint that includes bike lanes, Mayor Andrea Horwath says. "More people on foot and on bike help businesses," Coun. Craig Cassar said.hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 131 of 500

Workers say their goodbyes to Ontario Science Centre: The 'magic is gone' from almost-empty building, leftover exhibits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/workers-say-their-goodbyes-ontario-science-centre/docview/3129466377/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Workers+say+their+goodbyes+to+Ontario+Science+Centre%3A+The+%27magic+is+gone%27+from+almost-empty+building%2C+leftover+exhibits&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the sun shone on an unusually warm day at the end of October, Emma Meadley Dunphy took one final, emotional stroll through the Ontario Science Centre.
The centre's volunteer co-ordinator wandered the cavernous spaces trying to soak it all in, before workers had to be out of the building by midday on Oct. 31.
"I kept running into lots of different people who were doing it themselves," she said. "Everybody was clearly having a moment in each of the spaces."
It had been a furious few months of packing up and sending countless truckloads of stuff to storage facilities in numerous locations.
A few things too difficult to move were still in the building. Questions hung in the air, too. Did it really need to be this way? Would they ever come back?
Ontario officials announced the abrupt and permanent closure of the science centre in late June, saying engineers found structural issues with the roof.
The province plans to move the science centre to a new location as part of a revamped Ontario Place.
The move sparked outrage among workers, nearby residents and visitors and has become a political hot potato for Premier Doug Ford at Queen's Park.
On her final day in the building, Meadley Dunphy visited a few places that had been meaningful to her.
She visited the camp room where she worked as a volunteer in high school some 20 years ago.
Then she stopped by the rainforest, or what remains of it.
The animals that lived there - turtles, snakes, fish and poisonous frogs - have been re-homed, while much of the vegetation was ripped out and transplanted at the Toronto Zoo.
Five large trees, too big to move, are still there and should survive, so long as the building's automated watering system continues working and the heat remains on.
On stressful work days, Meadley Dunphy said she would often find herself visiting the rainforest. The dewy, earthy smell relaxed her.
Despite the lack of vegetation, the smell remains. But it's not the same, she said.
"That magic is gone."
Meadley Dunphy spent the past few months winding up the centre's volunteer operations, helping with camps that still ran off-site.
Like most other workers, she also helped pack.
"It's not my normal job, but it's a very sad reminder about what's happening and there's no way to escape that," she said, tearing up.
Before they had to clear out that day, science centre workers had an ice cream party for 28 cleaning staff who were laid off by Dexterra, the company that managed them.
It was their final day on the job. While the pay hadn't been great at $16.50 per hour, the cleaners had union jobs with benefits and a full pension.
Ford pledged to help find them a job "within the system," but that never happened, said Martin Fischer, the president of Local 549 of Ontario Public Service Employees Union, which represents about 500 science centre workers. But they were buoyed by support from the public after news of the layoffs broke in September, he said.
He said he is still holding out hope that workers might someday return to the building.
He pointed to ongoing repair work as the source of that hope. There is scaffolding in the great hall and the auditorium that allows access to the roof, where engineers had identified panels in danger of collapsing. The heating in Building B, which housed many exhibits, theatres and the great hall, has been fixed.
"We do appreciate that the building is being repaired, but, of course, everybody wonders, what for? What is it going to be?" Fischer said. "So, I haven't given up hope that we can return."
The Canadian Press has reached out to the science centre with several questions about the state of affairs. Infrastructure Ontario and Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma's office did not respond to requests for comment.
The province is looking for an interim location for the science centre with its permanent new space set to open in 2028 at the earliest.
The production team, which makes exhibits for science centres around the world, does not have a new home yet. The shop's machines and production materials are stored away in Huntsville, Ont., a three-hour drive north of Toronto, Fischer said.
Other items are stored in facilities in Guelph, northern Toronto and at Sherway Gardens, a mall in western Toronto.
Ward Kennedy can clearly remember his first visit to the science centre in 1970, when he was six years old.
He grew up in nearby Flemingdon Park, so his family visited the centre often.
"I remember the 25-cent submarine sandwiches and the old cafeteria, which was very, very tiny, and I remember the parabolic dishes at either end of one of the buildings where we could talk and our voice would travel across to the other side," Kennedy said. "It's a special place."
He served as a volunteer ham radio operator and helped run the science centre's amateur radio station.
As things wound down this October, Kennedy helped dismantle the radio equipment and send it off to storage.
"There are no plans in the future to install a new amateur radio station at a new Ontario Science Centre," he said.
He listed off more of the items that are gone now, sent away to places across the province.
The Toronto Bee Collective came and took the dozen or so bee hives that are now set up at Black Creek Pioneer Village, he said.
Gone too are the iconic Canadarm and the fin whale skeleton, which he said were especially difficult to take down.
But Kennedy said staff left a few big-ticket items behind, and he isn't sure why. There's a ship's propeller, and the cross-section of a massive Sitka spruce tree trunk.
He choked up as he recalled taking his daughter, years ago, to KidSpark - an interactive environment at the centre for younger children. This past spring, he brought her again, this time with his granddaughter in tow.
"It just meant so much," he said.
"I wish I could have taken them more often because it's a one-of-a-kind building."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press
