Document 132 of 500

Canada's $1-trillion Trump tariff question

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-1-trillion-trump-tariff-question/docview/3129466192/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+%241-trillion+Trump+tariff+question&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a challenge familiar to many businesses: your biggest corporate customer has a new boss who wants to re-evaluate everything.
It's even harder when they start with a bias that everyone wants to rip them off.
That's Canada's challenge as Donald Trump returns to the White House in January.
For business, the campaign was alarming. We witnessed disdain for international alliances, and promises of tariffs and trade wars. We heard anti-immigrant rhetoric and a proposal to deport some 11 million people, which would damage America's economy and risk a migrant crisis at our border. We see America going down an unsustainable, inflationary fiscal path, with deep tax cuts while deficits are at levels usually reserved for recessions or wars.
These things can seem abstract, so let's translate them into everyday realities: Imagine a car costing $8,000 more because of tariffs. Imagine running a business that depends on imports that have become prohibitively expensive. Imagine the agony of shutting down a plant or laying off workers in Ontario, Illinois, Michigan or Pennsylvania.
That's the reality when our two countries have the world's largest bilateral trading relationship. In Ontario alone, some $1 billion of goods and services cross the border every day. Across Canada, it's $3.6 billion a day. That makes the annual economic stakes to the U.S. and Canada more than $1 trillion.
The landscape is harsh but not hopeless. Campaigning and governing are very different processes- even for populists. Further, Canada has economic reality on its side. We have influential U.S. business allies. And we have products, services and capabilities of enormous value.
Here are the top ten things we can do:
Present a united front
In both approach and message, we need alignment between provincial and federal governments, opposition leaders, and the business community.
Together, we have a vast range of relationships - in Washington, in state capitals, with chambers of commerce, and with countless businesses.
Make it 'North America first'
We must consider the world the new administration inherits, and the forces it fears. Canada's strategy - previewed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford - should include proactive proposals to strengthen North America's economy and security at a time of shifting alliances and threats from America's rivals.
It's (still) the economy, stupid
For the second time in eight years, Trump will inherit a thriving U.S. economy.
Job one is not to mess it up just as ordinary people start to feel it. He needs to hear from the business community that America's prosperity depends on an open market that embraces innovation and competition.
The reality of integration
Given global trends and Trump's predispositions, tariffs are inevitable. However, they need not be universal.
Given the integration between our economies and supply chains, there's an airtight case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely, and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement in 2026.
Invest in security
While America's southern border took centre stage in the election, Canada's concern is the one we share - and our own northern border with Russia.
We must strengthen our commitment to North American and global security, and meet our defence spending commitments.
Share our assets
We can make a powerful case that Canada has assets the U.S. needs: energy. Minerals. Nuclear power. AI research excellence. Doing business with us is strategically smarter than the alternatives, notably China.
Be competitive
We must ensure the cost of investing and doing business in Ontario and Canada remains competitive with the U.S. Investment will go where it's wanted, welcomed and rewarded. And with America going through uncertainty, we can attract investment in Canada, and the jobs that come with it.
Attract top talent
Canada must double down on its investments in research at our post-secondary institutions. During the first Trump presidency, we learned that when the U.S. is less welcoming to international research talent, Canada is an outstanding alternative - provided that our institutions have the funding to do the work.
Diversify our markets over time
While every business must look after its biggest customer, it must also grow business elsewhere.
Canada has more trade agreements than any other G7 country; we need to leverage them. Within Canada, we also need to eliminate interprovincial trade barriers, benefiting businesses across the country.
Articulate Canadian values
The best of friends can disagree, so let's be unapologetic in standing for Canadian values.
This does not mean lecturing or vacuous virtue-signalling.
It means acting with humility; being reliable partners in international alliances; celebrating the contributions of immigrant communities; meeting our climate commitments; and upholding democracy, security and the rule of law- particularly through unflagging support for Ukraine.
Imagine, once more, that you're that business worried about its relationship with its biggest customer.
Would you sit and wait for them to make decisions on their own? Or would you take the initiative?
The smart business does the latter- and is more likely to hold the Trump card.
Daniel Tisch is president and
CEO of the Ontario Chamber
of Commerce.
CAPTION: Although Trump tariffs appear inevitable, they needn't be universal, writes Daniel Tisch. There'sa strong case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.- Mexico Agreementin 2026.SeanKilpatrick The CanadianPress file photo

Document 133 of 500

Insiders say Doug Ford will use the spectre of Trump to justify an early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/insiders-say-doug-ford-will-use-spectre-trump/docview/3129215830/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Insiders+say+Doug+Ford+will+use+the+spectre+of+Trump+to+justify+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians are expected to go to the polls this spring in an early election that Premier Doug Ford will contend is necessary due to the spectre of Donald Trump.
Thanks to the economic uncertainty triggered by a second Trump presidency, Ford's planned pitch to voters would be a simple one, Progressive Conservative sources told the Star.
"Stability, competence and someone experienced in dealing with Trump in order to protect jobs in Ontario," said one senior official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss an election strategy that is still being finalized.
While Ford already has a stable majority government and a provincial election is not scheduled until June 2026, his team believes the North American political landscape has so radically shifted that a fresh mandate is required.
The premier's New Democratic, Liberal and Green rivals insist he is just scrambling to get ahead of any charges that could be laid as a result of the RCMP's ongoing criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
They believe he wants to face voters well before that investigation, which began 13 months ago, is concluded.
Whatever the rationale for a snap election, Ford himself has already begun setting the table to make his economic argument in a campaign that could be launched as early as March.
After Trump was elected Nov. 5, the premier was quick to point out that Ontario did $493 billion in two-way trade with the U.S. last year and is "the number one trading partner to 17 states, number two to 11 other states."
"The (president-elect) knows that nine million Americans wake up every morning to produce products to ship to Ontario and we're their third-largest trading partner in the world," Ford told reporters Nov. 6 as he expressed concern about threatened U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods.
"We're going to work with President-elect Trump, as we did at the beginning of the administration," he said, referring to the incoming president's previous tenure in the White House between 2017 and 2021.
When the U.S. slapped tariffs on steel during a 2019 trade war, Ford went to Washington to help the federal Liberal government make Canada's case to then U.S. trade representative Robert Lighthizer, who is expected to again play a key role in the Trump administration.
"It was a federal government that sent me down to negotiate with Bob Lighthizer since we're the engine of Canada," the premier reminded journalists on Nov. 12.
A week earlier, Ford had noted that dispute with the U.S. "didn't last too long, because - when I talked to Chrystia (Freeland, the then foreign minister, and I) went down there, talked to Lighthizer and I went back and said, 'tariff everything' ... they pulled the tariffs off extremely quickly after we did this."
The premier's recent unexpected blast at Mexico, the third partner with Canada and the U.S. in NAFTA, for serving as a "backdoor" to allow artificially cheap Chinese electric vehicles into North America, was also not unhelpful to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government.
That's because, as leader of a subnational jurisdiction, insiders say Ford is free to speak his mind in a way that would be impolitic for Trudeau or Freeland.
"What I'm proposing to the federal government, we do a bilateral trade deal with the U.S. If Mexico wants a bilateral trade deal with Canada, God bless them," said the premier in a Nov. 12 trial balloon that, pointedly, was not shot down by the prime minister.
Indeed, Ford and Trudeau, who presides over a shaky minority government that could be toppled at any time, spoke by phone a few hours after the Mexico gambit.
It is likely that before a spring provincial election call, the premier will travel to Washington to meet with Trump administration officials to further burnish his credentials as a steady hand on the tiller in potentially stormy times.
"This is a smart strategy," said another senior Ontario official. "The premier can speak credibly to the Trump people because he's done so before. He can be a broker to a Liberal government in Ottawa that they (the Trump administration) might not trust."
In that vein, Ford has been appealing to the federal Liberals to delay the implementation of its controversial digital sales tax, a three-per-cent levy on U.S. tech giants like Apple, Google, Meta, Netflix and Amazon, as a show of goodwill toward the Americans.
"They're furious with Canada for even considering what they view as an unfair tax on American companies," he told 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon last month.
"We cannot put millions of Canadian jobs and our historic economic partnership with the U.S. at risk because of a stubborn refusal to listen to the concerns of our American friends."
Such lofty statesmanlike rhetoric aside, Ford is also making more prosaic decisions that signal an election could be called more than one year ahead of schedule.
Quietly, on the afternoon of Nov. 6, with media attention focused on the U.S. presidential contest, his government announced that public funding of Ontario political parties, which was to end Dec. 31, will be extended another two years.
The annual $2.54 per-vote subsidy of parties is paid out in quarterly installments based on the results of the 2022 election.
Every three months, Ford's governing Tories, who handily out-fundraise their rivals, receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000, and the Ontario Party $53,000.
While the premier had long opposed public funding of parties, sources say he changed his tune over concerns the NDP might not be raising enough money to mount a decent campaign that would siphon votes from the Liberals, thereby helping his Tories.
That's because Ford - apparently attempting to play a kind of three-dimensional electoral chess - wants to slightly boost Marit Stiles' official opposition New Democrats, who are currently in third place in public-opinion polls, in order to hinder Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, who sit second in most surveys.
Vote-splitting between the NDP and the Grits is a factor in the Tories winning many ridings across the province.
In 2022, the NDP won 31 seats with 23.7 per cent of the popular vote while the Liberals elected just eight MPPs despite receiving 23.9 per cent. The Greens won one seat with six per cent of the vote. One Independent MPP was elected.
Ford's party took 83 seats in the 124-member legislature with 40.8 per cent of the vote.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends question period as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21, 2024.

Document 134 of 500

HHS staff shortages halt new cancer studies at Juravinski Hospital

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hhs-staff-shortages-halt-new-cancer-studies-at/docview/3128965965/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=HHS+staff+shortages+halt+new+cancer+studies+at+Juravinski+Hospital&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 20 upcoming studies have been put on hold at Juravinski Cancer Centre, prompting concern from patients who rely on the clinical trials for last-ditch treatments.
"I was dumbfounded," said Clive Webb, a cancer patient at Juravinski Hospital who also co-leads Hamilton support group network Myeloma Canada. "People who are on their last treatments normally go for clinical trials so we know how this affects people."
None of the studies had started yet and Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS) did not answer questions about when they will get back on track.
In a statement, the hospital network said the clinical trials had been paused as a result of "temporary staffing shortages."
It's not clear if HHS is currently recruiting the staff needed to launch the trials. The hospital network has a projected deficit of $112 million for the fiscal year that ends March 31 and two unions have said some job openings are not being posted while HHS reviews programs and evaluates the necessity of the positions.
The hospital network has been in a staffing crisis since the pandemic with 1,502 unfilled jobs as of Sept. 1.
The Ontario Nurses' Association (ONA) and the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE) - together representing more than 7,750 staff - have both said many of the job postings have been removed without being filled.
HHS did not answer specific questions about whether jobs needed for the clinical trials are currently posted or if recruitment will take place before the new fiscal year starts April 1.
"HHS is working to build the capacity needed to introduce approximately 20 new clinical research trials that had yet to be assessed or planned, as well as more over time," HHS stated. "We've recently hired seven individuals and recruitment is ongoing."
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said during a stop in Hamilton Wednesday that no front-line patient-facing staff will be laid off at HHS to balance the budget.
"They are not cutting staff," Jones said. "The hospital has made a determination that they will not fill nonclinical, non-frontline staff while they work with the ministry, with regional Ontario Health, to ensure we and they work together to provide the necessary services."
However, CUPE says removing vacancies or failing to post openings "is cutting jobs."
"It's really disingenuous to say there aren't any layoffs," said Michael Hurley, president of CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions. "There often aren't layoffs because there are so many vacancies that when jobs are eliminated, people can be redeployed to a vacancy. But these are huge cuts of staff for this hospital and it's very troubling."
Hurley also took issue with Jones suggesting that nonclinical, non-frontline job openings could be eliminated without affecting necessary services. He said these are the jobs that "keep the hospitals running," such as cleaning, repair and maintenance, sterilization of equipment, scheduling, preparing and delivering food, and working in the pharmacy.
"Her remarks are quite hurtful," said Hurley. "All of these functions are vital. It's so patrician to just suggest that they don't have a meaningful function and can be eliminated. I wouldn't agree with that at all."
St. Joseph's Healthcare is also facing a shortfall this fiscal year, projecting a deficit of $24.3 million on its roughly $800-million budget. St. Joseph's is made up of the Charlton, West Fifth and King campuses.
This is the fourth fiscal year in a row HHS has struggled to balance its $1.6-billion budget. It finished the last fiscal year with a shortfall of $20.4 million after ending the year before with a $31.1 million deficit and was in the red $900,000 the year before that. HHS includes Hamilton General, Juravinski, McMaster Children's, West Lincoln Memorial and St. Peter's hospitals as well as the West End Clinic and other health centres.
"We have insufficient capacity to meet the demand of the public at the moment," Hurley said. "We're going to be pushing back hard because our belief is that Hamilton hospitals are already significantly understaffed and the thought that they could do without hundreds of more staff is really a dangerous notion."
The staff shortages that halted the launch of the 20 clinical trials at HHS, have not affected the 217 studies already underway there with more than 800 participants.
"Patients enrolled in existing research trials are not impacted and, where possible, some patients are being supported to access clinical trials at other hospitals," stated HHS. "We are continually evaluating our ability to support existing trials as well as launch new trials."
Overall, HHS said it is "actively planning and working to significantly expand the clinical trials program."
But in the meantime, Webb worries about what will happen to patients who have run out of treatment options and don't have time to wait out the pause.
It can also be hard on cancer patients to travel to hospitals in other cities such as London and Toronto because a clinical trial is on hold in Hamilton.
"There's people now that we have in our group where the treatment isn't working anymore so they have to find something else," said Webb. "If they have to wait to get into a clinical trial ... that's very stressful."
Webb knows firsthand the difference the research makes, considering advancements made due to clinical trials have given him a much better prognosis since his multiple myeloma returned in September 2023 than in the past. The 66-year-old west Hamilton man was first diagnosed in 2014 with the blood cancer that develops in plasma cells found mainly in the bone marrow. After a stem cell transplant and six years of treatment, he went into remission in 2020.
Webb is back in treatment now and was at Juravinski for chemotherapy recently when he found out the studies were put on hold. It was a particular blow because Myeloma Canada does a walk every year that raises tens of thousands of dollars for cancer studies at Juravinski.
"I was gobsmacked," said Webb. "We raised over $136,000 with 300 people in September and half of that money goes back to Juravinski for research. (Premier Doug) Ford wants to give everybody $200 ... and we're stopping stuff that's potentially saving people's lives."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: The launch of about 20 new studies have been put on hold at Juravinski Cancer Centre.Michael Hurley, President of the Ontario Council of Hospital Unions, which is part of the Canadian Union of Public Employees

Document 135 of 500

It's the $1-trillion economy, stupid: How Canadian business can stickhandle the looming Trump tariff reality

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/1-trillion-economy-stupid-how-canadian-business/docview/3128965887/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+the+%241-trillion+economy%2C+stupid%3A+How+Canadian+business+can+stickhandle+the+looming+Trump+tariff+reality&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a challenge familiar to many businesses: your biggest corporate customer has a new boss who wants to re-evaluate everything.
It's even harder when they start with a bias that everyone wants to rip them off.
That's Canada's challenge as Donald Trump returns to the White House in January.
For business, the campaign was alarming. We witnessed disdain for international alliances, and promises of tariffs and trade wars. We heard anti-immigrant rhetoric and a proposal to deport some 11 million people, which would damage America's economy and risk a migrant crisis at our border. We see America going down an unsustainable, inflationary fiscal path, with deep tax cuts while deficits are at levels usually reserved for recessions or wars.
These things can seem abstract, so let's translate them into everyday realities: Imagine a car costing $8,000 more because of tariffs. Imagine running a business that depends on imports that have become prohibitively expensive. Imagine the agony of shutting down a plant or laying off workers in Ontario, Illinois, Michigan or Pennsylvania.
That's the reality when our two countries have the world's largest bilateral trading relationship. In Ontario alone, some $1 billion of goods and services cross the border every day. Across Canada, it's $3.6 billion a day. That makes the annual economic stakes to the U.S. and Canada more than $1 trillion.
The landscape is harsh but not hopeless. Campaigning and governing are very different processes - even for populists. Further, Canada has economic reality on its side. We have influential U.S. business allies. And we have products, services and capabilities of enormous value.
Here are the top ten things we can do:
Present a united front
In both approach and message, we need alignment between provincial and federal governments, opposition leaders, and the business community.
Together, we have a vast range of relationships - in Washington, in state capitals, with chambers of commerce, and with countless businesses.
Make it 'North America first'
We must consider the world the new administration inherits, and the forces it fears. Canada's strategy - previewed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford - should include proactive proposals to strengthen North America's economy and security at a time of shifting alliances and threats from America's rivals.
It's (still) the economy, stupid
For the second time in eight years, Trump will inherit a thriving U.S. economy.
Job one is not to mess it up just as ordinary people start to feel it. He needs to hear from the business community that America's prosperity depends on an open market that embraces innovation and competition.
The reality of integration
Given global trends and Trump's predispositions, tariffs are inevitable. However, they need not be universal. Given the integration between our economies and supply chains, there's an airtight case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely, and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement in 2026.
Invest in security
While America's southern border took centre stage in the election, Canada's concern is the one we share - and our own northern border with Russia.
We must strengthen our commitment to North American and global security, and meet our defence spending commitments.
Share our assets
We can make a powerful case that Canada has assets the U.S. needs: energy. Minerals. Nuclear power. AI research excellence. Doing business with us is strategically smarter than the alternatives, notably China.
Be competitive
We must ensure the cost of investing and doing business in Ontario and Canada remains competitive with the U.S. Investment will go where it's wanted, welcomed and rewarded. And with America going through uncertainty, we can attract investment in Canada, and the jobs that come with it.
Attract top talent
Canada must double down on its investments in research at our post-secondary institutions. During the first Trump presidency, we learned that when the U.S. is less welcoming to international research talent, Canada is an outstanding alternative - provided that our institutions have the funding to do the work.
Diversify our markets over time
While every business must look after its biggest customer, it must also grow business elsewhere. Canada has more trade agreements than any other G7 country; we need to leverage them. Within Canada, we also need to eliminate interprovincial trade barriers, benefiting businesses across the country.
Articulate Canadian values
The best of friends can disagree, so let's be unapologetic in standing for Canadian values.
This does not mean lecturing or vacuous virtue-signalling.
It means acting with humility; being reliable partners in international alliances; celebrating the contributions of immigrant communities; meeting our climate commitments; and upholding democracy, security and the rule of law - particularly through unflagging support for Ukraine.
Imagine, once more, that you're that business worried about its relationship with its biggest customer.
Would you sit and wait for them to make decisions on their own? Or would you take the initiative?
The smart business does the latter - and is more likely to hold the Trump card.
CAPTION: While Trump tariffs appear inevitable, writes Daniel Tisch, they needn't be universal. There's a strong case that Canada-U.S. trade should flow freely and that there's mutual benefit in renewing the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement in 2026.
CREDIT: Daniel Tisch Contributor

Subject: Political campaigns; Business community; Alliances; Competition; Tariffs

Document 136 of 500

A politician with no enemies? How Lincoln Alexander became Canada's Great Consensus Maker

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/politician-with-no-enemies-how-lincoln-alexander/docview/3128965814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+politician+with+no+enemies%3F+How+Lincoln+Alexander+became+Canada%27s+Great+Consensus+Maker&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've met a lot of politicians in my day and I can honestly say that Lincoln Alexander was the finest by far.
Among his many qualities, he's the only one I ever knew who had no enemies. None. He managed to work his way higher in life without stepping on anyone. Alexander's father, from St. Vincent and the Grenadines, was a CPR sleeping car porter.
His mother, a maid born in Jamaica, insisted her son, born in Toronto, get a good education, telling him, "Go to school, you're a little black boy." He graduated from McMaster University, then Osgoode Hall law school, becoming one of a handful of Black lawyers in Ontario in the 1960s.
But even at Osgoode, he suffered the slur of racism. During an event in his final year, a speaker referred to something as being the equivalent of "looking for a n--r in the woodpile." When questions were invited, Alexander stood up, cited the offensive words, and asked what exactly did the phrase mean? The speaker shook it off by saying that everyone used those words. Alexander told him that as someone in a position of authority, he should be showing leadership, and not be so disrespectful.
Fortunately, voters were more positive. In 1968, Alexander successfully ran for election as a Progressive Conservative in Hamilton, Ont., becoming an MP for twelve years as the first Black member of Parliament and the first Black cabinet minister.
As press secretary to Robert Stanfield in the early 1970s, I attended weekly caucus. At times, debate would get so heated I'd wonder if unity on that topic would ever be possible. And then Alexander, all six-foot-three-inches of him, would rise in his place, and say, "I can sense a consensus emerging here."
I'd think to myself, "Whaaaat?" Alexander would then take a few strands from this diatribe, a few threads from that tirade, and so on, until he had woven together a garment that everyone could wear. The strength of any political party flows from its ability to reach consensus. Alexander's leadership qualities could achieve that goal like few others.
I also watched him from the gallery in the House of Commons on that infamous day in February 1971 when Alexander and John Lundrigan, a Progressive Conservative MP from Newfoundland, questioned prime minister Pierre Trudeau about training programs for the unemployed. Exasperated by their rigorous inquisition, Trudeau mouthed the words "F-k off" at them, claiming later that all he said was "Fuddle duddle." Alexander knew exactly what he said.
In the 1990s, I was involved in organizing an event to honour the philanthropic efforts benefitting Ryerson (now Metropolitan University of Toronto) by Jack Cockwell, president of Brookfield Asset Management. Among my assignments was obtaining a suitable congratulatory message from Alexander. I called him at his Hamilton home, and asked if he would come to Toronto to film a tribute interview in front of the Ontario Legislature where he had served as lieutenant governor for six years.
Just as we were just completing our filming with the pink granite building behind him aglow in the morning sun, two busloads of students arrived, presumably for a tour.
As it turned out, they were from Hamilton. The teachers recognized Alexander and introduced him to the kids who were all about eight or nine years old. The cameraman and I organized everybody into three rows so we could get some additional footage of Alexander surrounded by the children. I then withdrew but even from where I stood about ten metres away, I could hear Alexander saying in such a low voice that it was almost as if he were speaking to himself: "Vote for Alexander, vote for Alexander."
Filming finished, as he bid goodbye to the students, he said, "When you go home tonight, don't forget to tell your parents you saw Linc Alexander today." He was ever the campaigner, even though he hadn't run for public office in about fifteen years.
Subsequently, I'd see him arriving in a wheelchair to watch the Raptors. We always had an enjoyable chat before the game. On other occasions, when I visited my daughter Alison in Hamilton and we'd pick up a few things at Denninger's Foods, we'd see Alexander, having his lunch, constantly interrupted by well-wishers.
On Jan. 21, Lincoln Alexander Day across Canada, a bust of Alexander was unveiled at the Ontario Legislature. It's certainly unusual. Rather than the normal grey, it's taupe with aspects of green. The glasses are too big and the mouth is open rather than closed as is traditional for a bust.
If Alexander were alive, I can't imagine he'd be upset. For most politicians, an ego trip is the only journey worth taking. Not Lincoln Alexander. He cared more about walking with others rather than leading any parade.
CAPTION: Erika and Marissa Alexander, look at a bust of their grandfather, during its unveiling this year on Jan. 21, Lincoln Alexander Day. For most politicians, writes Rod McQueen, an ego trip is the only journey worth taking. Not Alexander, who cared more about walking with others rather than leading any parade.Lincoln Alexander broke through many barriers, writes Rod McQueen, becoming Canada's first Black MP and cabinet minister en route to being made Lt.-Gov. for Ontario in 1985

Document 137 of 500

HHS staff shortages halt new cancer studies: Patient says those needing last-ditch treatment can't wait out temporary pause

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hhs-staff-shortages-halt-new-cancer-studies/docview/3128944045/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=HHS+staff+shortages+halt+new+cancer+studies%3A+Patient+says+those+needing+last-ditch+treatment+can%27t+wait+out+temporary+pause&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 20 upcoming studies have been put on hold at Juravinski Cancer Centre, prompting concern from patients who rely on the clinical trials for last-ditch treatments.
"I was dumbfounded," said Clive Webb, a cancer patient at Juravinski Hospital who also co-leads Hamilton support group network Myeloma Canada. "People who are on their last treatments normally go for clinical trials so we know how this affects people."
None of the studies had started yet and Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS) did not answer questions about when they will get back on track.
In a statement, the hospital network said the clinical trials had been paused as a result of "temporary staffing shortages."
It's not clear if HHS is currently recruiting the staff needed to launch the trials. The hospital network has a projected deficit of $112 million for the fiscal year that ends March 31 and two unions have said some job openings are not being posted while HHS reviews programs and evaluates the necessity of the positions.
The hospital network has been in a staffing crisis since the pandemic with 1,502 unfilled jobs as of Sept. 1.
The Ontario Nurses' Association (ONA) and the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE) - together representing more than 7,750 staff - have both said many of the job postings have been removed without being filled.
HHS did not answer specific questions about whether jobs needed for the clinical trials are currently posted or if recruitment will take place before the new fiscal year starts April 1.
"HHS is working to build the capacity needed to introduce approximately 20 new clinical research trials that had yet to be assessed or planned, as well as more over time," HHS stated. "We've recently hired seven individuals and recruitment is ongoing."
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said during a stop in Hamilton Wednesday that no front-line, patient-facing staff will be laid off at HHS to balance the budget.
"They are not cutting staff," Jones said. "The hospital has made a determination that they will not fill non-clinical, non-frontline staff while they work with the ministry, with regional Ontario Health, to ensure we and they work together to provide the necessary services."
However, CUPE says removing vacancies or failing to post openings "is cutting jobs."
"It's really disingenuous to say there aren't any layoffs," said Michael Hurley, president of CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions. "There often aren't layoffs because there are so many vacancies that when jobs are eliminated, people can be redeployed to a vacancy. But these are huge cuts of staff for this hospital and it's very troubling."
Hurley also took issue with Jones suggesting that nonclinical, non-frontline job openings could be eliminated without affecting necessary services. He said these are the jobs that "keep the hospitals running," such as cleaning, repair and maintenance, sterilization of equipment, scheduling, preparing and delivering food, and working in the pharmacy.
"Her remarks are quite hurtful," said Hurley. "All of these functions are vital. It's so patrician to just suggest that they don't have a meaningful function and can be eliminated. I wouldn't agree with that at all."
St. Joseph's Healthcare is also facing a shortfall this fiscal year, projecting a deficit of $24.3 million on its roughly $800-million budget. St. Joseph's is made up of the Charlton, West Fifth and King campuses.
This is the fourth fiscal year in a row HHS has struggled to balance its $1.6-billion budget. It finished the last fiscal year with a shortfall of $20.4 million after ending the year before with a $31.1-million deficit and was in the red $900,000 the year before that. HHS includes Hamilton General, Juravinski, McMaster Children's, West Lincoln Memorial and St. Peter's hospitals as well as the West End Clinic and other health centres.
"We have insufficient capacity to meet the demand of the public at the moment," Hurley said. "We're going to be pushing back hard because our belief is that Hamilton hospitals are already significantly understaffed and the thought that they could do without hundreds of more staff is really a dangerous notion."
The staff shortages that halted the launch of the 20 clinical trials at HHS, have not affected the 217 studies already underway there with more than 800 participants.
"Patients enrolled in existing research trials are not impacted and, where possible, some patients are being supported to access clinical trials at other hospitals," stated HHS. "We are continually evaluating our ability to support existing trials as well as launch new trials."
Overall, HHS said it is "actively planning and working to significantly expand the clinical trials program."
But in the meantime, Webb worries about what will happen to patients who have run out of treatment options and don't have time to wait out the pause.
It can also be hard on cancer patients to travel to hospitals in other cities such as London and Toronto because a clinical trial is on hold in Hamilton.
"There's people now that we have in our group where the treatment isn't working anymore so they have to find something else," said Webb. "If they have to wait to get into a clinical trial ... that's very stressful."
Webb knows firsthand the difference the research makes, considering advancements made due to clinical trials have given him a much better prognosis since his multiple myeloma returned in September 2023 than in the past. The 66-year-old west Hamilton man was first diagnosed in 2014 with the blood cancer that develops in plasma cells found mainly in the bone marrow. After a stem cell transplant and six years of treatment, he went into remission in 2020.
Webb is back in treatment now and was at Juravinski for chemotherapy recently when he found out the studies were put on hold. It was a particular blow because Myeloma Canada does a walk every year that raises tens of thousands of dollars for cancer studies at Juravinski.
"I was gobsmacked," said Webb. "We raised over $136,000 with 300 people in September and half of that money goes back to Juravinski for research. (Premier Doug) Ford wants to give everybody $200 ... and we're stopping stuff that's potentially saving people's lives."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Spectator.
jfrketich@thespec.com

Document 138 of 500

Former student sues HWDSB for $5M over alleged sexual assaults: School board and now-retired teacher deny allegations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-student-sues-hwdsb-5m-over-alleged-sexual/docview/3128943510/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+student+sues+HWDSB+for+%245M+over+alleged+sexual+assaults%3A+School+board+and+now-retired+teacher+deny+allegations&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A former student, who alleges he was sexually assaulted by a now-retired teacher in the early 1990s, is suing Hamilton's public school board for $5 million.
The plaintiff alleges in the Jan. 31 claim the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board "was negligent and failed in its duty" to care for him after he was allegedly sexually assaulted by teacher John Hashimoto while a student at Dr. J.E. Davey Elementary School downtown.
Both the board and Hashimoto deny the allegations.
The plaintiff said in the claim Hashimoto "repeatedly sexually, verbally and physically abused, assaulted and molested" him, including fondling his clothed penis, scrotum and buttocks, and engaged in other sexual activities.
Hashimoto used his "position of authority and trust" to develop a "close personal relationship" with the plaintiff, who was then approximately nine years old, the claim reads.
"The relationship that Hashimoto developed with the plaintiff ..., under guise of a teacher, allowed Hashimoto an opportunity to be alone with the plaintiff ... and exert influence and control over him, prey upon him, and sexually abuse him," the claim reads.
The plaintiff alleges the school board taught students "teachers can be trusted" and "created an opportunity for Hashimoto to exert power and authority."
The board, according to the claim, failed to adequately supervise and train staff, including Hashimoto, educate students about such behaviours, investigate Hashimoto's background before hiring him and have a proper reporting system.
The board also fostered a culture "whereby the reporting of such deviant behaviour of an employee, teacher and/or principal by its students would be considered to be 'wrong'" and was "wilfully blind" to Hashimoto's behaviour, the claim reads.
The plaintiff says he's suffered damages, including physical pain, shame, depression and impairment of his ability "to experience a normal adolescence and adulthood."
In 2023, Hashimoto pleaded guilty in criminal court to one count of child luring and two counts of sexual interference involving victims who were under 14.
He admitted in court to sexually touching two boys in separate incidents between 1991 and 2003.
While Hashimoto was a teacher at Dr. Davey, he touched a 10-year-old boy while reviewing school work, and again at Hashimoto's house on their way to soccer, which the teacher had signed the boy up for, The Spectator previously reported.
He also admitted to touching a young player he'd befriended while working as a volunteer baseball coach.
The plaintiff in the civil case is one of the victims in the criminal case, and can't be named due to a publication ban.
In a June 27 statement of defence, the school board denied there was "any breach of a duty of care," stating that it "appreciated, fulfilled and exceeded" its obligations, acting in a "competent manner, and in accordance with the standard of care of a reasonably prudent board of education."
The board confirmed in the statement Hashimoto was employed from 1990 to 2019, and that he taught at Dr. Davey from 1990 to 1999.
The Spectator previously reported that Hashimoto retired in 2019 after a nearly 30-year teaching career during which he also taught at Billy Green Elementary School, Queensdale Elementary School and Sherwood Secondary School.
The board denied any knowledge of alleged actions of behaviours "or that it attempted, desired or took any steps to cover up the alleged abuse," the statement reads.
When contacted by The Spectator, the board declined further comment.
In a third party defence filed July 23, Hashimoto denied the sexual assault allegations and that the plaintiff sustained "damages or losses."
"Specifically, Mr. Hashimoto denies that he engaged in any act of sexual misconduct, assault, abuse, interference, or molestation as alleged in the statement of claim," the document reads.
In the statement, Hashimoto pleaded the action is "barred" because Ontario's limitations act, which states that proceedings must be started within two years of discovery of the claim. He also requested the claim be dismissed.
Lawyers for neither the plaintiff nor Hashimoto responded immediately to requests for comment.
Kate McCullough is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. kmccullough@thespec.com
CAPTION: A former student alleges in a statement of claim he was sexually assaulted by teacher John Hashimoto, pictured here, in the 1990s and that the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board was negligent. The board and Hashimoto deny the allegations.

CREDIT: Kate McCullough Reporter

Document 139 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3128943487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Swift ticket prices are unbelievable
Re: How Swifties were
short-shrifted by their
hero, Nov. 14
I cannot believe the amount of money kids or their parents are paying for tickets and merchandise to attend this concert. I can't imagine what my father/mother would have said to me had I asked them for money to attend a concert of this magnitude given my father was on strike more than he was working trying to feed three healthy boys. Wow, things have changed and I'm not sure for the better. What are we teaching our kids? Money grows on trees?
Ralph Corning, Stoney Creek
Trustees' Italy
saga shameful
Re: Italy-bound school trustees stayed in Germany to recover from jet lag, says board chair, Nov. 13
As a practising Catholic and visual arts grad, I am troubled by the "reasons" given for huge expenses to buy a statue of St. Padre Pio.
This dishonours this saint's life story, a Capuchin priest who took a vow of poverty at the age of 15, and lived it until his death at 81. He had painful, unhealing wounds on his hands and body (stigmata marks) like the body of the crucified Jesus. The sculpture studio's website has two interpretations of the saint: A. the opulent garb of the press photo, with a hint of the stigmata; and B. a modest Capuchin habit, wearing the gloves that bandaged his hands. The trustees chose A.
In this climate of political and economic unrest, B. is more suitable for a high school chapel. Furthermore, there are esthetically pleasing and modestly priced life-size versions of B. available from North American suppliers.
The trustees complained of jet lag and delays while travelling first class, but were sightseeing the second day in Munich. It appears the system was "gamed" both in expense limits and approval protocols. Shame on them; if they hold the Catholic belief of saints being omnipresent intercessors, I'd like them to reflect on what St. Padre Pio would think.
Christine Suchy, Simcoe
Shopping must
be exhausting
Gosh, it's so exhausting to shop for European religious art, isn't it? Surely the Lord would understand the need for lavish hotels and meals for those trustees who sacrificed themselves to travel for the quest of an artistic acquisition.
Surely Jesus, whose parents had to sleep in a barn to give birth to him, would complete be OK with this, ahem, art-seeking holy trip. Yes?
Ana Long, Hamilton
Offering up
a bit of sarcasm
One of your fine journalists, Matthew Van Dongen, in writing about the current Catholic school trustee issue, has unfortunately made a serious spelling error. As a dedicated reader and one who truly values print media, I feel I have an obligation to draw your attention to my concern.
He begins the story with "Embattled school trustees ...," where the word "embattled" should accurately and correctly be spelled "entitled." Perhaps even pronounced as such.
Gayle Dyment, Ancaster
Why not use
student art?
Rick Petrella, chair of the Brant Hamilton Norfolk Catholic District School Board, should offer more than explanations to parents, staff and students. More questions have been left unanswered, re: an unnecessary trip to buy art for a new school. Was it the only option to spend over $100,000 to obtain Italian art in a time where there are more urgent educational needs?
I would have considered a contest inviting all students to submit their own art for the new school. As a former teacher, I can guarantee that students would have presented works of quality.
Such an approach would have given the school a more personal touch and made the students proud.
Pierre Drouin, Ancaster
Looking for
some jet lag tips
I did some research into jet lag recovery techniques. I could not find fine dining, alcohol or sightseeing on any of the tip lists. Perhaps we should send our "trustees" on more trips so they can hone their jet lag recovery skills. Oops, I forgot. We don't send them. They send themselves.
Jake Ireland, Simcoe
We can coexist with railways
Re: Hamiltonians in 1853
celebrated a noisy railway yard that opened in their backyard. In the 2020s? Not so much, Nov. 12
As always, I enjoyed Mark McNeil's recent Flashbacks column on the history of railways in Hamilton. His insight and information help us to appreciate Hamilton's past with hopes for preserving heritage going forward.
Sadly, although the railways contributed to the building of Canada, lately they are an impediment to progress on housing. CN Rail has concerns about noise, vibration, etc.
Yet, incredibly, people living there have dealt with these issues in the past. Now, it seems the railway cannot handle the odd complaint?
History is repeating itself. In the 1990s, CP Rail objected to housing across from Chedoke Golf Course for the same reasons - noise, etc. However, people living in the townhomes knew exactly what they were getting into as the rail yard was right next door. And builders made some design changes that minimized exposure and windows that faced the rail yard, mitigating noise issues.
Hamilton should take a cue from the very successful Regent Park project in Toronto. Built in the 1940s and poorly designed with substandard housing, a redevelopment over the past decade has transformed this once sketchy neighbourhood into an attractive, award-winning development that is the envy of cities worldwide for its progressive ideas and affordability. Displaced tenants were promised they could return at comparable rents they paid pre-development, which many did.
Railways are under federal jurisdiction, so I would urge our MPs to do some arm-twisting to get the railway to back off its frivolous claims and get on with building this badly needed affordable housing project ASAP.
Grant Ranalli, Hamilton
We just don't
see eye to eye
Re: Where's the pro-Trump voice? Nov. 13
Well, I'm sure you will find your answer south of the border, where they belong. It is their country, their voice, their vote.
I thought the Spectator was gracious and very smart in its approach in dialogue and space given to Donald Trump's electoral win. If you received the New York Times in your Saturday Spectator, you would have found all the Trump hoopla that you are longing to hear.
We are in Canada, not America. What was needed to be conveyed was done and done with class. Your berating is of no consequence or blame to the Spectator. Perhaps your fellow Canadians just don't see eye to eye with Trump. The border is not so far a hike if the grass looks greener.
Pam Rathbone, Hamilton
Trump voice is where it belongs
The short answer to the question "Where's the pro-Trump voice?" is: "down in the States." You know, where he won an election.
Janice Beamer will find Donald Trump is far less popular up here, and neither the Hamilton Spectator, nor anyone else, is obliged to pretend there's an even split. Nor are they obliged to pay equal attention to Joe Biden's alleged wrongdoings, as unlike Trump, he has yet to be convicted of any of them in court.
Steve Dylag, Burlington
We're in for
a rough ride
It is beyond me how any Canadian can be celebrating Donald Trump's election victory. Come Jan. 20, Canada will be in for a long, rough ride. The Trump administration will have absolutely no interest in what we believe to be our "mutual interests." Its mantra will be "U.S.A. first, midst and last." The idea of "old friendship" will mean nothing to the new president. As far as he is concerned, friends, whether individuals or countries, are there to be used and then discarded.
The message from our federal government so far has been "not to worry; everything will be fine." Meanwhile, Premier Doug Ford has floated the idea that Canada should abandon Mexico and the CUSMA in order to negotiate a new trade agreement between us and the U.S., as if America would not hesitate to jump at the chance to divide and conquer in the next round of trade negotiations.
In 1891's "Canada and the Canadian Question," Goldwin Smith advocated for the assimilation of Canada into the United States. I fully expect to hear echoes of Smith as the challenges to our economy, political structures and social fabric mount over the next couple of years.
Those of us who still believe in the ideal of an independent country on the northern half of our continent will have to be prepared to advocate tirelessly for a Canada united a mari usque ad mare (from sea to sea).
Jim Young, Dundas

Document 140 of 500

On Trump, Ford's instincts are correct

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-trump-fords-instincts-are-correct/docview/3128943430/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+Trump%2C+Ford%27s+instincts+are+correct&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So it turns out Doug Ford is basically right. A lot of people jumped on the premier this week for pointing out that Mexico is the weak link among the Three Amigos in the North American trading partnership and suggesting Canada should work out its own two-way deal with the United States.
Ford, they said, is straying out of his lane. He's freelancing instead of acting as a loyal member of "Team Canada" in pushing back against Donald Trump's America First-ism. But Ford was just saying out loud what others are thinking. Trump blames Mexico for allowing itself to be used as a backdoor for cheap Chinese imports into the U.S., but all he's doing is shouting what the Biden administration says in more polite tones.
If Ford was way off base, you'd expect the Trudeau government to point that out quite firmly. But it didn't. On the contrary, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland conceded the premier's main point. She said she's heard those worries about Mexico not being "aligned" against China and added: "I think those are legitimate concerns for our American partners and neighbours to have. Those are concerns that I share."
Freeland, no surprise, wouldn't be drawn into speculating about a separate Canada-U.S. deal somewhere down the road. But it might eventually come to that if Mexico doesn't clean up its act. And in the meantime it's useful for Ford to send a strong signal to Trump that he shouldn't lump Canada (Ontario, really) in with Mexico as he goes about putting up a high tariff wall against China.
In fact, Mexico was always an awkward fit with Canada and the U.S. in a continental trade deal. Part of the idea was that investment would flow into Mexico, speed development and raise wages there. But three decades after NAFTA went into effect the wage gap between Mexico and the U.S./Canada is as wide as ever.
Canada and the United States are natural partners, with similar standards of living and complementary economies. That's the message our governments have always pressed home with Washington and will continue to push with Trump. Adding Mexico to the mix muddies the waters, and it's quite possible the three-way deal won't survive a second Trump presidency.
We knew Trump and the people around him don't particularly care for Canada and its government: too lefty, sanctimonious, "woke," etc. But it still comes as a shock to hear the depth of contempt they have for this country and for Justin Trudeau in particular.
Most of the big names Trump nominated this week for top positions have had things to say about Canada, and it's not good. Mike Waltz, his pick for national security adviser, tweeted that Pierre Poilievre will "send Trudeau packing in 2025 (finally) and start digging Canada out of the progressive mess it's in." And Tulsi Gabbard, his nominee as director of national intelligence, has called Trudeau "the autocratic leader in Canada who has resorted to genuinely authoritarian and tyrannical means" during the pandemic.
There's more: Trump's new "border czar," Tom Homan, calls the U.S. northern border a "huge national security issue."
All of which adds up to a generalized mistrust bordering on contempt for Canada and its leaders coming from the team Trump is forming around him. If Poilievre wins the next election, the likes of Waltz and Gabbard won't have Trudeau to kick around anymore. But there really does seem to be a deeper disdain for this country that goes way beyond disagreements over policy. No doubt that's partly just the mean-spirited attitude Trump and his loyalists feel toward anyone or any country that doesn't fall into line behind them. There's probably not much any Canadian government can do about that.
The experts will point out that the economic fundamentals mean Canada and the U.S. will keep doing business, regardless. But the early signs for the relationship are, if anything, worse than expected. No wonder Ford is scrambling to get on the good side of Team Trump.
Andrew Phillips is a staff columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Subject: National security; Decades

Location: China; Mexico; United States--US; Canada

Title: On Trump, Ford's instincts are correct

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

Publication date: Nov 16, 2024

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Was it impossible to obtain such art in our own backyard, for example Toronto?

Such an approach would have given the school a more personal touch and made the students proud. The chair and the four trustees should tender their resignation. Apologies and refunding of expenses are not the solution.

Well, I'm sure you will find your answer south of the border, where they belong. It is their country, their voice, their vote.

Steve Dylag, Burlington
A cringeworthy proposal
A Spectator reader questions why now that Donald Trump has won the presidency that the newspaper doesn't publish more positive things about him. I will write a glowing review about how wonderful a human being he is when I see a public display of affection between him and the first lady where Melania doesn't cringe when he tries to kiss her.
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
We're in for a rough ride
It is beyond me how any Canadian can be celebrating Donald Trump's recent election victory. Come Jan. 20, Canada will be in for a long, rough ride. The Trump administration will have absolutely no interest in what we fondly believe to be our "mutual interests." Its mantra will be "U.S.A. first, midst and last." The idea of "old friendship" will mean nothing to the new president. As far as he is concerned, friends, whether individuals or countries, are there to be used and then discarded.
The message from our federal government so far has been "not to worry; everything will be fine." I imagine a similar message was broadcast on the Titanic. Meanwhile, Premier Doug Ford has floated the idea that Canada should abandon Mexico and the CUSMA in order to negotiate a new trade agreement between us and the U.S., as if America would not hesitate to jump at the chance to divide and conquer in the next round of trade negotiations. It could then hammer Mexico and deal with us at leisure.
In 1891's "Canada and the Canadian Question," Goldwin Smith advocated for the assimilation of Canada into the United States. I fully expect to hear echoes of Smith as the challenges to our economy, political structures and social fabric mount over the next couple of years. Those of us who still believe in the ideal of an independent country on the northern half of our continent will have to be prepared to advocate tirelessly for a Canada united a mari usque ad mare (from sea to sea).
Jim Young, Dundas
We're losing our heritage
Re: Horwath fumes at collapse of 'neglected' Gore Park buildings, Nov. 13
Once again, city council has proven it doesn't care about our heritage buildings. It is sad that once again developers were allowed to leave the Gore Park buildings in such a state of neglect that now the heritage designation does not matter. The owners will get to demolish the buildings, including the heritage facades, and put monstrosities up in place of these once beautiful buildings. Much like the once beautiful church on James and Hunter, it is sad to walk by these buildings every day knowing the next generations will never know we had such wonderful heritage.
One day, decades from now, I will walk those same streets and very few old buildings will remain. I am glad that Whitehern and Dundurn Castle were made into museums. I shudder to think what would happen if the city sold those buildings to the highest bidder and let these parts of our history deteriorate into states of disrepair. As our city becomes vastly unaffordable for many of us, we should at least still have some beautiful heritage buildings to remind us of when Hamilton was a bustling city filled with people who worked hard every day at their trades, while enjoying beautiful views.
Aha Blume, Hamilton
We can coexist with railways
Re: Hamiltonians in 1853 celebrated a noisy railway yard that opened in their backyard. In the 2020s? Not so much, Nov. 12

So let me get this straight. After Hamilton elects an NDP leader as mayor, buildings in the core are literally falling down into the street, tent cities are taking over Hamilton parks where children use to play and a cyberattack does millions of dollars of damage. I can't wait to see what Andrea is going to do for an encore!
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.

Document 142 of 500

Bike lanes a 'distraction' from 'failures of their government': Councillors don't want Doug Ford meddling in city business

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bike-lanes-distraction-failures-their-government/docview/3128943410/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bike+lanes+a+%27distraction%27+from+%27failures+of+their+government%27%3A+Councillors+don%27t+want+Doug+Ford+meddling+in+city+business&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton will tell Premier Doug Ford to stay out of its bike lanes, but that doesn't mean councillors are pedalling in the same direction when it comes to the city's cycling network.
This week, in a 10-6 vote, council backed a resolution to formally oppose an Ontario government bill that would allow the province to ban new bike lanes that displace existing lanes of automobile traffic.
The recently proposed anti-gridlock legislation would also require cities to secure provincial approval for new cycling infrastructure and give the government power to review and potentially remove existing bike lanes.
Hamilton's expression of opposition is about bike lanes, but also throwing the brakes on an attempt to curb "municipal decision-making and autonomy," said Coun. Craig Cassar, who seconded Coun. Cameron Kroetsch's motion.
Helping people get around the city using modes of transportation other than cars can help them save money by avoiding expenses like repairs and insurance, said Cassar, rhyming off some of the benefits of bike lanes.
"More people on foot and on bike help businesses," added the Ancaster councillor, who regularly rides to city hall. "Cars don't support businesses. People do."
In an op-ed last month, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Ontario needs a "common sense, evidence-based bike lane policy - one that takes all the very real concerns about bike lanes on main streets into account."
"Instead of crowding everyone onto the same streets, a more reasonable bike lane policy would redirect cyclists to quieter streets and neighbourhoods, where they can get around quickly and safely without being jammed in next to cars and trucks."
But the Tory government's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, isn't about bike lanes, Coun. John-Paul Danko said during Wednesday's debate at city hall.
In fact, it's is about using them "as a distraction from the failures of their government," argued Danko, pointing to a litany of problems ranging from homelessness to health care.
And the Ministry of Transportation (MTO) wouldn't have the capacity or interest to review local cycling master plans in the first place, he said.
"The idea that the MTO would take on this work frankly is ludicrous."
Likewise, Coun. Maureen Wilson argued the province boils down to "wedge politics."
"It's the worst kind of politics with the worst kind of outcomes, and that's death and that's injury and that's also equity."
But expressing official resistance to the Ford's government's proposed legislation could lead to an unintended political collision, warned Coun. Brad Clark.
"Toronto is being targeted and by doing what you're doing you are saying, 'Here's the bull's-eye. Come and get us,'" he said. "So there are times when you step on Superman's cape and there are times when you don't."
Moreover, Clark suggested, councillors were "talking quite openly about a war on automobiles" in a province and city that relies on the interrelated car and steel industries.
On a similar note, Coun. Tom Jackson said he was "supportive of union workers who are building vehicles in this province."
Jackson also said the motion "tied in" with council's decision to take what had been a "prudent" plan to expand Hamilton's cycling network and turn it into a $60-million fast-tracked development strategy that he opposed.
Constituents on the east Mountain, meanwhile, "overwhelmingly" appreciate expanded "recreational" bike lanes like along the brow and waterfront, but "not necessarily commuter lanes," he said.
Mayor Andrea Horwath, suggested the "politicization of this issue is happening" in the city hall chambers (and not exclusively at Queen's Park.)
But the fact is council has supported Vision Zero, the city's road-safety action plan, a blueprint that includes bike lanes, she said.
"This is something that affects all municipalities; it's not just the City of Toronto," the mayor said of the Ford government's proposed legislation.
Municipalities "shouldn't have to get permission" to plan their infrastructure, an exercise that works best based on local knowledge and expertise, said Horwath, calling the bill a "slippery slope."
It's important to maintain that autonomy, Kroetsch said, who took exception with Coun. Jeff Beattie's suggestion that the motion was "rather divisive."
Moreover, there's "nothing" in the formal call that wages "a war on cars," he fired back.
Environment Hamilton has joined counterpart organizations in Ottawa, Toronto and Windsor in expressing concern over the proposed legislation, saying it would "severely limit municipalities' ability to protect all road users, fight climate change and ease congestion."
In Hamilton, the Keddy Trail, a "vital" escarpment-climbing connection on the Claremont Access for pedestrians and cyclists, "would potentially not be possible under the proposed legislation, as it required the removal a vehicle lane."
City staff are expected to review Bill 212 and report to council on its potential implications.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: "More people on foot and on bike help businesses," Coun. Craig Cassar said."Toronto is being targeted and by doing what you're doing you are saying, 'Here's the bull's-eye. Come and get us,'" Coun. Brad Clark said in opposition to a motion that expresses opposition to a provincial anti-gridlock bill.Hamilton council has supported Vision Zero,the city's road-safety action plan, a blueprint that includes bike lanes, Mayor Andrea Horwath says.
CREDIT: Teviah Moro, Reporter

Document 143 of 500

Nowhere to relocate those living in tents

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nowhere-relocate-those-living-tents/docview/3128943269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nowhere+to+relocate+those+living+in+tents&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We know that homelessness in Ontario is a crisis. Encampments are in every community. But a small group of Ontario mayors have chosen a dark path to address the problem - one that should never even be taken, let alone entertained.
Two weeks ago, 13 mayors - from Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Guelph, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury, and Windsor - wrote a letter to the premier asking him to use the Charter of Rights and Freedom's notwithstanding clause to bypass the court system and allow them to evict encampments - even when there is nowhere else for people to go.
For those of us on the front lines, we truly have nowhere to refer people. We've given up on searching for shelter beds because they're full. Same with voluntary detox and crisis beds.
And housing? Anyone we've put on the list for social housing languishes for years - sometimes for more than a decade. Sometimes they don't live to see their number come up.
Workers like me know what people need: affordable housing, decent shelter in the interim and voluntary supports to stay healthy and well. These don't exist. And this is the case in Toronto, where I work, as it is for every municipality across the province.
It's why the 444 members of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario are calling for solutions like investing in deeply affordable housing and supportive housing and expanding access to voluntary and evidence-based treatment.
But instead of responding to these sensible requests, Premier Doug Ford is entertaining a handful of mayors - now expanded to 15 mayors, including Midland and St. Thomas - who would rather remove homeless people's Charter-protected rights.
These mayors are referencing a 2023 ruling in Waterloo. In this decision, the court ruled that clearing the Waterloo encampment would be unconstitutional: the city couldn't remove the encampment without providing adequate shelter beds, and to do so would be a violation of homeless people's Charter-protected right to life, liberty, and security of the person.
There is no human right more foundational than the right to life, and no ask more extreme than removing this right from an entire demographic group - homeless people.
In fact, using the notwithstanding clause to override the right to life for homeless people would take away the responsibility of Ontario cities for homelessness altogether - for housing, for shelter, everything.
Nothing about this would make the issue of encampments better, it would mean fewer resources available to a growing number of Ontarians - 234,000 and counting - who are currently or at risk of homelessness. The problem would become more prolific, more visible, and ultimately more criminalized in public spaces.
The request is too ludicrous to be entertained. We elect our leaders to do difficult work, including solving the housing and homelessness crisis - not to take away the Charter rights of those most impacted by the crisis.
These 13 individual mayors do not represent the majority of their mayoral colleagues, let alone their respective councils and constituents, none of whom were consulted on their letter. The premier can't seriously consider their request - it's undemocratic and un-Canadian.
Indeed, the issue is bigger than just homelessness: this is about protecting the integrity of the Charter and the foundations of Canadian society.
If we fully remove the right to life for one group of people, who's next? What does the Charter mean if the elected leaders of the day can blithely abuse the notwithstanding clause - which was meant to be used in the rarest circumstances - to take away the very rights the Charter protects?
These are big questions and they supersede political affiliation. Whether you support encampment residents, or you want them gone from your communities, or you care about the implications to your rights, everything about this request is reckless, abusive, and dangerous.
To these 15 mayors and the premier I say: solve the homelessness crisis. Protect the Charter. And do your damned jobs.
Diana Chan McNally
is a front-line worker
supporting homeless people
in Toronto.
CAPTION: Using the notwithstanding clause to vacate encampments would take away the responsibility of Ontario cities for homelessness altogether, writes Diana Chan McNally.Cathie CowardThe Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Diana Chan McNally;
