Document 156 of 500

Nov. 14: Forever grateful for Mount Nemo, investigate building collapse and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-14-forever-grateful-mount-nemo-investigate/docview/3128295450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+14%3A+Forever+grateful+for+Mount+Nemo%2C+investigate+building+collapse+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Forever grateful for Mount Nemo
Re: Staff mourn closure of Burlington senior's home that was like a 'big family,' Nov. 8
I felt immense sadness after reading about the closure of Mount Nemo Christian Nursing Home in Burlington. Losing these long-term-care beds will have a significant impact on already lengthy wait lists. It was unfortunate that the fire sprinkler upgrade required by the province proved to be an impossibility, due to their reliance on well water.
Our family benefited from the loving and compassionate care our father received at Mount Nemo. The staff definitely made a difference in the lives of all their residents as they provided care with dignity and respect. We will be forever grateful for the staff and thank you for 40 years of exemplary care.
Nancy Dingwall, Hamilton
Investigate downtown building collapse
Re: Horwath fumes at collapse of 'neglected' Gore Park buildings, Nov. 11
So, forces of both neglect and gravity brought down some very old structures in downtown Hamilton. How convenient for would-be developers who have probably eyed these aged buildings for some time. When a building simply caves in on itself, some type of investigation is absolutely essential and warranted.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
Demand for change underestimated
The authors of "Dreading a repeat performance" and "Will Canada be next?" (Spec letters on Nov. 8 and 9 respectively) and others involved in the ongoing public and media rhetoric following the recent U.S. election should remember that America had a choice between a rock and a hard place, as polls prior to the election indicated a majority considered neither candidate suitable for the role of president.
Both writers note concern at the spread of right-wing rhetoric, but seemingly have no concern for the divisive left-wing rhetoric that has influenced Liberal policies for the past nine years and resulted in a Canada that many no longer recognize and are so concerned they have resorted to calling for the prime minister to resign and call an early election.
More often than not, elections are won by voting out a party rather than voting in a party and this was maybe the case in the U.S. which chose to vote out a left-wing government with out-of-touch policies and priorities that persistently failed to read the room. Sound familiar Canada?
And those who repeatedly insist Pierre Poilievre offers "little in the way of clear policy," I would suggest have purposely not been listening.
Leslie Farrington, Ancaster
Poilievre is no Trump clone
Re: Will Canada be next? Nov. 9
I read a recent Spectator letter from a writer who suggests Pierre Poilievre is a Donald Trump clone. How absurd that statement is. This fear-based conjecture is totally unfounded. Most Americans realize Trump leans to the extreme right, whereas our Canadian Conservative party is centre-right leaning, supporting a market economy, social populism and free enterprise. How can you criticize Poilievre when he hasn't had a chance to govern yet? He is a sharp, well spoken and honest politician, simply trying to put Canada on solid ground. Let's give him that chance.
The Trudeau government has been plagued with scandals. Justin Trudeau has been the first elected PM to break federal conflict of interest rules and even the Auditor General of Canada reported that Trudeau has turned the Sustainable Development Technology program into a slush fund. The most recent polls indicate that we are in for a much needed change.
Allan Avery, Binbrook
Cycling deserves more respect
It seems that a lot of people in Hamilton think that the bicycle lanes are for "passion" cycling or for fun. However, being a middle-aged female all-year all-weather cyclist, I can assure you that most people using the Cannon bike lanes are going to work. It's their mode of transportation and in fact, is a right and not a privilege like driving a car is. My bike is my car.
People need to read a few traffic studies. More roads, more lanes, more catering to "The Car" only creates something called "induced demand." The phenomenon sees the new roads and lanes just fill up with cars and the gridlock remains. It is not a solution.
To really truly get rid of gridlock and traffic jams, the actual proven solution is to create other ways of transportation like public transit (buses, light rail, subways) and provide for other ways, like bike lanes. A great side effect is that you will get in shape! Sorry, but this city could really use some exercise.
Cycling is also proven to be great for the local economy. The $1,000 I don't spend on a car is spent locally (as I can't drive to the big box outlets). We also have to think of the fact that more and more people simply cannot afford a car or even a monthly transit pass. Are you going to drive them to work or let them sit on welfare?
Natasha Huyer, Hamilton
War a waste of needed funds
Wars rage in the Middle East, Ukraine and elsewhere around the world. To think that the world could ever be free of conflict seems a quaint notion.
Politicians, like Vladimir Putin, calculating, power hungry, egotistical men, initiate wars for purely political or ideological reasons or their own enrichment. Once started, the burden of fighting then falls on ordinary young men and women who are sacrificed to the war machine as cannon fodder.
Wars exact incalculable, unjustifiable costs on human life, but the cost to the planet, and all of its inhabitants, human and non-human alike, is sometimes lost in the discussion. In 2022, military spending worldwide was $2.24 trillion. The increase of 3.7 per cent largely attributable to the war in Ukraine. Good for the military-industrial complex, not so great for the rest of us.
Money used to wage war is money lost to improve the lives of ordinary people, money that could be used to fight poverty, homelessness, hunger, disease, improve education and raise living standards. If we must wage war let it be against our common enemy, climate change, an existential threat to all mankind.
Wayne Poole, Dundas
Bring fairness to fee structure
The College of Nurses of Ontario (CNO) is the governing body for registered nurses in Ontario. The Office of the Fairness Commissioner (OFC) oversees the registration practises of the college to ensure they are transparent, objective, impartial and fair for anyone who wants to work as a nurse or practical nurse in this province.
I am an RPN, and have been a member in good standing for over 30 years. During this time, I have witnessed the prices of our annual membership quadruple. I am aware, as my colleagues are, that we face increases every year. However, this year left many of us outraged at the price tag attached to our being able to work. What I am referring to is the $400-plus price required by CNO to be paid by the end of December, or we don't work, as our licence will be suspended.
Nurse Practitioners (NP), Registered Nurses (RN) and Registered Practical Nurses (RPN) are all governed under this body. With the average hourly wage of an NP ranging from $60 to 64 per hour, compared to that of an RN, which ranges from $27 to $48-plus per hour and the RPN, ranging from $27 to $38 per hour, how can anyone call the $400 membership fee (across the board) fair?
Susanne Swayze, Hamilton
'I am with CN on this'
Re: 'Intransigent' CN still blocking Jamesville project, mayor says, Nov. 11
The recent stories about the delay in the new housing next to the rail yard has vilified CN. I am with CN on this. That rail yard has existed for more than 150 years. It has been a part of Hamilton and the business community. So how is it newcomers to a community could dictate rules for the railroad when the railroad was there first? In Mississauga there is a subdivision along the flight path of the north runway of Pearson. You want a house there, you understand planes come and go. In Hamilton's case, the trains were there first. Accept it or don't live there.
Kevin Argue, Jordan Station
CAPTION: Caretaker Rob Hogeveen, left, and PSWs Karen Griffin, right, and Dora Nsiah, centre, pack the closing Mount Nemo Christian Nursing Home. The staff made a difference in the lives of all their residents as they provided care with dignity and respect, a reader writes.

Document 157 of 500

Council denies call for 'notwithstanding clause' to deal with encampments: City politician ejected from meeting during tense debate over measure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/council-denies-call-notwithstanding-clause-deal/docview/3128140306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Council+denies+call+for+%27notwithstanding+clause%27+to+deal+with+encampments%3A+City+politician+ejected+from+meeting+during+tense+debate+over+measure&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton council has rejected asking the province to consider using "any tools available" to bar encampments from parks, including the use of the "notwithstanding clause" if necessary.
After a pitched debate Wednesday, city politicians landed on a 9-6 vote to dismiss the call, but not before the expulsion of Coun. Matt Francis from the meeting after Mayor Andrea Horwath complained about his "inappropriate" remarks.
Francis denied that he'd impugned fellow city officials in speaking to his motion and refused to apologize, prompting Coun. Brad Clark, stepping in as chair to stickhandle the conflict, to have him leave the meeting.
The fireworks stem from Premier Doug Ford's recent suggestion that mayors ask him to use the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms clause to deal with tents in parks as municipalities face encampment-related court cases.
Several mayors have taken Ford up on his invitation and signed a letter to the province asking it to pass tougher laws and use the Charter mechanism, which allows for legislation that quashes certain rights, if need be.
In speaking to his motion, Francis argued the city has "signalled to all the homeless people" in Ontario that "Hamilton is a place to come and camp," a reference to an encampment protocol that allows tents in public spaces subject to certain spatial restrictions.
Instead of "being so intent on standing up for drug addicts" who live in parks, council should uphold the "rights of everyday taxpayers to use the amenities that they pay for," Francis said before Horwath interjected, flagging a "point of order."
The mayor contended the Ward 5 councillor unfairly suggested that city officials have "certain motivations" as they tackle the encampment issue, which was "inappropriate."
In turn, Clark told Francis he shouldn't "be impugning the motives" of his colleagues, a suggestion he denied.
"I will not apologize for standing up for the rights of women, mothers, children and families to use their parks safely," Francis said before leaving the chambers.
The political drama plays out as Hamilton and other cities struggle to respond to people living in public spaces, as shelter beds fill, the supply of affordable housing runs short, rents spike and social-assistance rates lag.
Of roughly 1,600 people who are homeless in Hamilton, about 300 live outside, as the city grapples with the overlapping crises of untreated mental illness and addiction to toxic street drugs.
As a $18.3-million stopgap, the city is creating 192 additional temporary shelter spaces and 40 "micro shelters" for up to 80 people that are to be situated on a municipally owned brownfield off Barton Street West with an array of services and 24-7 staffing before the end of the year.
Meanwhile, a human rights-based legal challenge against the city over how it has approached encampments has factored into council's consideration of changes to the protocol as the court date approaches in December.
That litigation challenges the constitutionality of bylaws that bar tents in parks and other public spaces and argues enforcement violates human rights, notably amid a shortage of adequate shelter spaces.
During Wednesday's debate, Horwath said city officials have consistently expressed that the province needs to step up when it comes to housing, mental health and addiction services as the municipality shoulders too much of the multimillion-dollar burden.
But wielding the notwithstanding clause "doesn't put a lick of money" into responding to those problems and ultimately does nothing to "help us out of the crisis," she added.
No, it won't "immediately solve the issue," but it could be used "to give parks back to the everyday taxpayer," argued Coun. Mike Spadafora, who was left to shepherd Francis's motion after he was ejected.
The outcome, however, will be more "people in harm's way," by forcing them into further seclusion, Coun. Cameron Kroetsch said.
In an email, Ford's office told The Spectator the government "will explore every legal tool available" to it in order "to clear encampments and restore safely to public spaces."
"While cities remain at the forefront of issues relating to municipally owned lands, we are examining which additional tools the province can provide to help municipalities effectively manage these ongoing challenges," spokesperson Grace Lee added without providing details.
Amber Ross, a 34-year-old who lives outside at Bayfront Park, said she struggles to understand how the notwithstanding clause will help.
"Well, I just don't understand where we're all going to go," said Ross during a news conference organized by the Hamilton Encampment Support Network at the park Tuesday.
Enforcement will just shuffle people from one spot to another, she said. "Some of us are going to have to go under a bridge somewhere."
As it stands, Ross and others living at Bayfront will have to move, owing to a council decision to bar tents at the park and others within a one-kilometre radius of the eventual modular units at the Barton West property and with the provision of the extra shelter beds.
Those micro shelters aren't a solution, either, she said, expressing concern about contamination on the vacant property and sharing the space with so many people.
"We have more room where we are now, and we have more freedom where are now," Ross said.
The city has received a consultant's report that notes measures to mitigate contamination at the former industrial site, said Michelle Baird, director of housing services.
"Those plans are underway and the mitigation steps are being put in place," said Baird, noting they involve paving the site and laying an impermeable barrier underneath.
The Spectator has requested a copy of the report.
Kristen Baker, who also lives at Bayfront, said she, too, is concerned about the contamination and grouping of dozens of people at the same site.
But the heated micro shelters and showers would be beneficial for her, she said.
"For now, until I can get housed," said Baker, 49, who noted she relies on a disability pension to get by. "Even if it's short term."
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Coun. Matt Francis made no apologies for remarks he made while introducing a motion Wednesday asking the province to consider using the "notwith-standing clause" to deal with encampments.Amber Ross speaks about her experience living at Bayfront Park during a news conference on Tuesday, as James Lambert, a member of the Hamilton Encampment Support Network, listens.Teviah Moro The Hamilton Spectator

Document 158 of 500

This Donald Trump ally thinks Canada's relationship with the U.S. will continue to be strong. Here's why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-donald-trump-ally-thinks-canadas/docview/3128139396/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+Donald+Trump+ally+thinks+Canada%27s+relationship+with+the+U.S.+will+continue+to+be+strong.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Chrystia Freeland has called him a "proof point" of the enduring Canada-U.S. relationship. Fortune Magazine has asked if he could be the Elon Musk of steel.
But Lourenco Goncalves says Canada's trade partnership with the U.S. can thrive during Donald Trump's second administration because of the close ties between the two nations.
"I really have conviction that the economic, political and social relationship between our two countries is the real deal," he said in an interview with the Star.
Goncalves is the chairman, CEO and president of Cleveland-Cliffs, a major steel producer in the U.S. that just this month completed a purchase of Canadian steel company Stelco. He said he's committed to keeping Stelco's name and the company's headquarters in Hamilton.
Born in Brazil, Goncalves rose through the metals industry there before moving to several U.S. firms.
When he took over Cleveland-Cliffs in 2014, the company was a small player, but is now the largest flat rolled steel company in North America.
During the first Trump administration, Canada faced tariffs on steel and aluminum that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau called insulting. On the campaign trail this time, Trump pledged ten per cent tariffs on all goods entering the U.S.
The day after Trump's re-election, Freeland, Canada's finance minister, said Goncalves' acquisition of Stelco is proof that the trade relationship will be fine.
"He has been a long-standing supporter of President Trump. He chose to acquire a major Canadian steelmaker. Now, would he do that if he did not believe in the fundamentals of the economic relationship between our two countries? I don't think so."
Cleveland-Cliffs' political action committee has supported both Democratic and Republican candidates. In an interview on CNBC just days before the 2024 election, he said both Trump and Vice-President Kamala Harris understand the value of his industry.
"As far as steel goes, we are going to be OK no matter who is the next president of the United States."
Goncalves told the Star Freeland is representing him appropriately, and that Trump's tough talk on trade shouldn't apply to Canada.
"Canada and the U.S. should not even be in the conversation when we talk about other countries. We see the two countries really as the only close ones that you could claim in this complicated world," he said.
He said Canada's economy is closely connected to the U.S. and also has critical minerals America needs, which it is currently buying from unfriendly countries like Russia and China.
"I feel like it's good practice to carve out Canada, but it's not a favour, it's a necessity."
Despite that close relationship, many of Trump's early picks for his new administration have been highly critical of Canada.
Congresswoman Elise Stefanik, who has been picked as UN ambassador, said Canada wasn't living up to its trade obligations. Mike Waltz, Trump's choice for national security adviser, cheered a clip of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre attacking Trudeau in question period, writing, "This guy is going to send Trudeau packing in 2025."
Trump's likely pick for Secretary of State, Marco Rubio, was highly critical of Canada's program to allow Gazans to come to Canada on temporary visas.
Goncalves said he feels the people around Trump understand that on trade, Canada is different, and highlighted Ottawa's decision to match U.S. tariffs on Chinese steel, aluminum and electric vehicles.
"That's the type of thing that I believe the future Trump administration will appreciate," he said.
Goncalves said too many U.S. allies say the right things, but their actions try to undercut the U.S. economy.
"We don't like countries that talk out of both sides of their mouths," he said, singling out Mexico and Japan as two U.S. allies that speak positively about the U.S., but make economic moves that hurt America's economy.
"These fake friends or friends of convenience will not have a good time when President Trump goes back to the White House."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday that Canada should consider a new trade agreement with the U.S. alone, leaving Mexico out of the trade deal. Mexico has not matched Canada and U.S. tariffs on steel, aluminum and EVs and Ford argued they were being used to skirt tariffs.
"If Mexico won't fight transshipment by, at the very least, matching Canadian and American tariffs on Chinese imports, they shouldn't have a seat at the table or enjoy access to the largest economy in the world," he said on social media.
Goncalves said Ford's suggestion is in line with what the new Trump administration will want.
"That's actually extremely in sync with Donald Trump's playbook. He doesn't like multilateral trade agreements. He likes bilateral trade agreements."
Freeland was more circumspect, but in a news conference Wednesday said she has heard similar concerns about Mexico from people in the Trump administration and she shares them.
"I have heard from them, actually. I've heard also from the Biden administration, and I've heard also from U.S. business leaders some real concerns about whether Mexico is fully aligned when it comes to its policies vis-à-vis China."
CAPTION: U.S. President-elect Donald Trump speaks at a House Republicans Conference meeting at the Hyatt Regency on Capitol Hill on Nov. 13, 2024 in Washington, DC.

Document 159 of 500

Doug Ford wants to rip up bike lanes - but how many people in Toronto actually cycle?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-rip-up-bike-lanes-how-many-people/docview/3127646463/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+rip+up+bike+lanes+-+but+how+many+people+in+Toronto+actually+cycle%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's often said that numbers don't lie. But do they bike to work, or do they drive?
Since the Ford government announced plans to limit new bike lanes and "review" some of Toronto's existing ones, one number has received a lot of scrutiny: how many Torontonians actually cycle. Wildly different statistics have been being tossed out. Those who go looking could come up with numbers as low as one per cent, or as high as 70 per cent, depending on the source.
Why are these numbers so different - and what's the most accurate one? The answer, and how it changes going forward, will influence not just the current debate over bike lanes and gridlock, but policies as consequential as the city of Toronto's plan to fight the climate crisis.
Taking the narrow view
Since Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives unveiled legislation last month that would limit new bike lanes and review existing ones, he and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria have returned to the same statistic: that only 1.2 per cent of Toronto residents commute by bike. The Star has also cited this number.
The statistic comes from a sterling source, but it has three problems: It is old, and it is - stay with us - both overly broad and overly narrow.
Here's the 'old' part: Statistics Canada surveys how Canadians get to their jobs through the census, which is collected every five years. The 1.2 per cent number comes from the 2011 version of this survey - three census cycles ago, and a time, some have pointed out, before Toronto had any significant biking infrastructure.
It's unclear why Ford and Sarkaria have touted this statistic when the most current one, from the 2021 census, might seem to prove their point better. After ticking up slightly in the 2016 census to 1.4 per cent, the number of cyclist commuters in the Toronto region dipped to 0.97 per cent in 2021.
So is that the best number? That brings us to the 'broad' part. This data comes from a geographic region that Statistics Canada calls a "census metropolitan area." Toronto's CMA covers an enormous swath around the city proper. It includes a number of small towns and their semirural surroundings, such as Uxbridge and King, which have 10 cyclist commuters each. It also includes big, car-centric suburbs such as Brampton, where Sarkaria's riding is, and where the cycling rate is just a notch above zero - 0.1 per cent of its 238,000 commuters bike.
Luckily for us, Statistics Canada has also calculated the same figure for what we would call the city of Toronto proper (and what StatCan, bless its heart, would call a "census subdivision," not to be confused with the above-mentioned "census metropolitan area"). Excluding all those suburban and exurban areas and looking just at Toronto, the percentage of cyclist commuters in 2021 was two per cent, or about 16,000 people.
But wait. Now comes the 'narrow' part. The question on the long-form census is very specific: It asks workers to choose their "main mode of commuting," and they can only pick one. If you bike to a GO station, you choose the mode you used for longest distance (probably the GO train). If you drive from fall to spring but cycle in the summer, you pick the mode you used the first week of May (probably car).
To capture more information, the 2021 survey added an extra question on commuters' other modes of transportation. Counting these sometimes-cyclists nearly doubles the number: In 2021, 3.8 per cent, or nearly 30,000 commuters in the city of Toronto used a bicycle as one of their modes of commuting.
Thank you for joining us on this ride: If you want to use a Statistics Canada source for the number of Toronto residents who cycle to work, the best one is probably 3.8 per cent.
But with apologies to the good and rigorous folks at Statistics Canada, there is reason to think we should keep looking for our answer.
Cyclists are more than just commuters
If we're counting cyclists, who qualifies as one? Getting to work isn't the only reason people hop on a bicycle.
Different polls have tried to tackle a more expansive definition of a "cyclist," which bumps the number higher still.
In 2019, the city of Toronto commissioned Nanos to undertake a cycling-related survey, which sampled 1,516 people across all parts of the city on their bike habits. The "70 per cent" figure is from this poll: Nanos found that seven in 10 respondents cycled for some reason, whether for fitness or recreation, getting to school or work, to go shopping or visiting friends.
Like the 0.97 per cent statistic from StatCan, this number isn't wrong, it's just the result of asking the question in a particular way: In this survey, someone who bikes for fun a couple of times a year counts as much as someone who cycles to work daily.
Then, there is yet another survey - perhaps the Holy Grail of transportation surveys.
For almost 40 years, the Ministry of Transportation and a group of municipalities and transit agencies have partnered on the Transportation Tomorrow Survey. Collected every five years, these are "the largest travel surveys ever undertaken anywhere," according to the University of Toronto data management group. The 2016 version involved more than 160,000 interviews.
The survey asks every household to count all trips in the past 24 hours, and are a richly detailed source of information.
One thing it shows is how varied residents' transportation choices are, even within the city of Toronto. In Etobicoke and North York, during the morning rush hour, only eight per cent of respondents either walked or cycled (both modes of "active transportation" are bundled together). Within the old city of Toronto borders, that number more than triples, to 26 per cent. One of the city's climate goals is for three-quarters of school and work trips under five kilometres to be walked, biked or by transit by 2030.
Statistics Canada's data also shows enormous variation between neighbourhoods. Some Toronto census tracts in Palmerston-Little Italy, the Annex and Casa Loma have bike-commuting rates between 15 and 19 per cent, compared to the regionwide one per cent. (The highest is the census tract that covers the Toronto Islands, where personal cars aren't allowed: 43 per cent. Census tracts with no cyclist commuters are overrepresented in the city's north, including more than 10 in north Etobicoke, which Ford represents.)
These figures were taken from the 2016 Transportation Tomorrow Survey, but another survey was completed in 2022 after being delayed by the pandemic. Those results were supposed to be published in "fall 2024," which should be about now. Asked when the results would be released, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Transportation said that "the target date for releasing the Transportation Tomorrow 2022 survey data and reports is still 2024."
As we wait, it's worth asking what number would settle this debate - if any would.
Whatever the true number of cyclists, it will not be bigger than the 61 per cent of Torontonians and 76 per cent in the region who drive to work. Those who oppose bike lanes will likely still argue cyclists are a minority whose interests are being prioritized above the much bigger majority who drive, and who are stuck in productivity-depressing gridlock. Those who support bike lanes will probably say we will never find our way out of the congestion problem, or make a dent our contributions to the climate crisis, or stop seeing cyclists killed on our roads, without creating a safe and efficient cycling network that encourages residents to ditch their cars for bikes.
with files from David Rider
CAPTION: The bike lanes on University Avenue are in the cross-hairs of Premier Doug Ford's Bill 212, along with those on Yonge and Bloor Streets.

Subject: Automobiles; Polls & surveys; Statistics; Commuting; Censuses; Bicycles; Bicycling

Company / organization: Name: Statistics Canada; NAICS: 926110

Nov. 13: It's shameful to demolish the Tivoli, legion branches need to adapt and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-13-shameful-demolish-tivoli-legion-branches/docview/3127645876/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+13%3A+It%27s+shameful+to+demolish+the+Tivoli%2C+legion+branches+need+to+adapt+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shameful to demolish the Tivoli
Re: 'Demolition is the only viable option': Is this the final curtain call for Hamilton's Tivoli Theatre? Nov. 11
How sad and such a shame to wake up to the news, on top of everything else, that the city is once again going to swing the sledgehammer down on another part of history and demolish the once beautiful Tivoli theatre. Probably to put in place more huge, ugly, concrete condo towers that none of the homeless, incidentally, are going to afford to live in. Once again Hamilton proves to one and all that it doesn't appreciate preserving its heritage, just more consultations with one committee after another. Yet another embarrassment for Hamilton. Shame on all of you at city hall as well as the owners of the Tivoli, Aventus Development Corporation.
Lisa Johnston, Stoney Creek
Fond memories of theatre
Oh, what a great shame! It would have been so nice if it could have been updated through the years. I remember its huge screen and very big auditorium - and the balconies! It was such a great way to see a movie. It is truly missed.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
Legion branches need to adapt
Re: Some legion branches struggle to keep doors open, Nov. 7
My husband and I are in our 50s and we have tried to support our legions in the Hamilton area. Their members are aging, however, they appear reluctant to make changes to attract younger people. The music provided at dances appears to be for those 70 and over. On several occasions we have suggested they provide music and other forms of entertainment that would appeal to those in their 40s, 50s and 60s. We have also asked for at least some healthier food options. Many people are trying to eat healthier these days and don't want hot dogs and greasy fish and chips. We really do want to support the legion, but they have to be open to change.
Rose Balmoral, Hamilton
Health care should be priority
Re: Unions say $136-million deficit at Hamilton hospitals puts patients at risk, Nov. 11
Talk about a mismanagement of taxpayers' dollars! The rhetoric spilling out of Health Minister Sylvia Jones mouth is laughable. Doug Ford and his team need to wake up and listen to the taxpayers of Ontario: fix the health-care system first. No tunnel. No spa for the rich. No cheques for every person in Ontario. And definitely no LRT in Hamilton. Put our money where it is needed the most first. If one does not have their health, they have nothing!
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Sad to see posters removed
On Saturday we spent the afternoon taping posters on the poles up and down Locke Street to advertise our parish bazaar. By Sunday, the posters had been removed - presumably by the city.
The sign of a vibrant community is grassroots organizing of community events. Posters are a key way to get the message across. The visible signs of city involvement in the most creative and dynamic neighbourhoods across Hamilton are parking tickets, poster removal and ever-increasing business taxes. It's a shame.
Colleen Francisci, Hamilton
Where's the pro-Trump voice?
I'm tired of the Trump trashing in the Hamilton Spectator. I thought there would be a more democratic dialogue showing both sides of the election, not just one opinion and one opinion only. Is the Spec so biased they can't find a journalist that can say something positive or can report on the Joe Biden failures?
Janice Beamer, Hamilton
A question for Trump supporters
A question for Donald Trump supporters: What would Trump have to do to lose your vote? We know that it's not for being convicted of a sexual offence; it's not for asking another world leader for damaging information on a political opponent; it's not for telephoning a governor asking to find more votes; it's not for inciting a mob to attack the Capitol; it's not for using the Department of Justice to go after political rivals; it's not for denying the results of a fair election; it's not for continuous lying; it's not for appointing judges who overturned Roe v. Wade; it's not for using the office of the presidency to enrich himself and his family.
Again, what would it take for Trump to lose your vote?
Lou Masi, Hamilton
CAPTION: Aventus hopes to redevelop the site of the former Tivoli Theatre into high-rise towers with elements that pay homage to the downtown heritage landmark.

Document 161 of 500

Former student sues HWDSB for $5M over alleged sexual assaults

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-student-sues-hwdsb-5m-over-alleged-sexual/docview/3127645820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+student+sues+HWDSB+for+%245M+over+alleged+sexual+assaults&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A former student, who alleges he was sexually assaulted by a now-retired teacher in the early 1990s, is suing Hamilton's public school board for $5 million.
The plaintiff alleges in the Jan. 31 claim the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board "was negligent and failed in its duty" to care for him after he was allegedly sexually assaulted by teacher John Hashimoto while a student at Dr. J.E. Davey Elementary School downtown.
Both the board and Hashimoto deny the allegations.
The plaintiff said in the claim Hashimoto "repeatedly sexually, verbally and physically abused, assaulted and molested" him, including fondling his clothed penis, scrotum and buttocks, and engaged in other sexual activities.
Hashimoto used his "position of authority and trust" to develop a "close personal relationship" with the plaintiff, who was then approximately nine years old, the claim reads.
"The relationship that Hashimoto developed with the plaintiff ..., under guise of a teacher, allowed Hashimoto an opportunity to be alone with the plaintiff ... and exert influence and control over him, prey upon him, and sexually abuse him," the claim reads.
The plaintiff alleges the school board taught students "teachers can be trusted" and "created an opportunity for Hashimoto to exert power and authority."
The board, according to the claim, failed to adequately supervise and train staff, including Hashimoto, educate students about such behaviours, investigate Hashimoto's background before hiring him and have a proper reporting system.
The board also fostered a culture "whereby the reporting of such deviant behaviour of an employee, teacher and/or principal by its students would be considered to be 'wrong'" and was "wilfully blind" to Hashimoto's behaviour, the claim reads.
The plaintiff says he's suffered damages, including physical pain, shame, depression and impairment of his ability "to experience a normal adolescence and adulthood."
In 2023, Hashimoto pleaded guilty in criminal court to one count of child luring and two counts of sexual interference involving victims who were under 14.
He admitted in court to sexually touching two boys in separate incidents between 1991 and 2003.
While Hashimoto was a teacher at Dr. Davey, he touched a 10-year-old boy while reviewing school work, and again at Hashimoto's house on their way to soccer, which the teacher had signed the boy up for, The Spectator previously reported.
He also admitted to touching a young player he'd befriended while working as a volunteer baseball coach.
The plaintiff in the civil case is one of the victims in the criminal case, and can't be named due to a publication ban.
In a June 27 statement of defence, the school board denied there was "any breach of a duty of care," stating that it "appreciated, fulfilled and exceeded" its obligations, acting in a "competent manner, and in accordance with the standard of care of a reasonably prudent board of education."
The board confirmed in the statement in Hashimoto was employed from 1990 to 2019, and that he taught at Dr. Davey from 1990 to 1999.
The Spectator previously reported that Hashimoto retired in 2019 after a nearly 30-year teaching career during which he also taught at Billy Green Elementary School, Queensdale Elementary School and Sherwood Secondary School.
The board denied any knowledge of alleged actions of behaviours "or that it attempted, desired or took any steps to cover up the alleged abuse," the statement reads.
When contacted by The Spectator, the board declined further comment.
In a third party defence filed July 23, Hashimoto denied the sexual assault allegations and that the plaintiff sustained "damages or losses."
"Specifically, Mr. Hashimoto denies that he engaged in any act of sexual misconduct, assault, abuse, interference, or molestation as alleged in the statement of claim," the document reads.
In the statement, Hashimoto pleaded the action is "barred" because Ontario's limitations act, which states that proceedings must be started within two years of discovery of the claim. He also requested the claim be dismissed.
Lawyers for neither the plaintiff nor Hashimoto responded immediately to requests for comment.
Kate McCullough is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. kmccullough@thespec.com
CAPTION: A former student alleges in a statement of claim he was sexually assaulted by teacher John Hashimoto, pictured here, in the 1990s and that the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board was negligent. The board and Hashimoto deny the allegations.A former student who alleges he was sexually assaulted by a teacher in the 1990s is suing the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board for $5 million. The board and teacher deny the allegations.

Subject: Behavior; Students; Sex crimes; Elementary schools; School boards

Title: Former student sues HWDSB for $5M over alleged sexual assaults

Document 162 of 500

Premier Ford is taking us back to the '50s

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/premier-ford-is-taking-us-back-50s/docview/3127645814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Ford+is+taking+us+back+to+the+%2750s&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford seems determined to take Ontario back to the 1950s.
Back to the time when homes were tucked away in the suburbs, jobs were in the big cities, and workers commuted back and forth in their big cars.
The current government in Ontario seems to have missed the news that communities are healthier, more equitable and sustainable, and less expensive to service and build when they are designed to support public transit, with walkable neighbourhoods that include sidewalks and separated bike lanes.
Traffic congestion is a huge problem in southern Ontario - particularly in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area (GTHA). It costs people and businesses time and money. It increases air pollution, lung and heart diseases, premature deaths, and health care costs. And it increases greenhouse gas emissions that are fuelling climate change.
In the land use planning and transportation sector, it has long been understood that we cannot solve traffic congestion unless we reduce demand. To accomplish that, we must design and build our communities so we can easily get to where we want to go without needing a car.
This means designing our communities so they support efficient transit service that provides people with easy access to popular destinations. It requires developing walkable neighbourhoods that put homes in proximity to common amenities such as restaurants, schools, parks and grocery stores. And it means building complete roads that make it safe and pleasant for people to walk, cycle, and drive.
As public health professionals, we understand that land use planners and transportation engineers know how to do this; they just need permission and the authority to do it. But we also know that when we do these things, we can create healthier and more equitable communities.
An efficient public transit system can make jobs, schools, essential services and recreational opportunities more affordable and more accessible to people of all ages, income levels and abilities. It also increases levels of physical activity and reduces air pollution, both of which decrease negative health impacts and health care costs.
Walkable neighbourhoods increase levels of physical activity, decrease chronic diseases such as diabetes and cardiovascular disease, and prevent premature deaths. They also encourage social interaction which is good for mental health.
Active transportation infrastructure, such as separated bike lanes, pedestrian bridges and sidewalks, encourage people to cycle and walk as modes of transportation. This increases levels of physical activity. It reduces vehicle-related injuries and deaths. It reduces air pollution. And it also increases independent and affordable access to jobs, schools and services.
Walkable and transit-supportive communities with safe and connected active transportation infrastructure improve health, foster equity, and fight climate change. They also reduce traffic congestion, save taxpayers money and reduce pressure on farmland and natural areas.
Premier Ford, we don't want to back to the '50s.
CAPTION: We must design and build our communities so we can easily get to where we want to go without needing a car, Kim Perrotta and Ronald Macfarlane write.
CREDIT: Kim Perrotta and Ronald Macfarlane

Subject: Air pollution; Exercise; Health care expenditures; Land use; Public transportation; Outdoor air quality; Neighborhoods; Traffic congestion; Premature mortality

Business indexing term: Subject: Land use

Title: Premier Ford is taking us back to the '50s

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 13, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Language of publication: English

A question for Trump backers
A question for Donald Trump supporters: What would Trump have to do to lose your vote?
We know that it's not for being convicted of a sexual offence; it's not for asking another world leader for damaging information on a political opponent; it's not for telephoning a governor asking to find more votes; it's not for inciting a mob to attack the Capitol; it's not for using the Department of Justice to go after political rivals; it's not for denying the results of a fair election; it's not for continuous lying; it's not for appointing judges who overturned Roe v. Wade; it's not for using the office of the presidency to enrich himself and his family.
Again, what would it take for Trump to lose your vote?
Lou Masi, Hamilton

Document 165 of 500

Why is it so expensive to see Swift?: Some resale tickets selling for 20 times the presale price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-is-so-expensive-see-swift/docview/3127514857/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+it+so+expensive+to+see+Swift%3F%3A+Some+resale+tickets+selling+for+20+times+the+presale+price&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before Taylor Swift tickets went on presale, Maha Ansari asked about 10 family members and friends to sign up as verified fans on her behalf to help secure tickets. A year later, she is still on the wait-list.
While checking secondary ticketing platforms and Facebook groups every day, Ansari, a Toronto lawyer who lives minutes away from Rogers Centre, was put off by the skyrocketing prices of resale tickets.
For Swift's first show in Toronto on Thursday, tickets for seats in the upper bowl were offered for at least $4,000 on StubHub, more than 20 times the presale price.
"I still don't think I can justify spending $4,000 on one night of my life to see a concert," said Ansari.
With a budget of $2,000, her hopes grew dim as the concert date drew closer. Ansari said she and many fans desperate to see the star continued to receive scam messages on social media.
"As a Swiftie, I am heartbroken ... As a lawyer, I'm disappointed in the Canadian government, Ontario's government, their failure to really regulate the ticket resale and the ticket pricing market," said Ansari.
Ontario's previous Liberal government introduced legislation in 2018 to outlaw computer "bots" that scoop up huge blocks of tickets and cap resale markups at 50 per cent above face value. However, the resale markup cap was scrapped after Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives came into office.
Ansari said she was disheartened that not much progress had been made on the legal front since the cap was lifted.
On the other hand, she saw Swifties flying overseas to Europe to see the superstar perform as they found the resale tickets there less costly and there is legislation against for-profit resale.
Is the removal of the resale cap to blame for the high resale prices?
Supply and demand explain the exorbitant price of the resale tickets in the secondary market, said Catherine Moore, a music professor at the University of Toronto.
What's different from previous shows, Moore added, is that Toronto and Vancouver dates are the last two shows in The Eras Tour.
Kelly Cronin from New Hampshire is one of the American fans who want to secure a Toronto ticket. She saw Swift perform in Los Angeles last year by buying a last-minute resale ticket at $850 (U.S.).
"The appeal to go again is that she changed her set list," said Cronin.
Cronin booked the hotel and flight to Toronto but hasn't found a ticket that fit her $2,500 budget.
While many fans are in favour of a cap on the price of resale tickets, researchers warned that in practice, people can always find ways to get around the legislation.
When overpriced tickets can't be sold on resale platforms, transactions can easily flow into a grey market where ticket sellers charge extra, and people agree to higher prices, said Pascal Courty, an economist at the University of Victoria.
In a statement, Ticketmaster said it believes capping concert resale prices is the only way to remove the incentive, adding that the company supports further regulations.
The government "requires those who facilitate the resale of a ticket for more than its face value to provide the purchaser with either a confirmation from the primary seller that the ticket is valid or a money-back guarantee," said Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement spokesperson Matthew D'Amico.
For-profit ticket resale is banned in some European countries.
While for-profit ticket resales are not banned in Germany, event promoters can include a clause in the ticket's terms and conditions prohibiting resale above 25 per cent of its face value. These tickets must be personalized with a box on the ticket for the buyer's name.
When the Tories reviewed the Ticket Sales Act in 2019, they kept the bot prohibition intact and increased fines for non-compliance with the act from $10,000 (Canadian) to $25,000, but it's hard to tell how effective the ban has been.
Hours after tickets to Swift's Toronto show went on sale on Ticketmaster in 2023, tickets appeared on resale site, with prices ranging from thousands to hundreds of thousands of dollars.
It's not clear if any of this activity is inappropriate, but enforcing the law requires co-operation from ticket platforms, said Courty.
Ticketmaster required a presale code to purchase tickets through the Ticketmaster Verified program, which is aimed at cracking down on resellers. Following a surge in reported scams, the platform implemented a new rule that ticket transfers for Swift's concerts can only begin 72 hours before the event.
One approach China has taken to eliminate for-profit ticket resale that could also apply in Canada is to tie tickets to ID for major, rare events like the Eras Tour, which have excess demand, Courty said.
"The best way to do that is to have limited tickets with one ticket, one name, one admission, and you check identity at the gate," he said, thus completely prohibiting the resale and exchange of tickets while still allowing people to get refunds.
Cyrenne agrees with the approach, saying it would take a lot of manpower and resources to police the rule, and he was unsure if concert promoters would have the incentive to go through the hassle.
CAPTION: Some Toronto Eras Tour resale tickets are going for at least $4,000.Scott A Garfitt invision file photo

CREDIT: Estella Ren toronto star

Earlier today, he posted on Twitter, formerly X, about the Free Trade Agreement between Canada, the United States and Mexico.

Doug Ford takes aim at Mexico in order to protect Ontario's auto sector

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-takes-aim-at-mexico-order-protect/docview/3127361519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+takes+aim+at+Mexico+in+order+to+protect+Ontario%27s+auto+sector&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is taking a run at the border - the Mexican border.
With the prospect of U.S. President-elect Donald Trump slapping hefty tariffs on Canadian products after he takes office in January, Ford is moving to insulate Ontario from American levies.
The premier, who has been advocating a "Buy Can-Am mindset" that would more closely tie Canada with the U.S., on Tuesday took aim at Mexico.
"Free trade needs to be fair," Ford said in a statement.
"Since signing on to the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, Mexico has allowed itself to become a backdoor for Chinese cars, auto parts and other products into Canadian and American markets, putting Canadian and American workers' livelihoods at risk while undermining our communities and doing enormous harm to our shared economic success," he said.
Ford was one of the Canadian leaders who convinced Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to match President Joe Biden's 100 per cent tariff on artificially cheap Chinese electric vehicles that are subsidized by Beijing in order to protect Ontario's burgeoning domestic EV industry.
Both the premier and the prime minister were also concerned that Canada might serve as a backdoor to import inexpensive Chinese EVs into the U.S. if matching tariffs weren't introduced.
"If Mexico won't fight transshipment by, at the very least, matching Canadian and American tariffs on Chinese imports, they shouldn't have a seat at the table or enjoy access to the largest economy in the world," said Ford.
"Instead, we must prioritize the closest economic partnership on earth by directly negotiating a bilateral U.S.-Canada free trade agreement that puts U.S. and Canadian workers first."
His salvo comes as Canadian officials are bracing for the impact of a second Trump presidency that would be even more protectionist than when he was in office between 2017 and 2021.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who works closely with Ottawa and Queen's Park on trade issues, said Ford "is hitting a note that's been making the rounds in Washington for some time."
"The Chinese are relentless in their strategies to access the North American market and all partners have a share in the responsibility to protect it," said Volpe, noting the domestic industry is integrated with Mexico's automotive sector.
"Canadian suppliers are heavily invested in Mexico to serve carmakers where they build cars," he said in an interview on Tuesday.
"We've been working closely with Mexican officials for sometime now as local investors on navigating this issue to our collective benefit."
CAPTION: In a statement, Premier Doug Ford said free trade needs to be fair.

Document 168 of 500

Crombie's Liberals pledge income tax breaks that they say Doug Ford has failed to deliver

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/crombies-liberals-pledge-income-tax-breaks-that/docview/3127359841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%27s+Liberals+pledge+income+tax+breaks+that+they+say+Doug+Ford+has+failed+to+deliver&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals are promising a $2.8-billion tax cut plan ahead of an expected early provincial election with an income tax break Premier Doug Ford has not delivered since pledging it in 2018.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Tuesday she would cut middle-class income taxes 22 per cent if elected premier in addition to axing the eight per cent provincial portion of the Harmonized Sales Tax on home heating fuels and hydro bills.
The measures would save the average family $1,150 a year in "permanent financial relief," she said, contrasting the breaks to the $200 tax rebate cheques Ford's Progressive Conservative government will send to 15 million Ontarians in January to defray the cost of living after a bout of high inflation.
"Doug Ford thinks a one-time $200 rebate cheque makes up for the cost of living crisis he created. Well, I don't," Crombie said Tuesday at a Gabby's restaurant location in Scarborough.
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation applauded her move.
"Premier Doug Ford promised income tax relief for the middle class six years ago, but taxpayers are still waiting," said Jay Goldberg, the organization's Ontario director.
"While the federal government's carbon tax keeps making those basics more expensive, Crombie's promise to take the provincial portion of the HST off home energy bills will make a big difference."
Ford wouldn't commit to matching the Liberal tax cuts at a campaign-style stop in the Barrie area to announce funding for a new facility to train crane operators for Ontario's construction industry.
He accused Crombie of planning to give municipalities broader taxing powers while revoking his temporary gasoline tax cut of 10.7 cents per litre, elimination of tolls on Highways 412 and 418 and scrapping of licence plate renewal fees.
"What Bonnie Crombie doesn't understand is there's an affordability crisis right now. Taxing people does not work," Ford said.
"They're going to have a very clear choice," he added, referring to Ontarians. "Vote for a government that's creating opportunities, creating jobs, getting people trained, building infrastructure ... and making sure we're prudent fiscal managers."
The premier's income tax cut never materialized as the province was engulfed in the COVID-19 pandemic, which sent Ontario's deficit skyrocketing as the government was forced to dramatically increase spending on health.
Ford's $200 rebate cheques, which opposition parties have dubbed a pre-election bribe, will cost the provincial treasury $3 billion this fiscal year as the province runs a deficit of $6.6 billion.
Crombie said her tax cut plan would cost $2.8 billion, but there is no clear accounting of where the money will come from.
The Liberals said it will be paid for by ending "shady backroom deals" that Ford has made with "wealthy friends and insiders over the last six years ... at the expense of the real people of Ontario."
Crombie cited the abrupt closure of the Ontario Science Centre and its planned move to Ontario Place with a parking garage, a deal with the European company Therme to build a spa at Ontario Place, up to $1 billion spent to put beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails into convenience stores a year ahead of schedule, and the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
"We'll just stop the giveaways," said Crombie.
The Liberals now have nine seats and hold a distant third place in the legislature behind the Official Opposition NDP. Ford's PCs have a solid lead in public opinion polls.
Crombie's tax cut plan comes a couple of weeks after Ford began touting his rebate cheques and taunting her with them.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers ... she wants to increase taxes," Ford said late last month.
Crombie's income tax break would reduce the tax rate on taxable income between $51,446 and $75,000 to 7.15 per cent from the current 9.15 per cent.
CAPTION: Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie (left) and Premier Doug Ford.

Document 169 of 500

Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will pledge tax cut on home heating fuels

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-liberal-leader-bonnie-crombie-will-pledge/docview/3126871508/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Liberal+Leader+Bonnie+Crombie+will+pledge+tax+cut+on+home+heating+fuels&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will unveil a major tax cut plan that includes lifting the provincial levy on home heating fuels, the Star has learned.
Taking aim at Premier Doug Ford's $200 tax rebate cheque scheme, a senior Liberal campaign source said "people need something lasting and not just a one-time handout."
The Liberals' "More For You" tax package proposal will be released Tuesday as Ford is considering an early election next spring instead of waiting until the scheduled vote in June 2026.
"She's going to deliver tax cuts that Doug Ford promised but didn't deliver and that the New Democrats and Greens would never touch," added the source, speaking confidentially Monday in order to discuss the plan before its release.
While Ford pledged a middle-class income tax cut in the 2018 campaign that brought him to power, he has not followed through.
However, his Progressive Conservative government has eliminated licence plate renewal fees, tolls on Highways 412 and 418, and has a temporary cut on gasoline taxes.
"The whole plan will cost less than Doug Ford's cheques but bring ... more relief on an annual basis," said the Liberal source.
The $200 cheques, which will cost the provincial treasury $3 billion as the province runs a deficit of $6.6 billion this year, were a cornerstone of the government's fall economic statement Oct. 30.
Ford accused the Liberal leader of planning to raise taxes when he made the cheque announcement in Scarborough.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers," the premier said in a partisan boast. "She wants to increase taxes, increase the carbon tax."
Crombie shot back with a reminder "the only reason" Ontarians pay the federal carbon levy is because Ford axed the province's cap-and-trade environmental pact with Quebec and California in 2018.
"We will not implement a carbon tax," she added.
The Liberals and other opposition parties have accused Ford's PCs, who have a solid lead in public opinion polls, of using the $200 rebate cheques to bribe Ontarians into voting for them as the government faces an RCMP investigation into its $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Under Crombie's proposal, the eight per cent provincial portion of the 13 per cent Harmonized Sales Tax on home heating fuel would be eliminated.
The Liberal source said other consumer friendly tax relief measures will be included in a "fiscally responsible" package paid for by ending the government's "multibillion-dollar deals" that will benefit a private spa at Ontario Place, Staples stores into which ServiceOntario counters are being moved, and as much as $1 billion being spent to move beer, wine and pre-mixed cocktails into convenience stores a year earlier than planned.
Ford's tax-free $200 cheques will be sent to all taxpayers 18 and over. Families who qualify for federal Canada Child Benefit payments receive an additional $200 for every child under 18.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks at a party meeting and policy convention in London, Ont., on Sept. 21, 2024.

Document 170 of 500

Canada's most decorated citizen gives Doug Ford an order on Remembrance Day: 'There's one other thing I want him to do'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-most-decorated-citizen-gives-doug-ford/docview/3127396176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+most+decorated+citizen+gives+Doug+Ford+an+order+on+Remembrance+Day%3A+%27There%27s+one+other+thing+I+want+him+to+do%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The general has given an order.
Lt. Gen. Richard Rohmer, Canada's most decorated citizen, wants Premier Doug Ford to engrave the name of the Veterans Memorial at Queen's Park on the back of the black granite monument at Queen's Park.
Rohmer, 100, who chaired the committee that got the 30 metre memorial built in 2006 under premier Dalton McGuinty, delivered some instructions to Ford at Monday's Remembrance Day ceremony.
"I want to say to the premier there's one other thing I want him to do," said the retired general, whose honours and medals include the highest awards of Canada, France and Belgium, among others.
"There's one final task that I tried to get done to this wall. On the north side of the wall, it is a blank black, black, black, black," he said from the dais in front of the wall he championed.
"And I tried fruitlessly - I don't like the word fruitlessly - but I tried without result in getting the name of this wall put on the north side of the wall so that the people coming here to pay respect ... to this monument ... will know what this wall is."
Turning to Ford, who was seated nearby, Rohmer said: "A few words, letters ... premier, on that side would be helpful. At the moment it's just a blank wall."
With a glimmer in his eye, the centenarian - who has advised premiers dating back John Robarts in the 1960s - reminded the gathered crowd of several hundred people in front of the Ontario legislature of Ford's campaign mantra.
"The premier is the kind of man who likes to 'get things done,' I'm giving him another good suggestion."
Ford's office said later Monday that they are looking into the general's request.
Lest anyone forget Rohmer's powers of persuasion, the P-51 Mustang fighter plane he flew on D-Day in 1944 is one of the engraved scenes of war depicted on the veterans memorial.
"The challenge that was given to me at one point was, 'you can't do that.' I said, 'oh really, watch me.' It's in the wall."
Since its opening 18 years ago, the monument - designed by Charlottetown-born artist Allan Harding MacKay and Vancouver-based landscape architects Phillips Farevaag Smallenberg after a national competition - has become a major landmark at Queen's Park.
At Ford's behest in 2020, a new monument was added to complement the memorial to commemorate the 158 Canadian Armed Forces personnel killed in the war in Afghanistan.
It features a zigzagging bronze ribbon to symbolize Afghanistan's mountainous terrain and includes a stone from an inukshuk dedicated to the fallen that was erected by Canadian soldiers at the Kandahar air field.
CAPTION: Richard Rohmer, left, one of the most decorated Canadian veterans, is shown at the Government of Canada ceremony to mark the 80th anniversary of D-Day, at Juno Beach, in Courseulles-sur-Mer, Normandy, France, on June 6, 2024.

Subject: Memorials & monuments; Veterans

Title: Canada's most decorated citizen gives Doug Ford an order on Remembrance Day: 'There's one other thing I want him to do'
