Document 171 of 500

Doug Ford wants to put the pedal to the metal to build Ontario's next highway. Here's why that may not work

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-put-pedal-metal-build-ontarios/docview/3126679118/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+put+the+pedal+to+the+metal+to+build+Ontario%27s+next+highway.+Here%27s+why+that+may+not+work&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's government is pushing ahead with new legislation it says will kick construction of Highway 413 into high gear by streamlining the environmental assessment process, allowing for around-the-clock construction and eliminating the right to appeal for expropriations.
The 52-km highway project has been stuck in gridlock at the environmental assessment (EA) stage for the past three years, after the federal government decided in 2021 to oversee the province's work.
Earlier this year, the two parties struck a deal removing the federal oversight - for now. That move propelled the province to push through legislation last month called Bill 212 Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, in the hopes of getting the project to the next stage.
But experts say while a streamlined EA may hasten this phase of the process, there are still several steps - and years to go - before the highway can become close to a driveable reality.
"The government has positioned this as if the environmental assessment is the one thing standing in the way of the highway," said Alexander Wray, a research associate in the department of geography and environment at Western University, who has worked on EAs for the government on other infrastructure projects.
"They are positioning that with the finger snap, you can build a highway, but there are many steps within an EA and afterwards before any highway can be built," he said.
A complex construction job
Highway 413, or the GTA West Highway, is a four- to six-lane highway that will connect Vaughan to Milton, passing through Caledon and Brampton. The highway has become a key part of the province's election platform and its regional transportation plan, but has also generated strong opposition from environmental groups. The government says it will shorten commute times by more than 30 minutes, and help keep the region moving. Critics say the project will cause environmental damage, by cutting through farmland, Greenbelt forests and dozens of waterways across the GTA.
But highway building in Ontario is a lengthy process that according to the province's own estimates can take upwards of a decade to plan, design, expropriate land and construct, with each phase taking an estimated two to three years to complete. On a website describing the provincial highway building process, it says it takes about two to three years to build a 10-kilometre section of four-lane highway.
"It's not a simple project," said Wray, considering it will go through the Oak Ridges Moraine, and cut through numerous sensitive environmental areas including the Greenbelt and Oak Ridges Moraine. "On an Ontario scale, it's complex engineering."
Tamer El-Diraby, a professor in the department of civil and mineral engineering at University of Toronto, said there are three main parts to highway building, which include "planning and decision-making process," the detailed design process, and then construction. The EA is included in the first part, he said.
Currently, the 413 project is in Stage 2 of the environmental assessment, which according to the province, includes "refining the route, determining interchange crossing and preliminary design" in addition to carrying out extensive field studies to understand the local environment, and how to mitigate potential concerns.
Once the EA is finished, made public for feedback and approved by the minister - which can take months - the project would then proceed to the detailed design process.
"The design process is long, but generally very methodical," said El-Diraby, adding that it can take time - often years to finalize - as it involves fine-tuning the details of how the project will look, and operate.
"It's the construction stage where the rubber hits the road ... someone has to come up with the money to keep the project going," said El-Diraby. "It often happens that politicians will say during the planning stage that we will get this money. But in the construction phase, some times there are budgetary concerns, or there is no political will, and the money is not there. So that can become the sticking point."
Cost of 413 a mystery
In February, the province allocated $28 billion over the next 10 years to "build, repair, and expand roads and highways." No new money was added for the 413 in this October's provincial budget, which lists dozens of other proposed highway projects across Ontario.
The province has yet to give an estimate of how much the 413 will cost.
Wray said that even after an EA is completed, the highway still needs to be designed in detail, land has to be expropriated, the project has to be resourced, and logistics such as determining if there's enough sand and gravel to build the roadway needs to be determined.
"In my opinion, the accelerated EA may speed up the entire process by at most a few months, and not a few years, which I think the government is trying to position this as," said Wray.
A new curve in the road
And now, in what appears to be a last-ditch effort, environmental groups have once again petitioned federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault for the project to be designated for environmental oversight under the Impact Assessment Act.
Last month, hours after the 413 bill was introduced, advocacy group Environmental Defence made the request, citing a slew of adverse impacts to species and areas within federal jurisdiction. The highway will cut through large portions of the Greenbelt, and dozens of water ways, including the Humber River, Credit River and Etobicoke Creek, and will impact the habitat of several species of federal concern such as a minnow called the Redside Dace.
Guilbeault has roughly until mid-January to reply to the request.
In March, Ottawa and Queen's Park agreed to cancel a federal review of the province's environmental assessment of the 413 after a Supreme Court decision cast doubt on its constitutionality.
Environmental Defence's Phil Pothen and his group believes the legislation has been amended since that ruling to allow for a new federal review.
If the federal government intervenes, the project could be delayed by several years, said El-Diraby.
If the federal government doesn't step in, the provincial legislation will fast-track the process, but it's hard to know by how much, said El-Diraby.
"I would be shocked if the project starts in the next year," he added.
Dakota Brasier, director of media relations for Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said that the accelerated EA will build on studies done over the past few decades.
"As part of the proposed legislation, MTO will build upon the considerable studies and consultation efforts already undertaken, over the past 30 years to advance the project under the existing environmental assessment process," said Brasier.
"While MTO is accelerating the process - we will be maintaining environmental protection throughout the detail design of Highway 413."
Earlier this year, the province said it is planning to issue construction contracts for "early works", which include enhancements on roadways and bridges that will connect to the 413, in 2025. The new proposed legislation exempts early works from having to go through any EA review.
Pothen said by allowing the early work to start before the EA, or detailed design or route is finalized "gives an impression to the public that the highway is a done deal before we know what the highway will look like or the exact route," he said.
El-Diraby said that even if the province is able to clear the EA process, the courts could also add to the delays. He said once an EA is finalized, including the route, it can trigger stakeholders to turn to courts to appeal the province's decisions.
The proposed legislation does try to pre-empt some of those changes, including eliminating the ability of landowners to dispute decisions around expropriations.
Such provisions will take some time off the length of the entire process, but cannot be avoided entirely, said Wray.For example, in the case of the 407 East extension, which was 22 kilometres long, it took 30 months to acquire the almost 700 properties along the route.
Wray said that the current messaging appears to be about making clear what the priorities are for this government - something not uncommon for governments of the day.
"It's clear that this highway is important to the Ford government," he said. "This is about making it a priority for the public but also those who work there."
CAPTION: Critics say the 413 will cause environmental damage, by cutting through farmland, Greenbelt forests and dozens of waterways across the GTA.Ontario Premier Doug Ford during an announcement for the building of 413, in Caledon earlier this year. Even once it passes the EA, the highway has to go through detailed design stage before construction can start.The province has said it is planning to issue construction contracts for "early works" on the 413, which will happen even before the route is finalized.Premier Doug Ford says his government's new legislation is designed to kick 413 construction into high gear, after being stuck in an environmental assessment for the past three years.Members of 'GASP' Grandmothers Act to Save the Planet as well as other supporters, like Fred Wieler and Charlene Pauls protested the proposed GTA West Highway 413 at the four points of the intersection of Trafalgar road and Cornwall/Speers Road. Document 172 of 500

The remote work factor in Trump's win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/remote-work-factor-trumps-win/docview/3126679005/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+remote+work+factor+in+Trump%27s+win&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Following Donald Trump's decisive win in the 2024 U.S. presidential election, remote work migration has emerged as an unexpected factor favouring the Republican Party.
While a study by the Centre for Economic Policy Research initially suggested that remote work could weaken Republican strongholds by enabling people to relocate from Democratic urban centres to more affordable areas, the opposite effect appears to have strengthened Trump's support in critical states.
The flexibility introduced by remote work allowed Americans from high-cost, left-leaning states like California and New York to move to suburban or rural areas with lower costs and, frequently, Republican-leaning communities.
Census Bureau data show a fourfold increase in Americans working from home over the past three years, fuelling a 12 per cent rise in interstate moves since 2019. This migration pattern led many mobile voters to explore new living options outside of densely populated Democratic hubs, yet, rather than eroding the conservative base in traditionally red states, this shift appears to have consolidated it, amplifying Trump's appeal.
The trend holds particular significance for states like Texas and Florida, where an influx of remote workers from blue states had sparked Democratic hopes of a shift. However, rather than bringing progressive values, many new residents have aligned with the prevailing political culture in their new communities.
In Texas and Florida, the influx of newcomers has not only reinforced conservative support but also helped solidify these states as Republican strongholds. In states like Idaho, conservative transplants from California, in particular, have further bolstered the local GOP base, thwarting any anticipated progressive shift.
This "doughnut effect" - a pattern of migration from urban centres to suburban and rural areas - has reshaped political demographics across regions without destabilizing state-level Republican dominance.
For instance, in suburban areas around Democratic cities like New York and San Francisco, migration has brought a modest diversification in voter profiles.
Yet, rather than making these areas politically mixed, the influx often consolidates the existing voting blocs, reinforcing either Democratic or Republican tendencies, depending on the area's broader ideological landscape.
Remote workers now represent a unique voter demographic that prioritizes lifestyle and affordability over proximity to workplaces. Younger workers, in particular, are drawn to remote work's lifestyle flexibility, moving to areas with different political climates that sometimes shift their political inclinations.
Although these mobile voters sought areas with diverse political landscapes, the effect on voting patterns has been more conservative-leaning than initially projected.
For Republicans, these migration trends represent an opportunity to appeal to a geographically diverse and ideologically mixed voter base that has found alignment with conservative values in new regions.
The election has demonstrated that remote worker mobility is not a straightforward predictor of Democratic support, as expected shifts in key states largely reinforced Republican gains.
Campaigns now face the reality that voter mobility has added layers of complexity and unpredictability, which may favour conservatives in many regions.
Ultimately, this election cycle has underscored a new era of U.S. politics shaped by the interplay of remote work and voter mobility. America's political landscape is now defined by a more mobile electorate, which challenges long-standing assumptions about party loyalties and regional strongholds.
Trump's victory illustrates how demographic shifts tied to remote work can bolster Republican influence in critical areas.
The emerging dynamic suggests that, as political strategies evolve to account for this more flexible and mobile voter base, the Republicans may stand to benefit from the lasting influence of remote work on the American electorate.
Gleb Tsipursky is CEO of the
future-of-work consultancy Disaster Avoidance Experts and author of seven books. Based in Columbus, Ohio, he is a freelance contributing columnist for The toronto Star.
CAPTION: There has been a 12 per cent rise in interstate moves since 2019 as many urban voters went rural, writes Gleb Tsipursky. This shift appears to have weakened Democratic urban hubs while consolidating Donald Trump's appeal in rural constituencies.MATIAS J. OCNER Tribune New Service

CREDIT: Gleb Tsipursky Toronto star Gleb Tsipursky is CEO of the future-of-work consultancy Disaster Avoidance Experts and author of seven books. Based in Columbus, Ohio, he is a freelance contributing columnist for The toronto Star.

Document 173 of 500

Pharmacists say power to prescribe often comes with pressure to prescribe, and generate profits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pharmacists-say-power-prescribe-often-comes-with/docview/3126546585/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacists+say+power+to+prescribe+often+comes+with+pressure+to+prescribe%2C+and+generate+profits&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Ontario government proposes expanding the list of "minor ailments" that pharmacists can treat - a plan the province says will ease strain on family doctors and emergency rooms - pharmacists themselves are sounding the alarm over the business practices of major chains, which they say pose risks to patient safety.
Fuelling the concerns are accusations that large chain pharmacies, which dominate the profession in Ontario, have stripped back support staff and issued quotas to pharmacists for OHIP-billable services. A February CBC exposé reported that pharmacists at Shoppers Drug Mart and other chains pressure patients to access unnecessary services including medication reviews, or "MedsChecks," an Ontario program allowing pharmacists to bill the government up to $75 for what often amounts to a phone call completed within a few minutes.
Earlier this year, Shoppers billed OHIP $1,423,900 for MedsChecks alone, in just one week.
Kathleen Leach, a Hamilton pharmacist, said expanding pharmacists' roles could degrade the care at major chains, as pharmacists working under service quotas are compelled to rush through their work, or face losses to their staffing and job security.
"Things get missed," said Leach, adding there is already widespread worry among pharmacists over the lack of support and staffing in corporate pharmacies.
A recent survey of nearly 4,300 Ontario pharmacy workers showed that over a third have felt pressure to rush through minor ailment assessments in under five minutes. According to Leach, the issues with MedsChecks are just one symptom of a bigger problem: growing corporate influence over the pharmacy profession.
The Ontario College of Pharmacists, the body responsible for regulating the province's pharmacists, is trying to address the issue. Surveys and town halls held by the OCP revealed that 85 per cent of pharmacists feel pressure to meet service quotas; a sizable majority of them, according to an OCP report, work at Loblaw and Shoppers pharmacies.
A separate report from B.C. found that these issues occurred in chain pharmacies at more than twice the rate of independent pharmacies. And Saskatchewan's pharmacy regulator concluded that "focus on (business) targets leads to errors and increased patient risks."
In April, pharmacists in Ontario filed a class-action lawsuit against Shoppers and its parent company, Loblaw, arguing that corporate practices such as stripping back staff and enforcing service quotas pose significant risks to patients.
"The class action lawsuit has no merit whatsoever and we will vigorously defend against it," said Loblaw, in an email. Pharmacists bear sole responsibility in deciding whether to provide a professional service, the company said, adding that in the wake of public concern, it has worked toward "a more collaborative approach to patient care."
Corporate planning documents sent to store owners confirm Shoppers continues to issue service and revenue targets for pharmacists, with targets in-place for 2025, including for MedsChecks, Naloxone kits, and minor ailment assessments.
Kyro Maseh, an independent pharmacist in Scarborough, says he's had to "rehabilitate" pharmacy students coming from corporate pharmacies, as they're used to rushing through their work.
"If you're doing a MedsCheck in three minutes, you're false billing," he said, noting that a proper MedsCheck should take 20 minutes to an hour to complete. Rushed assessments, he warns, can leave patients with a false sense of security, risking missed medical issues.
Annie Jaeger, a Shoppers Drug Mart customer in Peterborough, Ont., said lately she's felt increased pressure from her pharmacist to undergo health assessments, even for things she usually manages on her own.
"A lot of the things they're going to be prescribing for are things we can already manage over the counter," Jaeger said, adding that as opportunities for revenue-generation increase, so too does the potential for overbilling, unnecessary assessments and "lots of abuse."
Many pharmacists, including those worried about the effects of corporate quotas on their work, welcome an expanded role.
"We genuinely want to help," said Leach, who says she's excited to provide care to patients without having to refer them to clinics for every issue.
NDP Opposition Health Critic France Gélinas says she has no issue with giving pharmacists more responsibilities, but letting them bill OHIP for health services is problematic. Gélinas pointed out that nurse practitioners provide the same services to patients without OHIP billing fees attached.
Her fellow NDP MPP Chris Glover said "Pharmacists have a lot of knowledge and skills which should be put to use. But that's not what's happening. What (Premier Doug) Ford has done is find a way to divert more tax dollars out of vital services and into the hands of companies like Loblaw."
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks during a press conference at a Shoppers Drug Mart pharmacy in Etobicoke, Ont., on Wednesday, January 11, 2023. THE CANADIAN PRESS/ Tijana Martin
CREDIT: Nick Tsergas

Document 174 of 500

'The person using that bike lane could be your doctor': Hundreds rally to protest Ford government's plan to remove bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/person-using-that-bike-lane-could-be-your-doctor/docview/3126483201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+person+using+that+bike+lane+could+be+your+doctor%27%3A+Hundreds+rally+to+protest+Ford+government%27s+plan+to+remove+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Loudly ringing their bells, and chanting "save our bike lanes," hundreds of cyclists rode along Bloor Street on Saturday afternoon to protest the Ford government's plan to rip out Toronto bike lanes and keep more from getting built.
The noisy but peaceful protest against the province's Bill 212 began with a rally at Christie Pits Park, with cyclists holding up signs saying "bike lanes save lives," and "bike lanes keep cars moving."
The bill - introduced in the legislature last month - would require municipalities to seek provincial approval to install bike lanes that mean removing a lane of vehicle traffic. Both Premier Doug Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria have also said the province could force the removal of existing bike lanes, including those on Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street, to reduce gridlock.
In one of the more sombre moments of the afternoon, NDP MPP Jessica Bell read an open letter to Ford and Sarkaria from Karen Orme, whose daughter Alex Amaro was struck and killed while cycling on Dufferin Street north of College Street in 2020.
"Minister Sarkaria, you say that this ... legislation is about giving people more time with their families. Our family would give anything to have more time with Alex. To hug her, to speak with her, to celebrate holidays and life milestones," Orme said in the letter read by Bell, as many in the crowd became emotional.
Radiologist Derek Tsang also told the crowd that bike lanes allow him to navigate between various downtown hospitals where he sees patients.
"I was getting around by bicycle so I can deliver timely care to the patients who need it," said Tsang. "Ontarians need to remember ... that the person using that bike lane could be your doctor, your personal support worker, or your DoorDash delivery person."
While most drivers on Saturday waited patiently as the cyclists rode past after the rally - in the bike lanes and on the road - some expressed their frustration.
"Hurry up. People have places to go!" shouted one driver, leaning on their horn.
"Lady, where do you think we're going to be riding when the bike lanes are gone?" one cyclist retorted while pedalling past, shaking their head.
For James Macfarlane, the fight against the province's Bill 212 is a matter of life and death for Toronto cyclists.
"Removing bike lanes won't remove cyclists. It will just put them in front of cars, in harm's way," said Macfarlane, who has vivid memories of run-ins with angry motorists.
"I had some driver try to run me off the road because I swore at him for parking in the bike lane," said Macfarlane, who cycled from his east-end home to join the protest.
Rahul Sharma, who bikes to his job at Yonge Street and St. Clair Avenue from his downtown home, said "For me, the bike lanes are about safety and access. When I lived in Scarborough, it really wasn't an option."
Organizers Eva Stanger-Ross and Zev Godfrey, who both bike to classes at the University of Toronto, pleaded for the provincial government to reconsider Bill 212, officially known as the "Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act."
Stanger-Ross argued that bike lanes make Toronto more accessible, and said the city should be allowed to proceed with its planned expansion of bikeways.
"I'm confident that the city's plans to build 100 kilometres of new bike lanes would've opened up the city to many others," Stanger-Ross said, to cheers from the crowd at Christie Pits.
Godfrey said cyclists and drivers need to have a better understanding of each other's needs.
"There's a really dangerous effort to frame the issue as us vs. them, and this just isn't productive or true," Godfrey told the crowd at the rally, to more loud cheers and bell-ringing.
"For many people, driving just is the best way to get around, just as for many of us here biking is the only or most-viable option."
CAPTION: Protesters hold signs at Saturday's rally at Christie Pits Park in support of bike lanes.A sign on a protester's bike as he joins hundreds of other cyclists along Bloor Street on Saturday afternoon.A cyclist brought his corgi dogs to Saturday's rally in support of bike lanes in Toronto.Cyclists fill the bike lane and roadway on Bloor Street after a rally on Saturdayto protest the Ford government's plans to remove bikeways and require municipalities to get approval before installing more.

Subject: Automobile drivers; Automobile safety; Bicycling

Title: 'The person using that bike lane could be your doctor': Hundreds rally to protest Ford government's plan to remove bike lanes

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 175 of 500

There are warning signs for Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre in Donald Trump's win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-are-warning-signs-justin-trudeau-pierre/docview/3126412524/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+are+warning+signs+for+Justin+Trudeau+and+Pierre+Poilievre+in+Donald+Trump%27s+win&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Don't let anyone tell you the re-election of Donald Trump is not a jolt to Canada's political landscape. Or that we've been there, done that.
The fallout is about to overwhelm the Canadian government's agenda for the next four years.
Just how it all plays out in a Canadian election expected at any time in the next year with a deeply unpopular Liberal prime minister and a right-leaning Conservative opposition leader way ahead in the polls is a central question.
There are certainly tactical lessons to be learned by Canadian political practitioners.
What's less clear is whether Donald Trump's victory represents a lasting political realignment south of the border and whether it signals a shift in Canadian political coalitions to come.
First, the early takeaways.
Liberals and Conservatives, along with other political parties, can draw three lessons from the United States election results.
Addressing cost of living anxiety is the political play that matters right now. A moralizing campaign - whether it's signalling how virtuous you are or how detestable your opponent is - doesn't cut it.And finally, "change" is a powerful vote driver. Incumbents are dropping around the world.
Those are the tactical takeaways that many politicos identified in the halls of Canada's Parliament, on television panels and analysis pieces, and in hot takes on social media.
Yet a surprising number of MPs and advisers trying to chart the path for leaders aspiring to the prime minister's seat warn against drawing too many comparisons between the Canadian and American political landscape.
In conversation after conversation with the Star most, including those who were granted anonymity because they were not authorized to publicly discuss political strategy, said the two countries, the campaigns, and the personalities of the main party leaders are too different to read too much into Trump's win and the Democrat's defeat.
But Fred DeLorey, the Conservative party's 2021 campaign director and a longtime party strategist, said that's "complete and utter nonsense."
DeLorey said the biggest lessons are for the Liberals, who he said are "heading down the exact same path the Democrats went down. They're trying to run the same playbook, the same messaging, the same everything. And it didn't work there. It's not going to work here."
The Biden-Harris Democrats "lacked a strong, powerful narrative on why they should be elected. Instead, it was like, things are bad, but our opponent's really bad."
DeLorey said negative comparisons between Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Trump won't work, as they are patently not the same. The Democrats, he said, had "real material to work with. The problem is, though, the positive message - why vote for us - for the Liberals and the Democrats is almost the same, in that there isn't one. There is no narrative (other) than we've got your back."
For voters, including the Black men, Latinos and women who shifted votes to Trump, who were worried mostly about the economy, Trump didn't offer a detailed plan, Delorey acknowledged, but at least he "talked about issues that they felt he would champion."
In fact, a senior Liberal cabinet minister who assessed the results early on the morning the Trump victory was called agreed with the broader point: that the Liberal party must focus its re-election bid on cost of living and affordability, and forget the rest.
"Inflation's a killer," he said. Voters will be driven by their household economic concerns and no campaign that relies on "moral superiority" - anything that looks like tone-policing, virtue-signalling, or culture wars - will succeed, the insider said.
Bill Clinton's campaign strategist James Carville coined the slogan "it's the economy, stupid." The phrase has found a whole new generation's ears in the past week.
Two other senior Liberal sources agreed that is the party's biggest takeaway from the U.S. election: that, as one put it, the economy is "number one, number one, number one."
"And it's not just in the United States where you've seen an election result like that, it's hard for incumbents everywhere right now," he said.
However, those insiders don't accept there is no value in attacking your opponent.
The second insider said it is "it is not good enough, although you have to do it, to only try to demonize your opponent. You also have to have a positive offering of your own which meets the moment, and you can't only have one of those things these days. You have to have both."
The bigger question about whether voting coalitions have irreversibly shifted is more complicated. Analysts with AP's VoteCast who combed through a deep dive on voter data said "big shifts within small groups and small shifts within big groups helped propel" Trump back to the White House and eroded the Democrats' voter base.
Some Conservatives here believe that is a sign of what's to come.
A Conservative MP said Poilievre has already expanded the Conservative coalition in the two years since becoming leader, drawing in more voters from Canada's working class, from unionized labour, and from ethnic or visible minority groups by focusing on inflation, the cost of living, jobs and affordability almost to the exclusion of all else.
Howard Anglin, a top staffer to then-Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper and to then-Alberta premier Jason Kenney, said Latino and East and South Asian voters, especially the men, "voted like issue voters rather than as 'ethnic' block voters," adding it bolsters the theory that there is a "multi-ethnic groundswell against out-of-touch progressive social policy." But Anglin said that was secondary to the overwhelming drive for change.
First and foremost, Anglin said, "it was a change election and Trump was the candidate of change and Kamala was the candidate of continuity."
"In 2020, Biden won independents in key swing states; in 2024, Trump won them. Trump also won the votes of "double haters" - people who said they didn't like either candidate - by 20 points.
"People voted for change ... because of policies that they can feel making their lives worse: illegal immigration, cost of living, crime, and general quality of life. Those are progressive Democratic policies and Kamala said she wouldn't change them."
André Turcotte, a pollster with Pollara Insights and an associate professor at Carleton University, agrees the anti-incumbent wave is strong, but takes a slightly different view, saying it's "too simplistic to simply say it's all about the economy."
"Identity was probably also a factor in the shifts that saw young men turn up and vote in greater numbers for Trump, Black men and Latinos supporting Trump," he said in an interview. As for whether that is a trend likely to be seen here, Turcotte said "I don't think we play identity politics the same way they do it."
He said Canada doesn't have "big racial - for lack of a better word - groups" that can be mobilized in the same way the U.S. taps into racial voting blocs, while identity in Canadian politics tends to fall along gender or social identity lines - for example, LGBTQ identity.
Turcotte believes the U.S. result makes it easier for Trudeau to resist calls for him to leave, because he can argue it didn't help Democrats to ditch Biden. It also complicates Poilievre's bid to replace Trudeau.
Pollara's research shows that half of Conservative supporters don't like Trump, but the other half does. So he's got to be careful, when for example he's chewing on an apple as he chews out a reporter. "He has to be conscious that he has many people in this coalition that really don't like that."
And yet, Turcotte observes, Democrat strategists bet big - but wrongly, it turns out - on the old axiom that "hope is better than anger." Instead, Trump's win shows that anger works. For the past few years, Pollara's polling has tracked a Rage Index in Canada. It shows Canadians are angry - about governments, institutions, media, wars, the housing market, you name it. So he suggests the Canadian political market is ripe for political parties to try to use that to political advantage.
"The anger is there," he said. "We say we're civil, until we're not."
In the end, what voter coalition can a Liberal government seeking its fourth mandate under Trudeau mobilize to resist the anti-incumbent forces, and not face Kamala Harris' fate?
Even the Liberals are wondering. Their base is still in urban and suburban Ontario, Quebec and B.C., with some semi-rural and rural support in Atlantic Canada, less so in Quebec. The Liberals have to recover support among the under 35-year-old youth vote to win the next election. Polls show it's going to be a tough hill to climb.
Conservative Fred DeLorey said voter coalitions are shifting, but he cautions all shifts are transactional at the outset, and can be elusive.
"For sure, there is a political realignment happening across the world, it seems, coming out of post-pandemic," said DeLorey. "But at the end of the day, it's going to be results based. It's going to be- will Trump and the Republicans deliver what these voter groups want. And that's what will determine whether this solidifies.
"Voters lend you their vote. They can take it back next election and people can move around. So it's all about whether they can deliver or not. And that's the same thing Poilievre is going to have when he wins. He's going to have to deliver. If they (voters) don't get the change they want or are happy with, they'll move back somewhere else."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will be looking to see what worked and what didn't for Donald Trump and Kamala Harris.

Document 176 of 500

Working-class voters can't be ignored

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/working-class-voters-cant-be-ignored/docview/3126331420/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Working-class+voters+can%27t+be+ignored&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like a lot of Canadians, I thought I knew quite a bit about the United States.
After all, I lived there for five years. I reported from almost half the states and travelled in most of the others. I was married to an American for 45 years and spent time with her family in Hawaii, California and Colorado. My son is a dual citizen. I even speak their language, more or less.
Obviously, I don't know as much as I thought I did. I thought Kamala Harris had a good shot at winning on Tuesday. Donald Trump's take-that victory came as a surprise - not so much that he won, but that the voters' verdict was so decisive. I'm struggling to figure out why.
There are basically two choices if you're in this situation: condemn or try to understand.
I see quite a few people taking door No. 1, what I'd call the "Apocalypse Now" approach. You're going to read a lot of this stuff over the next few weeks - adjective-laden pieces lambasting Trump as a lying, bullying, race-baiting, fascistic, etc. Or blaming his victory on flaws in the American people themselves, on the racism, misogyny and sheer ignorance allegedly baked into the benighted population beyond the reach of the New York Times, Washington Post and so on.
Right now some of that is taking the form of beating up on tranches of voters who didn't follow the script progressives had laid out for them. Sizable numbers of Black men and especially Latinos went for Trump. And many women, in particular white women, did the same despite the Democrats' call to vote on abortion rights above all.
Blaming voters for a result you don't like is obviously a dead end. The alternative is trying to understand electoral choices that seem inexplicable from afar. This comes with the risk of being seen to justify or "normalize" support for that lying, bullying, race-baiting and so on candidate, or for the misguided world view of his supporters. But it has to be done.
That discussion is just starting. It's dawning on Democrats that their mainly coastal, urban, affluent party is fundamentally out of tune with voters. They've become the voice of the establishment, defending institutions at a time when the popular mood favours tearing them down and starting over.
Somehow, they let Trump become the champion of those who feel cast aside or, worse, disrespected by the system. They didn't take that message on board when Trump won the first time. But after this second victory, when no one could claim to be ignorant of the risks, it better sink in.
One strain of this thinking comes from the likes of Bernie Sanders, who has long urged Democrats to stop defending a status quo that isn't working for many Americans. "It should come as no great surprise," he says, "that a Democratic Party which has abandoned working class people would find that the working class has abandoned them."
But it will take more than different economic policies to woo ordinary people away from the likes of Trump. Many feel they've been cast off by the system and their concerns have been ignored.
There are lessons for Canadian politicians in all this. Of course, the personalities are very different and our political system doesn't work the same way as the Americans'. Pierre Poilievre is no Trump but he's trying to do something along the same lines as Trump - steal the votes of working-class "ordinary Canadians" away from parties that should be speaking to their concerns, the Liberals and NDP.
Polls show Poilievre's Conservatives are well on the way to doing that by focusing relentlessly on basic issues like housing and the cost of living.
If those trends continue into the next election, Canada's progressive parties will find themselves having to do the same kind of painful soul-searching as their cousins to the south.
Andrew Phillips is a staff columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Premier slams request that vets not wear uniforms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-slams-request-that-vets-not-wear-uniforms/docview/3126331204/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+slams+request+that+vets+not+wear+uniforms&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Halifax-area school is walking back its request that service members not attend its Remembrance Day ceremony in uniform, following an outburst of anger including from the Nova Scotia premier, who said the initial ask was "disgraceful and demeaning."
The comments by Premier Tim Houston, who is campaigning ahead of the Nov. 26 provincial election, were part of a chorus of criticism levelled at the school - Sackville Heights Elementary. A November newsletter from the school in Middle Sackville, N.S., invited Armed Forces members to attend its ceremony but asked that they arrive in civilian attire to "maintain a welcoming environment for all."
In response, Houston, leader of the Progressive Conservatives, accused the school administration of "disgracing themselves while demeaning the people who protect our country" in a post on the social media platform X Thursday night.
"If the people behind this decision had a shred of the courage that our veterans have, this cowardly and insulting idea would have been rejected immediately," Houston said.
The premier's message was uploaded to Reddit, collecting more than 670 comments, most of which were critical of the school.
In an email to families Thursday night, principal Rachael Webster apologized and welcomed veterans to attend "in the attire that makes them most comfortable."
"I recognize this request has caused harm and I am deeply sorry," Webster said.
The school had asked that service members not attend in their uniforms out of concern for some students who come from countries experiencing conflict and who she said expressed discomfort with images of war, including military uniforms. Students who don't want to see Armed Forces members in uniform can be accommodated in a way that makes them feel safe, she said in the email. Webster did not immediately respond to a request for comment, and a reply from a spokesperson for the school offered no new information.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A school in the Halifax area has backed away from its request that service members not wear military uniforms tothe school's Remembrance Day ceremony. The principal said the request was out of concern for some students who come from countries experiencing conflict and who she said expressed discomfort with images of war.Andrew Vaughan The Canadian Press

Document 178 of 500

$4,000 for the upper bowl: Why Taylor Swift ticket prices are so expensive in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/4-000-upper-bowl-why-taylor-swift-ticket-prices/docview/3126421663/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%244%2C000+for+the+upper+bowl%3A+Why+Taylor+Swift+ticket+prices+are+so+expensive+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before Taylor Swift tickets went on presale, Maha Ansari asked about 10 family members and friends to sign up as verified fans on her behalf to help secure tickets. A year later, she is still on the wait-list.
While checking secondary ticketing platforms and Facebook groups every day, Ansari, a Toronto lawyer who lives minutes away from Rogers Centre, was put off by the skyrocketing prices of resale tickets.
For Swift's first Nov. 14 show in Toronto, tickets for seats in the upper bowl were offered for at least $4,000 on StubHub, more than 20 times the presale price.
"I still don't think I can justify spending $4,000 on one night of my life to see a concert," said Ansari.
With a budget of $2,000, her hopes grew dim as the concert date drew closer. Ansari said she and many fans desperate to see the star continued to receive scam messages on social media trying to trick them into paying large deposits.
"As a Swiftie, I am heartbroken ... As a lawyer, I'm disappointed in the Canadian government, Ontario's government, their failure to really regulate the ticket resale and the ticket pricing market," said Ansari.
Ontario's previous Liberal government introduced legislation in 2018 to outlaw computer "bots" that scoop up huge blocks of tickets to concerts and cap resale markups at 50 per cent above face value. However, the resale markup cap was scraped from the law as "unenforceable" after Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives came into office.
Ansari said she was disheartened that not much progress had been made on the legal front since the cap was lifted. On the other hand, she saw Swifties flying overseas to Europe to see the superstar perform as they found the resale tickets there less costly and there is legislation against for-profit resale.
Is the removal of the resale cap to blame for the high resale prices? Here's what the government and experts say about it.
Why are resale tickets so expensive?Supply and demand explain the exorbitant price of the resale tickets in the secondary market, said Catherine Moore, a music professor at the University of Toronto.
"If there are people out there willing to pay these prices, then people in the resale market are going to offer them at these prices," she said.
What's different from previous shows, Moore added, is that Toronto and Vancouver dates are the last two shows in The Eras Tour and a number of people from outside Canada are also peeking at the market.
Kelly Cronin from New Hampshire is one of the overseas fans who want to secure a Toronto ticket. She went to see Swift perform in Los Angeles last year by buying a solo resale ticket at US$850 four days before the show.
"The appeal to go again is that she changed her set list when she launched her new album," said Cronin.
Cronin said she has booked the hotels and flights to Toronto but hasn't found any tickets that fit her US$2,500 budget.
How feasible is it to cap the price of resale tickets?While many entertainment consumers are in favour of a cap on the price of resale tickets, researchers who study ticketing warned that in practice, people can always find ways to get around the legislation.
When overpriced tickets can't be sold on resale platforms, transactions can easily flow into a grey market where ticket sellers charge extra, and people agree to higher prices, said Pascal Courty, an economist at the University of Victoria.
"In the end, the market exists, but it's in a much more grey area and with more fraud and dysfunctionality," he said.
These underground activities are hard to detect because resale is essentially a transaction that benefits both the buyer and the seller, said Phil Cyrenne, an economist at the University of Winnipeg. "If it's going to make both of them better off, they really don't have an incentive to report it."
The law is also difficult to enforce when transactions occur outside of a jurisdiction. A ban on reselling tickets at higher prices in Manitoba became history in 2023 after the provincial government found it put its local resellers at a disadvantage when much of the reselling is conducted out of province, the Canadian Press reported.
In a statement, Ticketmaster said it believes capping concert resale prices is the only way to remove the resale incentive, adding that the company supports further regulations.
The Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement did not answer the Star's questions on the potential return of a resale cap.
The government "requires those who facilitate the resale of a ticket for more than its face value to provide the purchaser with either a confirmation from the primary seller that the ticket is valid or a money-back guarantee," said spokesperson Matthew D'Amico.
How are other countries doing?For-profit ticket resale is banned in some European countries including Ireland, Italy, Belgium, Norway, Poland, Denmark and France.
In France, it is illegal for anyone to resell their event tickets above face value and only the promoter or ticket platforms authorized by the promoter are allowed to resell tickets. Unauthorized resale is deemed as a criminal offence and can attract a fine equivalent to about $22,500.
While for-profit ticket resales are not banned in Germany, event promoters can include a clause in the ticket's terms and conditions prohibiting resale above 25 per cent of its face value. These tickets must be personalized with a box on the ticket for the buyer's name.
Asked about the laws in France, Courty said such legislation does deter some people from reselling for profit, but it won't solve the whole problem
"At least authorities should systematically track the websites where resale for profit happens, which is rarely happening, I believe," he said.
How effective is the ban on bots?When the Tories reviewed the Ticket Sales Act in 2019, they kept the bot prohibition intact and increased fines for non-compliance with the act from $10,000 to $25,000, but it's hard to tell how effective the ban has been.
A few hours after Swift's Toronto show went on sale in Ticketmaster in August 2023, tickets appeared on resale sites like SeatGeek and StubHub, with prices ranging from thousands to hundreds of thousands of dollars.
It's not clear if any of this activity is inappropriate, but enforcing the law requires co-operation from ticket-selling platforms, said Courty. For example, StubHub could ask vendors to reveal their identity in order to detect large-scale reselling through the same person. (The part of the law explicitly restricting this was removed, along with resale markup cap.)
Ticketmaster required fans to secure the presale code to purchase tickets through the Ticketmaster Verified program, which is aimed at cracking down on resellers. Following a surge in reported scams, the platform implemented a new rule that ticket transfers for Swift's concerts can only begin 72 hours before the event.
"StubHub reported that fan sellers doubled for this tour, indicating our success in initially getting tickets to fans," Ticketmaster said.
A new way out?One approach China has taken to eliminate for-profit ticket resale that could also apply in Canada is to tie tickets to ID for major, rare events like the Eras Tour, which have excess demand, Courty said.
"The best way to do that is to have limited tickets with one ticket, one name, one admission, and you check identity at the gate," he said, thus completely prohibiting the resale and exchange of tickets while still allowing people to get refunds.
Cyrenne agrees with the approach, saying it would take a lot of manpower and resources to police the rule, and he was unsure if concert promoters would have the incentive to go through the hassle.
"Taylor Swift and those other people could do that, but it'll take a long time for 30,000 people to go through and show them their ID."
Some artists have attempted to fight with ticket resellers by making special rules for their tours. When Billie Eilish's 2024 tour tickets went on sale, she restricted her tour tickets from transfer and only allowed fans to resell tickets at face value on Ticketmaster.
The tour wants to give fans, not resellers, "the best chance to buy tickets at face value," the ticketing website read.
CAPTION: Resale ticket offerings for Taylor Swift's first Toronto concert date at Rogers Centre, seen here in a Nov. 8 screengrab from StubHub.Taylor Swift fans poseoutside Wembley Stadium in London on June 21, 2024 in London.You can still buy tickets to Taylor Swift's Toronto hotly anticipated concert series - you'll just have to stomach the cost of buying on the resale market.

Location: United States--US; Canada; France

People: Swift, Taylor

Company / organization: Name: Ticketmaster Entertainment Inc; NAICS: 561599; Name: StubHub Inc; NAICS: 561599

Title: $4,000 for the upper bowl: Why Taylor Swift ticket prices are so expensive in Toronto

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 8, 2024

Section: entertainment

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 179 of 500

Give us housing money before Pierre Poilievre can block it, GTA mayors tell Trudeau government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/give-us-housing-money-before-pierre-poilievre-can/docview/3126421598/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Give+us+housing+money+before+Pierre+Poilievre+can+block+it%2C+GTA+mayors+tell+Trudeau+government&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Several GTA mayors say they support a new request from Mississauga for the federal government to provide all funds in a major housing program now, as the Liberals ring the alarm over Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's plan to scrap the initiative to help pay for his proposed tax cut to boost home construction.
But federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser's office was quick to cast doubt on the idea, telling the Star that the $4.4-billion Housing Accelerator Fund is meant to roll out in tranches until 2028 to make sure cities spend the money on ways to speed up homebuilding.
"If we disbursed all of the cash at once, we would have no way to ensure that communities are on track to meet the ambitious goals outlined in their agreements," said a statement sent by Fraser's spokesperson, Matthew Dillon-Leitch.
It's the latest development in the political battle over housing policy at the federal level, as the opposition parties rail against Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal government, with the Conservatives blaming it for the surge in housing costs in recent years.
In October, Poilievre promised his party would eliminate the federal sales tax on new-built homes that sell for less than $1 million. The Conservative leader said this would cost roughly $4 billion per year and boost annual home construction by 30,000 units.
To cover the cost, the Conservatives said they would save billions of dollars by cutting the Housing Accelerator Fund and another $6-billion program to fund construction of water and sewer systems for housing developments. Outside of Quebec, both programs involve municipalities applying directly to Ottawa for funding, rather than having the money flow through provincial governments.
In an interview with the Star, Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish said she believes cities are being used as "battering rams" as the major federal parties gear up for the next election campaign. After Poilievre's pledge to cut the programs, Fraser penned more than 141 letters to municipal leaders, warning them that millions of dollars in promised funding for housing was at risk under Poilievre's plan. His office has also tried to expose Conservative MPs who have expressed a desire for cities in their ridings to get money from the program their party promises to cut.
In her response to Fraser, Parrish said the Liberal government should provide all of the Housing Accelerator Fund money up front, instead of giving it out in chunks, so that the program is saved from the Conservative chopping block. Mississauga is supposed to receive $113 million under the program; like other cities, it has been given 25 per cent up front, with the rest slated to come if the city hits agreed-upon milestones in the coming years.
According to Fraser's office, $1.2 billion of the program's $4.4-billion funding has been distributed so far, while the housing infrastructure fund started taking municipal applications this week.
"Send us the other three quarters, just in case the worst happens," Parrish said.
"If you're very worried and you don't have the confidence you're going to win the next election, you should pay the rest of it out now."
The Star spoke with four other GTA mayors, all of whom expressed concerns about the program getting cut and agreed with Mississauga's call for all of the money to be provided sooner. They said the program is helping their municipalities streamline approval processed for construction permits, and is paying for initiatives to build more affordable housing.
The Conservatives, meanwhile, have argued the housing accelerator is a failure that provides nothing more than "photo ops," pointing to statistics that show new housing starts are lower in some cities than they were last year. The Liberal government says the program is estimated to help create 750,000 new homes over the next decade, in part by helping cities speed up permitting of new construction.
Shaun Collier, the mayor of Ajax, Ont., said slashing the program would have "significant effects" and could force his town to hike property taxes if the cuts come partway through construction that the money is earmarked to pay for, including a new community centre. Collier said he expects Ontario's 29 "big city" mayors to discuss the situation at their next meeting on Dec. 6.
"As you know, municipalities are not able to run a deficit. We're not about to abandon a project. We would have to go back to the taxpayers, potentially in the form of a tax increase," he said.
Whitby Mayor Elizabeth Roy said her town wouldn't be able to afford initiatives like fast-tracking adoption of electric permits and donating municipal land for social housing construction without the almost $25 million it is slated to get through the accelerator fund. Roy said she agrees "100 per cent" with Mississauga's request to get all the money up front.
In Richmond Hill, Mayor David West said his community has already spent more than a year working on a plan, soon to be released, that will include how it intends to use the $31-million it is supposed to get through the Housing Accelerator Fund. Cutting the program would not only prevent the city from implementing initiatives like streamlined construction permits, but would also mean wasted resources on planning, he said.
While West added that he appreciates the "checks and balances" built into how the program doles out money based on performance milestones, he said he would have to support getting the money up front if it is truly threatened by the Conservatives' promised cuts.
"That would be the best alternative if the worst thing happened," he said.
To Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, the situation shows the need for better housing programs to support cities that aren't set up for cities to apply for funding and hope that it comes.
"You can't plan long-term infrastructure that way. You can't plan cities that way. The government should be direct-delivering dollars to municipalities under a new funding deal," Meed Ward said.
"Don't just show us the money, give us the money."
CAPTION: Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish is calling on the federal government to give municipalities all of the money it has promised them to spur new housing development.

Subject: Construction; Conservatism; Mayors; Funding; Building permits; Housing starts; Cities; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing starts Tax increases

Document 180 of 500

How the rise of remote work may have played a role in handing Trump the White House

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-rise-remote-work-may-have-played-role-handing/docview/3126421567/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+the+rise+of+remote+work+may+have+played+a+role+in+handing+Trump+the+White+House&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Following Donald Trump's decisive win in the 2024 U.S. presidential election, remote work migration has emerged as an unexpected factor favouring the Republican Party.
While a study by the Centre for Economic Policy Research initially suggested that remote work could weaken Republican strongholds by enabling people to relocate from Democratic urban centres to more affordable areas, the opposite effect appears to have strengthened Trump's support in critical states.
The flexibility introduced by remote work allowed Americans from high-cost, left-leaning states like California and New York to move to suburban or rural areas with lower costs and, frequently, Republican-leaning communities.
Census Bureau data show a fourfold increase in Americans working from home over the past three years, fuelling a 12 per cent rise in interstate moves since 2019. This migration pattern led many mobile voters to explore new living options outside of densely populated Democratic hubs, yet, rather than eroding the conservative base in traditionally red states, this shift appears to have consolidated it, amplifying Trump's appeal.
The trend holds particular significance for states like Texas and Florida, where an influx of remote workers from blue states had sparked Democratic hopes of a shift. However, rather than bringing progressive values, many new residents have aligned with the prevailing political culture in their new communities.
In Texas and Florida, the influx of newcomers has not only reinforced conservative support but also helped solidify these states as Republican strongholds. In states like Idaho, conservative transplants from California, in particular, have further bolstered the local GOP base, thwarting any anticipated progressive shift.
This "doughnut effect" - a pattern of migration from urban centres to suburban and rural areas - has reshaped political demographics across regions without destabilizing state-level Republican dominance. For instance, in suburban areas around Democratic cities like New York and San Francisco, migration has brought a modest diversification in voter profiles.
Yet, rather than making these areas politically mixed, the influx often consolidates the existing voting blocs, reinforcing either Democratic or Republican tendencies, depending on the area's broader ideological landscape.
Remote workers now represent a unique voter demographic that prioritizes lifestyle and affordability over proximity to workplaces. Younger workers, in particular, are drawn to remote work's lifestyle flexibility, moving to areas with different political climates that sometimes shift their political inclinations.
Although these mobile voters sought areas with diverse political landscapes, the effect on voting patterns has been more conservative-leaning than initially projected.
For Republicans, these migration trends represent an opportunity to appeal to a geographically diverse and ideologically mixed voter base that has found alignment with conservative values in new regions.
The election has demonstrated that remote worker mobility is not a straightforward predictor of Democratic support, as expected shifts in key states largely reinforced Republican gains.
Campaigns now face the reality that voter mobility has added layers of complexity and unpredictability, which may favour conservatives in many regions.
Ultimately, this election cycle has underscored a new era of U.S. politics shaped by the interplay of remote work and voter mobility. America's political landscape is now defined by a more mobile electorate, which challenges long-standing assumptions about party loyalties and regional strongholds.
Trump's victory illustrates how demographic shifts tied to remote work can bolster Republican influence in critical areas.
The emerging dynamic suggests that, as political strategies evolve to account for this more flexible and mobile voter base, the Republicans may stand to benefit from the lasting influence of remote work on the American electorate.
CAPTION: There has been a 12 per cent rise in interstate moves since 2019 as many urban voters went rural, writes Gleb Tsipursky. This shift appears to have weakened Democratic urban hubs while consolidating Trump's appeal in rural constituencies.

Subject: Workers; Migration; Demographics; Suburban areas; Political parties; Work at home; Political campaigns; Rural areas; Voters

Business indexing term: Subject: Workers Work at home

Location: Texas; New York; United States--US; California; Florida

Company / organization: Name: Republican Party; NAICS: 813940

Document 181 of 500

Voters are the losers in too many elections. Here's the solution

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/voters-are-losers-too-many-elections-heres/docview/3126341716/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+are+the+losers+in+too+many+elections.+Here%27s+the+solution&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Forget the U.S. election for a moment - it's out of our hands anyway.
Focus instead on an election closer to home that also took place earlier this week, in the shadow of the American campaign. While all eyes were on Donald Trump's triumph, a bitterly contested municipal race in Toronto's Don Valley West looked awfully good for local progressives.
But look a little closer, because there are sobering lessons for our own democratic elections going forward - how we choose our future leaders across this country.
On the surface, Monday night's local results seemed like a pleasing counterpoint to the bigger battle south of the border Tuesday night: Rachel Chernos Lin, a progressive backed by centre-left Liberals, won a convincing victory against a provocateur and polemicist favoured by right-leaning Tories, Anthony Furey.
A good night for progressives and their preferred candidate? Perhaps this time, but not necessarily next time.
In fact, until the final few days of the campaign, the results could easily have gone the other way - for reasons largely beyond the control of Chernos Lin. That's because her biggest challenge in Ward 15 came not from her ideological opponent, Furey, but from several of her rival Liberals - which is to say, fellow Liberals - siphoning progressive votes away from her.
Ultimately, these also-rans pulled out under pressure from party elders keen to prevent them from splitting the vote and allowing Furey -- a controversial former Toronto Sun columnist - to win by default.
But this outcome doesn't so much qualify as a happy ending as a haphazard election.
By splitting the vote, time after time, progressives keep repeating the same mistake - not merely municipally, but especially provincially and federally. When New Democrats and Liberals compete against one another, they are often hurting themselves more than the Tories.
In politics, egotism and obstinacy often get in the way of progress, even among progressives. How can our democracy faithfully reflect - and respect - people's preferences?
Think of the voter. And think of Ward 15's byelection as a thought experiment in Canadian democracy.
Here in Toronto, a behind-the-scenes battle took place after the death of longtime city councillor Jaye Robinson. Local trustee Chernos Lin, as chair of the Toronto District School Board, was the clear front-runner, but she had to watch her flank.
Two Liberal loyalists also entered the race: Dhruv Jain, who worked as executive assistant to the riding's Liberal MPP, Stephanie Bowman; and Evan Sambasivam, a former federal Liberal staffer.
That's a lot of vote-splitting for one ward. Bowman and former premier Kathleen Wynne (her predecessor as MPP) beseeched the progressive rivals to settle on a winning candidate - the high-profile Chernos Lin - rather than running one another into the ground.
Jain and Sambasivam at first refused to fold. Belatedly, as their prospects grew dimmer, the two Liberal holdouts grudgingly folded their cards, allowing Chernos Lin to scoop up a commanding 54.8 per cent of the vote, compared to 31.1 per cent for Furey.
Had they not withdrawn, siphoning votes from Chernos Lin, Furey might well have triumphed with that modest plurality of 31.1 per cent. But why must we depend on party elders to strong-arm various headstrong also-rans to bow out so that the popular choice might prevail?
Isn't there a better way for voters to have their democratic say?
Remarkably, the ranked ballot - also known as an electronic runoff - is designed to achieve precisely that democratic outcome. Rather than allowing an ideological outlier to win with a plurality of, say, one-third of the vote, the ranked ballot requires a true majority of at least 50 per cent.
It achieves this by inviting voters to "rank" their choices by number on the ballot; if no candidate achieves a majority, the lowest-performing candidate is dropped and their votes redistributed until someone gets over 50 per cent.
That's what would have, could have happened in Don Valley West last week with a ranked ballot: Instead of laggards being cajoled into withdrawing voluntarily and randomly, you'd see it automatically, electronically and democratically.
A runoff is the best reflection of the people's will, which is why every major political party keeps the balloting going at a leadership convention until someone emerges as the final choice. If it's good enough for the Tories, Liberals and NDP when choosing their party leaders, why not offer it up to the rest of us at election time?
That's the system San Francisco used this week to elect a new mayor. Why not Toronto?
As premier, Wynne approved a pilot project for municipalities to try the ranked ballot, but Doug Ford nixed it when he defeated her in 2018. Tories prefer vote-splitting, because they always profit from progressive infighting - not just in general elections, but in electoral reform debates when the proportional representation brigade fights passionately against ranked ballots.
The voters of Don Valley got their wish this week, but it almost didn't happen. A ranked ballot system would give us the clarity that a democracy deserves.
CAPTION: Ward 15 byelection candidate Rachel Chernos Lin casts her ballot in Toronto on Nov. 4, 2024.

Subject: Electoral reform; Leadership; Progressivism; Democracy; Voters; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Title: Voters are the losers in too many elections. Here's the solution
