Document 199 of 500

For progressives, there's a silver lining to Donald Trump's victory

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/progressives-theres-silver-lining-donald-trumps/docview/3124645841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+progressives%2C+there%27s+a+silver+lining+to+Donald+Trump%27s+victory&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive-leaning voters are going to be looking for some kind of silver lining in Donald Trump's return to power in the United States.
Here is one: all those people whose faith in democracy was shaken by Trump's loss four years ago, braced to declare this 2024 election corrupt too, can no longer complain that the system is rigged against them.
Democracy, in other words, has prevailed, even with a leader who is poised to shake it to its foundations and even with Trump's declared promise to be a dictator on day one of his presidency.
Trump had been carefully laying the groundwork to declare any victory by Kamala Harris as proof that Democrats cheat.
"The only thing that can stop us is the cheating. It's the only thing that can stop us," Trump told supporters late last week in Arizona. Judging by the person-in-the-street interviews being broadcast on the news in recent days, that allegation was being swallowed whole by Trump's base.
So had Harris won, it was almost inevitable that Trump supporters would rebel, perhaps in larger and more violent ways than the Jan. 6, 2021 storming of Capitol Hill. Trump's victory, then, has at least spared the world that kind of spectacle.
But there will be a different kind of chaos. Trump himself forecast it during his flurry of end-of-campaign appearances over the weekend. "You watch. It's going to be so good. It's going to be so much fun. It'll be nasty a little bit at times, and maybe at the beginning, in particular," Trump said in Georgia, one of the many states that helped send him back to office. "But it's going to be something."
Here in Canada, there will be reverberations for Justin Trudeau's government, on the policy and political front.
Trudeau and his cabinet have been preparing for a possible Trump victory. Immigration Minister Marc Miller delivered a flat "yes" when asked in the Commons last week whether he was braced for a post-Trump flood of asylum seekers to this country.
Trudeau's team has also kept its lines of communication open with Trump's circle, even with him out of power, and those conversations will intensify in the days and weeks ahead.
There's an assumption that there's personal antagonism between Trump and Trudeau, but in fact, the two men had many cordial conversations after the 2018 blow-up, when the former president called Trudeau "weak" and "dishonest." This summer, they had a friendly chat when Trudeau reached out to convey sympathy for the assassination attempt on Trump.
The prime minister was prepared this week, naturally, with two sets of remarks to make in the wake of Tuesday's election. Had Harris won, Trudeau probably would have been more expansive on the progressive alliance between Democrats and Liberals.
His statement on Trump's victory, though, was also convivial. "The friendship between Canada and the U.S. is the envy of the world," the prime minister posted on X early Wednesday morning. "I know President Trump and I will work together to create more opportunity, prosperity, and security for both of our nations."
They're going to need all the friendliness they can muster - Trump's promises for sweeping tariffs could hit Canada hard.
The hard-won, renegotiated free-trade deal between Canada, the U.S. and Mexico is also up for review in 2026, halfway through Trump's next presidency, and that could prompt another existential crisis in the Canada-U.S. trading relationship.
But if the current polls hold in Canada, it won't be Trudeau overseeing that review, but Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Poilievre is going to be facing a lot of questions in the coming days about how he would deal with Trump, and why Canadians should expect him to handle a disruptive president with minimal damage to this country.
Conservatives will, of course, be taking heart from the fact that the United States was ready for a rightward turn and hoping Canada is in the same mood. They will also realize it is a bit more politically awkward for Trudeau and his team to cast Conservatives as the northern branch of Trump Republicanism, given that Canada has to maintain civil ties with the re-elected president.
On the other hand, if Trump's return to power does unleash the chaos that seems likely, Canadians could be looking for the kind of stability that an incumbent government, experienced at dealing with Trump, could provide. That will be a faint hope that Liberals will cling to in the days ahead.
Someone recently offered me this metaphor for how Canada might react to a Trump victory. "It will be like seeing your neighbour go off the wagon again," he said. For progressive voters, that feels about right. At least there won't be another storm-the-Capitol party.
CAPTION: Donald Trump smiles at an election night watch party at the Palm Beach Convention Center, Wednesday, Nov. 6, 2024, in West Palm Beach, Fla.

Subject: Political campaigns; Presidential elections; Voters; Verbal communication; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: For progressives, there's a silver lining to Donald Trump's victory

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 6, 2024

Section: politics/political-opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Document 200 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3124325109/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Providing a
positive message
Re: He's been in jail. He's using drugs. But a new court program sees his potential, Oct. 31
Thanks to Hamilton Spectator reporter Susan Clairmont for the excellent articles entitled "Second Chances."
As a retired mental health social worker who worked at East Region Mental Health Services, I thought the article provided a really positive message in an area where there is often a lot of tragedy and failures.
The interplay between social problems, addictions and mental health illness is often confusing and complex.
Chris Cardey, Hamilton
What happened to public safety?
Re: 'Stay in your lane': Premier Doug Ford's bike lane restrictions spurs loud opposition, Nov. 4
City of Toronto data found that, following installation of bike lanes on Bloor Street, "safety improved for all (road) users." So, Ontario's attack on bike lanes is an attack on security of the person. This is consistent with the province's other reckless actions.
After allowing the biggest expansion in alcohol sales since Prohibition ended, Queen's Park increased highway speeds to 110 km/h and is considering raising them to more than 120 km/h. Driving is often deadly already. It seems Ontario sees no problem in making it more dangerous still. Where is common sense in all of this? Is there simply no regard for public safety?
Gideon Forman, Toronto
Cyclists should ride with traffic
Re: Premier Doug Ford's bike lane restrictions spurs loud opposition, Nov. 4
I am neither for or against bike lanes. But I had to laugh at the picture on the front page. A cyclist going the wrong way on the bike lane. As we all know, or should know, cyclists are supposed to ride their bikes with traffic (and stop at stop signs and lights), and pedestrians should walk facing traffic. Unfortunately, many break these common sense rules.
Brian Cross, Burlington
Gun found after fire is worrisome
Re: Police find pieces of a long gun in the debris after tent fire at the Delta, Nov. 3
Another fire in an encampment occurred on Sunday. Not in Gage Park this time, but the Delta parkette. The Spectator also reported that police found evidence of a gun in the debris. Does anyone else find this alarming?
I would like to amend my suggestion from my letter to the editor in August recommending searches/confiscation of propane tanks in encampments; I would like to amend that suggestion to include search and seizure of firearms. And while we're at it, illegal drugs. Drugs and firearms do not mix.
Anne Budzynski, Hamilton
City has forgotten the majority
Three hundred people in encampments have taken the use of our parks away from half a million Hamiltonians. A vanishingly tiny number of cyclists who pursue their "passion" half of the year have impeded everyone else's ability to traverse this city. Count me among those who are now prepared to stand and say we're through being intimidated by the tyranny of the minority.
Reg McGuire, Mount Hope
No one answering the phones?
Recently - as confirmed by many people - one cannot contact the Mountain police station via telephone on many occasions. This inability to reach the Rymal station is an absolute outrage. No amount of reasoning by the police can quell this policy of "no one answering." Why have a police station at all if this nonsense continues?
Unless it is high profile, the police seem to pick and choose their responsibilities.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
Something
isn't adding up
Re: Council house reno restores 'gathering point' for Mississaugas of the Credit community, Nov. 1
Congratulations to the Mississaugas of the Credit for the beautiful renovation of their council house. What I find amazing is all the renovations cost $1.8 million. This begs the question; it will cost over a $1 million to build a washroom in one of Hamilton's parks? Something doesn't add up. Am I missing something?
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Binbrook is in
need of attention
Hamilton is actively severing off one of the fastest growing communities in Hamilton. It appears that development of public transport which will make Hamilton a "vibrant, booming city" has been a major priority on the minds of Hamilton's developers for the past few years.
Why is it then that a growing community full of young families such as Binbrook is still yet to receive a bus route more than 13 years after being amalgamated into Hamilton. Hamilton has extended its urban sprawl into what once was a quaint farming community, but time and time again it has shown it is afraid to act on this mistake. Gas stations have been in supposed development for years, and yet townhomes spring up like dandelions.
No one goes to Binbrook to shop or for work or even to get gas. It is a dead-end town. A rapidly growing population of over 10,000 people cannot continue to rely on the single-laned Highway 56 to take them to and from work and school every day. This is a road that has no bus routes, no sidewalks and is perpetually under construction.
So much effort, time and money has been put into revitalizing Hamilton into a "vibrant, booming city where more Canadians are choosing to live, work and play than ever before" and yet we can't even build the necessary infrastructure for those who already live here.
Daniel Lemieux, Hamilton
Silver lining
in controversy
Re: Booze, flights and hotels. Here's how four Catholic school trustees blew through $50,000 in one week, Nov. 1
The only good thing from this total lack of good judgment is the fact that the Spectator has repeatedly printed an image of Saint Padre Pio. Padre Pio was the son of Italian peasant farmers and shepherded a small flock of sheep for the family.
As a Capuchin friar he took the vows of poverty, chastity and obedience. He lived an exemplary life while suffering through two world wars. More than 100,000 people attended his funeral. Now back to the decisions of Chair Rick Petrella and the other trustees.
Joe Benedetti, Hamilton
Cootes Paradise at critical point
Re: Budget for Dundas sewage treatment plant overhaul spikes to $252M, Oct. 3
Kayaking in Cootes Paradise, I felt so at peace listening to the lapping water, eyeing a pair of sunbathing swans. Something bobbed out from underneath a patch of lily pads and I did a double take, locking eyes with a dead fish floating beside me. Jarred from my reverie of birdwatching, it felt like the lake's cry for help.
The proposed $252-million update to Dundas' sewage treatment plant, that filters waters feeding into Cootes Paradise, is crucial to preserving an invaluable ecosystem. I was horrified to learn that Cootes, as part of Hamilton Harbour, is an internationally recognized area of concern for pollution and experts say we are at a "critical" point to save it. The plant's new bioreactor will more thoroughly filter sewage and drastically reduce the amount of phosphorus flowing into Cootes, which "means more oxygen for fish, better water clarity, and much less algae," according to the head of ecosystem restoration at the Royal Botanical Gardens. While the new plant is expensive, it will be cheaper in the long-term. But like anything with higher upfront costs, I wouldn't be surprised if it doesn't get approved by city councillors. Already Alex Wilson is more concerned about protecting Dundas' little league baseball diamond, which will have to be relocated for the new treatment plant. Little league can use one of the countless other diamonds; there are 22 baseball diamonds to choose from in Dundas alone - there is only one Cootes Paradise.
Jules Ciulpan, Hamilton
Higher taxes
impact housing
Yes, I need to gripe about residential property taxes and the poor people it creates; the correlation between higher taxes and higher numbers of poor people. You cannot talk about creating affordable housing and increasing property taxes at the same time; you cannot talk about keeping rents down and asking more from taxpayers. Higher property taxes increase rental rates and reduces affordability. Hopefully Hamilton mayor and council understand.
Roman Radojewski,
Hamilton
Direct funds where needed
So many Ontario taxpayers will receive $200. Well, I have a roof over my head and groceries in the fridge and you do not see me running out of any must-have necessities any time soon, Mr. Ford.
That being said, there is a call for food at pretty much every food bank in Ontario and needs at most all shelters in Ontario, but cheques are going out to so many people that will just deposit it in their accounts and life will go on. Give the money where it is urgently needed, please! Say, health care or education. What an incredibly stupid use of tax money.
Bradford Widerman,
Stoney Creek
Council must live within its means
Congratulations to the recent letters regarding a need for greater fiscal responsibility at city hall. Exorbitant tax increases have created a residential affordability crisis. Both citizens and businesses are being forced to find efficiencies while council refuses to prioritize spending until we get our house in order. "Solve the crisis" indeed. What will it take for council to realize it has an integral role to play in keeping Hamilton affordable for all its citizens? What is the council's plan for a future where both seniors and those less fortunate have to default on their taxes? It's time for tax increases be tied to the cost of living forcing council to live within its means. This era of rampant entitled spending needs to end.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Does political neutrality help?
Entering the field of teaching I have consistently been told that as a teacher I need to be politically neutral. Whether it's talking about your party alignment, the working class or international issues such as Palestine, we're constantly told we shouldn't bring up politics in our classroom. But what does it mean to be political? Matters of the past are constantly becoming curriculum today, and rightfully so. There's a relatively direct focus on inclusive education, yet somehow talking about current events in an honest manner still appears to be taboo in the profession.
What could possibly be more important in the classroom than social justice and preparing our students to fight for a better world? There are hundreds of issues that we as a society need to be doing better on, and some of them, like climate change, could determine the planet's future. At almost no point in history has neutrality been the best position.
As teachers, we need to challenge our students to do better than we are right now, not to tow the same line that is causing our issues.
Chance Rashid, Hamilton
Life seems to
hold little value
Re: Assisted suicide is being used to relieve people of poverty, isolation and social suffering. This is not OK, Nov. 4
I felt Andrew Phillips made a big omission by not mentioning how little we value life in this world. Where is our horror when dozens of women and children die and are maimed on a daily basis in just one war and how many wars are going on around the world right now? How many Canadians have pleaded with the prime minister to convene a world conference to end all wars? Probably very few. It's a fact of life that we are a bloody species, but a closer look at nature shows that blood and death is the way of the world for all species.
Also, Andrew shouldn't second guess psychiatric advice to patients. Has he lived the hell that other people have in their bodies and minds? I doubt it.
Aldo Statti, Burlington

Document 201 of 500

Ontario expected to share procurement plans for long-delayed Hamilton LRT project Wednesday

Full text: Ontario is poised to start vetting prospective bidders for early construction on Hamilton's long-delayed, off-and-on-again light-rail transit project, The Spectator has learned.
Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria is slated to join Mayor Andrea Horwath in Hamilton on Wednesday to make an unspecified announcement at 2:30 p.m., according to a ministry release.
But sources say the province is expected to share long-anticipated plans for an LRT-related "request for qualifications" process - basically the screening of prospective contractors or consortia ahead of formally going to tender on the first stage of construction.
Specifically, that first stage is expected to include a package of "civil works" to dig up and relocate utilities along the Main-King LRT corridor, as well as build or reconstruct bridges, roads and sidewalks associated with project.
A separate, later phase of construction bidding is expected to follow focused on building the actual rail line, signals, LRT cars and other transit-specific construction.
So far, public LRT designs call for a 14-kilometre rail line connecting McMaster University to Eastgate Square - mostly along the Main-King-Queenston corridor, but with a new jog up-and-down Dundurn Street.
Metrolinx, the province's regional transit agency in charge of the project, made public its plan to split the massive, multi-year construction into at least two phases as far back as 2022.
But at that point, the agency also circulated a project schedule suggesting major construction could start as early as this year - which did not happen.
No public reason for the procurement delay has ever been provided, but The Spectator has reported on provincial documents showing the cost of rapid transit projects has skyrocketed since the pandemic due to inflation and other factors.
It is not clear whether the $3.4-billion in construction money provided by the province and federal governments is still considered sufficient to cover total project building costs.
The project - first funded by the provincial Liberals in 2015 - was famously cancelled by the Tory government over budget concerns in 2019, only to be resurrected with joint provincial-federal dollars less than two years later.
Over that period, around 60 buildings along the Main-King corridor have been purchased and knocked down, with another 30 expected to be bought or expropriated before construction begins in earnest.
A documentary about the impact of LRT on dozens of tenants, home and business owners forced to relocate by the project - "Trainwreck" - recently screened at the Hamilton Film Festival.
More to come ...
Matthew Van Dongen is a transportation and environment reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Residents have been waiting on a timeline for LRT construction since the once-cancelled project was resurrected in 2021.

U.S. election live updates: Bomb threats at Georgia polling stations originated in Russia, official says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/u-s-election-live-updates-bomb-threats-at-georgia/docview/3124106678/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=U.S.+election+live+updates%3A+Bomb+threats+at+Georgia+polling+stations+originated+in+Russia%2C+official+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's election day in America. The U.S. presidential race between U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris and Donald Trump is expected to be extremely close. Follow our live rolling coverage today and read the Star's full coverage of the U.S. election. Read more coverage by Rosie DiManno and Richard Warnica.
3:40 p.m. Swing state Michigan on track to break voting records
In a morning press conference outside a polling station in Detroit, Michigan Secretary of State Jocelyn Benson said the state is on track to break voter turnout records this year.
According to Benson, 45.8 per cent of registered active voters, or 3.3 million people, had either voted early or mailed in a ballot ahead of Election Day. In 2020, the state saw a 70 per cent turnout rate, with 5.5 million people voting in total.
3:20 p.m. 'My faith in this country has been so restored,' Walz says of 2024 experience
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz told reporters Tuesday that his experience as Vice President Kamala Harris' running mate "restored" his faith in the nation.
"This is truly a remarkable thing we do every four years," he said in a conversation with reporters outside his campaign plane. "It's democracy. It's messy. It's beautiful. It's the people."
"My faith in this country has been so restored," he added.
Walz said he had not talked with Harris on Election Day but projected confidence that they would prevail over former president Donald Trump.
"I just can't describe the difference in the vibe that is out there with folks and how hungry they are for something different," he said.
3:15 p.m. Trump refuses to say how he voted on Florida's abortion ballot measure
Asked about the measure, which would keep the state's six-week restriction in place, he avoided answering by simply saying he'd done "a great job bringing it back to the states."
The second time, he snapped at a reporter, saying: "You should stop talking about it."
Trump had previously indicated he would back the measure, but then changed his mind, saying he would vote against it.
The abortion measure would prevent lawmakers from passing any law that penalizes, prohibits, delays or restricts abortion until fetal viability, which doctors say is sometime after 21 weeks. If it's rejected, the state's current abortion law would stand.
2 p.m. Police have arrested a man trying to enter the U.S. Capitol with a torch and flare gun
U.S. Capitol Police say the man was stopped Tuesday during a security screening at the Capitol Visitor Center. Authorities say he smelled of fuel and was carrying the flare gun and torch.
Officials have cancelled public tours of the Capitol for the remainder of the day.
Police say they are still investigating.
The arrest comes as authorities are on heightened alert for security issues around the nation's capital and have increased patrols in areas downtown and near the White House around Election Day. Nearly four years ago, a mob of Donald Trump's supporters attacked the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021.
1:30 p.m. Bomb threats at Georgia polling stations from Russia, deemed non-credible
Bomb threats that briefly disrupted two polling stations in Georgia originated in Russia, the state's secretary of state said on Tuesday.
"We've heard some threats that were of Russian origin. I don't know how to describe that that's viable - we don't think they are, but in the interest of public safety, we always check that out, and we'll just continue to be very responsible when we hear about stuff like that," Georgia Secretary of State George Raffensberger said before the media.
"We identified the source, and it was from Russia."
Raffensberger also referenced another threatbelieved to have been carried out by Russia late last month that targeted Georgia's online absentee voter portal.
According to the Associated Press, that attack was an apparent attempt to crash the site ahead of the election. The attack was thwarted by state election officials.
"They're up to mischief, it seems, and they don't want us to have a smooth, fair and accurate election," Raffensberger continued at Tuesday's press conference.
"The FBI is aware of bomb threats to polling locations in several states, many of which appear to originate from Russian email domains. None of the threats have been determined to be credible thus far," the agency said in an X post Tuesday afternoon.
The bomb threats were among multiple disturbances that U.S. officials are tracking.
But Cait Conley, a senior adviser to the director of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, told reporters on a call Tuesday there were no national-level security incidents that were threatening to disrupt the election on a wide scale.
Officials continue to warn of what they say is an unprecedented level of foreign influence and disinformation that they expect will persist beyond Election Day.
1:15 p.m. Election day voting is going mostly smoothly with some scattered issues
Election Day voting unfolded largely smoothly across the nation Tuesday but with scattered reports of extreme weather, ballot printing errors and technical problems causing delays.
Most of the hiccups occurring by midday were "largely expected routine and planned-for events," said Cait Conley, senior adviser to the director of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, in a press briefing. She said the agency was not currently tracking any national, significant incidents impacting election security.
Helping voting run relatively smoothly on Election Day was the fact that tens of millions of Americans had already cast their ballots. Those included record numbers of voters in Georgia, North Carolina and other battleground states that could decide the winner.
More from the Associated Press
12:40 p.m. Trump says he won't challenge results - as long as it's 'fair'
Trump is suggesting he won't challenge the results of the election - as long as it's fair
"If it's a fair election, I'd be the first one to acknowledge," the results, Trump said, though what meets that definition wasn't clear.
Speaking to reporters after voting in Florida, Trump said that he had no plans to tell his supporters to refrain from violence should he lose.
"I don't have to tell them," because they "are not violent people," he said.
Trump planned to visit a nearby campaign office to thank those working on his behalf.
12:30 p.m. Man arrested in N.Y. town near border after threatening to burn down polling site
The man went to vote in the town of Fowler, N.Y., near the Canadian border at about 6:30 a.m., New York State Police said in a news release.
The man, who had previously been convicted of a felony, was told that he was ineligible to vote because he had not re-registered after being released from prison.
The man became irate and began threatening to return with a gun or to burn the place down, police said.
The man fled but was later picked up by state police and brought to the station for questioning. Charges against him were pending.
12 p.m. NFL, NBA facilities in use on Election Day
There are no NFL or NBA games today. Plenty of fans will be going to stadiums anyway.
At least 17 NFL and NBA facilities are either polling locations or ballot drop-off stations. Some teams even offered voters personalized "I Voted" stickers with team logos.
Tuesdays aren't game days in the NFL.
11:50 a.m. Florida's voter information website is experiencing issues
Florida voters turning to a state-run website to check their voter registration status were getting an error message Tuesday morning.
A spokesperson for Florida Secretary of State Cord Byrd confirmed the state's online Voter Information Lookup tool was experiencing technical issues but did not answer questions about what was causing the problem.
"We're working to resolve it," spokesperson Mark Ard said. "We're providing alternative websites and locations for voters to find their voter information, their precinct."
11:05 a.m. Foreign election interference issues quiet so far
Cait Conley, senior adviser to the director of the Cybersecurity Infrastructure and Security Agency, said during a briefing that "we are not currently tracking any national level, significant incidents impacting the security of our election infrastructure. We are tracking instances of extreme weather and other temporary infrastructure disruption to certain areas of the country, but these are largely expected routine and planned for events."
Conley said CISA, the FBI and intelligence communities did anticipate that foreign actors would try to influence the election later today and in the following weeks.
11 a.m. Opinion: My generation was supposed to put a woman in the White House. We've been waiting for Kamala Harris for too long
In all the anxiety about a possible second Donald Trump presidency, it's easy to forget that America might, in a historic first, elect a woman as president.
My female generation X friends and I cannot forget this dream. We have booked our Washington hotel for the January inauguration weekend. We have waited long enough. We are ready to celebrate.
But first, we need to apologize for the delay.
Read the column from Katharine Lake Berz
10:45 a.m. (updated) FBI 'aware' of fake news clip urging people not to vote
In a statement from Tuesday morning, the FBI said it was aware of two fake news clips using its name and insignia to promote false narratives around the election.
The first clip falsely reports the FBI said Americans should "vote remotely" due to a high terror threat at polling stations. Meanwhile, the second clip contains a fabricated FBI press release alleging that the management of five prisons in three swing states - Pennsylvania, Georgia and Arizona - rigged inmate voting.
Both video clips, the FBI said, are not authentic and their contents are false.
"Attempts to deceive the public with false content about FBI threat assessments and activities aim to undermine our democratic process and erode trust in the electoral system," the agency wrote.
10 a.m. Democrats hope to flip a reliably Republican Louisiana congressional seat with new boundaries.
In a critical election year, Democrats are looking to flip a once reliably Republican Louisiana congressional seat, where political boundaries were recently redrawn to form the state's second mostly Black congressional district.
With five people on the ballot for Louisiana's Sixth Congressional District, Democrats have thrown their support behind longtime politician Cleo Fields, 61. The state senator has been involved in state politics for three decades and served two terms in Congress after being elected in 1992.
9:30 a.m. Voters are gearing up to head to the polls to cast their ballots for either Donald Trump or Kamala Harris in one of the nation's most historic presidential races. They'll also be determining which party will control the House and Senate.
Read AP's live rolling coverage
9:17 a.m. Which celebrities have endorsed Trump or Harris?
Joe Rogan has endorsed Donald Trump in today's U.S. election. The podcaster - who has hosted both Trump and his running mate, U.S. Senator J.D. Vance, this election cycle on his widely-listened "The Joe Rogan Experience" - voiced his support for the Republican nominee on X late Monday night, writing that he believed Elon Musk had made the "most compelling case for Trump."
"I agree with (Musk) every step of the way," Rogan wrote.
Musk, for his part, has been a vocal supporter of the former president throughout the campaign, from attending multiple campaign events to offering $1 million to people who go out and vote for Trump.
Read the full story from the Star's Nathan Bawaan
9:15 a.m. A Republican lawyer who interned in the White House under Donald Trump is challenging Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis, the Georgia prosecutor who brought charges against the former president over efforts to overturn the 2020 presidential election.
Courtney Kramer worked in the White House counsel's office during the Trump presidency and is active in GOP organizations. She's the first Republican to run for district attorney in Fulton County since 2000.
Fulton County, which is home to 11 per cent of the state's electorate and includes most of the city of Atlanta, is a Democratic stronghold.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
9 a.m. Polls open across the U.S.
Polls opened across the nation Tuesday morning as voters faced a stark choice between two candidates who have offered drastically different temperaments and visions for the world's largest economy and dominant military power. Millions of Americans had already cast their ballots, voting by mail and early in-person voting.
8:50 a.m. What to watch for as the U.S. presidential votes are counted
The race to become America's 47th president looks like it will be a photo finish between Republican Donald Trump and Democrat Kamala Harris as Americans head to the polls Tuesday.
The two main candidates have been neck and neck through the last weeks of the campaign, but how they got to the finish line could not have been more different.
Harris, the current vice-president and a former U.S. senator and California attorney general, has pledged to create opportunities for the middle-class families, to support the push to restore abortion rights, and to heal the country after years of bitter political division.
Read the full story from the Star's Allan Woods
8 a.m. Canadians prepare for any outcome
Millions of Americans are heading to the polls Tuesday as a chaotic presidential campaign reaches its peak in a deeply divided United States, where voters in only a handful of battleground states will choose the country's path forward.
Vice-President Kamala Harris and former president Donald Trump have presented starkly different visions for America's future, but polling shows the two remain in a dead heat.
"Any election in the U.S. is important and impactful for us," said Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to the United States. "They are central to our economic prosperity. They are a vital security partner."
Read the full story from The Canadian Press
7:35 a.m. Opinion: Kamala Harris can't stop America from hating women
America hates women. Were I writing this for a major American newspaper - within days of an election that might deliver the nation its first woman president, or in general - I would likely be asked to hedge my assertion. "It may seem that America hates women," I would probably write, bequeathing women-hating newspaper readers just enough plausible deniability to hang onto their paid subscriptions.
Fortunately for all involved, I can drop those pretences here. That's because, outside of the U.S., the country's hatred for women is plain. As of the 2022 reversal of Roe v. Wade, abortion is fully banned in 13 states and heavily restricted in eight more; in parts of the country, women have been criminalized for miscarried pregnancies and stillbirths. The U.S. accounts for 70 per cent of female homicide victims from high-income countries and has the highest maternal mortality rate of the so-called "developed" world. Perhaps most telling of all, the country elected Donald Trump to be its president, and may do so again.
Read the column from Kelli Maria Korducki
7 a.m. American reaches Election Day
A presidential campaign marked by upheaval and rancour approached its finale on Election Day as Americans decided whether to send Donald Trump back to the White House or elevate Kamala Harris to the Oval Office.
Voters on Tuesday faced a stark choice between two candidates who have offered drastically different temperaments and visions for the world's largest economy and dominant military power.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
6:30 a.m. Opinion: 'Tomorrow! The sun's coming up now!' Inside Kamala Harris's overly complicated, utterly exhausting final act in Philadelphia
Kamala Harris's final argument to the American people - an exhausting and often baffling rally in Philadelphia - played out Monday night like a conservative fantasy of the worst that can happen when liberals are left in charge: It was expensive. It was overcomplicated. It left many of the people it purported to serve unsatisfied, annoyed and increasingly alienated as the night went on.
From a logistics point of view it was easily the worst large political event I have ever attended, and I covered Donald Trump in 2016. If it was a sign of how the broader Harris campaign was operating heading into election day, I fear it was a catastrophic one, at least for anyone desperate to move on from the last, endless decade of Donald Trump.
Read the full story from the Star's Richard Warnica
Can Canadians watch the election?
Canadians are eager to see how the results of the contentious U.S. presidential race between Kamala Harris and Donald Trump will shape the next four years.
But before they can get to that, they'll have to tune in on Tuesday - not so simple, thanks to a media landscape that's more fragmented than ever.
"A good chunk of Canadians are getting their news mostly from the internet," says Philip Mai, co-director of the Social Media Lab at the Ted Rogers School of Management.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
What will the weather be like in battleground states on Election Day
Weather conditions can be one factor in how many people vote in person on Election Day.
The strongest weather in the U.S. forecast for Tuesday is in Montana where there could be blizzard conditions, but that state is not a battleground and is strongly favoured to go for Donald Trump.
Key battleground states including Michigan and Wisconsin are expected to see some rainfall.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Monday 10 a.m. Doug Ford warns of the perils of protectionism on the eve of U.S. election
Ahead of Tuesday's U.S. presidential election, Premier Doug Ford is warning that protectionism would hurt Ontario, America's third-largest trading partner.
Ford - who has expressed concern about the threat by former president Donald Trump, the Republican nominee, to slap hefty tariffs on foreign goods and services - underscored the importance of trade between the province and U.S. states.
"Regardless of the outcome of this week's elections, we stand ready to work with our partners south of the border," the premier said in a statement Monday.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
Here's what will really matter if Trump is re-elected
Word came unexpectedly in January 2017 that President Donald Trump wanted to talk to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Officials scrambled to connect the two leaders and racked their brains: what urgent matter had they not foreseen?
Trudeau had already had a friendly congratulatory call with the newly inaugurated Republican president. Trump, who campaigned on ripping up the North American free trade deal and forcing Canada and all NATO allies to spend more on defence, riffed on Trudeau's famous father, the former prime minister Pierre Elliot Trudeau.
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles
A look at U.S. presidential candidates' ties to Canada ahead of this week's election
Among the millions glued to their TV sets Tuesday night watching U.S. election results will be a group of people in Montreal with a particular connection to Democratic candidate Kamala Harris - her high school classmates.
Long before she became an American vice-president and presidential candidate, Harris spent several years in Montreal and attended Westmount High School from 1978 to 1981.
While she doesn't talk much about that time, one of her former classmates believes her high school years helped shaped who she would become.
Read the full story form the Associated Press
Opinion: A final U.S. election plea to Trump's MAGA supporters: Make America Sane Again
Dear MAGA:
We've had a rough ride over the last decade. I've written mean things about you. You've emailed mean things to me. It's a hostile standoff in which I unfairly label you as dipsticks and you casually wish for my death in a fiery car crash.
Bygones! With the U.S. election less than 100 hours away, I come to you with an olive branch and one question: Can you survive a Trump 2.0?
As the Washington Post reported in 2021: "No other modern president has left the U.S. with a smaller workforce than it had when they took office." Politico: "America's trade gap soared under Trump." FactCheck.org: "The federal debt held by the public went up, from $14.4 trillion to $21.6 trillion." The Atlantic: "Trump is the worst president in history."
Read the full story from Vinay Menon
Opinion: Donald Trump is going to win the 2024 U.S. presidential election. Here are three reasons why
If there is one thing I've learned in my time writing this column, it's that readers, especially those who read regularly, would rather I be direct, precise and dead wrong, than prevaricating, wishy-washy and possibly correct.
So here goes.
Donald Trump is going to win the 2024 U.S. presidential election. On election night. Decisively.
Read the full story from Jaime Watt
Opinion: 'When he talked about Puerto Rico, it was like, 'That's not them, that's me':' Will one bad joke sink Donald Trump in the swingiest swing state in America?
The suburban collar counties of Philadelphia, a leafy stole draped on the shoulders of Pennsylvania's largest city, exist in the American political imagination much like the Toronto suburbs do in the Canadian. No one in the country at large thinks about them all that much, in other words, until election years, when everyone thinks about them all of the time.
Read the full story from the Star's Richard Warnica
Opinion: Pennsylvania is on fire: What we don't talk about when we talk about Donald Trump
The weather in Philadelphia, just about everyone in Philadelphia is eager to say, is not normally this hot in November. The grass isn't normally this yellow, either. The sidewalk cafes aren't normally full at night. There aren't normally lineups for ice cream outside in the month when the snow used to come, and the air used to freeze, and the late fall winds used to strip the trees of their last, carotenoid-laden leaves.
I landed in Philadelphia to cover the final days of the U.S. presidential election on Nov. 1. It was the region's 33rd consecutive day without rain. The city itself had tied a record the day before for the hottest Halloween in history. It hit 28 C that afternoon and stayed hot overnight. On Friday, in nearby West Chester, a red-faced Secret Service agent desperately tried to inch into the shade while searching guests headed into an appearance by Doug Emhoff, the Second Gentleman of the United States. Earlier that day, in Allentown, north of the city, the mayor, wearing a black T-shirt and dark blue jeans, begged to do an interview in the shade after hosting a press conference with Rosie Perez.
Read the full story by Richard Warnica
Opinion: Why Kamala Harris should worry that Michigan's blue collar could be turning red
Working class. Muscle class. Blue collar.
Whatever you call them - pollsters define the demographic as people without college degrees - they're a great big chunk of labour that Kamala Harris is reportedly shedding by the day.
In Michigan, automotive workers, factory workers, steel mill workers - trade unionists - who have long flocked to the Democratic Party, a reliable voting bloc down through the decades. The union members who, in 2020, allowed President Joe Biden to carry the state - and its 16 electoral votes - by 154,000 votes, seizing the battleground state back from Donald Trump, who'd surprisingly claimed Michigan in 2016 by a razor-thin 0.23 per cent.
Read the column from Rosie DiManno
Opinion: This U.S. election campaign is stuffed with malice and bile. Can Kamala Harris or Donald Trump restore the America that was?
The American election is deadlocked.
That is, each side sees the other through a glass darkly, existentially calamitous for the country. In these waning hours of full throttle door-knocking and frenetic phonebank calling, foreboding rhetoric has been ratcheted to a smoke alarm pitch.
But this isn't the end of days, whatever happens Tuesday. The republic survived Donald Trump once, it can do so again, should it come to that. And Kamala Harris isn't a Marxist, or "crazy," or "low I.Q." - among the milder insults her opponent has thrown at her. Neither can undo 248 years of democracy forged in the fire, though only one has threatened to terminate the Constitution.
Read the full story from the Star's Rosie DiManno
How did we get here?
It's the election that no one could have foreseen.
Not so long ago, Donald Trump was marinating in self-pity at Mar-a-Lago after being impeached twice and voted out of the White House. Even some of his closest allies were looking forward to a future without the charismatic yet erratic billionaire leading the Republican Party, especially after his failed attempt to overturn an election ended in violence and shame. When Trump announced his comeback bid two years ago, the New York Post buried the article on page 26.
At the same time, Kamala Harris was languishing as a low-profile sidekick to President Joe Biden. Once seen as a rising star in the Democratic Party, she struggled with both her profile and her portfolio, disappointing her supporters and delighting her critics. No one was talking about Harris running for the top job - they were wondering if Biden should replace her as his running mate when he sought a second term.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
What to watch for as U.S. Election Day approaches
Election Day is nearly upon us. In a matter of hours, the final votes in the 2024 presidential election will be cast.
In a deeply divided nation, the election is a true toss-up between Democrat Kamala Harris and Republican Donald Trump.
We know there are seven battleground states that will decide the outcome, barring a major surprise. But major questions persist about the timing of the results, the makeup of the electorate, the influx of misinformation - even the possibility of political violence. At the same time, both sides are prepared for a protracted legal battle that could complicate things further.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
How does the election work?
The U.S. presidential election is here. Tomorrow, millions of Americans will go to the polls in what will be one of the world's most-watched elections.
Swing states, faithless electors, the electoral college - there's a lot to know about the uniquely American spectacle that happens every four years. As our largest trading partner and border-sharing neighbour prepares to elect its next head of state, Canadians are waiting with baited breath to see who will land in the White House: Democratic nominee Kamala Harris or the Republican alternative, Donald Trump.
Read the full story from the Star's Andy Takagi

Title: U.S. election live updates: Bomb threats at Georgia polling stations originated in Russia, official says

Hamilton-area Today: Alexander La charged in another summer shooting + Public high school violence + Second World War changed city forever

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-area-today-alexander-la-charged-another/docview/3124106663/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 204 of 500

Dofasco's green steel project delays are cause for concern

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dofascos-green-steel-project-delays-are-cause/docview/3124106356/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dofasco%27s+green+steel+project+delays+are+cause+for+concern&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford announced nearly $1 billion in government support in 2022 for ArcelorMittal Dofasco's green steel project, the iconic Hamilton steelmaker became a model for decarbonization.
But in the last few weeks, doubts have surfaced about Dofasco's commitment to the venture. With hundreds of millions of dollars in government funding waiting in the wings, it appears the company still has not made a final decision to move it forward.
Dofasco launched the project two years ago to great fanfare as the federal and provincial governments committed $900 million, half its $1.8-billion cost. Under this ambitious plan, the company pledged to reduce CO2 emissions by three million tonnes a year and eliminate most of its local air pollution, all while maintaining current steel production.
The company has promised to replace its aging, CO2-intensive blast furnaces and coke ovens with a natural-gas fired iron-making furnace and a second electric arc furnace by 2028. The plan also permits Dofasco to produce emission-free steel by enabling it to switch to climate-friendly hydrogen when it becomes available in the future.
With a little more than three years to go, however, major demolition and construction scheduled for 2023 and 2024 has not yet happened. The company said the holdup is due to design and engineering delays and insists it is "unwavering" in its support of the project.
It's not unusual for projects of this scale to have design and engineering challenges. Budget issues are also undoubtedly a factor with recent high inflation driving up construction and borrowing expenses.
But what is concerning is ArcelorMittal, Dofasco's multinational parent company, is also delaying other decarbonization projects. Earlier this year, the European climate advocacy group SteelWatch revealed ArcelorMittal has delayed hydrogen steelmaking projects in Europe, reversed blast furnace closures in Belgium and Germany and is building new blast furnaces in Brazil and India.
Hanging over the steel industry is the election in the United States. Donald Trump has threatened to end climate subsidies and take the U.S. out of the United Nations climate agreement. If elected, he plans to send a message to business in the U.S. and around the world that climate action is no longer a priority.
Already some auto companies such as Ford are pulling back electric vehicle production targets. If this continues it could depress demand for strong, lightweight automotive steel, a Dofasco specialty.
On the positive side, however, Dofasco has a powerful incentive to reduce emissions through Canada's carbon price on large industrial emitters. The carbon price requires CO2-intensive companies like steel producers to pay a tax or buy credits if they don't reduce emissions.
While Pierre Poilievre has promised to cancel the carbon tax paid by consumers if he is elected in Canada, he has not pledged to repeal the industrial version of the tax. This could end up being one of those rare policies that receive long-term cross-party support.
The policy is expected to become even more important as the world, led by Europe, introduces carbon tariffs to keep CO2-heavy products out of their markets. Canada and the U.S. are expected to follow suit.
Meanwhile, economic conditions are shifting in Dofasco's favour. A recent 25 per cent tariff on Chinese imports is expected to strengthen steel prices at the same time inflation is falling and make project costs more manageable. The federal government recently released preliminary guidelines for "transition investments" aimed at increasing private capital for climate projects like those in the steel industry.
The project is one of Canada's largest carbon reduction initiatives. It would reduce serious air pollution issues in Hamilton and position the city as a leader in low-emission manufacturing. It would also help to address the climate emergency creating hotter temperatures, severe droughts and increasingly violent storms around the globe.
Dofasco needs to confirm it is moving ahead with this plan.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford stands between rolls of galvanized steel. The news conference took place at ArcelorMittal Dofasco's Line 5 galvanized steel line in 2022 where the province announced $500 million in funding for the company's green energy project.Ron Bedard, vice-president of ArcelorMittal and CEO of ArcelorMittal Dofasco, speaks at a 2022 news conference as Premier Doug Ford stands behind him. The province announced $500 million for a green energy project.
CREDIT: Eugene Ellmen

Subject: Press conferences; Steel industry; Air pollution; Emissions control; Alternative energy; Carbon; Galvanized steel; Steel production

Document 205 of 500

Predictability hangs in the balance as Justin Trudeau's government awaits results of the U.S. presidential election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/predictability-hangs-balance-as-justin-trudeaus/docview/3124106338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Predictability+hangs+in+the+balance+as+Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+awaits+results+of+the+U.S.+presidential+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The tag line on one of the new Liberal ads - "We won't go back" - is a very unsubtle hint on just how closely Justin Trudeau's team is watching the U.S. election, and which side it is on.
The line is, of course, a direct borrow from Democrat Kamala Harris's campaign chant: "We're not going back."
Officially, the Trudeau government says it's not taking sides in this momentous choice for Americans, and that it will work with Harris or Donald Trump when the dust settles.
But it's also pretty evident that the Liberals would prefer a Harris win, for the same reason it was hoping the Democrats would triumph over Trump four years ago: it's not just ideological alignment between Democrats and Liberals, it's predictability.
Here's how Trudeau put it last January when I sat down for an interview with him and asked whether he was taking sides in the U.S. election.
"I mean, the first time through four years of Donald Trump was a real challenge," the prime minister said. "Given what Mr. Trump has said about his intentions if he gets re-elected, it would certainly be challenging once again."
He also said, "There's no question that when there is significantly more alignment, like there is on environment and human rights and international engagement with the current president, with Mr. Biden, I obviously would hope to continue to work with people who are more aligned."
Back in the early part of this year, Trudeau and his team were a little less guarded in their views of Trump, constantly casting Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as a northern version of Trump.
They have since dialed that back a bit, as former ambassador David MacNaughton urged them to do in a conversation with the Star's Tonda MacCharles.
But they haven't totally abandoned that attack line. Just last week, Housing Minister Sean Fraser ripped into Poilievre as a Republican knock-off, even going so far to make a connection between Poilievre's support for the so-called "freedom convoy" of 2022 and the Jan. 6, 2021 siege of Capitol Hill in Washington.
"It is almost as though the Conservative party, when looking for a new leader, hopped on Temu and typed in 'far-right Conservative,' so it spat this guy out," Fraser said. "He borrows lessons from his cousins south of the border when he opposes birth control for women. During the Jan. 6 of Canada, the convoy, he was not only telling people to stand by, but he was also bringing them coffee in the streets. It might be election season in America, but we do not need this far-right, right-wing populism here at home."
A potential Trump win this week is one reason the Trudeau Liberals will want to keep those kind of shots to a minimum. As MacCharles reported in an analysis piece over the weekend, this government is preparing for that very real possibility.
But there are domestic reasons as well for the Liberals to take a more neutral tack on Trump's politics.
The political landscape here, for instance, is not exactly the same as it was eight or even four years ago, when the vast majority of Canadians, polled on how they felt about Trump, were very much against his presidency.
An Environics Institute poll this fall suggested that a Harris win is still the preferred outcome for 60 per cent of respondents, but support for Trump has picked up in Canada since 2020, especially in Ontario and the West. Moreover, when Environics focused just on who Conservative voters would support, Trump edged out Harris - which is a big change from the last two presidential elections. (Environics conducted landline and cellphone interviews with 2,016 Canadians between Sept. 9 and 23, and results are considered accurate 2.2 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.)
So when Liberals say "we won't go back" in their ads, they are not likely to find a mark among these Conservatives, who appear to be very open to Poilievre's oft-repeated argument that Canada was a lot better before Trudeau came along.
A Harris victory could carry a mixed message for Liberals. On the one hand, it would be evidence that American voters are more disposed to progressive politics. "Progressive" is a word that also features large in the Liberal ads. However, it could also be viewed as what happens when the progressive party pulls off a switch of leaders, something that even some Liberals are seeking this fall.
For political and governance reasons, Trudeau will also be keeping an eye on the level of post-vote chaos in the U.S. He's already kept his cabinet shuffle on hold until after the election and he may even want to prorogue Parliament while the U.S. - and Canada-U.S. relations - remain in limbo.
Trudeau and the Liberals have vivid memories of the chaos that Trump can insert into that relationship. "We won't go back" is what many will be saying to each other as they watch results roll in.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau takes in the view with U.S Vice-President Kamala Harris from the balcony outside her office in Washington on Nov. 18, 2021.

Subject: Conservatism; Progressivism; Political parties; Political campaigns; Election results; Presidential elections; Voters; Prime ministers; Political advertising

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Harris, Kamala; Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Predictability hangs in the balance as Justin Trudeau's government awaits results of the U.S. presidential election

Document 206 of 500

When Ottawa brought in a new anti-flipping tax, savvy renovators already had a way around it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-ottawa-brought-new-anti-flipping-tax-savvy/docview/3124106325/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+Ottawa+brought+in+a+new+anti-flipping+tax%2C+savvy+renovators+already+had+a+way+around+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While Canada Revenue Agency has ramped up efforts to enforce a new anti-flipping tax law to cool housing prices, savvy investors likely won't be turning over more of their profits to the government anytime soon, real estate accountants tell the Star.
But the federal tax agency's net is catching some ordinary homeowners who hastily sold their properties for personal reasons, unaware of the steep financial penalties now tied to quick sales.
As of Jan. 1, 2023, anyone who buys and sells a property, including pre-built condos, within 365 days must declare their profit as business income rather than a capital gain or principle residence exemption, which would have resulted in a significant tax break or a tax-free payday.
The CRA told the Star it has assigned four specialized audit teams to monitor real estate transactions in the "high-risk regions of Ontario and British Columbia."
"It's closing a loophole," says Silas Xuereb, a policy analyst with Canadians for Tax Fairness. "But housing financialization will continue driving up the cost of homes and rents if there's not a wider policy in place to prevent it."
When the federal government implemented the tax policy, the Liberals said the change would "reduce speculative demand in the marketplace and help to cool excessive price growth." The 2022 budget also forecast the tax change would yield an annual revenue of $25 million.
Chartered accountants who work with real estate investors across the province aren't so sure.
"People will wait a little longer to sell their property or set up a corporation to flip houses at a much cheaper tax rate," says Tyler Powers, a partner at Gauvreau Accounting Tax Law Advisory, a Peterborough-based firm that works with real estate and construction clients across Canada and the U.S. "It's business as usual."
How savvy flippers get around the tax
The Department of Finance said it's too early to speak to the success of the new law and stands by its revenue projections.
"Comprehensive data for the 2023 tax year -- the first year the anti-flipping measure applied -- will only be available in 2025," a spokesman from the Department of Finance told the Star in an email.
The relatively new tax policy also applies to assignment sales, for those who hold the rights to a pre-construction residential property and sell those rights for a gain within 12 months.
If a property owner clears $300,000 on a sale, for example, declaring the gain as business income means paying roughly half that amount - $160,590 - to the federal government. That's roughly double the amount that would have been owed on a capital gain.
But many who flip houses for a living typically incorporate their businesses to write-off numerous expenses and declare their profits as active business income at the much lower corporate tax rate of 12.2 per cent (for those with an annual net business income of less than $500,000).
"It's business as usual," says, Powers, adding the new law does provide much-needed clarity on how real estate transactions should be taxed.
"It's only the people who are trying to exploit a loophole, people who intentionally buy a property and move in and sell it and try to claim as primary residence that will be affected by this change," add Powers, noting this group represents a minority of flippers.
"We're expecting the CRA will spend more time dealing with local municipalities and land registry offices to see when properties are moving hands and are reported incorrectly," Powers says.
The CRA will apply a penalty equal to 50 per cent of the additional tax payable if a taxpayer knowingly makes a false statement when filing a return.
"The flipping rules are meant to address the government's concern that certain taxpayers engaged in flipping residential real estate were incorrectly reporting their profits as capital gains and, in some cases, claiming the principal residence exemption," a CRA spokesperson told the Star.
"While the CRA has focused much of its efforts on real estate transactions in the high-risk regions of Ontario and British Columbia, where the compliance risk was higher due to growth in prices transactions, the compliance risk profile across the country is evolving and monitored."
'It almost seems too little too late'
Like Powers, accountant Cherry Chan of Oakville says the flippers she's worked with have been reporting their profits as business income through corporations they've set up for real estate investments.
"We're not just buying, fixing and selling," he says.
"We're paying trades fair wages to trades. We're taking properties most people wouldn't want to live in and figuring out how to make them livable. Yes, it's increasing the price of housing because nobody wants to buy a house with a kitchen doesn't work."
The new tax law, Chan says, "is not going to cool the market.
"It almost seems too little too late," adds Powers, noting most of his flipping clients haven't seen significant gains within a 12-month period because of high interest rates.
Chan says most of her flipping clients are losing money right now because it's taking longer for deals to close and at lower prices than they anticipated.
In such cases, she has a simple piece of advice: don't sell before a year is up.
Property owners who did not purchase their home through a corporation, she says, should hold on to it for longer than 365 days before selling to claim it as a business loss.
Under the new rules, a business loss cannot be reported on flipped properties.
"You can carry it backward three years or forward 20 years to offset against future business profits."
Policy unfairly pins housing crisis on renovators, investor says
Tax-averse real estate investors may also want to steer clear of B.C. Earlier this year, the province imposed its own anti-flipping tax in addition to the federal one. Those who buy and sell properties within a period of two years will have to pay a tax of up to 20 per cent of their profit.
The B.C. tax applies to properties sold on or after Jan. 1, 2025.
It's an attempt to quell rising home costs.
In the GTA, where nearly 30 per cent of existing housing was built in 1960 or before, flipping has become big business. Re/Max Canada flagged renovations and rebuilds as "one of the most underestimated factors" driving up the value of detached houses, which has shot up 35 per cent from 2019 to 2023. A Toronto Star analysis of demolition permit and sales records for single family homes in Toronto found the work of flippers has resulted in home prices more than doubling in some pockets of the city in recent years.
Andrew Cowe, a real estate investor whose recent flip of a Toronto home was featured in the Star, says the new tax policy unfairly pins the nation's housing affordability crisis on people like him.
"We're not just buying, fixing and selling," he says.
"We're paying trades fair wages to trades. We're taking properties most people wouldn't want to live in and figuring out how to make them livable. Yes, it's increasing the price of housing because nobody wants to buy a house with a kitchen doesn't work."
CAPTION: A new tax on people buying and selling a home within a year was supposed curb flipping activity, but real estate accountants say for savvy flippers "it's business as usual."

Subject: Fiscal policy; Housing; Tax rates; Sales; Business income; Capital gains; Accountants; Housing prices; Profits

Business indexing term: Subject: Fiscal policy Tax rates Sales Business income Capital gains Accountants Housing prices Profits

Location: Canada; British Columbia Canada

Title: When Ottawa brought in a new anti-flipping tax, savvy renovators already had a way around it

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 5, 2024

Section: business/real-estate

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Dofasco's green steel project delays are cause for concern

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dofascos-green-steel-project-delays-are-cause/docview/3123997662/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dofasco%27s+green+steel+project+delays+are+cause+for+concern&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Hanging over the steel industry is the election in the United States. Donald Trump has threatened to end climate subsidies and take the U.S. out of the United Nations climate agreement. If elected, he plans to send a message to business in the U.S. and around the world that climate action is no longer a priority.
Already some auto companies such as Ford are pulling back electric vehicle production targets. If this continues it could depress demand for strong, lightweight automotive steel, a Dofasco specialty.
On the positive side, however, Dofasco has a powerful incentive to reduce emissions through Canada's carbon price on large industrial emitters. The carbon price requires CO2-intensive companies like steel producers to pay a tax or buy credits if they don't reduce emissions.
While Pierre Poilievre has promised to cancel the carbon tax paid by consumers if he is elected in Canada, he has not pledged to repeal the industrial version of the tax. This could end up being one of those rare policies that receive long-term cross-party support.
The policy is expected to become even more important as the world, led by Europe, introduces carbon tariffs to keep CO2-heavy products out of their markets. Canada and the U.S. are expected to follow suit.
Meanwhile, economic conditions are shifting in Dofasco's favour. A recent 25 per cent tariff on Chinese imports is expected to strengthen steel prices at the same time inflation is falling and make project costs more manageable.
The federal government recently released preliminary guidelines for "transition investments" aimed at increasing private capital for climate projects like those in the steel industry.
The project is one of Canada's largest carbon reduction initiatives. It would reduce serious air pollution issues in Hamilton and position the city as a leader in low-emission manufacturing. It would also help to address the climate emergency creating hotter temperatures, severe droughts and increasingly violent storms around the globe.
Dofasco needs to confirm it is moving ahead with this plan.
Eugene Ellmen writes
on sustainable business and finance. He lives in Hamilton.

Document 208 of 500

Don't want Doug Ford's $200 taxpayer rebate? Here's how you can send it right back to the province to pay down debt instead

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dont-want-doug-fords-200-taxpayer-rebate-heres/docview/3124165389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+want+Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+taxpayer+rebate%3F+Here%27s+how+you+can+send+it+right+back+to+the+province+to+pay+down+debt+instead&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is planning to hand out $200 cheques to some 15 million people in the province.
The money is expected to be in the mail in early 2025.
This includes $200 each for some 12.5 million eligible taxpayers, while the families of some 2.5 million children who qualify for a Canada Child Benefit (CCB) in 2024 will also receive, if eligible, $200 per child. So, as the government points out, an eligible family of five could receive $1,000.
To be eligible for the tax-free taxpayer rebate, you must have been 18 years or older at the end of 2023, a resident in Ontario on Dec. 31, 2023, have filed your 2023 income tax and benefit return by Dec. 31, 2024, and have not be bankrupt or incarcerated in 2024. There is no means test, so even high-income earners will get the money.
Why is the government doing this?
"We're doing everything we can to keep costs down for Ontario families, but the federal carbon tax and high interest rates are still hurting taxpayers across the province," said Premier Doug Ford in a news release. "This $200 taxpayer rebate will give Ontario taxpayers, especially families with children, some much-deserved help so they can make ends meet."
How to return the rebate
But let's just say you have no need for this money, or don't want it for whatever reason - you can send it right back to the province.
The province has something called the Ontario Opportunities Fund and your contributions to this fund go directly to paying down the province's debt. You also get a tax receipt for your generosity.
In case you are wondering, Ontario's publicly held debt, as of March 31, 2024, is forecasted at approximately $429.7 billion. There's also $8 billion in non-public debt, which consists of debt instruments issued mainly to the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board.
Every year, Ontario residents can donate all or a portion of their tax refund - if they are entitled to a tax refund - to this fund at the time they are filing their taxes.
But you can also contribute to this fund at any other time of the year by sending a cheque or money order to the appropriate government department. In return, you get a receipt, which can help lower your tax obligation the year you make the contribution, or it can be carried forward for any of the next five years.
So, if you'd like to put your $200 toward the government's debt - which looks like this if written out in numbers: $429,700,000,000 - now you know how to do so.
More information on the Ontario Opportunities Fund is available on the government of Ontario's website.
People who are feeling extra generous can also make contributions to help pay down Canada's national debt.
While the federal government doesn't have an equivalent program to the Ontario Opportunities Fund, it will still accept your donation. The government issues and accepts payments through the Receiver General, so you can send your money there.
Information on contacting the Receiver General is available on the Government of Canada's website.
CAPTION: The Ontario government is planning a taxpayer rebate in early 2025.
