Another Tory MP wants Liberal housing money that Pierre Poilievre has pledged to cut

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/another-tory-mp-wants-liberal-housing-money-that/docview/3124165363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+Tory+MP+wants+Liberal+housing+money+that+Pierre+Poilievre+has+pledged+to+cut&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser is exposing another Opposition Conservative MP who has voiced a desire for federal money from a program the party promises to cut, as the Liberals and Tories dashed off duelling letters over housing policy to mayors and premiers across the country.
On Monday, Fraser's office shared a letter from Conservative MP Frank Caputo, who wrote to the housing minister in April to express his disappointment over the fact that the biggest city in his British Columbia riding did not get any money from the federal Housing Accelerator Fund.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre promised last week to slash that fund to help cover the lost revenue from his plan to cut the federal sales tax from newly-built homes that sell for less than $1 million.
"Kamloops is in dire need for federal housing funds and the people of Kamloops are tired of being left out of funding announcements," Caputo wrote in the letter, which Fraser's office provided to the Star.
Caputo did not respond to a request for comment from the Star on Monday.
Fraser's disclosure comes days after his office revealed how five other Conservative MPs - B.C's Dan Albas, Alberta's Michael Cooper, Ontario's Adam Chambers and Lianne Rood, and Rob Moore from New Brunswick - had written to Fraser seeking federal dollars for their communities from the Housing Accelerator Fund. The Canadian Press first reported on the letters.
In a statement to the Star on Monday, Fraser defended the fund and claimed that some of Poilievre's own MPs disagree with his desire to cut the program.
"In fact, they've been going behind his back to support the Housing Accelerator Fund," Fraser's statement said.
"I hope more Conservative MPs will add their names to the list of representatives who are standing up for their communities by standing against Mr. Poilievre's proposed cuts to housing in their hometown."
Poilievre's spokesperson did not directly address Fraser's claims that Tories support the accelerator fund, but echoed the party leader's criticism that Liberal policies have failed to rein in rising housing costs.
"Unlike their multibillion dollar so-called "housing accelerator" photo op fund, this common sense policy benefits all Canadians without bureaucratic applications and costly photo ops designed for Liberal political gain," Sebastian Skamski said by email, referring to Poilievre's promised tax cut.
Poilievre told reporters last week that a future Conservative government could save $3 billion by cutting the accelerator fund, and another $5 billion by slashing the Liberal government's Housing Infrastructure Fund, a program that is meant to start doling out money next year to help pay for water and sewer systems for housing developments.
Those savings would help cover the lost revenues of Poilievre's pledge to eliminate the federal sales tax from new homes and condos that sell for less than $1 million - a policy that was welcomed by many housing experts, including the Smart Prosperity Institute's Mike Moffatt, who has presented to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his cabinet about Canada's housing crunch on at least two occasions.
Poilievre said last week that the tax cut would spur construction of an additional 30,000 homes, and cost roughly $4 billion, each year.
In the days since, Fraser has jumped on Poilievre's pledge to cut the Housing Accelerator Fund, claiming the pledge jeopardizes more than $3 billion in funding that has been committed to municipalities who have agreed to change zoning rules and local policies to speed up and densify housing construction. According to Fraser's office, the minister had sent 141 letters as of Monday to municipal leaders across Canada - including Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and officials from at least 10 other cities in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area - warning them about Poilievre's planned cut.
A spokesperson for Fraser explained that $1.2 billion of the accelerator's $4.4 billion budget has already been distributed, with the remainder set to be rolled out to municipalities over the next three years if they continue to hit milestones agreed to in more than 170 Housing Accelerator Fund deals that have been signed across the country.
Fraser's letter to Chow, dated Oct. 30, noted how the city inked an agreement to get more than $471 million in federal funding through this program, which the missive said is supposed to lead to an additional 53,000 permits for new homes over 10 years.
During question period, Conservative MP Kyle Seebak raised questions about how this money is going to Toronto while housing starts have dropped. Seebak claimed that Liberal policies are "funding bureaucracies" while raising development charges and not getting more new homes built, prompting Liberal MP Peter Fragiskatos to respond that the Conservative plan would "only" see cuts to programs.
But while Fraser writes to municipalities, Poilievre has penned his own letter to all the premiers, using them to match the Conservatives' plan to eliminate the federal sales tax from new homes that sell for under $1 million by similarly cutting provincial sales taxes.
"Across the country, taxes and government fees add unnecessary costs to homebuyers," said Poilievre's letter, which was provided to the Star, and outlines the Conservatives' long-standing criticism of how housing costs have jumped significantly since the Trudeau Liberals took office in 2015.
"I understand that all provinces are different. But if you match my announcement with a provincial sales tax cut of your own on new homes, you will save your residents tens of thousands of dollars," the letter said.
Last year, when the Liberal government announced it would cut the federal sales tax on new rental buildings to make it cheaper to build them, provinces like Ontario followed suit.
Colin Blachar, a spokesperson for Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, told the Star by email Monday that the province already provides a 75-per-cent rebate on provincial sales tax on new homes. But the value of that tax break is capped at $24,000.
Though less generous than what Poilievre is proposing, Blachar noted the provincial rebate is larger than the current rebate for federal sales tax that was created in 1991, which covers 36 per cent of the federal sales tax for homes that sell for less than $350,000.
"We will continue to work with all levels of government to get more homes built faster so that more people can afford to buy a home," Blachar said.
CAPTION: Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser rises during question period, in Ottawa on Oct. 29, 2024.

Housing minister wants former staffer to hand over personal emails related to Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/housing-minister-wants-former-staffer-hand-over/docview/3124165286/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Housing+minister+wants+former+staffer+to+hand+over+personal+emails+related+to+Greenbelt&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's housing minister is calling on the former political staffer at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal to hand over any emails from his personal account that may relate to the province's controversial and since-reversed decision to open up the protected area to development.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra spoke to reporters at Queen's Park on Monday after the Star published an article about how Ryan Amato, the former chief of staff to Calandra's predecessor, Steve Clark, had refused to provide any Greenbelt-related emails he may have sent or received from his personal Gmail account. Amato's refusal was in response to a freedom of information request from the Star.
Amato did not respond to questions for this story. He resigned from the government in August 2023 in the wake of a scathing Auditor General's report, which denounced the use of personal email by political staff in the housing ministry involved in Greenbelt decision-making.
On Monday, Calandra said his ministry will co-operate with all authorities - including the RCMP, which is still investigating the ill-fated scheme - to provide whatever information it can.
"If there's anything outstanding that the ministry needs to provide, we will provide it," he said.
Amato's refusal to share his personal emails came to light in submissions by the housing ministry to the Information and Privacy Commissioner (IPC) as part of an appeal by the Star, which filed an FOI in August 2023 for Amato's personal emails related to the Greenbelt.
The province initially only provided a handful of emails Amato had forwarded from his personal account to his government account. The Star is arguing that additional records exist, something Amato's lawyers have described as "merely speculation."
Lawyers for the province said they have formally asked Amato to hand over any Greenbelt-related emails he may have sent or received from his personal account and that Amato "twice refused." They told the IPC they have no "legal mechanism" to force their former employee to co-operate.
Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles also asked about the Star's story in Question Period on Monday, directing a question to Premier Doug Ford about why political staff used personal email to communicate with Greenbelt lobbyists in the first place.
Responding in Ford's place, PC MP Will Bouma said the Ford government has "zero tolerance" for any wrongdoing and the province will "pursue every avenue to request that any relevant records be turned over."
CAPTION: Ryan Amato, former chief of staff of then housing minister Steve Clark.

Liberal MP spars with Jagmeet Singh over weekend clash at Hindu temple in Brampton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-mp-spars-with-jagmeet-singh-over-weekend/docview/3123997482/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MP+spars+with+Jagmeet+Singh+over+weekend+clash+at+Hindu+temple+in+Brampton&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A Liberal MP's claim that pro-Khalistan "extremists" may have infiltrated Canadian politics and law enforcement agencies parrots the lines of an Indian government accused of threatening and killing Canadians, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said Monday.
But backbencher Chandra Arya fired back, accusing Singh of refusing to acknowledge the presence of "violent Khalistani extremism" in Canada and saying he has "zero credibility" on the issue.
Their comments followed a violent clash between Sikh separatists and Hindu worshippers outside a Hindu temple in Brampton on Sunday, which garnered condemnation from Canadian political leaders and a swift rebuke from the Modi government amid simmering tensions between Canada and India over explosive allegations that linked Indian officials in Canada to homicides and threats against Canadians, which New Delhi has denied.
The pro-Khalistan Sikh groups said they were protesting the presence of Indian consular officials at the temple when the violence erupted, leading to three arrests and the suspension of a police officer who attended the demonstration while off-duty.
Arya said the pro-Khalistan protesters had crossed a "red line," blaming them for the violence that he said "shows how deep and brazen Khalistani violent extremism has become in Canada."
"I begin to feel that there is a small grain of truth in the reports that in addition to Canadian political apparatus, Khalistanis have effectively infiltrated into our law enforcement agencies," Arya wrote Sunday on X. "No wonder that under the 'freedom of expression' Khalistani extremists are getting a free pass in Canada."
As Singh also condemned the weekend violence, he accused Arya of escalating tensions with conspiracy theories.
"It is very problematic that an elected Canadian official is parroting the lines of a foreign government that engaged in allegations of serious violence and terror against Canadians, so he should stop," Singh told reporters Monday.
"What we need to see is an end to the violence, we need to see a firm stance against Indian government, we need to be united in our condemnation of violence happening at a place of prayer."
Arya fired back in a written statement to the Star, saying Singh "has zero credibility when it comes to speaking on extremism in Canada." He said the NDP leader "refuses to acknowledge the presence of violent Khalistani extremism in Canada, despite the RCMP's clear statement during their Thanksgiving Day press conference that the national task force is actively investigating Khalistani violent extremism, among other threats," referring to an announcement last month that intensified diplomatic tensions between Canada and India.
A spokesperson for the Prime Minister's Office said Monday that the PMO would "let MP Chandra Arya speak to his tweet," but added that "Canada stands ready to work with India to advance the relationship between our peoples - anchored in human rights, diversity, and the rule of law."
Earlier in the day, Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi had called on the Trudeau government to "ensure justice and uphold the rule of law" in the wake of what he characterized as the "deliberate attack on a Hindu temple in Canada."
Police have not commented on how the violence started.
"Equally appalling are the cowardly attempts to intimidate our diplomats," Modi said. "Such acts of violence will never weaken India's resolve.
Sikhs for Justice, a diaspora group pushing for the creation of a Sikh state called "Khalistan" in Punjab, India, said the protest was over concerns that Indian officials have attended similar events in the past to find informants and target Sikh separatists.
They claimed the demonstration had been peaceful before the worshippers attacked the protesters, and have accused Indian officials and Arya of encouraging violence against them. Videos posted on social media show protesters fist fighting, attacking one another with flags and throwing what appear to be stones at one another.
In social media posts on Sunday, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford were quick to condemn the violence at a place of worship.
"The acts of violence at the Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton today are unacceptable," Trudeau said in a post on X. "Every Canadian has the right to practice their faith freely and safely."
Ford said on X that "the violence at the Hindu Sabha Mandir in Brampton this afternoon is completely unacceptable and must be condemned. No one should feel unsafe in their place of worship." Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre posted that the "violence targeting worshippers" was "completely unacceptable."
Green Party Leader Elizabeth May, like Singh, has the security clearance to read an explosive report alleging some MPs are complicit in foreign interference. She told reporters Monday she did not believe pro-Khalistan extremism has infiltrated law enforcement, but said it "remains a stain on all Canadians, that we have permitted extremism to exist."
"We are strongly in favour of immigration to Canada, of welcoming people from all corners of the world, but it is important that diaspora communities leave their domestic political grievances in their home country, and don't bring them to Canada," May said.

Document 212 of 500

Is Remembrance Day a stat holiday in Ontario? Most provinces give the day off or provide holiday pay

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-remembrance-day-stat-holiday-ontario-most/docview/3123785959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Remembrance+Day+a+stat+holiday+in+Ontario%3F+Most+provinces+give+the+day+off+or+provide+holiday+pay&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the weather cools and boots crunch the fallen leaves on the sidewalk, another sign of the seasonal change is the emergence of distinct red poppy pins on the lapels of coats to mark Remembrance Day.
Nov. 11 marks the anniversary of the armistice that ended the First World War, but has since evolved into a day to commemorate all Canadian Armed Forced and RCMP veterans who have been killed in the line of duty. A moment of silence at 11 a.m. is usually observed at schools and workplaces to mark the time when the armistice went into effect.
But the day isn't like other holidays - it's marked on calendars and there are official ceremonies - but it isn't a statutory holiday, at least not for Ontarians.
Here's what you need to know about why you probably aren't getting the day off.
Is Remembrance Day a statutory holiday in Canada?
Remembrance Day is a stat holiday and workers get holiday pay or the day off in all the provinces and territories except for Ontario, Manitoba, and Quebec. While the day isn't a federal stat holiday in Nova Scotia, the province gives most workers the day off through its own provincial act.
Across the country, workers of federally regulated businesses, like banks and Canada Post offices, get the day off as well.
British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan, Yukon, Northwest Territories, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador and Prince Edward Island treat Remembrance Day like a federal stat, allowing workers either holiday pay or the day off.
When Remembrance Day falls on a weekend or a non-working day for employees working in certain provinces like Newfoundland and Labrador or Saskatchewan, or for federally regulated businesses, they can get holiday pay or a day off in lieu - but that's not the case for Ontario.
Why isn't Remembrance Day a stat holiday in Ontario?
Besides certain exceptions, Ontario remains one of the few provinces that continues to have schools and businesses open on Remembrance Day. And while there have been attempts to change that, the Royal Canadian Legion opposes making the day a stat, fearing that the holiday would transform into just another long weekend instead of a day of reflection.
Both the federal and provincial governments have tried to change Remembrance Day into a stat. In 2010, Tory MPP Lisa MacLeod (Nepean) put forward a private member's bill proposing to swap the Family Day stat holiday for Remembrance Day. A few years later in 2015, NDP MP Dan Harris (Scarborough Southwest) put forward a bill to change Remembrance Day to a stat holiday across the country. Neither bill made it past a second reading.
Where can I get a poppy?
The Royal Canadian Legion distributes poppies to large businesses that usually carry them in-store. Certain banks like RBC, BMO and HSBC, or big box stores like Bulk Barn, Costco, Home Depot, Shoppers Drug Mart, Best Buy and Michaels usually carry poppies at some locations. Many LCBO locations also have poppies available. The Royal Canadian Legion also sells a reusable pin online for $2.
While poppies are free, the Legion "gratefully accepts donations to the Poppy Fund," a fund that provides supports for veterans.
Poppies, according to the Royal Canadian Legion, should be worn on the left side, over the heart. The Legion also encourages people to wear the poppy through the last Friday of October to Remembrance Day.
CAPTION: Remembrance Day marks the anniversary of the armistice that ended the First World War.

Document 214 of 500

Doug Ford warns of the perils of protectionism on the eve of U.S. election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-warns-perils-protectionism-on-eve-u-s/docview/3123785921/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+warns+of+the+perils+of+protectionism+on+the+eve+of+U.S.+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ahead of Tuesday's U.S. presidential election, Premier Doug Ford is warning that protectionism would hurt Ontario, America's third-largest trading partner.
Ford - who has expressed concern about the threat by former president Donald Trump, the Republican nominee, to slap hefty tariffs on foreign goods and services - underscored the importance of trade between the province and U.S. states.
"Regardless of the outcome of this week's elections, we stand ready to work with our partners south of the border," the premier said in a statement Monday.
"Above all, we must avoid falling into a 'Buy Canada' or 'Buy America' mindset that would jeopardize our existing trading relationship and instead adopt a 'Buy Can-Am' mindset that creates new jobs and opportunities for workers and businesses on both sides of the border," he said.
That was a pitch Ford used in June with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, who is now Vice-President Kamala Harris's running mate on the Democratic ticket.
While the premier has stressed he is remaining neutral in the contest between Harris and Trump, he said in August he "was happy as punch to see the governor nominated as ... (her) running mate."
"We tossed the football around a little bit in my office - we gave him a CFL football," the premier said, noting their friendly, closed-door meeting focused on trade.
"He understands that. We're stronger together."
Ontario is the largest trading partner to 17 states and the second-largest to another 11 states with two-way trade worth $493 billion last year. Only Canada and Mexico do more trade with the U.S.
"As Americans prepare to vote in this week's elections, it's more important than ever to preserve and build on our longstanding ties of friendship, trade and co-operation that unite Canada and the United States," the premier said in his Monday statement.
"Together, we can support the millions of workers on both sides of the border whose paycheques and livelihoods rely on this critical relationship," added Ford.
"As we work to build stronger economic ties with our closest ally and the largest economy in the world, Canada must remain aligned with U.S. trade policy, like we did by matching American tariffs on Chinese imports of electric vehicles and steel," he said, reiterating his call for Ottawa to hold off on its planned 3 per cent tax on American services like Google, Netflix and Amazon.
"Similarly, I continue to urge the federal government to delay the implantation of the digital services tax, which is putting Canadian jobs at risk."
At the Council of the Federation meeting of the premiers in Halifax in July, Ford rallied his fellow provincial and territorial leaders against U.S. tariffs.
But in a July interview with the Star's Kristin Rushowy, he conceded he himself has promoted protectionism in the past.
"I'm first to admit, I'm probably the worst culprit when I told governors 'buy Ontario, buy Ontario' - but I think we are changing the language with the U.S. governors now."
In the past year, Ontario made new "economic co-operation agreements" with Illinois, Indiana, Michigan, Nevada and New Jersey.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Fordunderscored the importance of trade between the province and U.S. states on Monday.

Document 215 of 500

'Stay in your lane': Premier Doug Ford's bike lane restrictions spurs loud opposition

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stay-your-lane-premier-doug-fords-bike/docview/3123785917/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Stay+in+your+lane%27%3A+Premier+Doug+Ford%27s+bike+lane+restrictions+spurs+loud+opposition&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Local opposition is growing against a contentious Tory provincial bill that would restrict new bike lanes in Hamilton and other Ontario cities - and potentially, rip some out.
Premier Doug Ford's government introduced an anti-congestion bill in October that allows the province to ban new bike lanes that replace an existing lane of motor vehicle traffic. Residents can comment on the bill until Nov. 20 at the Environmental Registry of Ontario (ERO) website.
The proposed law requires cities to seek provincial approval for such bicycle infrastructure going forward and also allows the government to "review" - and possibly remove - existing bike lanes. On Thursday, the bill was updated to include regulations to specifically guarantee removals in Toronto, where Ford has repeatedly blamed traffic woes on cycling lanes.
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes," Ford said earlier this month in a speech at the Empire Club of Canada. "We need to, and will, remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill."
It is not yet clear what would trigger a review of existing bike lanes in Hamilton. Via email, a spokesperson for Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria would only say the province expects to review bike lanes installed within the last five years "from municipalities across the province."
While some critics have labelled the bill pre-election politics, the legislation is still "very concerning" to anyone who cares about Hamilton's years-long efforts to protect cyclists, promote active transportation and prevent traffic fatalities, said Abbie Little, co-chair of the Cycle Hamilton group spearheading local opposition to Bill 212.
"I think there is a serious danger that (lane removals) could happen," Little said in an interview. "It would be a terrible step backwards for us to lose any existing infrastructure."
The bill could also throw a wrench in safe-cycling planning just when the city is about to ramp up such work with an eye to connecting the city's fragmented bike network and cutting down on traffic collisions.
Hamilton's master cycling plan spun its wheels for many years before a recent $60-million council commitment to accelerate the addition of new bike lanes and paths - particularly "protected" infrastructure that separates cyclists from cars with barriers, curbs or parking.
Under the proposed new provincial law, some of Hamilton's most popular and valued bike projects - like the Keddy Access Trail up the Mountain - might not have been possible, noted Environment Hamilton in a joint letter with other climate action groups opposing the bill.
The Claremont-climbing Keddy trail - named after cyclist Jay Keddy, who was struck and killed by a truck on the access road - required the removal of a car lane. So too did the addition of a protected cycle track on Victoria Avenue North. Both projects were completed within the last five years.
Environment Hamilton emailed all of its members earlier this month urging them to "flood" the province with comments opposing the proposed bike lane provisions of the bill.
Cyclists rallied in Toronto against the bill last week and a group of close to 30 did likewise at Hamilton city hall, noted Little.
Hamilton politicians are also weighing in - in some cases pointedly challenging the province's ability to prevent municipal decision-making on road safety.
"What happens when ... municipalities simply tell (the Tory government) to go pound sand," wrote Mountain councillor John-Paul Danko on social media in response to a Progressive Conservative party message about the bill.
"How exactly do they intend on forcing cities to remove - or block new cycling lanes on city streets? Could (Hamilton) then go install some speed bumps on (Highway) 403?"
Ward 1 councillor Maureen Wilson previously appealed to the premier to "stop playing wedge politics" and support - rather than undermine - cities. "Stay in your lane and step up, step where you are desperately needed ... fund supportive housing and health care."
In a statement, Mayor Andrea Horwath called bike lanes a "vital" element of Hamilton's transportation network, climate action strategy and council's goal to make sure residents can move around safely whether by foot, bike, bus or car.
She noted city staff will review the proposed legislation closely and report to council with any local implications.
City staff are working on that report now - but with some questions as yet unanswered, said transportation planning director Brian Hollingworth.
He noted the exact criteria to trigger provincial bike lane approval or review remains under development. The public commenting site says the criteria will be developed later after consultation with "targeted stakeholders."
That leaves it uncertain if Hamilton's dramatic conversion of one-way Main Street to two-way traffic - with a section of new bike lanes - would require provincial permission, for example. That project is actually designed to slow chronic speeding and make all road users safer on the collision-prone artery.
Also up for debate: does replacing "parking lanes" with bicycle infrastructure, as is planned on Victoria Avenue South, qualify as reducing a "traffic" lane?
On the upside, Hollingworth said "many" of the city's planned bike lane installations in the next few years will not need to take motor-vehicle traffic lanes out of commission.
And for those that do?
"Just because the province is asking municipalities to submit for approval of future bike lanes doesn't mean they're going to say 'no,'" he said. "Hopefully, it's just an extra step."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Cyclists opposed to a new provincial bill designed to restrict bike lane construction gathered for a ride-and-rally at Hamilton city hall earlier this month.A ghost bike on the sidewalk alongside the bike lanes on York Boulevard at James Street North where a cyclist was killed last year.Hamilton plans to temporarily remove a painted bike lane on York Boulevard while upgraded infrastructure is designed - but councillors are worried new provincial rules might prevent future bike safety additions on the busy street.

Subject: Transportation planning; Automobiles; Infrastructure; Councils; Climate action; Politics; Cities; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Title: 'Stay in your lane': Premier Doug Ford's bike lane restrictions spurs loud opposition

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 4, 2024

Document 216 of 500

The former Ford government staffer at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal used personal emails to do government business. He is refusing to hand them over

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-ford-government-staffer-at-centre/docview/3123785912/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+former+Ford+government+staffer+at+the+centre+of+the+Greenbelt+scandal+used+personal+emails+to+do+government+business.+He+is+refusing+to+hand+them+over&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The former Ford government staffer at the heart of the Greenbelt scandal is refusing to hand over any emails he may have sent from his personal account about the ill-fated plan to develop the protected area.
Ryan Amato used his personal email in at least three interactions with lobbyists or a fellow government staffer regarding the Greenbelt project, according to records obtained by the Star.
These emails are only known about because they were forwarded to Amato's or another political staffer's government email account.
It's not known how many other times Amato used his personal email to discuss Greenbelt matters.
Ontario's auditor general denounced staffers' use of personal emails to conduct Greenbelt business, saying it "creates the perception of preferential access," and noted that any emails Amato or others sent from their personal accounts should still be subject to Freedom of Information (FOI) laws as they concern government business.
So in August 2023, shortly after the auditor general's report was published, the Star filed an FOI request for Amato's personal emails related to the Greenbelt.
The Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing said its staff asked Amato to hand over any records but he has repeatedly refused to cooperate. The ministry says it has no "legal mechanism" to compel him.
What Amato wrote - and to whom - from his personal email while he was selecting which lands would be removed from the Greenbelt is one of the unanswered questions from the scandal, which is under RCMP investigation and continues to dog the Ford government.
The fact it remains unanswered also illustrates how outdated and toothless access-to-information laws are in Canada, said Matt Malone, a Balsillie Scholar at the Balsillie School of International Affairs in Waterloo who researches government secrecy.
Government work on personal devices is 'an enormous problem'
Government officials using personal devices is "a huge weak point in the (access-to-information) system," Malone said. "It's an enormous problem when it comes to transparency and accountability."
Amato, who resigned in the wake of the auditor general's report, did not respond to the Star's requests for comment, and neither did his lawyer.
Ford himself has come under fire for using his personal cell phone for government business. For nearly two years Global News has been trying to get access to call logs for Ford's personal phone, arguing the Premier regularly uses it for work. (Records obtained by Global show Ford's government-issued phone went unused for months at a time.)
Initially, according to Global, the government said Ford's personal phone should not be subject to Freedom of Information laws. They subsequently argued his call logs should be kept confidential to protect the privacy rights of those who call him.
Ford, who often gives out his personal cell phone number to large audiences and encourages people to contact him directly with their problems, said those who call him need to be assured of confidentiality.
"Just imagine if every single politician made their personal conversations with their constituents public. It'd be a disaster. There'd be zero trust. They'd never call an elected official for help because they know the whole world would know their conversation," he said at a news conference earlier this year.
The case is currently before the province's Information and Privacy Commissioner (IPC).
Amato has twice refused government's requests for emails, province says
When the province disclosed to the Star only emails Amato had forwarded to his government account, the Star appealed to the IPC, arguing that additional records exist.
That appeal is under consideration and no decision has been made.
As part of the adjudication process, government lawyers wrote in their submissions that the ministry has sent letters to Amato - who is not named in the documents and referred to only as "the former employee" - formally requesting access to his personal email and that Amato has "twice refused to provide copies of any responsive records that may exist on [his] personal email account."
They said the only emails within their "custody and control" are those he forwarded to his government account.
Those emails include one from Ford-connected lobbyist Nico Fidani-Diker, in which he simply forwarded a Microsoft Teams meeting link, and four from lobbyist Peter Van Loan, a former federal Conservative cabinet minister who sent Amato examples of municipal councils that had passed resolutions to request Greenbelt removals in recent years.
Contacted by the Star in January about the emails, Van Loan said he shared the information with Amato "in an effort to be helpful." He said the council resolutions were not connected to any of his clients and he was not lobbying for their removal.
Van Loan said there was "no particular reason" why he used Amato's personal rather than government email address. "I did not take note at the time of the e-mail used," he said in an email to the Star. "It was not part of any file on which I was lobbying."
Lawyers for Amato provided a brief submission to the IPC, describing the Star's contention that additional records exist as "merely speculation."
The Star's submissions include evidence that at least one of Amato's personal emails related to the Greenbelt was not disclosed. Amato sent an email from his personal account to Patrick Sackville, Ford's chief of staff, which Sackville later forwarded to his own government account. The email was disclosed to the provincial NDP as part of a separate FOI request.
A spokesperson for the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing said it could not answer specific questions about the Star's FOI request or the ministry's submissions to the IPC. In a short statement, the spokesperson said the ministry is committed to its obligations under FOI laws.
"We have zero tolerance for any wrongdoing and expect anyone involved in the decision-making about the Greenbelt lands to have followed the letter of the law."
CAPTION: Ryan Amato, the former chief of staff to Ontario Minister of Municipal Affairs, is at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal.In her 2023 report, then Auditor General Bonnie Lysykdenounced Ford government staffers' use of personal emails to conduct Greenbelt business, saying it "creates the perception of preferential access."The $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal is under RCMP investigation and continues to dog Premier Doug Ford's government.

Subject: Electronic mail systems; Lobbyists; Lobbying; Freedom of information

Title: The former Ford government staffer at the centre of the Greenbelt scandal used personal emails to do government business. He is refusing to hand them over

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Document 217 of 500

Proposed provincial bike lane restrictions spur opposition: Bill 212 would allow the province to reject planned projects, rip out others

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/proposed-provincial-bike-lane-restrictions-spur/docview/3123658418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Proposed+provincial+bike+lane+restrictions+spur+opposition%3A+Bill+212+would+allow+the+province+to+reject+planned+projects%2C+rip+out+others&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

While some critics have labelled the bill pre-election politics, the legislation is still "very concerning" to anyone who cares about Hamilton's years-long efforts to protect cyclists, promote active transportation and prevent traffic fatalities, said Abbie Little, co-chair of the Cycle Hamilton group spearheading local opposition to Bill 212.
"I think there is a serious danger that (lane removals) could happen," Little said in an interview. "It would be a terrible step backwards for us to lose any existing infrastructure."
The bill could also throw a wrench in safe-cycling planning just when the city is about to ramp up such work with an eye to connecting the city's fragmented bike network and cutting down on traffic collisions.
Hamilton's master cycling plan spun its wheels for many years before a recent $60-million council commitment to accelerate the addition of new bike lanes and paths - particularly "protected" infrastructure that separates cyclists from cars with barriers, curbs or parking.
Under the proposed new provincial law, some of Hamilton's most popular and valued bike projects - like the Keddy Access Trail up the Mountain - might not have been possible, noted Environment Hamilton in a joint letter with other climate action groups opposing the bill.
The Claremont-climbing Keddy trail - named after cyclist Jay Keddy, who was struck and killed by a truck on the access road - required the removal of a car lane.
So too did the addition of a protected cycle track on Victoria Avenue North.
Both projects were completed within the last five years.
Environment Hamilton emailed all of its members earlier this month urging them to "flood" the province with comments opposing the proposed bike lane provisions of the bill.
Cyclists rallied in Toronto against the bill last week and a group of close to 30 did likewise at Hamilton city hall, noted Little.
Hamilton politicians are also weighing in - in some cases pointedly challenging the province's ability to prevent municipal decision-making on road safety.
"What happens when ... municipalities simply tell (the Tory government) to go pound sand," wrote Mountain councillor John-Paul Danko on social media in response to a Progressive Conservative party message about the bill.
"How exactly do they intend on forcing cities to remove - or block new cycling lanes on city streets? Could (Hamilton) then go install some speed bumps on (Highway) 403?"
Ward 1 councillor Maureen Wilson previously appealed to the premier to "stop playing wedge politics" and support - rather than undermine - cities. "Stay in your lane and step up, step where you are desperately needed ... fund supportive housing and health care."
In a statement, Mayor Andrea Horwath called bike lanes a "vital" element of Hamilton's transportation network, climate action strategy and council's goal to make sure residents can move around safely whether by foot, bike, bus or car.
She noted city staff will review the proposed legislation closely and report to council with any local implications.
City staff are working on that report now - but with some questions as yet unanswered, said transportation planning director Brian Hollingworth.
He noted the exact criteria to trigger provincial bike lane approval or review remains under development. The public commenting site says the criteria will be developed later after consultation with "targeted stakeholders."
That leaves it uncertain if Hamilton's dramatic conversion of one-way Main Street to two-way traffic - with a section of new bike lanes - would require provincial permission, for example. That project is actually designed to slow chronic speeding and make all road users safer on the collision-prone artery.
Also up for debate: does replacing "parking lanes" with bicycle infrastructure, as is planned on Victoria Avenue South, qualify as reducing a "traffic" lane?
On the upside, Hollingworth said "many" of the city's planned bike lane installations in the next few years will not need to take motor-vehicle traffic lanes out of commission.
And for those that do?
"Just because the province is asking municipalities to submit for approval of future bike lanes doesn't mean they're going to say 'no,'" he said. "Hopefully, it's just an extra step."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Hamilton plans to temporarily remove a painted bike lane on York Boulevard while upgraded infrastructure is designed, but councillors are worried new provincial rules might prevent future bike safety additions on the busy street. Cyclists opposed to a new provincial bill designed to restrict bike lane construction gathered for a ride-and-rally at Hamilton City Hall recently.Cathie Coward The Hamilton SpectatorAbbie Little photo

Document 218 of 500

The worst parts of American politics are creeping into Canada. Here's why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/worst-parts-american-politics-are-creeping-into/docview/3123545627/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+worst+parts+of+American+politics+are+creeping+into+Canada.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is the question bedevilling progressive-leaning people north and south of the Canada-U.S. border throughout this raucous, election-filled fall - how has the race against the conservative right become so close?
You hear it from people voting for Democratic contender Kamala Harris in Tuesday's presidential vote. How on earth is Donald Trump, a convicted felon, an election denier, the man who inspired a mob to stage a deadly riot on Capitol Hill four years ago, even a serious contender?
You heard it in British Columbia's recent election, which finally produced a squeaker of a majority win for incumbent premier David Eby this week. The B.C. Conservatives came very close to denying the New Democrats that victory, even in an election peppered with racist comments by candidates and a leader, John Rustad, who has flirted with anti-vaccine sentiment.
Shachi Kurl, of the Angus Reid Institute, which has done some interesting polling on polarization in recent months, says of the B.C. election: "I've never seen the kind of negativity and polarization - in terms of language, in terms of campaign advertising."
This how-did-we-get-here question is also being asked by some of Justin Trudeau's beleaguered Liberals, incredulous that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is so far ahead in the polls, when he seems to break all the rules of civil disagreement in politics. How has the battle between Liberals and Conservatives become so polarized and personal in this country?
At least on the surface, it looks like Canada is drifting toward the deep divisions that have characterized the Trump-Harris race and have made Tuesday's result look like it's going to be too close to call. Of course we're seeing polarization creep across the border, says Kurl. "We're not insulated ... It's here already here, and it's been here for some time."
To explore why it's come to this in the United States, I talked to Canada's own David Frum, staff writer for The Atlantic and one of the many prominent Republicans who worked for George W. Bush, who is supporting Harris over Trump. Frum mailed in his vote for Harris this past week.
Cautiously, Frum wonders whether Tuesday will be all that close. Certainly, he admits, that's an optimistic view, coming from someone who has written deeply and profoundly over how Trump has damaged the Republican brand.
While he doesn't want to come out sounding totally dismissive of polling, Frum says he is thinking a lot these days about how "robust" it is, compared to even five or 10 years ago. He cites the familiar reservations - how accurate a sample can be when ordinary voters are so hard to reach and engage, and what he describes as the "herding" effect, in which pollsters tend to herd toward similar results, so as not to be an outlier.
"I'm not saying the polls are wrong. I'm just saying polls have been less reliable in the 2010s and 2020s than they were in the 1980s," Frum says.
But what if polling itself is making people more polarized, forcing them into binary choices? That sentiment has been expressed by Yale University's Timothy Snyder, a historian who delves into American political culture, including his new book, On Freedom.
Last weekend, Snyder sat down for a conversation with the CBC's Matt Galloway at a Vancouver writers' festival and their conversation was broadcast on The Current.
Snyder says that all the attention to horse-race polling forces people into making binary, either-or choices, when your average voter tends to drift toward the complicated middle.
"Most people, the vast majority, actually prefer Kamala Harris's policies, but that's not reported in America, let alone the rest of the world, because we're stuck on the polls," Snyder said.
Kurl, naturally, doesn't buy that idea, saying polls don't create the problem, they just report it. She and Frum agree that partisan politics, as it is now conducted, creates the conditions for the us-versus-them polarization.
In real life, Frum explains, people can believe in contradictory things, but partisan politics doesn't have much tolerance for contradiction. "If you're someone who's a highly connected person in politics, you know there's liberal, there's conservative, and then it makes sense" to divide the world that way, says Frum. This is also why he's skeptical of the claims that ordinary voters are polarized over policy. "They don't think it's as important as other people do, and they don't organize their policy preferences the way people more connected to politics do."
One of the most interesting polls I've seen this year - one I wrote about recently, was the Angus Reid report on the "missing middle." Without drilling again into the numbers here, it essentially found that most Canadians consider themselves political orphans - centrist-leaning people who believe political parties are either too hard left or too hard right for them.
Kurl says we saw an early warning of this in the last federal Conservative leadership race two years ago, where former Quebec premier Jean Charest's more moderate, red-Tory conservatism was trounced by Poilievre and his harder-right base.
Frum has an interesting explanation for how we got here.
"Polarization is something we see in every developed country, and it's partly the price we pay for education," he says. "As people become better informed, as people become more affluent and they're more prone to vote on the basis of cultural identity."
He describes it this way. "In 1880, you could win somebody's vote by giving them a turkey or a slug of whiskey. In 1960, you could win somebody's vote by bringing them a new dam or a new high school," he says. "Today, you win people's vote by deep social, cultural, tribal identification ... We're just in a more affluent place and our needs are less immediate, so people are much better informed and much more individualistic."
This is why Canadians should be on the lookout for spillover effects from Tuesday's historic showdown between Trump and Harris. We're not just spectators.
Kurl believes Canada could resist the excesses of that polarization before the next election here, but it would take leadership, and at present she doesn't see any leaders trying to bridge the partisan divides.
I asked Frum whether he was worried about Tuesday. "Of course," he said. He's worried even when people cautiously predict that Trump won't win, as Frum so hopes, and compares it to how humans would feel if told that a giant comet "probably" won't hit the Earth.
By that same metaphor, Canada's political culture won't be unaffected either. The impact of the too-close-to-call race will reverberate here, because when polarization hits the U.S., it hits Canada, too.
CAPTION: Republican presidential nominee and former President Donald Trump, left, and Democratic presidential nominee Vice President Kamala Harris.

Subject: Nominations; Political campaigns; Conservatism; Culture; Voters; Political leadership; Political advertising

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Harris, Kamala; Trump, Donald J

Document 219 of 500

Justin Trudeau and Donald Trump had some bad moments and some oddly friendly ones - but here's what will really matter if Trump is re-elected

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-donald-trump-had-some-bad-moments/docview/3123545554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+and+Donald+Trump+had+some+bad+moments+and+some+oddly+friendly+ones+-+but+here%27s+what+will+really+matter+if+Trump+is+re-elected&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Word came unexpectedly in January 2017 that President Donald Trump wanted to talk to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Officials scrambled to connect the two leaders and racked their brains: what urgent matter had they not foreseen?
Trudeau had already had a friendly congratulatory call with the newly inaugurated Republican president. Trump, who campaigned on ripping up the North American free trade deal and forcing Canada and all NATO allies to spend more on defence, riffed on Trudeau's famous father, the former prime minister Pierre Elliot Trudeau.
When the call was patched through, tension quickly evaporated.
A jovial Trump told Trudeau it was his first time on Air Force One and he was trying out the new secure phone line, marvelling at the technology.
If politics is personal, not all personal calls are politics. No official readout from either office shows their phone chat. Yet Trump was impressed with his new ride. "Beautiful. Great plane," Trump said, according to the media pool report.
It was an early sign theirs would be no ordinary relationship.
It had highs and lows. It was marked by very public disagreements, and an oddly personal connection that appears to have survived those turbulent years and the gap since Trump was in power.
Trudeau's call to Trump after the July assassination attempt against the Republican candidate was described as genuinely warm, even though the two hadn't spoken since Trump left office in 2021. U.S. Sen. Lindsey Graham, while golfing with Trump, had been texting with Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, and relayed Trudeau's wish to speak to Trump, with two sources describing the call as extraordinarily friendly.
Yet the impulsive Trump challenged Trudeau - and Canada - in ways that frequently threw the prime minister's team for a loop, according to several sources who were part of Trudeau's inner circle through the tumultuous time and who spoke to the Star on a background-only basis.
The Liberals believe if Trump wins, it will be messy, fraught and rocky. They also believe the many relationships formed then - and the lessons learned - could come in handy once more.
But is this an era where the Canada-U.S. relationship thrives because personalities align, the way it did when Brian Mulroney forged strong ties with Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush, or when Bill Clinton and Jean Chrétien clicked?
Whatever the public impression may be of a relationship, the reality is prime ministers and presidents can get things done when their political interests line up.
Stephen Harper and George W. Bush signed a seven-year softwood lumber deal not because of any ideological alignment; Conservatives say Bush pushed it over the line because he appreciated Harper's military stance on Afghanistan.
Trudeau, Trump and Mexico's president signed a new North American trade pact despite all the public flare-ups. It comes up for review in two years. What happens next is anyone's guess.
"There's no question that a personal relationship matters," said David MacNaughton, who was Canada's ambassador to Washington from 2016 to 2019. "Having said that, I think sometimes people put too much emphasis on it."
MacNaughton was an integral part of the Trudeau team that began outreach to Trump's circles well before the 2017 inauguration, and in some cases even before the election, to establish connections with Trump's family members, advisers and business associates that they leaned on in later difficult discussions.
But friendly ties are no guarantee, he said in an interview. MacNaughton recalled that when Trudeau was invited to the White House by then-president Barack Obama for a state dinner, the buzz was all about the "bromance" between two progressive leaders. MacNaughton, who had just become ambassador, said he thought Canada could strike a new deal on U.S. tariffs on Canadian softwood lumber after the previous one had expired.
The ambassador met three times with Obama's U.S. trade representative, who finally told him, "'This president is not going to do anything on softwood lumber.'"
"I said, 'Why?' and he said, 'Because he's in legacy mode, and what he wants to have done is he wants the Trans-Pacific Partnership (trade deal) to get through Congress. In order to do that, he's gotta get key senators onside and a couple of senators he needs onside are against any deal with Canada on softwood lumber. So it ain't happening.'
"No matter how warm and friendly and cosy the relationship," MacNaughton said, the U.S. will do what "the president thinks is the right politics or the right thing for the country.
"A personal relationship can help push things over the line if it's close, or stop really bad things from happening, but it isn't the be-all and end-all."
No matter who wins on Tuesday - Trump or Kamala Harris - and no matter who is Canadian prime minister throughout the next president's four-year term - Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre - MacNaughton said Canada has to understand the "substantive reasons" the U.S. takes positions it does on important matters like trade, defence and security.
The same sentiment would apply if Harris wins the election. He said American Democrats are more protectionist and isolationist now than at any time in recent history, and no Canadian politician should fail to heed their concerns.
No surprise that a senior Conservative source, with knowledge of how Conservatives dealt with the U.S. under Harper and how they might approach a Trump or Harris administration, agrees.
"Personalities do matter. Competency, backbone, charm, all of those things matter, yeah. But at the core of it, competency is the most important part. That's where I think Harper and Bush got along. Harper and Obama got along," at least to a certain extent. "They had a professional relationship, and they worked at it," the insider said.
As for whether a Poilievre-led government would get along with Trump or Harris, the Conservative insider said it would take a "level-headed and adult approach" to the relationship, no matter the stripe of the administration, or ideological affinities, if any, adding there are far fewer now between Canadian Conservatives and the Republicans than in Mulroney's day.
"I don't think American demands will change between administrations. Tone might be different, but demands don't change. National interests tend to be pretty evergreen."
Poilievre has not met either presidential candidate, nor has he attended any political conventions in the U.S., his office said.
Harris lived with her mother in Montreal for a time, but Trudeau and she didn't know each other then. As vice-president, Harris hasn't visited Canada, but the pair has met on a number of occasions, in Washington and elsewhere.
Two weeks after Russia invaded Ukraine, Trudeau and Harris crossed paths in Poland and sat down at her hotel for a meeting at a critical time when Western allies were trying to align the NATO response to Russia's aggression. It went an hour over time. Most recently, they met on the sidelines of the Service Employees International Union convention in Philadelphia in May.
According to a Canadian official, Harris said it was a "nice surprise" and showed they both support the North American labour movement. They talked about Haiti, and military issues, with Harris noting she knew Canada was "focused on the Arctic." When Trudeau said Canada was ratcheting up defence spending from 1.3 per cent of GDP to 1.7 per cent, she said "'that's good to hear.'" (That is still shy of NATO's targets for all allies - and still a sore point for Trump.)
Harris told the Canadians the election fight was important to her. It was three months before Joe Biden's disastrous performance at the election debate upended the presidential campaign, leading to his withdrawal, and her nomination.
An official recalled her analysis was Democrats were "going to win, but it's going to be bloody difficult," pointing to how aggressive Trump and the MAGA movement were in their ongoing denial of the 2020 election result, and how they represented "'unapologetic threats to democracy.'"
At Wednesday's Liberal caucus meeting, a source said, ministers and MPs were instructed not to talk about the U.S. election before the outcome is known. Brian Clow, Trudeau's deputy chief of staff, delivered the same message to ministerial chiefs of staff, telling them to ensure people make "absolutely no comment whatsoever" in social media posts or news stories, saying, "we have to work with whoever is elected."
The stakes are obviously high. The Liberal government has ramped up efforts to get ready for any outcome.
Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador in Washington, and two cabinet ministers were tasked in January with leading the outreach effort, supported by a special group at Global Affairs Canada.
Hillman's team has been criss-crossing the states, talking to governors, local officials and federal representatives. Premiers, business and union leaders were integral to Canada's charm offensive last time, and will be again as needed.
In an interview, Hillman said the relationship between political leaders is important insofar as they need to be able to talk to each other and do business together. But she emphasized the Canada-U.S. relationship is deeper and broader than just at the top. It relies on strong cabinet minister relationships with counterparts, and at other legislative levels of government.
"We have relationships at a political level across this entire country. We have relationships with governors, with senators and they represent constituencies across the whole country," she said. "And this is not a parliamentary system - the executive must have the support of Congress in order to fulfil its legislative agenda. And so we pay a lot of attention to building relationships and common purpose, yes with the administration, but also with our allies in Congress."
It pays off. Hillman had prime spots at both the Democratic and Republican national conventions this summer, seated with the extended family of Harris's spouse Doug Emhoff in Chicago, and attending key Republican events at the Milwaukee convention, including a small gathering where the keynote speaker was Donald Trump Jr., and another with Trump's former energy secretary.
During the last Trump administration, the Trudeau government's strategy deliberately focused on responding only to what Trump did, not to everything he said. But a key lesson learned was that Trump almost always did what he said he would do, several officials said.
Expect the same this time around.
It's how the Liberal government believes it survived even Trump's infamous blow-up at Trudeau after the G7 meeting in Charlevoix in 2018.
None of the Canadians - Liberal or Conservative - who spoke to the Star this week would state who they expect to win Tuesday's election.
MacNaughton's hope is that it will be a clear result whatever the final tally, and that the world does not see a close result and ensuing uncertainty that in 2000 required a Supreme Court ruling to settle the George W. Bush-Al Gore battle.
"I think we can deal with either eventuality from a presidential point of view," he said. "But what would be terribly, terribly challenging for the world would be something where the result is legitimately unclear for some period of time."
CAPTION: U.S. president Donald Trump, left, and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau talk prior to a NATO round table meeting in Watford, Hertfordshire, England, on Dec. 4, 2019.

Subject: Trade agreements; Political parties; Political campaigns; Presidential elections; Union leadership; Personal relationships; Prime ministers; Professional relationships

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade agreements Union leadership Professional relationships

Location: Russia; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Title: Justin Trudeau and Donald Trump had some bad moments and some oddly friendly ones - but here's what will really matter if Trump is re-elected

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 3, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Document 220 of 500

Doug Ford's $200 cheques are yesterday's solution to today's worries about inflation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-200-cheques-are-yesterdays-solution/docview/3123363270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+cheques+are+yesterday%27s+solution+to+today%27s+worries+about+inflation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prices are high. An election is nigh.
Affordability is top of mind.
Which is why 15 million Ontarians are getting an anti-inflation consolation prize: $200 cheques in the mail soon.
Thank you, Doug Ford.
What better solution for inflation and affordability both?
Except that Ford's pre-election generosity and grandiosity may be the wrong prescription for the wrong problem.
What if our continuing obsession with inflation is a holdover from the past? Is Ford fighting yesterday's war with outdated cheques to win next year's vote?
It's a question of public perception and political calculation versus economic reality.
Public opinion polls aren't lying when they identify affordability as the top issue for voters. People assuredly believe prices are too high.
Which is why Ford is so shamelessly riding the affordability bandwagon. So, too, is federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who coined the slogan "Justinflation" as a way to pin blame for rising prices on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau alone.
No one questions that inflation surged post-pandemic, thanks mostly to supply chain disruptions caused by COVID shutdowns, plus massive government stimulus and a record influx of temporary workers (who stoked demand for housing). Inflation peaked at 8.1 per cent in mid-2022, and Canadians felt the pinch in food and housing prices.
But that was then. This is now.
The inflation rate has dropped below three per cent so far this year, according to Statistics Canada. In fact, inflation fell to 1.6 per cent in September - a historically low figure, and well below the two per cent target set by the Bank of Canada.
Which is why the central bank has revised its key lending rate steadily downward this year, and did so again last week.
So if that rock bottom inflation rate is evidence enough for the bank to conclude that inflation is increasingly under control, why do ordinary Canadians still fret about affordability? And if central bankers can get inflation under control, why can't politicians make life feel more affordable?
First, homebuyers will always feel nostalgia for the good old days when times were bad and mortgage rates reached record lows in mid-pandemic. Back then, the Bank of Canada resorted to emergency stimulus measures that dropped lending rates to near-zero, but those rates are not coming back - nor should they, barring another massive crisis. Get used to it.
Second, today's remarkably low inflation rate - which has reassured even our stickler bankers - is small comfort for ordinary consumers. People may understand that the rate of increase has since slowed, but they also know that the rapid increases of recent years are now baked in - so prices absolutely feel higher than they once were.
Put another way, it's small consolation if the inflation rate has ebbed, belatedly, if today's prices are stuck at cumulatively higher levels. StatCan figures show that a basket of goods that cost $137 in September 2020 would cost $161 in September 2024 - an increase of 17.5 per cent over the past four years.
Admittedly, that was long ago. But people still recall pre-pandemic prices as if they were yesterday - and that's the benchmark that comes to mind when they see today's higher price tags.
People aren't so much fussed about the latest 1.6 per cent inflation rate; they're fuming that prices are at least 10 times higher - 17.5 per cent - than they once were.
So are we stuck with the cumulative increases of recent years?
One serious remedy is to raise wages for working people to make up for lost ground, and wages did indeed go up post-pandemic - albeit not for everyone, and the increases didn't always keep up. But wage hikes can only get you so far before you fall into an inflationary cycle of higher pay causing higher prices.
Another perception problem that economists have long observed is that workers feel that they've "earned" a pay hike by dint of hard work. They don't look upon a wage increase as compensation for inflation.
Workers understandably want to get ahead, not see their higher pay cheque eaten up by rising food prices. They resent inflation - even if that's what's driving their catch-up wage increase - because it erodes their hard-won purchasing power.
Rather than increasing wages, what about rolling back price hikes? Be careful what you wish for.
Economists refer to price decrease as deflation - the evil twin of inflation - which is almost always bad news. Historically, a deflationary economy goes hand in hand with recession and depression.
Producers respond to price decreases by reducing their payroll costs - either by cutting wages or laying off workers, which in turn lowers aggregate consumer demand. Also, consumers and businesses may delay new purchases if they know prices will be lower tomorrow than they are today - further depressing demand.
All that said, questions of affordability are not imaginary. But the solutions are illusory.
People are feeling the pinch. But don't be fooled by Ford's political pitch, because $200 ain't what it used to be.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Novocol Pharma in Cambridge, Ont. on Nov. 1, 2024.

Subject: Anti inflation; Consumers; Inflation rates; Pandemics; Housing prices; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Anti inflation Consumers Inflation rates Housing prices Wages & salaries

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

Title: Doug Ford's $200 cheques are yesterday's solution to today's worries about inflation
