Document 221 of 500

Court battle over Hamilton encampments on horizon

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/court-battle-over-hamilton-encampments-on-horizon/docview/3123363250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Court+battle+over+Hamilton+encampments+on+horizon&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A legal battle over how Hamilton deals with encampments is coming to a head next month as the city scrambles to create hundreds of new shelter spaces to get people out of tents in time for winter.
The case proceeds as Premier Doug Ford challenges Ontario municipalities to formally ask him to use the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms "notwithstanding clause" to help them deal with encampments, a route Mayor Andrea Horwath has dismissed as a "step too far."
The human rights-based litigation on behalf of 19 people who live outside or once did is slated for three days starting Dec. 16, a court date that city officials have their eyes on as they grapple with growing community tensions over tents pitched in parks.
"It's probably, I would say, one of the paramount considerations, that is really driving our encampment policy," said Coun. John-Paul Danko, noting the litigation is "severely restricting" the city's ability to enforce bylaws relating to encampments.
Other municipalities are also "held hostage" to such legal challenges, added Danko, who in recent months has emphasized the impact of the legal case mounted by "encampment supporters."
That's "Trumpian politics," responded Wade Poziomka, one of the lawyers behind the litigation, which dates to 2021 but was later amended and refiled. "It's fake news and mischaracterizing what's actually happening."
He and his colleagues have offered to settle with the city, but to no avail, and the issue shouldn't have to be hashed out in court in the first place, Poziomka said.
"We should be recognizing that there's a portion of our population that's vulnerable, that needs help, and we should be figuring out how do we do that in a way that makes sense for everybody."
The litigation challenges the constitutionality of city bylaws that bar tents in parks and other public spaces and argues enforcement violates the applicants' rights, notably amid a shortage of adequate shelter spaces to meet their needs.
"Of course, we're not encampment supporters. Nobody is. I don't know anyone on our team that supports encampments, but we support people having a right to exist," Poziomka said.
'More about politics'
Ford's remarks this week about the notwithstanding clause, which allows for legislation that quashes certain rights, are "highly problematic," said Poziomka, calling the Charter mechanism a tool of "last resort" that needs "safeguards built around it."
Horwath, meanwhile, said Friday she won't sign a letter several mayors have penned asking the province to pass tougher laws, using the Charter mechanism if necessary.
The province has "every ability" to do that, without the urging of municipal leaders, she said.
"So this is more about politics, I think, than it is about actual solving of problems. And I'm here to solve problems."
Instead, the province should focus on affordable and supportive housing, mental health supports, addiction services, high rents and social-assistance rates - all of which are driving a homelessness crisis municipalities can't solve on their own, Horwath said.
"I don't believe the focus should be taking away people's rights. I think the focus should be how do we fix a broken system."
Beds, shelters a temporary boost
Of roughly 1,600 people who are homeless in Hamilton, 300 are known to live outside, a fluid contingent that has been on the rise but can fluctuate according to the season.
As municipalities call on upper levels of government for more support to respond to the overlapping issues, advocates have filed legal challenges vying for the rights of those living outside who face being uprooted from tent sites.
Last year, in an oft-cited decision, an Ontario justice ruled that evicting people from an encampment in Kitchener would violate their rights.
That decision was based on "complex economic, personal and social circumstances, including the shortage of accessible shelter spaces" in the area, Justice Michael Valente wrote.
In September, Hamilton council backed an $18.3-million plan to create 192 more shelter and transitional beds and 40 "micro shelters" for up to 80 people with "wraparound" services at a city-owned site off Barton Street West.
That temporary boost is meant to serve as a stopgap to get people indoors in time for winter as the city looks to senior levels of government to shore up more affordable and supportive housing.
It will help Hamilton's most vulnerable and address tents in parks while not losing sight of the legal landscape, Horwath said after councillors backed the strategy.
"This is a pathway to get us there in a way that doesn't jeopardize any of our other obligations in terms of current case law and court situations that we're facing."
It's too soon to know how the plan for 272 more temporary spaces will influence the court case, Poziomka said. "But I think looking at options and solutions is obviously in everybody's interest."
The legal battle, which was moved from an October court date to December, hasn't proceeded in a straight line, with side skirmishes over witnesses and most recently, the inclusion of the Ontario Human Rights Commission as an intervener, a matter that's still to be decided.
City-wide tent ban eyed
Council, meanwhile, has made tweaks to an increasingly publicly scrutinized encampment protocol that allows for clusters of up to five tents in parks and other outdoor spaces subject to certain rules and spatial limitations.
Recent changes include exemptions that outlaw encampments in specific lower-city parks with the outdoor Barton site on the horizon and potential spillover effect of the extra indoor beds on local green spaces taken into account.
But the tent prohibition should apply to parks across Hamilton once the 272 additional spaces are available, said Coun. Esther Pauls, who plans to introduce a motion to that effect Wednesday.
"Let's put an end to it. We have enough beds. No more parks. This is the perfect time," said Pauls, who noted she fields complaints from residents about tents in various parks in Ward 7 on the Mountain.
"If we have a law that says downtown you can (ban tents), up the Mountain, you can't, people are going to revolt, whether there's 20 tents, or four or five tents."
Danko has urged caution about tinkering too much with the protocol with the court case still on the docket, noting an unfavourable decision could present "pretty severe ramifications" for the city.
Nonetheless, a city-wide parks ban is "in line" with council direction for staff to report back in January on the potential "sunsetting" of the encampment protocol as the additional shelter spaces come online, he pointed out.
But Coun. Cameron Kroetsch contended Pauls' upcoming motion is ill-advised with the outcome of the city's plan to add the temporary beds still to be determined.
"That seems very reactive and it's not going to lead us down a good path," the downtown-and-area councillor said.
The city must have a "balanced approach" to encampments that's rooted in human rights and not solely reliant on enforcement with some people not staying in shelters, he said.
"There will always be a need for us to have a protocol to address those folks who are living outside."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Municipalities, including Hamilton, are "held hostage" by litigation that challenges their ability to enforce bylaws barring tents in parks, Coun. John-Paul Danko says.Mayor Andrea Horwath says she won't join other Ontario mayors in calling for the provincial government to use the "notwithstanding clause" to help municipalities deal with encampments.People have pitched tents on a Barton Street West property where the city plans to locate 40 "micro shelters" for up to 80 people in time for winter.

Nov. 2: Micro shelters are not a solution, donate 'rebate' cheque where it's needed and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nov-2-micro-shelters-are-not-solution-donate/docview/3123363219/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nov.+2%3A+Micro+shelters+are+not+a+solution%2C+donate+%27rebate%27+cheque+where+it%27s+needed+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Micro shelters no solution
Re: 'Micro shelters' on their way to Barton West site, Oct. 31
So the tiny shelters (now renamed "micro shelters") are finally on their way to Hamilton. Does anyone believe these are going to rid our city of its homeless problem? The "do-gooders" can dream, but for sure these "micro shelters" will soon be a dumping ground for all the mess that existed around the illegal tents.
And the council members who state there will be a "protocol" for use and behaviour in the area should be reminded that you currently have a protocol for the encampments you chose to ignore. Point in case, the terrible state you have allowed our Peace Park to become, by allowing it to exist illegally, as your protocol states there be no encampment within 100 metres of a school. I have been stating that to city councillors and staff for two years. And they are going to manage the "micro shelter" site? Not a chance.
Terry Butlin, Hamilton
Concert trip too stressful
I left my home in Burlington at 6:30 p.m. for a 21-minute drive to FirstOntario Concert Hall in Hamilton Saturday night. It took me 17 minutes to get to the Main Street exit. Then it took me 30 minutes to even approach Bay Street.
Being a woman on her own in downtown Hamilton, I had intended to park in the parking garage with all the other concert goers, as there is safety in numbers. I realized that would put me in jeopardy of missing the first 20 minutes of the concert so I turned right and parked in an exposed lot with three tents set up around the perimeter.
The entire concert I was distracted by worry about my safety when I returned to my vehicle.
Between paying for the ticket, the stress of all the traffic and my safety of being alone in downtown Hamilton after dark, I will think long and hard about attending another concert in Hamilton.
Elizabeth Lucas, Burlington
Donate 'rebate' where it's needed
So even the wealthiest Ontarians will receive a $200 "gift" from Premier Doug Ford in the new year. Let's empower the people to do the right thing. If all who are able to donate those dollars could support affordable housing and health-care initiatives, think of the difference we could make. You're welcome, premier.
Connie Smith, Ancaster
Funds needed for the public good
As much as its sounds like finding lost money, bear in mind that these are your tax dollars. At the cost of $3 billion. Doug Ford is brazenly trying to buy your vote. Funds that could be allocated to health care, provincial and municipal infrastructure, working toward providing better shelter for the marginalized population and a plethora of other needs. We need to step outside ourselves and become part of a cause that is much larger than oneself, one that brings a greater good to a greater community. Beware the Trojan horse.
Ross Prince, Hamilton
Use 'rebate' to fund opposition
The best use I can think of for the $3-billion ("vote for me") gift that Premier Ford is giving Ontarians is to donate all or part of the cheque to the Liberal, New Democrat or Green party in the name of the Progressive Conservative Party.
Carl Easton, Hamilton
Ford takes a page from Trudeau
Re: Taxpayers are funding premier's $200 rebates, Oct. 31
Certainly an accurate description of where the rebate is coming from. And the column is both insightful and well written. It is noteworthy that very early on, Matthew Lau points out this is similar to the carbon tax rebate also funded by taxpayers. Might I suggest a more balanced headline that reflects the body of the article: Ford learns from Trudeau, gives taxpayers their own money back.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Rebate funded by health-care cuts?
Re: Changes to home-care equipment contracts halted during crisis, Oct. 30
I am deeply disturbed by this government's inexcusable handling of the Ontario Health atHome supply procurement changes. Not being able to get timely and appropriate supplies is putting people's lives at risk. Before September this was not an issue. The changes you put in place have directly led to this crisis. Platitudes like saying you are going to fix this came only after over a month of problems became public in the press, and these serious problems still exist.
Are the cost savings these changes will generate going toward funding the $200 "rebate" to citizens including the highest income earners? Ontarians need and deserve an independent review of this gross mismanagement.
Margaret Tremblay, Dundas
Not a fan of leaving leaves
Re: A reminder to leave the leaves, Oct. 23
A carpet of dead leaves becomes a sodden mat that encourages mould and disease of lawn grasses. It also provides cover for rodents to travel on their trails beneath it. If possible, mow the dry leaves into small pieces and/or rake and bag them for disposal as many do. Their value as fertilizer is vastly overrated. Lastly, don't tell others how to manage their lawn unless asked.
Gord Baker, Carlisle
CAPTION: MicroShelters Inc., an Indigenous-owned company, says it expects 40 shelters for homeless people it's providing the city for a site off Barton Street West to arrive in December.

Document 223 of 500

Editorial cartoonists are society's 'canaries in the coal mine'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-cartoonists-are-societys-canaries-coal/docview/3123363153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Editorial+cartoonists+are+society%27s+%27canaries+in+the+coal+mine%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For those who have followed the pages of The Hamilton Spectator over the decades, the names Blaine and Doug Wright likely resonate as beloved editorial cartoonists who left an indelible mark on this city and its newspaper from the 1960s to the 1990s.
Their distinctive satire and social commentary paved the way for future generations, including my own.
The Hamilton Spectator has a long-standing tradition of celebrating the craft of visual satire, presenting it daily alongside written opinion pieces. This legacy stretches back well before either of the two giants whose shadows I now walk in.
In the mining industry, canaries were once taken into tunnels to test air quality. If a canary dropped dead, it signalled danger and warned miners not to proceed.
This old cliché of "canaries in the coal mine" aptly applies to editorial cartoonists, whose illustrations reveal hard truths about injustice, the folly of those in power, and looming crises. We deliver quick, poignant commentary on pressing issues, often in seconds, contrasting with the time-consuming nature of the surrounding written text.
One such canary, now largely forgotten, is Ivan Glassco, who served as an editorial cartoonist for The Hamilton Spectator from 1933 to 1940. Those familiar with history know this was a momentous period, as Canada was emerging on the world stage - a transformation well documented in this paper of record.
Glassco, compelled by the inhumanity of his time, enlisted in the RCAF but tragically lost his life during training in Toronto when his revolver accidentally discharged. He was just 38 years old.
Glassco's artistic style is reminiscent of the cartoons from the 1920s and '30s, bringing to mind the character of Rich Uncle Pennybags from the Monopoly board game. This portly figure, complete with a top hat and tails, symbolizes crony politics and rampant capitalism - an image Glassco embraced and incorporated into his own work, which continues to influence contemporary cartoonists, including myself.
His cartoons, shaped by the styles of renowned cartoonists such as Herbert Block from the U.S. and David Low from the U.K., reflect a diverse artistic background. Growing up in Hamilton and studying architecture at McGill University, Glassco's unique perspective was further enriched by the artistic parallels to cubism and the style of fellow cartoonist Robert LaPalme.
The themes present in Glassco's work remain strikingly relevant today. The economic upheaval of the Great Depression resonates with the challenges we've faced during the recent pandemic.
Similarly, the rise of fascism in the 1930s echoes certain political trends we see today, marked by authoritarianism and populism in various parts of the world. While direct comparisons may be tenuous, history provides valuable insights into our current circumstances.
On a local level, Glassco's cartoons reflecting frustrations with ineffective leadership still resonate with contemporary audiences. The longing for capable leaders is a timeless sentiment that rings true today.
However, it is no secret that the platform for which editorial cartoonists like Glassco once relied is rapidly disappearing.
The mission of editorial cartoons - afflicting the comfortable and comforting the afflicted - has been compromised. In recent years, cartoons, (thankfully not at the Hamilton Spectator) have been diluted to fit corporate partisan agendas, reduced to humour that avoids controversy, or worst of all, eliminated from newspapers altogether.
Editorial cartoonists have not done their best to champion the true purpose of our work.
We have overlooked the importance of honouring the legacy of pioneers like Glassco. Although we gather at annual conventions, after the beer swilling, self-promotion often leads us to view our colleagues as competitors rather than allies in the fight against powerful forces.
The last reference to Glassco in The Hamilton Spectator dates back to 1961, a stark reminder that we have allowed others to redefine our role as mere providers of humorous content. This misconception has contributed to a growing ignorance that dismisses the value of editorial commentary. This attitude is not just echoed by online trolls; it has been voiced by politicians, from the highest offices to local councils, who prefer unverified alternative means to spread their messages and misinformation.
I've drawn for nearly 56 years of my life, including 27 privileged years on these pages. I embrace the bias that comes with editorial cartooning. We are biased in favour of democracy, rights, science and truth. A world that does not prioritize these values is one we do not wish to inhabit. This bias is for the better, whether through the work of current cartoonists or the enduring legacies from those of the past.
The way we receive editorial cartoons is evolving, but I believe that the craft will endure. It is vital that we adapt and continue to engage our audiences with the same passion and purpose that defined the work of pioneers like Ivan Glassco.
CAPTION: A June 1934 cartoon by Ivan Glassco depicts a taxpayer examining their bank book, visibly burdened by the weight of municipal, provincial and federal taxes. Its lower caption reads, "I saw a man clothed with rags, a book in his hand, and a great burden upon his back. As he read he wept and trembled saying, 'What shall I do?'" Published in The Hamilton Spectator on June 2, 1934.The righteous City of Hamilton bathing suit inspection commission is made up of Mr. False Modesty, Miss Uplift, Mrs. Grundy and Miss Killjoy. By Ivan Glassco, published in The Hamilton Spectator, July 18, 1936.Ivan Glassco, born in Hamilton in 1902, transitioned from architecture, gained recognition for realistic caricatures, and became the editorial cartoonist for the Hamilton Spectator in 1934, before his death in 1941 while serving with the Royal Canadian Air Force.The voter portrayed as Solomon before two women (William Earl Rowe representing the Conservative party, and Mitchell Hepburn of the Liberals) pleading over a baby representing Ontario. By Ivan Glassco, published in The Hamilton Spectator on Sept. 28, 1937.A 1939 cartoon by Ivan Glassco comments on the United States' reluctance to become directly involved in the Second World War. Published in The Hamilton Spectator on Sept. 9, 1939.A 1934 cartoon by Ivan Glassco depicts "Mother Hamilton" sweeping away useless candidates attempting to enter city hall with her vote broom. The cartoon likely satirizes the political climate in Hamilton during the time, highlighting the voters' discerning attitude towards candidates seeking office. Overall, the cartoon reflects a commentary on the importance of informed and selective voting in local elections, as well as the power of the electorate to influence political outcomes. Published in The Hamilton Spectator on Nov. 30, 1934.A 1938 cartoon by Ivan Glassco features imagery of an authoritarian regime fuelling racial hatred and division through the dissemination of malicious propaganda. The lower caption, "There's no fuel like the old fuel!" Published in The Hamilton Spectator on Oct. 26, 1938.An Ivan Glassco cartoon suggests the militarization and fortification of the Rhine region under Adolf Hitler's regime. By Ivan Glassco, published in The Hamilton Spectator March 21, 1936.Apathy in the West risks emboldening authoritarian expansionism, as seen in Putin's Russia. This cartoon parodies an original from an Ivan Glassco editorial cartoon in 1936. It shows a braided German girl symbolizing the "Rhine" donning a dress of armour labelled "Hitler Gowns," highlighting Hitler's militarization of the Rhine region.

Subject: Coal mining; Local elections; Authoritarianism; Cartoonists; Editorial cartoons; Books

Business indexing term: Subject: Coal mining

Location: United States--US

Title: Editorial cartoonists are society's 'canaries in the coal mine'

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Document 224 of 500

Canaries in the coal mine: While fewer in number today, the mission of editorial cartoonists remains just as important as ever

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canaries-coal-mine/docview/3123255277/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canaries+in+the+coal+mine%3A+While+fewer+in+number+today%2C+the+mission+of+editorial+cartoonists+remains+just+as+important+as+ever&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

In recent years, cartoons, (thankfully not at the Hamilton Spectator) have been diluted to fit corporate partisan agendas, reduced to humour that avoids controversy, or worst of all, eliminated from newspapers altogether.
Editorial cartoonists have not done their best to champion the true purpose of our work.
We have overlooked the importance of honouring the legacy of pioneers like Glassco. Although we gather at annual conventions, after the beer swilling, self-promotion often leads us to view our colleagues as competitors rather than allies in the fight against powerful forces.
The last reference to Glassco in The Hamilton Spectator dates back to 1961, a stark reminder that we have allowed others to redefine our role as mere providers of humorous content. This misconception has contributed to a growing ignorance that dismisses the value of editorial commentary. This attitude is not just echoed by online trolls; it has been voiced by politicians, from the highest offices to local councils, who prefer unverified alternative means to spread their messages and misinformation.
I've drawn for nearly 56 years of my life, including 27 privileged years on these pages. I embrace the bias that comes with editorial cartooning. We are biased in favour of democracy, rights, science and truth. A world that does not prioritize these values is one we do not wish to inhabit. This bias is for the better, whether through the work of current cartoonists or the enduring legacies from those of the past.
The way we receive editorial cartoons is evolving, but I believe that the craft will endure. It is vital that we adapt and continue to engage our audiences with the same passion and purpose that defined the work of pioneers like Ivan Glassco.
Graeme MacKay has been the Hamilton Spectator's staff
editorial cartoonist since 1997. He has been twice cited by the United Nations for excellence in satire, and has been recognized by the Association of Canadian Cartoonists with the George Townsend Award for best
cartoon of the year.

Court battle over Hamilton encampments on horizon: Mayor says she won't sign letter to Ford urging use of 'notwithstanding clause'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/court-battle-over-hamilton-encampments-on-horizon/docview/3123253776/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Court+battle+over+Hamilton+encampments+on+horizon%3A+Mayor+says+she+won%27t+sign+letter+to+Ford+urging+use+of+%27notwithstanding+clause%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A legal battle over how Hamilton deals with encampments is coming to a head next month as the city scrambles to create hundreds of new shelter spaces to get people out of tents in time for winter.
The case proceeds as Premier Doug Ford challenges Ontario municipalities to formally ask him to use the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms "notwithstanding clause" to help them deal with encampments, a route Mayor Andrea Horwath has dismissed as a "step too far."
The human rights-based litigation on behalf of 19 people who live outside or once did is slated for three days starting Dec. 16, a court date that city officials have their eyes on as they grapple with growing community tensions over tents pitched in parks.
"It's probably, I would say, one of the paramount considerations, that is really driving our encampment policy," said Coun. John-Paul Danko, noting the litigation is "severely restricting" the city's ability to enforce bylaws relating to encampments.
Other municipalities are also "held hostage" to such legal challenges, added Danko, who in recent months has emphasized the impact of the legal case mounted by "encampment supporters."
That's "Trumpian politics," responded Wade Poziomka, one of the lawyers behind the litigation, which dates to 2021 but was later amended and refiled. "It's fake news and mischaracterizing what's actually happening."
He and his colleagues have offered to settle with the city, but to no avail, and the issue shouldn't have to be hashed out in court in the first place, Poziomka said.
"We should be recognizing that there's a portion of our population that's vulnerable, that needs help, and we should be figuring out how do we do that in a way that makes sense for everybody."
The litigation challenges the constitutionality of city bylaws that bar tents in parks and other public spaces and argues enforcement violates the applicants' rights, notably amid a shortage of adequate shelter spaces to meet their needs.
"Of course, we're not encampment supporters. Nobody is. I don't know anyone on our team that supports encampments, but we support people having a right to exist," Poziomka said.
'More about politics'
Ford's remarks this week about the notwithstanding clause, which allows for legislation that quashes certain rights, are "highly problematic," said Poziomka, calling the Charter mechanism a tool of "last resort" that needs "safeguards built around it."
Horwath, meanwhile, said Friday she won't sign a letter several mayors have penned asking the province to pass tougher laws, using the Charter mechanism if necessary. The province has "every ability" to do that, without the urging of municipal leaders, she said.
"So this is more about politics, I think, than it is about actual solving of problems. And I'm here to solve problems."
Instead, the province should focus on affordable and supportive housing, mental health supports, addiction services, high rents and social-assistance rates - all of which are driving a homelessness crisis municipalities can't solve on their own, Horwath said. "I don't believe the focus should be taking away people's rights. I think the focus should be how do we fix a broken system."
Beds, shelters
a temporary boost
Of roughly 1,600 people who are homeless in Hamilton, 300 are known to live outside, a fluid contingent that has been on the rise but can fluctuate according to the season.
As municipalities call on upper levels of government for more support to respond to the overlapping issues, advocates have filed legal challenges vying for the rights of those living outside who face being uprooted from tent sites.
Last year, in an oft-cited decision, an Ontario justice ruled that evicting people from an encampment in Kitchener would violate their rights.
That decision was based on "complex economic, personal and social circumstances, including the shortage of accessible shelter spaces" in the area, Justice Michael Valente wrote.
In September, Hamilton council backed an $18.3-million plan to create 192 more shelter and transitional beds and 40 "micro shelters" for up to 80 people with "wraparound" services at a city-owned site off Barton Street West.
That temporary boost is meant to serve as a stopgap to get people indoors in time for winter as the city looks to senior levels of government to shore up more affordable and supportive housing.
It will help Hamilton's most vulnerable and address tents in parks while not losing sight of the legal landscape, Horwath said after councillors backed the strategy.
"This is a pathway to get us there in a way that doesn't jeopardize any of our other obligations in terms of current case law and court situations that we're facing."
It's too soon to know how the plan for 272 more temporary spaces will influence the court case, Poziomka said. "But I think looking at options and solutions is obviously in everybody's interest."
The legal battle, which was moved from an October court date to December, hasn't proceeded in a straight line, with side skirmishes over witnesses and most recently, the inclusion of the Ontario Human Rights Commission as an intervener, a matter that's still to be decided.
City-wide tent ban eyed
Council, meanwhile, has made tweaks to an increasingly publicly scrutinized encampment protocol that allows for clusters of up to five tents in parks and other outdoor spaces subject to certain rules and spatial limitations.
Recent changes include exemptions that outlaw encampments in specific lower-city parks with the outdoor Barton site on the horizon and potential spillover effect of the extra indoor beds on local green spaces taken into account.
But the tent prohibition should apply to parks across Hamilton once the 272 additional spaces are available, said Coun. Esther Pauls, who plans to introduce a motion to that effect Wednesday.
"Let's put an end to it. We have enough beds. No more parks. This is the perfect time," said Pauls, who noted she fields complaints from residents about tents in various parks in Ward 7 on the Mountain.
"If we have a law that says downtown you can (ban tents), up the Mountain, you can't, people are going to revolt, whether there's 20 tents, or four or five tents."
Danko has urged caution about tinkering too much with the protocol with the court case still on the docket, noting an unfavourable decision could present "pretty severe ramifications" for the city.
Nonetheless, a city-wide parks ban is "in line" with council direction for staff to report back in January on the potential "sunsetting" of the encampment protocol as the additional shelter spaces come online, he pointed out.
But Coun. Cameron Kroetsch contended Pauls' upcoming motion is ill-advised with the outcome of the city's plan to add the temporary beds still to be determined.
"That seems very reactive and it's not going to lead us down a good path," the downtown-and-area councillor said.
The city must have a "balanced approach" to encampments that's rooted in human rights and not solely reliant on enforcement with some people not staying in shelters, he said.
"There will always be a need for us to have a protocol to address those folks who are living outside."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: People have pitched tents on a Barton Street West property where the city plans to locate 40 "micro shelters" for up to 80 people in time for winter.Mayor Andrea Horwath says she won't join other Ontario mayors in calling for the provincial government to use the "notwithstanding clause" to help municipalities deal with encampments.Cathie Coward the hamilton Spectator file photothe Spectator file photo

Full text: Micro shelters no solution
Re: 'Micro shelters' on their way to Barton West site, Oct. 31
So the tiny shelters (now renamed "micro shelters") are finally on their way to Hamilton. Does anyone believe these are going to rid our city of its homeless problem? The "do-gooders" can dream, but for sure these "micro shelters" will soon be a dumping ground for all the mess that existed around the illegal tents.
And the council members who state there will be a "protocol" for use and behaviour in the area should be reminded that you currently have a protocol for the encampments you chose to ignore. Point in case, the terrible state you have allowed our Peace Park to become, by allowing it to exist illegally, as your protocol states there be no encampment within 100 metres of a school. I have been stating that to city councillors and staff for two years. And they are going to manage the "micro shelter" site? Not a chance.
Terry Butlin, Hamilton
Concert trip too stressful
I left my home in Burlington at 6:30 p.m. for a 21-minute drive to FirstOntario Concert Hall in Hamilton Saturday night. It took me 17 minutes to get to the Main Street exit. Then it took me 30 minutes to even approach Bay Street.
Being a woman on her own in downtown Hamilton, I had intended to park in the parking garage with all the other concert goers, as there is safety in numbers. I realized that would put me in jeopardy of missing the first 20 minutes of the concert so I turned right and parked in an exposed lot with three tents set up around the perimeter.
The entire concert I was distracted by worry about my safety when I returned to my vehicle.
Between paying for the ticket, the stress of all the traffic and my safety of being alone in downtown Hamilton after dark, I will think long and hard about attending another concert in Hamilton.
Elizabeth Lucas, Burlington
Donate rebate where needed
So even the wealthiest Ontarians will receive a $200 "gift" from Premier Doug Ford in the new year. Let's empower the people to do the right thing. If all who are able to donate those dollars could support affordable housing and health-care initiatives, think of the difference we could make. You're welcome, premier.
Connie Smith, Ancaster
Funds needed for public good
As much as its sounds like finding lost money, bear in mind that these are your tax dollars. At the cost of $3 billion. Doug Ford is brazenly trying to buy your vote. Funds that could be allocated to health care, provincial and municipal infrastructure, working toward providing better shelter for the marginalized population and a plethora of other needs.
We need to step outside ourselves and become part of a cause that is much larger than oneself, one that brings a greater good to a greater community. Beware the Trojan horse.
Ross Prince, Hamilton
Use rebate to fund opposition
The best use I can think of for the $3-billion ("vote for me") gift that Premier Ford is giving Ontarians is to donate all or part of the cheque to the Liberal, New Democrat or Green party in the name of the Progressive Conservative Party.
Carl Easton, Hamilton
Rebate funded by health cuts?
Re: Changes to home-care equipment contracts
halted during crisis, Oct. 30
I am deeply disturbed by this government's inexcusable handling of the Ontario Health atHome supply procurement changes. Not being able to get timely and appropriate supplies is putting people's lives at risk. Before September this was not an issue. The changes you put in place have directly led to this crisis. Platitudes like saying you are going to fix this came only after over a month of problems became public in the press, and these serious problems still exist.
Are the cost savings these changes will generate going toward funding the $200 "rebate" to citizens including the highest income earners? Ontarians need and deserve an independent review of this gross mismanagement.
Margaret Tremblay, Dundas
Not a fan of leaving leaves
Re: A reminder to leave the leaves, Oct. 23
A carpet of dead leaves becomes a sodden mat that encourages mould and disease of lawn grasses. It also provides cover for rodents to travel on their trails beneath it.
If possible, mow the dry leaves into small pieces and/or rake and bag them for disposal as many do. Their value as fertilizer is vastly overrated. Lastly, don't tell others how to manage their lawn unless asked.
Gord Baker, Carlisle

Document 227 of 500

'The hostages are a footnote:' Canadian-Israeli fights to see her parents' bodies returned from Hamas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hostages-are-footnote-canadian-israeli-fights-see/docview/3123392204/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+hostages+are+a+footnote%3A%27+Canadian-Israeli+fights+to+see+her+parents%27+bodies+returned+from+Hamas&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Partway through an interview Friday, Iris Weinstein Haggai - a Canadian-Israeli who grew up on a kibbutz devastated in last year's Oct. 7 assaults - stopped to review a statement that would soon appear on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's social media page.
She frowned briefly, reading on her lawyer's phone, then smiled and shook her head. "Amazing," she said. "Amazing."
The prime minister had done what she had asked of him, one day earlier, in a private meeting on Parliament Hill. He had said her mother's name.
Judi Weinstein Haggai.
"I can't say he didn't deliver with this," Iris told the Star. "So ... that's a start."
For more than a year, Iris and her family have anguished over the absence of her parents, Judi and Gadi. Weeks after the Oct. 7 attacks, Iris said the family learned through Israeli and American intelligence that her parents were among those that Hamas assailants had killed. The organization that Canada considers a terrorist group is still believed to be holding their bodies in the war-ravaged Gaza Strip.
Judi, Iris's mother, was born in the United States but grew up in Toronto and was a Canadian citizen. This fact, to Iris, means that a Canadian remains hostage to Hamas, a fact that has been "forgotten" as the conflict grows. More than 43,000 Palestinians have been killed in Israel's offensive in the Gaza Strip since the Oct. 7 massacre, according to local health officials in the occupied territory, which has included reports of deadly airstrikes on refugee camps, hospitals and schools. With Hezbollah firing rockets into northern Israel from Lebanon, Israel also invaded and launched airstrikes against that country, where officials say 2,800 people have been killed since last October.
Hamas assailants killed more than 1,200 people in their Oct. 7 attacks on Israel, an assault that has been described as the largest attack on Jews since the Holocaust. At the same time, Hamas took more than 250 people hostage, and Israeli media have reported that 97 of them are believed to remain in Gaza, including the bodies of at least 34 who have been confirmed dead.
Iris said she wants the conflict to end, but that the plight of hostages and the bodies of those kidnapped should be addressed separately. Too often, she argued, officials in Canada and elsewhere demand the release of hostages as part of broader statements calling for a ceasefire or demanding Israel allow more aid to flow into the Gaza Strip whose borders it controls.
"The hostages are a footnote," she said. "It's always tied to something."
She was pleased to see the prime minister take up her cause and call for the release of the Oct. 7 hostages in a post on X Friday afternoon that included direct reference to her mother's killing. Iris said she wants the federal government to use Canada's moral heft in the international community to push harder for the unconditional release of the hostages, both dead and alive, and to champion that cause at every opportunity.
"This is a person that shouldn't be there," Iris said of her mother, "just as much as a poor four-year-old doesn't need to be in the middle of a building falling down on him in Gaza."
In her visit to Ottawa this week, Iris met with Trudeau and Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, as well as Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and the NDP's Jagmeet Singh, she said. Iris said she pressed Canadian politicians to advocate for the Oct. 7 hostages, including by calling for the International Committee of the Red Cross to finally gain access to those Hamas kidnapped last year.
Joly's spokesperson, James Fitz-Morris, told the Star that the minister committed to Iris that she would continue raising the need to return Judi Weinstein Haggai's remains.
It's part of her effort to bring attention to the Oct. 7 hostages that has seen her visit Washington, D.C. and engage with American officials from her home in Singapore, where she now lives with her husband and children.
Iris's efforts also extend to the Canadian courts, where she is involved in two legal cases that she said are aimed at seeking "accountability" for the attacks against Israel. One is a judicial challenge of Canada's decision last March to reinstate funding for the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees (UNRWA), which the Israeli parliament voted to ban this week after the country accused 12 of its staff members - which the agency says includes about 30,000 people in the region - of involvement in the Oct. 7 attacks. UNRWA has denied knowingly aiding any armed groups and says it moves quickly to purge any suspected militants from its staff.
Iris is one of two plaintiffs involved in a lawsuit filed in Ontario Superior Court on last month's anniversary of the Oct. 7 attacks, seeking $250 million in damages from Hamas and a host of other Palestinian groups they accuse of involvement in the assault. The suit also targets the governments of Iran and Syria, which are designated state sponsors of terror under the federal Justice for Victims of Terrorism Act, a law that is meant to help people seek damages from countries that support terrorist violence.
The legal cases are occurring alongside others that include a challenge seeking to prevent Canadian military exports to Israel, as well as an effort to pressure Ottawa to try and grant visas under a special program to the family members of Canadians who are stuck in war-torn Gaza.
For Iris, she is trying to channel the trauma she has endured into action, and to make sure more Canadians know that one of their co-citizens, even in death, is believed to be in the hands of her captors and killers all these months later.
"Canada needs to join me in this fight," she said. "Everyone should know my mom's name."
With files from Star wire services
CAPTION: In this undated photo released by Iris Weinstein Haggai, Judi Weinstein Haggai and Gadi Hagi hold each other as they pose for a photo in Toronto.

Document 228 of 500

Doug Ford accused of 'double standard' over push for Amazon IT services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-accused-double-standard-over-push/docview/3123392203/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+accused+of+%27double+standard%27+over+push+for+Amazon+IT+services&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A week after Premier Doug Ford ordered the LCBO to buy Ontario-made paper bags, his government is looking to U.S.-based giant Amazon for IT services, the Star has learned.
A request for proposals to supply software to the province's call and contact centres - such as ServiceOntario and the Family Responsibility Office - seeks vendors who use Amazon Web Services.
"Bidders must demonstrate ownership or authorization as (an) AWS reseller," says a 76-page document from the Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement that sets a Dec. 4 deadline for bids with five-year contracts to begin next April.
"The bidder must provide documentation validating their status as an AWS reseller. Additionally, the bidder must maintain this status throughout the term of agreement."
The request for bids is raising concerns Ontario-based software developers already holding such contracts are being frozen out and raising questions as to why Amazon Web Services was chosen, with no known bidding process giving other companies a shot.
"It's very disappointing to see an unfair process after the government has spent so much time about supporting Ontario businesses when they've pre-determined this is going to an American multinational corporation," said an industry source speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations.
The ministry headed by Todd McCarthy did not immediately reply to a list of questions from the Star, including why the Amazon platform was selected, what it can provide that Ontario suppliers cannot, and whether existing Ontario suppliers were asked if they could match its services.
A spokesman for McCarthy said the ministry was not prepared to respond until Monday.
Supply Ontario, which is listed as a contact for potential bidders with queries, referred questions to the ministry.
Late last month, Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy made it clear they wanted the LCBO to buy bags from suppliers in Ontario after executives signed a $10-million contract with a Quebec company.
The premier, who has not ruled out an early election next year instead of waiting until the scheduled date in June 2026, wrote a letter to LCBO chair Carmine Nigro on the issue. Ford's office released it to the media.
"We've been very clear we want them to use Ontario companies," said Bethlenfalvy. "I think Ontario companies are the best around."
Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), an accountant, said the Amazon procurement reveals an apparent "double standard" in the Progressive Conservative government.
"It might not be unusual for a request for bids to have specific technical requirements. That being said, it is important for Ontario companies to bid on Ontario contracts because it gives them credibility in the international marketplace," she added.
"I've talked to a lot of Ontario companies, who say 'the first thing we get asked is ... does your own government buy from you?' And when you say no, that's a lost opportunity."
McCarthy's ministry came under fire earlier this year for moving nine ServiceOntario centres into Staples stores to offer longer hours, easy parking and save $1 million over three years.
But critics said the move ended contracts with independent "mom and pop" private operators in the centres in favour of an American-based retail chain. The deal is under review by the province's independent Financial Accountability Office.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford makes an announcement at a gas station saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales, in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Subject: Bids; Suppliers

Document 229 of 500

What time do we 'fall back' to end daylight saving time in Canada this weekend?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-time-do-we-fall-back-end-daylight-saving/docview/3123392202/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+time+do+we+%27fall+back%27+to+end+daylight+saving+time+in+Canada+this+weekend%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's officially been the fall season for weeks, but when do we turn the clocks back to end daylight saving time?
Daylight saving time ends on Sunday, Nov. 3, in Canada. At 2 a.m., we roll back the clocks by one hour and gain an extra bit of sleep.
POLL: Should Ontario keep or scrap daylight saving time in spring and fall?
The practice can also be called "fall back" as clocks fall back by an hour, whereas in the spring we "spring forward" on time.
The general idea of adjusting time twice a year is to allow for better use of natural light, both in winter and summer. However, there's been a lot of chatter over the years about scrapping the practice.
Ontario passed a law back in 2020 to stop observing daylight saving time - however, nothing has happened with that because the Ontario plan is dependent on Quebec and New York state also scrapping the practice.
A petition on change.org started by Irene Shone, which has received more than 87,000 signatures as of this writing, is asking the Canadian government, as well as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario Premier Doug Ford and several government ministers, to stop the daylight saving time practice and to instead have a permanent standard time.
There are areas of Canada that don't observe daylight saving time, including Saskatchewan and Yukon, along with a few communities in several provinces, including a few areas of northwestern Ontario.
According to timeanddate.com, fewer than 40 per cent of the world's countries use daylight saving time. The publication points out that in North America, eight countries observe DST, 11 stopped and 20 never have.
Also, not all countries make the time change on the same dates. Canada's spring-forward and fall-back dates coincide with those of the United States.
History of daylight saving time
Canada, as a nation, first began using daylight saving time in 1918 during the First World War. Canada, the United States and much of Europe picked up the practice shortly after the Germans started it.
The German government needed ways to conserve energy for battle and needed its citizens to reduce the use of electric light and conserve fossil fuels, according to University Canada West. The idea was that by pushing clocks forward by an hour in the spring, it gave people more daylight hours during working hours and then people would also use less energy to light their homes.
In Canada, the idea was that daylight saving time could help increase production during the war.
After the First World War, Canada dropped the practice, but then brought it back during the Second World War.
While Canada as a nation started utilizing daylight saving time in 1918, the first instance of it happening in Canada is said to have happened a decade earlier.
According to timeanddate.com, Port Arthur, now Thunder Bay, turned its clocks forward by an hour on July 1, 1908, and turned it back on Sept. 1, 1908, but the community did not continue the practice in successive years.
CAPTION: Clocks will fall back an hour in November.

Subject: Daylight saving time; World War I

Location: United States--US; Canada

Title: What time do we 'fall back' to end daylight saving time in Canada this weekend?

Document 230 of 500

Doug Ford's $200 cheques are yesterday's solution to today's worries about inflation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-200-cheques-are-yesterdays-solution/docview/3123392114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+cheques+are+yesterday%27s+solution+to+today%27s+worries+about+inflation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prices are high. An election is nigh.
Affordability is top of mind.
Which is why 15 million Ontarians are getting an anti-inflation consolation prize: $200 cheques in the mail soon.
Thank you, Doug Ford.
What better solution for inflation and affordability both?
Except that Ford's pre-election generosity and grandiosity may be the wrong prescription for the wrong problem.
What if our continuing obsession with inflation is a holdover from the past? Is Ford fighting yesterday's war with outdated cheques to win next year's vote?
It's a question of public perception and political calculation versus economic reality.
Public opinion polls aren't lying when they identify affordability as the top issue for voters. People assuredly believe prices are too high.
Which is why Ford is so shamelessly riding the affordability bandwagon. So, too, is federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who coined the slogan "Justinflation" as a way to pin blame for rising prices on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau alone.
No one questions that inflation surged post-pandemic, thanks mostly to supply chain disruptions caused by COVID shutdowns, plus massive government stimulus and a record influx of temporary workers (who stoked demand for housing). Inflation peaked at 8.1 per cent in mid-2022, and Canadians felt the pinch in food and housing prices.
But that was then. This is now.
The inflation rate has dropped below three per cent so far this year, according to Statistics Canada. In fact, inflation fell to 1.6 per cent in September - a historically low figure, and well below the two per cent target set by the Bank of Canada.
Which is why the central bank has revised its key lending rate steadily downward this year, and did so again last week.
So if that rock bottom inflation rate is evidence enough for the bank to conclude that inflation is increasingly under control, why do ordinary Canadians still fret about affordability? And if central bankers can get inflation under control, why can't politicians make life feel more affordable?
First, homebuyers will always feel nostalgia for the good old days when times were bad and mortgage rates reached record lows in mid-pandemic. Back then, the Bank of Canada resorted to emergency stimulus measures that dropped lending rates to near-zero, but those rates are not coming back - nor should they, barring another massive crisis. Get used to it.
Second, today's remarkably low inflation rate - which has reassured even our stickler bankers - is small comfort for ordinary consumers. People may understand that the rate of increase has since slowed, but they also know that the rapid increases of recent years are now baked in - so prices absolutely feel higher than they once were.
Put another way, it's small consolation if the inflation rate has ebbed, belatedly, if today's prices are stuck at cumulatively higher levels. StatCan figures show that a basket of goods that cost $137 in September 2020 would cost $161 in September 2024 - an increase of 17.5 per cent over the past four years.
Admittedly, that was long ago. But people still recall pre-pandemic prices as if they were yesterday - and that's the benchmark that comes to mind when they see today's higher price tags.
People aren't so much fussed about the latest 1.6 per cent inflation rate; they're fuming that prices are at least 10 times higher - 17.5 per cent - than they once were.
So are we stuck with the cumulative increases of recent years?
One serious remedy is to raise wages for working people to make up for lost ground, and wages did indeed go up post-pandemic - albeit not for everyone, and the increases didn't always keep up. But wage hikes can only get you so far before you fall into an inflationary cycle of higher pay causing higher prices.
Another perception problem that economists have long observed is that workers feel that they've "earned" a pay hike by dint of hard work. They don't look upon a wage increase as compensation for inflation.
Workers understandably want to get ahead, not see their higher pay cheque eaten up by rising food prices. They resent inflation - even if that's what's driving their catch-up wage increase - because it erodes their hard-won purchasing power.
Rather than increasing wages, what about rolling back price hikes? Be careful what you wish for.
Economists refer to price decrease as deflation - the evil twin of inflation - which is almost always bad news. Historically, a deflationary economy goes hand in hand with recession and depression.
Producers respond to price decreases by reducing their payroll costs - either by cutting wages or laying off workers, which in turn lowers aggregate consumer demand. Also, consumers and businesses may delay new purchases if they know prices will be lower tomorrow than they are today - further depressing demand.
All that said, questions of affordability are not imaginary. But the solutions are illusory.
People are feeling the pinch. But don't be fooled by Ford's political pitch, because $200 ain't what it used to be.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Novocol Pharma in Cambridge, Ont. on Nov. 1, 2024.

Subject: Anti inflation; Consumers; Inflation rates; Pandemics; Housing prices; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Anti inflation Consumers Inflation rates Housing prices Wages & salaries

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

Title: Doug Ford's $200 cheques are yesterday's solution to today's worries about inflation

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 1, 2024

Document 231 of 500

Doug Ford wanted cities to ask him to use the notwithstanding clause to end encampments. Twelve mayors just said do it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wanted-cities-ask-him-use/docview/3123392112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wanted+cities+to+ask+him+to+use+the+notwithstanding+clause+to+end+encampments.+Twelve+mayors+just+said+do+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayors of 12 of Ontario's largest cities - including Brampton, Oshawa, Pickering and Oakville - say they want the province to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments, homelessness and addictions and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts won't be overturned by the courts.
Earlier this week, Premier Doug Ford said he wanted Ontario's big city mayors, a group of 29 municipal leaders, to show "backbone" and support using the notwithstanding clause by putting it in writing "if they really want the homeless situation to improve."
In response, the leaders of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury and Windsor sent a letter to Ford on Thursday asking for the province to "become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments" and "strengthen the existing system of mandatory community-based and residential mental health care and to expand service to treat those who have severe and debilitating addictions," among other changes.
"We request that your government consider the (measures) ... and where necessary use the notwithstanding clause to ensure these measures are implemented in a timely and effective way."
Grace Lee, a spokesperson for the premier, said in response to the letter that "we have been clear that we will explore every legal tool available to the province to clear encampments and restore safety to public spaces. While cities remain at the forefront of issues relating to municipally owned lands, we are examining which additional tools the province can provide to help municipalities effectively manage these ongoing challenges."
Last year in Waterloo, a judge ruled that it's a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict those in encampments if there are no shelter spaces available for them. A similar ruling was also handed down in Kingston.
The charter's notwithstanding clause allows a government to pass legislation that overrides certain rights. Ontario's big city mayors have previously written to Ford for help, but there was no consensus among the entire group to support using the clause.
Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, chair of the mayors' group, wants to see "one point person, a specific minister or ministry, in charge of solving this" and a province-wide plan including more supports.
She said the "issue becomes, if you are using the notwithstanding clause to close down encampments, but people have nowhere to go, we're no farther ahead."
Ford frequently notes that he receives "endless calls about people camping out in the parks, needles being all over the park" and families feeling unsafe.
But opposition parties said the solution to encampments is housing that vulnerable people can afford, not taking such a major step as invoking the notwithstanding clause.
"If the housing isn't there ... it just ends up moving the encampment, right? You're not actually solving the problem," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles.
"I would hope that the government would actually be working with municipalities to build the housing we really need."
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician who has worked with several homeless shelters, called on the Ford government to improve "affordable, wraparound and supportive housing" to solve the problem.
"As long as this government continues to fail on that, municipalities will feel as though they have no choice but to ask for things like the notwithstanding clause. It is not necessary."
The request from the mayors shows the "complete failure of the Ford government to build deeply affordable non-profit, co-operative and supportive housing," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"If they're going to take the extreme measures of taking constitutional rights away from people experiencing homelessness, where are those people going to go?" he said.
"There are no homes for them to go to, because the Ford government has failed to build homes that they can afford to live in."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford this week suggested the homelessness crisis had grown so extreme it was time to override the Constitution.

Subject: Charter of Rights-Canada; Mayors; Homeless people; Municipalities

Doug Ford quietly abandons plan to control police board appointments by municipalities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-quietly-abandons-plan-control-police/docview/3123392105/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+quietly+abandons+plan+to+control+police+board+appointments+by+municipalities&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has quietly quashed a controversial plan that would have "increased provincial control and influence" over police service board appointments by municipalities, the Star has learned.
Against the backdrop of a possible spring election - one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - sources say Ford has quashed a scheme that would have given Queen's Park far more power in the oversight of municipal policing.
Solicitor General Michael Kerzner's ministry had been preparing amendments to the Community Safety and Policing Act, according to a draft of confidential cabinet briefing documents obtained by the Star and shared with ministers last week.
"With regards to the community representative appointment proposal, the intended outcomes are increased provincial control in the municipal appointment of community representatives to specified police service boards by requiring these appointments to happen from a short list of candidates provided by the Solicitor General," says the internal document.
"SOLGEN notes a strong negative reaction is anticipated from the municipal sector and stakeholders, given the proposal would reduce municipal flexibility and autonomy for appointments," it continues.
Kerzner's office said Friday that the draft proposal was "not accurate."
Progressive Conservative sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, said Ford was mindful of the potential political headaches such changes could cause in municipalities across Ontario.
Tory MPPs also privately warned the premier this week that the plan would be problematic in their communities.
Despite the upheaval the proposed amendments could have triggered, they had been okayed by cabinet committees in July and September and were moving quickly through the legislative process.
They were to have been included in a bill to be tabled next week and proclaimed into law by April 1.
Under the aborted plan, Kerzner would have given municipalities an approved "short list" of candidates to serve as community representatives on police service boards.
While there are already provincial appointees to the oversight bodies, cities select their own candidates for the panels.
Of the seven members of the Toronto Police Service Board, for example, four were appointed by city council while three, including chair Ann Morgan, were chosen by the province.
Mayor Olivia Chow had a direct hand in three of the current city appointments: Coun. Amber Morley (Ward 3, Etobicoke-Lakeshore), Coun. Shelley Carroll (Ward 17, Don Valley North) and Chris Brillinger, executive director of Family Services Toronto.
The fourth, Coun. Lily Cheng (Ward 18, Willowdale), joined the board two years ago when John Tory was mayor; her term expires on Dec. 31.
It is unclear whether elected city councillors would have automatically been included in any approved list from the solicitor general.
"SOLGEN notes that municipalities would retain the requirement to appoint a community representative, but would need to choose from a short list if the regulations end up requiring this," the document says.
"Policing stakeholders, such as the Ontario Association of Police Service Boards, are expected to have a negative reaction related to board efficiency and appointment delays," it acknowledges.
But ministry bureaucrats predicted front-line police officers might have embraced the changes.
"These amendments are expected to have limited to positive reactions from policing stakeholders and the public, since an element of the proposal for police assistance provides that chiefs of police, rather than police service boards, may request temporary assistance" for additional resources, the documents add.
"There may be some concern from police service boards regarding the transfer of responsibility. However, it is notable that police service boards will be made aware of the request for temporary assistance."
The abandoned push comes as Ford's Tories, who lead in public opinion polls, are pondering an early election in March or April.
CAPTION: Solicitor General Michael Kerzner, right, and Premier Doug Ford appearat a newsconference at the Toronto Police College in Etobicoke on April 25, 2023.

Title: Doug Ford quietly abandons plan to control police board appointments by municipalities
