Document 245 of 500

Doug Ford's $200 cheques are a cynical attempt to buy your vote with borrowed money

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-200-cheques-are-cynical-attempt-buy/docview/3122729131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+cheques+are+a+cynical+attempt+to+buy+your+vote+with+borrowed+money&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Doug Ford, everyone's a winner.
What's not to like about a $200 cheque from Ford's Tories ahead of the next election?
That's the good news. Now for the bad news:
Everyone's a loser.
This $200 giveaway isn't a gift from the gods, or from the premier personally. It comes at a cost of $3 billion to the provincial treasury - which is to say, you and me, personally and individually.
Think of it as a phoney lottery without the money to back it up, and no worthy winnings to give back. Just 15 million pointless prizes for voters and suckers whose support Ford is buying with their own money, just in time for an early election.
More precisely, it's all borrowed money. Ford's Progressive Conservative government remains deep in debt and deficit: The latest budgetary projection released Wednesday shows $6.6 billion in red ink, on top of the accumulated debt of $429 billion.
It's only money.
On the one hand, it's a handout for virtually every adult and child in Ontario. On the other hand, by cutting a cheque for everyone, Ford is cutting back on the helping hand available for anyone in need - health-care services, educational investments and help for homeless people.
There's not enough money for you. Yet here's some money for you.
To understand the inanity and insanity of this gesture, consider the bizarre timing - beyond the electoral calendar and political calculus. Focus on the fiscal hocus-pocus:
This pre-election goody is the main pillar of this year's fall economic statement (FES), an annual production of the mighty Ministry of Finance. The FES is supposed to be a serious deal - a sober accounting of the province's balance sheet at the mid-point of the fiscal year, in between budgets.
Now, Ford's Tories have given new meaning - if not a new name - to the FES: Ford Electioneering on Steroids.
Vote-buying only works if customers are buying what you're selling. Happily for the premier, the voting public has a built-in bias that credits Conservatives with automatic economic acumen.
Perhaps it helps that a folksy Ford is awfully good at persuading people that he feels their pain. In pre-election mode, the premier is all about affability and affordability, invoking pocketbook politics at every turn.
He trash talks taxes, laments the federal carbon levy and rages over road tolls. It worked in 2022 when he won re-election by slashing tolls and taxes, and cutting cheques for rebated licence fees, so why not more of the same to win again in 2025?
It's not as if he hasn't tried this before. As a city councillor, he famously handed out a wad of crisp $20 bills to voters at a Toronto Community Housing building.
The difference, of course, is that Ford was spending his own money back then. Now, as premier, he's gifting us with our own borrowed money to buy our own votes.
But he's not doing it alone.
His accomplice is Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, a seemingly sensible Bay Street veteran who had a life before politics. Back then, he proudly headed Dominion Bond Rating Service, handing out credit rating downgrades to profligate politicians.
Now, he's joined the club. Rather than acting as a prudent steward of the province's finances, Bethlenfalvy has repurposed himself into a partisan enabler of Ontario's populist premier.
On Bethlenfalvy's watch, buried in this year's mini-budget, is yet another unjustifiable and unsustainable giveaway: more than $7 billion in annual subsidies from the treasury to perpetuate the fiction that electricity rates are being kept low. This uncontrolled transfer of wealth, from hapless taxpayers to hydro ratepayers, has only gotten worse since Ford took power.
But there are more Bethlenfalvy blunders that merit scrutiny. In one of his first acts as president of the Treasury Board, before taking over at Finance, he imposed a pay restraint law on public servants that never made any sense, because there was no fiscal crisis to justify it. Predictably, the courts overruled him and ordered the government to make up lost pay amounting to billions of dollars for workers across the public sector.
All that said, the FES talks up "prudence in 2024-25," as if words matter more than numbers. Yet it sugar-coats Ontario's economic stasis by noting, ever so gingerly, that growth "is projected to ease" from 1.4 per cent in 2023 to 0.9 per cent this year - because "ease" is easier said than "decline."
Oh, and unemployment? It keeps climbing under this government - from 5.6 per cent when Ford took over from the last Liberal government in 2018 to a projected 6.9 per cent this year (revised upward from the 6.7 per cent Bethlenfalvy predicted in his budget last March, it peaked at 7.1 per cent in August, according to Statistics Canada).
"The news is good!" Bethlenfalvy boasted Wednesday, as if saying so makes it so. But the bad news is that cutting $200 cheques is an empty gesture that achieves nothing beyond buying votes.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks during a news conference at the Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.

Subject: Electric rates; Rating services; Bond ratings; Voters; Politics; Homeless people

Changes to home-care equipment contracts halted during crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/changes-home-care-equipment-contracts-halted/docview/3122180029/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Changes+to+home-care+equipment+contracts+halted+during+crisis&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Upcoming changes to how home-care equipment is provided have been put on hold as the province grapples with a crisis that has left vulnerable patients without vital medical supplies.
New contracts were supposed to start next week, altering how equipment such as hospital beds, walkers and wheelchairs are provided to home-care patients.
The changes have now been postponed for an unknown amount of time as patients continue to face desperate shortages since new contracts for medical supplies and infusion medicine started Sept. 24.
"There is no plan to change equipment contracts next week," Ontario Health atHome said in a statement.
The provincial agency that co-ordinates in-home and community-based care for over 650,000 Ontario patients a year refused to answer questions about the contracts, including when they will be implemented and what companies were awarded the business.
Ontario Health atHome won't say whether the changes to home-care equipment are similar to medical supplies and infusion medicine, for which sources say a smaller number of companies now procure provisions and fulfil orders for larger parts of the province. In addition, sources say the items and quantities available to order have changed.
The transition in September has been described as utter chaos by patients, home-care workers and doctors from across Ontario, who describe almost instantly having no supplies for wound care, changing catheters or draining fluid from around a cancer patient's lungs. There were long delays to getting antibiotics, sedation and pain medication. Doctors said kits for medical assistance in dying were missing critical parts.
"It's maddening for nurses," said Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO) "They go everyday to work wanting to do good care but they can't do it without supplies. It's stress no one needs."
Dr. Adil Shamji, the Liberal health critic and MPP for Don Valley East, has asked Ontario's auditor general to investigate Ontario Heath atHome, and the major issues surrounding the procurement and distribution of home-care supplies.
"We need to know how this happened, why this happened, and who needs to be held responsible," Shamji said in a statement.
Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday that his government is "fixing the problem."
"I'm holding people accountable on this one," Ford said. "We're going to make sure that never happens again."
Ford was asked about the home-care crisis after announcing $200 rebate cheques to about 15 million Ontarians.
"Forget this $200," Grinspun said, calling the rebate cheques a distraction. "I want to hear, 'We will fix home care for everybody' ... They need to figure this out."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario, said nurses can't provide good care without supplies.Ontario Premier Doug Fordsaid Tuesday that his government is "fixing the problem" with home care supplies.

Document 247 of 500

Liberal dissenters need to show some backbone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-dissenters-need-show-some-backbone/docview/3122179962/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+dissenters+need+to+show+some+backbone&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There has been speculation in the media recently about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau facing a caucus revolt.
His polling numbers are a disaster and if a federal election were held today, the odds are strong Pierre Poilievre and the Conservative Party of Canada would win a massive majority.
Some members of the Liberal caucus as well as many Canadians feel Trudeau should step down and give the Liberals a chance to fight the next election with a new leader.
When Trudeau met with his caucus on Oct. 23, there were reports the members would order him to step down. Apparently there was a letter demanding this, signed by 24 Liberal members of Parliament.
It appears, though, the demand "fizzled."
There are plenty of unnamed Liberals expressing concern over Trudeau's leadership. But they don't want to go public. This refusal by the Liberal caucus to remove a flawed leader and go public with their concerns is a prime example of a caucus lacking in intestinal fortitude.
The first sign of this was their refusal to apply the provisions of the 2015 Reform Act, written by Conservative MP Michael Chong. This act of legislation was written to restore power to the party caucuses after decades of power being centralized under the party leaders.
The Reform Act created four sets of provisions relating to: the expulsion/readmission of caucus members; the election and removal of the caucus chair; the removal of the party leader; and the election of an interim leader.
At the first sitting of the House after an election, members of each party vote on whether any or all of these should apply to their caucus. The approval of the provisions by the caucuses is valid until Parliament dissolves. After the following election another vote is held.
The Liberal caucus has never voted in favour of giving themselves the powers available to them under the Reform Act.
Why would a responsible caucus take deliberate steps to strip their ability to remove an unpopular leader? Are Liberal MPs simply scared of "crossing" Trudeau?
I remind readers the Conservative caucus voted to have this ability and in fact invoked the Reform Act to remove Erin O'Toole as their leader in 2022.
Conservatives showed great courage in the spring of 2001 when 12 Canadian Alliance MPs led by Chuck Strahl left the Alliance to sit as an independent caucus, over the refusal of unpopular party leader Stockwell Day to resign.
No Liberals have taken such action.
Individual Canadian politicians have also shown far more courage than has the current Liberal caucus.
In 1988, Jim Walding, a New Democratic Party member of the Manitoba legislature, who was known to challenge his own party and government, voted against his own party's budget, thereby defeating the NDP government.
Even more dramatically, in February 1963, Doug Harkness, the Progressive Conservative national defence minister, publicly resigned from prime minister John Diefenbaker's cabinet over Diefenbaker's inept performance during the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, and Diefenbaker's refusal to accept nuclear warheads for the Bomarc missiles stationed in Canada.
Harkness was livid over Diefenbaker's "waffling" on the warhead issue as the missiles were purchased by Canada with the clear understanding they only operated with nuclear warheads. Harkness, a man of strong principle, left cabinet to sit as a back bencher. That took courage.
These are only a couple of examples of courage shown by past Canadian politicians. It is a shame the current Liberal caucus cannot be included here.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way to caucus on Parliament Hill on Oct. 23. Those Liberal MPs who want him to step aside before the next election need to speak out publicly, Craig Wallace says.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Subject: Leadership; Members of Parliament; Decades; Prime ministers

Document 248 of 500

Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-spending-nearly-half-ontarios-tax/docview/3122179781/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+spending+nearly+half+of+Ontario%27s+tax+windfall+on+%24200+%27rebate%27+cheques&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is spending nearly half of a $6.9-billion tax windfall on $200 pre-election "rebate" cheques.
Bolstered by higher than projected revenue from personal income tax, corporations tax and sales tax, Ford's Progressive Conservatives have earmarked $3 billion to send cheques to 15 million Ontario adults and children in the new year.
But Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement tabled Wednesday at Queen's Park said the Tories are still running a $6.6-billion deficit.
While that's lower than the surprise $9.8-billion shortfall for 2024-25 Bethlenfalvy forecast in his March 26 budget, it's far from the $200-million surplus he had projected last year.
With Ford expected to call a spring election - one year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote - Bethlenfalvy hinted to reporters that the books could be balanced as early as his March budget even though he is currently projecting a $1.5-billion deficit for 2025-26.
"I'm an optimist, I'm always hoping I can beat the numbers," he said.
In a partisan speech in the legislature, where he twice attacked "the previous Liberal government" in power from 2003 until 2018, Bethlenfalvy painted a rosy picture of Ontario's finances.
"Today we are in a position to do more," the treasurer said, insisting "this government will balance the budget" in 2026-27.
Bethlenfalvy defended the Tories' decision to mail out cheques, first revealed by the Star on Oct. 16, instead of further reducing the deficit to a more manageable $3.6 billion for 2024-25. (He said the government has not calculated the administrative cost of printing and mailing millions of them.)
"With our new stronger fiscal position, we can now afford to provide real relief to families to help them in this time of challenge. We know that people aren't looking for some money back in time for next year's taxes," said the finance minister.
"They need real support, and they need it now. We are providing $200 per child under the age of 18 - meaning a family of five will receive $1,000," he said.
"I'm under no illusions that this will relieve all of the affordability pressures facing Ontario families, but it will help. It's real support and, most importantly, it allows the people of Ontario to choose how this money can best help them."
Questioning the wisdom of the one-time payments, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "people in Ontario are tired of gimmicks."
"Where are they not spending those dollars right now? Where people really need them," said Stiles, referring to a severe shortage of housing and family doctors and growing homeless encampments.
"People deserve a government that puts their tax dollars to work and spends (them) responsibly to make life easier. With Doug Ford, you're not getting what you paid for," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Bethlenfalvy's mini-budget fails to "address the issues that really matter to Ontario families."
"Instead of making real investments to keep emergency rooms open, helping the over 2.5 million people without a family doctor, addressing the urgent need for long-term financial relief, or making housing more affordable, Doug Ford introduced yet another failed fall economic statement that puts Doug Ford's rich friends first, and you and your family last," said Crombie.
"People are dying on wait-lists and Doug Ford is spending billions on booze, foreign spas and the Greenbelt scandal," she said, referring to the expansion of beer and wine sales, the Ontario Place redevelopment and the RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap debacle.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said there are better ways to spend $3 billion of public money for the greater good of all Ontarians.
"Two hundred dollars isn't going to buy them a home or pay the rent or boost somebody from a tent (encampment) to permanent supportive housing," said Schreiner, who plans to donate his $200 cheque to charity.
On the growing problem of homelessness, the organization representing 29 of Ontario's largest cities expressed disappointment that there was nothing new to address encampments, addictions and mental health issues.
"Municipalities have done everything we can, but we don't have the resources or expertise to take this on alone," Ontario Big City Mayors said in a statement.
Bethlenfalvy bristled at that, noting the province has already raised annual funding for the Homelessness Prevention Program by $200 million.
He said that was a 40 per cent hike "in one year upfront (that) continues for the next five years, which is about a $1 billion increase to to help municipalities as we work together on the challenges of homelessness."
The fall economic statement came against the backdrop of Ontario's debt skyrocketing to a projected $439 billion this year since Ford took office in 2018.
That's up $116 billion - or 36 per cent - from $323 billion left by the previous Liberal government six years ago.
However, interest on the provincial debt is expected to dip by $1.2 billion to $12.7 billion - more than the budget of every department except the Ministry of Health, the Ministry of Education and the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services.
The annual cost of servicing the debt, which is the largest of any subnational jurisdiction in the world, is anticipated to increase to $14.5 billion in two years.
"Ontario now has the lowest borrowing costs of any province in Canada, saving taxpayers $1.2 billion that we are now able to reinvest back into people," boasted Bethlenfalvy.
"In fact, our interest on debt relative to revenues is currently at its lowest level since the 1980s and our debt to GDP ratio, which is a measure of our fiscal health, remains at the best level it's been in a decade. This is very good news for Ontario taxpayers. It means that when the province needs to borrow, we can do it at a lower cost."
The costs of abiding by a court ruling that struck down the Bill 124 salary restraint legislation for public servants, which limited pay hikes to one per cent, has resulted in retroactive raises for workers totalling $6.7 billion.
CAPTION: Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy answers questions prior to delivering the 2024 budget in the Ontario Legislature on March 26, 2024.

Subject: Economic development; Homeless people; Budget deficits

Document 249 of 500

Nursing agencies are undermining the public health-care system

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nursing-agencies-are-undermining-public-health/docview/3122179734/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nursing+agencies+are+undermining+the+public+health-care+system&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In September, the Canadian Federation of Nurses Unions (CFNU) released the report, Opening the black box: Unpacking the use of nursing agencies in Canada.
The CFNU worked with Queen's University to conduct the mixed methods study led by Dr. Joan Almost. The study shows the impact agencies have on human resources planning, privatization of the public health-care system, retention and recruitment of nurses.
The report stated that spending on for-profit agency nurses is expected to surpass $1.5 billion in Canada in the current fiscal year. That's a sixfold increase in just over three years.
That $1.5 billion is an underrepresentation because at times researchers had to rely on media and auditor general reports since only seven provinces and territories make this data available.
Agency nurses, also called travel nurses, are employed by for-profit nursing agencies whose services are contracted out to short-staffed public health-care employers to fill temporary staffing needs.
While agency nurses are used throughout the health-care system, they were never meant to replace full-time nursing staff.
Currently, 472 for-profit nursing agencies operate across Canada, although growing demand means this number is on the rise.
That growth is compounded by the number of nurses leaving the public health-care system due to stagnated wages, burnout and the use of incentives by agencies.
It's inconceivable that hospital administrations actively fight against wage increases for nurses while paying agencies two to two and a half times the rate they pay public nurses, who earn about $40 per hour.
Some agencies also pay private nurses a bonus for working an overnight shift. Then, there's the agency's fee, which can bring the total cost per agency nurse to more than $300 per hour. That's an estimate because these private corporations don't answer to the public.
Private nurses are often not able to do the entire job because there's no orientation, no training, little to no supervision and a lack of continuity.
The privatization of nursing not only costs taxpayers significantly more money, but also pulls experienced health-care workers from the public system.
That should concern everyone, because it's been well documented that every time a patient is added to a registered nurse's workload, the risk of an adverse effect or even death goes up by seven per cent.
The CFNU report states that the use of nursing agencies can't be immediately curtailed because of the adverse impact that would have on staffing.
The report also states, "While nursing shortages and deteriorating working conditions predate the pandemic, a tsunami has taken place across the country with many nurses deciding that they have had enough, spurring them to retire early, leave the public system, or leave the profession."
What happens within the health-care system in the U.S. comes to Canada via Alberta. The United Conservative Party then shares its playbook with Conservatives in Ontario, who implement covert privatization plans.
Kieran Fong's documentary, "The Myth of Canadian Healthcare/Hippocrates: A Guide to Treating Healthcare Workers," shows how Alberta's public health-care system was gutted under premiers Ralph Klein and Jason Kenney.
In Ontario, former premier Mike Harris laid the groundwork, ensuring his retirement income by privatizing long-term residential care and opening the door for private for-profit nursing agencies like his own, Nurse Next Door.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford continued diverting public health-care funds to private nursing agencies even while Bill 124 limited nurses to increases of one per cent for three years.
Bill 124 was declared unconstitutional in November 2023. That means $1 billion in back pay is now being reimbursed to nurses. Yet, not every health service board chose to set that money aside.
Time to pay public nurses for their invaluable labour, Doug.
CAPTION: Nurses are leaving the public health-care system due to stagnated wages, burnout and the allure of incentives from for-profit agencies, Doreen Nicoll writes.
CREDIT: Doreen Nicoll

Subject: Workforce planning; Public health; Privatization; Nursing; Health care; Nurses

Document 250 of 500

A messy government boondoggle in Toronto. Could it happen in Hamilton?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/messy-government-boondoggle-toronto-could-happen/docview/3122179717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+messy+government+boondoggle+in+Toronto.+Could+it+happen+in+Hamilton%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Playwright Michael Healey is pretty much theatre royalty. He's won five Dora Mavor Moore Awards for his biting plays about life, human frailty and the dark side of politics.
He's also the guy who gave audiences "The Drawer Boy," a warm and touching look at life and getting older.
Given the man's theatrical stature, you expect a giant to duck under the door when he comes into the room. Instead, a pleasant, slightly zoftig man of middle years walks in. And he gets right down to business talking about his award-winning satire "The Master Plan."
"Hamilton will love it," he chuckles.
He's probably right. After all, it's a play that disses Toronto.
"And yes, it's very Toronto-centric. Still, the political boondoggle at its centre could just as easily have happened in Hamilton. Political disasters are always just a few blocks away."
In Healey's play, sharp New York City operators come to Toronto backed by Google. Their subsidiary, called Sidewalk Labs, tackles Waterfront Toronto, the organization responsible for the development of the city's waterfront.
So, is everything on the up and up? Of course not.
Here in Hamilton, we understand such debacles. We could be talking about the so far phantom LRT and many other money-eating mistakes.
And let's not even think about the sewage that found its way into Princess Point. Then, too, there's the troubling downtown. It frightens some folks away from its empty streets at night.
For all its Toronto issues, Healey's "The Master Plan" could be happening in the shadow of our very own Gore Park Fountain.
"Thank goodness for humour," Healey laughs. "It lets us process the tragic and the infuriating. We certainly need to learn to laugh.''
Healey's play, a huge success down the road in Toronto, has to do with urban planning. "It's about the intent to create the city of the future, whatever that might be," he shrugs. "We're in the middle of a crisis to do with the climate, the need for a much bigger electricity grid and how we're going to house the hundreds of thousands of people coming into the country to live."
No matter how scary all this is, Healey's the guy who finds humour to leaven the load. And no, you don't need to be politically savvy to get his play.
Based on journalist Josh O'Kane's bestseller, "Sideways: The City Google Couldn't Buy," it's been lightened with a free flow of humour. "O'Kane's book is so carefully researched. It's 100 per cent accurate. If something was only 99 per cent true, it was thrown out," Healey said. "My play, on the other hand, has the luxury of being able to laugh at everything. I'll do anything to give the audience a good time. I want them to have fun."
Healey has terrific allies in making his revolution of comedy work. The play is staged by Chris Abraham, the hottest director on the Canadian theatre scene. He's so astute theatrically, he can make you laugh or cry and sometimes both at once. His company, Crow's Theatre, is bringing the play to Theatre Aquarius, before a return engagement in Toronto where it sold out last year.
And it has a dream team of a cast: Mike Shara, Philippa Domville, Ben Carlson, Tanja Jacobs, Rose Napoli and Christopher Allen are all seasoned theatre stars who've played the top playhouses in the land.
And for bonus value, there's Healey himself, who steps on stage in the role of the narrator and speaks his own lines. "Trying to remember them, you mean," he laughs. "I haven't been on stage in 10 years and writing and acting are two different mindsets. But you know, I don't analyze my lines during rehearsal. I just try to remember the darn things.
"Forgetting is humiliating and it's humbling when you can't remember your own material. Gasping for a line is one thing but trying to make something up is quite another. A play, well even a joke, has a rhythm of its own. You can't alter it and make it work."
Healey is both complimentary and critical about Hamilton. Living close to downtown while his play is at Aquarius, he admires the escarpment and the beauty of the trees and leaves. He likes the little bars on Augusta Street. But there are other things that are not so felicitous. "The women in the cast are a bit nervous walking back to their accommodation at night. A lot of things downtown are closed. This doesn't make for a comforting atmosphere."
"The Master Plan" is a look at the way government is always trying to do what will help achieve re-election, even when they know what they're doing is wrong.
"But you know, this is a comedy," Healey shrugs. "Don't expect dull facts and figures. Don't expect too much information. Just come ready to enjoy.
"Yes, I know it's about asking tough questions. I want people to ask them. What we're going to do about the problems we're facing? I mean how are we going to house all the people who are coming here, for instance? We need to stop thinking individually. And you know, Doug Ford's $200 gift isn't really part of the answer. But hey, behind every laugh, there's something serious to think about, right?"
CAPTION: "Yes, it's very Toronto-centric. Still, the political boondoggle at its centre could just as easily have happened in Hamilton," says playwright Michael Healey.Mike Shara, Philippa Domville, Christopher Allen and others in a scene from "The Master Plan" atTheatre Aquarius.

Subject: Theater; Electricity distribution; Humor; Dramatists

Document 251 of 500

Catholic school board tightens trustee travel rules after controversial trip to Italy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/catholic-school-board-tightens-trustee-travel/docview/3122179716/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Catholic+school+board+tightens+trustee+travel+rules+after+controversial+trip+to+Italy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Catholic school board facing a ministry probe over an art-buying trip to Italy has walked back controversial changes to its expense policy made just weeks before trustees travelled to Europe last summer.
In July, the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board spent around $145,000 to send four trustees to northern Italy for a week to buy statues, crosses and other religious art for new high schools.
Following a public uproar - and the announcement of a looming provincial investigation - the board announced it "deeply regrets" the trip. At a board meeting last week, chair Rick Petrella said he was seeking donations to cover art costs and vowed individual travelling trustees would make "personal repayment" of the apparent $45,000 in trip expenses.
The board has not released detailed trip expenses, despite repeated requests.
Parents, residents and educators expressed outrage after The Spectator reported the trip to South Tyrol went ahead just weeks after the board approved loosening its policy for travel expenses for trustees to allow for business-class flights, upgraded hotel rooms and, in certain cases, the expensing of alcohol and valet parking.
Meal cost maximums were also removed in the June board decision that also switched approval authority for expense exceptions to the board chair instead of the treasurer and superintendent of business.
Petrella and other top board officials have not answered Spectator questions about why the expenses policy was updated ahead of the trip, or whether the travelling trustees - Petrella, Dan Dignard, Bill Chopp and Mark Watson - expensed the newly upgraded travel options. Board minutes from early summer note, however, that at least some changes had been suggested by the chair.
On Monday, the board held a special meeting to ratify a new overhaul of the expenses policy after a review by an unspecified law firm.
Reached by email, Petrella said the expenses policy is now "fully compliant" with Ontario's public-sector expenses directive - but he again refused to talk about why the policy changed in June. "The board has no further comment on this matter," he wrote Tuesday.
Notable changes in the new policy approved Monday include:
Any international travel from now on requires approval from the provincial education minister;Travel within North America needs pre-approval by the board and only standard/economy flight fares will be reimbursed;"Under no circumstances" can alcohol be claimed or reimbursed;Meal price maximums have been reinstated;The board treasurer now replaces the chair as the approval authority on various exceptional expense circumstances.
It's not clear what, if any impact the repaid expenses, art donations and policy changes will have on the governance review announced by Education Minister Jill Dunlop.
A spokesperson for Dunlop said this week details on the identity of the investigator, timeline and scope for the probe have yet to be settled.
Last week, Dunlop told the Toronto Star her view is that international trips for school trustees should happen rarely, if at all.
And Premier Doug Ford, weighing in on a Newstalk 1010 radio show, slammed the school board and suggested residents should "fire" travelling trustees during the next election.
"They need to keep the money with the schools, with ... the children and with the teachers. That's where the money should be, in the classroom, not going on these fancy-dancey trips, spending 45 grand."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter with The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Signage on the north side of Powerline Road in Brantford announces St. Padre Pio Catholic Secondary School, which is expected to open in September 2026. Trustees went to Italy to buy art for the school.Rick Petrella says the trustee expense policy for the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board is now "fully compliant" with provincial directives.

Location: Italy

Title: Catholic school board tightens trustee travel rules after controversial trip to Italy

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 30, 2024

Section: news/hamilton-region

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 252 of 500

Privatization of nursing a costly problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/privatization-nursing-costly-problem/docview/3121968907/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Privatization+of+nursing+a+costly+problem&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In September, the Canadian Federation of Nurses Unions (CFNU) released the report, Opening the black box: Unpacking the use of nursing agencies in Canada.
The CFNU worked with Queen's University to conduct the mixed methods study led by Dr. Joan Almost. The study shows the impact agencies have on human resources planning, privatization of the public health-care system, retention and recruitment of nurses.
The report stated that spending on for-profit agency nurses is expected to surpass $1.5 billion in Canada in the current fiscal year. That's a sixfold increase in just over three years.
That $1.5 billion is an underrepresentation because at times researchers had to rely on media and auditor general reports since only seven provinces and territories make this data available.
Agency nurses, also called travel nurses, are employed by for-profit nursing agencies whose services are contracted out to short-staffed public health-care employers to fill temporary staffing needs.
While agency nurses are used throughout the health-care system, they were never meant to replace full-time nursing staff.
Currently, 472 for-profit nursing agencies operate across Canada, although growing demand means this number is on the rise.
That growth is compounded by the number of nurses leaving the public health-care system due to stagnated wages, burnout and the use of incentives by agencies.
It's inconceivable that hospital administrations actively fight against wage increases for nurses while paying agencies two to two and a half times the rate they pay public nurses, who earn about $40 per hour.
Some agencies also pay private nurses a bonus for working an overnight shift. Then, there's the agency's fee, which can bring the total cost per agency nurse to more than $300 per hour. That's an estimate because these private corporations don't answer to the public.
Private nurses are often not able to do the entire job because there's no orientation, no training, little to no supervision and a lack of continuity.
The privatization of nursing not only costs taxpayers significantly more money, but also pulls experienced health-care workers from the public system. That should concern everyone, because it's been well documented that every time a patient is added to a registered nurse's workload, the risk of an adverse effect or even death goes up by seven per cent.
The CFNU report states that the use of nursing agencies can't be immediately curtailed because of the adverse impact that would have on staffing.
The report also states, "While nursing shortages and deteriorating working conditions predate the pandemic, a tsunami has taken place across the country with many nurses deciding that they have had enough, spurring them to retire early, leave the public system, or leave the profession."
What happens within the health-care system in the U.S. comes to Canada via Alberta. The United Conservative Party then shares its playbook with Conservatives in Ontario, who implement covert privatization plans.
Kieran Fong's documentary, "The Myth of Canadian Healthcare/Hippocrates: A Guide to Treating Healthcare Workers," shows how Alberta's public health-care system was gutted under premiers Ralph Klein and Jason Kenney.
In Ontario, former premier Mike Harris laid the groundwork, ensuring his retirement income by privatizing long-term residential care and opening the door for private for-profit nursing agencies like his own, Nurse Next Door.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford continued diverting public health-care funds to private nursing agencies even while Bill 124 limited nurses to increases of one per cent for three years.
Bill 124 was declared unconstitutional in November 2023. That means $1 billion in back pay is now being reimbursed to nurses. Yet, not every health service board chose to set that money aside.
Time to pay public nurses for their invaluable labour, Doug.
Doreen Nicoll is a journalist and podcaster.
Find her work at doreenn.substack.com
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Doreen Nicoll Doreen Nicoll is a journalist and podcaster. Find her work at doreenn.substack.com;

Document 253 of 500

Changes to home-care equipment contracts halted during crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/changes-home-care-equipment-contracts-halted/docview/3121966010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Changes+to+home-care+equipment+contracts+halted+during+crisis&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Upcoming changes to how home-care equipment is provided have been put on hold as the province grapples with a crisis that has left vulnerable patients without vital medical supplies.
New contracts were supposed to start next week, altering how equipment such as hospital beds, walkers and wheelchairs are provided to home-care patients.
The changes have now been postponed for an unknown amount of time as patients continue to face desperate shortages since new contracts for medical supplies and infusion medicine started Sept. 24.
"There is no plan to change equipment contracts next week," Ontario Health atHome said in a statement.
The provincial agency that co-ordinates in-home and community-based care for over 650,000 Ontario patients a year refused to answer questions about the contracts, including when they will be implemented and what companies were awarded the business.
Ontario Health atHome won't say whether the changes to home-care equipment are similar to medical supplies and infusion medicine, for which sources say a smaller number of companies now procure provisions and fulfil orders for larger parts of the province. In addition, sources say the items and quantities available to order have changed.
The transition in September has been described as utter chaos by patients, home-care workers and doctors from across Ontario, who describe almost instantly having no supplies for wound care, changing catheters or draining fluid from around a cancer patient's lungs. There were long delays to getting antibiotics, sedation and pain medication.
Doctors said kits for medical assistance in dying were missing critical parts.
"It's maddening for nurses," said Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO) "They go everyday to work wanting to do good care but they can't do it without supplies. It's stress no one needs."
Dr. Adil Shamji, the Liberal health critic and MPP for Don Valley East, has asked Ontario's auditor general to investigate Ontario Heath atHome, and the major issues surrounding the procurement and distribution of home-care supplies.
"We need to know how this happened, why this happened, and who needs to be held responsible," Shamji said in a statement.
Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday that his government is "fixing the problem."
"I'm holding people accountable on this one," Ford said. "We're going to make sure that never happens again."
Ford was asked about the home-care crisis after announcing $200 rebate cheques to about 15 million Ontarians.
"Forget this $200," Grinspun said, calling the rebate cheques a distraction. "I want to hear, 'We will fix home care for everybody' ... They need to figure this out."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday that his government is "fixing the problem" with home-care supplies.canadian press file photo

Document 254 of 500

Political disasters are always just a few blocks away: Dora award-winning play is tough, tense and very funny

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/political-disasters-are-always-just-few-blocks/docview/3121966007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Political+disasters+are+always+just+a+few+blocks+away%3A+Dora+award-winning+play+is+tough%2C+tense+and+very+funny&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Playwright Michael Healey is pretty much theatre royalty. He's won five Dora Mavor MooreAwards for his biting plays about life, human frailty and the dark side of politics.
He's also the guy who gave audiences "The Drawer Boy," a warm and touching look at life and getting older.
Given the man's theatrical stature, you expect a giant to duck under the door when he comes into the room. Instead, a pleasant, slightly zoftig man of middle years walks in. And he gets right down to business talking about his award-winning satire "The Master Plan."
"Hamilton will love it," he chuckles.
He's probably right. After all, it's a play that disses Toronto.
"And yes, it's very Toronto-centric. Still, the political boondoggle at its centre could just as easily have happened in Hamilton. Political disasters are always just a few blocks away."
In Healey's play, sharp New York City operators come to Toronto backed by Google. Their subsidiary, called Sidewalk Labs, tackles Waterfront Toronto, the organization responsible for the development of the city's waterfront.
So, is everything on the up and up? Of course not.
Here in Hamilton, we understand such debacles. We could be talking about the so far phantom LRT and many other money-eating mistakes.
And let's not even think about the sewage that found its way into Princess Point. Then, too, there's the troubling downtown. It frightens some folks away from its empty streets at night.
For all its Toronto issues, Healey's "The Master Plan" could be happening in the shadow of our very own Gore Park Fountain.
"Thank goodness for humour," Healey laughs. "It lets us process the tragic and the infuriating. We certainly need to learn to laugh.''
Healey's play, a huge success down the road in Toronto, has to do with urban planning. "It's about the intent to create the city of the future, whatever that might be," he shrugs. "We're in the middle of a crisis to do with the climate, the need for a much bigger electricity grid and how we're going to house the hundreds of thousands of people coming into the country to live."
No matter how scary all this is, Healey's the guy who finds humour to leaven the load. And no, you don't need to be politically savvy to get his play.
Based on journalist Josh O'Kane's bestseller, "Sideways: The City Google Couldn't Buy," it's been lightened with a free flow of humour. "O'Kane's book is so carefully researched. It's 100 per cent accurate. If something was only 99 per cent true, it was thrown out," Healey said. "My play, on the other hand, has the luxury of being able to laugh at everything. I'll do anything to give the audience a good time. I want them to have fun."
Healey has terrific allies in making his revolution of comedy work. The play is staged by Chris Abraham, the hottest director on the Canadian theatre scene. He's so astute theatrically, he can make you laugh or cry and sometimes both at once. His company, Crow's Theatre, is bringing the play to Theatre Aquarius, before a return engagement in Toronto where it sold out last year.
And it has a dream team of a cast: Mike Shara, Philippa Domville, Ben Carlson, Tanja Jacobs, Rose Napoli and Christopher Allen are all seasoned theatre stars who've played the top playhouses in the land.
And for bonus value, there's Healey himself, who steps on stage in the role of the narrator and speaks his own lines. "Trying to remember them, you mean," he laughs. "I haven't been on stage in 10 years and writing and acting are two different mindsets. But you know, I don't analyze my lines during rehearsal. I just try to remember the darn things.
"Forgetting is humiliating and it's humbling when you can't remember your own material. Gasping for a line is one thing but trying to make something up is quite another. A play, well even a joke, has a rhythm of its own. You can't alter it and make it work."
Healey is both complimentary and critical about Hamilton. Living close to downtown while his play is at Aquarius, he admires the escarpment and the beauty of the trees and leaves. He likes the little bars on Augusta Street. But there are other things that are not so felicitous. "The women in the cast are a bit nervous walking back to their accommodation at night. A lot of things downtown are closed. This doesn't make for a comforting atmosphere."
"The Master Plan" is a look at the way government is always trying to do what will help achieve re-election, even when they know what they're doing is wrong.
"But you know, this is a comedy," Healey shrugs. "Don't expect dull facts and figures. Don't expect too much information. Just come ready to enjoy.
"Yes, I know it's about asking tough questions. I want people to ask them. What we're going to do about the problems we're facing? I mean how are we going to house all the people who are coming here, for instance? We need to stop thinking individually. And you know, Doug Ford's $200 gift isn't really part of the answer. But hey, behind every laugh, there's something serious to think about, right?"
Gary Smith has written about theatre and dance for the Hamilton Spectator for 40 years, as well for a variety of international publications. gsmith1@cogeco.ca.

The Master Plan
WhoMichael Healey
What'The Master Plan' presented by Crow's Theatre and Theatre Aquarius
WhereTheatre Aquarius,
190 King William St.
WhenOct. 30 through Nov. 16. Evenings at 7:30 (No Sunday or Monday evenings). Matinees
Saturdays and Sundays at 1 p.m.
TicketsCall 905-522-7529 or theatreaquarius.org

Document 255 of 500

Liberal MPs need to show backbone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-mps-need-show-backbone/docview/3121966005/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MPs+need+to+show+backbone&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There has been speculation in the media recently about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau facing a caucus revolt.
His polling numbers are a disaster and if a federal election were held today, the odds are strong Pierre Poilievre and the Conservative Party of Canada would win a massive majority.
Some members of the Liberal caucus as well as many Canadians feel Trudeau should step down and give the Liberals a chance to fight the next election with a new leader.
When Trudeau met with his caucus on Oct. 23, there were reports the members would order him to step down. Apparently there was a letter demanding this, signed by 24 Liberal members of Parliament.
It appears, though, the demand "fizzled."
There are plenty of unnamed Liberals expressing concern over Trudeau's leadership. But they don't want to go public. This refusal by the Liberal caucus to remove a flawed leader and go public with their concerns is a prime example of a caucus lacking in intestinal fortitude.
The first sign of this was their refusal to apply the provisions of the 2015 Reform Act, written by Conservative MP Michael Chong.
This act of legislation was written to restore power to the party caucuses after decades of power being centralized under the party leaders.
The Reform Act created four sets of provisions relating to: the expulsion/readmission of caucus members; the election and removal of the caucus chair; the removal of the party leader; and the election of an interim leader.
At the first sitting of the House after an election, members of each party vote on whether any or all of these should apply to their caucus.
The approval of the provisions by the caucuses is valid until Parliament dissolves. After the following election another vote is held.
The Liberal caucus has never voted in favour of giving themselves the powers available to them under the Reform Act.
Why would a responsible caucus take deliberate steps to strip their ability to remove an unpopular leader? Are Liberal MPs simply scared of "crossing" Trudeau?
I remind readers the Conservative caucus voted to have this ability and in fact invoked the Reform Act to remove Erin O'Toole as their leader in 2022.
Conservatives showed great courage in the spring of 2001 when 12 Canadian Alliance MPs led by Chuck Strahl left the Alliance to sit as an independent caucus, over the refusal of unpopular party leader Stockwell Day to resign.
No Liberals have taken such action.
Individual Canadian politicians have also shown far more courage than has the current Liberal caucus.
In 1988, Jim Walding, a New Democratic Party member of the Manitoba legislature, who was known to challenge his own party and government, voted against his own party's budget, thereby defeating the NDP government.
Even more dramatically, in February 1963, Doug Harkness, the Progressive Conservative national defence minister, publicly resigned from prime minister John Diefenbaker's cabinet over Diefenbaker's inept performance during the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, and Diefenbaker's refusal to accept nuclear warheads for the Bomarc missiles stationed in Canada.
Harkness was livid over Diefenbaker's "waffling" on the warhead issue as the missiles were purchased by Canada with the clear understanding they only operated with nuclear warheads. Harkness, a man of strong principle, left cabinet to sit as a back bencher. That took courage.
These are only a couple of examples of courage shown by past Canadian politicians. It is a shame the current Liberal caucus cannot be included here.
Craig Wallace
is a Hamilton resident
and author of five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace OPINION Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of five books.;

$200 cheques will cost province $3 billion: Money expected in mail early next year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/200-cheques-will-cost-province-3-billion/docview/3121965649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%24200+cheques+will+cost+province+%243+billion%3A+Money+expected+in+mail+early+next+year&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario taxpayers are set to receive $200 cheques in the mail early next year, a $3-billion plan the premier suggests is not tied to a possible early election - though the opposition leaders have their doubts.
Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy made the announcement Tuesday, a day ahead of the province's fall economic statement, which serves as a mini budget.
Everyone who filed 2023 tax returns should expect to receive a $200 cheque, plus an additional $200 per child, Ford said.
"These $200 rebates will make a big difference for families across the province, with cheques arriving early in the new year," he said.
"That's money that families can use to pay off their credit card after the holidays, to buy a winter coat for their kids, or to cover the cost of gassing up the family car to make up for the cost of the Liberal carbon tax."
The announcement comes as Ford has refused to rule out the possibility of an election sooner than the next fixed date of June 2026, and while he has said he will not call one this year, opposition parties are preparing for a possible spring contest.
The two are not related, Ford suggested, saying his government has been looking for ways to save Ontarians money all along, including on transit fares, a gas tax cut and licence plate renewal fees. He then went on to attack the Liberals.
"They'd increase taxes, take money out of your pockets," he said, concluding his answer by saying the Liberals and NDP are "basically the same party."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she will donate her $200 cheque to the party.
She did not mince words on what she called a money-giving "gimmick."
"The premier is attempting to bribe Ontarians with their own money," she said.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said the cheques do not provide any long-lasting help.
"I think a lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis for Ontarians," she said.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said it doesn't make sense to dole out this money without any means testing.
"It is ridiculous that millionaires and billionaires like Galen Weston are getting a $200 cheque when so many Ontarians can't afford a place to live tonight," he said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 257 of 500

Catholic board tightens trustee travel rules after Italy trip: Trustees expensed $45,000 on art-buying trek to Europe but now say they'll repay it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/catholic-board-tightens-trustee-travel-rules/docview/3121965606/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Catholic+board+tightens+trustee+travel+rules+after+Italy+trip%3A+Trustees+expensed+%2445%2C000+on+art-buying+trek+to+Europe+but+now+say+they%27ll+repay+it&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Catholic school board facing a ministry probe over an art-buying trip to Italy has walked back controversial changes to its expense policy made just weeks before trustees travelled to Europe last summer.
In July, the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board spent around $145,000 to send four trustees to northern Italy for a week to buy statues, crosses and other religious art for new high schools.
Following a public uproar - and the announcement of a looming provincial investigation - the board announced it "deeply regrets" the trip. At a board meeting last week, chair Rick Petrella said he was seeking donations to cover art costs and vowed individual travelling trustees would make "personal repayment" of the apparent $45,000 in trip expenses.
The board has not released detailed trip expenses, despite repeated requests.
Parents, residents and educators expressed outrage after The Spectator reported the trip to South Tyrol went ahead just weeks after the board approved loosening its policy for travel expenses for trustees to allow for business-class flights, upgraded hotel rooms and, in certain cases, the expensing of alcohol and valet parking.
Meal cost maximums were also removed in the June board decision that also switched approval authority for expense exceptions to the board chair instead of the treasurer and superintendent of business.
Petrella and other top board officials have not answered Spectator questions about why the expenses policy was updated ahead of the trip, or whether the travelling trustees - Petrella, Dan Dignard, Bill Chopp and Mark Watson - expensed the newly upgraded travel options. Board minutes from early summer note, however, that at least some changes had been suggested by the chair.
On Monday, the board held a special meeting to ratify a new overhaul of the expenses policy after a review by an unspecified law firm.
Reached by email, Petrella said the expenses policy is now "fully compliant" with Ontario's public-sector expenses directive - but he again refused to talk about why the policy changed in June. "The board has no further comment on this matter," he wrote Tuesday.
Notable changes in the new policy approved Monday include:
Any international travel from now on requires approval from the provincial education minister;
Travel within North America needs pre-approval by the board and only standard/economy flight fares will be reimbursed;
"Under no circumstances" can alcohol be claimed or reimbursed;
Meal price maximums have been reinstated;
The board treasurer now replaces the chair as the approval authority on various exceptional expense circumstances.
It's not clear what, if any impact the repaid expenses, art donations and policy changes will have on the governance review announced by Education Minister Jill Dunlop.
A spokesperson for Dunlop said this week details on the identity of the investigator, timeline and scope for the probe have yet to be settled.
Last week, Dunlop told the Toronto Star her view is that international trips for school trustees should happen rarely, if at all.
And Premier Doug Ford, weighing in on a Newstalk 1010 radio show, slammed the school board and suggested residents should "fire" travelling trustees during the next election.
"They need to keep the money with the schools, with ... the children and with the teachers. That's where the money should be, in the classroom, not going on these fancy-dancey trips, spending 45 grand."
Matthew Van Dongen is a
reporter with The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Signage on the north side of Powerline Road in Brantford announces St. Padre Pio Catholic Secondary School, which is expected to open in September 2026.Rick Petrella says the trustee expense policy for the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board is now "fully compliant" with provincial directives.Hamilton spectator file photohamilton Spectator file photo

Document 258 of 500

Ask me to use notwithstanding clause to end encampments, Doug Ford tells mayors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ask-me-use-notwithstanding-clause-end-encampments/docview/3122287831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ask+me+to+use+notwithstanding+clause+to+end+encampments%2C+Doug+Ford+tells+mayors&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Urban mayors grappling with homelessness and encampments need to show "backbone" and toughen up their push for stronger reforms, Premier Doug Ford says.
"Why don't the big city mayors actually put in writing that they want the province to change the homeless program, make sure that we move the homeless along - and why don't they put in the use of the notwithstanding clause or something like that" so reforms can't be overturned by the courts, Ford said Tuesday.
"That's what they should be doing, rather than just going up to the edge ... Let's see if they have the backbone to do it - if they really want the homeless situation to improve."
Ford - referring to a decision last year in Waterloo in which a judge ruled that it's a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict those in encampments if there are no shelter spaces available for them - said if the mayors of the province's biggest cities "want something done, they have to put it in writing to us to make sure that they're 100 per cent behind us. You can't put your butt on both sides of the fence."
The charter's "notwithstanding clause" allows a government to pass legislation that overrides certain rights.
Ontario Big City Mayors, representing 29 of the province's largest communities, met recently in Markham and urged both the provincial and federal governments to "step up and take action now on the mental health, addictions and homeless crisis" they all face, and in particular to fund and build shelters and supportive housing.
They also want a review of the Mental Health and the Health Care Consent acts, and a ban on the public use of illegal drugs, among other measures.
Burlington Mayor Marianne Meed Ward, chair of Ontario Big City Mayors, said leaders have sent multiple written requests to the government about addressing the crisis, in particular asking for "one point person, a specific minister or ministry, in charge of solving this" and a province-wide plan.
While municipal leaders discussed requesting the province enact new legislation using the notwithstanding clause, they could not reach consensus, she added.
However, "we've been very specific in what we, as a caucus, believe is going to achieve the best results for our community - and the premier has received all of that in writing ... and if he is of the opinion that the notwithstanding clause is the right way to go, he has every opportunity to implement that," she said.
"We have put into our request what we think are the best outcomes."
She said the "issue becomes, if you are using the notwithstanding clause to close down encampments, but people have nowhere to go, we're no farther ahead."
Answering questions after making an unrelated announcement in Scarborough on Tuesday morning, Ford said he receives "endless calls about people camping out in the parks, needles being all over the park."
Parents, he added, are "scared to put their kids out in the park by themselves, no matter what age, and we have to make a move. But big city mayors, you want to get it done? I need that letter."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, however, accused Ford of taking "cheap shots."
"He never, ever takes true responsibility or accountability," she told reporters at Queen's Park. "The mess that we are experiencing right now, it has happened under his watch. He is responsible. He is the premier of this province and he has not done his job."
Barrie Mayor Alex Nuttall fully supports the province using the notwithstanding clause to help "restore municipal governments' ability to maintain order and safety," and especially to "ensure our children walking to school are safe in each and every park."
However, in a written statement he also acknowledged such a move "will not solve all problems, but at least give our mayors the opportunity to move forward and protect our cities' most innocent and vulnerable citizens."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: The Tiffany Barton homeless encampment in Hamilton is shown on Oct. 9, 2024.

Document 259 of 500

Doug Ford announces details of $200 cheques for 15 million Ontarians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-announces-details-200-cheques-15/docview/3122287615/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+announces+details+of+%24200+cheques+for+15+million+Ontarians&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is mailing out $200 "rebate" cheques to 15 million Ontarians in a $3-billion hit to a provincial treasury awash in red ink.
Confirming a scheme first revealed by the Star on Oct. 16, Ford on Tuesday said the cheques would be in the mail early in the new year, "because life is expensive right now."
The payments - a cornerstone pledge of Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement Wednesday - set the stage for a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Striking a partisan tone at the campaign-style government announcement in a Scarborough gymnasium, Ford tried to contrast his two-term Progressive Conservatives with the opposition Liberals and the New Democrats.
"The difference between our party and the Liberals and NDP - they believe in one thing: tax the taxpayers to death; drive companies out of this province. We've taken a different approach," the premier insisted.
"Instead of holding on to this money like Bonnie Crombie would, we're putting it back into the pockets of taxpayers. She wants to increase taxes, increase the carbon tax," he said in a swipe at the Liberal leader.
Crombie, for her part, reminded Ford "the only reason" Ontarians pay the federal carbon levy is because he scrapped the province's cap-and-trade environmental alliance with Quebec and California in 2018.
"We will not implement a carbon tax," she said, accusing the premier of using $3 billion of "taxpayers' money to buy their vote" next spring.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles dubbed the cheques, which will bear Bethlenfalvy's signature, a "cynical" ploy.
"A lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis," said Stiles.
"A $200 cheque, it might help you through groceries for that week, but it isn't going to help you the next month or the month after that," she said.
The tax-free cheques will be sent to all taxpayers 18 and over and families who qualify for federal Canada Child Benefit (CCB) payment receive an additional $200 for every child under 18.
"For families with children who did not receive the CCB for 2024, the government would provide an opportunity for a taxpayer rebate payment of $200 per child through an alternative process," the PC government said in a statement.
That means 15 million payments will be sent out - 12.5 million for adults and 2.5 million for children. Only those Ontarians who were "bankrupt or incarcerated" this year will not receive a payment.
But Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "if you're going to do something like this, you should have it means-tested," so only the people most in need receive it.
"I don't understand why millionaires and billionaires like Galen Weston should get a $200 cheque when there are so many Ontarians who can't afford a home, 2.5 million can't access a doctor," said Schreiner, referring to the grocery tycoon.
"It is outrageous that people with seven-figure incomes are getting a $200 cheque."
Asked by a journalist why the rich should also be mailed a cheque, Ford, multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family, bristled: "Because they're taxpayers. These are tax dollars going back to the taxpayers and we're going to give it back to every single person in Ontario."
The cheque-mailing is a reprise of what former Tory premier Mike Harris did in 2000.
But when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques, the province's books were balanced. (That $200 payment a generation ago would be worth around $335 today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.)
Bethlenfalvy's fiscal update Wednesday is expected to show a hefty deficit, though the shortfall should be less than the surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25 he projected in the March 26 budget.
That was $10 billion more than the $200 million surplus he had forecast beforehand.
With files From Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queen's Park as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Monday.

Document 260 of 500

'Nothing quite like being a parent': Ontario expands fertility support, announces new tax credit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nothing-quite-like-being-parent-ontario-expands/docview/3122287280/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Nothing+quite+like+being+a+parent%27%3A+Ontario+expands+fertility+support%2C+announces+new+tax+credit&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is setting aside $150 million over the next two years to help more people become parents.
In a recent announcement, Premier Doug Ford said he was expanding the Ontario Fertility Program to cut wait-lists and triple the number of families getting access to government-funded fertility services in hospital and community clinic settings.
"There's nothing quite like being a parent," Ford said on X. "We're tripling the number of Ontario families able to access fertility treatments so more people have the chance to start or grow their family."
Launched in 2015, the Ontario Fertility Program allowed patients to access in vitro fertilization (IVF), fertility preservation and intrauterine insemination, regardless of gender, sexual orientation or family status.
It meant each patient was eligible for one publicly funded IVF cycle in their lifetime, and an additional cycle if they have acted as a surrogate.
So far, more than 107,000 patients have used the service, with 42,741 using IVF to try and build a family.
"There's no role more special or important than being a parent," Deputy Premier and Health Minister Sylvia Jones said in a press release. "I'm thrilled that our government is helping more people start and grow their families by expanding the Ontario Fertility Program."
The announcement means more clinics will offer services to patients who need financial support.
"Everyone in Ontario, no matter their economic means, deserves the special chance at parenthood," Jones said.
Another part of the province's announcement is the introduction of a new tax credit to help with the sometimes overwhelming expenses of fertility treatments.
More details about this will be shared in the 2024 economic outlook and fiscal review on Oct. 30.
The government's new tax credit is above and beyond Ontario's already existing medical expense tax credit, and would cover up to 25 per cent of eligible expenses, up to a maximum of $5,000 per year.
For example, a family spending $20,000 for a privately funded IVF treatment could be eligible to receive $5,000 in support, the province said.
The tax credit would include IVF cycles, fertility medications and travel for treatment and diagnostic testing.
"This government is listening to patients and giving us renewed hope and support as we look to build our families," fertility patient and Conceivable Dreams board chairperson Zane Colt said in a press release.
"With continued advances in the reproductive medicine field, fertility clinic teams have valuable knowledge and technology to achieve more successful patient outcomes," The Fertility Partners medical director Dr. Dan Nayot said in a press release. "This very welcomed investment by the Ontario government will reduce difficult barriers for people needing vital treatment."
For more information about the Ontario Fertility Program, visit ontariofertility.ca.
CAPTION: The Ontario government has announced an expansion to the Ontario Fertility Program, including a tax credit.
