Document 261 of 500

Doug Ford government confirms $200 rebate cheques coming to Ontario families. Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-confirms-200-rebate-cheques/docview/3122287262/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+confirms+%24200+rebate+cheques+coming+to+Ontario+families.+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has confirmed he's proposing a $200 rebate for all "taxpaying" adults and children in the province.
First shared in the Toronto Star on Oct. 16, the province is proposing the rebate to assist families financially.
"We're doing everything we can to keep costs down for Ontario families, but the federal carbon tax and high interest rates are still hurting taxpayers across the province," Premier Doug Ford said in an Oct. 29 press release. "This $200 taxpayer rebate will give Ontario taxpayers, especially families with children, some much-deserved help so they can make ends meet."
The announcement is expected with the 2024 Ontario Economic Outlook and Fiscal Review, coming Oct. 30.
The rebate would be for eligible taxpayers and eligible children, with cheques mailed to recipients in early 2025, the province shared.
It would mean a family of five with two adults and three children would receive $1,000, if eligible.
The province is spending $3 billion on the idea, for approximately 12.5 million adults and 2.5 million children.
"At a time when the cost of living remains stubbornly high, our government is taking action for the families and workers of this province with much-needed relief to their household budgets," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said in a press release. "We encourage taxpayers to watch their mailboxes for this taxpayer rebate in early 2025 as we continue making life more affordable for Ontarians while retaining prudent, responsible fiscal management."
Those eligible for the tax-free taxpayer rebate must be 18 years or older and be a resident in Ontario in December 2023. Eligible people will file their 2023 Income Tax and Benefit Return by Dec. 31, 2024, and not be bankrupt or incarcerated in 2024.
Families who qualify for a Canada Child Benefit (CCB) payment for 2024 would receive an additional $200 for each eligible child under age 18.
When word of this potential rebate first made headlines last week, it wasn't received well by the opposition.
"This is another one-time gimmick from a Premier who has promised and failed to cut taxes for more than six years," Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie said in an earlier interview. "Rather than try to buy your vote in an election year, Ontario's Liberals will cut taxes for all middle class families - permanently."
But not everyone was against the rebate.
"Gotta hand it to Dougie Ford," @B3man1 said on X. "Handing out $200 per resident to make the federal backstop carbon tax more affordable. I love it! $600 more added to the almost $1,500 my household gets back in federal carbon rebates. That's $2,100!"
"It's like winning the lottery."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has confirmed a plan to give a $200 tax rebate cheque for adults and children in the province.

Document 262 of 500

Oct. 29: Bike lanes are the future, changes to alcohol sales are a mess and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-29-bike-lanes-are-future-changes-alcohol/docview/3121581286/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+29%3A+Bike+lanes+are+the+future%2C+changes+to+alcohol+sales+are+a+mess+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bike lanes are the future
Re: Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones, Oct. 21
The provincial government is shooting itself in the foot through its recently tabled bill that will give omnipotent power in the building of Highway 413, along with removal and review of existing and future bike lanes.
Most Ontarians living in the GTHA struggle with commuting and traffic. What our cities need right now is more connectivity and more options for people to get to the places they need. Bike lanes consume little space and are a safe and environmentally friendly way for commuters to travel.
I agree with the points made by the conservative government; our cities need less traffic and gridlock. An effective way to start is by reducing the dependency on heavy, bulky, and mostly single-user motor vehicles on the road - also known as cars. Having grown up in St. Catharines, I know how badly we are lagging behind in cycling infrastructure when compared to cities like Hamilton. This legislation will only slow down progress.
As a young civil engineering student, I envision a future for Ontario where our public and active transit systems are the primary method of travel, and infrastructure is built to promote safety, health and prosperity of Ontarians.
Why are we investing billions of dollars into removing bike lanes that give commuters the ability to travel safely, and building highways that will destroy Ontario's precious farmland and ecosystems while creating urban sprawl?
The Ontario government needs to invest in reliable forms of public and active transportation that connect and serve citizens' needs.
Vlad Brundula-Ilija, Hamilton
Reduce parking to free up space
A question for Premier Doug Ford and his minister of transportation: If gridlock is such a problem in our cities, why don't you tackle the biggest problem, aside from too many vehicles, and remove on-street parking?
Park in your garage, driveway or in a private or public parking lot. Perhaps work with local businesses that have excess parking to allow for people to utilize their lots during off peak hours to park overnight. In most if not all municipalities, parking on municipal streets do have time limits, but are rarely enforced.
If removing on-street parking never crossed your mind, then Bill 212 was never about gridlock in the first place. Perhaps it's just a personal vendetta against Toronto and cyclists in general that all municipalities will pay for?
Erin Shacklette, Hamilton
Bike lanes get people out of cars
Re: Ontario to give itself veto power over city bike lanes, citing worsening gridlock, Oct. 15
In her article, Allison Jones discusses the province's new requirement for municipalities to seek permission before adding bike lanes. While the article remains neutral, it under-represents the perspectives of cyclists and urbanists.
While some see this legislation as a victory, it raises significant concerns about prioritizing cars in urban planning.
The convenience of personal vehicles often overshadows their negative impact on urban livability. Research shows that cars are inefficient in dense settings; for instance, traffic congestion costs the Canadian economy billions each year in lost productivity and wasted fuel.
This inefficiency is compounded by our reliance on automobiles, which contribute to significant air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions.
Though bike lanes may seem to contribute to gridlock, they are not the root of the problem. The real issue lies in the design of our streets, which often prioritizes vehicles over sustainable transport options.
Instead of simply responding to existing demand, bike lanes aim to encourage a shift toward cycling, fostering healthier and more sustainable urban environments. This complex issue warrants deeper exploration.
I highly encourage The Hamilton Spectator to speak with environmentalists and urban planners to better inform readers about the consequences of car dependency and the benefits of investing in cycling infrastructure for a more sustainable future.
Oscar Gauthier, Hamilton
Bike lanes a misguided priority
As much as the city stresses on providing the population with incentives to use active transportation, bike lanes deployed in Hamilton are not helpful.
Proponents of bike lanes claim that they relieve traffic congestion, but they decrease general lanes and thereby leads to congestion. This is most irritating in the areas where the lanes occupy half the road as can be evidenced by the circumstances surrounding the downtowns.
The worst part is these lanes are going to become unusable during the coming winter months when cycling is far less practical. These lanes are not used for a larger part of the year due to the severe winters in Hamilton, but still cause interference with other traffic and difficulty in snow management.
Furthermore, from what has been said about the city's expansion on bike-only lanes seems to miss the point that most people use their cars to make their commutes, and not bicycles. This is where, rather than helping to ease congestion through channels off-loading many lanes, (bike lanes) only result in the formation of traffic choke points and longer, more stressful journeys.
I would like city planners to expand their thinking on the size of this project and focus on measures that would help most people in Hamilton. However, cycling has its importance, but this (is) at the cost of having bike lanes crossing over the efficient and accessible road network.
Luca Zotti, Hamilton
Keep the speed bumps coming
Speed bumps have recently been installed on Old Ancaster Road. I am very pleased because drivers need to slow down on this stretch of road.
The posted speed limit is 40 km/h. Despite this, motorists travel 60-70 km/h. There is a three-way stop at Pleasant Avenue and the Old Ancaster Road, where 90 per cent of motorists do not fully stop. They slow down and simply roll through, never once putting on the brakes.
On the road between Dundas and Ancaster the average speed is 60-70 km/h on a road where the limit is 40 km/h. It is posted at this speed because it is a narrow, twisty road and not meant to be driven fast. I have been passed four times on this road by very aggressive and angry drivers simply because I was driving the speed limit.
It is time motorists started obeying the traffic laws. Keep the speed bumps coming or do whatever it takes to slow down motorists.
Patricia Muir, Dundas
Local play was outstanding
Re: Hamilton playwright Peter Rosser's 'A Question of Justice' is risk-taking theatre, Oct. 23
After reading Gary Smith's review of local playwright Peter Rosser's "A Question of Justice" now being performed at The Player' Guild of Hamilton, my wife and I were prepared to endure a tedious, boring long-winded production. Despite Smith's quibbles about the play, we thoroughly enjoyed it. We found it thought-provoking, captivating and it dealt with real emotional issues.
It ranks as an outstanding drama in our estimation. Go see it. Judge for yourself.
Dr. Peter Banting, Hamilton
Play tackles important issue
Today I saw the play, "A Question of Justice" at The Players Guild, written by Peter Rosser. It was thought provoking, with its theme - a very important "real" and ongoing issue - performed in a most believable way with main leads giving powerful performances.
All the actors were great! The set was "minimalistic" in comparison to the gravity of what we were hearing, probably intentionally, as not to deter from the importance of the theme and message: Drunk driving kills and lives are destroyed forever!
When I was 19 years old in Toronto, our 22-year-old friend Tommy, who had just become engaged, was killed by a drunk driver. I've never forgotten him. You probably know of someone in your lives too.
What can each of us do to prevent these tragedies?
Some plays do require lots of colour and fancy backgrounds to transform the empty stage into fantasy land. But this play wasn't about fantasy! It was about reality, so the simple furniture and props were there to be seen and used, but the main characters were the focus. The plot and the reality of it all was the focus. Well done cast and crew! We certainly have lots to think about. Five stars!
Hope Leon, Hamilton
More good than harm expected
Re: Neighbours up in arms over Hamilton's plan for cabin shelters on Barton site, Oct. 23
With winter quickly approaching in Hamilton, the homelessness crisis is an extremely urgent issue and emergency.
Residents see this proposed development of cabin-like shelters for the homeless as a "blatant disregard for the personal experiences of the residents," but this issue should be seen as a blatant disregard for the personal experiences of the homeless. They are humans, too.
One in 10 of us humans in Canada experience some form of homelessness in their life. For this reason, we need to work together to support each other. I notice an increasing amount of homeless individuals each year in Hamilton and I can only imagine the worry of winter instilled in them all.
I understand the residents' concerns and see where they are coming from, but I believe this development will do more good than harm. Actually, the residents may even benefit from this development. There is already a large encampment in the neighbouring lot that is unmonitored and now it can be monitored by the city, actually making it "safer" for everyone.
Also, the development conditions include that tents will not be allowed within one kilometre of the site under the city's encampment protocols. This includes some of the city's largest encampments like Pier 4 and Bayfront Park, benefiting the city's housed residents by reclaiming their recreational green space, while also providing unhoused individuals with a warm, roofed space to live.
With support in place, I believe this development is a viable temporary solution causing limited harm.
Camryn Bowler, Hamilton
Changes to alcohol sales a mess
Re: Ford in an LCBO mess of his own brewing and "Bottle returns plan in need of sober second thought, Oct. 28
With the mess that Premier Doug Ford has created selling liquor in convenience stores, I think the only "convenient" solution to this dilemma is for individuals to drop off their empties in an LCBO paper bag at their local Conservative MPP office. I'm sure an efficient and speedy response will be forthcoming from the premier's office!
Chris Cardey, Hamilton
No need for paper bags
Regarding the article regarding Doug Ford bringing back paper bags in liquor stores. It's a no-brainer, bring your own cloth bags, something we already do for every other store we shop in! It is very seldom that I see "bare bottles in public," as stated by Mr. Ford.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Important plan can't be rushed
The city of Hamilton is in desperate need of action against the ongoing homelessness crisis.
I believe the plan for 40 cabin-like shelters on Barton Street West land is necessary in attacking this problem head on. Cabin-like structures have the ability to minimize the frustrations around public fires, drug use, theft, vandalism, fights and defecation. These cabins are to be supported by staff and services who will be able to give these people the support they need to be a functioning member of this community.
However, due to the council's rushed plans excluding the thoughts and opinions of those living in the area, there is no trust in the project.
It is evident within Hamilton that homelessness is an issue, and the 192 promised temporary indoor shelters are not only crucial in addressing the encampment problem but long overdue. I find the lack of thoughtful action extremely disheartening. I have seen a rise in homeless individuals suffering the effects of extreme weather in just the three years I have lived in Hamilton.
A project this important in the development of the community cannot be rushed and needs to include the community. Public forums are not enough, we need councillors who can stand behind their plan and confidently address our concerns.
Emily Kozak, Hamilton
Cartoon disappointing, disgusting
Re: Oct. 25 editorial cartoon
I generally enjoy Graeme MacKay's political cartoons, however, I found this one disappointing and, frankly, disgusting. I have seen the slogan depicted on the back benchers' shirts far too often and really wish that the supposed adults who insist on displaying it would grow some brains and decency.
Bill Millar, Stoney Creek
Wrong call on choice of imagery
Many of us are completely exhausted by Justin Trudeau's presence and he needs to go on the fastest rocket out, however, that was a disgusting attempt at humour by Graeme MacKay. Highlighting this perceived profanity comment is just so wrong and offensive!
The vulgarity doesn't need to be displayed to make a point of what Liberal backbenchers might think. Paper bags on the heads, sitting with backs turned would have been just as effective.
Gordon Franklin, Clinton
Schools no place for hatred
Re: Westmount high school vandalized with 'violent' and 'antisemitic' graffiti, Oct. 24
Hamilton has always been a safe place for every kind of culture and for communities as well. I was very saddened to hear about the vandalism at Westmount school.
As an Ahmadi Muslim, I believe that hatred can never bring peace, and our community motto is "Love for all, hatred for none." By harming the country's schools and writing hateful words, peace cannot be created, instead it will result in spreading even more hatred and restlessness.
Our schools are nurturing the new generation and our new generation should stay away from all these things filled with hatred.
I hope that the issue of Palestine and Israel is resolved soon because as result, our country and our children are also being affected.
Khalida Iffat, Hamilton
CAPTION: Coryn Urquhart (as Brendan), left, and Michael Mavin (as Liam) in "A Question of Justice."Instead of simply responding to existing demand, bike lanes aim to encourage a shift toward cycling, fostering healthier and more sustainable urban environments, a reader argues.

Document 263 of 500

Giving money to millionaires: This is how critics should be attacking Doug Ford's $200 'rebate' scheme

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/giving-money-millionaires-this-is-how-critics/docview/3121581282/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Giving+money+to+millionaires%3A+This+is+how+critics+should+be+attacking+Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+%27rebate%27+scheme&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Love him or hate him, Doug Ford knows a thing or two about politics, including how to lay a trap for his critics.
The most recent example is his scheme to give every Ontarian $200 to help them deal with cost-of-living issues. Although not yet officially announced, Ford has done all but confirm that the payout is going to be the centrepiece of his upcoming fall economic statement.
The chattering classes have already gone nuts.
"Give money directly to Ontarians. How dare they? Don't they understand that there are more important things the government could be spending these dollars on?"
And on cue, opposition politicians and pundits have been trotting out a long list of areas where these government dollars could be better spent from health care to social assistance to tax cuts.
Be prepared for that list to grow longer once the rebate is officially announced.
Despite agreeing with this argument, I doubt that it will succeed.
I spent years in elected life, knocked on tens of thousands of doors and met with thousands of constituents and what I learned is that many people who are struggling don't believe that government can accomplish much.
When it comes to things like health care, no matter how much money the government puts into the system, nothing seems to get better. You still can't find a family doctor and need to sit for hours in emergency rooms. They may like the sound of future tax cuts, but are skeptical that it is going to actually translate into more dollars in their paycheque.
They want to see something they can touch and feel, and $200 bucks isn't a bad start. It may not be a huge thing, but it might help buy some groceries or put gas in a car.
So how do you criticize Ford's cash bonanza in a way that resonates with people who don't have much faith in government and are feeling the pinch?
Talk about fairness.
Part of the reason many Ontarians don't like government is that it always seems to be helping the well off. Rich people seem to get all the tax loopholes and business subsidies. It was the extremely wealthy who were going to benefit from the Greenbelt scandal.
Which leads to the flaw in Ford's rebate scheme - he is apparently going to give money to every single Ontarian which presumably includes the rich and famous.
If I were an opposition politician, I would immediately drop all talk of how this money could be better spent elsewhere and remind Ontarians that Ford is giving money to millionaires.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner seems to have realized this by pointing out that Galen Weston will be getting $200.
Galen Weston! What a brilliant example. The idea that the head of Loblaws, the company that fixed the price of bread and is making billions selling eye-wateringly expensive groceries is going to get $200 of your hard-earned tax dollars is enough to send many people into a frenzy.
Put it on a billboard. Film a commercial.
I doubt that's what's going to happen. Although the millionaire argument will be made it will be lost in rambling word salads about the underfunding of public services that many people will tune out as they cash their $200 cheque.
Maybe it's a sad testament to his record, but Ford understands that many people don't trust government. Although over the long-term his critics may want to change that mindset, when it comes to the $200 rebate, focusing solely on fairness might help them beat Ford at his own game.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford understands that many people don't trust government, which is why a $200 cheque for everyone will likely solidify his support, John Milloy writes.
CREDIT: John Milloy

Subject: Tax cuts; Wealth

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax cuts Wealth

People: Weston, Galen

Document 264 of 500

The real reason you can't trust politicians to be in charge of their own salaries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/real-reason-you-cant-trust-politicians-be-charge/docview/3121581279/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+real+reason+you+can%27t+trust+politicians+to+be+in+charge+of+their+own+salaries&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You cannot trust politicians with approving the size of their own paycheques.
This seems kind of obvious to everyone. But, in my experience, it isn't true for the reasons most people think. We think they'll line their own pockets by overpaying themselves.
In reality, they usually underpay themselves.
On Monday, the Star's city hall bureau chief Ben Spurr wrote about a staff report he obtained claiming Toronto city councillors were drastically underpaid, which was quietly buried by Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson in August 2023.
According to that report, city councillors in Toronto represented the most constituents of any in Canada, yet were paid less than representatives in many comparable cities, especially those close by. They made at least $24,000 per year less than councillors in Markham, Vaughan, Mississauga and Brampton. Toronto councillors were paid on average $1.06 per constituent they represent, while those in Markham were paid $5.27 per constituent.
The last time city councillors gave themselves a raise other than automatic inflationary adjustments was 2006. In 2021, they voted against giving themselves a raise despite a report from city staff recommending one.
Up the road at Queen's Park, MPPs haven't seen a raise since 2008, as the Star's Robert Benzie reported early this year. Without even inflationary increases, they earn roughly $12,000 per year less than Toronto city councillors (and $78,000 per year less than federal MPs). And yet, despite some unnamed insider grumbling and a report urging a raise from the provincial integrity commissioner, Premier Doug Ford has no plans to increase salaries.
If you understand politics at all, it isn't hard to understand why this is the case. Chow was preparing to put forward a big tax increase and suggest spending increases on a bunch of things, and didn't need the political noise of people suggesting she and her colleagues were in it to make themselves rich. Ford has always been a "stop the gravy train" advocate of low taxes, who would be naturally averse to anything that looks like a benefit for government leaders (his brother, famously, paid his office expenses out of his own pocket at city hall).
Politically, it is never a good time for elected representatives of any political stripe to be seen giving themselves payments or perks. Even in Ottawa, where federal politicians are paid comparatively better, past scandals over expense-account orange juice and hotel rooms have created an atmosphere where successive prime ministers have left the official residence to rot in place rather than approve any spending to maintain it in a state suitable for human habitation.
And so, in the absence of any independent outside authority setting elected officials' pay, they don't raise it.
Many people will think that's fine. Good, even. Saving taxpayers money, and all that. Especially since they aren't exactly paid poverty wages: at $128,347 per year for Toronto city councillors and $116,550 for MPPs, both jobs pay better than the average family income in Canada.
They are important jobs. And in the world of professionals and executives, these aren't attractive salaries. It's reasonable to think that they severely limit the pool of candidates to those who are either wealthy enough as to not care about the salary (say, being born into wealth like the Ford brothers) or those who could not get an executive-level job anywhere else. There's no job security for MPPs and city councillors, and no pension. Applicants often have to take months off unpaid from other jobs to obtain them.
It is entirely reasonable to expect people won't seek public office to enrich themselves. It is less reasonable to expect that qualified management- and professional-level people who might be good candidates need to sacrifice the possibility of, say, qualifying for a Toronto mortgage, in order to do the job.
Elected officials shouldn't finance a lavish lifestyle on the public dime. But they ought to earn a competitive upper-middle-class salary that reflects the importance and difficulty of the job they do. No private sector company would expect to attract great candidates if they insisted on paying executives significantly less than they'd earn in comparable work elsewhere - even if they justified the decision by claiming to want employees who felt a sense of mission.
That logic is unlikely to change anything. No active elected official publicly quoted in the Star's recent stories advocated for a raise. After all, even if they'd secretly appreciate the money, they're already doing the job for the pay offered now. It's those who declined to run based on economics whose voices are missing. Maybe some of those are ones we'd benefit from hearing.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow and city manager Paul Johnson shelved a report that recommended giving Toronto councillors a raise.

Subject: Politicians; Public officials; Wages & salaries; Executives

Business indexing term: Subject: Wages & salaries Executives

Location: Canada

Title: The real reason you can't trust politicians to be in charge of their own salaries

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 29, 2024

Section: opinion/columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 265 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3121512725/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bike lanes
are the future
Re: Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones, Oct. 21
The provincial government is shooting itself in the foot through its recently tabled bill that will give omnipotent power in the building of Highway 413, along with removal and review of existing and future bike lanes.
Most Ontarians living in the GTHA struggle with commuting and traffic. What our cities need right now is more connectivity and more options for people to get to the places they need. Bike lanes consume little space and are a safe and environmentally friendly way for commuters to travel.
I agree with the points made by the Conservative government; our cities need less traffic and gridlock. An effective way to start is by reducing the dependency on heavy, bulky, and mostly single-user motor vehicles on the road - also known as cars. Having grown up in St. Catharines, I know how badly we are lagging behind in cycling infrastructure when compared to cities like Hamilton. This legislation will only slow down progress.
As a young civil engineering student, I envision a future for Ontario where our public and active transit systems are the primary method of travel, and infrastructure is built to promote safety, health and prosperity of Ontarians.
Why are we investing billions of dollars into removing bike lanes that give commuters the ability to travel safely, and building highways that will destroy Ontario's precious farmland and ecosystems while creating urban sprawl?
The Ontario government needs to invest in reliable forms of public and active transportation that connect and serve citizens' needs.
Vlad Brundula-Ilija,
Hamilton
Reduce parking
to free up space
A question for Premier Doug Ford and his minister of transportation: If gridlock is such a problem in our cities, why don't you tackle the biggest problem, aside from too many vehicles, and remove on-street parking?
Park in your garage, driveway or in a private or public parking lot. Perhaps work with local businesses that have excess parking to allow for people to utilize their lots during off peak hours to park overnight. In most if not all municipalities, parking on municipal streets do have time limits, but are rarely enforced.
If removing on-street parking never crossed your mind, then Bill 212 was never about gridlock in the first place. Perhaps it's just a personal vendetta against Toronto and cyclists in general that all municipalities will pay for?
Erin Shacklette, Hamilton
Bike lanes get people out of cars
Re: Ontario to give itself veto power over city bike lanes, citing worsening gridlock, Oct. 15
In her article, Allison Jones discusses the province's new requirement for municipalities to seek permission before adding bike lanes. While the article remains neutral, it under-represents the perspectives of cyclists and urbanists.
While some see this legislation as a victory, it raises significant concerns about prioritizing cars in urban planning.
The convenience of personal vehicles often overshadows their negative impact on urban livability. Research shows that cars are inefficient in dense settings; for instance, traffic congestion costs the Canadian economy billions each year in lost productivity and wasted fuel.
This inefficiency is compounded by our reliance on automobiles, which contribute to significant air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions.
Though bike lanes may seem to contribute to gridlock, they are not the root of the problem. The real issue lies in the design of our streets, which often prioritizes vehicles over sustainable transport options.
Instead of simply responding to existing demand, bike lanes aim to encourage a shift toward cycling, fostering healthier and more sustainable urban environments. This complex issue warrants deeper exploration.
I highly encourage The Hamilton Spectator to speak with environmentalists and urban planners to better inform readers about the consequences of car dependency and the benefits of investing in cycling infrastructure for a more sustainable future.
Oscar Gauthier, Hamilton
Bike lanes are a
misguided priority
As much as the city stresses on providing the population with incentives to use active transportation, bike lanes deployed in Hamilton are not helpful.
Proponents of bike lanes claim that they relieve traffic congestion, but they decrease general lanes and thereby leads to congestion. This is most irritating in the areas where the lanes occupy half the road as can be evidenced by the circumstances surrounding the downtown.
The worst part is these lanes are going to become unusable during the coming winter months when cycling is far less practical. These lanes are not used for a larger part of the year due to the severe winters in Hamilton, but still cause interference with other traffic and difficulty in snow management.
Furthermore, from what has been said about the city's expansion on bike-only lanes seems to miss the point that most people use their cars to make their commutes, and not bicycles. This is where, rather than helping to ease congestion through channels off-loading many lanes, (bike lanes) only result in the formation of traffic choke points and longer, more stressful journeys.
I would like city planners to expand their thinking on the size of this project and focus on measures that would help most people in Hamilton. However, cycling has its importance, but this (is) at the cost of having bike lanes crossing over the efficient and accessible road network.
Luca Zotti, Hamilton
Keep the speed bumps coming
Speed bumps have recently been installed on Old Ancaster Road. I am very pleased because drivers need to slow down on this stretch of road.
The posted speed limit is 40 km/h. Despite this, motorists travel 60-70 km/h. There is a three-way stop at Pleasant Avenue and the Old Ancaster Road, where 90 per cent of motorists do not fully stop. They slow down and simply roll through, never once putting on the brakes.
On the road between Dundas and Ancaster the average speed is 60-70 km/h on a road where the limit is 40 km/h. It is posted at this speed because it is a narrow, twisty road and not meant to be driven fast. I have been passed four times on this road by very aggressive and angry drivers simply because I was driving the speed limit.
It is time motorists started obeying the traffic laws. Keep the speed bumps coming or do whatever it takes to slow down motorists.
Patricia Muir, Dundas
Local play
was outstanding
Re: Hamilton playwright Peter Rosser's 'A Question of Justice' is risk-taking theatre, Oct. 23
After reading Gary Smith's review of local playwright Peter Rosser's "A Question of Justice" now being performed at The Player' Guild of Hamilton, my wife and I were prepared to endure a tedious, boring long-winded production. Despite Smith's quibbles about the play, we thoroughly enjoyed it. We found it thought-provoking, captivating and it dealt with real emotional issues.
It ranks as an outstanding drama in our estimation. Go see it. Judge for yourself.
Dr. Peter Banting, Hamilton
Play tackles
important issue
Today I saw the play, "A Question of Justice" at The Players Guild, written by Peter Rosser. It was thought provoking, with its theme - a very important "real" and ongoing issue - performed in a most believable way with main leads giving powerful performances.
All the actors were great! The set was "minimalistic" in comparison to the gravity of what we were hearing, probably intentionally, as not to deter from the importance of the theme and message: Drunk driving kills and lives are destroyed forever!
When I was 19 years old in Toronto, our 22-year-old friend Tommy, who had just become engaged, was killed by a drunk driver. I've never forgotten him. You probably know of someone in your lives too.
What can each of us do to prevent these tragedies?
Some plays do require lots of colour and fancy backgrounds to transform the empty stage into fantasy land. But this play wasn't about fantasy! It was about reality, so the simple furniture and props were there to be seen and used, but the main characters were the focus. The plot and the reality of it all was the focus. Well done cast and crew! We certainly have lots to think about. Five stars!
Hope Leon, Hamilton
More good than harm expected
Re: Neighbours up in arms over Hamilton's plan for cabin shelters on Barton site, Oct. 23
With winter quickly approaching in Hamilton, the homelessness crisis is an extremely urgent issue and emergency.
Residents see this proposed development of cabinlike shelters for the homeless as a "blatant disregard for the personal experiences of the residents," but this issue should be seen as a blatant disregard for the personal experiences of the homeless. They are humans, too.
One in 10 of us humans in Canada experience some form of homelessness in their life. For this reason, we need to work together to support each other. I notice an increasing amount of homeless individuals each year in Hamilton and I can only imagine the worry of winter instilled in them all.
I understand the residents' concerns and see where they are coming from, but I believe this development will do more good than harm. Actually, the residents may even benefit from this development. There is already a large encampment in the neighbouring lot that is unmonitored and now it can be monitored by the city, actually making it "safer" for everyone.
Also, the development conditions include that tents will not be allowed within one kilometre of the site under the city's encampment protocols. This includes some of the city's largest encampments like Pier 4 and Bayfront Park, benefiting the city's housed residents by reclaiming their recreational green space, while also providing unhoused individuals with a warm, roofed space to live. With support in place, I believe this development is a viable temporary solution causing limited harm.
Camryn Bowler, Hamilton
Changes to booze sales a mess
Re: Ford in an LCBO mess of his own brewing and "Bottle returns plan in need of sober second thought, Oct. 28
With the mess that Premier Doug Ford has created selling liquor in convenience stores, I think the only "convenient" solution to this dilemma is for individuals to drop off their empties in an LCBO paper bag at their local Conservative MPP office. I'm sure an efficient and speedy response will be forthcoming from the premier's office!
Chris Cardey, Hamilton
There's no need for paper bags
Regarding the article regarding Doug Ford bringing back paper bags in liquor stores. It's a no-brainer, bring your own cloth bags, something we already do for every other store we shop in! It is very seldom that I see "bare bottles in public," as stated by Mr. Ford.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Cartoon was
disappointing
Re:Oct. 25 editorial cartoon
I generally enjoy Graeme MacKay's political cartoons, but I found this one disappointing and, frankly, disgusting. I have seen the slogan depicted on the back benchers' shirts far too often and really wish that the supposed adults who insist on displaying it would grow some brains and decency.
Bill Millar, Stoney Creek
Wrong call on choice of imagery
Many of us are completely exhausted by Justin Trudeau's presence and he needs to go on the fastest rocket out. That was, however, a disgusting attempt at humour by Graeme MacKay. Highlighting this perceived profanity comment is just so wrong and offensive! The vulgarity doesn't need to be displayed to make a point of what Liberal backbenchers might think. Paper bags on the heads, sitting with backs turned would have been just as effective.
Gordon Franklin, Clinton
Schools no place for hatred
Re: Westmount high school vandalized with 'violent' and 'antisemitic' graffiti, Oct. 24
Hamilton has always been a safe place for every kind of culture and for communities as well. I was very saddened to hear about the vandalism at Westmount school.
As an Ahmadi Muslim, I believe that hatred can never bring peace, and our community motto is "Love for all, hatred for none." By harming the country's schools and writing hateful words, peace cannot be created, instead it will result in spreading even more hatred and restlessness.
Our schools are nurturing the new generation and our new generation should stay away from all these things filled with hatred. I hope that the issue of Palestine and Israel is resolved soon because as result, our country and our children are also being affected.
Khalida Iffat, Hamilton

Document 266 of 500

'Rebate' critics should focus on fairness

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rebate-critics-should-focus-on-fairness/docview/3121512307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Rebate%27+critics+should+focus+on+fairness&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Love him or hate him, Doug Ford knows a thing or two about politics, including how to lay a trap for his critics.
The most recent example is his scheme to give every Ontarian $200 to help them deal with cost-of-living issues. Although not yet officially announced, Ford has done all but confirm that the payout is going to be the centrepiece of his upcoming fall economic statement.
The chattering classes have already gone nuts.
"Give money directly to Ontarians. How dare they? Don't they understand that there are more important things the government could be spending these dollars on?"
And on cue, opposition politicians and pundits have been trotting out a long list of areas where these government dollars could be better spent from health care to social assistance to tax cuts.
Be prepared for that list to grow longer once the rebate is officially announced.
Despite agreeing with this argument, I doubt that it will succeed.
I spent years in elected life, knocked on tens of thousands of doors and met with thousands of constituents and what I learned is that many people who are struggling don't believe that government can accomplish much.
When it comes to things like health care, no matter how much money the government puts into the system, nothing seems to get better. You still can't find a family doctor and need to sit for hours in emergency rooms. They may like the sound of future tax cuts, but are skeptical that it is going to actually translate into more dollars in their paycheque.
They want to see something they can touch and feel, and $200 bucks isn't a bad start. It may not be a huge thing, but it might help buy some groceries or put gas in a car.
So how do you criticize Ford's cash bonanza in a way that resonates with people who don't have much faith in government and are feeling the pinch?
Talk about fairness.
Part of the reason many Ontarians don't like government is that it always seems to be helping the well off. Rich people seem to get all the tax loopholes and business subsidies. It was the extremely wealthy who were going to benefit from the Greenbelt scandal.
Which leads to the flaw in Ford's rebate scheme - he is apparently going to give money to every single Ontarian, which presumably includes the rich and famous.
If I were an opposition politician, I would immediately drop all talk of how this money could be better spent elsewhere and remind Ontarians that Ford is giving money to millionaires.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner seems to have realized this by pointing out that Galen Weston will be getting $200.
Galen Weston! What a brilliant example. The idea that the head of Loblaw, the company that fixed the price of bread and is making billions selling eye-wateringly expensive groceries, is going to get $200 of your hard-earned tax dollars is enough to send many people into a frenzy.
Put it on a billboard. Film a commercial.
I doubt that's what's going to happen. Although the millionaire argument will be made it will be lost in rambling word salads about the underfunding of public services that many people will tune out as they cash their $200 cheque.
Maybe it's a sad testament to his record, but Ford understands that many people don't trust government. Although over the long-term his critics may want to change that mindset, when it comes to the $200 rebate, focusing solely on fairness might help them beat Ford at his own game.
John Milloy, a former Liberal MPP
and cabinet minister, is the director
of the Centre for Public Ethics at
Martin Luther University College.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: John Milloy John Milloy, a former Liberal MPP and cabinet minister, is the director of the Centre for Public Ethics at Martin Luther University College.;

Document 267 of 500

'I need him to be onside:' Why Oliva Chow has been so frustrated with Doug Ford these days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-need-him-be-onside-why-oliva-chow-has-been-so/docview/3121719704/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+need+him+to+be+onside%3A%27+Why+Oliva+Chow+has+been+so+frustrated+with+Doug+Ford+these+days&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Olivia Chow is all about empathy with an adversary.
Except when she's talking tough with Premier Doug Ford.
The mayor initially tried building bridges after winning power. Now she's watching Ford burning bridges by rolling back municipal bike lanes.
Why is the premier so feisty lately? And the mayor so frustrated?
Blame democracy, for better or for worse.
According to Chow, the fault lines lie with that tried and true democratic tactic known as electioneering.
"He has an election that he's facing," Chow told the Democracy Forum that I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University.
Which means the premier is exploiting wedge issues that pit cyclists against car commuters. While squeezing the mayor on her own municipal turf.
That's the paradox of power in our democratic system: You run against your opponents, then you try to work together with your counterparts, but it's back to the barricades at election time.
"Having said that, I need him to be onside" - not just on bicycles but transit, drug treatment, mental health and housing, Chow mused. "Those are big things ... It's based on hope, but I know we can do it."
Chow's argument is that she has no choice but to find a way. The premier has most of the power.
Finding a middle way worked a year ago, when she found common ground with Ford shortly after becoming mayor. She pointed out that they'd both experienced loss - his late brother Rob, the former mayor, and her late husband Jack Layton, who worked with him on city council.
Back then, they built an "empathetic bridge," as Chow puts it. That term is borrowed from one of her political mentors, Marshall Ganz, who teaches leadership at Harvard University's Kennedy School of Government and joined her onstage at TMU last week.
"The empathetic bridge actually resulted in a lot of agreement on how we could work together," she told the audience. "One thing about Marshall's teaching is that if we connect through experience and not always fight each other on opinions ... find the core value, and then you can move ahead together."
Chow and Ford bridged the gap at first - reaching an unprecedented agreement to shore up the city's finances. Ultimately, though, it was a bridge too far for bicycle lanes, because the premier now sees them as a path to victory on the campaign trail ahead.
Ganz, whose organizing techniques have inspired generations of political activists, said he's proud of Chow, who studied his methods after she lost her first mayoral campaign in 2014. How's she doing, a decade later?
"I've got a pretty simple answer - she won," he quipped.
More seriously, power is the prerequisite to action. It's a necessary, but not sufficient condition - for if you don't lay the groundwork for substantive change, power changes little.
"Winning the thing is part of it. But coming out of it stronger is even more important. And that's what brings it back to organizing, and brings it back to developing leadership."
Ganz, who worked on Barack Obama's campaigns, said the former U.S. president didn't do enough in power to create lasting change: "We had a chance to do that in the Obama campaign ... Unfortunately, he didn't take advantage of it."
He argues for an alternative approach, both in his teachings at Harvard and in his new book, "People, Power, Change: Organizing for Democratic Renewal." It stresses grassroots organizing and people power over the power of money politics, cautioning against the "philanthropic colonization of civil society."
In a democracy, there's a big difference between winning power and wielding power, between grassroots organizing and governing, between transformational politics and transactional governing. Which is why the mayor said she invited Ganz to talk about his teachings and readings in a presentation to senior city hall staff last week.
Chow recounted how he challenged the staff: "Why are you a public servant? Why do you serve? Why are you working as a planner, a transportation manager, a communicator?"
Good questions. One question the mayor herself struggled with at our Democracy Forum, however - and for which she remains answerable and accountable - is the housing crisis in Toronto.
More than a year after she won power, the city has just conceded it doesn't have nearly enough emergency shelter space to get people off the streets. While Chow has publicly acknowledged that public parks are no place for the unhoused, she rattled off a list of policy possibilities for the future.
But her long answer on long-term solutions - which gave short shrift to the short-term crisis - prompted grumbling from an otherwise supportive audience hungry for action from the activist in chief. That, too, is democracy.
* * * *
Speaking of organizing, I'll be hosting three of Canada's top labour leaders - Unifor's Lana Payne, the CLC's Bea Bruske and OPSEU's JP Hornick - at the TMU Democracy Forum on Oct. 30, talking about "labour's new leadership - unions and the future of work." Free for all.
CAPTION: People, Power and Change, with Marshall Ganz and Mayor Olivia ChowStar columnist Martin Regg Cohn, left, speaks with Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, centre, and Marshall Ganz, who teaches leadership and organizing at the Harvard Kennedy School, and the Toronto Metropolitan University Democracy Forum on Oct. 25, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Local elections; Grass roots movement; Mayors; Democracy; Bicycling

Document 268 of 500

Critical shortage of medical supplies has Ontario Liberals demanding auditor general probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/critical-shortage-medical-supplies-has-ontario/docview/3121719389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Critical+shortage+of+medical+supplies+has+Ontario+Liberals+demanding+auditor+general+probe&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberals are calling on Ontario's auditor general to investigate a critical shortage in medical supplies that has left vulnerable home-care patients - some in their final days - without pain medications and other key items.
"I'm still hearing the same horror stories that we've all been hearing for the last couple weeks," MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician, said Monday.
Those problems include shortages of drugs, needles and other equipment that in some cases has prompted doctors to send palliative care patients to hospital emergency rooms in their final days. As well, cancer patients have been left without IV bags and needles. Others do not have bandages for wound care after surgeries.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones has said the troubles are "unacceptable" and that ministry staff are working with suppliers of the new Ontario Health atHome agency to fix them.
Her parliamentary assistant Anthony Leardi (Essex) said Monday that there are "special assistance teams" trying to get supplies to patients and reiterated anyone purchasing supplies out of their own pockets can be reimbursed.
But complaints continue to roll in to MPPs' offices, New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said in backing the Liberal push for an investigation.
"They are still not seeing supplies. Something's still not working," she told the legislature's daily question period.
Shamji's letter to auditor general Shelley Spence requests a value-for-money audit into Ontario Health atHome's ability to procure and deliver supplies.
The agency took over the functions last year under legislation passed by Premier Doug Ford's government to change the system, making a handful of suppliers and providers responsible for delivering across the province. Previously, local pharmacies and other suppliers focused on servicing smaller geographic areas.
Shamji said he wants to know how the new suppliers were chosen and whether any political connections with the governing Progressive Conservatives were a factor.
"Ontarians need to understand why this catastrophe has occurred, how it can be stopped, who will be held accountable and the manner in which it will be prevented in the future," he wrote in a detailed three-page letter.
"As I write, countless Ontarians do not know if their scheduled home-care appointment will be honoured and, if so, whether the provider will have the necessary medications and supplies."
Spence will deliver her annual report by early December, so it is too late to include any probe into Ontario Health atHome in it. But that does not preclude her auditing teams from investigating if the office determines that's warranted.
"When selecting and planning our audits, we carefully consider all information available to us, including information that has been sent to us by members of provincial parliament and the public," auditor general spokesperson Becky Fond said after Shamji's request was received.
Liberal MPP John Fraser (Ottawa South), who recalled how he and his sisters cared for their elderly parents at home in their final days, said he could not imagine the suffering and frustration at doing it without proper supplies.
"It should make all of us angry," he said, challenging Premier Doug Ford to support the call for an investigation "if he is truly a man of his word."
On Friday, Ford told reporters "they had to pull me off the ceiling" when he first heard reports of the home-care supply problem.
"I actually get calls on my phone saying, 'I need gauze, I need Band Aids.' But people are going to be held accountable, as sure as I'm talking to you."
CAPTION: Liberal MPP Adil Shamji has sent a letter to Ontario's auditor general requesting a value-for-money audit into Ontario Health atHome's ability to procure and deliver supplies.

Document 269 of 500

Pierre Poilievre would replace Liberal housing programs with tax cut on new homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-would-replace-liberal-housing/docview/3121512840/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+would+replace+Liberal+housing+programs+with+tax+cut+on+new+homes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A future Conservative government would scrap two multi-billion-dollar federal housing programs the party argues have failed, to help pay for a new tax cut that could cost $4 billion per year and is designed to boost home construction.
Staging a campaign-style announcement in the suburbs of Ottawa on Monday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said he would cut the five-per-cent federal sales tax on newly-built homes that sell for less than $1 million, and that this would "spark" construction of an additional 30,000 homes per year.
"Common-sense Conservatives will stop giving the money to bureaucrats (and) instead leave it in the pockets of homebuilders and home buyers," Poilievre said.
Several housing experts welcomed the promised tax cut, and the Ford government at Queen's Park signalled a desire to match it. Meanwhile, the federal Liberal housing minister accused the Conservatives of wanting to slash programs that are "making it easier to build homes," and suggested it would be better to cater new policies to lower-income Canadians instead of delivering a broad-based tax cut that he suggested could benefit wealthy property owners.
"He's talking about cutting the programs that are actually going to deliver housing for middle class and low-income families," Housing Minister Sean Fraser said.
Poilievre predicted his promised tax cut would cost about $16 billion in lost federal revenue over the next four years, but claimed slashing government "bureaucracy" and increased economic activity from new home construction would make up the shortfall.
"This includes about ... $16 billion in additional revenue and lower costs to the taxpayer, which is about the cost of axing the sales tax on homebuilding over the next four years," Poilievre said.
Poilievre said the Conservatives could find $3 billion in unspent money by cancelling the existing $4.4-billion Housing Accelerator Fund, which the Tory leader argued has failed to build homes despite the Liberals' claims that the program will help construct 750,000 units through deals with municipalities across the country.
Another $5 billion would come from scrapping the government's Housing Infrastructure Fund, Poilievre said. That program, announced this spring and slated for launch next year, is meant to finance construction and upgrades to the infrastructure, like sewer and water systems, required for new home construction.
On top of that, Poilievre claimed the tax cut would generate government revenues through increased economic activity around homebuilding worth about $2 billion per year.
Asked about Poilievre's claim the two Liberal housing programs are failures, Fraser shot back that the accelerator fund - through more than 170 deals with the federal government - has helped prompt municipalities to rezone areas for greater housing density and cut red tape by adopting digital permits. Fraser's office confirmed Monday that $1 billion of the fund has been distributed, and the remaining more-than $3 billion has been committed through these deals.
"If you want to go back to municipalities and pull that money out, that would make no sense," Fraser said.
At Queen's Park, sources close to Premier Doug Ford said the Progressive Conservative government would "love" to match Poilievre's tax cut and remove the eight per cent provincial portion of the harmonized sales tax from new homes under $1 million.
Insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss deliberations with the federal Liberal government, said the provincial Tories have broached that change with Ottawa in the past without success.
That's because the Trudeau government prefers to earmark money toward programs like the housing accelerator fund that result in building affordable rental units as opposed to homes for purchase, sources said.
Fraser said Monday that the Conservatives were perhaps inspired by the federal government's decision last year to slash the federal sales tax on new rental apartment construction, but said Poilievre's new tax cut could give benefits to the "investor class" of property owners.
While several housing industry players on Monday said they were awaiting more details of the Conservatives' proposal, such as projected uptake, the vow was broadly welcomed.
The federal government already has a sales tax rebate for the sale of new homes priced under $450,000, a policy that was introduced in 1991. That rebate returns some of the tax paid, but not all as the Conservatives are now promising.
But Richard Lyall, president of the Residential Construction Council of Ontario, said the rebate has lost its effect over the years, in part because it was "never adjusted for inflation, as was supposed to have been the case."
Housing policy expert Steve Pomeroy, a senior research fellow at Carleton University's Centre for Urban Research and Education, said "adjusting the index is good tax policy," noting how housing costs have grown over the decades since the rebate was introduced.
In a city like Toronto, or even in the surrounding 905 region, buyers looking for detached houses are still "realistically never going to qualify," said Building Industry and Land Development Association (BILD) executive Justin Sherwood.
But he sees Poilievre's tax cut as offering some relief to buyers of new condos in Toronto, as well as some stacked townhomes and other mid-sized offerings within the broader 905. BILD has been among development groups calling for such a change in recent years, added association president David Wilkes.
"Our position has been that it needed to be increased to a million dollars," he said. "In the GTA, nothing is being sold that's $350,000 or lower. Right now, no one qualifies for the current rebate."
David Amborski, director of Toronto Metropolitan University's Centre for Urban Research and Land Development and a member of the Ontario government's recent housing affordability task force, was pleased to see the federal Conservatives turning an eye to the taxes and fees applied to new housing, which he sees as important to tackle unaffordability. He, too, sees the policy's reach as somewhat limited, but welcomed the promise.
"I'm not sure it's going to make a monumental difference - but I'd expect it to have some impact for people on the margins," he said.
Such a policy would also likely stimulate construction at a time when development has slowed, added University of Toronto Chair in Housing David Hulchanski - though he is skeptical of increased subsidies for home purchases, arguing that governments should more squarely focus on those with the most pressing housing need, including developing policies aimed at renters and lower-income households.
"There is nothing similar in terms of the value of home ownership tax system subsidies, the already more than $10 billion annually, available for Canadian households who are renters and may never be able to purchase a house," Hulchanski said Monday.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre leaves a press conference in Ottawa on Oct. 28, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Construction; Tax cuts; Sales taxes; Land development; Subsidies; Economic activity

Document 270 of 500

Union demands transparency for Stelco sale: 'It was basically a secret document'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/union-demands-transparency-stelco-sale-was/docview/3121266988/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Union+demands+transparency+for+Stelco+sale%3A+%27It+was+basically+a+secret+document%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Canada signs off on a proposed sale of Stelco, the government should "make public" any legally enforceable jobs, investment or production conditions set on the sale, says the United Steelworkers union.
U.S. steelmaking giant Cleveland-Cliffs announced in July it had reached an agreement to buy its Hamilton-based Stelco rival in a deal worth $3.85 billion.
Since then, the sale has cleared several hurdles, including a vote of support from Stelco shareholders in September and more recent thumbs-up from the U.S. Justice Department and Canadian Competition Bureau.
But Canada is still conducting a "net benefit" review of the proposed sale under the Investment Canada Act, federal officials confirmed.
Steelport art installation a nod to Hamilton's industrial past and future
In theory, that review process allows the government to set conditions on - or in the alternative, quash - the sale to ensure an "overall net benefit" to the country of any proposed major foreign takeover.
The United Steelworkers are not opposing the sale, said Canadian union president Marty Warren in an interview, noting the would-be new owners are "saying all the right things" about preserving jobs and production at Stelco locations in both Hamilton and Nanticoke.
But he noted the last time Canada approved the sale of Stelco to a foreign buyer - back in 2007, to U.S. Steel - the legal obligations agreed to under the act were kept largely confidential. "It was basically a secret document."
Workers then "went through hell" as U.S. Steel subsequently reneged on its public vows around jobs and production amid a recession, shut down the Hamilton location's last blast furnace and ultimately ended steelmaking at the historical Hilton Works on the bayfront.
Stelco was later rescued out of bankruptcy by Bedrock Industries founder Alan Kestenbaum, who returned the company to profitability before negotiating the current proposed sale.
Cleveland-Cliffs has not responded to Spectator requests for an interview since the sale was announced, but CEO Lourenco Goncalves appeared to speak directly to workers - and Canadian regulators - when told analysts in July that the company would preserve Stelco's name, Hamilton headquarters and unionized workforce of around 2,200.
"I want to say loud and clear, we know what you have gone through under previous ownership (by U.S. Steel) ... We are not going to disappoint you," he said. "We have committed to no layoffs."
Federal government officials have said they cannot comment on the net benefit review because of confidentiality provisions built into the act.
That's part of the problem, argued Warren, who wrote a letter to Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne late last month urging him to "make public" any legal commitments around job protections, pensions, production and environmental sustainability made by the company if the sale is approved.
"Given the lessons learned from previous takeovers ... workers remain cautious," he wrote. "We must ensure that the same mistakes are not repeated."
New Democrat MP for Hamilton Centre Matthew Green publicly backed the steelworkers' call in a statement earlier this month.
"There has to be transparency this time," the NDP labour critic said in an interview, noting more than one incoming foreign buyer has reneged on "lofty promises" after scooping up a valued Hamilton industry.
If the government approves the sale based on such promises, he said, "they should be both public and enforceable."
Theoretically, the government could also reject the proposed sale following the net benefit review.
South of the border, the proposed sale of U.S. Steel to Japanese steelmaker Nippon has spurred political outrage - including vows by two different presidential election candidates to block a foreign takeover. Cleveland-Cliffs - a recently jilted suitor for U.S. Steel - has publicly cheered the idea of the U.S. government preventing that sale.
In Canada, analysts have suggested the Stelco sale faces significantly less political opposition, in part because of the important trade relationship with the U.S. As well, the Hamilton-based steelmaker has been owned by an American company before and is currently headed by significant shareholder Kestenbaum, founder of U.S.-based Bedrock Industries.
The federal government could still set conditions on the sale and be "more transparent" about the details than in the past, suggested steel industry expert Peter Warrian, a former United Steelworkers research director and chief economist for Ontario.
Champagne made a public point in July of announcing "strict conditions" for the sale of B.C.-based Teck Resources' metallurgical coal business to a European multinational after a months-long review.
But Warrian said he would be very surprised if the government rejected the sale outright. "I think this is going through," he said.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter with The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: The federal government is reviewing the proposed sale of Hamilton's Stelco to U.S. steelmaker Cleveland-Cliffs.

Full text: British Columbia
Homes evacuated after heavy rain
The District of North Vancouver has declared a state of local emergency and ordered three homes evacuated in the Deep Cove neighbourhood over concerns debris left behind from last weekend's atmospheric river could pose a safety risk. A statement late Saturday from the district says that during the heavy rain, debris filled the channels of Ostler and Panorama Creeks "limiting their capacity to withstand future heavy rainfall events." The District of North Vancouver was hit with 350 millimetres of rain last weekend and six properties were initially ordered evacuated, but those orders were lifted on Friday. The system drenched B.C.'s south coast, triggering a mudslide and localized flooding that killed at least three people.
Ontario
Ford moves
to extend gas tax cut to June 2025
Ontario is moving to extend a 5.7 per cent cut to the provincial gas tax until next summer. Premier Doug Ford says his government will soon put forward legislation that, if passed, will keep the tax at nine cents per litre until June 30. The legislation would also extend the 5.3-cent cut to the price of diesel fuel. The province first temporarily slashed the gasoline and diesel tax rates in July 2022 but have repeatedly extended the cuts since. Ford estimates the succession of moves will help the average household save $380 over the three years since the cuts were first introduced.

Document 273 of 500

Ford in an LCBO mess of his own brewing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-lcbo-mess-his-own-brewing/docview/3121176587/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+in+an+LCBO+mess+of+his+own+brewing&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has boxed himself in on paper bags at the LCBO.
Now watch him squirm out of a trade trap of his own making. Problem is, we may all pay a price for his folly.
When Ontario's teetotalling premier first got wind that booze bags were toast at the LCBO, he rode to the rescue of the wine-swilling, beer-guzzling, spirits-imbibing masses. Huffing and puffing, the man of the people laid down the law for liquor board's tree-hugging bean counters: Bring back bags!
Ford fired off a letter telling the liquor board that people shouldn't have to carry bare bottles in public. Besides, Canadians are blessed with trees, which are a renewable resource, he reasoned.
And so the LCBO did as it was told. But by doing the right thing - getting the best price with a publicly tendered contract, untainted by corruption, free of favouritism and protectionism - the alcohol giant wrong-footed the premier a second time.
That's because it signed a deal with a Quebec supplier, Rosenbloom Groupe Inc., instead of a homegrown Ontario company.
Never mind that Quebec is still part of Canada. No matter that our neighbouring province happens to be the birthplace of Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, who oversees the LCBO.
How, Ford wondered, could the LCBO - which revels in protecting Niagara wines from out-of-province competition - not apply the same pugnacious, protectionist logic to local bag suppliers? If every winery is protected, why not our forestry?
The premier personally put pen to paper again, "directing the LCBO to also initiate a new tender process as soon as possible that will be limited to Ontario suppliers."
Bethlenfalvy dutifully told the arm's-length, Crown-owned LCBO that its due diligence was misplaced. When it comes to contracting, he ordered, don't go low - go local.
Now, if you are a protectionist then perhaps you are onside with Ford's blustering (although not the notion of making it his top priority).
Perhaps predictably, NDP Leader Marit Stiles echoed Ford's logic, arguing "our procurement should always be focused on how we're going to actually drive Ontario's economy and create jobs here."
But if you believe in lowering trade barriers between Canadian provinces in order to foster national unity and forge economic ties, then the premier's musings make no sense.
Lest Ford forget, he is the rotating chair of the Council of the Federation until next summer, which means his mission is to bring all the premiers together to promote interprovincial procurement and trade. One can only imagine the conversation when Ford convenes Quebec Premier François Legault and his counterparts in Toronto and sheepishly confesses that Quebec bags are banned.
Is our forestry industry so feeble that it needs to be propped up at any price - even at the risk of retaliation by our trading partners? The problem with protectionism is that it's a two-way street, and not just along east-west lines. Think north-south.
With the U.S. presidential election just days away, Ford told the Empire Club last week that the stakes are perilously high for this province: Two-way trade between Ontario and the United States exceeds $500 billion.
"If Ontario were a stand-alone country, we'd be America's third-largest trading partner," he mused.
That's why it's so important for all governments, "no matter the political stripe, to reject protectionism and embrace a 'Buy Can-Am' mentality."
Ford argued against the "Fortress America" mentality that American politicians talk up on the campaign trail: "We're encouraging U.S. lawmakers to think big - to build fortress North America (my italics)."
That was last week. This week it's a different story, the premier now practising the protectionism he preaches against with a double-barrelled "Buy Ontario" and "Ban Quebec" procurement policy.
By so blatantly contradicting himself, by placing paper bags at the top of his protectionist wish list, Ford has undermined his strategy to safeguard the Ontario economy from Buy American provisions.
To be sure, there may be some strategic or sensitive sectors of the economy that might be worth going to the wall for. But building a protective wall around paper bags doesn't add up.
For the record, the brown bag contract is worth $10.9 million. That's a rounding error when measured against our $500-billion trading relationship with the U.S.
It reveals a premier who runs the province by the seat of his pants and goes by his gut. Instead of using his head to think things through, he's putting a paper bag over it.
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial
politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.;
