Document 274 of 500

Bottle returns plan in need of sober second thought

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bottle-returns-plan-need-sober-second-thought/docview/3121176550/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bottle+returns+plan+in+need+of+sober+second+thought&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Oct. 31 - Halloween - all grocery stores in Ontario will ostensibly have the right to sell beer, wine and cider products.
But the strings that come along with that right have left grocers in the province wondering if this was the Ontario government's idea of a provincewide trick-or-treat program.
Ontario's independent grocers, who represent about 59 per cent of the brick-and-mortar retail grocery stores in the province, initially applauded the announcement in 2023 that the government intended to open up the sale of alcohol products to all grocery stores. This was a welcome change from the system established by the previous Liberal government who, by limiting the number of licensed retailers who could sell beer and wine, created "winners and losers" on the retail grocery landscape. That should not be the role of government.
However, when ending that system, the current government is now forcing all grocery stores that opt to sell alcohol to also accept dirty bottles and cans into their stores, creating more winners and losers. It's tantamount to a bad hangover from the previous government's incongruous approach.
Where, exactly, does this government suggest deposit returns should be dropped off in a grocery store? By the meat, produce or bakery sections?
Or perhaps by the front entrance so that the smell of stale beer wafting through the store can enhance the shopping experience for Ontario consumers.
These dirty cans and bottles will also be coming into the store often broken and accompanied by cigarette butts, discarded needles and insects. As such, the risk of cross-contamination in a food environment, particularly in a smaller store, is more pronounced.
As well, where will an independent grocer operating on margins of two per cent - half that of a retail grocery chain - find the space to sort and store these empties? What products in the store do the bureaucrats in the Ontario government recommend be taken out to make room for these bottles and cans? How will that independent make up for the revenue loss from that forfeited space? How will that small business pay for the costs of storing and sorting these soiled cans and bottles with their margins further reduced? Has the government also looked at this within the context of the existing Blue Box program, which has seen costs more than triple in the past two years?
This past summer, the Ontario government rightly squashed the concept of grocers having to take back dirty, non-alcoholic containers. How then can the same government now foist that concept on grocers when it's a dirty, alcoholic container?
The alcohol changes announced in May contained a stark illustration that the Ontario government had not grasped that the challenges and realities for smaller, family-owned independent grocers are significantly different than those faced by monolithic retail chains.
In revising provincial Alcohol and Gaming Commission licensing fees, the Ford government aligned the existing fees for the grocery chains and independents to the same level, reducing fees for the chains, but tripling them for the independents.
Independent grocers welcomed Finance Minister Bethlenfalvy reversing that miscue, but it was disconcerting that Main Street and Bay Street grocers had been lumped together in the first place.
Last year, the Competition Bureau of Canada released its long-awaited study into the retail grocery industry. One of the key recommendations coming out of that exhaustive study, was that all governments - federal, provincial and territorial - need to do more to encourage the growth of Canada's independent grocers.
How can the Ontario government reconcile its imposition of a costly and impractical deposit return system on its own independent grocers, with the findings and recommendations of that report?
Independent grocers have conveyed to the government our desire to work together to find and enhance solutions to recycling. Looking at community depots that exist in other jurisdictions, or returning empties to the LCBO - which does not sell fresh food - are but two options that come to mind.
The decision of the Ontario government to expand where beer, wine and cider can be sold should be a win-win. A win for retailers, for Ontario craft breweries and wineries - and a win for consumers wanting more convenience.
But the low number of independent grocers seeking beer and wine licences - combined with current sellers indicating they will surrender their licences in 2026 when the exemption to operate in proximity to a Beer Store ends - attests to the fact that the province needs to adjust its alcohol modernization plan.
In a refreshing departure from the political norm, Premier Ford has said, to his credit, that when his government recognizes something may not be the right decision, changes are made. Such a change is needed now.
It is hard to imagine that this is an issue the Ontario government intended to create.
Gary Sands
is senior vice-president
of the Canadian Federation of Independent Grocers.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford should rethink the decision to force independent grocery stores to accept empty alcohol containers, Gary Sands writes.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Gary Sands Gary Sands is senior vice-president of the Canadian Federation of Independent Grocers.;

Document 275 of 500

'It's going to be a free-for-all': What a Donald Trump victory means for Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/going-be-free-all-what-donald-trump-victory-means/docview/3121050765/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+going+to+be+a+free-for-all%27%3A+What+a+Donald+Trump+victory+means+for+Canada&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians, brace for impact if Donald Trump is elected president on Nov. 5.
Forget the long-standing Ottawa-Washington friendship, the trade and security alliances, the integrated economy connecting businesses with consumers on both sides of the border.
With the radical Republican candidate, nothing is sacred. Everything is renegotiable. This is the dark art of Donald Trump's deal.
Prepare instead for a frenzy at the border, tariff wars that side-swipe the Canadian economy and disputes over Canada's laggardly approach to defending North America and the world.
Prepare for outright hostility if it is Prime Minister Justin Trudeau squaring off against Trump. But don't expect substantial differences if Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre wins power.
"It's going to be a free for all, let's face it," said Lloyd Axworthy, a former Liberal immigration minister.
"I think Canada more than almost any other country will be impacted simply because so much of what we have is integrated or interdependent with the United States."
The Star spoke to policy experts to find out where Canada is most vulnerable to the impacts of a Trump presidency.
Defence
America's traditional role as a global arbiter and police force could drastically change under Trump and that could adversely affect Canada both abroad and at home.
Trump has vowed to end the Russia-Ukraine war in order to limit American spending. Owing in part to its large Ukrainian diaspora, Canada has been one of Kyiv's staunchest backers, but the NATO alliance that is contributing to Ukraine's defence has been an object of Trump's scorn.
Earlier this month, he spoke of his disregard for NATO's collective defence pact, boasting that he had told member states during his first term that if they did not pay the agreed-upon two per cent of GDP on defence spending, "I will not protect you under any form."
Canada is one of NATO's most notorious financial laggards, this year spending just 1.37 per cent of GDP on defence - 27th out of the 31 member states. At the last NATO summit, Trudeau pledged that Canada had a plan to meet the two per cent spending goal, but not until 2032.
"We're already more vulnerable on security writ large," said Kerry Buck, Canada's former ambassador to NATO. "U.S. unpredictability under Trump would make it worse for us."
The impact could occur on two fronts.
A weakened NATO could reduce Canada's global influence and the country's abilities to act internationally alongside other like-minded nations. But Trump could go further, according to his former national security adviser.
"The odds that he will withdraw from NATO are very high," John Bolton said earlier this month.
If Trump undermines NATO solidarity or its existence, Buck said, "it can take away any or all of those advantages that we have."
And Canada isn't much better positioned on the home front where it is seen as a weaker partner in the task of continental security. Buck said the argument that Canada poses a security threat to the United States is "politicking."
"But I do think there's a risk to Canada from Canada underinvesting (in military defence) and the only reason we're able to do it is because we count on the U.S. looking out for us," she said. Under a Trump presidency, "they'll be either unpredictable or unwilling to do so."
Immigration
In his first presidential term, Trump promised to build a wall along the southern border to keep irregular migrants out of the United States.
Getting tough on migrants is still among Trump's top election pledges. This time, he plans "the largest deportation operation in the history of our country" if he wins.
Such a threat inevitably turns the focus to Canada's border with the United States.
In the spring of 2017, Trump banned entry into the U.S. from seven Muslim countries and warned migrants from at-risk countries like Haiti, El Salvador and Honduras that they should prepare to be returned to their home countries.
The threats prompted thousands of people to flee to Canada through Roxham Road, a remote country route that starts in Quebec and continues on the other side of the border in New York state.
Experts predict that history could repeat itself in a second Trump term, with some changes.
"If - and I should say, maybe, when - he starts his major deportation effort, there will be a massive outpouring of people looking for an alternative," said Axworthy, who is chair of the World Refugee & Migration Council.
"If he's beginning to use the national guard and the police to enforce the deportations then all hell's going to break loose and people will be scrambling to find a place to go."
Sean Rehaag, director of York University's Centre for Refugee Studies, has little doubt those threatened with deportation will once again head north to Canada. But their journeys will be more dangerous and difficult than they were the last time Trump was in the White House.
In 2023, Trudeau and U.S. President Joe Biden agreed to amend the Safe Third Country Agreement, a deal that allows for the return of irregular migrants to the country in which they first set foot. The amendment closed a loophole that allowed those who cross into Canada from the U.S. at unofficial points, like Roxham Road, to make their asylum claim in Canada.
Rehaag said this will only increase migrants' reliance on human smugglers guiding them through more difficult-to-detect routes. One need only look to the enormous risks migrants take in crossing the Mediterranean Sea or the English Channel to get to Europe or the United Kingdom to see what could happen along the 49th parallel.
"To think that phenomenon won't happen in Canada with enough pressure in the United States is, I think, unreasonable," Rehaag said.
Ottawa will either be duty-bound or compelled by court challenges to scrap the Safe Third Country Agreement if Trump follows through on his promises
"The idea that an agreement based on the fact that both countries have a reasonably humanistic refugee program is clearly no longer going to be the case," Axworthy said.
"If Trump makes good on his promises of removing people ... then Canada can't remove people to the United States if (it) knows that by doing so they're going to be returned to a country where they'll potentially face persecution," Rehaag said. "It would violate international law to do so, and it would violate Canadian law."
Trade
It could be felt in grocery stores, at car dealerships, in the housing market and even in the arts.
If Trump wins the election, he wants to put the American economy first. And he plans to do it with tariffs intended to make it more enticing to build and hire and buy in America than to do business with foreign firms.
Economists predict Trump's tariff policies will be bad for the global economy - including the American economy. But it could be particularly disastrous for Canada, which owes much of its prosperity to its access to U.S. consumers and markets.
"Were a Trump administration to decrease U.S. reliance on trade, prioritizing domestic production and ensuring national independence of essential goods and services, Canada could expect the value of our trading relationship with the United States to decline," warned the Calgary Chamber of Commerce.
The situation could grow even graver in a situation where there are trade wars and retaliatory tariffs from other countries, particularly China.
Canadian exports could experience a COVID-19-style drop, inflation will take hold, the value of the dollar will weaken, interest rates will rise, the stock market will fall, consumer spending will slow under a "Full-Blown Trump Scenario," according to a September report by Oxford Economics, published in the magazine Policy.
"We are sitting on top of the wealthiest, most dynamic economy in the world and that's never going to change," said Brian Kingston, president and chief executive of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association, which includes Ford, General Motors and Stellantis.
Motor vehicles are Ontario's top export to the U.S. and Canada benefits from a duty-free deal under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA). But Trump says he wants to renegotiate that trade deal when it comes up for review in 2026.
His stated reasons are to prevent Chinese companies producing electric vehicles from building factories in Mexico to enter the American market, but "there's always a risk, particularly in a trilateral trade negotiation, where the measure that may not be aimed at Canada ultimately impacts us," Kingston said.
He said Ottawa needs to work diligently to resolve trade irritants between the two countries and send the signal to Washington "that we are their partner in the creation of this North American automotive supply chain."
But Canada also has some powerful ammunition, in the form of critical minerals needed to produce electric vehicle batteries.
"You cannot source these materials anywhere else in North America, and we're perfectly positioned to be that partner," Kingston said.
"It gives us a unique opportunity that we didn't have the last time we were in these (trade) discussions with the first Trump administration."
Energy and the environment
One of the early acts of office when Trump was first elected was to approve the Keystone XL pipeline, which would have sent Alberta petroleum flowing across the border and given a massive boost to oilsands producers.
This time, Trump isn't looking north to fill its energy needs.
"We will DRILL, BABY, DRILL, and we will become energy independent, and even dominant again," the Republican Party platform says. "The United States has more liquid gold under our feet than any other nation, and it's not even close."
This, plus his promised tariffs on foreign products could cause Canadian energy imports to decline.
But the bigger uncertainty for Canada is if, or how drastically, Trump plans to roll back American environmental policy, said Jennifer Winter, a professor of economics at the University of Calgary.
Trump has vowed to undo some aspects of American anti-inflation measures that have gone to fund clean energy projects while attempting to reduce the reliance on China for things like renewable energy and electric vehicle batteries. He has also vowed to weaken emissions targets and pull out of the global Paris climate accord.
The Oxford Economics report said that Trump's decisions could have various impacts on investment in Canadian clean-energy technologies, battery production or electric vehicle manufacturing, but classified the effects as a "mixed bag."
But if Trump pulls support for clean-energy initiatives, "it could make Canada relatively more attractive for green investment."
"I think it really depends on how aggressive Trump wants to be in isolating from the rest of the world on the climate file," Winter said, adding that no one has yet been able to separate the campaign rhetoric from the firm policy plans to anticipate what could be coming just months from now.
"Trump being president would create additional U.S. policy uncertainty and global policy uncertainty, and that's just bad for everyone."
CAPTION: Former U.S. President and Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump gestures as he arrives to speak during a Turning Point Action 'United for Change' campaign rally in Las Vegas, Nevada on Oct. 24, 2024.

Document 276 of 500

This is why Doug Ford's audit of child protection agencies misses the mark

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-why-doug-fords-audit-child-protection/docview/3120907236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+is+why+Doug+Ford%27s+audit+of+child+protection+agencies+misses+the+mark&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's call for an audit of Ontario's child protection agencies, prompted by concerns about youth with complex needs being housed in hotels and office buildings, misses the point.
While the situation is troubling, this audit merely repeats what the government already knows and avoids addressing the root causes.
Ontario's child protection system already operates under significant oversight. Child protection agencies face regular audits and are required to justify child removal decisions in court. The Ontario ombudsman investigates cases where families feel mistreated and is currently reviewing the practice of placing children in Airbnbs, children's aid society office buildings and hotels. The real question is whether the government will act on the ombudsman's recommendations when the review is complete.
Additionally, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) oversees these agencies by setting policies and managing funding. Agencies must sign annual accountability agreements to receive funding, but these agreements have not been updated since 2015. The ministry also conducts mandated annual reviews of children in care and requires agencies to audit their own protection cases for quality assurance, reporting back to the ministry regularly. Child protection agencies must also post performance indicators for public scrutiny, yet the government has not fully implemented all these indicators.
Beyond this, agencies must post audited financial statements and adhere to government-directed procurement policies. Oversight also comes from the Child and Family Services Review Board, which handles public complaints, and the coroner's office, which reviews child deaths and issues recommendations. With this multilayered system already in place, it's hard to see what another audit will uncover that isn't already known to ministry staff, the deputy minister and the minister.
The real issue is not a lack of oversight but a flawed, underfunded system. Ontario's child protection agencies are overburdened and under-resourced. Instead of another audit, Ford should review the entire children's services sector in Ontario, including an audit of the ministry. This would reveal inefficiencies, communication gaps and policy failures contributing to the problems Ford identifies.
The systemic issues within Ontario's child protection and mental health networks are complex. Agencies are not without fault, but it is misguided to hold them solely accountable without considering the larger framework they are forced to operate within. Ford's audit seems more like an attempt to gain public approval by appearing tough on child welfare agencies than a genuine effort to improve the system. It begs the question: what is the government doing with all the data it collects?
Ford claims to have heard of taxpayer money being misused by child protection agencies. To address these issues, he should first review his own actions. His recent $200 rebate per person, costing $3.2 billion, will divert funds from vulnerable children, the homeless, schools and hospitals.
Instead of another round of finger-pointing, it's time for the Ford government to take meaningful action to reform Ontario's child welfare system. Ford should do the right thing and provide the agencies with the resources they need right now to better serve Ontario's most vulnerable children and youth. Instead of his constant finger-pointing, he might direct the minister responsible to redesign the system with all involved at the table to discuss a pathway forward on behalf of the children, youth and families they serve.
In the end, Ford's audit will likely confirm what we already know: child protection agencies are working within a flawed system. But until the government addresses the bigger picture, including its own inefficiencies, little will change for the children and families who depend on these services.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford claims to have heard of taxpayer money being misused by child protection agencies. He should first review his own actions, like how his recent $200 rebate per person, costing $3.2 billion, will divert funds from vulnerable children, the homeless, schools and hospitals, saysRocco Gizzarelli.
CREDIT: Rocco Gizzarelli

Subject: Audited financial statements; Child welfare; Audits

Business indexing term: Subject: Audited financial statements Audits

Document 277 of 500

Medical schools going '100% Canadian': Province will not allow international students to enrol beginning in 2026, Ford announces

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/medical-schools-going-100-canadian/docview/3120806243/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Medical+schools+going+%27100%25+Canadian%27%3A+Province+will+not+allow+international+students+to+enrol+beginning+in+2026%2C+Ford+announces&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will not allow international students in medical schools beginning in the fall of 2026, and will also cover tuition for more than 1,000 students who commit to becoming a family doctor in Ontario, Premier Doug Ford said Friday.
Through upcoming legislation, the province aims to reserve at least 95 per cent of medical school spots for Ontario residents and the remainder for students from other parts of Canada.
"There was 18 per cent students from around the world taking our kids' seats and then not even staying here and going back to their country, and it's just not right," Ford said at a news conference.
"So now it's going to be 100 per cent Canadian, 95 per cent Ontario."
Health Minister Sylvia Jones echoed those comments, saying Ontario students "need to come first."
"We are going to prioritize Ontario residents because those are our taxpayers that are paying those students to go to school," Jones said.
The province is also expanding a "Learn and Stay" program that covers tuition and other educational costs to include students who commit to becoming family doctors in Ontario.
The grant program, also beginning in 2026, is expected to cost $88 million and be extended to 1,360 eligible undergraduate students. The province says the program should allow 1.36 million more Ontarians to connect to primary care.
The Ontario College of Family Physicians says 2.5 million people do not have a family doctor.
A study by the Canadian Institute for Health Information released on Thursday said 12 per cent of Ontarians do not have a family doctor.
It is an issue that has dogged the Ford government over the years, as the numbers of Ontarians without primary care rose during his tenure.
This week, the province appointed former federal Liberal health minister Jane Philpott to a new role with a goal of connecting every Ontarian to primary care within the next five years.
"She's going to be on the ground fixing that gap," Ford said.
The province is also expanding its health teams model, which sees patients connect to clinics where they have access to physicians, but also nurse practitioners and other services like physical rehabilitation and mental-health care.
The province said it is also reviewing the visa trainee program that trains international students sponsored by foreign governments in an effort to further protect Ontario students.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Agencies audit misses the mark

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/agencies-audit-misses-mark/docview/3120806178/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Agencies+audit+misses+the+mark&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

retired executive director of Catholic Children's Aid
Society of Hamilton.

Document 279 of 500

Is conservatism really on the rise?: Blaine Higgs's recent election loss in New Brunswick suggests it's not

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-conservatism-really-on-rise/docview/3120805966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+conservatism+really+on+the+rise%3F%3A+Blaine+Higgs%27s+recent+election+loss+in+New+Brunswick+suggests+it%27s+not&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Make no mistake, New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs lost big on Monday night.
The province's voters delivered a forceful rebuke of Higgs' Progressive Conservatives similar to the 1995 election, when the party won only six seats against Frank McKenna's Liberals.
This time, the PCs were reduced to 16 seats while the Liberals won 31. The Greens dropped to two seats.
This seat count downplays the Liberals' 13-point popular vote lead in a tough political environment.
Historically, the Liberals have had inefficient support that's been concentrated in safe francophone ridings.
This time, they made inroads with anglophones beyond Moncton.
Higgs, among Canada's most socially conservative premiers, lost his own safe seat of Quispamsis, which was among the province's most Conservative ridings in the 2020 election.
The result was a referendum on Higgs' brand of conservatism. Along with the failure of the resurgent Conservatives in British Columbia to win a clear victory on Oct. 19, Higgs' loss challenges the narrative that conservatism is on the rise across Canada.
Governing to the (far) right
Since gaining power in 2018, Higgs embraced a neoconservative social agenda.
Most notably, he triggered a national conversation on trans children's recognition in schools.
Using the language of "parental rights," Higgs introduced parent consent restrictions for name and pronoun changes for children under 16.
Research shows trans children have high rates of suicidal ideation, especially when they're not supported in how they identify.
Over time, Higgs supported anti-trans and anti-sex education protesters, even as many advocates, parents and educators raised concerns about the safety and mental well-being of LGBTQ-plus youth.
He also refused to deny what's known as the so-called Kitty Litter myth that falsely alleges students are allowed to identify as animals and use litter boxes.
When confronted by parents about a safe-sex presentation slide for a high school audience, Higgs banned the group that conducted the presentation.
It didn't end there.
Higgs erroneously suggested an Indigenous nation sought to claim most of the province from property owners. In 2021, his government discouraged land acknowledgments by provincial employees. Higgs also argued Indigenous people had ceded their land.
Taking aim
Higgs' relationship with francophones was just as bad. He refused to learn French in Canada's only bilingual province after promising he would. He alleged he was unfairly targeted as an anglophone.
When coming to power in 2018 with a minority government, Higgs weakened bilingual requirements for paramedic positions.
Later, he controversially proposed ending French immersion programs, arguing it was unfair to "English Prime" students in the province.
When he won a majority in 2020, Higgs lowered taxes on the highest income earners while constraining increases to health care and education.
Higgs was successful in uniting the right.
As a former leadership contender of the linguistic segregationist Confederation of Regions party, Higgs welcomed far-right People's Alliance representatives to his party.
But his tenure faced internal opposition. Atlantic conservatism tends to be closer to the political centre.
Higgs' Maritime counterparts, premiers Dennis King of Prince Edward Island and Tim Houston of Nova Scotia, have largely avoided social issues.
On the province's Policy 713, also called the Sexual Orientation and Gender Identity policy, six PCs voted with an opposition motion against the proposed changes. Four were cabinet ministers.
Several ministers resigned from cabinet with letters blasting Higgs' leadership.
Almost half of PC riding associations sought a leadership review. They fell just short of the minimum needed to trigger a review.
Most leaders recognize when their time was up. Not Higgs.
An embattled campaign
The PCs' tumultuous time in government made for an uninspired campaign.
Twelve of the 26 winning PC representatives from 2020 did not run again.
In their place came more social conservatives who would not oppose Higgs.
The PCs received bad news early. They were projected to fall short of their 2024-25 balanced budget aims.
Still, Higgs campaigned on his fiscal management. He offered a two per cent HST cut as a reward. For some, this proposal rang as vote-buying from a government that could have pursued a sales tax cut at any point in its six-year tenure.
The PCs campaigned on few other commitments. Their two-page platform made generic promises like "respect parents."
They also sought to "compel individuals into drug treatment" and "axe the carbon tax."
Meanwhile, the Liberals hammered the PCs on housing, health care and education. All three areas had been stressed by population growth and tight funding.
Housing policy was a particular weakness given the PCs' long-term resistance to rent caps and its record as a housing-starts laggard.
Higgs' confidence in his record was misplaced. While his social conservatism has an audience in New Brunswick, few saw it as a priority relative to the cost of living.
His other campaign efforts made little difference. Higgs sought to make his opponent Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
He also stirred anti-immigration sentiment over federal asylum-seeker plans. Both efforts seemed desperate.
Grievance politics
The Liberals' return to power could be attributed to a referendum on Higgs. There is no doubt Higgs had personal defects that cost him his own riding.
But his loss is more than a personal rejection. It also seems a rejection of a grievance politics that favours anger over substance.
After repeatedly focusing on social issues over matters like housing, the grievances lost their allure. Even for the most steadfast Conservative voters, Higgs' targeting of minorities came across as bullying.
While Higgs may be the worst offender, he is not the only practitioner of grievance conservatism.
Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith play the same tune.
Will their political fates be any different?
Noah Fry is a PhD candidate, Political Science,
at McMaster University.
This was originally
published on
The Conversation.
CAPTION: New Brunswick PC Party Leader Blaine Higgs delivers a concession speech Monday. While his social conservativism has an audience in New Brunswick, few saw it as a priority relative to the cost of living, McMaster's Noah Fry writes.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press

Document 280 of 500

Ontario grocers wary of new recycling rules with alcohol sales: Some feel 'hoodwinked' by new bottle return requirements

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-grocers-wary-new-recycling-rules-with/docview/3120805958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+grocers+wary+of+new+recycling+rules+with+alcohol+sales%3A+Some+feel+%27hoodwinked%27+by+new+bottle+return+requirements&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario grocery stores - particularly smaller, independent shops - say new bottle return requirements that were sprung on them a week before they're set to take effect may make it impossible to participate in Premier Doug Ford's expansion of alcohol sales.
Grocery stores that aren't already selling alcohol can start stocking their shelves with beer, wine and coolers as of Thursday, the next step in Ford's expedited rollout.
Uptake has been relatively low, with just over 400 new grocery stores signing on to join the existing 450 grocers licensed to sell alcohol, out of around 5,000 such stores in the province.
Grocery stores were already concerned that those who sell alcohol will also have to accept empties.
Having the smell of stale beer mingling with the smell of fresh food - and having to put not-quite-entirely-empty bottles that become fruit fly magnets near produce sections - would not be good for business, they said.
But with new and detailed requirements communicated to them this week by the Liquor Control Board of Ontario just days before they begin, retailers say they don't know how they will make it work, and some are planning to hand back their licences.
"We feel hoodwinked by this," said Brad Fletcher, president of The Village Grocer in Markham.
His store has been licensed since 2019, but he does not see any way to continue making it work once they have to accept, sort, clean and palletize empties and associated packaging.
"We, for a long time, have been questioning the profitability of carrying beer and wine in our store," he said.
"It has been a struggle with low margins that we've been receiving. We weren't making any money on it at all, and maybe, in fact, losing some. But we did it as a convenience to our customers."
Stores such as The Village Grocer that are within a five-kilometre radius of a Beer Store are exempt from having to accept empties until Jan. 1, 2026, so that is when the store is planning to stop selling alcohol, Fletcher said.
"We are prepared to hand our license back at the 11th hour," he said. "Our customers love it, but at the same time, we're not built to be a bottle depot for returns."
Stores were told earlier this year that there would be a requirement to accept empties, but the details released this week are taking many by surprise.
Stores will have to accept not just bottles and cans, but packaging such as bags, boxes, plastic rings and bottle caps.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 281 of 500

Doug Ford defends rebate cheque plan, says election is 'the last thing' on his mind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-defends-rebate-cheque-plan-says/docview/3120922946/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+defends+rebate+cheque+plan%2C+says+election+is+%27the+last+thing%27+on+his+mind&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Insisting an early election is "the last thing on my mind," Premier Doug Ford is defending his plan to mail cheques to 16 million Ontarians.
Asked by reporters Friday in Oshawa if he were trying to "bribe" voters on the eve of spring campaign, the premier bristled.
"No, not at all. It's moving the government forward," said Ford.
As first disclosed by the Star on Oct. 16, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement on Wednesday will outline a plan to send cheques to every Ontario adult and child.
While the exact amount of the "tax rebate" cheques is still being finalized, it is expected to be around $200.
The tax-free payments would cost the provincial treasury about $3.2 billion when the money is distributed in late January or early February.
Speaking to Newstalk 1010 host Deb Hutton earlier this week, Ford stressed "it's not the government's money, it's taxpayers' money."
"Anyone who doesn't want it, send it to the Treasury of Ontario, send your cheque back," he said Wednesday.
Ford has been signalling for months that he may choose to face voters in March or April, one year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 election.
His Progressive Conservatives, leading in public-opinion polls, have been blitzing the airwaves with ads attacking Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, and the premier has been road-testing campaign themes and making promises like building a massive Mississauga-Scarborough tunnel under Highway 401.
But he claimed he has been too busy governing to think about electoral concerns.
"That's the last thing on my mind to be very frank," Ford said at a campaign-style announcement at Lakeridge Health to tout measures to tackle Ontario's doctors' shortage.
"This job entails - with all our ministers and our partners here - to keep moving non-stop. Folks, it's 18 hours a day, make no mistake about it. You just go, go, go, go, go. A million things are coming at you," he said.
The premier was also pressed about what his government was going to do to address the problems at various school boards around the province.
"They frustrate me," said Ford, blasting the four trustees in the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic for a $45,000 summer trip to Italy where they purchased $100,000 in religious statues.
"Like, the cheese slipped off the cracker with these guys," he said, incredulously.
"I just don't get it - common sense."
After public outrage, they said they would reimburse the board for the $45,000 in travel expenses and find outside sources to bankroll the $100,000 artwork.
Warming to the subject, the premier said it was also "unacceptable" for Thames Valley public school board trustees to spend thousands of dollars for a three-day retreat in August at the Rogers Centre hotel during a Toronto Blue Jays homestand.
"That's why I directed our Minister of Education (Jill Dunlop) to start doing audits on these school boards. They're the first to scream they need more money and so on so forth. We're pouring money into school boards, and they're out partying and ... acting like a bunch of yahoos."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends question period as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21, 2024.

Document 282 of 500

Ford government to ban foreign students from Ontario medical schools

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-ban-foreign-students-ontario/docview/3120806378/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+ban+foreign+students+from+Ontario+medical+schools&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is planning to bar new foreign students from medical schools and limit students from elsewhere in Canada while offering free tuition to 1,360 Ontarians if they agree to work as doctors in the province for five years.
In a move to tackle Ontario's growing doctor shortage, Ford is expanding "learn and stay" grants to family medicine students and directing the province's medical schools to "prioritize" 95 per cent of spots for Ontario residents starting in the fall of 2026.
"That is a game changer," Ford said Friday in announcing what Health Minister Sylvia Jones called "bold action."
The other five per cent would be students from the rest of Canada. In the unlikely event spots go unfilled, international students could be accepted.
But the impact will be felt harder by prospective out-of-province students than international ones. Government figures show just 10 or 0.26 per cent of 3,833 Ontario medical students this year are from other countries.
However, 473 students or 12 per cent are from elsewhere in Canada while the vast majority, 3,350 students or more than 87 per cent, are from Ontario.
"Let's be clear," said Liberal MPP and health critic Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician, noting the tiny percentage of foreign medical students.
"Protecting more medical school spots for students in Ontario is a fair move, as other provinces do it all the time. But this won't actually add any family doctors to the workforce for six years. Where was he with this policy two, three or four years ago?"
The grants will cost $88 million over the next three years.
Ford's changes come with an early election expected next spring and the government vulnerable on the health-care front, with the Ontario Medical Association saying 2.5 million residents don't have access to a family doctor - a number expected to rise as aging physicians retire.
The premier said he gets frustrated hearing some Ontario students had to go elsewhere for medical school for lack of spaces here, with many never returning, and appeared to blame foreign students despite their low numbers.
"This has been gnawing at me for years," he said, standing with Jones in front of Queen's University students in the family medicine program on a satellite campus at Lakeridge Health in Oshawa.
Ford, whose government has already expanded medical school spots and is opening new medical schools at Toronto Metropolitan University and York University, noted he hears from Ontario parents whose sons and daughters are at medical schools abroad.
"You'd go out to events and say ... how are the kids? Well, they're studying in Ireland, or they're studying in the Caribbean, they're studying in the U.S.," he told reporters.
"And what happens next is, they meet someone and they don't come back home."
Ford suggested 18 per cent of students in Ontario medical schools are from other countries and less likely to set up shop here, but that percentage is actually for medical school graduates who are in residency training.
"Ontario students need to come first," said Jones, who has pledged that every Ontarian will have access to a family doctor within five years.
The government's problem is that family medicine is no longer a popular option, said Margaret Walton-Roberts, a professor at the Balsillie School of International Affairs at Wilfrid Laurier University in Waterloo.
"So it is not about international students. It is about making family medicine more appealing as a career," added Walton-Roberts, an expert in international migration of health-care professionals.
"Currently it is not, and most students opt for specialty training."
As Shamji pointed out, it will take years for the new measures - which include paying for the books and equipment of future family doctors - to have an impact because of the long time frame for training physicians.
Depending on the university, medical school is three or four years, followed by a two-year family medicine residency, said Dr. Dominik Nowak, a family physician who is president of the Ontario Medical Association.
He applauded the new grants as "an important, ambitious step" but urged the government to consider immediate measures, such as medical school debt forgiveness for new Ontario-trained doctors heading elsewhere if they agree to stay.
Nowak said many medical students graduate with "crushing debt" in the $100,000 to $200,000 range.
He declined to comment on the foreign medical student prohibition, saying, "It's up to the government to answer questions on that."
The Ontario Medical Students Association did not reply to a request for comment on Friday, while the Council of Ontario Faculties of Medicine said "we look forward to working with the provincial government to further understand the details with the shared goals to deliver high-quality medical education and to increase access to family physicians."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles called for more government funding for residency positions to get more doctors into service.
The "learn and stay" program was established three years ago to provide financial assistance for nursing, paramedic and medical lab technology students to work in "underserviced" areas of the province.
The government estimates medical students accepted for the "learn and stay" program will eventually be able to serve about 1.36 million patients once they become physicians.
Medical students will be able to apply for the grant program for the 2026-27 academic year.
By then, the government hopes to have up to 100 doctors who attended medical school outside Canada fully trained and cleared to serve in rural areas under the province's "Practice Ready Ontario" program.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, right, and Health Minister Sylvia Jones listen to questions from the opposition during question period at Queen's Park in Toronto on Dec. 6, 2023.

Document 283 of 500

Doug Ford steps into an LCBO mess of his own making - and we all may pay the price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-steps-into-lcbo-mess-his-own-making-we/docview/3120560442/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+steps+into+an+LCBO+mess+of+his+own+making+-+and+we+all+may+pay+the+price&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has boxed himself in on paper bags at the LCBO.
Now watch him squirm out of a trade trap of his own making. Problem is, we may all pay a price for his folly.
When Ontario's teetotalling premier first got wind that booze bags were toast at the LCBO, he rode to the rescue of the wine-swilling, beer-guzzling, spirits-imbibing masses. Huffing and puffing, the man of the people laid down the law for liquor board's tree-hugging bean counters:
Bring back bags!
Ford fired off a letter telling the liquor board that people shouldn't have to carry bare bottles in public. Besides, Canadians are blessed with trees, which are a renewable resource, he reasoned.
"As a government, we are focused on making life easier, more convenient, and more affordable for the people of Ontario," Ford fumed last April. "The decision to remove paper bags has had the opposite effect."
And so the LCBO did as it was told. But by doing the right thing - getting the best price with a publicly tendered contract, untainted by corruption, free of favouritism and protectionism - the alcohol giant wrong-footed the premier a second time.
That's because it signed a deal with a Quebec supplier, Rosenbloom Groupe Inc., instead of a homegrown Ontario company.
Never mind that Quebec is still part of Canada. No matter that our neighbouring province happens to be the birthplace of Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, who oversees the LCBO.
The premier was apoplectic. The minister was apologetic.
How, Ford wondered, could the LCBO - which revels in protecting Niagara wines from out-of-province competition - not apply the same pugnacious, protectionist logic to local bag suppliers? If every winery is protected, why not our forestry?
The premier personally put pen to paper again, "directing the LCBO to also initiate a new tender process as soon as possible that will be limited to Ontario suppliers."
Bethlenfalvy dutifully told the arm's-length, Crown-owned LCBO that its due diligence was misplaced. When it comes to contracting, he ordered, don't go low - go local.
Now if you are a protectionist - as most progressives are - then perhaps you are onside with Ford's blustering (although not the notion of making it his top priority). Perhaps predictably, NDP Leader Marit Stiles echoed Ford's logic, arguing that "our procurement should always be focused on how we're going to actually drive Ontario's economy and create jobs here."
But if you believe in lowering trade barriers between Canadian provinces in order to foster national unity and forge economic ties, then the premier's musings make no sense.
Lest Ford forget, he is the rotating chair of the Council of the Federation until next summer, which means his mission is to bring all the premiers together to promote interprovincial procurement and trade. One can only imagine the conversation when Ford convenes Quebec Premier François Legault and his counterparts in Toronto and sheepishly confesses that Quebec bags are banned.
Is our forestry industry so feeble that it needs to be propped up at any price - even at the risk of retaliation by our trading partners? The problem with protectionism is that it's a two-way street, and not just along east-west lines.
Think north-south.
With the U.S. presidential election just days away, Ford told the Empire Club last week that the stakes are perilously high for this province: Two-way trade between Ontario and the United States exceeds $500 billion.
"If Ontario were a stand-alone country, we'd be America's third largest trading partner," he mused.
That's why it's so important for all governments, "no matter the political stripe, to reject protectionism and embrace a 'Buy Can-Am' mentality."
Ford argued against the "Fortress America" mentality that American politicians talk up on the campaign trail: "We're encouraging U.S. lawmakers to think big - to build fortress North America (my italics)."
That was last week. This week it's a different story, the premier now practising the protectionism he preaches against with a double-barrelled "Buy Ontario" and "Ban Quebec" procurement policy.
By so blatantly contradicting himself, by placing paper bags at the top of his protectionist wish list, Ford has undermined his strategy to safeguard the Ontario economy from Buy American provisions.
To be sure, there may be some strategic or sensitive sectors of the economy that might be worth going to the wall for. But building a protective wall around paper bags doesn't add up.
For the record, the brown bag contract is worth $10.9 million. That's a rounding error when measured against our $500-billion trading relationship with the U.S.
It reveals a premier who runs the province by the seat of his pants and goes by his gut. Instead of using his head to think things through, he's putting a paper bag over it.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to media at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.

Subject: Protectionism

Document 284 of 500

These Ontarians are watching how MAID is being accessed and used. Here's what they found

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/these-ontarians-are-watching-how-maid-is-being/docview/3120560308/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=These+Ontarians+are+watching+how+MAID+is+being+accessed+and+used.+Here%27s+what+they+found&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The cases outlined in two recently released reports on Ontario's use of medical assistance in dying are stark.In one example, a woman in her 50s with a history of depression and suicidality and a severe sensitivity to chemicals ended her life through medical assistance in dying (MAID) after failing to secure a housing arrangement that met her medical needs. That was one of six cases described in the reports, which raised new questions about how MAID is granted in the province for people who are not nearing their natural deaths.Ontario's chief coroner released the reports last week on behalf of an oversight group that reviews how MAID is accessed in the province, focusing on complex cases that saw Ontarians choose the procedure to end their suffering, in some situations, partly because of social needs that were not being met. But what did experts conclude, and what's the scope of the problem?Here's what you need to know.Why does Ontario have a MAID oversight group?In 2016, the federal government passed legislation permitting those with serious and incurable medical conditions to access medical assistance in dying if their death was "reasonably foreseeable." Quebec's Superior Court later ruled that such criteria was too restrictive and unconstitutional, prompting the Liberals in 2021 to expand access to the procedure to those whose deaths were not naturally imminent. That law created two tracks, with one set of rules for those whose natural deaths were reasonably foreseeable and another set of safeguards for those whose deaths were not. It also banned MAID for those seeking to end their lives solely due to mental illness for two years - a period that has now been extended until March 2027. That second track - for those enduring intolerable suffering but not approaching their natural death - has been met with intense scrutiny, sparking fears that people with disabilities and marginalized people who lack support and resources would be disproportionately driven to obtain the procedure. In 2023, 116 patients out of 4,644 people who received MAID in Ontario followed that track. Because it's largely up to the provinces and territories to carry out and oversee MAID, Ontario's Chief Coroner, Dr. Dirk Huyer, this year struck the MAID Death Review Committee (MDRC), a 16-member group made up of legal, medical, social work, nursing, mental health and disability experts. The purpose of the committee is to assess how MAID is being accessed and used in Ontario, and provide recommendations and advice to the different levels of government, agencies, and professionals involved in the practice. What kinds of cases were discussed in the reports?Huyer's office released the committee's first reports last week. They focused on cases in which MAID was provided to marginalized people and those with complex medical conditions, all in situations where their natural deaths were not imminent. The coroner's office chose those themes to promote discussion on providing MAID in less common situations, with the reports noting that the circumstances described "often represent divergence from typical practice."In one case, a man in his 40s with inflammatory bowel disease, few social supports and a history of mental illness was first informed about MAID from a psychiatrist, and went through with the procedure without his family being consulted or being offered treatment for his addictions. Committee members noted that the man might not have received "sufficient care through mental health and social services."In another, a man in his 80s with longtime chronic back pain who had recently been diagnosed with an adjustment disorder - in which someone has excessive reactions to stress in ways that may alter their thoughts and actions - chose MAID to end his life. Some committee members argued that psychiatric assessments properly determined that the disorder was tied to his untreatable pain. Others expressed concern that the man's adjustment disorder could have been a temporary state. In the case of the woman who could not find appropriate housing to manage her chemical sensitivity syndrome, committee members did not reach consensus on her eligibility for MAID, because they could not agree on whether her suffering was primarily caused by a medical condition, a social issue, or her mental state. What conclusions were drawn?Because the MDRC is made up of people with differing opinions on MAID, members did not arrive at unanimous conclusions in interpreting each case. Dr. Ramona Coelho, a committee member and family physician who works with marginalized patients in London, Ont., told the Star the themes laid out in the reports were "quite worrisome."Coelho said that even though the group only looked at a small number of cases, "these are people's lives," and it's imperative that those on the margins of society be offered better mental health, disability, and community supports to avoid assisted death being viewed as the only solution. Donna Stewart, another committee member with expertise in psychiatry at the University of Toronto, said she's "very concerned" that atypical cases might spark fear-mongering about the practice, telling the Star that a balance must be struck between exhausting options to ensure social needs are met and still making MAID accessible in situations where that is not always possible. She said her takeaway from the themes identified in the reports is that the "psychiatric aspects" of some conditions are not always addressed, and "that needs to change." To that end, the coroner's office issued several recommendations to the federal government, including calling for more guidance on following or reconsidering MAID protocols when mental health conditions are a significant factor in someone's suffering. Huyer also called on Health Canada to provide more advice on disability care and approaching requests when suffering is predominantly "derived from an unmet social need" like housing. Ottawa was therefore also asked to consider broad reforms, like ushering in "actionable changes" to the country's social safety net.The office of federal Health Minister Mark Holland told the Star that new regulations introduced last year now allow Health Canada to collect more data on MAID requests, including "information on living arrangements, disability, race, and Indigenous identity."The statement also said the department is exploring the relationship between socioeconomic status and MAID, and cautioned that a "lack of access to social supports or to health services" does not make someone eligible for the procedure.
CAPTION: Dr. Dirk Huyer, chief coroner for Ontario,released the reports on behalf of an oversight group that reviews how MAID is accessed in Ontario, focusing on complex cases.

Subject: Committees; Mental health; Disability; Assisted suicide; Mental disorders

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Health Canada; NAICS: 923120

Title: These Ontarians are watching how MAID is being accessed and used. Here's what they found

Document 285 of 500

Is Dofasco's delay-plagued 'green steel' project at risk? Worried residents seek answers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-dofascos-delay-plagued-green-steel-project-at/docview/3120560226/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Dofasco%27s+delay-plagued+%27green+steel%27+project+at+risk%3F+Worried+residents+seek+answers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Unexplained delays to a vaunted, taxpayer-subsidized "green steel" overhaul at ArcelorMittal Dofasco have left pollution-weary residents worried the project - or its promise to end the use of dirty coal - is in jeopardy.
The Hamilton company's own community liaison committee raised concerns Tuesday about the apparent lack of visible demolition, construction or even regulatory approvals needed for the high-profile project, which is supposed to end more than a century of dirty coal-fired steelmaking at the site.
Major demolition at the bayfront plant was supposed to start early last year to make room for climate-friendly technology that would replace coal-fired steel and iron-making facilities that routinely spew lung-busting contaminants over the lower city. New structures were supposed to be under construction this year.
But the company - which originally announced plans to transition to coal-free steelmaking by 2028, if not sooner - would no longer offer a timeline for the $1.8-billion decarbonization project.
At the meeting, Dofasco officials would only say they are seeking a "rightsized solution" to unspecified challenges for the "highly complex" steelmaking overhaul, which remains in the engineering stage.
The company remains "unwavering" in its commitment to a decarbonization effort that will "determine the future of ArcelorMittal Dofasco," said environmental general manager Gas Gebara at the community meeting Tuesday, adding the company is also standing by its climate target to cut plant carbon emissions by 60 per cent by 2030.
He acknowledged resident frustration over the apparent delays and promised a future update - when possible. "We want this as much if not more (than) our stakeholders," he said.
The Spectator attended the virtual meeting and reached out by email with questions, but the company only responded two days later with a statement it had previously sent in September that did not address queries about the project timeline or whether the original planned green-steel technology is changing.
The federal Liberal and Tory provincial governments combined in 2022 to offer $900 million to the Hamilton steelmaker to kick-start a decarbonization project that includes new electric arc furnaces, a natural gas-fired direct reduced iron (DRI) plant and a new gas pipeline to feed it.
Those facilities would allow Ontario's biggest industrial carbon polluter to end the use of coal - making a huge dent in climate-warming emissions, but also slashing air pollution in the city.
The Spectator previously reported on the health implications of ending coal use in local steelmaking, which has historically contributed to rains of soot in the lower city and the worst cancer-causing benzene and benzo(a)pyrene pollution in Ontario.
Dofasco announced demolition of a mothballed coke plant would start in early 2023 to make room for new facilities and Enbridge held public meetings that year on a planned new 14-kilometre natural gas pipeline needed to fuel the DRI plant.
But this year, The Spectator learned the company had not submitted a leave-to-construct request for the pipeline to the Ontario Energy Board as expected by the start of 2024. No submission had been made as of August, either, meaning that part of the project was at least eight months behind the original public schedule.
In September, the CBC was first to report demolition of the old coke plant had not taken place, despite Dofasco's announcement that work was slated to begin in early 2023.
Given the apparent lack of progress, "should the public be a little bit worried?" asked Jochen Bezner, a community environmental advocate and longtime member of the liaison committee.
Environment Hamilton head Ian Borsuk also warned the missed project milestones have raised "alarm bells" for some observers. He said some residents are worried the delays are linked to the political uncertainty around a looming federal election and the potential for a new government that is "less serious about addressing climate change."
In a later interview, Borsuk argued citizens are owed "high-level" answers by the private business given the size of the public investment - not to mention the public health implications. "It is literally a question of what am I going to be breathing for the next however many years?"
Steel industry expert Peter Warrian suggested Dofasco's "go-slow approach" could be explained by a mix of global and domestic factors, including rampant inflation, the cost of competing with cheap Chinese steel and electric vehicles and the threat of U.S. protectionism after the November presidential election.
Closer to home, another concern is the uncertain fate of Canada's own controversial carbon tax heading into a federal election. That gradually increasing tax - which is supposed to rise to $170 per tonne of carbon by 2030 - acts as an incentive for Canadian steelmakers to invest in pricey technology conversions.
Federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre is leading in the polls and famed for his opposition to the consumer carbon tax, for example, but his party's plans for industry are less clear.
Warrian, a former provincial chief economist and United Steelworkers research director, said he wouldn't be surprised if those factors "stretched the timeline" for what is an "extremely complex" and costly project - although he noted such challenges haven't stopped Algoma Steel from "going great guns" on a similar green steel overhaul in Sault Ste. Marie.
Regardless, Warrian said he didn't think ArcelorMittal would "abandon" the Hamilton plant's decarbonization plan altogether.
He noted the Canadian site should only become more strategically important given the global steelmaker recently sold many of its U.S. assets to Cleveland-Cliffs. "Dofasco is their one major flat-rolled steel project in North America. These guys are not about to give up on that market," Warrian said.
At least publicly, both government funders also appear confident the project will go ahead.
"Every conversation I've had with (Dofasco) reaffirms they are committed to this," said Hamilton Mountain Liberal MP Lisa Hepfner in an interview last month.
Hepfner, who co-chairs the federal all-party steel caucus, said she could not speak to why Dofasco's progress has slowed, but argued such "hiccups" are inevitable with a big, industry-changing transition.
"Yeah, it's hard and yes, it is expensive and yes, you'll find hiccups," she said. "(But) I think absolutely it is going ahead and they are dealing with problems as they come."
Ontario Tory Premier Doug Ford also appears to be a believer, shouting out the project in a government YouTube video just two days ago that featured Ontario's top politician touring the Dofasco plant in coveralls and a hard hat extolling the plan to produce "clean, green steel."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter with The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at a news conference at ArcelorMittal Dofasco's Line 5 galvanized steel line in 2022 after announcing $500 million for the steelmaker's green steel project.Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau tours the ArcelorMittal Dofasco steel hot mill facility in late 2022, when company officials announced work on a new, taxpayer-subsidized green steel project was imminent.Resident are anxious about delayed action on a high-profile overhaul at ArcelorMittal Dofasco that would end a century of coal-fired steelmaking.

Subject: Demolition; Outdoor air quality; Carbon; Climate change; Galvanized steel; Pollution; Coal; Natural gas; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: United States--US

Company / organization: Name: ArcelorMittal Dofasco; NAICS: 331110

Title: Is Dofasco's delay-plag ued 'green steel' project at risk? Worried residents seek answers

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 25, 2024

Section: news/hamilton-region

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 286 of 500

Oct. 25: Encampment plan appalling, 'rebate' is blatant vote buying and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-25-encampment-plan-appalling-rebate-is/docview/3120560053/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+25%3A+Encampment+plan+appalling%2C+%27rebate%27+is+blatant+vote+buying+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Who's the city really for?
Re: Hamilton to make seven lower-city green spaces no-go zones for encampments, Oct. 17
With no substantial plan besides money dumping, our city has displaced 26 homeless people. Once again, the rich person has the right to green space. Grace Mater informs us they "hope" these people will relocate to temporary shelter spaces. I am appalled at the lack of planning.
We pride ourselves in being equitable, yet the system our cities are built upon tends to yield nothing but inequality. By forcefully removing the homeless, we reaffirm harmful stereotypes embedded in society.
As a resident in Hamilton, I am speechless. It seems like we find pleasure in glaring at the encampments. Why can't we utilize our time to protest for their right to a healthy living environment? If we want to overcome an issue affecting us all - poverty - then I urge a better approach. Importantly, the city must come up with better solutions than "hoping" our homeless community will relocate to another unsafe area. These solutions must start with recognizing the right of all people to be in green spaces.
Rielyn Charette, Hamilton
'Rebate' is blatant vote buying
Looks like Doug Ford is trying to outdo Elon Musk with buying votes. Difference is, Ford is using public money. There is not even a competition or a skill-testing question to answer - we all get the money, no matter what. This is outright, blatant vote buying - something that Elections Ontario should be looking into.
My wife and I are retired and live comfortably. We do not need a bribe to vote. In fact, we have decided to endorse the cheques and give them to the local food bank. And for the skeptics, we will not be asking for a tax receipt.
David Eyles, Hamilton
Fallen leaves aren't litter
We don't pay much attention to farming and the fertilizer used to produce crops, but the sorry fact is that over many decades of growing, nutrients in the soil have been depleted to the point that fertilizer is required to maintain production.
A similar cycle happens on our manicured lawns. Trees and other deep-rooted plants - including (gasp!) dandelions - draw nitrogen and other nutrients from deep in the ground, and by dropping foliage in the fall, deposit this good stuff on the surface to feed shallow-rooted plants.
So when we rake up and remove tree leaves, we "harvest" the production of these plants, effectively achieving an annual leaching out of the nutrition in the soil beneath our lawns, similar to what happened on farms. It puzzles me to no end that so many people look upon leaves as litter and expend huge amounts of time and effort to get rid of nature's fertilizer. Just stop it, OK?
Emil Andrew Sekerak, Beamsville
Henley's story is remarkable
Re: Ticat legend Garney Henley was great. His story is even better, Oct. 22
Thanks for this story about Garney Henley. He really was an amazing athlete in the days when athletes were paid working-class salaries. He lived in a working-class neighbourhood on the West Mountain, I believe. To see what he accomplished as such an unassuming superstar is truly remarkable.
Compare that to the multi-million-dollar athletes nowadays and it puts him even higher on a pedestal.
Andy Price, Ancaster
Sprawl will make matters worse
Re: Urban sprawl is not the answer, Oct. 22
This letter did a fantastic job at bringing forth concerning issues about the future of the Hamilton area. Understandably, Hamilton wants to facilitate growth within already existing urban areas, but developers have their eyes set on urban sprawl.
I am truly disappointed with the province's decision to allow for such legislation. Not only will it reduce the decision-making autonomy of both the city and its residents, but it will allow for development companies to harass the city and exhaust its resources until they get what they want. It is insulting.
This ordeal regarding the boundaries has faced public scrutiny before, and for good reason. The city has underlying issues that need its undivided attention, such as already struggling infrastructure. Allowing for more urban expansion will only exacerbate these issues.
The provincial government needs to take a step back and make it a priority to revise its recent decision as clearly they have not examined the long-term implications that it will have. Should they not, they will continue to facilitate an environment that fosters predatory behaviour, ignores effortful decision-making, and will end up negatively impacting residents of Hamilton within the foreseeable future.
Austin Daniels, Hamilton
CAPTION: Tents are set up around the pavilion in Carter Park in 2023. The Ward 3 green space is one of seven where the city plans to ban tents once temporary shelter beds open up in coming weeks.

Subject: Working class; Homeless people; Urbanization; Decision making; Fertilizers; Leaves

Business indexing term: Subject: Working class

Title: Oct. 25: Encampment plan appalling, 'rebate' is blatant vote buying and other letters to the editor

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 25, 2024

Section: opinion/letters-to-the-editor

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 288 of 500

Residents seek answers about Dofasco's delayed 'green steel' project: Steelmaker says it is committed to decarbonization project

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/residents-seek-answers-about-dofascos-delayed/docview/3120340814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Residents+seek+answers+about+Dofasco%27s+delayed+%27green+steel%27+project%3A+Steelmaker+says+it+is+committed+to+decarbonization+project&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Ontario Tory Premier Doug Ford also appears to be a believer, shouting out the project in a government YouTube video just two days ago that featured Ontario's top politician touring the Dofasco plant in coveralls and a hard hat extolling the plan to produce "clean, green steel."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter with The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at a news conference at ArcelorMittal Dofasco's Line 5 galvanized steel line in 2022 after announcing $500 million for the steelmaker's green steel project.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau tours the ArcelorMittal Dofasco steel hot mill facility in late 2022, when company officials announced work on a new, taxpayer-subsidized green steel project was imminent.Hamilton Spectator file photoThe Canadian Press file photo
