Document 289 of 500

Ford government to help more families get fertility treatments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-help-more-families-get-fertility/docview/3120652969/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+help+more+families+get+fertility+treatments&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is boosting its fertility program to help more people struggling to have children and proposing a new tax credit to defray the hefty costs of treatment.
The tax credit of up to $5,000 a year will be unveiled in next week's fall economic statement, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Thursday at an Etobicoke fertility clinic with Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
It comes as Premier Doug Ford sets the stage for an early provincial election next spring instead of waiting for the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
"More can be done to give everyone in Ontario the special chance at parenthood," Jones said, announcing an increase in funding for the Ontario fertility program to $150 million in each of the next two years.
That's more than double the $70 million now allocated annually and should triple the number of families getting government-funded in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles at more hospitals and community clinics, she added.
Jones noted fertility treatments can be "overwhelmingly expensive" and cost families tens of thousands of dollars as they ride an emotional roller coaster while trying to conceive.
"Many more women than we know have experienced this," said Progressive Conservative MPP Christine Hogarth (Etobicoke-Lakeshore), who advocated for an expanded fertility program.
Wait times for treatment will be "significantly" reduced by the increased funding, pledged Bethlenfalvy, who will present his fall economic statement next Wednesday.
About 13,000 people use the fertility program annually, so tripling that would put the number closer to 39,000.
Bethlenfalvy's proposed tax credit would cover up to 25 per cent of eligible expenses, including IVF cycles, fertility medications, travel for treatment and diagnostic testing starting next year.
As the Star first reported, the fall economic statement will also detail payments of about $200 each to every Ontarian to defray the cost of living following a bout of high inflation that sent prices for food, housing and other goods soaring.
Opposition parties have slammed it as a pre-election ploy as Ford seeks a third term before any potential charges in an RCMP criminal investigation of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
"Anyone who doesn't want it, send it to the Treasury of Ontario, send your cheque back," Ford said Wednesday on Newstalk 1010.
CAPTION: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the provincewill increase fertility program funding to $150 million in each of the next two years.

Subject: Fertility; Tax credits; In vitro fertilization; Funding

Document 290 of 500

Anonymously funded anti-Poilievre ad campaign has links to former Liberal, NDP staffers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/anonymously-funded-anti-poilievre-ad-campaign-has/docview/3120652960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Anonymously+funded+anti-Poilievre+ad+campaign+has+links+to+former+Liberal%2C+NDP+staffers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A third-party group run by former NDP and Liberal staffers has launched an advertising campaign against Pierre Poilievre, in an attempt to go "dollar for dollar" with the Conservatives on ad spending.
The campaign comes as the prospect of a snap election grows and as Liberals gripe over what they see as slow and inadequate attempts to contrast the Conservative leader's record with the Trudeau government through advertising.
The Tories, flush with cash from record fundraising, spent $8.5 million to get their message out last year - more than 20 times as much as the governing Liberals, who spent more than $380,000 on advertising.
Now, Protecting Canada, an anonymously funded group with ties to the NDP, Liberals and major unions, says it wants to "fill the vacuum" and unite progressives to stand up to the Conservative "media-machine that fuels hypocrisy."
"Our ambition is to match Conservative infrastructure, dollar for dollar, and spend as much as it takes," said spokesperson Megana Ramaswami.
But the financial backers of the group are shrouded in secrecy. Ramaswami, the only public face of the group, did not answer questions about specific funders, only saying the group was backed by progressive individuals and organizations wanting to unite Canadians against Poilievre.
Filings with the Ontario government shed some light on who's behind the non-profit group. Its directors are former senior NDP staffers Ian Wayne and David Hare; Bryan Leblanc, who worked for the Ontario Liberals in the Kathleen Wynne era and on former Liberal minister Sheila Copps's 2004 federal party leadership campaign; and former Canadian Labour Congress executive Barbara Byers.
Don Millar, an advertising specialist who has worked on federal Liberal campaigns and with former B.C. premier Christy Clark, a potential federal Liberal leadership candidate, is a former director of the group. He told the Star he is helping with advertising.
Ramaswami said Protecting Canada does not support any political party, and the NDP and federal and Ontario Liberal parties denied any connection with it. Efforts by the Star to contact the directors were either redirected to Ramaswami or not responded to.
The group, which formed in 2021 with an ad campaign against then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole in the months leading up to that year's election campaign, has launched its first anti-Poilievre televised ad, a 30-second clip attacking Poilievre's past votes for raising the retirement age and against boosting pensions, in contrast with his government pension.
Protecting Canada also plans to run ads attacking Poilievre on issues including gun control, health care, child care and abortion rights with a campaign dubbed "That's Poilievre. But it's just not Canada."
Elections Canada rules do not restrict third-party advertising until three months ahead of a scheduled election, or when an unscheduled campaign begins. Protecting Canada would only have to register - and disclose funding for its campaign - if it advertises during those periods.
The group has also spent at least $130,000 on digital ads in the last few weeks, according to Meta's ad library, which shows it outspent the Tories by $40,000 in the past week specifically. The vast majority of those ads have been in Ontario and B.C., in an attempt to target swing ridings the Tories hope can pave their way to a majority government.
The Liberals and NDP have spent less than $10,000 combined in the last month on Meta ads, though the New Democrats recently ran some anti-Poilievre ads. The Liberals have routinely attacked Poilievre, but without mass advertising, a point of contention among some in the party.
Trudeau recently defended the decision not to run political ads against Poilievre after he became Conservative leader, arguing it would have looked petty to voters if the Liberals took their eye off the bigger economic and financial woes facing Canadians. He has insisted there is still time to paint Poilievre as the risk to the environment, equality rights and social programs that the Liberals believe he is.
"Good luck to the prime minister to have a competitive fight if it's so lopsided," Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith said of the party's ad spending on Wednesday.
A spokesperson for Poilievre called the campaign a distraction from the federal government's "inflationary spending and carbon taxes."
"While the NDP-Liberal costly coalition and their insider friends will use dark money on their pathetic attempts to misinform Canadians, Pierre Poilievre and his common sense Conservatives will turn that hurt into hope and bring home more powerful paycheques, lower prices, and safer streets for all Canadians by axing the tax, building the homes, fixing the budget, and stopping the crime," Sebastian Skamski wrote in an email.
Unifor president Lana Payne said in a statement that "while Unifor has contributed to past campaigns," the union is not involved in the "current round of ads" from the group.
Canadian Labour Congress spokesperson Karyne Vienneau told the Star the CLC supports the message in the ad campaign and has had "chatter" about it with like-minded groups, but has not provided funding.
"The truth about Poilievre, his motivations, his policy positions on Canadians' pensions, our health-care system, needs to get out there," Vienneau said in an email.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period in the House of Commons on Oct. 22, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Canadians; Pensions; Advertising expenditures; Political advertising; Elections; Advertising campaigns

Document 291 of 500

Justin Trudeau 'disappointing' MPs who want him gone but other Liberals say he is still deciding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-disappointing-mps-who-want-him/docview/3120036420/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+%27disappointing%27+MPs+who+want+him+gone+but+other+Liberals+say+he+is+still+deciding&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The drama surrounding Justin Trudeau's leadership clouded another day on Parliament Hill, as dissenting Liberal MPs who want him to resign say they're disappointed the prime minister is trumpeting his intention to stay, while others suggested they don't believe he has yet made a final decision.
More than six Liberal sources have told the Star that Trudeau told his caucus he will remain as leader after he listened to roughly 20 of his MPs tell him behind closed doors on Wednesday that he should resign or at least reconsider whether his continued leadership is best for the party.
On Thursday, at a press conference on Parliament Hill, Trudeau repeated in public that he has no intention to step down, despite a demand in a letter from a group of 24 MPs asking him to respond to their call for him to resign by Monday.
Asked if he planned to stay on past that date, Trudeau simply said "yes," and later characterized the dissent over his leadership as part of his party's "robust conversations" about how best to defeat Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party, which has a strong and persistent lead in all publicly available national polls.
"We're going to continue to have great conversations about what is the best way to take on Pierre Poilievre in the next election, but that will happen with me as leader," Trudeau said.
Wayne Long, one of three Liberal MPs who have publicly confirmed they are among the dissenting group that wants Trudeau gone, told the Star on Thursday that the prime minister should take more time to consider what's best for the party.
Others who have called for Trudeau to resign include Prince Edward Island MP Sean Casey and Newfoundland's Ken McDonald. Multiple Liberal sources, who spoke on condition they not be identified since caucus deliberations are intended to be private, have told the Star that British Columbia MP Patrick Weiler and Toronto's Yvan Baker were also among those who told Trudeau this week that he should resign.
"There was a strong call yesterday asking him to step down," Long said. "I don't think 18 hours is enough time to really give pause. I honestly hoped he'd take the weekend.
"He needs to reflect more. A lot more."
Casey later told reporters that he finds Trudeau's decision "disappointing," but that he is personally moving on and focused on getting elected again in his P.E.I. riding.
"As far as I'm concerned, it's closed," he said.
Other Liberal MPs suggested Thursday that they don't believe Trudeau has truly decided on his future as leader, after hearing the grievances of so many of his parliamentarians this week.
"I'm confident he'll take the time to reflect," Baker told journalists on the Hill, after declining to say whether he supports the prime minister.
Quebec MP Alexandra Mendès - who said she has echoed her constituents' desire for Trudeau to resign, even though she thinks the opposition to him is "unfair" - told the Star that "of course" Trudeau has to tell the media he intends to stay on, because otherwise he would be a "lame duck" prime minister.
"He received a lot of information, a lot of thoughts and opinions from around the country. So, yeah - I'm sure he's going to take the time necessary to come to a decision one way or the other."
Long, meanwhile, said he is not willing to leave the Liberal caucus to sit as an independent, but that he's not sure what the dissenters can do if Trudeau refuses to resign.
"I love the Liberal party - in fact I'm trying to save it from itself," he said. "Look at Ontario. They still haven't recovered from the 2018 slaughter."
That was a reference to the provincial Liberals' crushing defeat under former premier Kathleen Wynne's leadership, when the party got walloped and dropped from 55 to seven seats at Queen's Park. The Ontario Liberals only gained one more seat in the following election in 2022, when they clinched eight ridings.
Concerns about such a bruising loss at the federal level are widespread in Liberal circles. One Liberal MP who is among those calling for Trudeau to resign - and spoke on condition of anonymity - said fears of such a defeat are the main factor behind their actions. If the Liberal government loses badly in the next election, the MP worried that a Conservative regime they view as wrong for the country could be in power for several years.
Those concerns also found voice in a grassroots petition circulating this week that declares the party is facing an "existential crisis." The petition demands that the federal caucus hold a secret ballot vote on Trudeau's leadership. It also calls for structural changes to improve party fundraising and riding-level organizing, and for a policy refresh to focus on policies to boost economic growth and wealth per capita.
Casey, the MP who has called for Trudeau to go, said this week he wished there was a formal mechanism for caucus to vote on Trudeau's leadership. And Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, the MP for Toronto's Beaches-East York, said he would support some sort of vote to provide "finality" on the question of whether Trudeau should stay.
Asked about that idea on Thursday, Health Minister Mark Holland dismissed a caucus vote as "nuts."
"That makes no sense at all," he said. "You're basically saying that a very small minority of people, a lot of whom aren't running again ... are going to impose that process ... I think that's profoundly unhelpful."
Holland suggested dissenters to Trudeau's leadership are missing how the Liberal government operates as a "team" that is more than just the person at the top.
"There is no one leader who's going to fix everything, and I think we should be very afraid of anybody who says there is," Holland said.
Several other MPs have also told the Star this week that support for Trudeau's leadership remains widespread inside the Liberal caucus. One MP, who spoke on condition of anonymity Thursday, told the Star there was a "massive round of applause" for caucus members who spoke in support of the prime minister's leadership at Wednesday's meeting.
The MP said that even some of those who called for Trudeau to resign this week said they would back him if he decides to stay.
CAPTION: Liberal MP Wayne Long saidthat the prime minister should take more time to consider what's best for the party.

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Document 292 of 500

How Ontario ended up desperately short of home-care supplies: 'It's chaos': Hamilton lung cancer patient needs equipment that is in scarce supply

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-ontario-ended-up-desperately-short-home-care/docview/3119911927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Ontario+ended+up+desperately+short+of+home-care+supplies%3A+%27It%27s+chaos%27%3A+Hamilton+lung+cancer+patient+needs+equipment+that+is+in+scarce+supply&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bruce Simpson wants to know who will be held accountable for potentially putting his life at risk.
The 59-year-old former Hamilton kindergarten teacher requires home-care equipment that has been in scarce supply across Ontario since the province suddenly implemented major changes on Sept. 24 to what provisions are available and how they are provided.
"It has fallen apart," Simpson said. "It's chaos."
Before the changes, Simpson said he reliably got all the supplies he needed for nurses to help him manage lung cancer at home.
"It was never a problem to get supplies and they were the same supplies that the hospital was using," Simpson said. "I didn't have any stress about delivery."
Since the provincewide changes in home care, the few supplies Simpson said he has received were the wrong fit or were not sterile. He said his home-care nurse already had to use nonsterile gloves once to drain fluid around his lungs despite Simpson being immunocompromised and vulnerable to infection.
"Someone has made this decision," Simpson said about the changes. "They say they're working hard to fix it. That's not really an apology. They did not apologize to me, who is more overwhelmed than I've ever been in my life."
For the first time, two separate sources with knowledge of the medical supply industry have spoken out about what went wrong to cause the ongoing crisis in home care. They are not named because they do not have permission from the province to speak publicly.
Both point to an overhaul of the provincial formulary - a listing of the drugs and supplies available to order for home- and community-care patients - as being one of the major reasons for the shortage.
They say the formulary came too late and held up ordering for the two companies that procure Ontario's home-care supplies - Cardinal Health and Ontario Medical Supply (OMS).
The sources also say the quantities set by Ontario Health atHome were far too low. The provincial agency co-ordinates in-home and community-based care for over 650,000 Ontario patients a year.
"It was completely devoid of reality as to what was needed," said one source, who is a manager with knowledge of the medical supply industry.
The source said Ontario Health atHome was told the quantities were wrong by companies that fulfil orders for home-care supplies and drugs.
"They wouldn't listen," said the source. "They're dealing with the consequences."
Ontario Health atHome did not answer The Spectator's questions about the sources' accusations, how the dire shortage happened or why it has gone on for a month.
"We take any impact to our patients very seriously, and we understand the distress the distribution issues are causing patients, their families and health-system partners," Ontario Health atHome said in a statement. "We are doing everything we can to address this situation and are continuing to look for opportunities to stabilize the delivery of these critical medical items."
The second issue at play was a major change in how the most recent five-year contracts were structured to get home-care supplies and infusion medicine delivered to patients.
Before Sept. 24, the manager said one organization would have the contract to fulfil orders for medical supplies and infusion medicine. The contract covered the geography of what used to be the local health integration networks (LHIN), so the province was split into 14 different areas, said the source.
In the restructuring, medical supplies and infusion medicine were split into two separate contracts and the geographic area covered was much larger, said the manager.
"They were trying to leverage Ontario's purchasing power," the manager said "Trying to trade size for lower price."
As a result, four companies each became responsible for delivering medical supplies to large swaths of the province: Bayshore Specialty Rx, OMS, Shoppers Drug Mart and Robinsons. A fifth company - Calea - was added to deliver infusion medicine to parts of Ontario.
In Hamilton, Niagara, Haldimand, Norfolk and Brant, the contract to deliver medical supplies went to Bayshore while OMS got Halton. For these same areas, Calea was awarded the contract to fulfil orders for medicine delivered intravenously such as antibiotics.
The manager said the trouble with being responsible for such a large area was that emergency supplies need to be delivered within four hours - up until midnight. There are also supplies that must be delivered same day by 9 p.m. or the next day.
The source said meeting these deadlines was doable in an area the size of a former LHIN, but became "enormously complicated" when covering massive areas of the province. For example, Bayshore is responsible for fulfilling orders for medical supplies from Windsor to Waterloo-Wellington and from North York to around Kingston.
"Bayshore works with community partners where a local presence for fulfilling orders in our contract areas is feasible," Bayshore said in a statement. "We will continue to add more local partners to ensure patients receive the supplies they need in a timely manner."
However since the new contracts started in September, health-care providers such as Dr. Drew Moore, a family physician in Trenton, Ont., said it has taken up to 48 hours for a pain pump to arrive that was supposed to be delivered within four hours.
The manager said the changes also led to at least one former longtime provider choosing not to bid on a medical supply contract. The office of NDP Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch said Calea did not bid to continue providing medical supplies to the former Hamilton Niagara Haldimand Brant LHIN as the company has done for years.
In addition, the manager said the new contracts contain penalty clauses that require roughly 98 per cent of the supplies to be delivered on time.
Ontario Health atHome did not answer questions about whether the penalties have been enforced during the supply crisis.
"Ontario Health atHome launched new contracts for medical equipment and supplies with the expiration of contracts presenting an opportunity to provide better access to standardized high-quality products across the province for our patients," stated Ontario Health atHome. "At this time, we are focused on solving the issues at hand ... Once these issues have been resolved, we can reflect on why this happened and take measures to prevent this from happening again."
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones also did not answer questions about how the shortage happened or why it has gone on so long.
"We know this is unacceptable and we are not going to allow this to continue," Jones said during question period at Queen's Park on Monday. "I am on it."
However, the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO) says the dire shortages never should have happened in the first place.
"All these issues could have been anticipated," CEO Doris Grinspun said. "We need a permanent solution and we need to learn not to repeat the errors ... This, yet again, devalues the importance of home care."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Dr. Doris Grinspun CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario says the shortages "devalue the importance ofhome care."Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said during question period at Queen's Park on Monday that the supply shortages are "unacceptable."RNAO photoChris Young The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com

Subject: Medicine; Shortages; Medical supplies; Nurses

Business indexing term: Subject: Shortages

Title: How Ontario ended up desperately short of home-care supplies:   'It's chaos': Hamilton lung cancer patient needs equipment that is in scarce supply

Document 293 of 500

Behind the scenes of how Ontario ended up desperately short of home-care supplies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/behind-scenes-how-ontario-ended-up-desperately/docview/3119911746/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Behind+the+scenes+of+how+Ontario+ended+up+desperately+short+of+home-care+supplies&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bruce Simpson wants to know who will be held accountable for potentially putting his life at risk.
The 59-year-old former Hamilton kindergarten teacher requires home-care equipment that has been in scarce supply across Ontario since the province suddenly implemented major changes on Sept. 24 to what provisions are available and how they are provided.
"It has fallen apart," Simpson said. "It's chaos."
Before the changes, Simpson said he reliably got all the supplies he needed for nurses to help him manage lung cancer at home.
"It was never a problem to get supplies and they were the same supplies that the hospital was using," Simpson said. "I didn't have any stress about delivery."
Since the provincewide changes in home care, the few supplies Simpson said he has received were the wrong fit or were not sterile. He said his home-care nurse already had to use nonsterile gloves once to drain fluid around his lungs despite Simpson being immunocompromised and vulnerable to infection.
"Someone has made this decision," Simpson said about the changes. "They say, they're working hard to fix it. That's not really an apology. They did not apologize to me, who is more overwhelmed than I've ever been in my life."
For the first time, two separate sources with knowledge of the medical supply industry have spoken out about what went wrong to cause the ongoing crisis in home care. They are not named because they do not have permission from the province to speak publicly.
Both point to an overhaul of the provincial formulary - a listing of the drugs and supplies available to order for home- and community-care patients - as being one of the major reasons for the shortage.
They say the formulary came too late and held up ordering for the two companies that procure Ontario's home-care supplies - Cardinal Health and Ontario Medical Supply (OMS).
The sources also say the quantities set by Ontario Health atHome were far too low. The provincial agency co-ordinates in-home and community-based care for over 650,000 Ontario patients a year.
"It was completely devoid of reality as to what was needed," said one source, who is a manager with knowledge of the medical supply industry.
The source said Ontario Health atHome was told the quantities were wrong by companies that fulfil orders for home-care supplies and drugs.
"They wouldn't listen," said the source. "They're dealing with the consequences."
Ontario Health atHome did not answer The Spectator's questions about the sources' accusations, how the dire shortage happened or why it has gone on for a month.
"We take any impact to our patients very seriously, and we understand the distress the distribution issues are causing patients, their families and health-system partners," Ontario Health atHome said in a statement. "We are doing everything we can to address this situation and are continuing to look for opportunities to stabilize the delivery of these critical medical items."
The second issue at play was a major change in how the most recent five-year contracts were structured to get home-care supplies and infusion medicine delivered to patients.
Before Sept. 24, the manager said one organization would have the contract to fulfil orders for medical supplies and infusion medicine. The contract covered the geography of what used to be the local health integration networks (LHIN), so the province was split into 14 different areas, said the source.
In the restructuring, medical supplies and infusion medicine were split into two separate contracts and the geographic area covered was much larger, said the manager.
"They were trying to leverage Ontario's purchasing power," the manager said "Trying to trade size for lower price."
As a result, four companies each became responsible for delivering medical supplies to large swaths of the province: Bayshore Specialty Rx, OMS, Shoppers Drug Mart and Robinsons. A fifth company - Calea - was added to deliver infusion medicine to parts of Ontario.
In Hamilton, Niagara, Haldimand, Norfolk and Brant, the contract to deliver medical supplies went to Bayshore while OMS got Halton. For these same areas, Calea was awarded the contract to fulfil orders for medicine delivered intravenously such as antibiotics.
The manager said the trouble with being responsible for such a large area was that emergency supplies need to be delivered within four hours - up until midnight. There are also supplies that must be delivered same day by 9 p.m. or the next day.
The source said meeting these deadlines was doable in an area the size of a former LHIN, but became "enormously complicated" when covering massive areas of the province. For example, Bayshore is responsible for fulfilling orders for medical supplies from Windsor to Waterloo-Wellington and from North York to around Kingston.
"Bayshore works with community partners where a local presence for fulfilling orders in our contract areas is feasible," Bayshore said in a statement. "We will continue to add more local partners to ensure patients receive the supplies they need in a timely manner."
However since the new contracts started in September, health-care providers such as Dr. Drew Moore, a family physician in Trenton, Ont., said it has taken up to 48 hours for a pain pump to arrive that was supposed to be delivered within four hours.
The manager said the changes also led to at least one former longtime provider choosing not to bid on a medical supply contract. The office of NDP Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch said Calea did not bid to continue providing medical supplies to the former Hamilton Niagara Haldimand Brant LHIN as the company has done for years.
In addition, the manager said the new contracts contain penalty clauses that require roughly 98 per cent of the supplies to be delivered on time.
Ontario Health atHome did not answer questions about whether the penalties have been enforced during the supply crisis.
"Ontario Health atHome launched new contracts for medical equipment and supplies with the expiration of contracts presenting an opportunity to provide better access to standardized high-quality products across the province for our patients," stated Ontario Health atHome. "At this time, we are focused on solving the issues at hand ... Once these issues have been resolved, we can reflect on why this happened and take measures to prevent this from happening again."
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones also did not answer questions about how the shortage happened or why it has gone on so long.
"We know this is unacceptable and we are not going to allow this to continue," Jones said during question period at Queen's Park on Monday. "I am on it."
However, the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO) says the dire shortages never should have happened in the first place.
"All these issues could have been anticipated," CEO Doris Grinspun said. "We need a permanent solution and we need to learn not to repeat the errors ... This, yet again, devalues the importance of home care."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Ontario has been short home care supplies since bringing in changes to what provisions are available and how they are provided on Sept. 24.Dr. Doris Grinspun CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO) says the shortages "devalue the importance of home care."Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said during question period at Queen's Park on Monday that the supply shortages are "unacceptable."

Liberals attempt show of unity after tense caucus meeting: MPs were tight-lipped about what happened behind the closed doors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-attempt-show-unity-after-tense-caucus/docview/3119910881/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+attempt+show+of+unity+after+tense+caucus+meeting%3A+MPs+were+tight-lipped+about+what+happened+behind+the+closed+doors&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told his MPs Wednesday that he would reflect on what they had to say in a three-hour caucus meeting where some Liberals confronted him about his leadership.
Trudeau was smiling when he left the meeting on Wednesday afternoon, and as he walked briskly past a horde of reporters on Parliament Hill he said only that the Liberal party is "strong and united."
While Liberal MPs were tight-lipped about what happened behind closed doors, most agreed with the prime minister that they were indeed united - though notably many expressed a unified desire to defeat the Conservatives rather than support for Trudeau.
"Yes there is some fatigue over Trudeau, it's true, it's clear," said Nova Scotia Liberal Kody Blois in French Wednesday.
"At the same time, I am a member of the Liberal party and I believe the best vehicle for the Canadians in the next election is the Liberal party, whether it's Mr. Trudeau or another option. I think our discussions in caucus were constructive... but we are united in the necessity to fight Poilievre."
Ontario Liberal MP Yvan Baker said it's up to Trudeau to decide whether he stays on as leader.
"I think our party is united on a path forward to make sure we're addressing the needs of Canadians, and stopping Pierre Poilievre," he said.
More than 50 people spoke at the meeting, said British Columbia MP Ken Hardie. He described the conversation as very respectful, and said that caucus must also reflect on what it has heard.
Trudeau made it clear that he feels he's the right person to lead the Liberals into the next election against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, Ontario Liberal MP Charles Souza said after the meeting.
"Justin Trudeau is reflecting and he's standing strong, and we're standing strong as well, as a Liberal party," Souza told reporters.
"We as a party recognize that the real threat here is Pierre Poilievre, and that's what we're fighting for."
Ontario Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith said the caucus was scheduled to hear details of the plan
for the next election from their newly appointed national campaign director Andrew Bevan, but the conversation about Trudeau's leadership pushed Bevan off the agenda.
"My colleagues need to turn the knives outwards and not inwards, and we need to focus on the most important thing, which is getting things done here in Parliament and taking the fight to Pierre Poilievre," he said.
Rumours have circulated for weeks that an undisclosed number of Liberal MPs have signed on to an effort to oust the prime minister in the hopes of improving the party's chances in the next election.
Trudeau has so far showed no signs that he plans to step down from the top job.
Poilievre gleefully poked Trudeau about the "chaos" in his caucus in question period, referencing the infighting within the Liberal party in each one of his questions, and demanding that Trudeau call an immediate election.
"He cannot fix what he broke because his caucus is revolting," Poilievre said.

Trudeau's personal poll numbers plummeted more than a year ago, and the Liberals have consistently trailed the Conservatives in national polls for a year.
Trudeau isn't just facing calls for his departure from within the caucus. A group of Liberal supporters launched a petition this week calling for caucus and the national executive of the party to vote on their confidence in Trudeau as leader in a secret ballot and report the results to Trudeau and party faithful.
The group won't yet say how many people have signed the petition.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way to caucus on Parliament Hill, Wednesday. Trudeau has so far showed no signs that he plans to step down.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Laura Osman, Rosa Saba and David Baxter The Canadian Press

Stiles says Jama won't run as NDP candidate: NDP leader ejected MPP Jama from provincial caucus last October for 'acting unilaterally too many times'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stiles-says-jama-wont-run-as-ndp-candidate/docview/3119910849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stiles+says+Jama+won%27t+run+as+NDP+candidate%3A+NDP+leader+ejected+MPP+Jama+from+provincial+caucus+last+October+for+%27acting+unilaterally+too+many+times%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles has ruled out taking back her controversial former MPP Sarah Jama as a candidate.
"As far as I know, and I'm the leader, it won't be her," Stiles said Tuesday on CBC Radio's "Metro Morning."
She had been asked by host David Common about the prospect of the Independent MPP for Hamilton Centre returning to the NDP fold for the next provincial election amid speculation Premier Doug Ford will call an early vote next year instead of waiting until the scheduled June 2026 date.
Stiles ejected Jama from the New Democratic caucus last October for "acting unilaterally too many times" and "undermining our work" in the wake of the Oct. 7, 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and that country's retaliatory strikes on Gaza.
Jama has been sitting as an Independent MPP following her ouster, which split the NDP caucus and alienated staunch supporters in a riding that has been solidly New Democrat for years.
Asked last month by the Star whether she would consider returning to the New Democrats, Jama replied in a Sept. 27 email, "I am prepared to run as an Independent, though I have always kept all options open and will continue to do so."
Jama declined to comment at the legislature on Tuesday.
"I'm not talking today," she said.
Stiles, who last month refused to explicitly rule out taking back Jama as a candidate, was asked by reporters after the legislature's daily question period about her comments four hours earlier on Metro Morning.
But she repeatedly refused to use the same wording in her answer, and was less definitive.
"This is not part of the conversation right now that we're having," Stiles said of Jama.
"The member is not a member of our caucus. She's confirmed she's running as an Independent, and we will be running NDP candidates in every riding ... our aim in the next election is to defeat this Conservative government."
Peter Graefe, an associate professor of political science at McMaster University in Hamilton, has told the Star that running an NDP candidate against Jama could split the progressive vote and allow a Liberal or Progressive Conservative to win the riding.

Document 296 of 500

Fall back: When does daylight saving time end in Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fall-back-when-does-daylight-saving-time-end/docview/3119910771/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fall+back%3A+When+does+daylight+saving+time+end+in+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's officially been autumn for weeks, but when do we turn the clocks back for fall daylight saving time?
Daylight saving time ends on Sunday, Nov. 3 in Canada. At 2 a.m., we roll back the clocks by one hour and gain an extra bit of sleep.
The practice can also be called "fall back" as clocks fall back by an hour, whereas in the spring we "spring forward" on time.
The general idea of adjusting time twice a year is to allow for better use of natural light, both in winter and summer. However, there's been a lot of chatter over the years about scrapping the practice.
Ontario passed a law back in 2020 to stop observing daylight saving time - however, nothing has happened with that because the Ontario plan is dependent on Quebec and New York state also scrapping the practice.
A petition on change.org started by Irene Shone, which has received more than 87,000 signatures as of this writing, is asking the Canadian government, as well as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario Premier Doug Ford and several government ministers, to stop the daylight saving time practice and to instead have a permanent standard time.
There are areas of Canada that don't observe daylight saving time, including Saskatchewan and Yukon, along with a few communities in several provinces, including a few areas of northwestern Ontario.
According to timeanddate.com, fewer than 40 per cent of the world's countries use daylight saving time. The publication points out that in North America, eight countries observe DST, 11 stopped and 20 never have.
Also, not all countries make the time change on the same dates. Canada's spring-forward and fall-back dates coincide with those of the United States.
History of daylight saving time
Canada, as a nation, first began using daylight saving time in 1918 during the First World War. Canada, the United States and much of Europe picked up the practice shortly after the Germans started it.
The German government needed ways to conserve energy for battle and needed its citizens to reduce the use of electric light and conserve fossil fuels, according to University Canada West. The idea was that by pushing clocks forward by an hour in the spring, it gave people more daylight hours during working hours and then people would also use less energy to light their homes.
In Canada, the idea was that daylight saving time could help increase production during the war.
After the First World War, Canada dropped the practice, but then brought it back during the Second World War.
While Canada as a nation started utilizing daylight saving time in 1918, the first instance of it happening in Canada is said to have happened a decade earlier.
According to timeanddate.com, Port Arthur, now Thunder Bay, turned its clocks forward by an hour on July 1, 1908, and turned it back on Sept. 1, 1908, but the community did not continue the practice in successive years.
CAPTION: Clocks will fall back an hour in November.

Subject: Daylight saving time; World War I

Location: United States--US; Canada

Doug Ford takes aim at bike lanes, touts rebate cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-takes-aim-at-bike-lanes-touts-rebate/docview/3120146272/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+takes+aim+at+bike+lanes%2C+touts+rebate+cheques&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Road-testing his campaign slogans for a possible spring election, Premier Doug Ford is railing against bike lanes on busy streets and boasting about rebate cheques for Ontarians.
Under fire in the legislature Wednesday from NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who was peppering the premier with questions about the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, he appeared to tip his hand on themes for a spring election.
"People know life is better in Ontario than it was six years ago," Ford chided Stiles.
"They have certainty now, they have jobs now, they're bringing home a paycheque that they never had before under your left-wing regime of 'don't build anything, let's kill businesses, let's all go around and hop on our bicycle' and go down the crowded streets that are just absolutely insanity right now on Bloor, on Yonge, on University," the premier said.
That's a reference to the bike lanes on major thoroughfares the Progressive Conservatives warn contribute to gridlock, which is why they tabled legislation Monday to "review" them.
"No, people want reasonable judgment, and that's what we're giving the people of Ontario. We're putting money back into their pockets instead of your greedy little pockets," he scolded the opposition New Democrats.
Speaking later Wednesday to radio host Deb Hutton on Newstalk 1010, Ford emphasized "it's not the government's money, it's taxpayers' money."
"Anyone who doesn't want it, send it to the Treasury of Ontario, send your cheque back," he said.
As first revealed by the Star last week, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will unveil payments of at least $200 to all 16 million Ontario adults and children in his fall economic statement next Wednesday.
Bethlenfalvy was tight-lipped when pressed about the rebate cheques.
"I'm not going to speculate what may or may not be in the fall economic statement, but what I can tell you is that since day one, our government has been focused on affordability, cost of living," he told reporters at Queen's Park.
Stiles said the Tories are "delusional" if they believe things are better today than they were when Ford took office in 2018.
"I'll tell you how people in Ontario are feeling right now after six years of this government: They're feeling stuck; they're struggling; they're stuck on wait-lists; they're stuck, yes, in traffic; they're stuck with a government that isn't interested in fixing their problems," she said.
The premier insisted to Newstalk 1010's Hutton that "we aren't against bike lanes," but said the government has been inundated with complaints about the bike lanes on Bloor and Yonge streets and University Avenue.
"It's insanity," he said. "Put them on the secondary roads and let the main arterial roads flow with the traffic."

Document 298 of 500

Ford government says Ontario needs more nuclear power to tackle rising demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-says-ontario-needs-more-nuclear/docview/3120146188/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+says+Ontario+needs+more+nuclear+power+to+tackle+rising+demand&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians can expect more nuclear power and a "significant expansion" of energy efficiency programs for families and businesses, says Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce.
The changes from Premier Doug Ford's government will come in a new energy plan being developed for release early next year to meet a forecast surge in demand for electricity by 2050.
As environmentalists call for more renewable energy like solar and wind power because of falling prices for those technologies, Lecce told reporters Wednesday that "nuclear energy will be the overarching focus of our expansion plan."
That's in addition to extensions, refurbishment and additions planned or underway to nuclear generating stations at Pickering, Bruce and Darlington, where a small modular reactor is also being built, Lecce said before introducing his proposed Affordable Energy Act in the legislature.
The bill sets a framework for developing an "integrated" energy plan for the next 25 years involving nuclear, natural gas and gasoline as well as more charging stations for electric vehicles.
But the prospect of more fossil fuel use raised concerns among critics as the world combats climate change and its effects on the environment, from air quality to higher temperatures and flooding from increasingly severe storms.
"Every time they talk about this plan, they never talk about renewable, even though the lowest-cost source of electricity generation anywhere in the world right now is solar, wind and storage," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"The lowest-hanging fruit to rapidly increase electricity supply, and to do it at low cost, is putting solar panels on all these roofs that you see all over the GTA."
Opponents of more nuclear-generated power also raise concerns about huge price tags for nuclear plants and the long-term radioactive waste they produce.
But Lecce did not rule out new nuclear plants or more new reactors at existing nuclear generating stations.
"We're going to need to bring forth additional options," Lecce said, describing nuclear power as the "only way we can reduce (carbon) emissions" while increasing the electricity supply to meet expected growth in the economy.
Last week, the province's Independent Electricity System Operator - which manages the day-to-day needs of the grid to keep the lights on and handles long-term planning - forecast the need for power will soar 75 per cent by 2050.
That's up from a previous forecast of a 60 per cent increase because of an explosion in energy-hungry data centres using artificial intelligence, population growth, growing electrification of cars, public transit, home heating, electric vehicle battery plants, and steelmaking as it transitions away from coal-fired to electric arc furnaces.
The forecast increase in demand is roughly equal to adding four-and-a-half cities the size of Toronto to the grid, said Lecce.
Consultations will soon begin on the plan with the public and interested stakeholders, but Lecce would not commit to public hearings - something Schreiner said is necessary for "transparency," especially with Ford considering an early provincial election next spring instead of waiting until June 2026 as scheduled.
"This is a major, long-term energy plan, and for them to not conduct any public hearings around it, I don't think that's adequate," the Green leader added, citing a short timeline for the process.
He called for building codes to be changed to require new homes to be "solar ready" so that a larger electrical box and wiring is in place for homeowners who want to install solar panels on the roof. Electrical panels should also be big enough to handle heat pumps instead of gas furnaces, and to provide for the installation of EV chargers, he said.
CAPTION: Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce is shown in Clarington, Ont. on June 14, 2024.

Subject: Expansion; Nuclear energy; Alternative energy sources; Electricity; Public hearings; Electric vehicles

Title: Ford government says Ontario needs more nuclear power to tackle rising demand

Document 299 of 500

Doug Ford orders LCBO to try again after it orders paper bags from Quebec

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-orders-lcbo-try-again-after-paper-bags/docview/3120146105/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+orders+LCBO+to+try+again+after+it+orders+paper+bags+from+Quebec&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The deal was in the bag. Until it wasn't.
After Premier Doug Ford directed the LCBO last April to end its environmentally-conscious bag ban and bring back paper bags for customers, the liquor store did so.
But a $10-million contract for bags was signed with a Quebec company, and the government - disappointed in the choice - has issued a new directive to seek bids from Ontario bag makers.
In a letter to LCBO chair Carmine Nigro this week, Ford wrote the decision "goes against our government's direction" in a July memorandum to the LCBO from Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy to source paper bags "from local pulp and paper sources when appropriate."
"We've been very clear we want them to use Ontario companies ... I think Ontario companies are the best around," Bethlenfalvy told reporters Wednesday.
"People want paper bags, which I think is very popular."
The LCBO said in a statement it plans to request bids from Ontario companies "at the soonest available opportunity."
The contract for Quebec-made bags from Rosenbloom Inc. remains in place and bags will be shipped to the LCBO's 670 stores "in the coming weeks," the liquor retailer added.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles questioned why the province doesn't have an established "Ontario first" procurement process.
"Ultimately, it's the government that's responsible for these decisions ... why is it they can't seem to do anything right?"
Other opposition parties said Fordshould be more concerned about major problems facing the province as he angles for an early election next spring instead of waiting for the slated date in June 2026.
"It is ridiculous that the premier is focused on bags at the LCBO when we have a record number of people experiencing homelessness, 2.5 million people can't access a doctor, our children are having to learn in overcrowded classrooms, and our infrastructure clearly isn't ready for the kind of floods we had last summer," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"This is serious stuff," added Liberal house leader John Fraser, MPP for Ottawa South. "He's got a serious job and his job is to take care of people's schools, their hospitals, their health care, and make sure they have an affordable place to live. It's maddening."
Urging customers to bring their own bags or buy reusable ones, the LCBO stopped supplying free paper bags in September 2023, saying the move would save 188,000 trees annually and divert 2,6654 tonnes of waste from landfill. Plastic bags were phased out in 2008.
Last spring, Ford questioned the merits of the end of free paper bags, calling them "an easily recyclable alternative to single-use plastic, which is why the LCBO adopted them in the first place."
With files from Kristin Rushowy
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford stands in the house as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Liquor

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 300 of 500

Liberal MPs emerge during caucus meeting with Justin Trudeau: 'The prime minister has to listen to the frustrations'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-mps-emerge-during-caucus-meeting-with/docview/3119660223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MPs+emerge+during+caucus+meeting+with+Justin+Trudeau%3A+%27The+prime+minister+has+to+listen+to+the+frustrations%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A critical caucus meeting was running more than an hour late Wednesday as Liberal MPs vented frustrations to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in person.
One of them, Toronto MP Nate Erskine-Smith, emerged and told reporters that he spoke in caucus and delivered a twofold message, urging Trudeau to take the dissent seriously and for his colleagues to end "the palace intrigue."
"The prime minister has to listen to the frustrations, and in some cases, really valid frustrations, of caucus colleagues, and incorporate that into changes moving forward, whether those are big changes or small changes or both," he said.
"And the second thing is, my colleagues need to turn the knives outwards and not inwards, and we need to focus on the most important thing, which is getting things done here in Parliament and taking the fight to Pierre Poilievre, because he's a disaster for this country."
Many other MPs said only that there was a "good discussion" behind closed doors
Immigration Minister Marc Miller wouldn't comment on the specifics but said MPs were "quite honest" in sharing their thoughts. Liberal MP John McKay told reporters the decision to leave is the prime minister's alone, but said MPs had "candid conversations" that he appreciated being a part of.
Erskine-Smith predicted the talks would go on for days to come, with the presentation of election plans that was supposed to have been delivered Wednesday being sidelined because of the internal turmoil.
Trudeau was all smiles as he walked into the meeting Wednesday morning, where dissidents in his own ranks were prepared to challenge his leadership while his supporters harshly condemned the damaging show of disunity.
At the same time, a grassroots petition describing an "existential crisis" in the Liberal party was demanding an urgent secret ballot vote - both inside the caucus room and at the party's national executive - on whether Trudeau really should stay on in the face of months of bad polls that place his party well behind Poilievre's Conservatives.
With Parliament Hill abuzz with speculation about Trudeau's future, entrances to West Block were jammed with reporters and staffers keen to press MPs on what will happen inside the morning's high stakes Liberal caucus meeting.
Francis Drouin, a Liberal MP from eastern Ontario, was among those to denounce the MPs involved in the push against Trudeau. As the Star has reported, more than 20 Liberal MPs have signed a letter calling on the prime minister to resign to make way for a new leader to better take on Poilievre and defend the legacy of the Liberal government's nine years in power.
"At some point, to keep knifing like this? Like, put up or shut up," Drouin said.
Judy Sgro said the dissenters have damaged the Liberal party and should have privately raised their concerns with the prime minister.
"When you look divided, you look weak," she said. "It's not necessary to do what is going on today."
Sameer Zuberi pushed back against the "put up or shut up" sentiment, saying it's the first time caucus is having this important conversation and he doesn't expect today to be the end of it.
The petition describing a crisis in the party - titled Code Red Petition - was started by a group of mostly young Liberals, said Andrew Perez, a Liberal strategist and political consultant acting as a spokesperson for the petition organizers. The petition is accompanied by a memorandum that details how the modern Liberal party is struggling in many corners of the country, and names Poilievre as "our enemy" who has harnessed the power of the internet to win support and rake in more donations than the federal Liberals.
"We are hoping that this petition lights a fire for the party leadership," said Perez, who added that "structural changes" are needed for the Liberals to improve fundraising and grassroots organizing.
While he declined to say how many people are behind the petition, he said it has been sent to "tens of thousands" of Liberals this week.
He also said the petition "is about much more than leadership," arguing the party brass should be accountable given events of recent months - including two byelection losses in long-safe Liberal seats, the departure of campaign director Jeremy Broadhurst and a host of cabinet ministers, and the recent caucus dissent.
The document also criticizes the Trudeau government's "policy problem," saying gross domestic product (GDP) per capita is too low and demanding fresh ideas to focus on productivity and economic growth. It calls for the party to address "the leadership issue" through a secret ballot vote in caucus and at the party's national executive, and that if Trudeau decides to resign he should announce his choice as quickly as possible.
Several MPs ignored questions about the petition's call for a secret ballot vote on Wednesday, although some expressed support for the idea.
Sean Casey, an MP from Prince Edward Island who is among the three MPs who have publicly called for Trudeau to go, told reporters on his way into the caucus meeting that he wished there was a formal mechanism to hold such a vote.
Unlike the Conservatives, who ousted Erin O'Toole as leader in a caucus vote, the Liberals decided not to adopt parliamentary rules that give their caucus that power.
Before the meeting, Erskine-Smith said he didn't expect a secret ballot vote on Wednesday. But he said he would like to see some sort of future vote, possibly in Trudeau's caucus, to provide "finality" to the question of the prime minister's leadership.
"I think a secret ballot vote in caucus, you know, at some time in the foreseeable future, makes a certain amount of sense," said Erskine-Smith, who has also voiced support for a partywide vote on the leader's status.
Helena Jaczek, a former Ontario and federal cabinet minister dropped by Trudeau after she said she wasn't running next election, told reporters ahead of the meeting, "I'm sure the prime minister will be taking it all in and listening intently, and no doubt he'll want to take a few days to consider his options."
Asked if she favours a secret ballot to test caucus confidence in the prime minister, she said, "I'm in favour of democracy at all times."
On a day when Canada's central bank cut the interest rate by half a percentage point, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland hung back and waited for cabinet colleague Lawrence MacAulay to chat with her and then walked briskly past cameras without commenting on either the rate cut or the caucus dissenters.
Down the hall, television cameras from all networks had set up outside the Commons chamber for live reporting on the critical caucus meeting.
Liberal MP Ruby Sahota predicted the dissent would be quelled because "a lot of the concerns you've been hearing are about a campaign chair and things like that. Those things are getting put in place, or have been put in place. And I think because of that, you're going to see that some of those concerns are going to be addressed."
Yukon MP Brendan Hanley, chair of prairies and northern caucus, told the Star that he expected a "very robust, very emotional conversation. And obviously there's going to be lots of different points of view expressed. Whether it ends today's up to the prime minister," adding he hoped the meeting gives Trudeau a lot to "reflect on."
But Hanley, who said he is running again, declined to state whether he personally had confidence in the prime minister.
Natural Resources Minister John Wilkinson said the caucus meeting would be a chance to air all the complaints.
"Everybody is a member of Parliament are free to voice their opinions at the end of the day, though, we do expect that we are a team, and it's important that we actually find pathways through which we can come to common agreement."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way to caucus on Parliament Hill on Oct. 23, 2024 in Ottawa.

Document 301 of 500

Oct. 23: Ticat Garney Henley was the G.O.A.T., provincial policies neglect the vulnerable and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-23-ticat-garney-henley-was-g-o-t-provincial/docview/3119504761/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+23%3A+Ticat+Garney+Henley+was+the+G.O.A.T.%2C+provincial+policies+neglect+the+vulnerable+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Garney was the G.O.A.T.
Re: Ticat legend Garney Henley was great. His story is even better, Oct. 22
He played defence. He played offence. He returned kickoffs and was the best at returning third-down punts, when in his day there was no blocking! Garney Henley's number should have been retired the day after he announced "retirement." He, in my opinion, is Canadian football's greatest of all time.
Dan Zilik, Stoney Creek
Parks need to be cleaned up
Re: Hamilton to make seven lower-city green spaces no go zones for encampments, Oct. 17
I applaud the fact that at long last Hamilton council has decided to give its green space back to its residents. Now that encampments will be banned in seven areas, I would be interested to know what the cost will be to remediate these spaces for public use. Clearly, they must be cleared and sanitized with a fine-toothed comb what with the likelihood of discarded needles and drug paraphernalia on site.
David Shupe, Dundas
Policies neglect the vulnerable
Re: Living on ODSP should not mean living in poverty, Oct. 19
In the case of ODSP, the broader factor at play is the increasing cost of living, which is pushing vulnerable populations deeper into poverty. In cities, where affordable housing is scarce and public services are strained, we need to foster an environment that allows for economic growth.
Surviving off $42 a day is outrageous. It leaves disabled individuals to face impossible choices between rent, food and medical care. As it stands, Hamilton is facing a growing housing affordability crisis, and the limited ODSP payments are pricing recipients out of the market.
Cities thrive on diversity and inclusion, but when urban policies neglect the needs of vulnerable groups, we create spaces of exclusion and inequality. It would be ideal for provincial funding to be reallocated toward these groups so that the ODSP rates can reflect the real costs of urban living. By providing adequate support to these individuals the local economy would be simulated as these recipients will be better equipped to afford basic necessities and participate in their communities. Municipalities should take this as a call to prioritize affordable housing solutions, ensuring that cities like Hamilton remain inclusive and accessible for all.
Eliza Marshall, Burlington
Doug Ford sizes up Ontario voters
There is an old anecdote attributed to Oscar Wilde. He is at a dinner party, seated beside an elegant lady. He asks whether she would consider spending the night with him for 1 million pounds. She is flustered, but upon consideration says "Yes." He then asks if she would go to bed with him for five shillings. She indignantly exclaims, "Of course not, what kind of woman do you take me for?" Wilde then replied: "We've already established that, madam. Now we are just haggling over the price."
Would you vote for Doug Ford's Conservatives for $1 million? How about $200? Where does the money come from, if taxes haven't increased, to finance Ford's enticements? No car licence renewal fee ($1 billion annually), $200 rebates ($3 billion), alcohol in corner stores, subsidized gas prices and pointless television ads (telling us how lucky we are to be living in Conservative Ontario). Ford takes populism to a new level.
David Woods, Dundas
Province going too far on bike lanes
Many residents of Hamilton survive on public transportation to get around the city, which includes the bike lanes connecting many communities for pedestrian and cyclist traffic alike. The provincial government using its power to overreach and prevent Hamilton and other municipalities from constructing bike lanes was already uncalled-for, but they have once again gone too far with their outrageous announcement they will force cities to remove existing bike lanes if they "impede the flow of traffic."
Over the years, countless studies have been conducted and have shown that bike lanes in urban areas not only reduce the number of vehicles on the road, but also make the roads safer for those inside and outside the vehicle. Considering the plethora of evidence supporting bike lane infrastructure in our cities, it is preposterous that the provincial government still chooses to throw away years of advocacy toward more sustainable methods of transportation for an agenda which will make traffic worse, going against their supposed goal of clearing gridlock from our city's streets. With more strides than ever being taken toward public transportation with the construction of the Hamilton LRT and greater connectivity between municipalities through GO Transit, bike lanes are crucial for the beginning and end of the journey between the train station or bus stop, and one's home.
If this half-baked plan does come to fruition, I think the premier's words on the issue will come to be true, "It (will be) an absolute disaster (Mr. Ford)."
Dominic Mothe, Hamilton
Make affordable housing a priority
Re: Kiwanis rolling out affordable home ownership pilot to the wider public, Oct. 20
Affordable housing within the city of Hamilton continues to be a serious issue. While initiatives by Kiwanis offer promising steps toward affordable housing, Hamilton's housing crisis requires larger and more inclusive plans to tackle the complexity of this issue. What Hamilton needs is a complete strategy that tackles both the issues of lack of housing and affordability.
Hamilton is going to need a direction of focus that prioritizes housing for all levels of income. We need a structural change that will allow the residents of Hamilton to live and create a future life here. We need to focus more on inclusive zoning, which will increase affordable housing, promote more diversity, and balance the housing market. Additionally, we need to have stronger rules and policies on speculative buying. Housing prices have risen 47 per cent in the last five years, and speculative buying plays a large contribution to this increase.
Hamilton must prioritize creating affordable housing for everyone and stop using the housing market as a commodity. Pushing for comprehensive structural changes instead of using Band-Aid solutions like Kiwanis is doing, will create real opportunities for home ownership. This will make Hamilton a place where all residents can live, pursue a successful life, and contribute to Hamilton's economy.
Jake May, Sault Ste. Marie
Attention for actor long overdue
Re: Special film night will feature silent movie with Hamilton's Florence Lawrence who became America's first movie star, Oct. 8
Thanks to Mark McNeil for his beautiful piece. Who knew one of America's first movie stars, Florence Lawrence, was from Hamilton? She also helped design improvements to cars, like the turn and brake signal technology that wasn't featured on cars at the time, and co-founded the Victor Film Company. Wow! Ms. Lawrence was a captivating woman. If anything, a plaque telling Lawrence's story should be unveiled in Hamilton, undoubtedly. But thanks again, to Mr. McNeil for bringing Florence Lawrence to life; thanks also to The Spec for featuring the article on the front page. Bravo.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Separate school system 'archaic'
Re: Brantford Catholic trustees backpedal after Italy art trip backlash, promise to pay back $45K travel tab, Oct. 18
The notion of publicly funded Catholic schools is as archaic as tunnelling under the 401. So do I expect this to end? Likely not under Doug Ford's reign. This latest scandal, however, would be a great launch pad to begin considering their abolishment. If Catholics want their own schools, let the billion-dollar church coffers pay for them. And while I have you here, let's start a conversation about churches and religious intuitions paying their share of taxes. Wouldn't it be nice if society were more socially progressive and less elite? One can dream, and vote.
John Zinck, Hamilton
Halloween humour is subjective
Re: Disability is not a Halloween costume, Oct. 19
Those who know me well are aware that I find humour in strange places. Keya Osborne's letter is such a case. As a totally blind person, I found her description of the "blind referee" costume quite funny, laughing out loud. In short, I'll decide whether or not I'm offended without any assistance.
Jerry Pryde, Stoney Creek
I know what it's like to be rendered invisible
Re: Political correctness not appreciated, Oct. 17
I sympathize with Sylvia Teaves when she wrote her gender identity was ignored by Dr. Rohan D'Souza, referring to "pregnant people" rather than women. As a non-binary trans person, I absolutely know what it's like to be rendered invisible by mainstream gynaecologists as well. It's great for me that Dr. D'Souza knows that gender minorities exist and uses language to reduce bias. I also understand when people of the privileged majority think they lose something if they don't get special treatment. Letting minorities into health care might mean there's less gynecological care for women, right? With the shortage of doctors, I wish Sylvia luck in finding the woman-only care she needs.
In the meantime, if you're feeling cisgender binary fragile, you can always go out into public, use the toilets, go shopping in a clothing store or fill out any form available and you will find that gender minorities don't exist. So you can still have your privilege in everyday life, despite outriders who are interested in including minorities and following the human rights code.
Key Straughan, Hamilton
A reminder to leave the leaves
In his Oct. 20 column, "In the Garden: Here we go again," David Hobson discussed possible options for what someone could do to handle the leaves on their lawn. I feel that the article could have dived deeper into the ecological effects of how this important idea can be extremely beneficial to local flora and fauna.
I admire Hobson's attempt to get people interested in and educated on the idea of alternative options, but the column could have gone further in depth when talking about the impacts of these actions. For the health of the environment it could have been more beneficial to teach people living in our urban environments to "leave the leaves." This motto encourages anyone with any amount of property or lawn to simply leave their leaves alone in the fall. This ensures hiding spots for important insect life over the winter which has positive impacts on other populations up the food chain. Leaves in landfills contribute to organic waste and the release of toxic greenhouse gases, but leaving your leaves will help suppress weeds, provide healthier soil, and have positive impacts on wildlife.
Although Hobson goes into detail about how using fallen leaves for one's garden or yard could be beneficial to them, this text lacks information about the larger ecological implications. Ultimately, the best thing to do with your leaves really is to leave them.
Katie Wildeman, Flamborough
We can't look away from Gaza
Re: The world is failing the children of Gaza, Oct. 19
Thanks to the Spectator for having the courage to publish the Save The Children description of the continuing genocide in Gaza.
The entire Gaza Strip is less than one-third the size of Hamilton, but in the last year it has endured more tonnes of bombs - almost all supplied by the U.S. - than were dropped in the Second World War.
We cannot look away from this. To do so would normalize mass murder and thus diminish the horror of earlier genocides like the Holocaust.
Don McLean, Hamilton
CAPTION: Owen Moore and Florence Lawrence in the 1912 silent film drama "The Redemption of Riverton." The Hamilton actress was one of America's first film stars.Garney Henley, left, and Angelo Mosca celebrate after the Ticats beat Saskatchewan at Ivor Wynne Stadium in 1972.
