Document 302 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3119504575/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Garney was
the G.O.A.T.
Re: Ticat legend Garney
Henley was great. His story
is even better, Oct. 22
He played defence. He played offence. He returned kickoffs and was the best at returning third-down punts, when in his day there was no blocking! Garney Henley's number should have been retired the day after he announced "retirement."
He, in my opinion, is Canadian football's greatest of all time.
Dan Zilik, Stoney Creek
Parks need
to be cleaned up
Re: Hamilton to make seven lower-city green spaces no go zones for encampments,
Oct. 17
I applaud the fact that at long last Hamilton council has decided to give its green space back to its residents. Now that encampments will be banned in seven areas, I would be interested to know what the cost will be to remediate these spaces for public use.
Clearly, they must be cleared and sanitized with a fine-toothed comb what with the likelihood of discarded needles and drug paraphernalia on site.
David Shupe, Dundas
Policies neglect the vulnerable
Re: Living on ODSP should not mean living in poverty, Oct. 19
In the case of ODSP, the broader factor at play is the increasing cost of living, which is pushing vulnerable populations deeper into poverty. In cities, where affordable housing is scarce and public services are strained, we need to foster an environment that allows for economic growth.
Surviving off $42 a day is outrageous. It leaves disabled individuals to face impossible choices between rent, food and medical care. As it stands, Hamilton is facing a growing housing affordability crisis, and the limited ODSP payments are pricing recipients out of the market.
Cities thrive on diversity and inclusion, but when urban policies neglect the needs of vulnerable groups, we create spaces of exclusion and inequality. It would be ideal for provincial funding to be reallocated toward these groups so that the ODSP rates can reflect the real costs of urban living.
By providing adequate support to these individuals the local economy would be stimulated as these recipients will be better equipped to afford basic necessities and participate in their communities.
Municipalities should take this as a call to prioritize affordable housing solutions, ensuring that cities like Hamilton remain inclusive and accessible for all.
Eliza Marshall, Burlington
Doug Ford sizes up Ontario voters
There is an old anecdote attributed to Oscar Wilde. He is at a dinner party, seated beside an elegant lady. He asks whether she would consider spending the night with him for 1 million pounds. She is flustered, but upon consideration says "Yes."
He then asks if she would go to bed with him for five shillings. She indignantly exclaims, "Of course not, what kind of woman do you take me for?" Wilde then replied: "We've already established that, madam. Now we are just haggling over the price."
Would you vote for Doug Ford's Conservatives for $1 million? How about $200? Where does the money come from, if taxes haven't increased, to finance Ford's enticements? No car licence renewal fee ($1 billion annually), $200 rebates ($3 billion), alcohol in corner stores, subsidized gas prices and pointless television ads (telling us how lucky we are to be living in Conservative Ontario). Ford takes populism to a new level.
David Woods, Dundas
Make affordable housing a priority
Re: Kiwanis rolling out affordable home ownership pilot to the wider public, Oct. 20
Affordable housing within the city of Hamilton continues to be a serious issue. While initiatives by Kiwanis offer promising steps toward affordable housing, Hamilton's housing crisis requires larger and more inclusive plans to tackle the complexity of this issue. What Hamilton needs is a complete strategy that tackles both the issues of lack of housing and affordability.
Hamilton is going to need a direction of focus that prioritizes housing for all levels of income.
We need a structural change that will allow the residents of Hamilton to live and create a future life here. We need to focus more on inclusive zoning, which will increase affordable housing, promote more diversity, and balance the housing market.
Additionally, we need to have stronger rules and policies on speculative buying. Housing prices have risen 47 per cent in the last five years, and speculative buying plays a large contribution to this increase.
Hamilton must prioritize creating affordable housing for everyone and stop using the housing market as a commodity.
Pushing for comprehensive structural changes instead of using Band-Aid solutions like Kiwanis is doing, will create real opportunities for home ownership. This will make Hamilton a place where all residents can live, pursue a successful life and contribute to Hamilton's economy.
Jake May, Sault Ste. Marie
Housing is
a basic need
According to "Hamilton renters need to make at least $33 per hour to afford a new apartment" (Oct.21) and Zoocasa's data, the income required to purchase a house is much higher than the median income, which fully reflects the pressure of the housing market and the economic burden on residents. Considering the continuous rise in rent, the economic pressure on low-income and social assistance dependent populations is increasing. This will lead to an increase in homelessness. The government needs to intervene more actively. Housing is a fundamental need and people's desire is simple - to have a place to live. It is time to restrain tenants from uncontrolled price increases. But controlling price increases is only part of it.
I call for the development of more social housing and affordable housing to alleviate market pressure. In the long run, balancing supply and demand is a better approach.
Shiyi Zeng, Hamilton
Spotlight is
long overdue
Re: Special film night will feature silent movie with Hamilton's Florence Lawrence who became America's first movie star, Oct. 8
Thanks to Mark McNeil for his beautiful piece. Who knew one of America's first movie stars, Florence Lawrence, was from Hamilton? She also helped design improvements to cars, like the turn and brake signal technology that wasn't featured on cars at the time, and co-founded the Victor Film Company. Wow! Ms. Lawrence was a captivating woman.
If anything, a plaque telling Lawrence's story should be unveiled in Hamilton, undoubtedly. But thanks again, to Mr. McNeil for bringing Florence Lawrence to life; thanks also to The Spec for featuring the article on the front page. Bravo.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Province goes too far on bike lanes
Many residents of Hamilton survive on public transportation to get around the city, which includes the bike lanes connecting many communities for pedestrian and cyclist traffic alike.
The provincial government using its power to overreach and prevent Hamilton and other municipalities from constructing bike lanes was already uncalled-for, but they have once again gone too far with their outrageous announcement they will force cities to remove existing bike lanes if they "impede the flow of traffic."
Over the years, countless studies have been conducted and have shown that bike lanes in urban areas not only reduce the number of vehicles on the road, but also make the roads safer for those inside and outside the vehicle.
Considering the plethora of evidence supporting bike lane infrastructure in our cities, it is preposterous that the provincial government still chooses to throw away years of advocacy toward more sustainable methods of transportation for an agenda which will make traffic worse, going against their supposed goal of clearing gridlock from our city's streets. With more strides than ever being taken toward public transportation with the construction of the Hamilton LRT and greater connectivity between municipalities through GO Transit, bike lanes are crucial for the beginning and end of the journey between the train station or bus stop, and one's home.
If this half-baked plan does come to fruition, I think the premier's words on the issue will come to be true, "It (will be) an absolute disaster (Mr. Ford)."
Dominic Mothe, Hamilton
A reminder to leave the leaves
In his Oct. 20 column, "In the Garden: Here we go again," David Hobson discussed possible options for what someone could do to handle the leaves on their lawn. I feel that the article could have dived deeper into the ecological effects of how this important idea can be extremely beneficial to local flora and fauna.
I admire Hobson's attempt to get people interested in and educated on the idea of alternative options, but the column could have gone further in depth when talking about the impacts of these actions.
For the health of the environment it could have been more beneficial to teach people living in our urban environments to "leave the leaves." This motto encourages anyone with any amount of property or lawn to simply leave their leaves alone in the fall. This ensures hiding spots for important insect life over the winter which has positive impacts on other populations up the food chain. Leaves in landfills contribute to organic waste and the release of toxic greenhouse gases, but leaving your leaves will help suppress weeds, provide healthier soil and have positive impacts on wildlife.
Although Hobson goes into detail about how using fallen leaves for one's garden or yard could be beneficial to them, this text lacks information about the larger ecological implications. Ultimately, the best thing to do with your leaves really is to leave them.
Katie Wildeman,
Flamborough
I know what it's like to be unseen
Re: Political correctness not appreciated, Oct. 17
I sympathize with Sylvia Teaves when she wrote her gender identity was ignored by Dr. Rohan D'Souza, referring to "pregnant people" rather than women. As a non-binary trans person, I absolutely know what it's like to be rendered invisible by mainstream gynaecologists as well. It's great for me that Dr. D'Souza knows that gender minorities exist and uses language to reduce bias. I also understand when people of the privileged majority think they lose something if they don't get special treatment. Letting minorities into health care might mean there's less gynecological care for women, right? With the shortage of doctors, I wish Sylvia luck in finding the woman-only care she needs.
In the meantime, if you're feeling cisgender binary fragile, you can always go out into public, use the toilets, go shopping in a clothing store or fill out any form available and you will find that gender minorities don't exist. So you can still have your privilege in everyday life, despite outriders who are interested in including minorities and following the human rights code.
Key Straughan, Hamilton
Humour
is subjective
Re: Disability is not a
Halloween costume, Oct. 19
Those who know me well are aware that I find humour in strange places. Keya Osborne's letter is such a case. As a totally blind person, I found her description of the "blind referee" costume quite funny, laughing out loud. In short, I'll decide whether or not I'm offended without any assistance.
Jerry Pryde, Stoney Creek
Separate school system 'archaic'
Re: Brantford Catholic
trustees backpedal after
Italy art trip backlash, promise to pay back $45K travel tab, Oct. 18
The notion of publicly funded Catholic schools is as archaic as tunnelling under the 401. So do I expect this to end? Likely not under Doug Ford's reign.
This latest scandal, however, would be a great launch pad to begin considering their abolishment. If Catholics want their own schools, let the billion-dollar church coffers pay for them. And while I have you here, let's start a conversation about churches and religious institutions paying their share of taxes. Wouldn't it be nice if society were more socially progressive and less elite? One can dream, and vote.
John Zinck, Hamilton

Document 303 of 500

City hall no place for toxic conduct

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-hall-no-place-toxic-conduct/docview/3119504567/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+hall+no+place+for+toxic+conduct&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPPs returned to Queen's Park this week where they will need to confront many pressing issues facing our province. One problem that must be addressed is violence and harassment in Ontario's municipal councils and local boards.
Over the last several years, municipal integrity commissioners have reported various acts of sexual misconduct committed by local officials, yet few politicians have faced any real consequences. The problem arises from weak or non-existent municipal laws, which do not allow for elected members to be punished for harassing behaviour beyond a 90-day suspension of pay.
In what other place of work would someone be permitted to engage in egregious acts of violence and still keep their job? Strengthening conduct rules for local officials who commit violence and harassment would improve accountability in Ontario, aligning its 444 municipalities with workplaces across the province.
More respectful and less toxic city halls would lead to more efficient decision-making and reduced operating costs. In Pickering, city council has been hindered from conducting its business and resources have been diverted to support three integrity commissioner investigations, all to address the hateful behaviour of one sitting councillor. Municipal taxpayers have needed to absorb these costs.
Violence and harassment in municipal politics have democratic costs, too. A 2024 report prepared for the Association of Municipalities of Ontario noted the mental health toll on local candidates who experience discrimination, bullying and harassment. Those who identify as Black or LGBTQ are particularly at risk.
In May, "Halton Elected Representatives" wrote an op-ed citing harassment and sexism against women leaders in the region. Their experiences are substantiated by academic research, which reveals gender-based violence suppresses women's political participation. In the 2022 municipal elections, less than one-third of elected or acclaimed candidates were women.
At Queen's Park, there is rare, cross-party agreement that this issue needs to be addressed. Liberal MPP Stephen Blais has twice introduced private member's bills to combat violence and harassment in municipalities, but neither passed.
In June, Jeff Burch, New Democrat MPP and municipal affairs critic, introduced Bill 207, the Municipal Accountability and Integrity Act, 2024. Like Blais' earlier bills, this bill deserves serious consideration when legislators return. It proposes to create a board of integrity commissioners to adjudicate claims of misconduct, taking them out of the hands of politicians.
It would obligate all politicians to receive annual training on violence, harassment and discrimination and it directs the minister to develop a range of penalties to be applied for code of conduct contraventions.
When a code has been violated, an integrity commissioner would be permitted to apply for judicial review to vacate a member's seat. This step would presumably be taken in the most egregious cases only. A member whose seat has been vacated by a judge would be eligible to stand as a candidate after two subsequent regular elections.
Some, including the premier, have mused that such measures could undermine the will of voters, when in fact they would strengthen local democracy. These provisions would ensure that politicians who commit serious acts of violence are held accountable for their actions within a reasonable time, as they would be if they were employed in many other workplaces. They would also ensure safer, more inclusive city halls for those who work in them.
At a time when civility and respect are at low points in federal politics, this fall provincial leaders have an opportunity to show voters they can work together to address this problem, finally.
Tracey Raney
is a professor at Toronto Metropolitan University.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Tracey Raney Tracey Raney is a professor at Toronto Metropolitan University.;

Document 304 of 500

'Attention, Hamilton': City plans open houses on urban expansion bids: Goal is to gather feedback and inform public on complex, shifting policy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/attention-hamilton-city-plans-open-houses-on/docview/3119504477/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Attention%2C+Hamilton%27%3A+City+plans+open+houses+on+urban+expansion+bids%3A+Goal+is+to+gather+feedback+and+inform+public+on+complex%2C+shifting+policy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city plans to hold open houses next month as part of a public feedback campaign on how Hamilton should handle bids to build homes on rural land despite its frozen urban boundary.
The exercise focuses on how to evaluate pitches by developers to expand the urban area in light of recent provincial changes that have paved the way for applications at any time and of any size even as the city aims to hold the line.
The outreach - including two open houses in November, social media posts and an online explainer video - is also meant to educate the public on the complex, oft-changing web of land-use policy framing the issue.
"This is almost like, 'Attention, Hamilton. Did you know this is pending and thanks for your previous input,'" Coun. Ted McMeekin said during a recent discussion at city hall about the city's soon-to-launch effort.
"This is about how we move forward. What can we control? What we can't control," the Waterdown councillor added.
In August, council gave staff the green light to start applying a draft "framework" to evaluate boundary-bending applications but with the intent to later shop it out for public feedback.
It includes many considerations to be backed by technical studies: how proposals affect farmland, infrastructure capacity and costs, climate change, natural environment, public transportation and the city's focus on handling growth through intensification.
The draft framework also "strongly encourages" aspiring urban expansion developers "to contact Indigenous and First Nations communities" that "may have an interest in the land" before making submissions to the city.
At issue are provincial legislative changes that stand to thaw Hamilton's frozen urban boundary and its strategy to concentrate growth inward rather than outward into outlying rural areas.
So far, the contentious file - with anti-sprawl advocates arguing for a fixed boundary and developers pushing for expansion - has been subject to a dizzying array of legislative swings.
In late 2022, the provincial Conservatives, citing the need to expedite residential construction in Ontario, imposed urban expansions on municipalities, including a roughly 5,000-acre leap into rural land in Hamilton.
About a year later, amid public outrage and a pair of scathing watchdog reports, Premier Doug Ford's government reversed course on the urban boundary and walked back Greenbelt carve-outs around the same time.
However, this past June, Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, opened the door to appeals to the Ontario Land Tribunal for when municipal councils reject urban expansion applications or don't decide them in time.
And a new provincial planning statement that took effect Sunday eliminates the requirement for comprehensive municipal land-use studies before urban areas can be expanded.
This clears the way for boundary applications "at any time and with no limit on the size," a city planning report advised this past summer.
Hamilton already faces tribunal challenges launched by developers hoping to fold their rural lands into the urban area to build homes, chipping away at the city's fixed boundary.
The Upper West Side Landowners Group, which hopes to build 2,450 homes on parcels off Twenty and Glancaster roads near the Mount Hope airport, is one consortium behind those appeals.
Meanwhile, a lawyer representing landowners in Elfrida, the city's largest potential urban expansion area, has signalled that her clients planned to file an application at city hall once the new provincial planning statement kicked in.
City staff recognize the proposals are coming, Anita Fabac, Hamilton's acting chief planner, told The Spectator.
"How do we best position the city and staff to review those applications to make a recommendation to the planning committee?"
The framework is also meant to support the city's stance while facing provincial tribunal appeals.
"Did we miss something? Did we get it right? Does that sort of encapsulate what the community's thoughts are in terms of how do you evaluate applications as they come in?"
With so many recent legislative changes, it's "critically important" that the public be "clearly informed" on the current landscape, Charlie Toman, the planning division's program lead on policy planning, told councillors.
To help with that, two open houses are planned: Nov. 26 (noon to 9 p.m. at the Hamilton Convention Centre) and Nov. 28 (6 to 10 p.m. at Ancaster High School). Staff will be on hand at information stations to respond to questions and receive feedback from drop-in attendees on the complex policy issues.
It's crucial that the information be "within the grasp" of nonexperts, suggested Coun. Maureen Wilson, noting the planning lexicon is "at times not accessible."
The technical language "is hard even for me, as a councillor," agreed McMeekin, a former Liberal minister of municipal affairs and housing.
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: A consortium of developers has appealed its urban expansion plans for Twenty Road West in Glanbrook to the Ontario Land Tribunal.Cathie Coward hamilton Specatorfile photo

Document 305 of 500

Holt knows she has to deliver on promises: 'There is a lot of big challenges that need to be tackled,' says New Brunswick's premier-elect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/holt-knows-she-has-deliver-on-promises/docview/3119504318/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Holt+knows+she+has+to+deliver+on+promises%3A+%27There+is+a+lot+of+big+challenges+that+need+to+be+tackled%2C%27+says+New+Brunswick%27s+premier-elect&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Susan Holt says it hasn't sunk in yet that she is New Brunswick's premier-designate.
The day after her Liberal Party won a convincing majority in Monday's provincial election, Holt says she feels grateful for the opportunity to serve, and that she recognizes the seriousness of the responsibility - and the amount of work ahead.
"There is a lot of big challenges that need to be tackled, and now it's on me and my team to deliver and to help make lives better for New Brunswickers," Holt said in an interview.
Winning 31 of 49 seats, the Liberals denied a third term in office for Blaine Higgs's Progressive Conservatives, who won 16 seats. The Green Party finished with two.
Now comes the hard part.
One of the party's central election promises is to open 30 health-care clinics across the province over the next four years. It'll be sometime next year before the first clinic opens, she said, under the Liberal model of "collaborative care" that puts nurses, doctors, pharmacists and other health-care workers under one roof, operating from at least 7 a.m. to 7 p.m.
"It's going to be in 2025 because there are some that are on the verge of happening, or are close to happening under the old model that the previous government was using," Holt said.
"We believe we can take some of that work and transform the model within .... Vitalité and Horizon (health networks) are really keen to advance this."
Other promises that New Brunswickers can expect to see realized next year include cheaper electricity bills - Holt's party pledged to remove the provincial sales tax on power, which she said will reduce bills by 10 per cent. That should happen by April, she said.
One of her first tasks will be to send a letter to the Canada Revenue Agency to remove the provincial sales tax on electricity. It takes about 120 days for the agency to make the change, which can be implemented the following quarter, in time for April 1, she said.
She said she would "like to challenge" that waiting period, however, to ensure people have lower bills during the coldest time of the year in December, January and February.
"We're going to find out whether there's any wiggle room with Canada Revenue Agency and the 120 days and the quarterly adjustment."

The Canadian Press
CAPTION: New Brunswick Liberal premier-elect Susan Holt speaks to supporters in Fredericton.Stephen MacGillivray The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Hina Alam The Canadian Press

Document 306 of 500

The province needs to make city halls more respectful and less toxic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-needs-make-city-halls-more-respectful/docview/3119503415/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+province+needs+to+make+city+halls+more+respectful+and+less+toxic&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPPs returned to Queen's Park this week where they will need to confront many pressing issues facing our province. One problem that must be addressed is violence and harassment in Ontario's municipal councils and local boards.
Over the last several years, municipal integrity commissioners have reported various acts of sexual misconduct committed by local officials, yet few politicians have faced any real consequences for their actions. The problem arises from weak or non-existent municipal laws, which do not allow for elected members to be punished for harassing behaviour beyond a 90-day suspension of pay.
This legislative oversight is out of sync with modern views of the workplace. In what other place of work would someone be permitted to engage in egregious acts of violence and still keep their job? Strengthening conduct rules for local officials who commit violence and harassment would improve accountability in Ontario, aligning its 444 municipalities with workplaces across the province.
More respectful and less toxic city halls would lead to more efficient decision-making and reduced operating costs. In Pickering, city council has been hindered from conducting its business and resources have needed to be diverted to support three integrity commissioner investigations, all to address the hateful behaviours of one sitting councillor. Municipal taxpayers have needed to absorb these costs.
Violence and harassment in municipal politics have democratic costs, too. A 2024 report prepared for the Association of Municipalities of Ontario noted the mental health toll on local candidates who experience discrimination, bullying and harassment. Those who identify as Black or LGBTQ are particularly at risk.
In May 2024, Halton Elected Representatives wrote an op-ed citing harassment and sexism against women leaders in the region. Their experiences are substantiated by academic research, which reveals that gender-based violence suppresses women's political participation. In the 2022 municipal elections, less than one-third of elected or acclaimed candidates were women.
Support for tackling this problem is growing. Largely due to the efforts of The Women of Ontario Say No - a non-partisan group advocating for government legislation to hold harassing councillors accountable - more than 200 municipalities have urged the government to act.
At Queen's Park, there is rare, cross-party agreement that this issue needs to be addressed. Liberal MPP Stephen Blais has twice introduced private member's bills to combat violence and harassment in municipalities, but neither passed.
In June 2024, Jeff Burch, New Democrat MPP and municipal affairs critic, introduced Bill 207, the Municipal Accountability and Integrity Act, 2024. Like Blais' earlier bills, this bill deserves serious consideration when legislators return. It proposes to create a board of integrity commissioners to adjudicate claims of misconduct, taking them out of the hands of politicians. It would obligate all politicians to receive annual training on violence, harassment and discrimination and it directs the minister to develop a range of penalties to be applied for code of conduct contraventions.
When a code has been violated, an integrity commissioner would be permitted to apply for judicial review to vacate a member's seat. This step would presumably be taken in the most egregious cases only. A member whose seat has been vacated by a judge would be eligible to stand as a candidate after two subsequent regular elections.
Some, including the premier, have mused that such measures could undermine the will of voters, when in fact they would strengthen local democracy. These provisions would ensure that politicians who commit serious acts of violence are held accountable for their actions within a reasonable time, as they would be if they were employed in many other workplaces. They would also ensure safer, more inclusive city halls for those who work in them.
At a time when civility and respect are at low points in federal politics, this fall provincial leaders have an opportunity to show voters they can work together to address this problem, finally.
CAPTION: Politicians who commit serious acts of violence should be held accountable for their actions within a reasonable time, as they would be if they were employed in many other workplaces, Tracey Raney writes.
CREDIT: Tracey Raney

NDP leader says Sarah Jama won't be her candidate in next Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-leader-says-sarah-jama-wont-be-her-candidate/docview/3119781290/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+leader+says+Sarah+Jama+won%27t+be+her+candidate+in+next+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles has ruled out taking back her controversial former MPP Sarah Jama as a candidate.
"As far as I know, and I'm the leader, it won't be her," Stiles said Tuesday on CBC Radio's Metro Morning.
She had been asked by host David Common about the prospect of the Independent MPP for Hamilton Centre returning to the NDP fold for the next provincial election amid speculation Premier Doug Ford will call an early vote next year instead of waiting until the scheduled June 2026 date.
Stiles ejected Jama from the New Democratic caucus last October for "acting unilaterally too many times" and "undermining our work" in the wake of the Oct. 7, 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and that country's retaliatory strikes on Gaza.
Jama has been sitting as an Independent MPP following her ouster, which split the NDP caucus and alienated staunch supporters in a riding that has been solidly New Democrat for years.
Asked last month by the Star whether she would consider returning to the NDP, Jama replied in a Sept. 27 email, "I am prepared to run as an Independent, though I have always kept all options open and will continue to do so."
Jama declined to comment at the legislature on Tuesday.
"I'm not talking today," she said.
Stiles, who last month refused to explicitly rule out taking back Jama as a candidate, was asked by reporters after the legislature's daily question period about her comments four hours earlier on Metro Morning.
But she repeatedly refused to use the same wording in her answer, and was less definitive.
"This is not part of the conversation right now that we're having," Stiles said of Jama.
"The member is not a member of our caucus. She's confirmed she's running as an Independent, and we will be running NDP candidates in every riding ... our aim in the next election is to defeat this Conservative government."
Peter Graefe, an associate professor of political science at McMaster University in Hamilton, has told the Star that running an NDP candidate against Jama could split the progressive vote and allow a Liberal or Progressive Conservative to win the riding.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on May 6, 2024.

Document 308 of 500

Doug Ford's government finds that giving every Ontarian a cheque isn't as simple as it sounds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-finds-that-giving-every/docview/3119781124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+finds+that+giving+every+Ontarian+a+cheque+isn%27t+as+simple+as+it+sounds&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All signs point to Premier Doug Ford calling an early spring election.
Sources say the province is working closely with Canada Revenue Agency officials to ensure "tax rebate cheques" are mailed out at the end of January or early February.
Ford, who enjoys a healthy lead in public-opinion polls, wants the cash in people's hands before triggering an election in late March or early April, more than a year ahead of a scheduled June 2026 vote.
As first revealed by the Star last Wednesday, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will formally unveil the payments of around $200 to all 16 million Ontario adults and children in his Oct. 30 fall economic statement.
But insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss a program that has yet to be officially announced, say collecting the names and addresses of every Ontarian is harder than it sounds.
Strict privacy laws forbid the government from using provincial health data kept by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan, drivers' licence information held by the Ministry of Transportation, or birth certificate records.
That means Queen's Park will be forced to rely mostly upon the CRA for help in determining who should receive cheques and where they can be mailed.
Progressive Conservative sources say Ford has made it clear the money must flow early in the new year, when people's paycheques are smaller due to Employment Insurance deductions and when holiday credit card bills are coming due.
"Put money into people's pockets and they'll go out and do something as simple as going out for dinner, maybe buying your kids sneakers or clothes, or maybe putting a little renovation on your house," the premier told 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon speech last Thursday.
"And they might do that because they have money in their pockets. The same with businesses. If you put money into businesses, they'll invest into their people, into technology, into their infrastructure, and they grow. That's what creates economic strength," said Ford.
"I risk stealing Peter Bethlenfalvy's thunder if I get into the fall economic statement. I'm sure the Toronto Star knows it already."
A spring election would take place immediately after the end of per-vote public subsidies for political parties that the premier is phasing out as of Dec. 31.
Each quarter, the Tories receive about $1.2 million, the Liberals $714,000, the New Democrats $710,000, the Greens $178,000, the New Blue Party $81,000, and the Ontario Party $53,000.
Even though his party benefits the most from the subsidies, Ford believes the PCs can continue to out-fundraise their rivals from individuals' donations.
The premier, who has long opposed public funding of political parties, would like to force his rivals to spend more of their precious time raising money to make up for the shortfall.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Tuesday that the Tories "intend to go to the polls early no matter what the people of Ontario want," and warned the plan could backfire as it did in 1990 for premier David Peterson's Liberals, who faced voters early and lost to Bob Rae's New Democrats.
"The last thing they want is the government to call an unnecessary election that's going to cost millions of dollars," said Stiles, noting Ford "already has a majority" and can pass his agenda through the house.
Peterson is now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said there's only one reason Ford would want to face voters sooner rather than later: the RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"I really believe they're trying to get ahead of the RCMP investigation, which, as we now know, is starting to speak to the core insiders. They're running frantically away from whatever could be revealed by the RCMP," she said.
Crombie said the rebate cheques are "just another gimmick" that could cost the treasury $3.2 billion.
"I've already announced that we would look at a sustainable tax cut for families, middle-income earners, so people could see savings each and every week in their paycheque rather than a one-time gimmick, which is your money being used to buy your vote," she said.
Recalling "the cautionary tale" that happened to Peterson's Liberals 34 years ago, Crombie said Ford would need to explain to voters why he needs a $150-million early election for a new mandate.
"When you have a majority government, there isn't anything - no piece of legislation - you can't get through the house, so it's going to be tough to create that narrative of why it's necessary to go now."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "the government doesn't have any rationale for an early election other than its own political self interest."
"We're in a housing crisis, our health-care system is collapsing, our students are learning in overcrowded classrooms and this government wants to call an election rather than putting forward solutions when they still have another year left in their mandate. It makes no sense."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives at the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Subject: Cost control; Voters; Subsidies; Political parties

Document 309 of 500

Doug Ford's war on bike lanes may help him get re-elected, but it won't solve gridlock

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-war-on-bike-lanes-may-help-him-get-re/docview/3119503611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+war+on+bike+lanes+may+help+him+get+re-elected%2C+but+it+won%27t+solve+gridlock&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has seen the enemy and it is thus:
Too many little-used bike lanes getting in the way of too many overused car lanes.
Of course, if you are a cyclist or leftist, that proposition is lunacy or heresy. But the premier is nothing if not a provocateur of progressives.
And he is not as dumb as he looks to them.
There's a reason his Progressive Conservative government introduced unprecedented legislation this week to rein in municipal bike lanes and potentially restore lost car lanes.
Ford's Tories see bike paths as political wedges that will pay dividends on the campaign trail. That's because fair-weather cyclists will never match the number of all-weather motorists stuck in the GTA's endless traffic snarls.
What makes this rightist-populist politician different from his leftist-righteous adversaries is that Ford can see around corners. By contrast, the bicycle brigade can't see what's coming down the path - which makes them their own worst enemies.
At city hall, bike lanes are seen as infinitely beautiful. The more the better.
Speaking personally, as both columnist and cyclist who uses bike lanes more than most, I know that you can have too much of a good thing in a bad location. (Fun fact: As a summer student at the Edmonton Journal decades ago, I wrote a weekly cycling column.)
I get the bicycle vision. But blinkers are bad for situational awareness - whether you're politicking or pedalling.
In the real world, traffic management is a two-way street. If you can't see the other side, you are headed for a crash.
What makes this political play so exasperating is the posturing and posing on both sides of the divide. For all the premier's huffing and puffing about fighting the war against the car, his over-the-top battle against bike lanes ultimately won't fix our underlying traffic problems.
It's more about distraction than congestion. It's a way to look away from the bigger jam that politicians refuse to face.
Bike lanes have become a symbolic bogeyman. Undoing them is not a serious solution.
As a thought experiment, suppose our all-powerful premier were to wave his wand and win this wedge, peeling bike lanes off Bloor Street West or Yonge Street. It might be the right thing to do, but by any measure it would have a marginal effect on travel times - a few minutes at most.
If the premier were serious, if Mayor Olivia Chow were serious, if commuters and voters were serious, they would steer the city towards more serious and substantive remedies instead of driving us to distraction.
Start with parking. Almost every major street is bogged down by an inert lane of locked vehicles - a parking lot shaped like an asphalt ribbon. In summer, some of those spots are repurposed into CaféTO tables whose time, like COVID, has come and gone (these spots were a good idea in mid-pandemic when traffic flows were minimal, but traffic is back).
When you add up protected bike lanes, parking lanes and CaféTO tables, you have major streets repurposed into multiplexes - leaving just a single lane of clogged traffic in each direction.
To be sure, there is a political constituency for each of these competing claims and lanes: local businesses crave the foot traffic from parking spots; restaurateurs revel in their overflow seating on asphalt; cyclists savour the freedom to sail past the congestion.
But someone has to tell everyone that you can't have it all.
Progressive proponents of protected bike lanes argue they save lives. They surely do, but if safety is so important, why does Toronto encourage the explosive growth in bike sharing knowing that so many users won't use helmets - leaving them perilously vulnerable to head injuries at intersections or crossing streetcar tracks? Why do we allow high-speed e-bike users with pretend pedals on those same bike lanes to overtake regular pedallers or menace pedestrians?
Safety, it seems, only goes so far.
To be clear, I love zooming along University Avenue's abandoned bike lanes, which are so much wider than the parallel north-south bike lanes a stone's throw away on Beverley Street. But I feel guilty looking at the traffic jams created outside our biggest hospital cluster - it feels like overkill in the name of bicycle safety.
While Ford might be right on the details, might doesn't make right in a democracy. The bottom line is that the province should stay in its own lane, rather than meddle from on high in matters of municipal jurisdiction.
If Toronto politicians want to tie traffic in knots, let them face voters and commuters at election time. Yet the premier can't resist the impulse to bigfoot the mayor, simply because he can.
Legally and constitutionally, the city is a creature of the province. He has the power to pass legislation banning bike lanes, just as he has the hubris to boast of digging a fantastical tunnel under the 401.
All along, Ford casts himself as the fearless defender of commuters, fending off the putative war against the car. In truth, he is waging - and winning - wedge politics, but it won't get us where we need to go.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queen's Park as the Ontario Legislature resumes on Oct.21, 2024.

Subject: Voters; Politics; Traffic accidents & safety; Traffic congestion; Bicycling

Document 310 of 500

Hamilton plans open houses on urban expansion bids

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-plans-open-houses-on-urban-expansion/docview/3119181162/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+plans+open+houses+on+urban+expansion+bids&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city plans to hold open houses next month as part of a public feedback campaign on how Hamilton should handle bids to build homes on rural land despite its frozen urban boundary.
The exercise focuses on how to evaluate pitches by developers to expand the urban area in light of recent provincial changes that have paved the way for applications at any time and of any size even as the city aims to hold the line.
The outreach - including two open houses in November, social media posts and an online explainer video - is also meant to educate the public on the complex, oft-changing web of land-use policy framing the issue.
"This is almost like, 'Attention, Hamilton. Did you know this is pending and thanks for your previous input,'" Coun. Ted McMeekin said during a recent discussion at city hall about the city's soon-to-launch effort.
"This is about how we move forward. What can we control? What we can't control," the Waterdown councillor added.
In August, council gave staff the green light to start applying a draft "framework" to evaluate boundary-bending applications but with the intent to later shop it out for public feedback.
It includes many considerations to be backed by technical studies: how proposals affect farmland, infrastructure capacity and costs, climate change, natural environment, public transportation and the city's focus on handling growth through intensification.
The draft framework also "strongly encourages" aspiring urban expansion developers "to contact Indigenous and First Nations communities" that "may have an interest in the land" before making submissions to the city.
At issue are provincial legislative changes that stand to thaw Hamilton's frozen urban boundary and its strategy to concentrate growth inward rather than outward into outlying rural areas.
So far, the contentious file - with anti-sprawl advocates arguing for a fixed boundary and developers pushing for expansion - has been subject to a dizzying array of legislative swings.
In late 2022, the provincial Conservatives, citing the need to expedite residential construction in Ontario, imposed urban expansions on municipalities, including a roughly 5,000-acre leap into rural land in Hamilton.
About a year later, amid public outrage and a pair of scathing watchdog reports, Premier Doug Ford's government reversed course on the urban boundary and walked back Greenbelt carve-outs around the same time.
However, this past June, Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, opened the door to appeals to the Ontario Land Tribunal for when municipal councils reject urban expansion applications or don't decide them in time.
And a new provincial planning statement that took effect Sunday eliminates the requirement for comprehensive municipal land-use studies before urban areas can be expanded.
This clears the way for boundary applications "at any time and with no limit on the size," a city planning report advised this past summer.
Hamilton already faces tribunal challenges launched by developers hoping to fold their rural lands into the urban area to build homes, chipping away at the city's fixed boundary.
The Upper West Side Landowners Group, which hopes to build 2,450 homes on parcels off Twenty and Glancaster roads near the Mount Hope airport, is one consortium behind those appeals.
Meanwhile, a lawyer representing landowners in Elfrida, the city's largest potential urban expansion area, has signalled that her clients planned to file an application at city hall once the new provincial planning statement kicked in.
City staff recognize the proposals are coming, Anita Fabac, Hamilton's acting chief planner, told The Spectator.
"How do we best position the city and staff to review those applications to make a recommendation to the planning committee?"
The framework is also meant to support the city's stance while facing provincial tribunal appeals.
"Did we miss something? Did we get it right? Does that sort of encapsulate what the community's thoughts are in terms of how do you evaluate applications as they come in?"
With so many recent legislative changes, it's "critically important" that the public be "clearly informed" on the current landscape, Charlie Toman, the planning division's program lead on policy planning, told councillors.
To help with that, two open houses are planned: Nov. 26 (noon to 9 p.m. at the Hamilton Convention Centre) and Nov. 28 (6 to 10 p.m. at Ancaster High School). Staff will be on hand at information stations to respond to questions and receive feedback from drop-in attendees on the complex policy issues.
It's crucial that the information be "within the grasp" of nonexperts, suggested Coun. Maureen Wilson, noting the planning lexicon is "at times not accessible."
The technical language "is hard even for me, as a councillor," agreed McMeekin, a former Liberal minister of municipal affairs and housing.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: This map shows Hamilton's urban boundary and "white belt" lands, where developers aim to build homes along Twenty Road, White Church Road and in Elfrida. The city has frozen the urban boundary, making those areas off-limits to development.A consortium of developers has appealed its urban expansion plans for Twenty Road West in Glanbrook to the Ontario Land Tribunal.Stop Sprawl HamOnt organized a campaign to persuade city council not to expand Hamilton's urban area in 2021.

Document 311 of 500

Oct. 22: Catholic board needs to rethink its priorities, urban sprawl is not the answer and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-22-catholic-board-needs-rethink-priorities/docview/3119181143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+22%3A+Catholic+board+needs+to+rethink+its+priorities%2C+urban+sprawl+is+not+the+answer+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Board needs to rethink its priorities
There was an interesting contrast between the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board and the Grand Erie District School Board in your Thursday paper. The former board decided it was a good idea to send four trustees at a cost of about $45,000 to Italy to purchase $100,000 in artwork for their schools. In the meantime, the public board developed a new program offering food hampers and wellness checks during the summer for students in need. I think the Catholic board needs to re-examine its priorities.
Janet L. MacNeil, Caledonia
It's time to end separate system
So Brantford Catholic trustees went on a spending spree in Italy to buy $100,000 worth of commissioned art. Why are we, the Ontario taxpayers, still paying for a fully-funded religious school system parallel to a secular one? Even more, why are we building beautiful new facilities that promulgate one faith when we live in such a diverse society? We could be reducing administrative costs, bringing kids of all faiths together and putting badly needed money into aging facilities. Instead, the status quo rules.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Why was this change made?
Re: Ontario home care in disarray amid supply shortages, Oct. 20
Our Ontario government has chosen a new distributor for medical supplies for visiting care nurses for their patients needs in home care. The nurses and patients have no idea when supplies will become available. One patient I am aware of needed special dressings for her cancer treatment. A little while back a package of dressings arrived, but of lower quality and a hazard to that particular patient, and her condition was compromised.
My husband has an offshoot of Parkinson's. He is very open to infection. He has a suprapubic catheter which needs to be flushed and changed frequently. We are not able to get catheters or Foley trays. Recently, we needed to purchase them ourselves. There is no communication to nurses and patients as to when these supplies will resume and be of top quality. This could result in a loss of life.
People would like to know why the government decided to change the distributor for medical supplies and compromised the quality. People would like to know why the government chose a new distributor, why there is no communication as to when the supplies are to come through, and why some were sent to some patients with lower quality.
Bev Traina, Hamilton
Get staff input on changes
This sounds familiar. We saw the same supply problem in Hamilton hospitals when they decided to save money through "bulk buying power." Hospital departments ended up with skewed amounts, either way too much or none, like only one size of mask. We had bags of prepacked connectors of which we only needed one. The rest went into the garbage. These are just two examples. Needless to say, it was a disaster then and it is a disaster now for home care. People sitting behind a desk make decisions that sound like wonderful cost savings for the budget, but fail to do their due diligence. They need the input of staff who actually use these products. Who verify that what they were getting was available and acceptable in quality and quantity before they signed this contract. Spoiler alert: if it's cheaper there is usually a reason. Ontario Health atHome has a serious responsibility to health-compromised people in this province. The CEO should be doing Amazon deliveries personally until they get this straightened out!
Janet Dwyer, Hamilton
Beer at ONroute locations?
I was returning from Brockville the day after Thanksgiving and I pulled into the first ONroute. As I looked up at the video billboard at the entrance, the sign said "BEER" in letters that filled the sign. So defensive drivers will need to watch out for tailgaters, lane jumpers and now, drivers who stop into Doug Ford's new beer outlet to get a six-pack for the way home. Thanks Doug.
Philip Davis, Burlington
Urban sprawl is not the answer
Re: Developers spar over participation in Hamilton urban expansion tribunal challenge, Oct. 5
As a university student, affordable housing is an absolute dream and an urgent problem in Hamilton. However, the Upper West Side Landowners Group's proposal to build 2,450 homes in Glanbrook represents an urban sprawl threatening the city's sustainability goals.
Car dependency and carbon emissions will skyrocket with this development, pushing residents away from the city centre. These new housing projects in Elfrida will force us to drive longer distances for work, school and daily needs because I have not seen any word on new infrastructure plans to support residents in that area. This clearly contradicts any attempts to reduce emissions and encourage environmental sustainability in response to climate change. As long-term citizens, we need housing solutions that do not worsen our carbon footprint.
Hamilton should instead concentrate on building within its current urban boundary, building a more compact, walkable urban community with easy access to public transit and infrastructure. Expanding outward might provide more homes in the short term, but it undermines the city's long-term goal of becoming a more sustainable, livable place.
Jade Fisher, Hamilton
We're not fooled by 'rebate'
The proposed $200 rebate for Ontario residents is insulting. Whilst we are in desperate need of physicians, nurses, health agents, school supports, housing, nutrition, etc., Premier Doug Ford has the nerve to make a proposal such as this. Is this meant to salve the hurt for inflating his cabinet, the number of underling ministers and their significant pay increases? Let's not worry that ODSP recipients live in poverty and OW recipients live a small cut above. Don't they deserve a raise to ensure they could live in dignity!
Or is it to try to have Ontarians forget about the hundreds of millions of dollars that this government has spent on lost court cases and appeals, the sticker fiasco at gas station pumps, the defective licence plates, the greenbelt disaster, media costs touting falsehoods about excellent hospital care and thriving schools, the unnecessary Highway 413 and the broken contract with LCBO, just to name a few.
But hey, let's give out 200 bucks to everyone just like Mike Harris did. Stimulate the economy! The premier believes Ontarians will thank him and return him to office. Shame on him and his caucus! Before being forced to rescind this asinine, thinly veiled "bribe" I encourage the premier to recognize the error of his ways and say, "I have heard you loudly and clearly. This money will go directly into public health care, schools and not for profit long-term-care facilities. And it will not sit somewhere for months while we boast about how much is being spent on these areas. We will provide quarterly transparent financial statements identifying exactly where and how the money has been spent. Each specific ministry will be mandated to ensure the accuracy and timeliness of these reports."
I would gladly be at his side when he makes this announcement. Hey, I would even write it for him!
Annette Webber, Freelton
CAPTION: The Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board needs to re-examine its priorities in the wake of sending trustees to Italy to buy art, a reader says.

Document 312 of 500

Viva Niagara Falls? Don't bet on it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/viva-niagara-falls-dont-bet-on/docview/3119063043/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Viva+Niagara+Falls%3F+Don%27t+bet+on+it&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Las Vegas, Ontario?
As absurd as it sounds, last month Doug Ford indicated he wants to turn Niagara Falls into more of a "Las Vegas of the North" with additional casinos - an effort to revitalize what is already one of Canada's most famous tourist destinations. He'd have better luck trying to add another waterfall to the Niagara River than turning Ontario into a true Las Vegas.
I love Vegas, but even if Ontario collectively decided it wanted to be like it, this old province couldn't pull it off. Vegas is a place with lots of casinos, but more importantly, a debauched sensibility. A vibe, really, one that is baked deeply into its DNA. Ontario has whatever the opposite of Vegas is in our DNA. Just plopping a casino or three down doesn't make Vegas.
It pains me to say this because I wish Ontario could be a bit more Vegas-y. For years I've written columns arguing that both Toronto and Ontario need to loosen up in various ways, whether grudgingly allowing people to drink in parks or providing space for nightclubs in cities. Ontario may not be the place where fun goes to die, but it sure can put a damper on it.
Consider Ottawa recently ticketing people parked in a dark area to see the northern lights. The city has a new "night commissioner" (sometimes called a "Night Mayor") to advocate for life after dark, but when contacted about the ticketing, he said this incident wasn't part of his mandate. As embarrassing as that is for Ottawa, it's a deeply Ontario kind of response.
"What happens in Vegas stays in Vegas" is a marketing catch phrase, but Vegas is truly different. There is a deep looseness to how that city works, a tolerance of vice, scandal, noise, architectural chaos and garishness. All things Ontario generally can't stomach much of. At least not overtly.
Ontario's first casino opened in my hometown of Windsor in 1994 with great fanfare and expectation when the city was still hard hit by a recession. My father even became a blackjack dealer soon after opening, staying on for the better part of a decade. He liked it and, like many others, made good money at the same tables where two of my university friends lost their OSAP loans in a matter of weeks that first September.
Casinos give, but they take a lot more. While it became a regular part of Windsor life, the casino didn't revolutionize the city's economy. Windsor's downtown has more challenges now than it did then.
I thought all casinos were like Windsor's until I actually went to Vegas and saw that its were quite different. More fun and easygoing, the line between casino and the city blurred as people carouse from one to another. Ontario casinos, and the experience in and around them, are tame, just a shadow of how Vegas works.
Walking into a casino in Ontario feels a bit guilty from the moment the security guard at the door sizes you up, while Las Vegas is a machine made for having unrepentant fun.
Ford's Vegas push is a window into how he and his government thinks though, shoehorning big ideas and concepts from elsewhere into Ontario. It explains the Therme Spa at Ontario Place. There's nothing inherently wrong with a spa, but Therme usually establishes them in suburban areas surrounded by lots of parking, not on prime public waterfront land. Ford has also mentioned a casino for Ontario Place, too. The same old ideas keep popping up.
Ontario is an endlessly interesting place with unique landscapes, urban and countryside, but you'd never know it based on the kind of projects that keep being proposed.
Sometimes we manage to get it right, though. Niagara-on-the-Lake built on its unique location and history, becoming an attractive theatre destination. Niagara Falls itself recently refurbished and opened the glorious Canadian Niagara Power Generating Station to the public. Few places in the world can boast an attraction like that.
Ford prides himself on being a businessperson, but many of the ideas he champions are derivative, tired and boring. Where are the bold, new, unique and entrepreneurial ideas that reflect Ontario itself? We did that, once, with Ontario Place and the Science Centre. Let's build on what Ontario has without trying to copy other unique places.
Shawn Micallef is a freelance contributing columnist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Shawn Micallef Shawn Micallef is a freelance contributing columnist.;

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3119063026/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Board should rethink priorities
There was an interesting contrast between the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board and the Grand Erie District School Board in your Thursday paper. The former board decided it was a good idea to send four trustees at a cost of about $45,000 to Italy to purchase $100,000 in artwork for their schools. In the meantime, the public board developed a new program offering food hampers and wellness checks during the summer for students in need. I think the Catholic board needs to re-examine its priorities.
Janet L. MacNeil, Caledonia
It's time to end separate system
So Brantford Catholic trustees went on a spending spree in Italy to buy $100,000 worth of commissioned art. Why are we, the taxpayers, still paying for a fully-funded religious school system parallel to a secular one? Even more, why are we building beautiful new facilities that promulgate one faith when we live in such a diverse society? We could be reducing administrative costs, bringing kids of all faiths together and putting badly needed money into aging facilities. Instead, the status quo rules.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Why was this change made?
Re: Ontario home care in disarray amid supply
shortages, Oct. 20
Our Ontario government has chosen a new distributor for medical supplies for visiting care nurses for their patients needs in home care. The nurses and patients have no idea when supplies will become available. One patient I am aware of needed special dressings for her cancer treatment. A little while back a package of dressings arrived, but of lower quality and a hazard to that particular patient, and her condition was compromised.
My husband has an offshoot of Parkinson's. He is very open to infection. He has a suprapubic catheter which needs to be flushed and changed frequently. We are not able to get catheters or Foley trays. Recently, we needed to purchase them ourselves.
People would like to know why the government decided to change the distributor for medical supplies and compromised the quality. People would like to know why the government chose a new distributor and why there is no communication as to when the supplies are to come through.
Bev Traina, Hamilton
Get staff input on changes

Janet Dwyer, Hamilton

Document 314 of 500

Supply woes for home care 'unacceptable,' minister says: 'We didn't realize this was going to be such a disaster': MD spokesperson

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/supply-woes-home-care-unacceptable-minister-says/docview/3119063000/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supply+woes+for+home+care+%27unacceptable%2C%27+minister+says%3A+%27We+didn%27t+realize+this+was+going+to+be+such+a+disaster%27%3A+MD+spokesperson&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is wrestling with delays and shortages of supplies needed for home and palliative care, with dying people unable to get sedatives and patients going to hospital because their supplies have run out, the province's doctors say.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones said Monday that she and her ministry are working hard to rectify the "frustrating" situation.
"(It) is absolutely unacceptable," she said in question period.
"We have been working with Ontario Health atHome to ensure that no patients, no patients' families, no clinicians are impacted by a logistics issue. I want to assure the people of Ontario that we have been on this issue since we first learned that there were shortages. ... We know this is unacceptable and we are not going to allow this to continue."
Jones said she has directed the agency to reimburse any patient, family or clinician who has paid out of pocket for necessary equipment.
Ontario Health atHome said in a statement that it has new supply contracts as of Sept. 24 and it is doing everything possible to stabilize the delivery of critical medical items.
"Ontario Health atHome takes any impact to our patients very seriously, and we understand the distress this is causing," the agency wrote in a statement.
"We want to assure patients, their families and health system partners that we are doing everything we can to address this situation and are continuing to look for opportunities to stabilize the delivery of these critical medical items."
Dr. Joyce Cheung, the Ontario Medical Association's chair of palliative medicine, said palliative care doctors across the province have been raising concerns about medication and supply delays that have sent patients to emergency rooms, and a lack of medication and needles leaving people in pain.
One of her palliative patients went 30 hours without a pain pump, she said, and another patient with metastatic cancer nearly ran out of ostomy bags for collecting stool.
"I don't know what to do," she said. "Are people buying supplies off Amazon where we cannot validate the quality of these supplies? We're hearing these stories."
The province passed legislation late last year to create Ontario Health atHome, in order to better co-ordinate home care across the province. Cheung said physicians were not part of any transition planning.
"We were all taken aback," she said. "We're just like, 'What is going on?' We're just seeing what's happening on the ground, and we don't even know how to pivot, because we didn't realize this was going to be such a disaster."
There were probably good intentions behind standardizing supply contracts, but the implementation has gone awry and it seems as though the vendors did not anticipate the amount of supplies they would need, she said.
Liberal health critic and emergency room doctor Adil Shamji said the situation is dire, and should have been anticipated.
"When I had a chance to speak with ministry bureaucrats and to debate the legislation that brought forward Ontario Health atHome, it was clear that, apart from knowing what the branding was - specifically that it would be called Ontario Health atHome - they had no other clue about how this centralized, mega agency would actually perform, would actually be able to deliver care for patients who require it from home," he said.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she wants clear answers as to why this happened in the first place.
"Not only is it unacceptable what's happening in home care right now, but it's unethical, deeply unethical," she said.
CAPTION: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said she has directed Ontario Health atHome to reimburse any patient, family or clinician who has paid out of pocket for necessary equipment.Chris Young The Canadian Press

National briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3119060269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick
Holt leads Liberals to win
New Brunswick voters have elected a Liberal majority government, tossing out Blaine Higgs's incumbent Progressive Conservatives after six years in power and handing the reins to the first woman ever to lead the province. Liberal Leader Susan Holt is a relative newcomer to the political scene, having won a byelection last year, eight months after she became the first woman to win the leadership of the party.

Ontario
Bike lane changes in bill
Ontario has tabled its first bill as a new legislative sitting got underway Monday, which would give the province the power to stop cities from constructing some new bike lanes. Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria introduced the bill in what he says is an effort to reduce traffic gridlock. The bill would require municipalities to ask the province for permission to install bike lanes when they would remove a lane of vehicle traffic.

Canada
MP calls for
Trudeau to quit
Liberal MP Sean Casey is the first to publicly confirm that he has signed a letter calling on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to resign, arguing it would be in the best interests of the country to let someone else take the reins. As an internal revolt brews, few Liberal MPs who are not in the cabinet are publicly defending the prime minister. The upcoming caucus meeting on Wednesday appears to present the most serious challenge to Trudeau's leadership to date. Casey, said Monday he has signed the letter asking the prime minister to resign, but would not say how many of his colleagues had also signed.

News services
