Document 316 of 500

What's the price of your vote?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whats-price-your-vote/docview/3119060248/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+the+price+of+your+vote%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government announced last week that it plans to bribe Ontarians with a $200 "rebate," sparking talk of an early election.
The government must be so flush with cash it can't find anything better to do with the $3.2 billion that's going to cost. Doug Ford says it will boost the economy. How can it not? In reality, for so many it'll be just a blip in the bank account, gone in the blink of an eye on the next hydro, phone or gas bill. But people's lives aren't going to change materially.
If the government wants to help Ontario families, it would giddy up and sign a deal for the national school lunch program. Outside of price controls, there's little the government can do in a free market about food sector "greedflation" which has resulted in smaller package volumes and rising sticker prices. Price controls? That's communist.
But helping families feed their kids by providing a nutritious lunch is something the government can do and be far more effective at in directing assistance where it's needed. Kids' tummies. And it would save families more than $200, not to mention the stress of preparing lunches for a busy family every morning. Ford says he's "all in" on talks with the feds, but why does it take so long to sit down and hammer out a plan? This should be a priority.
Let's hope it fares better than the $10-a-day daycare collaboration.
This "rebate" isn't the first time Ford has tried to outright bribe voters. Remember the $200 to families during the teachers' strike? The cancellation of car registration fees was another bribe taken up by the people of Ontario, happily, as a much-needed revenue stream disappeared from provincial coffers.
Ontario is seriously in debt. I normally wouldn't focus on debt - government debt is ubiquitous across every jurisdiction - but Ontario doesn't have the money to be giving rebates to anyone for anything right now. The interest alone is staggering.
With insistent demands on funds for necessary goods and services like housing, health care, education, infrastructure repair, social assistance reform etc., there is no extra money to "rebate." It's a bait and switch as homeowners will see it swallowed up in increased property taxes and renters will watch it go poof when their rent goes up. It's not meant to help anyone besides being a tiny little Band-Aid on a gaping, bleeding wound, falsely boosting the economy for optics.
Meanwhile, those people who don't need it - whose struggle isn't so close to the bone, who don't pay much attention to the news, who aren't sick or worried about elderly parents or special needs children, and who can't, or won't, see those struggling around them - will pocket the cash and remember what a great guy Ford is and how he gifted them when it comes time to cast their vote.
This is a blatant attempt by the Conservatives to buy votes. Why isn't it against election laws to give "rebates" in months leading up to an election? Might as well stand outside polling stations and give out money.
You know who does need the money way more than most of us? And they need a lot more than a token $200? People living on social assistance.
There is no metric that measures Ontario social assistance rates as livable in today's economy.
Anyone who can do simple math, very simple math, can see that the rates people receive on OW/ODSP are abysmally inadequate in the face of today's cost of living. Rents continue to remain outrageously high, food costs are going up, everything costs more, yet people on social assistance have gotten a paltry rate increase, an insulting five per cent, after losing 22 per cent under the Harris government and not regaining ground, beyond indexing, since.
So much of the homelessness issue could be addressed by simply giving people on social assistance enough money to find a home. That would be the price of my vote.
Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more, visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) and friend her on Facebook.
CAPTION: So much of the homelessness issue could be addressed by simply giving people on social assistance enough money to find a home, Margaret Shkimba writes.Clifford Skarstedt Examiner file photo

Title: What's the price of your vote?

Document 317 of 500

Doug Ford's $200 'rebate' will do little to help Ontario families. Here's where the money should go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-200-rebate-will-do-little-help-ontario/docview/3119060154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%24200+%27rebate%27+will+do+little+to+help+Ontario+families.+Here%27s+where+the+money+should+go&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government announced last week that it plans to bribe Ontarians with a $200 "rebate," sparking talk of an early election.
The government must be so flush with cash it can't find anything better to do with the $3.2 billion that's going to cost. Doug Ford says it will boost the economy. How can it not? In reality, for so many it'll be just a blip in the bank account, gone in the blink of an eye on the next hydro, phone or gas bill. But people's lives aren't going to change materially.
If the government wants to help Ontario families, it would giddy up and sign a deal for the national school lunch program. Outside of price controls, there's little the government can do in a free market about food sector 'greedflation' which has resulted in smaller package volumes and rising sticker prices. Price controls? That's communist.
But helping families feed their kids by providing a nutritious lunch is something the government can do and be far more effective at in directing assistance where it's needed. Kids' tummies. And it would save families more than $200, not to mention the stress of preparing lunches for a busy family every morning. Ford says he's "all in" on talks with the feds, but why does it take so long to sit down and hammer out a plan? This should be a priority.
Let's hope it fares better than the $10-a-day daycare collaboration.
This "rebate" isn't the first time Ford has tried to outright bribe voters. Remember the $200 to families during the teachers' strike? The cancellation of car registration fees was another bribe taken up by the people of Ontario, happily, as a much-needed revenue stream disappeared from provincial coffers.
Ontario is seriously in debt. I normally wouldn't focus on debt - government debt is ubiquitous across every jurisdiction - but Ontario doesn't have the money to be giving rebates to anyone for anything right now. The interest alone is staggering.
With insistent demands on funds for necessary goods and services like housing, health care, education, infrastructure repair, social assistance reform etc., there is no extra money to "rebate." It's a bait and switch as homeowners will see it swallowed up in increased property taxes and renters will watch it go poof when their rent goes up. It's not meant to help anyone besides being a tiny little Band-Aid on a gaping, bleeding wound, falsely boosting the economy for optics.
Meanwhile, those people who don't need it - whose struggle isn't so close to the bone, who don't pay much attention to the news, who aren't sick or worried about elderly parents or special needs children, and who can't, or won't, see those struggling around them - will pocket the cash and remember what a great guy Ford is and how he gifted them when it comes time to cast their vote.
This is a blatant attempt by the Conservatives to buy votes. Why isn't it against election laws to give "rebates" in months leading up to an election? Might as well stand outside polling stations and give out money.
You know who does need the money way more than most of us? And they need a lot more than a token $200? People living on social assistance.
There is no metric that measures Ontario social assistance rates as livable in today's economy. Anyone who can do simple math, very simple math, can see that the rates people receive on OW/ODSP are abysmally inadequate in the face of today's cost of living. Rents continue to remain outrageously high, food costs are going up, everything costs more, yet people on social assistance have gotten a paltry rate increase, an insulting five per cent, after losing 22 per cent under the Harris government and not regaining ground, beyond indexing, since.
So much of the homelessness issue could be addressed by simply giving people on social assistance enough money to find a home. That would be the price of my vote.
CAPTION: So much of the homelessness issue could be addressed by simply giving people on social assistance enough money to find a home, Margaret Shkimba writes.

Document 318 of 500

Die-in at Queen's Park targets Doug Ford's plan to close 10 safe injection sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/die-at-queens-park-targets-doug-fords-plan-close/docview/3119258082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Die-in+at+Queen%27s+Park+targets+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+to+close+10+safe+injection+sites&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Activists staged a die-in at Queen's Park on Monday in protest of the provincial government's plan to shutter 10 safe injection sites across Ontario, which some say will be a "death sentence."
About 200 advocates, front-line workers, health-care providers and families of people lost to the drug poisoning crisis were among those lying down outside the provincial legislature in opposition to the Doug Ford government's plan to shutter the safe injection sites.
Supporters chanted "Harm reduction saves lives" and brandished signs with slogans such as "Save our sites or fill your morgues" as they heard stories from people who have lost loved ones to overdoses or have been traumatized by the health-care system when seeking treatment.
One of those people is Hannah Stahl, a street nurse who lost her brother Dalton to an overdose in 2017 in Hamilton, where there were no safe injection sites at the time. The current site in Hamilton is one of the 10 slated to close.
"He had recently been released from jail and he was really falling through the cracks of the health-care system," Stahl said.
"It's really pertinent when we have a toxic drug supply as dangerous as it is right now that people have a safe place to go," Stahl added. "Because there's no point in offering more detox beds if people are dying."
In August, the Ford government announced new zoning restrictions requiring safe injection sites, also known as Consumption Treatment Service (CTS) sites, to be 200 metres from schools and daycares, which will result in 10 of the province's sites having to close by March 31, 2025, including five in Toronto.
The sites offer needle exchange, supplies, supervision and drug safety testing to people who use drugs.
Danny Dobric, who has used opioids for more than a decade, said he knows at least 30 people who have died of overdoses when using alone, including two former partners.
"The times that I wasn't there, look what happened," he said. His wife died in 2000, his girlfriend last year.
He said he has tried numerous treatment programs but still depends on safe consumption sites as a lifeline. He is anxious about what might happen if they close.
"I know if I go under, someone's right there, because you only have a few minutes," he said. "I use by myself. So anything can happen."
Supporters of the sites say they not only save lives, but provide a pathway for people to receive treatment services, while critics argue they increase crime and social disorder in the neighbourhoods that have them.
The sites have always been controversial, but they received renewed criticism after Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother of two, was killed by a stray bullet last summer near Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre.
Ford has said he's personally opposed to the sites, calling them a "failed policy" without providing clear evidence for his reasoning.
He said he's in favour of getting people into treatment, and the government announced plans to spend $378 million on 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs with 375 supportive housing units as well as treatment beds.
In a statement, Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Health, said each site slated to close will have the opportunity to convert into a HART hub.
"Communities, parents and families across Ontario have made it clear that the presence of drug consumption sites near schools and daycares is leading to serious safety problems ... That's why our government is taking action to keep communities safe, while supporting the recovery of those struggling with opioid addiction," she said.
Dr. Nanky Rai, a family doctor and community organizer who spoke at the event, said comparing treatment to consumption sites is a false dichotomy, adding that the injection sites offer wraparound services such as counselling and referrals to other health-care professionals, in addition to lifesaving measures such as overdose reversals and drug testing services.
"The provincial government is using this moment to be like, 'Let's attack these handful of sites that are doing incredibly lifesaving work and create this bogus dichotomy about there needing to be more treatment', as if treatment doesn't exist through these sites," Rai said.
She believes closing the sites will undoubtedly lead to more fatalities.
"It's a death sentence for clients I work with, for their loved ones, for people in my life personally, and we know that almost every family in Ontario at this point has been affected by the drug poisoning crisis."
CAPTION: Toronto street nurse Hannah Stahl lost her brother Dalton to an overdose in 2017 in Hamilton, where there were no safe injection sites at the time.A man draws a chalk outline around Toronto street nurse Hannah Stahl during the "die-in."About 200 people staged a "die-in" at Queen's Park in protest of the provincial government's plan to shutter 10 safe injection sites across Ontario.

Document 319 of 500

Doug Ford's bill to limit new bike lanes will also 'review' existing ones

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-bill-limit-new-bike-lanes-will-also/docview/3119063078/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+bill+to+limit+new+bike+lanes+will+also+%27review%27+existing+ones&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a salvo at the city of Toronto, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are hinting at the end of bike lanes on Yonge and Bloor streets and Eglinton and University avenues.
Under sweeping new legislation tabled Monday by Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, the Highway Traffic Act is being amended to say "the Ministry may review existing bicycle lanes" and municipalities must receive provincial approval before installing new lanes.
"We've seen Toronto become one of the most congested cities in the entire world and this is really about a reasonable approach," said Sarkaria, who commutes to Brampton daily and often speaks of being stuck on University Avenue south of Queen's Park.
"We're seeing gridlock in Toronto, whether it be Bloor, University or Yonge Street," said the minister, whose office later confirmed that the province would cover any costs incurred by municipalities to remove bike lanes.
His bill empowers the Ministry of Transportation to take "consideration of traffic flow" before approving any new lanes.
"When considering whether to approve the design for the construction of a bicycle lane, the Ministry may consider whether it would unduly diminish the orderly movement of motor vehicle traffic," the bill states.
Sarkaria stressed "we're not anti-bike lane," but insisted a "reasonable approach" is needed.
"There's absolutely a place for bikes and for bike lanes," he said, adding "major arterial roads" are not the best place for such lanes.
"We don't want lanes of traffic removed at a time where we're seeing a population growth that has been significant for the province of Ontario."
Those changes could impact existing bike lanes on Yonge, Bloor, Eglinton and University, among others.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario criticized the province for "significant overreach" into civic jurisdiction.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow has also pushed back at the usurping of municipal authority. Her office declined to comment on the legislation Monday and referred the Star to her previous statement, which warned that "ripping up our roads (to remove bike lanes) will make congestion worse," and said the province should tackle gridlock by completing the delayed Finch West and Eglinton LRTs.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford is relitigating battles from his tenure at city hall, where he was a councillor when his late brother, Rob Ford, was Toronto's mayor.
"He failed at city council ... and now he's just playing that all out again," said Stiles.
"But this is meddling in important issues that municipalities have a responsibility for. They're micromanaging municipal issues here when he's forgetting the basics of what a provincial government should be paying attention to: health care, housing, the cost of living, our schools," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ford clearly "regrets not being elected mayor of the city of Toronto" in 2014 when he lost to John Tory.
"Municipal politicians, they know their residents, they know their roads, let them make those decisions," said Crombie, a former Mississauga mayor.
"The premier needs to stay in his lane. He thinks the solution to our $13-billion congestion gridlock problem is removing bike lanes. We have very serious issues at hand that aren't going to be solved by that. We need investment in public transit," she said.
But the Tories - who are considering an early election in 2025, a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - believe curbing bike lanes would be popular with commuters.
According to Statistics Canada data from May, 71.2 per cent of Toronto-area residents took a car, truck or van to work while 22.7 per cent rode transit and 5.4 per cent used "active transportation," meaning they walked or cycled.
In 2011, the most recent year separate cycling information was available, 1.2 per cent of Torontonians commuted to work by bicycle while 69.9 per cent drove or were passengers in a car, 23.3 per cent took public transit and 4.6 per cent walked.
Sarkaria's omnibus legislation would also expedite construction of Highway 413, the 52-kilometre freeway linking Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan, with an "accelerated environmental assessment process" that has ecologists concerned.
It would also slap hefty fines on protesters of up to $50,000 for a first offence for trying to thwart construction of Highway 413 and $100,000 for a subsequent violation.
"To disagree with the government is one thing, but to block critical infrastructure from being built is absolutely not acceptable. It wouldn't be acceptable on this project or any other project," the minister said.
On Thursday, Ford received applause from many of the 1,200 people at his Empire Club luncheon speech when he said his government was "bringing sanity back to bike lane decisions."
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes. We need to - and will - remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill," the premier said.
The Toronto and Region Board of Trade signalled openness to the province's plans, saying in a statement "decisions limiting road capacity, including adding bike lanes to major roads, must be driven by traffic and cycling data and a clear understanding of the implications for travel and commute times."
Dr. Samantha Green, a family physician and co-founder of the Doctors for Safe Cycling advocacy group, said bike lanes have been shown to reduce deaths and injuries to cyclists, and a "robust network of connected, separated bike lanes" would encourage more people to ride.
"So removing bike lanes is a bad decision for the health and safety of people in the province," she said.
The bill was the first legislation tabled as the house resumed after an extended 137-day summer recess.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford gestures in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 21, 2024.

Document 320 of 500

Liberal MPs seek 'reflection and change' with letter calling on Justin Trudeau to step down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-mps-seek-reflection-change-with-letter/docview/3119063077/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MPs+seek+%27reflection+and+change%27+with+letter+calling+on+Justin+Trudeau+to+step+down&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Numerous Liberals MPs have signed a private letter calling on Justin Trudeau to step down and are prepared to make their case at Wednesday's national caucus meeting as some leadership hopefuls are beginning to organize.
Publicly, however, many are holding their cards close, refusing to divulge either the letter's contents, its signatories or its organizers.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long, who failed in June to elicit caucus support for his call on Trudeau to step down, told the Star "there is obviously a letter. I've signed it." Long, who is not the organizer of the letter, declined to provide much detail about it - even about the number of MPs who had signed it. "Is it over 20, most likely, and under 40 - yeah," he said.
"It's basically just a letter that's asking for reflection and change," Long said. "I think the letter basically sets the tone for a very frank and open discussion, and I mean, obviously, about leadership."
Those on board with the letter believe Trudeau should step aside as leader, but Long, who said he is not an organizer, added he isn't advocating for a particular candidate to replace him.
But speculation about the would-be contenders is hot. Economist and former central banker Mark Carney has confirmed he will run for office at some point, although he remains coy about when, or what job he wants.
For now, the top job is Trudeau's.
Several MPs told the Star on Monday they had not been approached to sign the letter and had only heard about it in media reports, downplaying its impact and describing the agitators as a small, if vocal, minority.
Mona Fortier, who is charged with writing the Liberal election platform, summed up the comments of nearly a dozen other Liberal MPs when she said, "Nobody asked me to sign a phantom letter."
The call for Trudeau to step down stems not just from the Atlantic caucus but "a collective from across the country," Long said, pointing to polls in B.C., Ontario and the Atlantic region that "tell us the same thing - that we are not in a good spot."
Among Liberals, he said, "there's just a growing concern about the lack of direction and lack of change. I think the approach that, 'Let's just wait it out and see if Canadians come around,' or 'Let's just kind of run the clock a bit, and maybe Pierre Poilievre will make a mistake' - it's not going to work."
Trudeau still has "a lot of support in caucus" among the Liberals' 153 MPs, Long admitted, but among voters he contends there is a "genuine, personal dislike of the prime minister."
In Long's view, things have only worsened since his failed attempt to call for a national caucus meeting to confront Trudeau with concerns after the byelection loss of what should have been a safe Liberal seat in Toronto-St. Paul's.
"We're four months closer to an election, not seeing any direction, not seeing any sense of urgency," he said. "The stay-calm-and-carry-on approach is, I think, concerning."
That's a common concern even among MPs who say they support Trudeau's decision - which he has repeatedly expressed both publicly and privately - to stay on to lead the party into the next federal election campaign.
Pickering-Uxbridge MP Jennifer O'Connell said Trudeau is "our leader and he should stay. I think he's in the best position to lead us, but I think there does have to be serious conversations happening at caucus for the mood and the reflection of what we're all hearing in our riding."
O'Connell said she does not hear the kind of voter dislike for Trudeau that Long and P.E.I. MP Sean Casey have talked publicly about, but that the party needs to ensure there is not a "disconnect" with voters.
She said it must ensure "we are actually staying focused on things that people in ridings like mine really care about - the economy, overall affordability. The environment is still really important, and how kids or young people are going to afford housing as they grow up. Things like that really matter.
"I do think we have to have some honest conversations about that direction, we can't just be told what that direction is," she said.
If those concerns "gets listened to, then I think we're in a good position to move forward properly," she said, cautioning that the caucus has been promised things like advertising and communications strategies "for a while now." Asked where those are, she said, "Good question. I think those are the conversations we very fairly need to have."
Several MPs predicted Wednesday's showdown would fizzle.
"I don't think anything's going to happen on Wednesday," said Humber River-Black Creek MP Judy Sgro. "We're here to get ready for the next election. In order to do that we need to all be working together, there needs to be one issue, and that is getting elected and serving Canadians. That's it."
Sydney-Victoria MP Jaime Battiste said he anticipated a "normal caucus meeting."
Although the job of Liberal leader is not open, there is already a sense of movement among his would-be successors, with some making their intentions clearer - albeit not all publicly.
Sources inside the Liberal party say François-Philippe Champagne and Mélanie Joly have had conversations with colleagues about organizing future leadership bids, although Champagne denied to the Star on Monday that his calls were about organizing.
Former B.C. Premier Christy Clark suggested to Radio-Canada she, too, is interested in the job.
Mark Carney, who was tapped by Trudeau last month to advise the Liberal platform on economic policy, confirmed in a podcast interview with Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith that he is "taking steps" towards putting his name on a ballot at some unspecified point.
Polls have suggested the Liberals would have as hard a time winning the next election under any of the long-rumoured aspirants to Trudeau's job.
Asked if he thinks Carney should be leader, Long said no, adding the party should beware of any coronation, pointing to Michael Ignatieff's troubled tenure after Stéphane Dion cratered as Liberal leader.
"I think we need a wide open race," Long said. "We don't need to coronate anybody. I think that we have a lot of people in the party that would be wonderful leaders ... That's what leadership contests are for. I mean, democracy is messy."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is shown in the House of Commons on Sept. 25, 2024.

Document 321 of 500

Four ways to mock Doug Ford for trying to buy our fleeting love

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/four-ways-mock-doug-ford-trying-buy-our-fleeting/docview/3118819888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+ways+to+mock+Doug+Ford+for+trying+to+buy+our+fleeting+love&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Early next year, Ontario Premier Doug Ford apparently plans to give all 16 million of us about $200, possibly just before an election. My household will rake in a whole $400. Tax-free. Gosh. Supernice!
I take it this is a bribe to win my vote. But no. How did Shakespeare put it: "Roses have thorns, and silver fountains mud; And loathsome canker lies in sweetest bud."
As the Shakespearean TV parody "Upstart Crow" makes clear, the playwright did over-egg his metaphors. Simply put, Shakespeare opposed corruption. He wouldn't have liked Ford's canker at all.
But my vote cannot be bought, especially not for a lousy 400 bucks. In 2000, the revolting Premier Mike Harris did the same. Even then, I donated his gift to charity, probably UNICEF.
In 2025, I suppose I'll do the same, sending it to the UN which feeds the children Israel is starving and maiming in Gaza. But that won't annoy Ford or even register with him. My aim is to make him squirm.
Here's a list of ways to spend money as a statement: (For example, the winner refusing the Scotiabank-assisted Giller Prize would be moving and infinitely stylish too.) 1. Decently to ease my conscience. 2. Publicly as a declaration. 3. Wastefully to show contempt. 4. Locally to help the city Ford despises.
We've done Decency. Let's move to public statements.
There are infinite ways to debase Ford's choice loudly but I could productively plant a sapling at Ontario Place, where they just razed 800 trees to make way for a huge spa. But memorial trees with a little plaque dedicated in my case "To Tommy Tree, the oak that once played happily here" cost $738. Perhaps I could ask my daughters to donate their bribe. I'll get back to you.
Next is wasting the money. Born to a penny-pinching Scottish mother and a free-spending Indian father, I take my dad's side. I'm good at this. I just spent 92 euros on a Svenskt Tenn birch tray celebrating the Swedish fish harvest plus another 55 euros to ship it to Canada so I can nail it to a wall.
But that only adds up to $220. They also sell a lovely placemat honouring the painfully shy Baltic turbot. I could use up my husband's bribe on feeding my passion for art displaying the tragic destruction of the natural world.
Or. I could buy a $400 pair of velvet lounge pants, the kind you can swim in all winter, to replace the sweatpants I wear to work at my home office, six steps down the hall from where we sleep and all pants reside. This life I am living, it is not a good life. Faites un effort, the French say. And I say, look at those fluffy trousers.
We move on to the local spending section of mocking Ford for trying to buy our fleeting love.
There's an economic concept Ford often overlooks. Give spare money to people who desperately need it, because they will spend every cent locally, enriching Toronto businesses, cooking nutritious local food for their children, keeping the local landlord quiescent, buying snowsuits, hockey gear, mittens and crayons and keeping tiny local Canadians distracted and unaware of their poverty.
In other words, raise welfare and disability payments. That money flows through Toronto like water, enriching all of us. It keeps people stable and housed, out of those shocking tent encampments that always make me think the end is near for this city.
This is how I imagine it all began, a blowsy Ontario premier sitting in his office with his feet on the desk like Trump, telling his pals, "Whores will have their trinkets" and blasting $100 bills out of a peashooter at every passing voter.
Will it work?
CAPTION: Heather Mallick's four ways to spend Premier Doug Ford's $200: 1. Decently to ease my conscience. 2. Publicly as a declaration. 3. Wastefully to show contempt. 4. Locally to help the city Ford despises.

Document 322 of 500

Auto theft in GTA fuels mounting costs and concerns over personal safety. The price tag might surprise you: The Insurance Bureau of Canada estimated $1.6 billion in costs last year associated with auto theft

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/auto-theft-gta-fuels-mounting-costs-concerns-over/docview/3118819837/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Auto+theft+in+GTA+fuels+mounting+costs+and+concerns+over+personal+safety.+The+price+tag+might+surprise+you%3A+The+Insurance+Bureau+of+Canada+estimated+%241.6+billion+in+costs+last+year+associated+with+auto+theft&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Driven to Steal: What is Fuelling Auto Theft in the GTA is a series of articles about who and what is behind this increasingly concerning crime wave, response to rising incidents and the cost to our wallets and public safety.
If you think auto theft isn't costing you because your vehicle hasn't been stolen, think again.
On average, the first $130 we pay on an auto insurance premium went to paying out insurance company auto theft claims, says the Insurance Bureau of Canada (IBC). In communities where auto theft is prevalent, that amount was likely higher.
The figure is in an annual report provided by the IBC, which monitors auto insurance costs.
Costs and concerns over what could be termed an auto theft epidemic, particularly in the Greater Toronto Area, have increased since 2018.
The Insurance Bureau of Canada estimated what it calls "the costs associated with auto theft - from law enforcement and court resources, to the mental anguish of being victimized, to the general economic costs" at $1.6 billion in 2023.
Between 2019 and 2023, provincial statistics showed a 116 per cent increase in stolen vehicles when thefts are tallied for police services in Peel, York, Durham, Hamilton and Niagara regions, the City of Ottawa and for the Ontario Provincial Police.
In Durham, Halton and Peel regions, auto thefts tripled between 2019 and 2023, while they more than doubled in York and Toronto.
In places like York, Peel, Durham and Halton, insurance premiums have gone up by far more than the average for drivers nationwide.
Even if you haven't been a victim of auto theft, the fear you might be has likely gone up exponentially since 2018.
A September 2023 Angus Reid Institute survey on behalf of Équité revealed that:
84 per cent of Canadians say the rise in auto theft makes them concerned about the increase in crime in their community.69 per cent say the rise in auto theft makes them concerned about their personal safety and/or the safety of their family.65 per cent say that government, auto manufacturers, law enforcement and the courts need to work together to fix this issue.Interpol: Canada a Top 10 nation for auto theft
Even the International Criminal Police Organization (Interpol) is involved in dealing with the problem that has put Canada on a global crime map.
"In recent years, Canada has emerged as a key source country for stolen motor vehicles, in part given its large supply of sought-after high-value models such as SUVs and crossovers. Many of the vehicles are shipped to the Middle East and West Africa, where they are then traded or resold," Interpol said in early 2024.
Interpol listed Canada as a top 10 nation for auto theft among the 137 nations it tracks for the crime, and tracked 1,500 Canadian vehicles sold around the world between February and May 2024.
All of that concern leads to residents purchasing more home and vehicle security devices, going out of pocket to the tune of hundreds, and sometimes thousands of dollars for cameras, spotlights and other theft deterrents such as the classic, relatively inexpensive steering wheel locks.
Government devoting hundreds of millions to issue
All levels of government are spending a combined hundreds of millions more in tax dollars to try to crack down on the problem.
Ontario's latest investment is $134 million for five new helicopters in the GTA and Ottawa in a Joint Air Support Unit to help combat carjacking and auto theft, among other criminal activity. It will take up to three years to acquire the state-of-the-art new helicopters, which will go to York, Peel, Durham, Toronto (to be operated by the OPP) and Ottawa.
That amount comes on top of a $51-million investment in the 2024 Ontario provincial budget to support the OPP's Provincial Auto Theft and Towing Team and Ontario's Major Auto Theft Prosecution Response Team. There was also an $18-million investment late in 2023 to enhance auto theft training for officers and, this past August, the creation of a junior ministry inside the Solicitor General's ministry dedicated solely to auto theft in Ontario.
This combined provincial funding comes on top of the annual funding of many millions of dollars provided municipally, primarily from property taxpayers, to every GTA police service to use on its own and in combination with other local forces to attack auto theft.
That means officers who could be working on other crime-solving or prevention work are now devoted exclusively or largely to auto theft, and regional police services are devoting a greater percentage of their expanding annual budgets to auto theft.
The Government of Canada has chipped in with $121 million over five years in aid to provincial agencies to help combat guns, gangs and auto theft and is attempting to prevent vehicles from being transported overseas. But that amount is expected to increase.
This adds up to auto theft hitting everyone in the pocketbook - even those who don't drive, but especially those who do.
While all of these numbers may be numbing, there is a glimmer of hope. The efforts that have been steadily increased in the last few years to fight auto theft may be bearing some fruit.
In the first half of 2024, reports of auto theft have slightly declined in Ontario over the previous year, which was a high-water mark. According to Équité, auto theft was down 14 per cent in Ontario and down 36 per cent in Quebec compared to the first six months in 2023.
It says that SUVs, as they have been for years, continue to be the top target for auto thieves. Whether insurance premiums will begin to fall in 2025 in correspondence with falling auto theft rates remains to be seen.
Whether drivers feel their personal safety, homes and automobiles are protected from thieves who will go to great lengths to steal their vehicles may depend on an expensive collaboration involving law enforcement, government, automakers and the insurance industry.
CAPTION: The issue of auto theft reached a crisis in 2023 when it gripped the attention of governments everywhere, but especially across the GTA and in Ontario, where vehicles were being stolen frequently by gangs. Though auto theft had been rising throughout the pandemic, 2023 was the worst year yet and the Ontario government invested heavily that year in combatting auto theft and again in 2024, even creating a junior ministry to deal with the issue.Vehicles are being discovered by law enforcement, usually at national ports of entry.In a July 29 news release, Premier Doug Ford said the new helicopters are part of the provincial government's"necessary investments to ensure that police services have the tools and resources they need to crack down on auto theft and car thieves."On March 20, 2024, Peel Regional Police in collaboration with a joint management team, hosted the second annual Auto Theft Summit. The joint management team comprised of: Durham Regional Police Service (DRPS) Halton Regional Police Service (HRPS) Hamilton Police Service (HPS) Niagara Regional Police Service (NRPS) Ontario Provincial Police (OPP) Toronto Police Service (TPS) York Regional Police (YRP) Criminal Intelligence Service of Ontario (CISO) Ontario Association of Chiefs of Police (OACP)Peel Regional Police Chief Nishan Duraiappah addresses the media following an auto theft summit in Mississauga where police proposed numerous recommendations aimed at addressing the ongoing vehicle theft crime wave.Stolen vehicles are so much more than a missing car, truck or motorcycle. They facilitate all types of crimes.

Subject: Crime; Automobile theft; Collaboration; Law enforcement; Costs; Robbery; Automobile insurance; Vehicles; Insurance industry

Document 323 of 500

Ford government appoints former federal Liberal minister to help tackle family doctor shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-appoints-former-federal-liberal/docview/3118819791/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+appoints+former+federal+Liberal+minister+to+help+tackle+family+doctor+shortage&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the face of mounting criticism over a worsening shortage of family doctors, Premier Doug Ford's government is turning to Queen's University medical school director Dr. Jane Philpott to solve the problem.
The former federal Liberal health minister will head a "primary care action team" aimed at connecting every Ontarian to a family doctor within five years. Her appointment takes effect Dec. 1.
Philpott's challenge is massive: 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a primary care physician, a number that is expected to top four million in the next couple of years as the province grows and aging family doctors retire. The Ontario Medical association estimates the province needs at least 2,500 more primary care physicians.
"There's no one I trust more than Dr. Philpott with her considerable experience to keep moving us forward and get across the finish line of connecting everyone in the province to more convenient primary care," Health Minister Sylvia Jones said in a statement Monday as the legislature resumed following an unusually long five-month summer break amid speculation about an early election next year.
Opposition parties signalled they will pressure the government on health care with Ford seemingly more concerned about bike lanes, building a tunnel under Highway 401 and spending as much as $1 billion to get beer, wine and other alcoholic drinks into convenience stores a year earlier than planned.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles was skeptical about the Philpott appointment.
"I have enormous respect for her," she said, but added "this is not a government that listens to experts, let along their own experts ... we'll wait to see what they do."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie noted that Ford promised in the 2018 election to end "hallway health care" in hospitals, a problem that has worsened to about 2,000 patients a day after six years of Progressive Conservative rule.
"Yet again, we see this government appointing an expert or a panel of experts only to write a report which they will ignore," Crombie told reporters, adding she also has respect for Philpott, who was a keynote speaker to the Ontario Liberal party's policy convention in London last month.
To put the doctor shortage into perspective, the Liberals pointed out the 2.5 million Ontarians without a family physician are roughly equivalent to the populations of Ottawa, Mississauga and Brampton combined.
In a statement released by the government, Philpott said "Ontario can build a health system where the guarantee of access to primary care is as automatic as the assurance that every child will be assigned to a public school in their neighbourhood."
"Our goal will be for 100 per cent of Ontarians to be attached to a family doctor or nurse practitioner working in a publicly funded team, where they receive ongoing, comprehensive care and people can access that care in a timely way," she continued.
Philpott's school analogy, however, does not account for the fact that in some fast-growing areas of Toronto school boards have posted signs warning new residents that their children may not be able to attend a neighbourhood school because of overcrowding and may have to go to a school further away.
Philpott, who is also dean of the faculty of health sciences at Queen's and has spent 30 years in family medicine and on global health initiatives, will oversee a Health Ministry team that will look to the "Periwinkle model" she devised with colleagues in the Kingston-area Frontenac, Lennox and Addington Ontario health team to expand access to primary care, including weekends and evenings.
While Jones has invested $110 million in primary health care teams across the province to connect about 328,000 more people to family doctors, the doctor shortage persists. In Sault Ste. Marie, for example, a major clinic cut loose several thousand patients earlier this year because it didn't have enough physicians to care for them.
The Ontario Medical Association has complained about a heavy administrative burden on family doctors that is taking time away from patient care, and encouraged the government to streamline paperwork.
Ontario is opening two new medical schools and has expanded the number of medical school spots available, but it will take time for those students to graduate. The province is also trying to recruit more doctors trained in other countries.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford arrives at Queen's Park as the Ontario Legislature resumes in Toronto on Monday.

Subject: Health care access; Primary care; Appointments & personnel changes; Global health; Physicians

Oct. 21: $200 won't buy my vote, wishing Filomena Tassi all the best and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-21-200-wont-buy-my-vote-wishing-filomena/docview/3118819760/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+21%3A+%24200+won%27t+buy+my+vote%2C+wishing+Filomena+Tassi+all+the+best+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: $200 won't buy my vote
Re: Doug Ford signals rebate cheques for all Ontarians will 'stimulate the economy,' Oct. 17
So Doug, you have $3.2 billion in your back pocket to buy my vote? Well, $200 isn't going to cut it.
What I want is a government that uses that money to build affordable family housing not one-bedroom condos. I want a government that makes a contract for home health supplies with a company that can actually supply the products. I want a government that finds shelter for the homeless. I want a government that aims to educate and care for every child in the province. I want a government that protects the Greenbelt. I want a government that questions why transportation projects are taking so long, and whether we even need some of them. I want a transparent government - I don't want to have to look for and read the fine print in government contracts, like the spa at Ontario Place.
I wish you well in retirement, because $200 is not going to buy my vote.
Jean Wise, Hamilton
Invest in health and education
Doug Ford plans to buy your vote with a $200 gift, which I don't deny many families need. My issue with this is there are people in this province who are doing just fine and don't need this handout to buy their vote.
Doug, give it to the needy and what remains can be used to fund our crumbling public health care (not to private clinics) and our schools struggling with providing proper teaching tools for our children. Those two issues will garner you more votes then anything else.
David Intini, Stoney Creek
Wishing Tassi all the best
Re: Hamilton MP Filomena Tassi will not run for re-election, Oct. 17
We were saddened by the recent news of Filomena Tassi's announcement, but respect her decision.
Politics aside, as a former neighbour in Dundas, Filomena (and her family) always took time to say hello and chat during what must have been a very busy time while she was minister of labour. A cheerful disposition and friendly smile accompanied her local walks, and a polite, gracious manner brought pride into our little neighbourhood.
My family and I wish her all the best, and heartfelt thanks for her unwavering representation.
Wray Brown, Dundas
Term is about being inclusive
Re: Why not just say 'women?' Oct. 17
I felt compelled to respond regarding the letter titled "Why not just say women?" I am going to err on the side of assuming that this person does not work in health care, particularly obstetrics/gynecology. The professionals working in this specialty have, for some time now, used the term "pregnant people" as it is inclusive for all those who are pregnant. There are a number of trans men and non-binary folk who become pregnant and don't identify as a "pregnant woman." I hope that my rather simplistic explanation is a polite way of enlightening this person regarding the changes in providing pregnancy care to all.
Kitty Stern, Burlington
Do Americans want a dictatorship?
I'm hoping Americans really think passionately about who they are voting for. Do you want a dictatorship or democracy? Look at dictatorship countries around the world, and ask yourself, "do I want to live there?"
I'm concerned for my country if Americans don't do the right thing for the rest of the world. I'm not kidding.
Jann Hanson, Hamilton
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford gestures to the crowd during the International Plowing Match and Rural Expo in Lindsay onOct. 1. Instead of sending cheques to every resident, he should use the money to improve health care, education and housing, readers say.

Document 325 of 500

Pierre Poilievre says he wants provinces to overhaul their disability programs - and he could withhold federal money to make it happen

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-says-he-wants-provinces-overhaul/docview/3118627098/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+says+he+wants+provinces+to+overhaul+their+disability+programs+-+and+he+could+withhold+federal+money+to+make+it+happen&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says he wants provinces to overhaul their disability assistance programs so that disabled Canadians don't see working income clawed back from provincial benefits, and he could put billions in federal transfers at stake to make it happen.
Poilievre made that commitment in a 2023 video recently viewed by the Star, in which he referred to a private member's bill he introduced as the Conservative finance critic in 2018. If passed, the bill would have required those changes as a condition to receiving the Canada Social Transfer, a federal payment to the provinces that helps to cover the cost of social programs.
"For those roughly a million Canadians with disabilities who do work, we need to reform the benefit programs to reward that work. Right now, there are clawbacks if you're a person on disability and you get a job. Often, they take away your income, rental support, even medication," he said in the video, posted online by political commentator Darshan Maharaja and viewed just over 2,000 times.
"I will pass the fairness for workers with disabilities act, which will require provinces, as a condition of getting their federal money, to reform their systems to make sure that every time a person with disabilities earns an extra dollar, they're made better off and that they're not punished for that. Everybody should have the chance to put their talents to work for this great country of ours."
The change would be one step to address what many see as a thorny flaw in the social assistance programs offered across Canada. However, it raises questions about the approach of a Conservative leader who's routinely accused the Trudeau government of overreach and could lead to pushback from provinces antsy about the precedent it would set, said policy experts who spoke to the Star.
"Anything that helps impoverished people with disabilities get to live above the poverty line is a good step," said David Lepofsky, a lawyer and disability advocate in Ontario. "If the Tories are going to take this approach to federal power, there is more to do."
But "imagine trying to put conditions on something that's been unconditional since 1995," said Gillian Petit, an economist at the University of Calgary, pointing to Poilievre's proposal to tie the Canada Social Transfer to the reforms. This year, provinces and territories are expected to receive $16.9 billion from the Canada Social Transfer, with Ontario getting $6.5 billion.
"It would probably be a bit of a political fiasco."
Faced with that question in 2018 - before the bill was voted down by the Liberals - Poilievre pointed to delivery standards set out in the Canada Health Transfer and said this scenario justifies adding conditions.
Whether he is still willing to put billions in federal transfers at stake to implement those changes, however, remains unclear.
In a statement to the Star, Poilievre repeated his criticism of current systems that penalize disabled Canadians that work, but did not say if he would use the threat of withdrawing federal money to force change. And that is no different from what he said in the 2023 video, his office said, though it would not provide details on his proposal.
"As prime minister, I will be working with provinces to ensure workers with disabilities always benefit from working another hour, taking another shift, or accepting a job opportunity," Poilievre said.
Among the unanswered questions is how he would get provinces on board, how much working income he would want exempted from clawbacks, and how this principle would apply to non-cash benefits and towards federal programs.
In the 2018 bill, Poilievre proposed disallowing provinces from clawing back benefits for disabled Canadians on employment income up to $30,000 and any province that did risked losing their portion of the Canada Social Transfer. The bill also said Finance Canada should use available data to calculate clawbacks on benefits and the finance minister should "identify and consider" changes that could be made to federal taxes and benefits in order to reduce those clawbacks.
He said the principle of allowing disabled Canadians to keep their working income should be consistent across all levels of government and argued it would save governments money if people are able to work.
Right now, only a small portion of people who receive disability assistance are able to work, said Alexi White, the director of systems change at Maytree. For example, out of the 367,828 Ontario Disability Support Program cases in 2022/2023, 10 per cent reported working income, according to data from the social policy group.
But Jen Robson, an associate professor of public policy at Carleton University, said the proposal as outlined in the 2018 bill could lead to strong pushback from provinces, including legal challenges. Referencing Poilievre's housing bill, she said that would be another piece of legislation from the Conservative leader that overrides provincial jurisdiction with the "Ottawa-knows best" approach he's accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government of.
"It's well-intentioned but inherently flawed," she said. "It's very heavy handed."
She said it would be best to achieve the results Poilievre wants through negotiations rather than trying to force the provinces, similar to how the Liberals have approached the Canada Disability Benefit (CDB), which is slated to roll out next summer.
The Liberals had argued it would have been unconstitutional to disallow clawbacks in the legislation despite repeated pleas from advocates and some senators. So far, four provinces and two territories have said they won't claw back their own supports to recipients of the CDB after discussions with Disabilities Minister Kamal Khera, while another has increased its own supports.
Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives have not outlined their position on the federal benefit, but all opposition parties have pressed the Liberal government over the historic program that has been slammed as a disappointment by disability advocates. That benefit, which is expected to reach 600,000 Canadians at its peak, will be clawed back from its $200 monthly maximum at $23,000 in annual income, but allows for up to $10,000 in working income exemptions for eligible individuals.
The Liberals and other opposition parties have accused Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives of wanting to cut social programs, focusing their criticism on the federal dental and pharmacare programs.
"If Poilievre truly cared about supporting Canadians with disabilities, instead of putting out slogans, he would support this benefit and our affordable dental care plan which also supports persons with disabilities," a spokesperson for Khera said in a statement to the Star.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in the Foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, on Oct. 8, 2024.

Document 326 of 500

MPPs return to Queen's Park on Monday. Their first order of business?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mpps-return-queens-park-on-monday-their-first/docview/3118626775/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPPs+return+to+Queen%27s+Park+on+Monday.+Their+first+order+of+business%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After an extended 137-day summer break, MPPs return to Queen's Park for what could be the last session of the legislature before an election next year.
With Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives signalling they may choose to face voters more than a year before the scheduled June 2026 election, the fall sitting that begins Monday should be eventful.
As first disclosed by the Star last Wednesday, the Tories will use Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's Oct. 30 fall economic statement to announce 16 million Ontarians will receive cheques of around $200 early in the new year.
That move will cost the treasury more than $3.2 billion.
In a campaign-style speech to the Empire Club last Thursday, Ford vowed to push the economy forward with measures "to build critical infrastructure faster" such as 24/7 construction on Highway 413 and the Bradford Bypass with Ontario's population expected to grow by two million by 2031.
"Basically everywhere you drive right now ... there's construction. I know it's a pain in the butt, but it's needed," he told a crowd of 1,200 at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre.
"Our government remains focused on continuing to rebuild Ontario's economy starting with creating conditions in the environment for businesses to grow, thrive and prosper," the premier added.
Ford made no mention in his 20-minute speech of the doctor shortage, long emergency room wait times or his unfulfilled 2018 election promise to end "hallway health care" in hospitals.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "with the legislature returning after five long months, New Democrats will be taking on Doug Ford's costly failures and holding his government to account."
"We will be fighting for the things that help people build a good life - a home they can afford, a family doctor when the need one, a good public school, and a more affordable life," said Stiles.
"Ontarians are working harder than ever but they're feeling like they just can't get ahead," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ford is scrambling to distract from the serious issues facing Ontarians, including the fact that more than 2.3 million people don't have a family doctor.
"They're all gimmicks and they're all distractions from the real issues," said Crombie, pointing to the Tories' intervention on municipal issues like curbing bike lanes and "fantasy" projects like a proposed 60 km tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough.
"You hear the premier talking about bike lanes, you hear him talking about fictitious, nonsensical tunnels, but he's not talking about access to a family doctor. He's not talking about the nursing shortage in the hospitals," she said.
"He's not talking about the teacher shortage, or the fact that we don't have special ed teachers or the fact that people can't afford housing. He's not talking about any of those things. He's talking about distractions."
Asked whether she expects Ford to call an election next year because he is currently leading in public-opinion polls, Crombie said: "The RCMP investigation will play a large role in when this election is."
That's a reference to the Mounties' ongoing probe into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
The Star reported Oct. 9 that RCMP detectives have already interviewed at least eight current and former members of the premier's staff.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Ford is "clearly out of touch," as evidenced by his Highway 401 tunnel "pipe dream and fiscal nightmare" and spending as much as $1 billion to get beer and wine into convenience stores and big box outlets a year early.
"It appears he's in campaign mode and it also appears that he's blowing a lot of smoke to distract people from the real concerns," Schreiner added.
"When is the premier actually going to end 'hallway medicine' (in hospitals), which he promised in 2018 and now it's ballooned to 2,000 people a day?"
Ford ended the spring session a week earlier than scheduled on June 6, shuffled his cabinet in a major overhaul, moving 14 ministers and expanding the executive council to 36, the largest in Ontario history.
While the summer recess usually ends the Monday after Labour Day, he extended it by six weeks, sparking criticism from opponents.
They charged the Tories were eager to avoid scrutiny on numerous challenges facing the province, such as homeless encampments, overcrowded classrooms, the dearth of doctors and the push to put more medical treatment in private clinics.
In a bid to change the channel on that narrative, the first piece of legislation being introduced Monday is from Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria: the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act."
It will slam the brakes on new bike lanes, requiring Toronto and other municipalities to get provincial approval before taking out lanes of traffic for cars and trucks on main roads.
"While strategically placed bike lanes are a critical part of every city ... what cities should not be doing, however, is taking away lanes of traffic on our most-congested roads," Sarkaria told reporters last week, controversially blaming gridlock on bike lanes.
When Ford used Thursday's Empire Club speech to tout his minister for "bringing sanity back to bike lane decisions," hundreds in the audience spontaneously cheered.
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes. We need to - and will - remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill," the premier said to more applause.
But the Association of Municipalities of Ontario criticized him for "significant overreach" into their jurisdiction and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow has pushed back at the attack on civic powers, saying that tackling gridlock requires "all types of transportation."
"The province should concentrate on their job of finally getting the Eglinton Crosstown and Finch LRT open, which will have a huge impact on congestion," said Chow.
With no date set for the opening of the Eglinton Crosstown, Schreiner questioned whether Ford is waiting until after an early election to get the transit line running in case there are service snafus that hurt him at the polls.
"If the premier's playing politics with transit, then he needs to be held accountable," the Green leader said.
Provincial transit agency Metrolinx has promised three months' advance notice of an opening date for the Eglinton line, but has not given that yet - meaning it will not open until late January at the earliest.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford walks through the halls of the Ontario legislature after speaking to media, in Toronto on May 6, 2024.

Subject: Hospitals; Legislatures; Scandals; Roads & highways

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3118626257/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: $200 won't
buy my vote
Re: Doug Ford signals rebate cheques for all Ontarians will 'stimulate the economy,'
Oct. 17
So Doug, you have $3.2 billion in your back pocket to buy my vote? Well, $200 isn't going to cut it.
What I want is a government that uses that money to build affordable family housing not one-bedroom condos. I want a government that makes a contract for home health supplies with a company that can actually supply the products. I want a government that finds shelter for the homeless. I want a government that aims to educate and care for every child in the province. I want a government that protects the Greenbelt. I want a government that questions why transportation projects are taking so long, and whether we even need some of them. I want a transparent government - I don't want to have to look for and read the fine print in government contracts, like the spa at Ontario Place. I wish you well in retirement, because $200 is not going to buy my vote.
Jean Wise, Hamilton
Invest in health and education
Doug Ford plans to buy your vote with a $200 gift, which I don't deny many families need. My issue with this is there are people in this province who are doing just fine and don't need this handout to buy their vote.
Doug, give it to the needy and what remains can be used to fund our crumbling public health care (not to private clinics) and our schools struggling with providing proper teaching tools for our children.
Those two issues will garner you more votes then anything else.
David Intini, Stoney Creek
Do Americans want a dictator?
I'm hoping Americans really think passionately about who they are voting for. Do you want a dictatorship or democracy?
Look at dictatorship countries around the world, and ask yourself, "do I want to live there?"
I'm concerned for my country if Americans don't do the right thing for the rest of the world. I'm not kidding.
Jann Hanson, Hamilton
Term is about
being inclusive
Re: Why not just say 'women?' Oct. 17
I felt compelled to respond regarding the letter titled "Why not just say women?" I am going to err on the side of assuming that this person does not work in health care, particularly obstetrics/gynecology.
The professionals working in this specialty have, for some time now, used the term "pregnant people" as it is inclusive for all those who are pregnant.
There are a number of trans men and non-binary folk who become pregnant and don't identify as a "pregnant woman." I hope that my rather simplistic explanation is a polite way of enlightening this person regarding the changes in providing pregnancy care to all.
Kitty Stern, Burlington
Wishing Tassi
all the best
Re: Hamilton MP
Filomena Tassi will not run
for re-election, Oct. 17
We were saddened by the recent news of Filomena Tassi's announcement, but respect her decision.
Politics aside, as a former neighbour in Dundas, Filomena (and her family) always took time to say hello and chat during what must have been a very busy time while she was minister of labour.
A cheerful disposition and friendly smile accompanied her local walks, and a polite, gracious manner brought pride into our little neighbourhood.
My family and I wish her all the best, and heartfelt thanks for her unwavering representation.
Wray Brown, Dundas

Document 328 of 500

Recounts set for key ridings: Elections BC says official result will take another week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/recounts-set-key-ridings/docview/3118626191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Recounts+set+for+key+ridings%3A+Elections+BC+says+official+result+will+take+another+week&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: British Columbia's political landscape is far from settled following an election that has yet to produce a clear winner, with recounts set for two key ridings that could determine who forms the next government, and the result won't be known for at least another week.
Elections BC said Sunday in a statement - the day after the election - recounts will take place from Oct. 26 to 28 in the ridings of Juan de Fuca-Malahat and Surrey City Centre, where the NDP and Conservative candidates are separated by fewer than 100 votes.
The New Democrat candidates are leading by very thin margins in both ridings.
The Conservatives would have to win both to secure the narrowest of majorities, and if not, David Eby's incumbent NDP is poised to form a minority government provided it secures the co-operation of two Greens elected on Saturday.
The NDP is leading or elected in 46 ridings, while John Rustad's Conservatives are leading or elected in 45, but neither have reached the 47 seats required to form a majority government in B.C.'s 93-seat legislature after the initial count that wrapped up Sunday.
The initial count does not reflect about 49,000 absentee and mail-in ballots that will be included in the final count starting next Saturday, Elections BC said.
The recounts will be part of the final count taking place between Oct. 26 and 28, though judicial recounts may occur if the difference between the top two candidates in a riding is less than 1/500th of the total ballots.
An estimated 57.4 per cent of registered voters cast ballots in the election, up from below 54 per cent in 2020.
With nearly 2,037,900 ballots cast, the statement from Elections BC said it's the most ever votes tallied in a B.C. provincial election.
Regardless of the outcome, the election represented a stunning moment for the B.C. Conservatives, who received less than two per cent of the vote last election, but are now elected or leading in 45 ridings.
The NDP, who started the campaign last month with 55 seats, was elected or leading in 46, while the Green Party captured two seats.
Green Campaign Chair Adam Olsen said it was too early to speculate on the legislative makeup, who will form government or even the likelihood of a minority mandate for the eventual winner.
"The reality is that there is a handful of seats right now that seemingly could go either way," he said Sunday in an interview. "Right now nobody has a majority government. Sometime here at the end of October they are going to count the votes and we'll know for certain then what the outcome is. But it's pretty tight."
Olsen said he also would not speculate now on the possibility of a minority situation following this election and the shape of the legislature. "What ever the outcome at the end of this, when those writs are returned to the legislature, the goal here now for all 93 members of that place is to make it function," he said. "(Green Leader) Sonia Furstenau said multiple times throughout the campaign that neither of these other parties deserve a majority government and it looks like so far the people of B.C. agree with her."
Rustad told supporters Saturday the B.C. Conservatives will look to topple the NDP as soon as possible if the election results in a minority government. "If we are in that situation of the NDP forming a minority government, we will look at every single opportunity from day one to bring them down ... and get back to the polls," he said.
Eby said in a muted speech that a "clear majority" of voters supported "progressive values."
But he acknowledged that Rustad "spoke to the frustrations of a lot of British Columbians" when it came to costs of living and public safety.
"We've got to do better," Eby said. "That was our commitment to British Columbians. We've got to do better, and we will do better."
The canadian press
CAPTION: The Conservatives will have to win two key ridings to secure the narrowest of majorities, and if not, David Eby's incumbent NDP is poised to form a minority government provided it secures the co-operation of two Greens elected on Saturday.Conservative Leader John Rustad said Saturday his party will look to topple the NDP as soon as possible if the election results in a minority government.DARRYL DYCK The Canadian PressThe Canadian Press
