Document 329 of 500

Critics say Ford keener on electioneering

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/critics-say-ford-keener-on-electioneering/docview/3118626167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Critics+say+Ford+keener+on+electioneering&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's legislature will resume sitting Monday after an unusually long summer break, and returns in the midst of intense speculation that Premier Doug Ford will call an early election.
Some of the premier's remarks and announcements over the past few months - from the idea to dig a tunnel under Highway 401 to spending $225 million to put beer, wine and coolers in corner stores earlier than planned - are evidence that Ford is more focused on electioneering than governing, opposition critics say.
Politicians on both sides of the legislature are already thinking ahead to a possible early contest, with more than half a dozen members of provincial parliament already announcing they will not run in the next election, even though it is officially a little over two years away.
The next fixed election date isn't until June 2026. But Ford has left the door open to calling one next year, giving his own caucus members a December deadline to decide if they will run again.
So far, two backbenchers have bowed out, as has Speaker Ted Arnott, after 34 years at the legislature. Kaleed Rasheed, who serves as an independent after getting kicked out of the Progressive Conservative caucus, made a similar announcement. Three NDP representatives have indicated they will instead seek federal nominations.
On Monday, when the legislature sits for the first time in 19 weeks, the government is set to introduce a bill aimed at easing the congestion that is frustrating some Greater Toronto Area voters. Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria announced that the legislation would facilitate construction 24 hours a day, accelerate property acquisitions and an environmental assessment for Highway 413 and require municipalities to ask the province for permission to install bike lanes when they would remove a lane of vehicle traffic.
Fighting gridlock was one of the main legislative priorities Ford highlighted Thursday in a speech, saying he calls his transportation minister every time he's stuck in a traffic jam.
"I was driving home the other night, it was about 11:30, I look on the 401 and I don't know what the reason was, it was packed on both sides of the highway," he told the Empire Club of Canada crowd. "People just want to get home. They want to get to work."
Opposition leaders say Ford's 401 tunnel idea appears half-baked and designed to shore up votes among frustrated drivers rather than offering any real solutions.
"It appears that he's in campaign mode and it also appears that he's blowing a lot of smoke to distract people from the real concerns people have," said Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
The opposition parties hope to steer the legislative focus to what they see as more pressing concerns, such as the 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor and a housing supply shortage.

The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford

CREDIT: Allison Jones and Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 330 of 500

Ford government must rethink plan to force independent grocery stores to accept empty alcohol containers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-must-rethink-plan-force/docview/3118299945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+must+rethink+plan+to+force+independent+grocery+stores+to+accept+empty+alcohol+containers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Oct. 31 - Halloween - all grocery stores in Ontario will ostensibly have the right to sell beer, wine and cider products.
But the strings that come along with that right have left grocers in the province wondering if this was the Ontario government's idea of a trick-or-treat program.
Ontario's independent grocers, who represent about 59 per cent of the brick-and-mortar retail grocery stores in the province, initially applauded the announcement in 2023 that the government intended to open up the sale of alcohol products to all grocery stores. This was a welcome change from the system established by the previous Liberal government who, by limiting the number of licensed retailers who could sell beer and wine, created winners and losers on the retail grocery landscape. That should not be the role of government.
However, when ending that system, the current Conservative government is now forcing all grocery stores that opt to sell alcohol to also accept dirty bottles and cans into their stores, creating more winners and losers. It's tantamount to a bad hangover from the previous government's incongruous approach.
Where, exactly, does this government suggest deposit returns should be dropped off in a grocery store? By the meat, produce or bakery sections? Or perhaps by the front entrance so that the smell of stale beer wafting through the store can enhance the shopping experience for Ontario consumers. These dirty cans and bottles will also be coming into the store often broken and accompanied by cigarette butts, discarded needles and insects. As such, the risk of cross-contamination in a food environment, particularly in a smaller store, is more pronounced.
As well, where will an independent grocer operating on margins of two per cent - half that of a retail grocery chain - find the space to sort and store these empties? What products in the store do the bureaucrats in the Ontario government recommend be taken out to make room for these bottles and cans? How will that independent make up for the revenue loss from that forfeited space? How will that small business pay for the costs of storing and sorting these soiled cans and bottles with their margins further reduced? Has the government also looked at this within the context of the existing Blue Box program, which has seen costs more than triple in the last two years?
This past summer, the Ontario government rightly squashed the concept of grocers having to take back dirty, non-alcoholic containers. How then can the same government now foist that concept on grocers when it's a dirty, alcoholic container?
The alcohol changes announced in May contained a stark illustration that the Ontario government had not grasped that the challenges and realities for smaller, family-owned independent grocers are significantly different than those faced by monolithic retail chains. In revising provincial Alcohol and Gaming Commission licensing fees, the Ford government aligned the existing fees for the grocery chains and independents to the same level, reducing fees for the chains, but tripling them for the independents. Independent grocers welcomed Finance Minister Bethlenfalvy reversing that miscue, but it was disconcerting that Main Street and Bay Street grocers had been lumped together in the first place.
Last year, the Competition Bureau of Canada released its long-awaited study into the retail grocery industry. One of the key recommendations coming out of that exhaustive study, was that all governments - federal, provincial and territorial - need to do more to encourage the growth of Canada's independent grocers. How can the Ontario government reconcile its imposition of a costly and impractical deposit return system on its own independent grocers, with the findings and recommendations of that report?
Independent grocers have conveyed to the government our desire to work together to find and enhance solutions to recycling. Looking at community depots like those that exist in other jurisdictions, or returning empties to the LCBO - which does not sell fresh food - are but two options that come to mind.
The decision of the Ontario government to expand where Beer, Wine and Cider can be sold should be a win-win. A win for retailers, for Ontario craft breweries and wineries- and a win for consumers wanting more convenience.
But the low number of independent grocers seeking beer and wine licences - combined with current sellers indicating they will surrender their licences in 2026 when the exemption to operate in proximity to a Beer Store ends - attests to the fact that the province needs to adjust their alcohol modernization plan.
In a refreshing departure from the political norm, Premier Ford has said, to his credit, that when his government recognizes something may not be the right decision, changes are made. Such a change is needed now. It is hard to imagine that this is an issue the Ontario government intended to create.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford should rethink decision to force independent grocery stores to accept empty alcohol containers, writes Gary Sands.
CREDIT: Gary Sands Contributor

Subject: Retail stores; Alcohol; Liquor; Beer; Independent grocery stores; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Retail stores Independent grocery stores Breweries

Document 331 of 500

Living on ODSP should not mean living in poverty

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/living-on-odsp-should-not-mean-poverty/docview/3118299941/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Living+on+ODSP+should+not+mean+living+in+poverty&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Someone please tell Premier Doug Ford that Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP) was designed for folks who literally cannot work.
These are some of the hardest working folks I know. Many volunteer and advocate for social justice and human rights within their communities and all of them spend an inordinate amount of time visiting food banks and combing the city for deals on staple goods.
They accomplish all of this while being continuously policed by a provincial government that thinks they are being lazy.
Some ODSP recipients can work part-time which provides socialization while supplementing their unlivable income. ODSP recipients can earn up to $1,000 per month. Any income over that is clawed back at a rate of 75 per cent. Most work minimum wage jobs, so at $17.20 per hour they can work 58 hours per month or 14 hours per week before claw backs.
Gifts of money from family or friends for birthdays and special occasions must be declared and will be deducted from their income. That begs an important question: hey, Doug, how much was clawed back from your paycheque after your daughter got all of those "gifts" from developers at her "stag and doe?"
Living on ODSP should not mean living in poverty. In Ontario, the official poverty line for a single person in 2022 was $27,343 with the deep income poverty threshold set at $20,508.
I challenge Doug to live on ODSP payments for a year - that's $1,368 per month, or $16,416 per year - to cover rent and all living expenses. So, Doug are you up to living the life of Riley while sitting on your butt raking in the big bucks? Kinda what you do now, isn't it?
And, Doug, you could fill some of that free time creating a list of all of the affordable rental units you find across the province to share with folks looking for housing. Please be sure to include photos of each unit in case there's black mould, holes in the walls, broken appliances, no window or closet in the "bedroom," shower or bed right next to the kitchen.
You can even move to Hamilton where the average one-bedroom goes for over $1,800 a month (May 2024) since you removed rent caps.
I guess this would be a good time to mention that for folks trying to survive on ODSP, $582 of the $1,368 monthly payment is dedicated to their shelter allowance. Kinda makes getting first and last month's rent deposit a bit difficult, eh?
I know a few amazing filmmakers who would be willing to document your year of living on ODSP, Doug. We could promote it as a reality show and who knows, you could end up on "Jimmy Kimmel Live!"
And, in case you pivot to target your vitriol at folks receiving Ontario Works (OW), remember more than 60 per cent of OW recipients are single adults trying to stretch $733 per month - $8,796 annually - to cover all expenses. The shelter allowance for folks trying to survive on OW is $390 of the $733 monthly payment.
Many folks on OW should really be receiving ODSP, but Doug, you changed the rules so they are "living" on OW awaiting approval to move onto ODSP and it's taking way longer to process each applicant than your site states.
That could be the sequel - Doug Ford living the high life on OW.
All kidding aside, Doug Ford's ODSP and OW rates are social murder. Doug, it is time to raise the rates.
CAPTION: It's time for Premier Doug Ford's government to raise its social assistance rates, Doreen Nicoll writes.
CREDIT: Doreen Nicoll

Title: Living on ODSP should not mean living in poverty

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 19, 2024

Document 332 of 500

New Hamilton plant keeps medicine production close to home

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-hamilton-plant-keeps-medicine-production/docview/3118299928/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Hamilton+plant+keeps+medicine+production+close+to+home&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A $200-million cell and gene therapy manufacturing plant at McMaster Innovation Park in Hamilton is expected to eventually create 500 jobs by using cutting-edge technology to bring innovative medicines to market.
"It's great news for Ontario," said Stacey Johnson, a spokesperson for OmniaBio Inc., which held an Oct. 17 grand opening for its 120,000-square-foot plant that drew Premier Doug Ford and other senior provincial cabinet ministers.
"It means that Ontario and Canadian-based cell and gene therapy companies don't have to move to other jurisdictions like Boston, for example, to have their manufacturing done," she said.
"A company wants to be close to their manufacturing and now they can. If it's an Ontario company, they can stay in Ontario. If it's a Vancouver company, we can do the work for them, too."
The Longwood Road South plant presently has 150 employees and will provide development and manufacturing on contract to companies that are near or ready to take medicines to market, Johnson said.
The "state of the art" facility will use robotics, biosensors and artificial intelligence to optimize production, quality and cost-efficiency, ultimately making medicines cheaper and more available to patients, she said.
OmniaBio will help its first commercial-stage customer, Medipost, make a stem-cell medicine for the North American market to treat knee cartilage defects in people with osteoarthritis.
The Hamilton plant is smaller than the $580-million facility announced two years ago, but could expand, as OmniaBio "is waiting to see what market demand is" before potentially adding a second building, Johnson said.
The Ford government contributed $40 million to the project through the province's Invest Ontario agency. Beyond creating good-paying jobs, the plant will "cement our province's role as a world leader in life sciences," Ford said in a media release.
CAPTION: OmniaBio Inc.'s $200-million cell and gene therapy manufacturing plant at McMaster Innovation Park in Hamilton is expected to eventually create 500 jobs by using cutting-edge technology to bring innovative medicines to market.

Subject: Innovations; Gene therapy; Manufacturing

Business indexing term: Subject: Manufacturing

Document 333 of 500

Oct. 19: Trustee trip reeks of abuse of power and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-19-trustee-trip-reeks-abuse-power-other/docview/3118299845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+19%3A+Trustee+trip+reeks+of+abuse+of+power+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Taxpayers deserve answers for trustees' trip
Re: Brantford Catholic board spends $145K on trustee trip to Italy to buy art for schools - weeks after loosening travel expense rules, Oct. 16
Gobsmacked is my reaction when reading about the $145,000 spent by the Brantford Catholic school board to send four trustees to Italy to buy art for a school. The fact that $45,000 of the total amount spent, or roughly $11,000 for each trustee, was for travel expenses is incredulous! The taxpayers of Brantford who direct their tax money to the Catholic board deserve answers, accountability and consequences for all of the individuals involved. Maybe next time the trustees could cancel the art junket to Italy and vet prospective local artists.
Mary Elizabeth Toth, Ancaster
This reeks of abuse of power
I don't expect the Brantford Catholic board of education to fill the halls of academia with pictures of cigar smoking dogs playing poker, but the spending of $145,000 by four trustees on a trip to Italy to buy art should be thoroughly investigated.
And if there is no good explanation but a free vacation on the taxpayers dime, they should be immediately terminated. As a baby boomer I can remember the halls of school proudly filled with the works of art students. There are numerous charity organizations like the Salvation Army, St. Vincent De Paul or even Value Village that have beautiful pictures and prints that have been donated and can be bought at a fraction of their cost. If it is original works of art they must have, there are numerous local artists, including Indigenous people who are creating beautiful paintings, photography and sculptures who could benefit from the exposure of their creations of art.
Most local artists are indeed suffering monetarily and buying local would be would help them out tremendously. At a time when countless people are homeless, living on the streets and the average person has to go to food banks to survive, this reeks of abuse of power and pure greed and must not be tolerated.
Ellie Chase, Hamilton
No need to travel to Italy for art
Seriously? Four school board trustees have to fly to Italy to source artwork? Do we not have Ontario, let alone Canadian, artists who could be vetted for sculptures and statuary for this new Catholic secondary school in the Brantford area? What about asking for submissions from fine art departments from all over this country?
Linda Ploen, Burlington
Remove trustees from office
Their blatant abuse of taxpayer money is almost laughable. I may have missed it, but isn't the fundamental question are there not competent artists in Canada who could supply the "required" art? Of course they should all be removed from office as a warning to other public officials. But this is Canada and we are too nice to actually make those easy decisions - or perhaps do not have the requisite laws in place to do so.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Board needs to re-evaluate its spending
How dare these employees do full expenses on a trip that was totally unnecessary. Schools are short of supplies, we have kids going to school with no breakfast and a shortage of school buses. It's time, obviously, that the school board looks at its expenses and who spends them and what is necessary. If they cannot order online like everyone else, they certainly don't need it. It does not warrant a week's trip. This is as bad as our mayor going to Europe to check out recycling. Enough already.
Debbie Escott, Hamilton
Trustee trip news unbelievable
While the majority of Canadians would love a trip to Italy, flying business class and staying in posh hotels, the reality for most of us is budgeting for the monthly bills and putting food on the table. The trustees at the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board (BHNCDSB) that spent $145,000 of public money to fly to Italy and buy religious artwork for a new Catholic high school must be brought up on the carpet.
While the BHNCDSB indicated it has a comfortable surplus in these challenging economic times, it does not give it the right to use public money for such an extravagant and needless trip. That public money could have been utilized in many of the following ways. Non-air-conditioned schools within the board could have A/C units supplied to them. More educational assistants could have been hired to help at-risk students. More funds could have been spent on mental health supports for students in need. If religious artwork was really needed, perhaps a local artist could have been hired.
A school board's ultimate responsibility is to the students, not to decorate a new building with lavish artwork. I doubt if students entering the new St. Padre Pio high school in the near future will give these expensive statues a second look or care. Clearly Chair Rick Petrella has only one option; step down and apologize.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
More mismanagement from Ford
Re: Hamilton nurses describe dire situation amid Ontario-wide medical supply shortage, Oct. 17
After reading this article my thought was, "how does Doug Ford sleep at night"? It's unconscionable that our premier wants to spend billions of dollars on a highway going nowhere; invests in a rail system that serves the few and a tunnel under an existing highway. These are just a few examples of mismanagement. If that earmarked money was directed at our failing health-care system, then maybe Doug might have a slim chance of winning any upcoming election. I certainly hope not!
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Research project very important
Re: Why are an increasing number of pregnant people getting sick or dying from heart disease? Oct. 9
The death of women or those who become seriously ill during pregnancy is one of the most devastating experiences that can happen in a family. Kudos to Dr. Rohan D'Souza and the dedicated team at McMaster's Obstetrics and Gynecology Department for leading a research project that will not only be looking into why more pregnant women are dying or getting sick from cardiovascular disease, but will also be providing them and their babies with specialized care and developing preventative strategies.
Last year, Canadian Federation of University Women National, along with the Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists, called on the government to develop a comprehensive program to track and prevent maternal deaths across this country. This project is an important step in making this happen.
Cathy McPherson, president, CFUW Hamilton
'Pregnant people' deserve respect
Re: Why not just say 'women?' Oct. 17
Someone wrote the letter named above (regarding the use of the term "pregnant people") and I'm game to throw a pair of propositions to her and others, plus gently point out something.
A. It is possible for a small number of men to get pregnant due to the fact they are - as two per cent of humankind is - intersex. Have a read through "Labour of Love," available through our city's library if you want, to improve your understanding to that effect.
B. Plenty of people who are women according to their anatomy (sex) have non-binary, gender-queer or masculine gender identities.
It should go without saying women should be respected and known for who they are. Sadly, that simple fact fails to be honoured every day, even among people identifying as women. Pregnant people should receive proper recognition in addition, whether they simply feel they are people or, like me and the writer of the original editorial, expect recognition of their womanhood to be visible and/or audible, however uncomfortable others might be left feeling.
God knows I've freaked many people out when gently asking where the men are when I'm mistakenly identified as a guy. May any and every pregnant human being on earth - and every woman, no matter her or their pregnancy status - receive proper recognition as promptly as possible.
Amy Soule, Hamilton
Move police to student crossings
They are looking for a crossing guard to man Governors Road at Huntingwood. There are three schools on Governors Road.
I would like to see police at the crosswalks with cruiser light flashing, but not sounding, rather than see officers for months on end at the bridge construction on Governors Road. Why can't city vehicles that do road work or contractors for that matter have a flashing light instead of police presence? What a waste of resources!
I'd rather see police helping kids safely get to and from school than to watch as a police car sits idle all day long at a construction site.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Author's conclusion is flawed
Re: Bloated school boards can't cry poverty while blowing money on lavish weekends, Oct. 16
After spending most of this piece critiquing the Thames Valley District School Board's priorities in spending, the author finally arrives at the point of the piece: He'd defund public education by transferring tax dollars to private schools so that parents can choose to place their kids in situations where they'll never encounter anyone who isn't like them.
One of the roles of public education is give children the opportunity to learn, first hand, of the differences among other members of their community. Kids siloed into private schools are denied that opportunity.
David Fawcett, Hamilton
Are bikes really the problem?
Re: Ontario to give itself veto power over city bike lanes, citing worsening gridlock, Oct. 15
Why is the province so concerned about bike lanes causing gridlock when it is going to force the closing of the two busiest east/west traffic lanes in the lower city to build a rail track? Considering the limited accesses across the Red Hill Valley, I can't wait for that gridlock to happen.
Ron French, Hamilton
Poilievre needs to reveal his plans
Re: Voters want more than talking points and rhetoric, Oct. 15
I agree with writer Craig Wallace. I am getting annoyed at the television commercials by Pierre Poilievre and his "Common Sense Plan." At no time has he ever said just what this plan entails. In fact, if you ask a Conservative they can't tell you either. It's time he told the Canadian voters the details of his plan, including all the "cuts" he is going to make in order to fulfil his promise to "axe the tax, reduce crime, make housing more affordable etc."
Will he cut the $10-a-day daycare or the new dental plan and privatize more health care?
To be honest, he frightens me and he should frighten all voters. I agree the choices are limited on the federal scene, but perhaps it's better to deal with devil you know than the devil you don't know.
Patricia Muir, Dundas
Disability is not a Halloween costume
In recent years, we have seen a greater social awareness as Canadians toward diversity and inclusion. Why is it then that we continue to allow the sale of Halloween costumes that mock people with disabilities and reduce them to a punchline?
One notable example is the "blind referee" costume, which includes dark sunglasses and a fake white cane. It is crucial to consider the broader social impact and the message that certain costumes can send, particularly when they perpetuate harmful stereotypes or stigmas. The "blind referee" costume trivializes and mocks the experiences of those who are blind or have low vision by reducing it to a joke. As a result, this reinforces damaging misconceptions and further marginalizes a group of individuals already facing significant challenges in society.
Just as words can be harmful, imagery has the same impact. There are many creative and fun costume options that don't involve mocking marginalized groups. We urge retailers to cease the sale of any costumes characterizing disability and ask that Canadians do not buy costumes that make fun of people with disabilities. It's 2024, and we are better than that.
Keya Osborne, disability justice director, CNIB
CAPTION: Signage on the north side of Powerline Road in Brantford announces the coming St. Padre Pio Catholic Secondary School.

Subject: Womens health; Students; Artists; Travel; Art; Governors; Budgets; Trustees; School boards; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Trustees

Location: Italy; Canada

Living on ODSP shouldn't mean living in poverty

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/living-on-odsp-shouldnt-mean-poverty/docview/3118182444/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Living+on+ODSP+shouldn%27t+mean+living+in+poverty&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Someone please tell Premier Doug Ford that Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP) was designed for folks who literally cannot work.
These are some of the hardest working folks I know. Many volunteer and advocate for social justice and human rights within their communities and all of them spend an inordinate amount of time visiting food banks and combing the city for deals on staple goods.
They accomplish all of this while being continuously policed by a provincial government that thinks they are being lazy.
Some ODSP recipients can work part-time which provides socialization while supplementing their unlivable income. ODSP recipients can earn up to $1,000 per month. Any income over that is clawed back at a rate of 75 per cent. Most work minimum wage jobs, so at $17.20 per hour they can work 58 hours per month or 14 hours per week before claw backs.
Gifts of money from family or friends for birthdays and special occasions must be declared and will be deducted from their income. That begs an important question: hey, Doug, how much was clawed back from your paycheque after your daughter got all of those "gifts" from developers at her "stag and doe?"
Living on ODSP should not mean living in poverty. In Ontario, the official poverty line for a single person in 2022 was $27,343 with the deep income poverty threshold set at $20,508.
I challenge Doug to live on ODSP payments for a year - that's $1,368 per month, or $16,416 per year - to cover rent and all living expenses. So, Doug are you up to living the life of Riley while sitting on your butt raking in the big bucks? Kinda what you do now, isn't it?
And, Doug, you could fill some of that free time creating a list of all of the affordable rental units you find across the province to share with folks looking for housing.
Please be sure to include photos of each unit in case there's black mould, holes in the walls, broken appliances, no window or closet in the "bedroom," shower or bed right next to the kitchen.
You can even move to Hamilton where the average one-bedroom goes for over $1,800 a month (May 2024) since you removed rent caps.
I guess this would be a good time to mention that for folks trying to survive on ODSP, $582 of the $1,368 monthly payment is dedicated to their shelter allowance.
Kinda makes getting first and last month's rent deposit a bit difficult, eh?
I know a few amazing filmmakers who would be willing to document your year of living on ODSP, Doug. We could promote it as a reality show and who knows, you could end up on "Jimmy Kimmel Live!"
And, in case you pivot to target your vitriol at folks receiving Ontario Works (OW), remember more than 60 per cent of OW recipients are single adults trying to stretch $733 per month - $8,796 annually - to cover all expenses. The shelter allowance for folks trying to survive on OW is $390 of the $733 monthly payment.
Many folks on OW should really be receiving ODSP, but Doug, you changed the rules so they are "living" on OW awaiting approval to move onto ODSP and it's taking way longer to process each applicant than your site states.
That could be the sequel - Doug Ford living the high life on OW.
All kidding aside, Doug Ford's ODSP and OW rates are social murder. Doug, it is time to raise the rates.
Doreen Nicoll is a
journalist and podcaster. Find her work at
doreenn.substack.com.

Document 335 of 500

New plant keeps medicine production close to home: $200M, 120,000-square-foot facility at McMaster Innovation Park expected to create 500 jobs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-plant-keeps-medicine-production-close-home/docview/3118182441/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+plant+keeps+medicine+production+close+to+home%3A+%24200M%2C+120%2C000-square-foot+facility+at+McMaster+Innovation+Park+expected+to+create+500+jobs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A $200-million cell and gene therapy manufacturing plant at McMaster Innovation Park in Hamilton is expected to eventually create 500 jobs by using cutting-edge technology to bring innovative medicines to market.
"It's great news for Ontario," said Stacey Johnson, a spokesperson for OmniaBio Inc., which held a grand opening on Thursday for its 120,000-square-foot plant that drew Premier Doug Ford and other senior provincial cabinet ministers.
"It means that Ontario and Canadian-based cell and gene therapy companies don't have to move to other jurisdictions like Boston, for example, to have their manufacturing done," she said.
"A company wants to be close to their manufacturing and now they can. If it's an Ontario company, they can stay in Ontario. If it's a Vancouver company, we can do the work for them, too."
The Longwood Road South plant presently has 150 employees and will provide development and manufacturing on contract to companies that are near or ready to take medicines to market, Johnson said.
OmniaBio will help its first commercial-stage customer, Medipost, make a stem-cell medicine for the North American market to treat knee cartilage defects in people with osteoarthritis.
The Hamilton plant is smaller than the $580-million facility announced two years ago, but could expand, as OmniaBio "is waiting to see what market demand is" before potentially adding a second building, Johnson said.
The Ford government contributed $40 million to the project through the province's Invest Ontario agency.
CAPTION:

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3118182219/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Taxpayers
deserve answers
Re: Brantford Catholic board spends $145K on trustee trip to Italy to buy art for schools - weeks after loosening travel expense rules, Oct. 16
Gobsmacked is my reaction when reading about the $145,000 spent by the Brantford Catholic school board to send four trustees to Italy to buy art for a school. The fact that $45,000 of the total amount spent, or roughly $11,000 for each trustee, was for travel expenses is incredulous! The taxpayers of Brantford who direct their tax money to the Catholic board deserve answers, accountability and consequences for all of the individuals involved. Maybe next time the trustees could cancel the art junket to Italy and vet prospective local artists.
Mary Elizabeth Toth,
Ancaster
No need to travel to Italy for art
Seriously? Four school board trustees have to fly to Italy to source artwork? Do we not have Ontario, let alone Canadian, artists who could be vetted for sculptures and statuary for this new Catholic secondary school in the Brantford area? What about asking for submissions from fine art departments from all over this country?
Linda Ploen, Burlington
Remove trustees from office
Their blatant abuse of taxpayer money is almost laughable. I may have missed it, but isn't the fundamental question are there not competent artists in Canada who could supply the "required" art? Of course they should all be removed from office as a warning to other public officials. But this is Canada and we are too nice to actually make those easy decisions - or perhaps do not have the requisite laws in place to do so.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Re-evaluate
board spending
How dare these employees do full expenses on a trip that was totally unnecessary. Schools are short of supplies, we have kids going to school with no breakfast and a shortage of school buses. It's time, obviously, that the school board looks at its expenses and who spends them and what is necessary. If they cannot order online like everyone else, they certainly don't need it. It does not warrant a week's trip. This is as bad as our mayor going to Europe to check out recycling. Enough already.
Debbie Escott, Hamilton
This reeks of abuse of power
I don't expect the Brantford Catholic board of education to fill the halls of academia with pictures of cigar smoking dogs playing poker, but the spending of $145,000 by four trustees on a trip to Italy to buy art should be thoroughly investigated.
And if there is no good explanation but a free vacation on the taxpayers dime, they should be immediately terminated. As a baby boomer I can remember the halls of school proudly filled with the works of art students. There are numerous charity organizations like the Salvation Army, St. Vincent De Paul or even Value Village that have beautiful pictures and prints that have been donated and can be bought at a fraction of their cost. If it is original works of art they must have, there are numerous local artists, including Indigenous people who are creating beautiful paintings, photography and sculptures who could benefit from the exposure of their creations of art.
Most local artists are indeed suffering monetarily and buying local would be would help them out tremendously. At a time when countless people are homeless, living on the streets and the average person has to go to food banks to survive, this reeks of abuse of power and pure greed and must not be tolerated.
Ellie Chase, Hamilton
Funds needed elsewhere
While the majority of Canadians would love a trip to Italy, flying business class and staying in posh hotels, the reality for most of us is budgeting for the monthly bills and putting food on the table.
The trustees at the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board (BHNCDSB) that spent $145,000 of public money to fly to Italy and buy religious artwork for a new Catholic high school must be brought up on the carpet.
While the BHNCDSB indicated it has a comfortable surplus in these challenging economic times, it does not give it the right to use public money for such an extravagant and needless trip. That public money could have been utilized in many of the following ways. Non-air-conditioned schools within the board could have A/C units supplied to them.
More educational assistants could have been hired to help at-risk students. More funds could have been spent on mental health supports for students in need. If religious artwork was really needed, perhaps a local artist could have been hired.
A school board's ultimate responsibility is to the students, not to decorate a new building with lavish artwork. I doubt if students entering the new St. Padre Pio high school in the near future will give these expensive statues a second look or care. Clearly Chair Rick Petrella has only one option; step down and apologize.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
Mismanagement again from Ford
Re: Hamilton nurses describe dire situation amid Ontario-wide medical supply shortage, Oct. 17
After reading this article my thought was, "how does Doug Ford sleep at night"? It's unconscionable that our premier wants to spend billions of dollars on a highway going nowhere; invests in a rail system that serves the few and a tunnel under an existing highway. These are just a few examples of mismanagement. If that earmarked money was directed at our failing health-care system, then maybe Doug might have a slim chance of winning any upcoming election. I certainly hope not!
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Research project very important
Re: Why are an increasing number of pregnant people getting sick or dying from heart disease? Oct. 9
The death of women or those who become seriously ill during pregnancy is one of the most devastating experiences that can happen in a family. Kudos to Dr. Rohan D'Souza and the dedicated team at McMaster's Obstetrics and Gynecology Department for leading a research project that will not only be looking into why more pregnant women are dying or getting sick from cardiovascular disease, but will also be providing them and their babies with specialized care and developing preventative strategies.
Last year, Canadian Federation of University Women National, along with the Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists, called on the government to develop a comprehensive program to track and prevent maternal deaths across this country. This project is an important step in making this happen.
Cathy McPherson, president, CFUW Hamilton
'Pregnant people' deserve respect
Re: Why not just say 'women?' Oct. 17
Someone wrote the letter named above (regarding the use of the term "pregnant people") and I'm game to throw a pair of propositions to her and others, plus gently point out something.
A. It is possible for a small number of men to get pregnant due to the fact they are - as two per cent of humankind is - intersex. Have a read through "Labour of Love," available through our city's library if you want, to improve your understanding to that effect.
B. Plenty of people who are women according to their anatomy (sex) have non-binary, gender-queer or masculine gender identities.
It should go without saying women should be respected and known for who they are. Sadly, that simple fact fails to be honoured every day, even among people identifying as women. Pregnant people should receive proper recognition in addition, whether they simply feel they are people or, like me and the writer of the original editorial, expect recognition of their womanhood to be visible and/or audible, however uncomfortable others might be left feeling.
God knows I've freaked many people out when gently asking where the men are when I'm mistakenly identified as a guy. May any and every pregnant human being on earth - and every woman, no matter her or their pregnancy status - receive proper recognition as promptly as possible.
Amy Soule, Hamilton
Move police to student crossings
They are looking for a crossing guard to man Governors Road at Huntingwood. There are three schools on Governors Road.
I would like to see police at the crosswalks with cruiser light flashing, but not sounding, rather than see officers for months on end at the bridge construction on Governors Road. Why can't city vehicles that do road work or contractors for that matter have a flashing light instead of police presence? What a waste of resources!
I'd rather see police helping kids safely get to and from school than to watch as a police car sits idle all day long at a construction site.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Conclusion of
column is flawed
Re: Bloated school boards can't cry poverty while blowing money on lavish weekends, Oct. 16
After spending most of this piece critiquing the Thames Valley District School Board's priorities in spending, the author finally arrives at the point of the piece: He'd defund public education by transferring tax dollars to private schools so that parents can choose to place their kids in situations where they'll never encounter anyone who isn't like them.
One of the roles of public education is give children the opportunity to learn, first hand, of the differences among other members of their community. Kids siloed into private schools are denied that opportunity.
David Fawcett, Hamilton
Poilievre needs
to reveal his plans
Re: Voters want more than talking points and rhetoric, Oct. 15
I agree with writer Craig Wallace. I am getting annoyed at the television commercials by Pierre Poilievre and his "Common Sense Plan." At no time has he ever said just what this plan entails. In fact, if you ask a Conservative they can't tell you either. It's time he told the Canadian voters the details of his plan, including all the "cuts" he is going to make in order to fulfil his promise to "axe the tax, reduce crime, make housing more affordable etc."
Will he cut the $10-a-day daycare or the new dental plan and privatize more health care?
To be honest, he frightens me and he should frighten all voters. I agree the choices are limited on the federal scene, but perhaps it's better to deal with devil you know than the devil you don't know.
Patricia Muir, Dundas
Are bikes really the problem?
Re: Ontario to give itself veto power over city bike lanes, citing worsening gridlock, Oct. 15
Why is the province so concerned about bike lanes causing gridlock when it is going to force the closing of the two busiest east/west traffic lanes in the lower city to build a rail track? Considering the limited accesses across the Red Hill Valley, I can't wait for that gridlock to happen.
Ron French, Hamilton
Disability is
not a costume
In recent years, we have seen a greater social awareness as Canadians toward diversity and inclusion. Why is it then that we continue to allow the sale of Halloween costumes that mock people with disabilities and reduce them to a punchline?
One notable example is the "blind referee" costume, which includes dark sunglasses and a fake white cane. It is crucial to consider the broader social impact and the message that certain costumes can send, particularly when they perpetuate harmful stereotypes or stigmas. The "blind referee" costume trivializes and mocks the experiences of those who are blind or have low vision by reducing it to a joke. As a result, this reinforces damaging misconceptions and further marginalizes a group of individuals already facing significant challenges in society.
Just as words can be harmful, imagery has the same impact. There are many creative and fun costume options that don't involve mocking marginalized groups. We urge retailers to cease the sale of any costumes characterizing disability and ask that Canadians do not buy costumes that make fun of people with disabilities. It's 2024, and we are better than that.
Keya Osborne, disability
justice director, CNIB
CAPTION: Signage on the north side of Powerline Road in Brantford announces the coming St. Padre Pio Catholic Secondary School. News of trustees travelling to Italy to purchase art for the school has raised the ire of Spectator readers.Celeste Percy-Beauregard The Hamilton Spectator

Document 337 of 500

Doug Ford wants to buy your silence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-wants-buy-your-silence/docview/3118182065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+buy+your+silence&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some politicians try to buy votes. Doug Ford wants to buy your silence. And acquiescence.
For a roughly $200 payout, to be handed out to everyone in Ontario, the premier hopes voters will forgive his political sins and forget his legal transgressions.
The planned cash bonanza, to be distributed early next year, is a calculated gamble.
The goal is to clear the way for an early election before any bad news - legal and political - erodes the support Ford's Tories now enjoy in public opinion polls.
As my Toronto Star colleague Robert Benzie reported this week, the premier's sudden generosity will be made public later this month. In its annual fall economic statement, the government is dropping any pretence of fiscal probity as it invests heavily and recklessly in its own political survival.
Ford's PCs want an early election, no matter the cost. Never mind the unnecessary $155-million election expense - that's the least of it.
If they settle on the $200 figure for every adult and child in Ontario, that works out to as much as $1,000 for a family of five - and perhaps $3.2 billion out of the treasury in total. That money is badly needed to shore up our schools, our hospitals and our homeless, but the premier believes he needs it more desperately to soften up voters.
There are twin legal challenges facing Ford.
The first is deadly serious: potential violations of Ontario's criminal laws now being probed by a high-stakes RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal. The headlines have faded, but few have forgotten the $8.28-billion imbroglio over protected land made available to private developers on the premier's watch (until he reversed course under pressure). That police probe could be released sometime next year.
Rather than wait for the police to rain on their re-election parade - scheduled for June 2026 under Ontario's fixed election law - the plan is to move the campaign up by more than a year to early 2025.
Fortuitously for Ford, that second potential legal peril is more symbolic than real. While the law mandates that any majority government should serve a full four-year term in power before triggering another, there is no legal penalty for flouting it - only a political price if the press and the people cry foul. Hence the payout. The premier is banking on voters being more interested in money than morality.
It's an elegant, if expensive, election plan: a kickback for voters, gifted by a government accused of kickbacks from developers (despite those opposition allegations in the legislature, no criminality has been proven and the police aren't talking).
Why aim for a potential $200 giveaway?
In the past, as a Toronto councillor, Ford famously doled out a wad of $20 bills to impoverished voters at a Toronto Community Housing building in 2013. He later apologized for the spectacle. And subsequently opted for juicier giveaways that, historically, have hit a sweet spot with voters.
Ahead of the 2022 election, Ford's Tories cut cheques to rebate motorists for licence plate fees that the government cancelled, at a cost of more than $1 billion to the treasury. During the COVID-19 pandemic, parents received as much as $250 per child. And the previous PC government of Mike Harris issued $200 "dividend" cheques.
How to justify such shameless pre-election (early election) vote-buying?
The unspoken reason is to satisfy the premier's lust for power. The official rationale is to support people's purchasing power.
The premier points to post-pandemic inflation raising the price of food and fuel, but it's not so simple. In fact, the inflation rate plunged to a remarkably low 1.6 per cent last month, thanks mostly to falling fuel prices, according to the latest Statistics Canada data.
And then there's the federal carbon levy, one of Ford's favourite targets. It is scheduled to increase again next spring, raising the price of gasoline. Ford has fulminated over gasoline prices for years - even though the amount taken in by the federal government is fully rebated to the average family. Before the 2022 election, he used the federal carbon levy as a pretext for the province to reduce its own fuel tax in another vote-buying gambit.
In a preview of his campaign rhetoric, Ford complained Thursday about the "impact of inflation and high interest rates, alongside the growing cost of the federal carbon tax, which in my opinion is the worst tax you could ever put on the backs of people."
There you have it - an evil levy imposed by Ottawa (albeit rebated), versus a helpful handout from the province, pre-election. Ford has it all figured out.
But does he have voters figured out?
Martin Regg Cohn covers
provincial politics for Torstar.
CAPTION: The unspoken reason Premier Doug Ford wants to send cheques to Ontarians is to satisfy his lust for power. The official rationaleis to support people's purchasing power, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Chris Young THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

'There is a better use of funds': People are reacting online after learning the Ontario government may offer $200 rebate cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-is-better-use-funds-people-are-reacting/docview/3118364371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27There+is+a+better+use+of+funds%27%3A+People+are+reacting+online+after+learning+the+Ontario+government+may+offer+%24200+rebate+cheques&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Ontario government prepares for Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement on Oct. 30, there's word that a rebate is in the plan.
On X on Oct. 16, Bethlenfalvy shared he would share his fall statement at the end of the month.
"Through our Plan to Build, we're working to rebuild Ontario's economy, fight gridlock, build infrastructure and keep costs down for families, workers and businesses," he said.
According to the Toronto Star, Premier Doug Ford is poised to send cheques to 16 million Ontarians to offset rising costs. The final amount is unknown, but could potentially be $200 for every adult and child in the province.
If so, it would cost the treasury at least $3.2 billion, the Star reported.
The payments would be tax-free. It could mean an additional $800 in household cash for a family of four, with the cheques coming in January or February.
A spokesperson for the minister's office told Metroland Media they would not be commenting on the cheques right now.
"This is another one-time gimmick from a Premier who has promised and failed to cut taxes for more than six years," Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie said. "Rather than try to buy your vote in an election year, Ontario's Liberals will cut taxes for all middle class families - permanently."
NDP leader Marit Stiles did not respond to a request for a comment.
Other people are reacting online to the news of rebate cheques.
"That's like ... 10 per cent of one month's rent," snoosh00 said on a Reddit post. "I feel like there is a better use of funds, even if it was literally just paying off provincial debt."
"It would be one thing if he was using his own money to do this, but public money? I'd rather it be kept and used to help health care and education instead," caffeine-junkie said on Reddit. "This kind of behaviour makes me sick as all he's trying to do is buy votes with our own money."
"Always glad to get money back from the government, who isn't," George wrote on thestar.com. "If everyone who could spare $50 dollars of that $200 cheque donated it to the Nature Conservancy, or the Greenbelt Foundation, we could protect more of the beautiful southern Ontario countryside, perhaps buy land along the Humber River for example, before this government allows it to be paved and driven over. Then there truly might be a place to stand and a place to grow."
MPPs return to the legislature in Toronto again on Oct. 21, as the house meets for the fall session.
On the legislature's website, an agenda is not yet available for Oct. 30.
- With files from Torstar News Service
Disclaimer: Poll results are not scientific. As the informal findings of a survey presented to the readers of this site, they reflect the opinions of those who have chosen to participate. The survey is available online to anyone interested in taking it. This poll does not restrict the number of votes each person can cast.

Document 339 of 500

Hamilton MP Filomena Tassi will not run for re-election: Personal reasons cited in 'bittersweet decision' to step away from office

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hamilton-mp-filomena-tassi-will-not-run-re/docview/3117870016/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+MP+Filomena+Tassi+will+not+run+for+re-election%3A+Personal+reasons+cited+in+%27bittersweet+decision%27+to+step+away+from+office&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas MP Filomena Tassi has announced that she will not run in the next federal election.
Tassi made the announcement on social media Thursday, explaining that her decision to not run again is "deeply personal," and it's time for her "to be closer to home" after representing the riding at Parliament Hill for the last nine years.
"This is a bittersweet decision," Tassi wrote in a statement shared on X. "I have truly, deeply loved serving the people of my riding."
Speaking to The Spectator, Tassi described Thursday as an "emotional day," noting that she had been "monitoring (her) home situation for the past while."
As previously reported by The Spectator, Tassi had shared in September 2022 that her husband suffered two strokes in early 2021 and that her family had been involved in his recovery.
At that time, Tassi had asked Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for a change of job, which saw her move from minister of public services and procurement to her current role as the minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario.
"I was really grateful for that," Tassi said in an interview. "But I know now that this is the right decision."
Tassi was elected in October 2015, an election that saw the Liberal party, led by Trudeau, form a majority government. She was re-elected in both the 2019 and 2021 federal elections.
Since then, Tassi had also served as the minister of seniors, the minister of labour, deputy whip and Receiver General for Canada.
Tassi told The Spectator that a highlight of her time as a politician was the amount of funding she was able to help bring to the city, pointing to the more than $5 billion the Liberal government has committed to projects in the city since 2015.
Another highlight was the creation of the "Team Hamilton" strategy, which Tassi helped to co-found alongside other Hamilton MPs from every corner of the house - including Chad Collins, Lisa Hepfner, Matthew Green and Dan Muys.
Tassi said that initiative saw the MPs meet as often as possible - sometimes with community stakeholders - in order to better advocate for the needs of the city. She noted that with the five of them all on the same page, more change was possible.
"We may have disagreements ... but we believe that coming together and working together puts us in a better place and helps us serve our constituents better," said Tassi. "It demonstrates that positive politics is possible."
Tassi also encouraged others to step forward into a career in politics, putting an emphasis on the need for more women and young people to come forward, while recognizing that the current political landscape is "very challenging."
"I have really had the experience of a lifetime," she said. "It's a tremendous opportunity, now it's time to pass the torch."
While Tassi's decision not to run for re-election comes at a time of looming turbulence for the federal Liberal party, she expressed her support for Trudeau in her statement.
"I wish him the very best now and in the future. I believed in him in 2015 and I believe in him now," she wrote.
The Canadian Press has reported that three other cabinet ministers will not seek re-election and in the backbenches, there has been an effort to push Trudeau to resign as leader of the party, as reported by several media outlets.
Fallon Hewitt is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. fhewitt@torstar.ca
CAPTION: MP Filomena Tassi, who represents the riding of Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas, was elected in October 2015.The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Fallon Hewitt Reporter

Bike lane rules a 'significant overreach': Municipalities say Ford should focus on new housing and leave bike decisions to local politicians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bike-lane-rules-significant-overreach/docview/3117867971/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bike+lane+rules+a+%27significant+overreach%27%3A+Municipalities+say+Ford+should+focus+on+new+housing+and+leave+bike+decisions+to+local+politicians&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Upcoming legislation that would require cities to get provincial approval for some bike lanes is a "significant overreach" of power, Ontario municipalities say, while Premier Doug Ford vowed Thursday to go even further and remove existing lanes.
None of the 444 members of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario were consulted or shown evidence the province is using to justify its proposed veto power over new bike lanes that would remove a lane for cars, the association wrote in a statement.
"Bicycle lanes are an essential element of urban transportation planning and road safety," the association said.
"Requiring provincial approval would be a significant overreach into municipal jurisdiction."
Ford's government is set to table the bike lane legislation next week as part of a suite of measures designed to tackle gridlock. Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said earlier this week the legislation is meant for future bike lanes.
On Thursday, Ford contradicted his minister.
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes," Ford said in a speech at the Empire Club of Canada. "We need to, and will, remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to an absolute standstill."
Ford has previously complained about bike lanes on Bloor Street West creating gridlock on a road that is about a 10-minute drive from his home in Toronto's west end.
"It's an absolute disaster," Ford said last month.
The association said municipalities develop transportation plans based on local knowledge and community input. They are also balancing car traffic flow with active transportation and take into consideration health and environmental concerns.
"Rather than micromanaging bike lanes, the Ministry of Transportation could focus on accelerating its own approval processes to help support new housing," the association said.
Sarkaria said Thursday that cyclist safety is a priority for the government. He said they are "more than happy" to work with cities on the issue.
"As we work with the city to approve those future bike lanes, we'll take into consideration ... elements of safety and how that can be done in a way that supports the future introduction of lanes where they do require a removal of traffic," Sarkaria said.
He also said communities can still implement bike lanes that do not remove a lane of traffic.
The Canadian Press

Document 341 of 500

'Freedom Convoy' is finding new friends

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/freedom-convoy-is-finding-new-friends/docview/3117867820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Freedom+Convoy%27+is+finding+new+friends&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The greatest danger of the so-called "Freedom Convoy" was not to Ottawa. Yes, the convoy tortured the city for more than a month, and that shouldn't be forgotten, or downplayed. But it passed, even if the lonely leftovers that still hang around to harass politicians remain a smaller symptom of a wider ill.
No, the most dangerous part of the convoy was always its most farcical element, which is that some of the main organizers, who had a history of delusional and hateful comments, aimed to overthrow the government. No matter how supine Ottawa police were, no matter how absent Premier Doug Ford or then-mayor Jim Watson was, no matter the overlapping paralysis of jurisdictions, we all laughed at that.
Two-and-a-half years later, and elements of the movement are being embraced by politicians more than ever before. What if the convoy is succeeding at changing our governments for the worse?
You could see it coming at the time. Former Saskatchewan premier Brad Wall gave convoy leaders advice, and they met with former CPC leader Andrew Scheer in Regina, and various MPs cheered. Then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole took a half-hearted approach in meeting organizers, and was criticized for it. He was ejected for many reasons, after which interim CPC leader Candace Bergen saw an opportunity, and so did Pierre Poilievre. He glad-handed with convoy folks, and is now the leader of the Conservative party, and quite likely to be our next prime minister.
Poilievre has dutifully lined up with some of the most deluded members of the public: marching on the day before Canada Day in 2022 with anti-vaccine veteran James Topp, or his private member's bill that would have banned vaccine mandates for travellers and federal employees.
But it's worsening. In Alberta, Danielle Smith's government fired Alberta's then-chief medical officer of health Deena Hinshaw, which Smith justified by saying of public health, "they shut down the economy, they put on masks, they put on restrictions, and I thought, we're not going to let that happen."
The Alberta Human Rights Act was updated to include the right to refuse a vaccine or to not wear a mask, and Smith plans to do the same with the Alberta Bill of Rights. Her new deputy chief of staff, by the way, owns a restaurant that in 2022 accepted puppy pictures instead of vaccination passports for entry.
And in B.C., Conservative Leader John Rustad crossed into a different place. He has not only told an anti-vaccine group he regretted getting vaccinated, but in a video unearthed by PressProgress, he was asked about a concept that the most angry and deluded anti-vaccine activists use: Nuremberg 2.0.
"Are you for or against a Nuremberg 2.0?" asked anti-vaccine activist Jedediah Ferguson, making it sound like "Newemberg."
"A do it bigger 2.0, sorry?" asked Rustad, confused.
"Nuremberg 2.0," repeated Ferguson, smiling.
"Nuremberg 2.0," said Rustad, a smile spreading across his face. "Ah, yes. That's probably something that's outside of my scope."
"I know I put you on the hot spot right there, for sure, but I had to ask," said Ferguson.
"No, no, it's fine," said Rustad. "I can say, that's something that's sort of outside the scope in terms of jurisdiction of British Columbia, but we would certainly be participating with other jurisdictions as we look at those sorts of issues."
Nuremberg 2.0 has long been a rallying cry among the most angry anti-vaccine activists: it is about show trials of doctors, politicians and journalists, and punishment ranging from prison to executions. Rustad later claimed to have misunderstood the question. The best you could probably say, based on his reaction and his answer, is that he was pandering to what he sees as his base.
Radicalization tends to go in one direction. The convoy to Ottawa was, at its heart, divorced from reality, even if you could understand some of its energy: As a young mother named Dasha told me in January 2022 at a convoy rally north of Toronto, "I'm just a regular person that wants to go back to normal. Why can't we just call it a day and say this is a regular flu just like any other flu?" She also, as it happened, thought hospitals were lying about being full of patients.
She was wrong. The anti-vaccine radicals are wrong. It was sad, talking to Dasha: she had been fed disinformation under strenuous circumstances, and was clearly vulnerable.
She's far from alone, and now the most unmoored reactions to the pandemic are leaching into governments across the country, and maybe into a federal government that has a habit of turning people who disagree with them into enemies.
The world of radical unreality is biting back, and we need conservative politicians with cognitive clarity and genuine principles beyond partisanship, who can transcend the angriest and most unhinged parts of their base. We face real-world problems, in the same way that we did under the COVID emergency, and we need people who deal with the world as it is. If that doesn't happen, we will all suffer.
Bruce Arthur is a columnist for the Toronto Star.
CAPTION:

Document 342 of 500

Doug Ford signals rebate cheques for all Ontarians will 'stimulate the economy'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-signals-rebate-cheques-all-ontarians/docview/3118094820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+signals+rebate+cheques+for+all+Ontarians+will+%27stimulate+the+economy%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stressing if "you want to stimulate the economy, put money into people's pockets," Premier Doug Ford signalled the cheque will soon be in the mail.
In a speech to 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon at the Metro Toronto Convention, Ford gave the Star a grudging nod for revealing his finance minister would be announcing tax rebate cheques for 16 million Ontarians on Oct. 30.
"I risk stealing Peter Bethlenfalvy's thunder if I get into the fall economic statement," the premier said in a campaign-style address Thursday.
"I'm sure the Toronto Star knows it already," he added, wryly.
His comments came after the Star reported Wednesday that Ford is planning to send cheques to every Ontario adult and child as a possible pre-election goodie for voters.
The move will be formally unveiled in Bethlenfalvy's economic update.
"He's tight as skin on a grape," the premier joked about his more fiscally conservative treasurer.
Although the exact amount of the "tax rebate" cheques is still being finalized by government officials, it will likely be at least $200. The payments would be tax-free.
That suggests the scheme could cost the provincial treasury about $3.2 billion when the money is distributed in January or February.
Sources - speaking confidentially to discuss a plan that has not yet been shared with all of cabinet, let alone caucus - said the timing would help Ontarians when their pay packets are reduced due to employment insurance deductions and when post-Christmas credit card bills are due.
But the cash injection could also give Ford a political boost as he considers calling an election more than a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"Put money into people's pockets and they'll go out and do something as simple as going out for dinner, maybe buying your kids sneakers or clothes, or maybe putting a little renovation on your house," he told the Empire Club.
"And they might do that because they have money in their pockets. The same with businesses. If you put money into businesses, they'll invest into their people, into technology, into their infrastructure, and they grow. That's what creates economic strength."
In this month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, the Tories were at 44 per cent while Bonnie Crombie's Liberals were at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats were at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens were at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians on Oct. 3-10 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
All three opposition leaders panned Ford's cheque gambit.
"Ford, if you really want to make life better, here's some advice: Build homes, hire doctors, fix schools, and give people a fair shot at planning for the future," said Stiles.
"Ontarians see right through these stunts from a government that's out of touch, out of gas, and clearly out of ideas."
Crombie, who attended Ford's speech, dismissed the plan as "another one-time gimmick from a premier who has promised and failed to cut taxes for more than six years."
"Rather than try to buy your vote in an election year, Ontario's Liberals will cut taxes for all middle-class families - permanently. More to come," she said, hinting at her party's election platform.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner suggested any such payouts should be means-tested.
"Imagine: Galen Weston getting a $200 cheque in the mail," said Schreiner, referring to the grocery tycoon.
"Ontarians need a government that's in their corner - not one that's trying to buy them out," said Schreiner.
"Some ways we could actually make life more affordable: build more homes, protect renters and invest in deeply affordable housing; stamp out oil and gas greed to bring down home energy costs; invest in public health care and education so Ontarians don't have to pay for things that should be covered publicly," he said.
While inflation has dipped to 1.6 per cent and interest rates are dropping, the Tories note that grocery prices remain high in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic and housing and energy costs continue to rise.
Ford's plan is a reprise of what former premier Mike Harris's Tories did in 2000 when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques. Ford pointedly gave a shout-out from the podium to Harris, who was also in attendance Thursday.
That $200 payment a generation ago, which came when Eves announced a budget surplus, would be worth around $335 today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.
Bethlenfalvy's update in a fortnight will not show a balanced budget, but is expected to signal the fiscal situation is much better than it was in his March 26 budget when he projected a surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25.
That was a $10-billion swing from the $200-million surplus the treasurer had forecast last year.
CAPTION: Premier Doug FordFord gave a nod to the Star for revealing his finance minister would be announcing tax rebate cheques for 16 million Ontarians on Oct. 30.
