Document 343 of 500

Doug Ford wants to buy your silence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-buy-your-silence/docview/3118094709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+buy+your+silence&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some politicians try to buy votes.
Doug Ford wants to buy your silence. And acquiescence.
For a roughly $200 payout, to be handed out to everyone in Ontario, the premier hopes voters will forgive his political sins and forget his legal transgressions.
The planned cash bonanza, to be distributed early next year, is a calculated gamble. The goal is to clear the way for an early election before any bad news - legal and political - erodes the support Ford's Tories now enjoy in public opinion polls.
As my colleague Robert Benzie reported this week, the premier's sudden generosity will be made public later this month. In its annual fall economic statement, the government is dropping any pretence of fiscal probity as it invests heavily and recklessly in its own political survival.
Ford's PCs want an early election, no matter the cost. Never mind the unnecessary $155-million election expense - that's the least of it.
If they settle on the $200 figure for every adult and child in Ontario, that works out to as much as $1,000 for a family of five - and perhaps $3.2 billion out of the treasury in total. That money is badly needed to shore up our schools, our hospitals and our homeless, but the premier believes he needs it more desperately to soften up voters.
There are twin legal challenges facing Ford.
The first is deadly serious: potential violations of Ontario's criminal laws now being probed by a high-stakes RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
The headlines have faded, but few have forgotten the $8.28-billion imbroglio over protected land made available to private developers on the premier's watch (until he reversed course under pressure). That police probe could be released sometime next year, delivering potentially bad news and a political death sentence.
Rather than wait for the police to rain on their re-election parade - scheduled for June 2026 under Ontario's fixed election law - the plan is to move the campaign up by more than a year to early 2025. But to bump up the date, the Tories would have to ignore the election law.
Fortuitously for Ford, that second potential legal peril is more symbolic than real. While the law mandates that any majority government should serve a full four-year term in power before triggering another, there is no legal penalty for flouting it - only a political price if the press and the people cry foul.
Hence the payout. The premier is banking on voters being more interested in money than morality.
It's an elegant, if expensive, election plan: a kickback for voters, gifted by a government accused of kickbacks from developers (despite those opposition allegations in the legislature, no criminality has been proven and the police aren't talking).
Why aim for a potential $200 giveaway? In the past, as a Toronto councillor, Ford famously doled out a wad of $20 bills to impoverished voters at a Toronto Community Housing building in 2013.
He later apologized for the spectacle. And subsequently opted for juicier giveaways that, historically, have hit a sweet spot with voters.
Ahead of the 2022 election, Ford's Tories cut cheques to rebate motorists for licence plate fees that the government cancelled, at a cost of more than $1 billion to the treasury. During the COVID pandemic, parents received as much as $250 per child. And the previous PC government of Mike Harris issued $200 "dividend" cheques.
How to justify such shameless pre-election (early election) vote-buying?
The unspoken reason is to satisfy the premier's lust for power. The official rationale is to support people's purchasing power.
The premier points to post-pandemic inflation raising the price of food and fuel, but it's not so simple. In fact, the inflation rate plunged to a remarkably low 1.6 per cent last month, thanks mostly to falling fuel prices, according to the latest Statistics Canada data.
And then there's the federal carbon levy, one of Ford's favourite targets. It is scheduled to increase again next spring, raising the price of gasoline.
Ford has fulminated over gasoline prices for years - even though the amount taken in by the federal government is fully rebated to the average family. Before the 2022 election, he used the federal carbon levy as a pretext for the province to reduce its own fuel tax in another vote-buying gambit.
For the Tories, the upcoming handout could be the gift that keeps on giving (if not voting). First, by soothing any public disgust over an early election; second, by serving as a counterpoint to the federal carbon levy, allowing Ford to fight an election on his perennial slogan, "Axe the tax."
In a preview of his campaign rhetoric, Ford complained Thursday about the "impact of inflation and high interest rates, alongside the growing cost of the federal carbon tax, which in my opinion is the worst tax you could ever put on the backs of people."
There you have it - an evil levy imposed by Ottawa (albeit rebated), versus a helpful handout from the province, pre-election. Ford has it all figured out.
But does he have voters figured out?
CAPTION: The unspoken reason Premier Doug Ford wants to send cheques to Ontarians is to satisfy the premier's lust for power. The official rationale is to support people's purchasing power, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Document 344 of 500

Appeal in landmark youth-led climate case gets go-ahead from Ontario court

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/appeal-landmark-youth-led-climate-case-gets-go/docview/3118094694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Appeal+in+landmark+youth-led+climate+case+gets+go-ahead+from+Ontario+court&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a landmark case, Ontario's top court has ruled that an appeal from seven young Ontarians suing the provincial government over its new greenhouse gas emissions targets can proceed.
However, the Court of Appeal declined to rule on the case's substance, extending a legal battle in Canada's courts over their role in overseeing government climate action.
In a decision released Thursday, three judges - Lois B. Roberts, Steve A. Coroza and Sally A. Gomery - sided with the appellants in Mathur v. Ontario that a Superior Court of Justice judge "erred" in deciding the young people's case was a "positive rights case" in an April 2023 decision.
"This is not a positive rights case. The application does not seek to impose on Ontario any new positive obligations to combat climate change," Judges Roberts, Coroza and Gomery wrote. In legal studies and political theory, positive rights refers to rights that give people something, such as a right to have an education or a right to housing.
But the judges fell short of determining the merits of the case's arguments, writing the appeals court was "not well placed" to make such a ruling. Instead, they called for a new hearing, which could be overseen by the original judge or a different one.
Thursday's ruling gave the seven young appellants - lead applicant Sophia Mathur, Madison Dyck, Shelby Gagnon, Beze Gray, Zoë Keary-Matzner, Alex Neufeldt and Shaelyn Wabegijig - a small victory in their lawsuit against the provincial government that was first filed in 2019.
At issue - which lawyers for the seven young people argued before the Court of Appeal earlier this year - was Ontario's weakened 2030 emissions targets passed by Premier Doug Ford's then-newly elected Progressive Conservative government in 2018 that reduced goals from 37 per cent lower emissions below 2005 levels to 30 per cent. According to the appellants claim, this reduction violates the charter rights of current youth and future generations, specifically sections 7 and 15 that provide the right to life, liberty and security of the person and equal protection under the law respectively.
Mathur v. Ontario is the first case of its kind to be tried in Canada, and Thursday's ruling could shape decisions in other cases facing courts in Canada and internationally asking judiciaries to more actively oversee government climate plans.
In the U.S., judges have ruled in favour of young people from Montana and Hawaii challenging governments for their alleged climate in action. And in 2019, the top court in the Netherlands ruled the government has a duty to protect its citizens from the potentially devastating effects of climate change, including by further cutting emissions.
Meanwhile, a similar case, La Rose v. Her Majesty the Queen, is awaiting a trial date at the federal level in Canada as 15 young people sue the Canadian government on similar grounds as the seven young people in Mathur v. Ontario.
With files from Marco Chown Oved.
CAPTION: The seven appellants, centre, and their lawyers from the firm Ecojustice photographed together in 2020. They have been in the court system since 2019 making their case.

Document 345 of 500

Justin Trudeau to shuffle cabinet as four more ministers say they won't seek re-election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-shuffle-cabinet-as-four-more/docview/3118094571/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+to+shuffle+cabinet+as+four+more+ministers+say+they+won%27t+seek+re-election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will shuffle his cabinet in the coming weeks as four more cabinet ministers have told the Liberal leader they will not run again in the next campaign.
Over the last three months, a total of six of Trudeau's cabinet ministers or 15 per cent have bailed ahead of a federal election which polls suggest the Liberals will lose. The drip-drip of names has meant that the coming shuffle will be the third in as many months.
The shuffle is not expected to occur next week, contrary to early reports, according to a senior source, but could come quickly in the weeks following that.
Parliament is in its Thanksgiving week recess, but MPs return next week, when Trudeau will face a restive caucus, with reports of more than two dozen, and possibly as many as 40 MPs, getting ready to challenge Trudeau to step aside as Liberal leader.
Hamilton's Filomena Tassi, the minister for the federal economic development agency for southern Ontario, announced Thursday she won't run for reasons "that are deeply personal. It is time for me to be closer to home with my family."
Tassi follows National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau expected to run for mayor of Sherbrooke, and two others who have advised Trudeau of their intention to leave politics: Northern Affairs Minister Dan Vandal who is in charge of economic development for the Prairies and the North, and Sport Minister Carla Qualtrough. Their departures were first reported by Radio-Canada.
Last month Trudeau's Quebec lieutenant Pablo Rodriguez left the transport portfolio, and Liberal caucus, to make a run for the provincial Liberal Party of Quebec leadership - a departure Trudeau filled in the interim with Treasury Board President Anita Anand. Likewise, he had a quick shuffle in July when Labour Minister Seamus O'Regan advised he wouldn't run again, also for "personal reasons." Steven MacKinnon replaced O'Regan.
Trudeau has had a challenging fall, with polls continuing to show his party struggling.
After returning from a Francophonie summit in Paris where Israel's military engagement with Hezbollah in Lebanon dominated talks, and before leaving for the ASEAN summit in Laos last week, the prime minister called off a planned trip next week to Samoa to take part in the Commonwealth leaders' summit.
Trudeau returned from Laos on the weekend, after a meeting in Germany on Ukraine was cancelled by U.S. President Joe Biden, only to confront new reports, including by the Star, of brewing caucus unrest.
All that amid a Canada-India diplomatic rupture as a result of the RCMP bombshell allegations against Indian diplomats and consular officials - and Trudeau's appearance at the public inquiry into foreign interference where the prime minister accused his main rival, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, of irresponsibly refusing to get the required security clearance to get briefings on how his own party is at risk.
Some MPs who spoke to the Star this week defended Trudeau in the face of growing caucus dissent, arguing those calling for his resignation are helping Poilievre.
"We are wasting our energy by focusing inwards rather than focusing on the needs of our constituents," said Ottawa MP Yasir Naqvi.
"I respect Sean (Casey) but I disagree with him ... Justin Trudeau has more to offer." Naqvi was referring to the PEI MP who went public this week saying it was time for Trudeau to step aside.
Another MP said it was "disappointing" to hear about these efforts in the media when those conversations should be held in private.
Others said they have not seen a letter reportedly calling for Trudeau's resignation and declined to take a stance on his leadership, but said they agree that conversations should be had about the future of the party. If even a small group of MPs sign a letter calling on the prime minister to resign, he will be put in a tough situation, they said.
With files from Mark Ramzy
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appears as a witness at the federal inquiry into foreign interference in Ottawa on Oct. 16, 2024. Trudeau is expected to shuffle his cabinet in the coming weeks.

Document 346 of 500

NDP calls on integrity commissioner to look into Doug Ford's Ontario Place spa deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-calls-on-integrity-commissioner-look-into/docview/3117868482/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+calls+on+integrity+commissioner+to+look+into+Doug+Ford%27s+Ontario+Place+spa+deal&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Leader Marit Stiles is asking the province's integrity commissioner to look into the government's spa deal at Ontario Place.
Stiles has filed a complaint "calling for an investigation into the Therme deal," the NDP said in a written release. "The complaint includes evidence suggesting that Therme received preferential treatment from the Ford government and seeks an investigation into the minister of infrastructure's involvement."
The NDP says that "over the past two years, we have collected evidence suggesting a deeply troubling pattern of behaviour surrounding the Ford government's deal with Therme. We have reason to believe that Therme received preferential treatment from this government when they were awarded the 95-year-long lease to build a luxury spa at Ontario Place."
Grace Lee, Premier Doug Ford's director of media relations, called the move "nothing more than the NDP reaching for headlines with a frivolous complaint largely based on conspiracy theories. We look forward to making Ontario Place a world-class destination once again."
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, who was not minister at the time the deal was struck, said she looks forward "to assisting the integrity commissioner."
Unlike Stiles, she added "I have too much respect for the commissioner to play politics with his office. I will continue to focus my efforts on getting shovels in the ground to make Ontario Place a place that families can enjoy once again."
In its submission to the integrity commissioner, the NDP says the bidding process for Ontario Place development was not a typically competitive process and that, among other irregularities, did not follow the normal bidding rules for larger projects. It also says the deadline for applications was extended, during which time Therme submitted its bid.
Earlier this month, the Ford government released details of the 95-year lease with the Austrian firm, which includes 1,600 parking spots for the spa resort, and a clause that will force the province to pay up to $2.2 million a year if those spots aren't in place when it opens or by 2030, whichever comes first.
Plans for Ontario Place's redevelopment include the spa, a water park and a new home for the Ontario Science Centre.
Therme's lease is for 75 years with an option to renew for another 20.
Questions the NDP says it is hoping the Office of the Integrity Commissioner will answer include "was the procurement process fair? Why is the government using taxpayer money to build Therme a parking lot after telling other bidders it would not consider proposals with publicly funded facilities? Billions of dollars and a cherished public asset are at stake. We are bringing our case to the integrity commissioner so we can finally get some answers on this shady scheme."
CAPTION: The NDP want an investigation into the Ford government's deal made with Therme to build a spa at Ontario Place.

Document 347 of 500

Hamilton MP Filomena Tassi will not run for re-election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-mp-filomena-tassi-will-not-run-re/docview/3117581193/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+MP+Filomena+Tassi+will+not+run+for+re-election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas MP Filomena Tassi has announced that she will not run in the next federal election.
Tassi made the announcement on social media Thursday, explaining that her decision to not run again is "deeply personal," and it's time for her "to be closer to home" after representing the riding at Parliament Hill for the last nine years.
"This is a bittersweet decision," Tassi wrote in a statement shared on X. "I have truly, deeply loved serving the people of my riding."
Speaking to The Spectator, Tassi described Thursday as an "emotional day," noting that she had been "monitoring (her) home situation for the past while."
As previously reported by The Spectator, Tassi had shared in September 2022 that her husband suffered two strokes in early 2021 and that her family had been involved in his recovery.
At that time, Tassi had asked Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for a change of job, which saw her move from minister of public services and procurement to her current role as the minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario.
"I was really grateful for that," Tassi said in an interview. "But I know now that this is the right decision."
Tassi was elected in October 2015, an election that saw the Liberal party, led by Trudeau, form a majority government. She was re-elected in both the 2019 and 2021 federal elections.
Since then, Tassi had also served as the minister of seniors, the minister of labour, deputy whip and Receiver General for Canada.
Tassi told The Spectator that a highlight of her time as a politician was the amount of funding she was able to help bring to the city, pointing to the more than $5 billion the Liberal government has committed to projects in the city since 2015.
That includes funding for the planned Hamilton LRT, the Randall Reef remediation project and green steel upgrades at ArcelorMittal Dofasco, as well as infrastructure, housing, and research, development and skills training at the city's post-secondary schools.
"Every sector - the federal government has invested in Hamilton and invested significantly," she said, pointing to the diversification of the city's economy in recent years. "That's transformative change."
Another highlight was the creation of the "Team Hamilton" strategy, which Tassi helped to co-found alongside other Hamilton MPs from every corner of the house - including Chad Collins, Lisa Hepfner, Matthew Green and Dan Muys.
Tassi said that initiative saw the MPs meet as often as possible - sometimes with community stakeholders - in order to better advocate for the needs of the city. She noted that with the five of them all on the same page, more change was possible.
"We may have disagreements ... but we believe that coming together and working together puts us in a better place and helps us serve our constituents better," said Tassi. "It demonstrates that positive politics is possible."
While Tassi will not be involved in the strategy once her time in office ends, she hopes to see the initiative continue with future Hamilton MPs.
Tassi thanked those who had supported her during her time in politics, specifically those in west Hamilton who elected her not once, but three times.
Tassi also encouraged others to step forward into a career in politics, putting an emphasis on the need for more women and young people to come forward, while recognizing that the current political landscape is "very challenging."
"I have really had the experience of a lifetime," she said. "It's a tremendous opportunity, now it's time to pass the torch."
While Tassi's decision not to run for re-election comes at a time of looming turbulence for the federal Liberal party, she expressed her support for Trudeau in her statement.
"I wish him the very best now and in the future. I believed in him in 2015 and I believe in him now," she wrote.
The Canadian Press has reported that three other cabinet ministers will not seek re-election and in the backbenches, there has been an effort to push Trudeau to resign as leader of the party, as reported by several media outlets.
Trudeau is expected to shuffle his cabinet, but not before all Liberal caucus members are expected to meet on Parliament Hill on Oct. 23.
Back in Hamilton, Tassi is the first federal MP to announce her intention not to run for re-election. Muys, Hepfner and Green are running for re-election, with Muys planning to run for in the future riding of Flamborough-Glanbrook-Brant North.
Fallon Hewitt is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. fhewitt@torstar.ca
CAPTION: Tassi LetterPrime Minister Justin Trudeau and MP Filomena Tassi walk along Locke Street on their way to The Burnt Tongue while in town for the cabinet retreat in 2023.MP Filomena Tassi, who represents the riding of Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas, says she is not seeking re-election.

Document 348 of 500

Ford government aims to fast-track construction of Highway 413, Bradford Bypass and Garden City Skyway

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-aims-fast-track-construction/docview/3117580950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+aims+to+fast-track+construction+of+Highway+413%2C+Bradford+Bypass+and+Garden+City+Skyway&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With an early provincial election looming, Premier Doug Ford's government wants to fast-track construction of Highway 413, the Bradford Bypass and a twin section of the Garden City Skyway in St. Catharines.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said legislation called the "Building Highways Faster Act" will be introduced after the legislature returns next week from an extended summer break to provide for "around-the-clock, 24/7 construction."
Taking aim at potential protests against the two highway projects which have been heavily opposed by environmentalists and others for using up prime Greenbelt farmland and threatening endangered or rare species, the bill would set "new penalties for obstructing access for field investigations" or "damaging equipment," Sarkaria said Thursday in Caledon.
It would also "accelerate" the process for buyouts or expropriations of farmland or other properties needed for the projects and "streamline" relocations of utility lines.
There will also be legislation to establish an "accelerated environmental assessment process" for Highway 413, from Highway 401 at Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan, allowing the province to proceed with early work on the controversial roadway rejected by a previous Liberal government.
"Projects like this need to get built," Sarkaria said, citing "unprecedented growth" in Ontario with projections the population will increase two million by 2031 and put existing 400-series highways at or over capacity within a decade.
"Gridlock suffocates our roadways."
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre) said the proposed legislation is dangerous.
"Slashing environmental laws, paving over our Greenbelt, Ontarians have made it clear that they're not OK with this," she added in a statement, referring to the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal under investigation by the RCMP.
"For the government to be trying it yet again with Highway 413 shows how they'll do anything to help their inner circle over the people of this province. We also need to build communities that don't require you to travel a great distance to get to work or school, by building more homes in existing neighbourhoods and especially around transit hubs."
The Bradford Bypass will stretch from Highway 400 in the Bradford area east to Highway 404 in a bid to ease congestion between there and Toronto. The Garden City Skyway traverses the Welland Canal on a busy stretch of the Queen Elizabeth Way. Plans call for a new four-lane, 2.2-kilometre bridge in addition to a rehabilitation of the deck on the existing bridge.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie reiterated her opposition to Highway 413 earlier this week.
"I have never supported the 413."
However, the highway promise is credited with helping Ford's PCs win all the Brampton ridings in the 2022 provincial election, taking three seats from the New Democrats.
The premier has repeatedly refused to rule out a snap election as early as March, instead of waiting until the next provincewide vote slated for June 2026. His government has also taken aim at bike lanes, pledging legislation that would require municipalities to get provincial approval before taking out any lanes of traffic on major roadways, and has stepped up feel-good government advertising about Ontario's economy.
As the Star's Robert Benzie reported, the province is planning to send cheques of at least $200 to 16 million Ontarians to offset the rising cost of living.
CAPTION: The government's legislation would"accelerate" the process for buyouts or expropriations of farmland or other properties needed for the projects.

Document 349 of 500

Home-care patients buying own medical supplies during Ontario-wide shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/home-care-patients-buying-own-medical-supplies/docview/3117580853/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Home-care+patients+buying+own+medical+supplies+during+Ontario-wide+shortage&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Hamilton woman said she had to buy her own medical supplies to get her dressing changed after cancer surgery because of a provincewide shortage of home care equipment that local nurses are calling a crisis.
"If I can buy my supplies on Amazon, it shouldn't be too hard to get this fixed," said Elizabeth Shaw. "It is unconscionable to me that people are not getting the care they require ... When I go to my appointments the waiting room is filled with people who clearly need medical attention. How many of them will see a worsening of their condition?"
Ontario Health atHome - the agency that co-ordinates home and community care for thousands of patients across the province every day - has been so low on medical supplies since at least Sept. 28 that appointments are being cancelled and some patients are being sent to urgent care or could end up in emergency departments.
The Hamilton Spectator spoke with three Hamilton home-care nurses who described a dire situation of having inadequate or no supplies to change catheters, treat oozing wounds and properly care for intravenous lines. They describe scrambling to find equipment themselves - checking if any patients have supplies they no longer need, pooling what they have together and combing through anywhere equipment might be stored. In some desperate cases, they say they have reused supplies or stretched out the amount of time between changes.
The Spectator is not naming the nurses because they are not authorized by their employer to speak but they felt compelled to alert the public because the shortages are affecting the care they provide.
"They've put people's lives at risk here," said one of the nurses. "These are actual people, not just numbers. Sick people - little tiny babies, cancer patients, diabetics."
Ontario Health atHome did not reply to questions from The Spectator.
On its website, the agency stated: "We have been aware of and working tirelessly with our vendors and suppliers to address the challenges we are experiencing with supply inventory levels and distribution. We are making good progress, with supply levels improving steadily and back-ordered items coming in daily."
The Hamilton nurses say this area had a reliable and good quality longtime supplier. The problem started when Ontario Health atHome switched to a new provincewide supplier that documents obtained by The Spectator say was to "modernize medical equipment and supplies" and leverage "provincial buying power." When the switch happened, the nurses say the majority of the supplies they needed were suddenly out of stock.
"We had no notice. It happened so fast," said one of the nurses. "Gauze and saline - don't have it. No catheters, no Foley trays. They don't even have medical tape."
The other issue is the nurses say the quality of the items they are receiving is vastly different.
"We normally have a triple absorbent dressing that we can order, but it's no longer available through this company," said one nurse. "They sent the skinniest little bandages that just leak."
The nurses say their patients have reached the point that they can't leave their homes because of the inadequate dressings. They are worried about getting infection or sepsis because of delays to their care.
After having to buy her own supplies online, Elizabeth Shaw reached out to MPP Sandy Shaw, her NDP representative in Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas.
"It's outrageous," said MPP Shaw (no relation), who confirmed the shortages were caused by a change in supply management that was implemented around Sept. 24.
"We need our health minister to reassure us that they will fix it," said MPP Shaw. "This is very serious."
Health Minister Sylvia Jones did not answer questions from The Spectator.
"We are working hard to ensure those who need medical supplies delivered to their homes, get the supplies they need," the minister's spokesperson Hannah Jensen said in a statement. "If an individual has yet to receive the supplies they need, they can call 1-866-377-7567 for their situation to be escalated and resolved."
Elizabeth Shaw said she didn't get any information or help when she called Ontario Health atHome.
"As a client, I would appreciate knowing how long is this going to last for, what's their plan on fixing it," she said. "Any kind of communication would have been better than nothing."
She needs a dressing changed every other day since having surgery on Sept. 16 to remove a cancerous growth on her shin that involved a skin graft. The 61-year-old west Mountain woman became concerned enough to buy her own supplies when a notice about the shortages appeared Sept. 28 at the Ontario Health atHome clinic at Pritchard Road, where she goes for wound care. She spent about $25 and said it was delivered to her home the next day.
"I didn't want a shortage of medical supplies to cause me to get an infection," she said. "It's very concerning."
The first call to cancel her dressing change came from the agency on Oct. 12. However, the appointment went ahead because Elizabeth had her own supplies. When she arrived, a new notice stated that some clients would "need to be deferred to urgent care or ER."
"For me, I'd describe it as inconvenience," she said. "I was able to fix this for myself. But I really worry about some people who may be less able to advocate for themselves."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: MPP Sandy Shaw, NDP Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas, said a new supply management system caused the equipment shortage in home care.Ontario Health atHome has been so low on supplies since at least Sept. 28 that appointments are being cancelled and some home-care patients are being sent to urgent care or emergency rooms.

Document 350 of 500

Home-care patients buying their own medical supplies during provincewide shortage: One Hamilton woman went on Amazon to get dressings as local nurses describe a dire situation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/home-care-patients-buying-their-own-medical/docview/3117295351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Home-care+patients+buying+their+own+medical+supplies+during+provincewide+shortage%3A+One+Hamilton+woman+went+on+Amazon+to+get+dressings+as+local+nurses+describe+a+dire+situation&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Hamilton woman said she had to buy her own medical supplies to get her dressing changed after cancer surgery because of a provincewide shortage of home care equipment that local nurses are calling a crisis.
"If I can buy my supplies on Amazon, it shouldn't be too hard to get this fixed," said Elizabeth Shaw. "It is unconscionable to me that people are not getting the care they require ... When I go to my appointments the waiting room is filled with people who clearly need medical attention. How many of them will see a worsening of their condition?"
Ontario Health atHome - the agency that co-ordinates home and community care for thousands of patients across the province every day - has been so low on medical supplies since at least Sept. 28 that appointments are being cancelled and some patients are being sent to urgent care or could end up in emergency departments.
The Hamilton Spectator spoke with three Hamilton home-care nurses who described a dire situation of having inadequate or no supplies to change catheters, treat oozing wounds and properly care for intravenous lines. They describe scrambling to find equipment themselves - checking if any patients have supplies they no longer need, pooling what they have together and combing through anywhere equipment might be stored. In some desperate cases, they say they have reused supplies or stretched out the amount of time between changes.
The Spectator is not naming the nurses because they are not authorized by their employer to speak but they felt compelled to alert the public because the shortages are affecting the care they provide.
"They've put people's lives at risk here," said one of the nurses. "These are actual people, not just numbers. Sick people - little tiny babies, cancer patients, diabetics."
Ontario Health atHome did not reply to questions from The Spectator.
On its website, the agency stated: "We have been aware of and working tirelessly with our vendors and suppliers to address the challenges we are experiencing with supply inventory levels and distribution. We are making good progress, with supply levels improving steadily and back-ordered items coming in daily."
The Hamilton nurses say this area had a reliable and good quality longtime supplier. The problem started when Ontario Health atHome switched to a new provincewide supplier that documents obtained by The Spectator say was to "modernize medical equipment and supplies" and leverage "provincial buying power." When the switch happened, the nurses say the majority of the supplies they needed were suddenly out of stock.
"We had no notice. It happened so fast," said one of the nurses. "Gauze and saline - don't have it. No catheters, no Foley trays. They don't even have medical tape."
The other issue is the nurses say the quality of the items they are receiving is vastly different.
"We normally have a triple absorbent dressing that we can order, but it's no longer available through this company," said one nurse. "They sent the skinniest little bandages that just leak."
The nurses say their patients have reached the point that they can't leave their homes because of the inadequate dressings. They are worried about getting infection or sepsis because of delays to their care.
After having to buy her own supplies online, Elizabeth Shaw reached out to MPP Sandy Shaw, her NDP representative in Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas.
"It's outrageous," said MPP Shaw (no relation), who confirmed the shortages were caused by a change in supply management that was implemented around Sept. 24.
"We need our health minister to reassure us that they will fix it," said MPP Shaw. "This is very serious."
Health Minister Sylvia Jones did not answer questions from The Spectator.
"We are working hard to ensure those who need medical supplies delivered to their homes, get the supplies they need," the minister's spokesperson Hannah Jensen said in a statement. "If an individual has yet to receive the supplies they need, they can call 1-866-377-7567 for their situation to be escalated and resolved."
Elizabeth Shaw said she didn't get any information or help when she called Ontario Health atHome.
"As a client, I would appreciate knowing how long is this going to last for, what's their plan on fixing it," she said. "Any kind of communication would have been better than nothing."
She needs a dressing changed every other day since having surgery on Sept. 16 to remove a cancerous growth on her shin that involved a skin graft. The 61-year-old west Mountain woman became concerned enough to buy her own supplies when a notice about the shortages appeared Sept. 28 at the Ontario Health atHome clinic at Pritchard Road, where she goes for wound care. She spent about $25 and said it was delivered to her home the next day.
"I didn't want a shortage of medical supplies to cause me to get an infection," she said. "It's very concerning."
The first call to cancel her dressing change came from the agency on Oct. 12. However, the appointment went ahead because Elizabeth had her own supplies. When she arrived, a new notice stated that some clients would "need to be deferred to urgent care or ER."
"For me, I'd describe it as inconvenience," she said. "I was able to fix this for myself. But I really worry about some people who may be less able to advocate for themselves."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Ontario Health atHome has been so low on supplies since at least Sept. 28 that appointments are being cancelled and some home-care patients are being sent to urgent care or emergency rooms.Toronto Star file photo

Document 351 of 500

Nobel winner wants us to be wise about technology

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nobel-winner-wants-us-be-wise-about-technology/docview/3117295274/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nobel+winner+wants+us+to+be+wise+about+technology&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's something delightfully amusing about Geoffrey Hinton's handling of the media maelstrom after he was informed that he had been named co-recipient of the 2024 Nobel Prize in physics.
That he hesitated about answering the phone, because who calls at 1 a.m.?
That the world-famous University of Toronto computer scientist was in California staying, as he explained to the New York Times, in "a cheap hotel room with no internet."
That he told the BBC that he dropped out of physics after first year university.
That in considering a question posed by the Toronto Star's Victoria Gibson, who wondered whether the neural networks of artificial intelligence could be deployed to improve the city of Toronto, offered that he was "not quite sure how a neural network could get rid of Doug Ford."
Hinton is a fierce critic of the premier's plans to build a downsized Ontario Science Centre on the waterfront.
And he's a passionate proponent of curiosity, from the spark that can ignite in the mind of a school child on a Science Centre outing, to the curiosity-driven research that has powered him across decades to global status as one of the godfathers of artificial intelligence.
What resonates is the way in which our newly minted Nobel laureate champions learning, including that of his own students who, as he has said, took his foundational research and "actually made things work."
Of course, most of us weren't paying attention in the early 1980s when Hinton and his fellow scientists experienced their first successes with computer algorithms.
Nor would the vast majority have had a clue about his forbearance working in the field of brain-mimicking artificial neural networks, denounced as a waste of time by many in the scientific community.
But we're all awake now, eager, perhaps even desperate, to fully understand the positive potential of artificial intelligence - and its dangers.
Hinton has said in interviews that he always had his worries about AI, from job displacement to the proliferation of fake news.
But the good works - he often emphasizes the benefits to health care in, say, the analysis of medical images, and huge improvements in productivity - are proving to be revolutionary.
A tipping point arrived in the spring of last year when Hinton announced that after a decade working with Google on its AI technology, he had quit, citing the existential risk of artificial intelligence outpacing the intelligence of mankind.
As he said to reporters this week, "We have no experience of what it's like to have things smarter than us."
How close are we to being outplayed by AI?
Take a look at the advanced reasoning capabilities of OpenAI's GPT-4. Or, just for fun, check out its single sentence explanation of the plot of "Cinderella" - posted on OpenAI's website - where the artificial brain was commanded to start each word with the next letter of the alphabet.
It may be a fairy tale. But it's also an example of the dizzying creative talents of AI.
Perhaps we should say "chillingly."
Hinton has raised the spectre of a threat that could get out of control. He has made the urgent call for more research into AI safety. Systems more intelligent than us could "go rogue," he has said.
"That's not science fiction."
He doesn't have all the answers. He's not that smart.
But perhaps in his wry, teacherly way he can again help lay a foundation stone for not just the mechanics of artificial intelligence, but the responsible way forward.
CAPTION:

Document 352 of 500

Electricity demand to soar by 75% in Ontario: Data centres need much more energy to power AI

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/electricity-demand-soar-75-ontario/docview/3117295267/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Electricity+demand+to+soar+by+75%25+in+Ontario%3A+Data+centres+need+much+more+energy+to+power+AI&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Demand for electricity in Ontario is set to soar by 75 per cent in the next couple of decades, far higher than was projected just last year, in part due to a sudden surge in data centres supporting artificial intelligence, the system operator said Wednesday.
The demand has been relatively flat for the past 20 years, but now it is building and shows no signs of levelling off, officials with the Independent Electricity System Operator said in a briefing. Just last year, they expected demand to grow 60 per cent higher by 2050.
Much of that additional pressure will come from industry, such as the three electric-vehicle battery plants that are in the works and associated supply-chain manufacturing. Industrial demand is anticipated to rise by 58 per cent by 2035, adding the equivalent of a city the size of Toronto to the grid, the IESO said.
The new demand is also coming from data centres that need much larger amounts of energy to power artificial intelligence, the IESO said.
"Previously, the data centres, they existed ... but the energy use that data centres now require, because of these AI functionalities, is infinitely higher than it was previously," said Chuck Farmer, the IESO's vice-president of planning, conservation and resource adequacy. "I'll be frank, we have been - as many system operators have been - caught a little off guard by how quickly this has happened."
At least 16 large data centres are forecast to be in service by 2035, accounting for 13 per cent of the new electricity demand.
Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce said he is working to ensure the system is reliable for those centres and all other forms of economic growth.
"This is a significant opportunity, but it creates pressure on the grid," he said.
"We don't deter investment or jobs or innovation that could take place in Ontario. AI and, as well, these data centres are very important when it comes to growing our economy, diversifying our economy and creating value-added jobs. So we understand that there's much more demand for energy on the system, on the grid, but we have a plan."
The IESO said the province has enough power for the rest of this decade, and Lecce said he will soon announce his "vision" for how to meet future demand with reliable, affordable and clean energy.
Ontario is already planning to bolster its output from nuclear generation, extending the life of the Pickering nuclear plant by two years while considering a larger refurbishment to get decades more out of it, considering building a new, large-scale nuclear plant on the site of Bruce Power's current generating station and building four small modular reactors.
As well, the IESO is preparing a new energy efficiency framework for programs to manage demand and conserve energy, and the province is procuring thousands more megawatts of electricity generation, with Lecce eyeing a mix of natural gas, hydroelectric, renewables, nuclear and biomass.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said he is worried the government will lean even more heavily on natural gas to fill the supply gap.
"My concern is the government's going to choose an expensive, dirty approach, which is ramping up gas plants - which is what they've been doing," he said.
In 2021, the electricity system was 94 per cent emissions-free, but that is now down to 87 per cent as the province relies more on natural gas generation.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A data centre sits on Parliament Street in Toronto. At least 16 large data centres are forecast to be in service by 2035 in Ontario, accounting for 13 per cent of the new electricity demand.Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 353 of 500

Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-poised-send-out-pre-election-cheques-16/docview/3117699568/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+poised+to+send+out+pre-election+cheques+to+16+million+Ontarians&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is poised to send cheques to 16 million Ontarians to offset rising costs as a possible early election looms, the Star has learned.
Sources say the premier's gambit will be announced in Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement on Oct. 30.
While the precise amount of the rebate cheques is still being finalized, it should be at least $200 for every adult and child in the province.
That means it could cost the provincial treasury about $3.2 billion when the cash flows out the door in January or February.
The payments would be tax-free. It could mean an additional $800 in household cash for a family of four at a time of year when paycheques are smaller due to employment insurance deductions and when holiday credit card bills are coming due.
Even though inflation has dropped to 1.6 per cent and mortgage rates are declining, Ford's Progressive Conservatives privately point to stubbornly high grocery prices and energy and housing expenses as the rationale for the payouts.
The move echoed what former premier Mike Harris's Tories did in 2000 when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques.
While Eves' budget that year was in surplus, Bethlenfalvy is not expected to balance the books until after the next election, which is scheduled for June 2026 but could come as soon as this March.
Still, the treasurer's fiscal update will show a significant improvement over his March 26 budget when he forecast a surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25.
That was a $10-billion swing from the $200-million surplus Bethlenfalvy had projected last year.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, noted Ford wants to get more money back in the pockets of Ontarians.
They pointed to his decision before the 2022 election to eliminate licence plate sticker renewal fees, which costs the treasury more than $1 billion annually but has been a popular measure with motorists.
Similarly, the premier scrapped tolls on Highways 412 and 418.
As well, during the COVID-19 pandemic, the provincial government gave parents $200 per child under age 12 and $250 per child and youth under 21 with special needs to offset online educational expenses.
Speaking to reporters last Friday in Brockville, Ford, the multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family, insisted he understands how strapped families are feeling these days.
"We're looking at cutting costs every which way we possibly can," he said.
"We're the only government in the history of this province - think of this - that has never raised a tax. We reduced taxes. We got rid of the sticker fee that people were having to pay every single year."
Warning of the next increase in federal carbon pricing on April 1, but not mentioning the rebate, the premier predicted "the crunch is coming down."
"The feds are going to add to the worst tax ever, the carbon tax, so you're going to be paying 20 cents a litre (on gasoline). They already have 17 cents, another three cents on every litre of gas."
Bethlenfalvy's office said Wednesday it could not comment on what would or would not be in the fall economic statement.
In a video announcing the date of his fall update, the treasurer said "we're keeping costs down for hardworking families by fighting the carbon tax and cutting government taxes and fees."
"But there's more work to do, and we can't afford to go backwards," he said.
Even though the cost to the treasury would be at least $3.2 billion, government officials believe most of the money would be re-injected into the economy by cash-strapped Ontarians.
The payments come as Ford has been musing about calling an election a year ahead of schedule.
According to the most recent Abacus Data monthly tracking poll for the Star, the Tories are at 44 per cent while Bonnie Crombie's Liberals are at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats are at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens are at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians on Oct. 3-10 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, the multi-millionaire scion of a wealthy family, insists he understands how strapped families are and says his government is "looking at cutting costs every which way we possibly can."

Document 354 of 500

'Ontario needs more power:' Ford government wants to boost electricity expansion to meet surging demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-needs-more-power-ford-government-wants/docview/3117055912/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Ontario+needs+more+power%3A%27+Ford+government+wants+to+boost+electricity+expansion+to+meet+surging+demand&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new forecast shows Ontario's electricity needs surging 75 per cent by 2050, sparking the need for a more "ambitious" electricity generation plan to come soon, says Energy Minister Stephen Lecce.
"It is clear today Ontario needs more power," he told a news conference Wednesday, setting the stage for more generation, battery storage and conservation programs in addition to nuclear refurbishments and other expansions of the power system now underway.
The forecast released by the province's Independent Electricity System Operator marks a "quite significant" increase from the previous prediction of a 60 per jump in demand, said Chuck Farmer, the IESO's vice-president of planning, conservation and resource adequacy.
"Everybody is dealing with a sudden surge," Farmer said of agencies like the IESO in other jurisdictions facing higher demand because of electrification from cars to public transit, home heating, steelmaking as it transitions away from coal to arc furnaces, other industries such as electric vehicle battery plants and electricity-hungry data centres using artificial intelligence.
Ontario is expecting 16 more such data centres by 2035 and their forecast demand for power is six times higher than previously thought, said Lecce.
"This is a significant opportunity but it puts pressure on the grid," he added.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner urged Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government to avoid more natural gas-fired power plants, which cause carbon emissions that fuel climate change, in favour of renewable energy.
"My concern is the government's going to choose an expensive, dirty approach, which is ramping up gas plants, which is what they've been doing and makes absolutely no sense when wind, solar and battery storage and water power are far cheaper, far cleaner, can be deployed much faster," he said.
"Let's do it in the cheapest and cleanest way."
Lecce called natural gas plants, often used to meet demand at times of peak usage, "an insurance policy" to keep lights on and factories powered.
Nuclear, which supplies more than half the province's electricity, will remain the "anchor" of the system, he added, noting Ontario's grid is almost 90 per cent emission-free, mainly because of nuclear, hydroelectric power.
The previous energy minister, Todd Smith, signalled in January that the Ford government will seek approval from federal regulators to refurbish four aging 1980s Candu nuclear reactors at Pickering to get another 30 years of electricity out of the power plant just east of Toronto.
While no cost estimate was provided, the project - rejected 15 years ago by a previous Liberal government as too expensive - would cost billions of dollars and take a decade to complete.
Smith dismissed calls to pour that money into wind and solar as the world increasingly moves to renewable sources of electricity, calling nuclear "the responsible, pragmatic move forward."
The province is also planning a massive expansion of the Bruce Nuclear plant on Lake Huron, almost doubling its capacity by adding as many as 4,800 megawatts of electricity. That is enough to power almost five million homes.
CAPTION: Darlington Nuclear Power Plant is currently ramping up to do a decade long refit of all four reactors.

Subject: Electricity; Nuclear power plants

Title: 'Ontario needs more power:' Ford government wants to boost electricity expansion to meet surging demand

Document 355 of 500

Cities to need permission to install bike lanes: Province also pausing the installation of any new bike lanes if lanes of traffic are removed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cities-need-permission-install-bike-lanes/docview/3116941897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cities+to+need+permission+to+install+bike+lanes%3A+Province+also+pausing+the+installation+of+any+new+bike+lanes+if+lanes+of+traffic+are+removed&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario cities would have to seek the province's permission to install new bike lanes that remove lanes of vehicle traffic under upcoming legislation, the transportation minister announced Tuesday.
Too many drivers are stuck in gridlock as a result of bike lanes installed during the COVID-19 pandemic, and the process is "frankly, out of control," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said at a press conference.
"When strategically placed, bike lanes are a vital part of every city, offering residents a safe and reliable way to move around," he said.
"What cities should not be doing, however, is taking away lanes of traffic on our most congested roads."
The first government bill to be introduced when the legislature resumes sitting next week will focus on tackling gridlock, the province said. It is expected to contain a requirement that municipalities receive provincial approval for certain bike lanes and demonstrate the proposed bike lanes won't negatively affect vehicle traffic.
Premier Doug Ford, who lives in the west end of Toronto, has complained about bike lanes that were installed on Bloor Street West, saying the street is jammed and first responders are having a hard time getting through the congestion.
"There's a petition just in Etobicoke for 50,000 people signing to get rid of these bike lanes," he said last month. "You might see four or five bikes as you see traffic lined up all the way down the road for kilometres and kilometres. ... It's an absolute disaster."
Sarkaria, who made his announcement Tuesday with the Bloor bike lanes as a backdrop, said the province is not ordering the removal of those lanes at the moment, but is asking for more data and pausing the installation of any new, similar bike lanes.
A City of Toronto report on an earlier stretch of bike lanes on Bloor, which were added in 2016 as a pilot project, found that collisions and near misses with cyclists decreased, the number of cyclists increased by 56 per cent, customer spending at area businesses increased and vehicle volumes decreased, while drivers' travel time increased.
The city's report said that travel time increased by up to 8 1/2 minutes, depending on the section of Bloor, but after traffic signal adjustments were made, that went down to four minutes during afternoon rush hour.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

national briefs: MP 'traitors' an exaggeration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3116938945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=national+briefs%3A+MP+%27traitors%27+an+exaggeration&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Annual inflation falls to 1.6%
The chances of a half-percentage point interest rate cut by the Bank of Canada became more likely Tuesday after Statistics Canada reported annual inflation fell to1.6 per cent in September. Economist Tu Nguyen of accounting and consultancy firm RSM Canada said she had expected the inflation rate to remain close to the central bank's two per cent target, where it was in August, for a few more months. "This is one of the instances where I'm happy to be wrong," she said.
MP 'traitors'
an exaggeration
It is an irresponsible, "partisan exaggeration" to claim there are traitors or treasonous people sitting in Parliament based on the findings a recent spy watchdog report, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc has told a federal inquiry Tuesday afternoon. "Those are criminal phrases that are not borne out by the evidence and by the work of the police or the security agencies," LeBlanc said during a public hearing of the inquiry into foreign interference. The National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians said in June that some parliamentarians were "semi-witting or witting" participants in the efforts of foreign states to meddle in Canadian politics.

Ontario
Ford wants jobs in life sciences
Ontario is making what a key minister describes as a "hard pivot" to life sciences, as it looks to replicate its aggressive investment in the electric-vehicle sector in a bid to become a world leader in biomanufacturing and health sciences. Premier Doug Ford outlined the second phase of the province's life sciences strategy Tuesday as he announced a $146-million investment in the industry. "

Document 357 of 500

The Freedom Convoy didn't die. It is finding friends among willing politicians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/freedom-convoy-didnt-die-is-finding-friends-among/docview/3117133337/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Freedom+Convoy+didn%27t+die.+It+is+finding+friends+among+willing+politicians&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The greatest danger of the so-called "Freedom Convoy" was not to Ottawa. Yes, the convoy tortured the city for more than a month, and that shouldn't be forgotten, or downplayed. But it passed, even if the lonely leftovers that still hang around to harass politicians remain a smaller symptom of a wider ill.
No, the most dangerous part of the convoy was always its most farcical element, which is that some of the main organizers, who had a history of delusional and hateful comments, aimed to overthrow the government. No matter how supine Ottawa police were, no matter how absent Ontario premier Doug Ford or mayor Jim Watson was, no matter the overlapping paralysis of jurisdictions, we all laughed at that.
Two-and-a-half years later, and elements of the movement are being embraced by politicians more than ever before. What if the convoy is succeeding at changing our governments for the worse?
You could see it coming at the time. Former Saskatchewan premier Brad Wall gave convoy leaders advice, and they met with former CPC leader Andrew Scheer in Regina, and various MPs cheered. Then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole took a half-hearted approach in meeting organizers, and was criticized for it. He was ejected for many reasons, after which interim CPC leader Candace Bergen saw an opportunity, and so did Pierre Poilievre. He glad-handed with convoy folks, and is now the leader of the Conservative party, and quite likely to be our next prime minister, at some point.
Poilievre has dutifully lined up with some of the most deluded members of the public: marching on the day before Canada Day in 2022 with anti-vaccine veteran James Topp, or his private member's bill that would have banned vaccine mandates for travellers and federal employees, marketed in anti-vaccine code words that implied more than COVID vaccines.
But it's worsening. In Alberta, Danielle Smith's government fired Alberta's then chief medical officer of health Deena Hinshaw, which Smith justified by saying of public health, "they shut down the economy, they put on masks, they put on restrictions, and I thought, we're not going to let that happen."
The Alberta Human Rights Act was updated to include the right to refuse a vaccine or to not wear a mask, and Smith plans to do the same with the Alberta Bill of Rights. Her new deputy chief of staff, by the way, owns a restaurant that in 2022 accepted puppy pictures instead of vaccination passports for entry.
And in B.C., Conservative Leader John Rustad crossed into a different place. He has not only told an anti-vaccine group that he regretted getting vaccinated, but in a video unearthed by the indefatigable PressProgress, he was asked about a concept that the most angry and deluded anti-vaccine activists use: Nuremberg 2.0.
"Are you for or against a Nuremberg 2.0?" asked anti-vaccine activist Jedediah Ferguson, making it sound like "Newemberg."
"A do it bigger 2.0, sorry?" asked Rustad, confused.
"Nuremberg 2.0," repeated Ferguson, smiling.
"Nuremberg 2.0," said Rustad, a smile spreading across his face. "Ah, yes. That's probably something that's outside of my scope."
"I know I put you on the hot spot right there, for sure, but I had to ask," said Ferguson.
"No, no, it's fine," said Rustad. "I can say, that's something that's sort of outside the scope in terms of jurisdiction of British Columbia, but we would certainly be participating with other jurisdictions as we look at those sorts of issues."
Nuremberg 2.0 has long been a rallying cry among the most angry anti-vaccine activists: it is about show trials of doctors, politicians and journalists, and punishment ranging from prison to executions. Rustad later claimed to have misunderstood the question. The best you could probably say, based on his reaction and his answer, is that he was pandering to what he sees as his base. After all, in an election full of conspiracists, the most controversial candidate, Brent Chapman, has said there are "probably 45, maybe 50 Freedom people that are running for this party, and I know that some of our media says, oh, they're wack jobs."
Smith and Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe have played footsie with chemtrail conspiracists, but Rustad's answer was a new line crossed, in a party that is way out there. And he could win.
Radicalization tends to go in one direction. The convoy to Ottawa was, at its heart, divorced from reality, even if you could understand some of its energy: As a young mother named Dasha told me in January 2022 at a convoy rally north of Toronto, "I'm just a regular person that wants to go back to normal. Why can't we just call it a day and say this is a regular flu just like any other flu?" She also, as it happened, thought hospitals were lying about being full of patients.
She was wrong. The anti-vaccine radicals are wrong. It was sad, talking to Dasha: she had been fed disinformation under strenuous circumstances, and was clearly vulnerable. She was struggling with the world.
She's far from alone, and now the most unmoored reactions to the pandemic are leaching into governments across the country, and maybe into a federal government that has a habit of turning people who disagree with them into enemies.
The world of radical unreality is biting back, and we need conservative politicians with cognitive clarity and genuine principles beyond partisanship, who can transcend the angriest and most unhinged parts of their base. We face real-world problems, in the same way that we did under the COVID emergency, and we need people who deal with the world as it is.
If that doesn't happen, as under the COVID emergency, we will all suffer.
CAPTION: Police move in to remove protesters from downtown Ottawa near Parliament Hill on Feb. 19, 2022 following weeks of demonstrations.

Subject: Federal employees; COVID-19 vaccines; Political activism; Politicians

Document 358 of 500

Second Liberal MP goes public with call for Justin Trudeau to resign: 'People have had enough'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/second-liberal-mp-goes-public-with-call-justin/docview/3116941843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Second+Liberal+MP+goes+public+with+call+for+Justin+Trudeau+to+resign%3A+%27People+have+had+enough%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A second MP has gone public with a call for Justin Trudeau to step aside from the Liberal leadership ahead of the next election, while another MP says it's time for the caucus to have a tough conversation about leadership in private.
The day after the prime minister dismissed word of a brewing caucus revolt and vowed to lead his party into the campaign, Sean Casey, the member of Parliament for Charlottetown, PEI, told CBC's Power and Politics host David Cochrane that he supports an overwhelming call by his constituents for Trudeau to go.
"My job has always been to project the voice of the people I represent in Ottawa, to be Charlottetown's representative in Ottawa, and not the reverse. And the message that I've been getting loud and clear and more and more strongly as time goes by is that it's time for him to go. And I agree."
Meanwhile, Montreal MP Anthony Housefather told CTV's Power Play that he believes it is time for the Liberals to talk about who will lead the party when Canadians go to the polls in an election expected at the latest in October 2025, but it is a conversation he wishes would not play out in headlines.
"I support whoever is leader in my party at all times," he said. "But that doesn't mean there shouldn't be a robust caucus discussion about who the best person to lead us in the next election is, and that discussion should happen in caucus. It shouldn't happen in the media."
Housefather's comments came at the end of an unrelated interview, after he was asked if he still supports Trudeau.
Until now, only New Brunswick MP Wayne Long had publicly declared his belief it was time for Trudeau to go, although the Star has reported on widespread unease in Liberal ranks about the dismal polls, and lack of party readiness for the next campaign.
More than two dozen caucus members have discussed signing a letter pledging to be part of an effort to force the issue, with some suggesting between 30 and 40 could join the effort. Casey said he had not been a part of that effort.
After the devastating Liberal byelection loss on June 24 of the Toronto-St. Paul's seat, Trudeau made rounds of calls to calm caucus dissent. Casey said he told Trudeau directly on July 5 that while history would be kind to the prime minister, the electorate was no longer listening.
Then came another byelection loss on Sept. 16 in Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun - a riding the Liberals lost to the Bloc Québécois.
MPs had been primed to expect a close call in Montreal, with party officials at the Nanaimo caucus retreat just a week earlier previewing that results would be tight.
However, sources told the Star that MPs also expected a clear response from Trudeau and his team about how the party would recover, and no clear plan has emerged.
On Sunday, the party finally named a new campaign director, Ontario veteran Andrew Bevan, to replace Jeremy Broadhurst, who himself had told Trudeau in August that he did not believe the prime minister could lead the Liberals to a fourth victory.
Casey told CBC that leadership concerns about Trudeau have replaced health care as the number one issue he encounters with voters at the door. "People have had enough. They've tuned him out, and they want him to go. I can't tell you the number of people that have said to me, 'Sean, I'm afraid of Poilievre. I want to vote for you, but I can't.'"
Casey said he intends to run again even if Trudeau remains, but noted the Liberal leader has not yet signed his candidate nomination papers and "I think this interview may have an impact on that."
Casey denied he was lobbying for any other would-be aspirant to Trudeau's job.
"I'm advocating for a leadership change, not for a particular candidate," but Casey said he believes a leadership race would spark new ideas and energy in the party.
"Quite frankly, if Justin Trudeau was to leave, there would be risk," said Casey. "If there was a divisive leadership contest, there would be risk, but the upside of having a new leader is significantly higher than if he stays in my view."
Housefather was one of three Liberal MPs who voted against a contentious pro-Palestine NDP motion and previously threatened to leave the Liberal caucus over the governing party's stance on the Israel-Hamas war and rising antisemitism in Canada.
While Trudeau was away at an international summit last week, growing unease in Liberal ranks snowballed into an effort to formulate a way for Liberal MPs to push Trudeau to a decision to leave. The Liberal caucus never agreed to sign on to rules under the Reform Act that would have allowed a critical mass of MPs to force the question.
Trudeau on Monday responded tersely when asked if he intended to stay at the helm of the party, saying, "Oui. Yes."
The question was asked during an extraordinary news conference following Canada's expulsion of six Indian diplomats believed to be associated with intimidation and violence against Sikh Canadians.
"I had many conversations with members of my team and caucus members over the past number of days, and all those conversations were focused on the well-being and the safety of Canadians faced with these significant security concerns and allegations," Trudeau told reporters.
"The safety of Canadians is the number one thing that any government, that any parliamentarians should be preoccupied with, and that is exactly what we're focused on. There will be time to talk about internal party intrigue at another moment."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau exits his vehicle as he arrives for a news conference on the investigative efforts related to violent criminal activity occurring in Canada with connections to India, on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, onOct. 14, 2024.
