Document 359 of 500

Ford government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-unveils-more-details-on-plan-curb/docview/3116663526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+unveils+more+details+on+plan+to+curb+new+bike+lanes+on+major+roads&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is hitting the brakes on new bike lanes, warning Toronto and other municipalities they'll need provincial approval before taking out lanes of traffic for cars and trucks.
The aim is to keep bike lanes off major thoroughfares like Bloor Street West, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria told reporters Tuesday as he previewed upcoming legislation from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Growth in bike lanes is "out of control," he charged at a pool hall and bar on Bloor near Islington, where local residents and businesses have complained new bike lanes are lightly used, particularly in cold or wet weather, and have slowed traffic.
"We're seeing an explosion in bike lanes, including many that were installed during the pandemic when their impact on traffic was unclear," said Sarkaria, who also announced plans to design new highways for speed limits above 120 km/h and to increase speed limits to 110 km/h on existing ones.
"While strategically placed bike lanes are a critical part of every city ... what cities should not be doing, however, is taking away lanes of traffic on our most congested roads," he added.
"Municipalities are still free to install bike lanes where they do not remove traffic."
There was quick pushback from Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and a prominent cycling lawyer as the Progressive Conservatives prepare to unveil the legislation when MPPs return from an extended summer break next Monday amid speculation about an early provincial election.
"I do not support limiting city powers," Chow said in a statement to the Star.
"It's always better when we work together to get things right," added the mayor, who last week proposed tweaking some bike lanes to ease concerns about overall traffic flow.
"To tackle congestion and keep people safe on city roads, we need all types of transportation. The province should concentrate on their job of finally getting the Eglinton Crosstown and Finch LRT open, which will have a huge impact on congestion."
The legislation would be a blow to Toronto city council's plan, passed over the summer, to add 100 kilometres of bike lanes to major routes over the next three years as part of efforts to reduce vehicle emissions.
Cycling lawyer David Shellnutt accused the province of starting an "us vs. them campaign" dividing road users and making life more dangerous for cyclists in a year that has seen six killed on Toronto streets in addition to deaths in the Belleville, Guelph and Niagara areas.
"Ontarians must see these bike lane attacks from what they are: a tool to distract us from the real problems pressing this province," Shellnutt added in a statement hinting at the RCMP investigation of Ford's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"Even in Toronto, most will recognize that it is the sheer number of cars on the roads ... and maybe the baffling number of construction projects that are actually increasing gridlock."
The legislation, which government sources confirmed will be called the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," comes as Ford's Progressive Conservatives position themselves for an early election, possibly by next spring - a year ahead of the next scheduled provincewide vote in June 2026.
It's expected the bike lane plan, limiting municipal powers, would appeal to suburban voters who rely more heavily on cars to get around.
It's not clear how long it would take the province to render a decision on proposed new bike lanes. Cycling advocates have expressed concerns that the process could effectively halt new lanes.
Advocates maintain that bike lanes encourage people to get out of their cars and onto two wheels at a time of increasing concern about the impact of vehicle exhaust, climate change, the cost of commuting and congestion.
"More people biking, walking or taking public transit means more people not in cars and less gridlock," said Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre).
Sarkaria said existing bike lanes will stay but the government will ask municipalities for data on all bike lanes that have been installed in the last five years.
Toronto Coun. Brad Bradford (Beaches-East York) said the bike lanes on Bloor Street West and Yonge Street should be "removed immediately" because they make it tougher for customers to patronize businesses and cause vehicular traffic to seek shortcuts through neighbourhoods.
Ford, a former city councillor who lives in Etobicoke, has frequently been critical of the expansion of bike lanes and faced criticism in 2018 for meddling in municipal powers by cutting the size of Toronto city council nearly in half in the midst of that year's local election campaign.
In contrast, the premier has repeatedly said municipalities are best placed to decide where to build new housing as the province reaches for a target of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
With files from Ben Cohen
CAPTION: The provincial government is moving to limit municipal powers when it comes to building bike lanes.

Document 360 of 500

Economy doesn't need populism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/economy-doesnt-need-populism/docview/3116541887/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Economy+doesn%27t+need+populism&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's next election is a year away, or perhaps less now that the federal New Democratic Party has withdrawn its support from the governing Liberals.
In announcing the split, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh took aim not just at Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, but also at his alleged shadowy supporters, charging that the Liberal leader is "too beholden to corporate interests."
It was like watching satire, except that good satire requires a grain of truth. Here there was none.
In recent years, the Liberal government has received little applause from business. It hiked taxes on banks, insurance companies and tech companies. It banned the use of replacement workers during strikes in regulated industries, stoking business concern that labour disruptions would become both more likely and more costly. And business leaders raised alarms when this year's federal budget both increased the effective tax rate on capital gains and phased out some tax breaks for new business investment.
These policies came at a time when Canada desperately needs more entrepreneurship and more investment to reverse its declining productivity.
While the prime minister is usually respectful in conversations with business leaders, he's also no stranger to populism. He regularly invokes "fairness" - the idea that businesses and individuals should "pay a little more" to enable spending on housing and other priorities.
There are two problems with this reasoning.
First, he presents a false choice. There is no indication the government has carefully and comprehensively reviewed its spending priorities and its tax policies, and then made strategic choices to incentivize business investment and job creation while maximizing value for taxpayer dollars.
Second, while the government offers good programs to help small businesses grow, there's an attitude among many politicians that small businesses stop being worthy when they become too successful. Canada must make it easier, not harder, for businesses of all sizes to grow by attracting investment and retaining capital and talent.
Across the aisle, the Conservative party enjoys a commanding lead in the polls. Its leader, Pierre Poilievre, has shown a refreshing focus on engaging working-class voters, an area of historic weakness for his party.
On business, however, he has played the populist card as aggressively as the NDP. He argues corporate Canada has been too collegial in pursuing dialogue with government, rather than being more aggressively hostile or running national public relations and advertising campaigns.
It's time for all political leaders to recognize a reality: when it comes to fixing Canada's productivity crisis, we need more partnership and less populism.
The NDP should emulate its provincial cousins, who are far more open to dialogue with business. They have stronger economic policy platforms, which correlate with better electoral track records.
The Liberals badly need a fresh message that will win votes back from the Conservatives. How about a vision of rewarding risk-taking rather than punishing it? How about leaning into free trade and supporting Canada's exporters? How about a comprehensive review of all government spending and taxes to ensure a laserlike focus on investing in people and technology, while attracting and rewarding investment and the productivity, jobs and growth that come with it?
The Conservatives seem poised to form the next government, and they need a governing agenda. While of course Poilievre can keep hammering away at his political rivals, with everyone else he has an opportunity to shift from the clenched fist of opposition to the open hand of a prime minister in waiting. That's where he and his team will find a rich vein of ideas.
At a time when many Canadians are either anxious, angry or both, we need leaders who can calm things down. Listen with empathy. Understand the complexity of our challenges. Act with urgency. Unite us, rather than divide us.
That means working with the nation's business leaders, entrepreneurs, job creators and investors. They are the ones who will create the inclusive, sustainable growth Canadians want, need and deserve.
Daniel Tisch is president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is not shy about playing the populist card when it comes to business, writes Daniel Tisch. But it's time for all political leaders to recognize a reality: when it comes to fixing Canada's productivity crisis, we need more partnership and less populism.Christinne Muschi The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Daniel Tisch toronto star Daniel Tisch is president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.

Document 361 of 500

Nearly two-thirds of Ontarians think Doug Ford must do more to deal with homeless encampments, survey finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nearly-two-thirds-ontarians-think-doug-ford-must/docview/3116324918/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nearly+two-thirds+of+Ontarians+think+Doug+Ford+must+do+more+to+deal+with+homeless+encampments%2C+survey+finds&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The vast majority of Ontarians feel homeless encampments are a "problem" and most believe Premier Doug Ford is not doing enough to deal with them, a new poll suggests.
Abacus Data found 54 per cent think tents in municipal parks are a "big problem," while 30 per cent say they are a "minor problem" and 16 per cent insist they are "not really a problem."
"It is something that signals to people that things aren't working right," said Abacus president David Coletto.
Indeed, 62 per cent feel Ford isn't doing enough to help municipalities deal with encampments, while 17 per cent said the premier is doing enough, five per cent said he's doing "more than enough" and 15 per cent weren't sure.
"This chalks up to a real potential liability for the Progressive Conservatives and for Mr. Ford," said Coletto.
"The public doesn't get a sense that things are getting better," he added.
But Ford's Tories, who are considering an early election next year, remain popular with 44 per cent support - well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians from Oct. 3 through Thursday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The poll was conducted after Ford signalled on Oct. 2 that the province would be taking some unspecified action on homeless tent encampments.
"It's a big problem," conceded the premier, whose office at the legislature is 400 metres south of a small encampment in city-run Queen's Park.
"We've got to find homes for these folks as well, and that's our goal - all three levels of government. But I'll tell you, communities across the province, they're just beside themselves," he said that day.
In Toronto, Dufferin Grove Park, Allen Gardens, Bellevue Square and Clarence Square are among the city parks that are home to large encampments despite concerns from area residents about public safety.
Ford's comments came the week after he had chided tent dwellers to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else," which sparked criticism.
Abacus found that 80 per cent of respondents think governments should "prioritize providing housing, mental health, and addiction services to help individuals transition out of homelessness and find long-term stability."
Only 20 per cent felt political leaders should "prioritize removing encampments and enforcing laws to address public safety and maintain order in communities."
There appears to be plenty of blame to go around - 63 per cent said Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is doing a poor job of handling the encampment crisis with 11 per cent saying he's doing a good job.
Fully 52 per cent felt Ford is doing a poor job on them, with 15 per cent saying he's doing a good job - while 48 per cent said Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow is doing a poor job on the encampments and 13 per cent saying she's doing a good job.
"The public wants to see a compassionate solution," said Coletto.
"There's clearly broad support for doing something."
Asked "which level of government is most responsible to deal with homeless encampments," 43 per cent said Queen's Park, 32 per cent said Ottawa and 25 per cent said municipalities.
Abacus also polled on Ford's proposals for dealing with traffic gridlock in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
While 67 per cent were aware of the premier's scheme to tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough - with 33 per cent unaware - 47 per cent said it was a bad idea while 35 per cent said it was a good idea and 18 per cent didn't know.
On the other hand, 39 per cent would like the province to buy back the long-term private lease on Highway 407 with 18 per cent opposing that and 21 per cent unsure.
"It demonstrates to that core base of supporters that he's listening to them because they are clearly telling him that traffic is a problem," said Coletto, noting respondents are divided on what can reasonably be done.
The premier's Sept. 25 tunnel idea could be a cornerstone pledge in a PC re-election campaign early next year.
Given Ford's current standing in the polls, Coletto said the Tories' plan for a vote one year early makes sense.
"There's no obvious headwind that's having an effect on holding his voting coalition together," the pollster said.
"The federal Liberal brand is not helping Bonnie Crombie," he said, referring to the persistent low polling of the Trudeau Liberals.
"And Marit Stiles just isn't breaking through."
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has indicated that the province would be taking some unspecified action onhomeless tent encampments.

Document 362 of 500

Is Justin Trudeau fated to lose just like Kathleen Wynne'? We asked MPs who were there for the last days of her government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-justin-trudeau-fated-lose-just-like-kathleen/docview/3115765119/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Justin+Trudeau+fated+to+lose+just+like+Kathleen+Wynne%27%3F+We+asked+MPs+who+were+there+for+the+last+days+of+her+government&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Is it déjà vu all over again?
That's a loaded question for the half dozen Liberal MPs who were part of the doomed Kathleen Wynne government that lost to Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives in 2018 and now sit in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal caucus facing Pierre Poilievre's surging Conservatives.
Two of those veteran politicians wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, their silence a telling indication of where things appear to be headed and how history could repeat itself.
Their more (self-described) "optimistic" colleagues prefer to emphasize - not the similarities of the electoral scenarios - but the differences between Queen's Park in 2018 and Ottawa now.
Of the six politicians now in the Commons, only two were able to hang onto their provincial seats in 2018 when the Ford juggernaut reduced an almost 15-year Ontario Liberal dynasty to a tiny rump without official party status in the legislature: community and social services minister Michael Coteau and solicitor general Marie-France Lalonde.
Charles Sousa, who had been finance minister, lost his seat as did attorney-general Yasir Naqvi, health minister Helena Jaczek, and Etobicoke Centre MPP Yvan Baker.
Jaczek - who sources said had been outspoken in Wynne's cabinet in the year leading up to the 2018 Liberal defeat and warned about the premier's unpopularity at the doorsteps due to soaring electricity bills - politely declined to comment when asked to reflect on what this period in federal politics feels like.
Jaczek was dropped from Trudeau's cabinet last summer after announcing she will not seek re-election in Markham-Stouffville whenever the next federal election is held.
Baker declined to weigh in, too.
But Coteau, Sousa, Naqvi and Lalonde insist the federal Liberals are not doomed to the same fate as the provincial party, stressing Trudeau is an effective campaigner and they still have time to turn things around.
In an interview, Coteau, who was one of Wynne's top ministers, said the comparisons between the former premier and the prime minister miss the mark.
"The players are different, the personalities are different, the time is different," he said, noting provincial and national issues also differ.
However, there is one key similarity, he said. After a certain time, all governments accumulate "challenges."
With Wynne - who had revived the provincial Grits after succeeding Dalton McGuinty in 2013 and won a surprise majority victory the following year - her predecessor's decision to cancel two gas-fired power plants in Oakville and Mississauga before the 2011 election ultimately hurt her in 2018, despite what her ex-cabinet colleagues say was a good governing record.
For Coteau, the weight of 15 years of Liberal rule bedevilled Wynne's second election bid from the get-go.
"A narrative hardened, and it was hard to change the talking points even when you did good things," said Coteau, pointing to Sousa's feat under Wynne of a balanced budget in 2017.
In contrast, he argued that the animosity to Trudeau is not so baked in that it can't be overcome with time, economic improvements, a good platform, and a strong campaign.
"I'm not saying the world is all rosy, but I think that the cement hasn't dried fully in regards to where Canadians want to go, what they're thinking about. I think there's still the ability to have a discussion," about "an alternative to the Pierre Poilievre approach," said Coteau, whose Don Valley East seat is disappearing in redistribution so he will be running in neighbouring Scarborough-Woburn.
Two of his former Ontario cabinet colleagues, not now in Ottawa, said they feel the antipathy toward Trudeau mirrors what they heard about Wynne six years.
"It may even be worse. You see these 'F--- Trudeau' signs everywhere; there were never 'F--- Wynne' signs and flags on people's trucks. People were mad at Kathleen about their hydro bills, but now it's unhinged toward the PM," said one ex-provincial minister who spoke on condition they not be named.
Another, whom the Star agreed not to identify so they could freely discuss internal deliberations, said the federal Liberals should be thinking of doing what the provincial Grits regret not doing: saving the furniture by switching leaders instead of allowing everyone to go down with the ship.
"Even if (Wynne) had stepped down (in 2017), we would have lost, but we wouldn't have gone down to seven or eight seats," said the retired second former minister. "We could have lived to fight another day. The OLP didn't do that; the LPC still could."
Wynne told the Star this summer the decision by a political leader to stay or go is a bruising and deeply personal one, and advised Trudeau to contemplate why he wanted to stay.
Naqvi said in an interview the federal Liberals' nine years in office is a big reason why "we are behind in the polls," but added, "I don't think it's lost."
"The next little bit matters a lot. The campaign matters a lot. I ran in 2011. It was my second election. I ran in 2018. Even in talking to people at the door, to me it feels more like 2011 than 2018," he said, referring to the McGuinty Liberals' third consecutive election victory.
By then, the premier had been in power eight years compared to Trudeau's nine.
McGuinty had weathered the 2008 economic crisis and recession and had brought in policies that Naqvi emphasized were good for the province - like the harmonized sales tax - but were "not popular" because some prices went up.
"We were able to pull that campaign and win that election, a minority government, by one seat," he said, referring to the Liberals winning 53 seats in what was then a 107-member legislature.
Debilitated by the gas-plants scandal, his popularity plummeting, McGuinty prorogued the legislature in October 2012 and resigned as leader, triggering the leadership race won by Wynne three months later.
Naqvi conceded he does hear similar comments at the door now as he heard when campaigning under McGuinty, when voters said "we love how you're doing but I think maybe it's time for you to change the leader."
Back then and now, Naqvi said, he shifts the focus onto the Liberals' opponent, insisting that in Ottawa Centre voters at the door are turned off by the Conservative leader.
As Ontario Liberal president, Naqvi oversaw the expedited leadership contest that replaced McGuinty. He said the federal party needs to do three things to turn the ship around: draw a clear contrast and show "how dangerous Poilievre is for the country"; produce a policy prescription on affordability that shows people "we get it," and "articulate a vision for the future."
Sousa said in an interview he sees "similarities" in that Ontario voters in 2018 faced a flood of "misinformation" in Conservative partisan rhetoric, including from groups like Canada Proud and Ontario Proud. Sousa argued Poilievre and his supporters are following the same playbook, "creating fear, division and promoting some of the hate that exists, especially in the toxic nature or the anonymity that exists, through social media. It feeds the beast."
He believes another "similarity" is that Poilievre, like Ford, cannot be trusted to leave social programs intact despite his pre-writ rhetoric. That is part of what Sousa said motivates him to run again despite the dismal poll showings for Trudeau. Sousa cited other personal priorities - to boost economic growth, protect national unity, and ensure Canada's voice is heard on the world stage - and why he believes there is a path to win under Trudeau.
"The prime minister is very competitive. He realizes that things could be in jeopardy - some of the programs have been put in place. And I believe he is trying to do what's best for the benefit of others, whereas Poilievre is using some of these issues as a platform to benefit himself."
Lalonde, in an interview, said she is "100 per cent" behind Trudeau and doesn't think there are parallels to be drawn between Wynne circa 2018 and Trudeau now.
"I don't think it's fair," she said. "For me, it's not about the polls. For me, it's about the delivery. My government wants to continue to support Canadians," she said. She intends to focus on the needs of her riding, but says she is not running a "local campaign. I'm fighting Pierre Poilievre."
All campaigns, she added, are "tough." In 2014, "no one thought I was going to win ... I won in 2018 ... And you know, (the) campaign matters."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau responds to a question during a news conference in Vientiane, Laos on Oct. 11, 2024.

Former public safety minister Blair tells inquiry he was not aware of CSIS warrant delays

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-public-safety-minister-blair-tells-inquiry/docview/3115717927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+public+safety+minister+Blair+tells+inquiry+he+was+not+aware+of+CSIS+warrant+delays&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former public safety minister Bill Blair told a federal inquiry Friday he had no knowledge about delays in approving a spy service warrant in 2021 that may have included references to people in his own government.
A commission of inquiry into foreign interference has heard it took 54 days for the Canadian Security Intelligence Service warrant application to be approved by Blair.
The average turnaround time for such applications is four to 10 days.
Michelle Tessier, CSIS deputy director of operations during the period, told the inquiry in previous testimony there was frustration with the delay, though there was no concern about interference of any sort or pushback from the minister's office.
In his testimony, Blair - who is now minister of national defence - said the existence of the warrant wasn't brought to his attention until he saw an entry in his calendar that he would need to be briefed on a confidential matter by CSIS.
"It was indicated on my calendar it was for the purposes of a warrant, but no information was provided in a non-secure setting about what that warrant was about," Blair said in his testimony Friday.
He didn't know about the content of the warrant until he got that briefing, 54 days after the CSIS director and deputy public safety minister asked for his authorization. He signed it the same day.
A past report in the Globe and Mail and a lawyer at the inquiry have suggested the subject of the warrant was Michael Chan, an influential Liberal and former Ontario minister. But federal officials have refused to confirm who CSIS hoped to surveil through the warrant, citing national security concerns.
Blair said he was briefed on the matter several months before CSIS asked him to approve the warrant, but he had no idea at that time where the investigation was headed.
This week, Blair's former chief of staff, Zita Astravas, faced questions about whether she tried to delay the warrant in order to protect connections in the Liberal government and party.
Blair's chief of staff was briefed about the situation 13 days after the initial request for the minister's approval was made, but Blair said no information about that briefing was passed on to him.
Blair said he never felt conflicted about approving the warrant; he simply didn't know it existed.
"I can't approve something I didn't know anything about," the minister told the inquiry.
"No one suggested to me that there were any difficulties bringing those matters forward to me in a proper way."
Blair said he still doesn't know why it took 54 days for the warrant to come to his attention.
Once he got the information, he said he didn't share any information with the prime minister, the Prime Minister's Office or anyone outside of the secure briefing.
The COVID-19 pandemic had a major affect on the flow of information in Blair's office, he explained.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Minister of National Defence Bill Blair appears as a witnessat the Foreign Interference Commission in Ottawa on Friday.Spencer Colby The Canadian Press

Document 364 of 500

Trudeau government's pharmacare bill is now law. What now?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-governments-pharmacare-bill-is-now-law/docview/3115819500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+pharmacare+bill+is+now+law.+What+now%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh refused to say Friday whether his party will help the Liberal minority government survive long enough to ink pharmacare deals with the provinces, as Ontario indicated it is still waiting for "any details" about the promised program.
On Parliament Hill, Singh hailed how legislation underpinning a national pharmacare program became law Thursday night, describing it as a victory that New Democrats have fought to achieve for generations. Singh claimed the legislation never would have passed without his NDP using its influence in Parliament to push the Liberals to act through the parliamentary alliance that his party killed in early September.
But he was less clear about how the NDP would use that influence now, despite urging the government to sign deals to provide the coverage as soon as possible.
The Liberals have said the program - budgeted at almost $1.5 billion over five years - will provide government-paid coverage for diabetes medication and birth control, and could expand in the future.
"Our focus is on forcing this Liberal government to get those deals signed," Singh said.
He declined to say several times whether he would keep the government in power to make sure that happens or try and topple them if it doesn't. With no direct negotiations with Trudeau at the moment, Singh said he is simply making his desire public.
"I want to see people that have free medication when it comes to diabetes, and free birth control," he said.
Earlier Friday, federal Health Minister Mark Holland said that formal negotiations with provinces can start now that the pharmacare bill has passed, but that "informal" talks have already been going on.
Holland told reporters Friday that he's confident Ottawa can sign deals with all provinces by next spring, describing talks that have happened so far as "very" positive.
"It's time to start signing deals with provinces," said Holland.
"This is an important opportunity to make sure the people who need medication will get it.
One province - British Columbia - has already signed a memorandum of understanding with Ottawa, agreeing to work together on a program to cover diabetes medication, birth control and hormone replacement therapy for menopause.
Alberta and Quebec have said they want to opt out of the new program but receive their share of the money. And Ontario is still waiting for details about the program from Ottawa, according to a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
Jones said in February that the province was "waiting for details" on the program and how it "matches or conflicts" with Ontario's existing coverage system.
"Ontario, like many provinces, has yet to receive any details on the federal government's proposal," said Jones's spokesperson Hannah Jensen by email on Friday.
"Instead of calling on the provinces to strike a deal with no detail, the federal government should focus on working collaboratively with provinces to adequately support health-care services across the country."
The future of the promised national pharmacare program is also unclear, since the Liberal minority government - without the guaranteed support of the New Democrats - could fall if the opposition Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and NDP band together to trigger an election.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has criticized the program, arguing it would be unfair to unions that fought for private coverage to have their prescription drug plans replaced by a government-paid "single-payer" alternative.
On Friday, Holland described the plan for government-paid coverage of diabetes medication and birth control as a "pilot" for single-payer pharmacare. The Liberals still haven't decided if the "end state" of a broader program will be a single-payer government scheme, he said.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh speaks at a news conference on pharmacare, in the Foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, on Oct. 11, 2024.

Subject: Provinces; Hormone replacement therapy; Diabetes; Prescription drug plans; Birth control; Decades

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Toronto police to reveal details of new helicopters to combat auto theft this morning

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-police-reveal-details-new-helicopters/docview/3115485072/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+police+to+reveal+details+of+new+helicopters+to+combat+auto+theft+this+morning&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police are about to announce details of new provincial air support to help combat surging automobile crime in the city.
Chief Myron Demkiw and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner will provide the update at 11 a.m. Friday at Downsview Airport. The conference will be streamed on Youtube.
Premier Doug Ford had previously pledged $134 million in funding for five new helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa, in a bid to stem an escalating auto theft crisis in the cities.
According to his plan, two helicopters will be purchased for the Ontario Provincial Police to work with the Ottawa and Toronto police, and three more will be purchased and operated by Durham, Halton and Peel police.
The new Greater Toronto Area Joint Air Support Unit (JASU) will help hinder crimes including auto theft, carjackings, street racing and impaired driving, Ford said during the announcement in late September.
Ontario's spring budget had previously provided enough funding to lease four helicopters, but the new money will allow the province to outright buy five choppers and cover ongoing operating costs.
Experts previously told the Star's Queens Park reporter Kristin Rushowy the move is "not the end all, be all," and that little research has been done in Canada into whether helicopters actually deter theft.
But having eyes in the skies can help in tracking stolen cars or criminals without having to resort to dangerous high-speed chases.
These types of aircraft are used by police agencies in major cities around the world can be "a highly valuable asset to put at the disposal of law enforcement" - especially given Toronto's notorious gridlock, Christian Leuprecht, a professor at both Queen's University and Royal Military College of Canada in Kingston, told Rushowy.
For their part, GTA police rejoiced at the news.
"Equipped with the latest technology, the helicopters will help keep Toronto safe from violent carjackings," Demkiw said at the time.
Peel Regional Police Chief Nishan Duraiappah also praised the "monumental investment," adding that it will "provide much-needed support to our officers and help them do the difficult work they do every day to enhance community safety."
CAPTION: Toronto police are expected to announce details of new provincial helicopters Friday morning, after Doug Ford pledged $134 million for their purchase.

Document 366 of 500

Pierre Poilievre supports mandatory drug rehab for some children, prisoners

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-supports-mandatory-drug-rehab/docview/3115577233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+supports+mandatory+drug+rehab+for+some+children%2C+prisoners&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Children and prison inmates who are "incapable of making decisions for themselves" should be subject to mandatory drug rehabilitation programs, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Thursday.
"I believe, for children and for prisoners who are behind bars, there should be mandatory drug treatment when they are found to be incapable of making decisions for themselves," Poilievre said Thursday during a campaign-style stop at a Toronto synagogue.
"For adults, again, I'm still doing a lot of research on how that would work."
Poilievre had previously raised the idea of involuntary treatment during a July news conference in London, Ont., although he acknowledged there that "I don't know if you can take someone off the street that has not committed a prison offence and successfully rehabilitate them."
While he expressed support Thursday for involuntary rehabilitation programs targeting some children and prison inmates, Poilievre did not say whether a Conservative government would attempt to pass legislation to set them up.
Health care, including most addiction and drug treatment services, is the domain of provincial and territorial governments, while the federal government provides funding.
Provincial law in Ontario requires medical or psychiatric assessment when a person doesn't consent to treatment, with reviews at regular intervals.
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms also contains legal protections for individuals against involuntary detention or treatment through clauses like Section 7, which guarantees "life, liberty and security of the person," and Section 15, which guarantees equal protection under the law.
Poilievre also used Thursday's news conference to blame Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for policies that he says are fuelling Canada's current drug crisis.
"We need to defund the unsafe government supply of drugs that the Trudeau and NDP regimes are pushing on the population," he said, referring to safe-supply services that Health Canada says "can help prevent overdoses, save lives, and connect people who use drugs to other health and social services."
At her own news conference in Toronto, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said "we need to take a science-based approach ... and we also need to take a public safety-based approach" when contemplating mandatory treatment policies.
"We have to be sure people get the treatment they need, and sometimes a person can be too sick to really recognize what treatment they need," she said, calling for an approach "led by compassion, led by a recognition that these are our sons and daughters, our brothers and sisters."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh echoed Freeland's calls for a compassionate, science-based approach to drug and addiction policy.
He said Thursday that the Conservatives have approached addiction policy with a "lack of awareness of the experts, a lack of recognition of the evidence."
Poilievre's latest comments did little to close a gap between the federal Tories and their provincial counterparts on addiction treatment policy.
"I have concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes we want," Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said following Poilievre's comments in July.
The Ontario government recently banned safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and daycares, which will result in the shuttering 10 of the 17 sites across the province.
In B.C., where voters head to the polls on Oct. 19, NDP Leader David Eby has committed to providing involuntary care for those struggling with overlapping addictions, mental illness and brain injuries. In New Brunswick, Progressive Conservative Leader Blaine Higgs said his party would table a bill on involuntary treatment if it is re-elected on Oct. 21. In Alberta, Premier Danielle Smith promised in 2023 to implement involuntary treatment.
Ontario's Big City Mayors caucus, a coalition of leaders from the province's largest municipalities cities, will vote Oct. 18 on whether to endorse a resolution that calls for involuntary treatment legislation.
With files from Mark Ramzy and Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a news conference at a synagogue in Toronto on Oct. 10, 2024.

Subject: Press conferences; Charter of Rights-Canada; Addictions; Prisoners; Prime ministers; Substance abuse treatment

Document 367 of 500

'Doug Ford needs to come clean' about Greenbelt scandal, opposition says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-needs-come-clean-about-greenbelt/docview/3115061883/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Doug+Ford+needs+to+come+clean%27+about+Greenbelt+scandal%2C+opposition+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the RCMP's investigation into the Greenbelt scandal enters its second year, Premier Doug Ford's political rivals say he "needs to come clean about his role" in the $8.28-billion land swap scheme.
In the wake of revelations in the Star that at least eight of Ford's current and former aides have already been interviewed by the Mounties, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "there is still so much we don't know about this mess."
The premier, who has not yet met with police, has insisted "we have nothing to hide" about his aborted plan to open up 7,400 acres of protected Greenbelt land to housing development, and apologized for the fiasco last year.
Stiles isn't buying that.
"Reports that the RCMP is investigating potential bank accounts in Europe show that the extent of this corruption may be bigger than anyone thought," she said, citing an exclusive story in the Star to mark Thursday's first anniversary of the launch of the criminal probe.
"An apology is not enough, Doug Ford needs to come clean about his role in this scheme to rob Ontarians of the Greenbelt to enrich a wealthy few," the New Democrat said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "the RCMP is focused on the premier's inner sanctum because he has been at the heart of this scandal since day one.
"Doug Ford isn't in it for you. He's given away billions of your money to his rich buddies while people struggle to find a family doctor, classroom sizes balloon and the cost of living skyrockets," said Crombie.
"I have the same question for Doug Ford that the RCMP has been asking ... Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?"
The Star reported Wednesday that members of the RCMP elite "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the branch that looks into political crimes and corruption, have been peppering interviewees with that query for months.
Armed with internal email and text messages they have gathered, the detectives have hinted that they may need to access banking records in Europe as part of their probe.
They have been interviewing current and former Progressive Conservative staffers and senior bureaucrats. The PC party is paying the Tories' legal fees and the tab is already approaching seven figures.
The RCMP's 2021 annual report noted the average length of time of a federal police investigation is 1.6 years, meaning the Mounties could wrap up their work next spring. That might impact Ford's timetable for a potential election a year or more ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to recount private conversations, say the RCMP has been concentrated on the premier's inner circle instead of the municipal affairs minister's office, which was initially blamed for the debacle.
The Mounties have not yet scheduled interviews with former municipal affairs minister Steve Clark or his then-chief of staff Ryan Amato, who left their posts in late summer 2023 over the imbroglio.
Both have denied any wrongdoing and are expected to meet with the RCMP later this year.
Thirteen months ago, Ford scrapped the Greenbelt land-swap scheme after separate reports by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk and integrity commissioner J. David Wake concluded that Tory-connected developers were "favoured" in an unusual process supposedly led by Amato.
Interviewees say Wake's 166-page report appears to be serving as blueprint for the detectives.
The Star is not identifying all of those interviewed by the RCMP because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling police.
So far, the line of questioning centres on the organizational reporting chart in the premier's office.
But investigators have also expressed interest in a 2020 trip to Las Vegas taken by Amin Massoudi, who was then Ford's principal secretary, with MPP Kaleed Rasheed, Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Jae Truesdell, who would later be hired as premier's housing policy adviser.
In September 2023, Rasheed left cabinet and the PC caucus after making what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner incorrect information about the date of the Vegas visit.
The well-liked Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP has yet to meet with the Mounties, but has maintained he did nothing wrong except inadvertently misleading Wake.
Truesdell, who was also forced from his government post despite being a private citizen at the time of the jaunt, has spoken to police. He's maintained he didn't do anything wrong.
Rehmatullah, a guest at the premier's daughter's wedding, owned stakes in two parcels of land totalling about 23 acres that were removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
It is not known whether Massoudi, now a private-sector lobbyist, has met with the RCMP yet.
He is a close friend and long-time adviser to the Ford family.
Last fall, the premier's office said it cut "formal" ties with him due to the Greenbelt affair.
In November 2022, a joint Star and Narwhal investigation found eight of the 15 parcels of Greenbelt land initially rezoned for development had been purchased in the four years after the Tories were elected in 2018.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Marit Stiles speaks in Welland, Ont., on Oct. 7, 2024.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals; Interviews

Location: Europe

Document 368 of 500

Where do the 75,000 Hamiltonians with no family doctor live in the city?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-do-75-000-hamiltonians-with-no-family/docview/3115061647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+do+the+75%2C000+Hamiltonians+with+no+family+doctor+live+in+the+city%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Roughly 75,000 Hamiltonians don't have a family doctor, the organization in charge of recruiting physicians to the city estimates.
Increasing access to primary care is a significant challenge considering the majority of family doctors recruited to Hamilton in the last fiscal year simply replaced those who left.
The worst shortage in the city is in Glanbrook - including Mount Hope and Binbrook - with only three family doctors practicing in the rural area. It is followed by the shortfall on Hamilton Mountain.
In total, Hamilton needs about 47 more family physicians - 22 of them in Glanbrook and 12 on the Mountain.
"We do not have enough family providers in our community and we are not graduating enough," Melissa McCallum, executive director of the Greater Hamilton Health Network (GHHN), told the city's public health committee Sept. 30. "This is not a problem unique to us just in Hamilton ... We need all hands on deck at all levels of government."
In fact, the doctor shortage has reached the point that the Ontario Medical Association (OMA) held a virtual town hall in Hamilton on Monday as part of a provincial tour to raise alarm about the "crisis" in primary care. The OMA estimates more than 11 per cent of Hamiltonians don't have a family doctor.
"Without a family doctor, health care is becoming more and more inaccessible," OMA president Dr. Dominik Nowak said at the town hall. "We're not seeing the changes that we need for this crisis to improve."
MPP Sandy Shaw said at the town hall that she is getting more and more calls to her NDP Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas office from constituents "stressed and concerned about access to primary care" in the city.
"People don't realize how desperate the situation has become until they are faced with it themselves," Shaw said. "Absolutely, we are at a crisis point ... and it needs to be underscored that everyone in Ontario deserves to have access to reliable primary care."
Ontario Health Team GHHN has taken over the task of physician recruitment from the city and managed to attract 26 new family doctors to Hamilton from April 1, 2023, to March 31, 2024.
However, 23 family physicians left during that time - nine retired, seven moved their practice to a different city, six stopped doing traditional family medicine for a different type of practice and one died.
As a result, Hamilton had a net gain of just three family physicians last fiscal year to bring the total to 366 citywide.
"The new docs are actively avoiding family practice. It's seen as a nonviable business model because of chronic underfunding," Nowak said. "We won't be able to recruit our way out in a system where we're not valuing primary care and family practice in the first place. We need to start with that and then when we recruit people they'll actually stay in family practice."
Shaw put some of the blame for the recruitment and retention challenges on the working conditions of family doctors that Nowak said has left them "buried in red tape" with not enough time for patients.
GHHN had to recruit 45 temporary family physicians last fiscal year to cover leaves and vacations as well as prevent burnout.
"We cannot underestimate the strain," Shaw said. "They're under immense pressure now. People are showing up sicker than before with complicated issues."
One of the biggest barriers GHHN has faced in recruitment is that family doctors want to practice in teams. A $20-million proposal by GHHN would have given all area family doctors access to team-based care, but it received just $2.2 million in one-time provincial funding in March.
"I would like to see more investment. This is a massive area of concern," McCallum said. "Gone are the days of solo physicians opening up a practice on their own. We're seeing younger graduates wanting to be part of a team along with other multidisciplinary professionals."
To combat the shortages, the province has expanded the number of medical school and residency spots in 2022 and 2023. In addition, a new medical school will open in 2025 at Toronto Metropolitan University (TMU) in Brampton and in 2028 at York University in Vaughan. These programs will have an emphasis on training family doctors.
But many of the Hamiltonians who joined the town hall asked about whether more internationally trained doctors could fill in the city's primary care gaps.
"With the deficit of over 3,000 doctors (in Ontario), we're not going to solve that by graduating new doctors alone," OMA executive vice-president Craig DuHamel said at the town hall. "We have to bring physicians in who have been educated internationally,"
Last year, Ontario created a streamlined route for internationally trained doctors to get licensed called Practice Ready Ontario. But both DuHamel and Nowak say more needs to be done.
"The other big pool of doctors that we have are Canadians who went internationally for school and there is such a big opportunity to bring them home and bring them back to caring for our communities," Nowak said. "Our residency program options for international grads, even if they're Canadian, are very limited, so that's what we're advocating for."
The shortage is a significant issue because family physicians are the foundation and gateway to care - they're trusted by their patients, know their history, provide preventative care and help them navigate the health system.
"It really matters that people have a family doctor," Nowak said. "If you have a family doctor, you end up getting more preventive care including cancer screening. For seniors ... that could mean the difference between breaking a hip and not if you're on the right medications proactively. It means fewer emergency department visits, fewer and shorter hospitalizations. People actually live longer if they have a family doctor and they cost the health-care system less money."
Is your community short doctors?
The GHHN provided a snapshot of family doctor recruitment in different parts of Hamilton in a report to the city's public health committee on Sept. 30.
Ancaster
Short six physicians since 2022. There are 26 active family doctors in Ancaster. The GHHN says doctors like the community's "desirable housing and education ... but the relatively high cost of housing continues to act as potential barrier to recruitment."
Dundas
Surplus of four doctors. It is one of two areas of the city that has too many family doctors, with 22 practicing in Dundas. In fact, one of the issues with recruitment is fewer patients are looking for a doctor compared to other parts of the city so it would take longer to build up a practice.
"Family medicine coverage in Dundas remains strong," the report states. "Physicians cite Dundas' small-town charm, quaint housing and access to greenspace among their top reasons for selecting this community."
Flamborough (including Waterdown and Carlisle)
Short seven physicians, which is an improvement from nine in 2022. Keeping up with population growth is one of the main challenges in this area that has 27 family doctors. The population increased by nearly 10 per cent from 2016 to 2021 compared to nearly 7.5 per cent across the city.
"Physicians cite desirable family housing and proximity to Burlington/Oakville as their primary reasons for selecting Flamborough (and in particular Waterdown)," GHHN states.
Glanbrook (including Mount Hope and Binbrook)
Short 22 doctors - the worst shortfall in the city. It has left many patients driving to Hamilton Mountain and Stoney Creek for primary care as there are only three physicians practicing in Glanbrook. The gaps are long-standing considering the community was short 23 physicians in 2022 and can't access provincial incentives to attract doctors that are available to other rural areas. GHHN says "population growth has exploded" by 17.5 per cent between 2016 and 2021.
"Other challenges include a lack of established clinic space suitable for the creation of large group practices, competition from other rural communities in Ontario ... and a relatively small pool of candidates interested in more rural practice."
Hamilton Mountain
Short 12 physicians, which is down from 17 in 2022. The community is up to 101 family doctors, partly because of lower city practices relocating to the Mountain.
"Access to primary care coverage is improving on the Hamilton Mountain," the report says. "Physicians cite access to suitable group practice space, the ability to build a roster, and access to amenities like shopping and restaurants as their primary reasons for practicing on the Hamilton Mountain."
Lower city
Surplus of five doctors despite a net decrease since 2022 to 140 physicians practicing in the lower city.
"Despite the overall strength in primary care coverage in this area, pockets of underserviced areas remain," the GHHC states. "Physicians often cite the ability to take over an established patient roster and access to priority patient populations as their reason for choosing to practice in lower Hamilton. Physicians who have left this area typically cite traffic/congestion, lack of space with sufficient parking, and the opportunity to practice in newer (often larger) spaces elsewhere in the city."
Stoney Creek
Short eight doctors - a slight improvement from a shortfall of nine in 2022. However, the population has grown by more than 10 per cent, which is expected to put an increasing strain on the 47 doctors practicing in Stoney Creek.
"Primary care shortages in the area are expected to increase ... due to rapid population growth," the report states. "Physicians often cite desirable (and relatively affordable) housing and access to amenities as primary reasons for locating in Stoney Creek."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Sandy Shaw, NDP MPP for Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas, said "people don't realize how desperate the situation has become" until they find themselves with no family doctor.Hamilton needs 47 more family physicians.

Subject: Health care access; Public health; Primary care; Patients; Population growth; Recruitment; Shortages; Rural areas

Business indexing term: Subject: Recruitment Shortages

Document 369 of 500

Review of children's aid societies launched: Ontario's premier suggests program being financially mismanaged, union calls it a 'smokescreen'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/review-childrens-aid-societies-launched/docview/3114752881/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Review+of+children%27s+aid+societies+launched%3A+Ontario%27s+premier+suggests+program+being+financially+mismanaged%2C+union+calls+it+a+%27smokescreen%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario launched a review of the province's 37 non-Indigenous children's aid societies Wednesday after Premier Doug Ford suggested they are being financially mismanaged, but the unions representing CAS workers say it is a mere "smokescreen."
Officials with the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services say the review will look at issues such as the quality of protection the societies provide and their finances.
As part of a background briefing Wednesday, they provided graphs that show annual funding for the sector steadily increased over the past decade to $1.7 billion this year, even as the number of children in care declined.
Ford indicated last week that his government was doing an audit, as he railed against "nightmare stories about the abuse of taxpayers' money." Officials said the process to look for an outside contractor to conduct the review was starting on Wednesday.
"I've heard stories of some of these agencies, they're working in Taj Mahals, they're paying rent, $100,000 for rent, that the managers are giving themselves a bonus," Ford said in response to a question at an unrelated news conference.
"All those managers giving yourself a bonus, not worrying about the kids, I'm coming for you."
The Ontario Association of Children's Aid Societies said it was disappointed Ford did not address the question of what the government was doing about reports of children's aid societies housing kids in hotels or offices because placements weren't available.
There are systemic barriers to providing services for kids with complex needs, the association wrote in a statement following Ford's news conference.
"This type of work is intensive, demanding significant time and resources," the group wrote.
"Additionally, the types of cases child welfare agencies encounter are becoming more and more complex. In a recent survey, 39 child welfare agencies reported that the number of children or youth requiring high-cost care (i.e., over $200,000 per year) has seen a significant increase over the past three years, doubling from 174 in 2021-2022 to 354 in 2023-2024."
Ministry officials who provided the background briefing on the review said societies' deficits are continuing to rise and the audit will examine the underlying issues and possible solutions.
The officials, who spoke on condition they not be identified, said the review will focus on service quality, consistency of services, out-of-home placements, finances, executive compensation, staffing models, cost structures, capital assets and integration with community supports.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Burlington council too small for growing city

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/burlington-council-too-small-growing-city/docview/3114752361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Burlington+council+too+small+for+growing+city&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Protesters here and abroad protest every "injustice" imaginable, even erecting blockades that affect neighbours. I just don't understand all the unrest.
One group starts a war and it escalates out of control. Will we be dragged into a Third World War? Dangerous egotists seek victory at any cost, ignoring impacts on their countrymen and the lives of those under attack. Too bad, innocents! Just collateral damage?
Imagine leaving a home, kids in tow, fleeing to God knows where (whoever will take you in?) all because despots harbour delusions of grandeur. And this at a time when there's a global housing shortage. How traumatic!
Speaking of egotists, our southern neighbour's election features an ex-president con-man, felon and consummate liar, who leads a cult of evangelicals who seemingly have overlooked their dedication to the Ten Commandments.
With a federal election looming here, it appears Justin Trudeau's "best before" date has expired. Pierre Poilievre's negativity scares me so much I could never support him. His recent performance in the House of Commons demonstrates his inability to act as the unifying leader so sorely needed today. These are our leaders?
This week, Burlington had an early update by its consultant on council size, composition and ward boundaries for the 2026 election. Debates on this from both outside and inside council (where turf protection seemed to be the top criterion) have taken place. Public consultation is underway.
Unfortunately elections are popularity contests, not ability contests. With only seven council members locally, absenteeism causes problems. With two absent, a majority of only three can make decisions for a city of close to 200,000!
The "strong mayors" system gives mayors veto power over council on some items, a huge democratic deficit. The workload is high and some of the 63 ABCs, (agencies, boards and commissions) require extensive involvement. There really should be minimum qualifications to run for council today.
Budget - actually, pre-budget - presentations have been making the rounds. The actual budget is proposed by the mayor (due Nov. 4) under this undemocratic "strong mayors" system from Premier Doug Ford. Pundits say the objective was to shift blame from the province for cutting cities' revenue sources to councils, because apparently Ford wants an early provincial election. Local taxpayers are angry at tax hikes and may well take their revenge on councils.
Our tone-deaf councillors approved the 2025 meeting calendar, despite two delegations in opposition. It mimics the 2024 one with one exception: meetings on development proposals. This past year most were held at 6:30 p.m. They will be at 9:30 a.m. instead. But there's flexibility (too bureaucratic to access). Some people can't attend day meetings. Coun. Lisa Kearns tried to get evening council meetings, but she alone supported her motion.
Staff will review establishing a resiliency fund, available to flooding victims. But here's an interesting situation. A resident experienced ankle-deep water in their laundry room on July 15 as a result of the next-door tenant changing his grading significantly and building a platform. The city agreed, and has made several attempts to contact both the tenant and homeowner (who purportedly owns several homes). Neither responded. The city is pursuing options, but this is mid-October! Does this resident have to worry all winter, with its snow, rain, freezing and thawing?
Joan Little, a freelance contributing columnist for the Spec,
isa former Burlington alderperson and Halton councillor.

Document 371 of 500

Where do the 75,000 with no family doctor live in the city?: Hamilton in need of about 47 more family physicians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/where-do-75-000-with-no-family-doctor-live-city/docview/3114752276/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+do+the+75%2C000+with+no+family+doctor+live+in+the+city%3F%3A+Hamilton+in+need+of+about+47+more+family+physicians&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Roughly 75,000 Hamiltonians don't have a family doctor, the organization in charge of recruiting physicians to the city estimates.
Increasing access to primary care is a significant challenge considering the majority of family doctors recruited to Hamilton in the last fiscal year simply replaced those who left.
The worst shortage in the city is in Glanbrook - including Mount Hope and Binbrook - with only three family doctors practising in the rural area. It is followed by the shortfall on Hamilton Mountain.
In total, Hamilton needs about 47 more family physicians - 22 of them in Glanbrook and 12 on the Mountain.
"We do not have enough family providers in our community and we are not graduating enough," Melissa McCallum, executive director of the Greater Hamilton Health Network (GHHN), told the city's public health committee Sept. 30. "This is not a problem unique to us just in Hamilton ... We need all hands on deck at all levels of government."
In fact, the doctor shortage has reached the point that the Ontario Medical Association (OMA) held a virtual town hall in Hamilton on Monday as part of a provincial tour to raise alarm about the "crisis" in primary care.
The OMA estimates more than 11 per cent of Hamiltonians don't have a family doctor.
"Without a family doctor, health care is becoming more and more inaccessible," OMA president Dr. Dominik Nowak said at the town hall. "We're not seeing the changes that we need for this crisis to improve."
MPP Sandy Shaw said at the town hall that she is getting more and more calls to her NDP Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas office from constituents "stressed and concerned about access to primary care" in the city.
"People don't realize how desperate the situation has become until they are faced with it themselves," Shaw said.
"Absolutely, we are at a crisis point ... and it needs to be underscored that everyone in Ontario deserves to have access to reliable primary care."
Ontario Health Team GHHN has taken over the task of physician recruitment from the city and managed to attract 26 new family doctors to Hamilton from April 1, 2023, to March 31, 2024.
However, 23 family physicians left during that time - nine retired, seven moved their practice to a different city, six stopped doing traditional family medicine for a different type of practice and one died.
As a result, Hamilton had a net gain of just three family physicians last fiscal year to bring the total to 366 citywide.
"The new docs are actively avoiding family practice. It's seen as a nonviable business model because of chronic underfunding," Nowak said. "We won't be able to recruit our way out in a system where we're not valuing primary care and family practice in the first place. We need to start with that and then when we recruit people they'll actually stay in family practice."
Shaw put some of the blame for the recruitment and retention challenges on the working conditions of family doctors that Nowak said has left them "buried in red tape" with not enough time for patients.
GHHN had to recruit 45 temporary family physicians last fiscal year to cover leaves and vacations as well as prevent burnout.
"We cannot underestimate the strain," Shaw said. "They're under immense pressure now. People are showing up sicker than before with complicated issues."
One of the biggest barriers GHHN has faced in recruitment is that family doctors want to practice in teams. A $20-million proposal by GHHN would have given all area family doctors access to team-based care, but it received just $2.2 million in one-time provincial funding in March.
"I would like to see more investment. This is a massive area of concern," McCallum said. "Gone are the days of solo physicians opening up a practice on their own. We're seeing younger graduates wanting to be part of a team along with other multidisciplinary professionals."
To combat the shortages, the province has expanded the number of medical school and residency spots in 2022 and 2023. In addition, a new medical school will open in 2025 at Toronto Metropolitan University (TMU) in Brampton and in 2028 at York University in Vaughan. These programs will have an emphasis on training family doctors.
But many of the Hamiltonians who joined the town hall asked about whether more internationally trained doctors could fill in the city's primary care gaps.
"With the deficit of over 3,000 doctors (in Ontario), we're not going to solve that by graduating new doctors alone," OMA executive vice-president Craig DuHamel said at the town hall. "We have to bring physicians in who have been educated internationally,"
Last year, Ontario created a streamlined route for internationally trained doctors to get licensed called Practice Ready Ontario. But both DuHamel and Nowak say more needs to be done.
"The other big pool of doctors that we have are Canadians who went internationally for school and there is such a big opportunity to bring them home and bring them back to caring for our communities," Nowak said. "Our residency program options for international grads, even if they're Canadian, are very limited, so that's what we're advocating for."
The shortage is a significant issue because family physicians are the foundation and gateway to care - they're trusted by their patients, know their history, provide preventative care and help them navigate the health system.
"It really matters that people have a family doctor," Nowak said. "If you have a family doctor, you end up getting more preventive care including cancer screening. For seniors ... that could mean the difference between breaking a hip and not if you're on the right medications proactively. It means fewer emergency department visits, fewer and shorter hospitalizations. People actually live longer if they have a family doctor and they cost the health-care system less money."
Is your community short doctors?
The GHHN provided a snapshot of family doctor recruitment in different parts of Hamilton in a report to the city's public health committee on Sept. 30.
Ancaster
Short six physicians since 2022. There are 26 active family doctors in Ancaster. The GHHN says doctors like the community's "desirable housing and education ... but the relatively high cost of housing continues to act as potential barrier to recruitment."
Dundas
Surplus of four doctors. It is one of two areas of the city that has too many family doctors, with 22 practising in Dundas. In fact, one of the issues with recruitment is fewer patients are looking for a doctor compared to other parts of the city so it would take longer to build up a practice.
"Family medicine coverage in Dundas remains strong," the report states. "Physicians cite Dundas' small-town charm, quaint housing and access to greenspace among their top reasons for selecting this community."
Flamborough (including Waterdown and Carlisle)
Short seven physicians, which is an improvement from nine in 2022. Keeping up with population growth is one of the main challenges in this area that has 27 family doctors. The population increased by nearly 10 per cent from 2016 to 2021 compared to nearly 7.5 per cent across the city.
"Physicians cite desirable family housing and proximity to Burlington/Oakville as their primary reasons for selecting Flamborough (and in particular Waterdown)," GHHN states.
Glanbrook (including Mount Hope and Binbrook)
Short 22 doctors - the worst shortfall in the city. It has left many patients driving to Hamilton Mountain and Stoney Creek for primary care as there are only three physicians practising in Glanbrook. The gaps are long-standing considering the community was short 23 physicians in 2022 and can't access provincial incentives to attract doctors that are available to other rural areas. GHHN says "population growth has exploded" by 17.5 per cent between 2016 and 2021.
"Other challenges include a lack of established clinic space suitable for the creation of large group practices, competition from other rural communities in Ontario ... and a relatively small pool of candidates interested in more rural practice."
Hamilton Mountain
Short 12 physicians, which is down from 17 in 2022. The community is up to 101 family doctors, partly because of lower city practices relocating to the Mountain.
"Access to primary care coverage is improving on the Hamilton Mountain," the report says. "Physicians cite access to suitable group practice space, the ability to build a roster, and access to amenities like shopping and restaurants as their primary reasons for practising on the Hamilton Mountain."
Lower city
Surplus of five doctors despite a net decrease since 2022 to 140 physicians practising in the lower city.
"Despite the overall strength in primary care coverage in this area, pockets of underserviced areas remain," the GHHC states. "Physicians often cite the ability to take over an established patient roster and access to priority patient populations as their reason for choosing to practice in lower Hamilton. Physicians who have left this area typically cite traffic/congestion, lack of space with sufficient parking, and the opportunity to practice in newer (often larger) spaces elsewhere in the city."
Stoney Creek
Short eight doctors - a slight improvement from a shortfall of nine in 2022. However, the population has grown by more than 10 per cent, which is expected to put an increasing strain on the 47 doctors practising in Stoney Creek.
"Primary care shortages in the area are expected to increase ... due to rapid population growth," the report states. "Physicians often cite desirable (and relatively affordable) housing and access to amenities as primary reasons for locating in Stoney Creek."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Sandy Shaw, New Democratic MPP for Hamilton West- Ancaster-Dundas, said "people don't realize how desperate the situation has become" until they find themselves with no family doctor. Hamilton had a net gain of just three family physicians last fiscal year to bring the total to 366 citywide.Spectator file photoJeff Robertson The Associated Press file photo
