Document 372 of 500

Ford government announces details of its audit of Ontario children's aid societies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-announces-details-audit-ontario/docview/3115173073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+announces+details+of+its+audit+of+Ontario+children%27s+aid+societies&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has revealed its plans for an audit of children's aid societies that officials say will give them a comprehensive look at the state of the province's child welfare system, including the quality of child protection services and a financial review to pinpoint factors driving rising costs and deficits.
The audit, which ministry officials on Wednesday said will be conducted by an outside contractor with a final report expected next spring, will examine agencies' operational costs, including executive compensation and staffing.
The sector-wide review of 37 children's aid societies will also analyze funding spent on services for kids with complex special needs coming into the care of welfare agencies. The review does not include Indigenous agencies.
The audit comes in the wake of Star reporting that revealed child welfare agencies are increasingly becoming a last-resort option for families of children with complex health needs unable to get care in their communities, and a separate investigation into the death of a four-year-old girl whose body was found in a Rosedale dumpster after children's aid closed her case.
It also follows an announcement by Ontario Ombudsman Paul Dubé that his office will investigate the practice of children's aid societies placing young people in unlicensed settings.
Child welfare leaders told the Star they are forced to house kids and teens with complex mental health and developmental needs in unlicensed settings, such as hotels, rental apartments, office buildings and Airbnbs due to a lack of treatment options, and say these emergency placements are driving up costs for their agencies and harming vulnerable children and youth.
In the year ending in March 2024, there were 354 children whose care had each cost child welfare agencies more than $200,000 per year, according to the Ontario Association of Children's Aid Societies. The Star has learned of at least four children and youth in CAS care for whom the annual cost of housing and supervision is roughly $1 million each.
Michael Parsa, the minister of children, community and social services, said in a written statement that funding to children's aid societies has increased, rules have been strengthened through legislation, but "we continue to see children falling through the cracks."
Plans for the audit were outlined in a technical briefing for journalists led by two ministry officials. To attend the virtual briefing, reporters had to agree not to name the officials or quote them directly. Parsa did not attend.
"We know more needs to be done to ensure that every dollar is spent effectively on supporting these children and youth," Parsa said in the statement. "That's why we are taking this next step with a sector-wide review to better understand the pressures children's aid societies face and to ensure they remain focused on providing high-quality services and support while protecting children receiving services from societies."
Many questions about the audit remain unanswered, including whether Premier Doug Ford directed the ministry to launch the review.
Last week, Ford pledged a "complete audit" of the child welfare sector, alleging misuse of taxpayer funds, including agencies renting "Taj Mahal" office buildings. Speaking to reporters, Ford said his government has increased funding to children's aid societies but claimed some are abusing taxpayer money, adding that any managers "giving yourselves a bonus - not worrying about the kids - I'm coming for you."
Kiaras Gharabaghi, dean of the Faculty of Community Services and professor in child and youth care at Toronto Metropolitan University, called an audit of the child welfare system that focuses on finances "a distraction" that does not respond to the challenges faced by families in crisis.
"Children's aid societies may not always have been on the right side of this problem, but auditing their finances is not a response to the problem, accomplishes nothing for families who need support right now, and ultimately reinforces the idea that if only people spent their money right, we wouldn't have a problem," Gharabaghi said.
The OACAS, the organization that represents most of the province's children's aid societies, previously told the Star that Ford's call for an audit of the sector does not address the lack of supports, services and "appropriate placements" for children and youth with complex needs.
Last year, child welfare agencies served 589 children or youth for which protection concerns were either not present or were a secondary concern to unmet health needs, such as getting timely mental health treatments, according to the OACAS.
Earlier this month, the organization sent an open letter to Ford calling on the province to take "immediate action" to ensure community-based organizations and service providers have enough funding and staff to "truly respond to the needs of children, youth and families." Close to 30 agencies and associations, including municipalities, non-profits and organizations in mental health care, among others, signed the letter.
More to come ...
With files from Wendy Gillis and Jennifer Pagliaro
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and mogilvie@thestar.ca

Document 373 of 500

Canadians watch too much American news and only get half the picture, says U.S. ambassador

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-watch-too-much-american-news-only-get/docview/3114754979/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+watch+too+much+American+news+and+only+get+half+the+picture%2C+says+U.S.+ambassador&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are watching "far too much American cable news," says the U.S. ambassador to Canada, emphasizing democracy will thrive stateside regardless of who wins the presidential election.
But David Cohen conceded to the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum "there's less polarization in Canada than there is in the United States" politically.
"Neither side may want to admit it, but there's stunningly broader agreement about the end state around some of these big issues - between or among the Conservative party, the NDP, the Liberal party - than there is disagreement," Cohen said Wednesday.
"What there is that is common between the United States and Canada - it's hard to say which one is better or worse - is a loss of civility in the dialogue that takes place around these important issues," he said.
"That's something that is hard for me to take because there's no reason for it."
The ambassador chided Canadians for their obsession with U.S. cable news channels like CNN, Fox News, and MSNBC where Democratic and Republican partisans performatively spar for the cameras.
"Canadians watch far too much American cable news. Canadians are watching this news without the context of living in the country," said Cohen, a former top executive at Comcast Corporation, which owns MSNBC among other broadcasters.
"Americans may watch too much of that news, too, but they live in the country, they're talking to other people, they're experiencing what the cable news is covering. They bring some American context to watching the cable news, Canadians don't have any of that."
Cohen's one-hour conversation with Cohn comes against the backdrop of a close election on Nov. 5 between Democrat Kamala Harris, departing President Joe Biden's vice-president, and Republican Donald Trump, the former president defeated by Biden in 2020.
While he was a major fundraiser for Biden and former presidents Barack Obama and Bill Clinton, the ambassador noted that under the U.S. Hatch Act he is prohibited from engaging in partisan political activities.
"I have unbelievable respect for the Hatch Act, but I hate to say this, I have even more respect for my reputation as a diplomat. When I was in the United States, I was extremely active in politics and a lot of people liked to hear me as a political commentator. In Canada, nobody's interested in their diplomats being a political commentator."
But Cohen stressed that even though "we have a turbulent decade of contention in our government systems and our government structures," U.S. democracy will survive as it did in the Civil War, the Great Depression, the McCarthy red scares of the 1950s and the upheaval of the civil rights movement.
"We've come out on the other side stronger than where we were before we went into those battles, our democracy stronger and more enduring."
Cohen, who meets with Canadian political leaders of all stripes, singled out Premier Doug Ford for praise.
"I like him a lot. He obviously has a huge affection for the United States," he said, adding Ford presented him with a signed CFL football that he now keeps in a glass case at the U.S. embassy in Ottawa.
Like Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, who is Harris's vice-presidential running mate and famously played catch with Ford last June, the ambassador enjoyed tossing a football with the premier around his office at Queen's Park.
"We had a good time and neither one of us broke anything," said Cohen.
CAPTION: U.S. Ambassador to Canada David Cohen during a visit to Niagara Falls on May 12, 2023.

Subject: Canadians; Presidential elections; Democracy; Politics; Public officials; Diplomatic & consular services

Location: United States--US; Canada

Politics near and far have big consequences

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/politics-near-far-have-big-consequences/docview/3114752997/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politics+near+and+far+have+big+consequences&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Protesters here and abroad protest every "injustice" imaginable, even erecting blockades that affect neighbours. I just don't understand all the unrest.
One group starts a war and it escalates out of control. Will we be dragged into a Third World War? Dangerous egotists seek victory at any cost, ignoring impacts on their countrymen and the lives of those under attack. Too bad, innocents! Just collateral damage?
Imagine leaving a home, kids in tow, fleeing to God knows where (whoever will take you in?) all because despots harbour delusions of grandeur. And this at a time when there's a global housing shortage. How traumatic!
Speaking of egotists, our southern neighbour's election features an ex-president con-man, felon and consummate liar, who leads a cult of evangelicals who seemingly have overlooked their dedication to the Ten Commandments.
With a federal election looming here, it appears Justin Trudeau's "best before" date has expired. Pierre Poilievre's negativity scares me so much I could never support him. His recent performance in the House of Commons demonstrates his inability to act as the unifying leader so sorely needed today. These are our leaders?
This week, Burlington had an early update by its consultant on council size, composition and ward boundaries for the 2026 election. Debates on this from both outside and inside council (where turf protection seemed to be the top criterion) have taken place. Public consultation is underway.
Unfortunately elections are popularity contests, not ability contests. With only seven council members locally, absenteeism causes problems. With two absent, a majority of only three can make decisions for a city of close to 200,000!
The "strong mayors" system gives mayors veto power over council on some items, a huge democratic deficit. The workload is high and some of the 63 ABCs, (agencies, boards and commissions) require extensive involvement. There really should be minimum qualifications to run for council today.
Budget - actually, pre-budget - presentations have been making the rounds. The actual budget is proposed by the mayor (due Nov. 4) under this undemocratic "strong mayors" system from Premier Doug Ford. Pundits say the objective was to shift blame from the province for cutting cities' revenue sources to councils, because apparently Ford wants an early provincial election. Local taxpayers are angry at tax hikes and may well take their revenge on councils.
Our tone-deaf councillors approved the 2025 meeting calendar, despite two delegations in opposition. It mimics the 2024 one with one exception: meetings on development proposals. This past year most were held at 6:30 p.m. They will be at 9:30 a.m. instead. But there's flexibility (too bureaucratic to access). Some people can't attend day meetings. Coun. Lisa Kearns tried to get evening council meetings, but she alone supported her motion.
Staff will review establishing a resiliency fund, available to flooding victims. But here's an interesting situation. A resident experienced ankle-deep water in their laundry room on July 15 as a result of the next-door tenant changing his grading significantly and building a platform. The city agreed, and has made several attempts to contact both the tenant and homeowner (who purportedly owns several homes). Neither responded. The city is pursuing options, but this is mid-October! Does this resident have to worry all winter, with its snow, rain, freezing and thawing?
CAPTION: Burlington had an early update by its consultant on council size, composition and ward boundaries for the 2026 election. Having just six councillors, plus a mayor, for a city this size isn't sufficient, Joan Little says.

Subject: Meetings; Provincial elections; Mayors; Councils

Document 375 of 500

Here's who RCMP's Greenbelt investigators are interviewing - and the one question they're asking everyone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-who-rcmps-greenbelt-investigators-are/docview/3114752972/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+who+RCMP%27s+Greenbelt+investigators+are+interviewing+-+and+the+one+question+they%27re+asking+everyone&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?"
That's one question RCMP investigators probing the $8.28-billion land swap scandal have been asking the political aides and bureaucrats they have been interviewing these past few months, sources told the Star.
As the Mounties' criminal probe marks its first anniversary Thursday, insiders say at least eight current and former top aides to Premier Doug Ford have been interviewed - in the presence of lawyers being paid for by the Progressive Conservative party, which has already spent hundreds of thousands of dollars on legal fees and is bracing for a seven-figure tab.
Members of the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that looks into political crimes and corruption, have been dropping hints to interviewees about where the probe is headed.
The detectives, whose questions indicate they have already obtained numerous internal email and text messages, have suggested they may need to access banking records in Europe, meaning the investigation could last many months or even years.
According to the RCMP's 2021 annual report, the average length of time of a federal police investigation is 1.6 years.
That timing is significant because Ford is considering a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote. Any major developments in the Greenbelt scandal could derail that timetable.
So far, the RCMP's focus has been on the premier's inner sanctum - not the municipal affairs minister's office that was initially blamed for the imbroglio.
Ford himself has not been interviewed, but he has emphasized his door is open to investigators.
"We have nothing to hide," he said in August.
"Come in and do whatever you have to do ... but I want full co-operation - they know that - because there's nothing to hide there. Let's get on with it."
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, say the Mounties have not yet scheduled interviews with former municipal affairs minister Steve Clark or his then-chief of staff Ryan Amato.
Clark and Amato, who both left their posts in the wake of the debacle, have denied any wrongdoing and are eager to meet with the RCMP - possibly before Christmas. In June, the premier appointed Clark as government house leader.
Ford abandoned the Greenbelt land-swap scheme on Sept. 21, 2023, a month after separate reports by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk and integrity commissioner J. David Wake found developers connected to the Tories had been "favoured" when the premier allowed homes to be built on the environmentally sensitive land.
Wake's 166-page report seems to be serving as something of a road map for the Mounties, according to those who have been interviewed at law firms and at an RCMP detachment in the Greater Toronto Area.
The Star is not identifying all of those who have met with the RCMP because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling detectives.
Officially, the Mounties would only confirm their work is ongoing.
"The RCMP's Sensitive and International Investigations team takes these allegations seriously. We would like to reassure the residents of Ontario that we are conducting a thorough investigation," the force said in a statement.
So far, the police line of questioning seems to be about the organizational structure in the premier's office - who reports to whom, what is the role of the bureaucracy in political decisions and who has the final say on green-lighting government initiatives.
Detectives also appear intrigued by a 2020 trip to Las Vegas that a top Ford aide, then-principal secretary Amin Massoudi, took with MPP Kaleed Rasheed, Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Jae Truesdell, at the time a private citizen who would later become the premier's housing policy adviser.
Rasheed was forced to step down as Ford's business minister and leave the PC caucus in September 2023, after making what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner wrong information about the date of the Vegas jaunt.
Friends confide he has yet to meet with the RCMP, noting the affable Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP feels like "collateral damage" because he lost his job over that vacation date inconsistency despite having no role in the Greenbelt decision.
Truesdell, forced from his position in Ford's office, was one of the first Tories to be interviewed by the Mounties. He has maintained he did nothing wrong.
Remtullah, who attended the premier's daughter's wedding, owned stakes in two small parcels totalling about 23 acres that were removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
It is unclear whether the RCMP has yet met with Massoudi, now a successful lobbyist working in the private sector.
Long one of Ford's closest advisers, the premier's office said it cut "formal" ties with him last fall due to fallout from the Greenbelt saga.
When Massoudi left Queen's Park after the 2022 election to launch his lobbying business, the premier said "since the early days at city hall, Amin has been by my side and has become one of my most-trusted advisers."
"He's been a steady hand in my office, first serving as deputy chief of staff and then principal secretary and chair of our successful re-election campaign, and has helped encourage many young people inside and outside government to get more involved in politics and public service," Ford said at the time.
"Amin is like family, and he'll continue to be a close adviser and friend. I wish him nothing but success in his next chapter."
Interviewees have told the Mounties that the decision to open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area dates back to November 2021 - more than six months before the last election and one year before Clark's surprise announcement the land would be developed.
A joint Star and Narwhal investigation in November 2022 found a majority of the areas slated for redevelopment had been bought up by developers when the land was still protected. Of the 15 parcels of land initially opened up, eight were purchased in the four years after Ford's 2018 election.
The premier, who has set a target of building 1.5 million new homes by 2031, has said he only allowed Greenbelt development to tackle Ontario's fast-growing housing crisis.
According to the auditor general's report, 14 of the 15 parcels included in the land swap were selected by then-minister Clark's chief of staff Amato - with input from some developers.
But Wake's report found Clark had violated the Members' Integrity Act by putting his "head in the sand" and not properly supervising his aide, who has always maintained he was just following orders.
CAPTION: Steve Clark announced his resignation housing minister amid the Greenbelt controversy.Ryan Amato was chief of staff to Ontario Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing Steve Clark.Amin Massoudi and Doug Ford are shown in this 2014 file photo.Premier Doug Ford answers an opposition question at Queen's Park legislature on Sept. 25, 2023.Ford abandoned the Greenbelt land-swap scheme four days earlier.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals; Politics; Interviews

Title: Here's who RCMP's Greenbelt investigators are interviewing - and the one question they're asking everyone

Document 376 of 500

Former Liberal MPP calls for public inquiry into CSIS amid warrant controversy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-liberal-mpp-calls-public-inquiry-into-csis/docview/3114752903/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Liberal+MPP+calls+for+public+inquiry+into+CSIS+amid+warrant+controversy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Markham deputy mayor Michael Chan is accusing Canada's spy service of wrongfully pursuing him in its efforts to confront foreign political interference, as he responded to media reports that he is the subject of a national security warrant under scrutiny at an ongoing public inquiry.
In a statement posted on social media Wednesday, Chan said he was subject of a "wiretap" by the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) that needed a government-approved warrant. Blaming "leaks" from security officials to the media, he also slammed recent media coverage as a "barrage" of falsehoods that implied he is "an 'agent' of the Chinese government."
Chan - a former Ontario MPP and Liberal cabinet minister - said the "wiretap" was related to allegations that he "engineered the removal" of former Liberal MP Geng Tan and pushed for former Liberal MPP Han Dong to replace him as the party candidate in the Toronto riding of Don Valley North. Dong is denying allegations of involvement in Chinese foreign interference.
Pointing to previous testimony and a report by former governor general David Johnston, Chan denied any involvement and said "nothing" has been found against him despite what he described as years of scrutiny by CSIS and the media, in particular by the Globe and Mail.
The Globe has reported that Chan is the subject of a CSIS warrant that took 54 days for a Trudeau cabinet minister to approve in 2021.
The federal government has not publicly identified the subject of the warrant.
"The failure to report in a fair and responsible manner has not only hurt me in unimaginable ways, it has gravely impacted by family and friends," Chan said in his statement, which called for a public inquiry into "the competency of CSIS."
Neither CSIS nor the Globe responded to requests from the Star for comment on Wednesday evening.
Shortly after Chan published his statement, former high-ranking Liberal staffer Zita Astravas testified at the inquiry, where she was questioned about how long it took for Bill Blair, then the public safety minister, to approve a CSIS warrant about an individual whose identity was kept secret during Wednesday's hearing.
Astravas, who was Blair's chief of staff at the time, acknowledged a timeline provided at the inquiry that showed it took 54 days for Blair to approve CSIS's request for the warrant, which the Federal Court approved three weeks later.
Blair told the inquiry in April that he signed off on the warrant within hours of being made aware of it.
On Wednesday, Astravas said the warrant was never "raised as a matter of urgency" and that she followed the usual process of putting things before her minister based on a "priority list" provided by CSIS. The warrant was never in danger of being denied, she said.
"As soon as the director had indicated that he would like to put this warrant on the agenda, it was arranged within days," Astravas said.
The inquiry has previously heard testimony that CSIS was frustrated by how long it took for Blair to approve the warrant.
Pressed by Gib van Ert, the lawyer for Conservative MP Michael Chong, Astravas said "there was an awareness" in Blair's office that the warrant was coming. But she refused to concede the Blair was aware of who the warrant targeted.
"He was aware of a warrant, but I do not have specific recollection if the name was included in that," she said.
Van Ert also suggested Astravas was wary of the warrant because it involved "high-ranking members of her party" and "people she had known for years."
"The accusation you're making is false," Astravas responded.
She also testified that she did not discuss the warrant with anyone in the Prime Minister's Office or outside the government.
The Hogue commission, which started exploring foreign meddling in Canada's 2019 and 2021 elections earlier this year, is in the midst of its second phase: a deep dive into Ottawa's ability to address foreign interference into the country's democratic processes.
The inquiry has frequently heard about one report prepared by CSIS and the Intelligence Assessment Secretariat (IAS) which summarized China's interference attempts abroad and in Canada. A document tabled with the inquiry on Wednesday showed that the IAS believed the report should be shared with some deputy and cabinet ministers.
On Wednesday, Hogue heard from Jody Thomas, who previously served as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's national security and intelligence adviser, and opted not to share the then-unfinished report with senior political figures because she believed the report failed to reach "new conclusions."
A second report, which looked at how China selects targets for influence operations, further highlights the lack of consensus over what information Trudeau and his cabinet should be shown.
Hogue has previously heard from CSIS that Trudeau should have seen the report. Thomas said the influence efforts outlined in the report did not necessarily constitute nefarious examples of interference.
Nathalie Drouin, the prime minister's current national security adviser, said Wednesday that another version of the report was made available to Trudeau after he appeared before Hogue in April. The prime minister is set to testify again on Oct. 16.
CAPTION: Former Markham-Unionville MPP and Liberal cabinet minister Michael Chan is shown at a meeting of Markham city council on Feb. 11, 2020.

Document 377 of 500

Oct. 9: Downtown revitalization should benefit all, revoke licences of habitual offenders and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-9-downtown-revitalization-should-benefit-all/docview/3114489293/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+9%3A+Downtown+revitalization+should+benefit+all%2C+revoke+licences+of+habitual+offenders+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Revitalization should benefit all
Re: HUPEG provides update on renovation project, Oct. 5
It is very worrisome to read of HUPEG's plans to revitalize the downtown entertainment district as those plans, as published, do not consider the poor. Revitalization, decoded, is gentrification. Yet before the rich find yet more things to buy and places to dump their money, the poor need the rich's things to live and their places to live in.
Neither policing services nor HUPEG's private Downtown Safety and Security task force are able to address meaningfully the concerns of the poor: unaffordable, unavailable and undignified housing; lack of work, or of meaningful work, at a living wage; in a phrase, equal dignity. Moreover, it is concerning to read of private security task forces working in concert with policing services: neither, largely unaccountable, should be present in the city, their budgets redirected toward non-carceral, humane supports such as housing, mental-health supports, and a harm-reduction approach to drug use.
In short, how is any of what HUPEG imagines connected to "placemaking done by and for the community it serves," "a piece of land belonging to or affecting the whole community." Which community is served and how is this "commons" theirs? Whose streets are these; for whom is this place made; and to whom does this land belong? These questions should be answered by the poorest, first, as care for those in need is vital, long before an entertainment district.
Joshua Weresch, Hamilton
Self-checkout good for those in a hurry
Re: Checking out from self-checkouts, Oct. 4
I for one will use a self-checkout if the lines are extremely long at regular checkout and if they are even open for customers to use. I actually like them and do not see a problem with them; gets you in and out if you are in a hurry. Being a senior, I have had no problem with self-checkouts. If you need help, just click on the button, "customer assistance." Chatting with the cashiers is great, however, when their lines are long I am sure they appreciate it when customers help out by using these machines.
Sally Carey, Hamilton
Revoke licences of habitual offenders
Earlier this year the Ontario government proposed legislation for a lifetime driving ban on motorists charged with impaired driving causing death. Perhaps the Ontario government should consider other reasons for permanently removing driving privileges such as habitual driving infractions: speeding, ignoring traffic lights and road signs; outstanding traffic tickets, or driving without a licence, for example. Perhaps refreshed road tests should be considered for all drivers every three to five years and written tests as well. Know the rules of the road. Respect the rules of the road. It could cost a driver their life, or that of someone else. This applies to pedestrians and cyclists, too.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Motorists don't feel safe either
Re: People will bike if they feel safe, Oct. 8
Yet another letter from a bicycle rider complaining about the inability to ride his bike where and when he wants and comparing us to European cities like Amsterdam, Copenhagen, etc. Well, guess what? This isn't Europe. It's Canada. And the last time I checked I didn't see any bike lanes on the autobahn.
While we're on the topic of Europe, you can also easily get from Point A to Point B without being stuck in constant gridlock. I can drive from my wife's hometown in Plovdiv, Bulgaria, to Istanbul, Turkey, for a weekend getaway without a problem and nary a bicycle or rickshaw in sight along the route.
I don't feel safe driving around here either, be it in my car or on my motorcycle, and in my opinion people who ride bicycles around in this insanity have a death wish.
Paul E. Casey, Ancaster
Bus ride provides ray of sunshine
Due to an unfortunate event my husband and I, both seniors, find ourselves using the bus in the Aldershot area. As we enter the bus, to our surprise, young people jump up and make the first few front seats available to us. Also, as people get off the bus, young and old, all shout out a "thank you" to the driver. How heart warming in today's chaotic world.
Isabelle Gainfort, Burlington
Cartoon an 'inversion of reality'
Graeme Mackay's Oct. 8 cartoon depicted Israel's prime minister fighting over a dove with Iran's ayatollah, standing over a mountain of bones. This caricature drew an obscene moral equivalence.
One year ago, Hamas (an Iranian-backed terrorist proxy), launched the biggest massacre of Jews since the Holocaust. The next day, Hezbollah, another Iranian proxy, began firing 10,000 rockets indiscriminately into Israel. Soon the Houthis in Yemen and other groups allied with Iran joined in. All of them are sworn to Israel's destruction.
And yet Israel - for refusing to lie down and die - is put in the same category as the genocidal maniacs who seek to destroy it, and whose followers have been rampaging on our streets for a year? This is akin to showing Hitler and Churchill fighting over a mountain of graves and is an absolute inversion of reality.
Robert Walker, assistant director, Honest Reporting Canada
Culture Days a great experience
I had the pleasure of performing during Culture Days at the Hamilton Public Library at the main branch. A jewel of a space bright and inviting in our downtown core. Inside I found a novel of one of my favourite local authors, Amy Jones, on display. Lots of friendly staff and a very appreciative crowd capped off a great experience. Did I mention that these concerts are free and are at many of the branches?
Robin Magder, Rocket and the Renegades
Common sense cuts welcome
Re: We need more than a slogan, Oct. 5
I try to keep up to date on the political scene and what the leaders of the main parties are proposing and the only "cuts" I have heard the Conservative leader propose are cuts to wasteful government spending, cuts to the bloated CBC, cuts to the national debt, cuts to the number of serious criminals let out on bail and the elimination of the carbon tax, which to me make perfect "common sense."
Any other so-called Conservative cuts are concocted in desperation by the Liberals and NDP.
Leslie Farrington, Ancaster
Change Trump coverage focus
Re: 'Uneven' debate made Walz more likable, Oct. 4
Again we were treated to an angst-ridden opinion piece about the evil Republican candidates for president and vice-president of the United States.
I understand The Spectator is publishing so many anti-Trump articles because the opinion page manager, or managers farther up the chain, believe that is what their readers want to read. And it may well be so.
But it begs the question of why. Why would I, as an individual Canadian citizen residing in Canada, think that I should be presented with such drivel in an endless cycle? Regardless of the veracity of the facts on which the opinions are based, the underlying reality is that I am completely unable to have any impact on the American election.
If Canadians are worried about Donald Trump's possible victory in November, we ought to be focused on how we can react to and perhaps even benefit from it. And our media, such as The Spectator, ought to be leading that conversation instead of constantly stoking the angst which has obviously already paralyzed the minds of so many Canadians.
Dave de Jong, Burlington
We can do better, right now
Today, I parked briefly in Summer's Lane by the entrance to the convention centre. It smelled of urine. In fact the whole of Summer's Lane is one long, low, dreary cement tunnel - certainly not a welcoming place for the well-dressed people I saw on their way in.
I turned the corner, walking onto King, right beside the art gallery. The stretch up to the Ellen Fairclough building also smelled of urine. A couple of homeless people sat surrounded by garbage in one alcove. All of this made me both sad and angry. Why can't we organize more housing? Even temporarily at Sir John A. for this winter? Why can't we get that stretch cleaned every single day? Do we really want this to be the face of Hamilton for visitors? We can do better, right now.
Carol Town, Hamilton
CAPTION: The welfare of the city's most vulnerable must be taken consideration when talking about downtown revitalization, a reader says.

Letters

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Self-checkout good for some
Re: Checking out from self-checkouts, Oct. 4
I for one will use a self-checkout if the lines are extremely long at regular checkout and if they are even open for customers to use. I actually like them and do not see a problem with them; gets you in and out if you are in a hurry. Being a senior, I have had no problem with self-checkouts. If you need help, just click on the button, "customer assistance."
Chatting with the cashiers is great, however, when their lines are long I am sure they appreciate it when customers help out by using these machines.
Sally Carey, Hamilton
Revoke licences of habitual offenders
Earlier this year the Ontario government proposed legislation for a lifetime driving ban on motorists charged with impaired driving causing death. Perhaps the Ontario government should consider other reasons for permanently removing driving privileges such as habitual driving infractions: speeding, ignoring traffic lights and road signs; outstanding traffic tickets, or driving without a licence, for example.
Perhaps refreshed road tests should be considered for all drivers every three to five years and written tests as well. Know the rules of the road. Respect the rules of the road. It could cost a driver their life, or that of someone else. This applies to pedestrians and cyclists, too.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Motorists don't feel safe either
Re: People will bike if they feel safe, Oct. 8
Yet another letter from a bicycle rider complaining about the inability to ride his bike where and when he wants and comparing us to European cities like Amsterdam, Copenhagen, etc. Well, guess what? This isn't Europe. It's Canada. And the last time I checked I didn't see any bike lanes on the autobahn.
While we're on the topic of Europe, you can also easily get from Point A to Point B without being stuck in constant gridlock. I can drive from my wife's hometown in Plovdiv, Bulgaria, to Istanbul, Turkey, for a weekend getaway without a problem and nary a bicycle or rickshaw in sight along the route.
I don't feel safe driving around here either, be it in my car or on my motorcycle, and in my opinion people who ride bicycles around in this insanity have a death wish.
Paul E. Casey, Ancaster
Revitalization should benefit all
Re: HUPEG provides update on renovation project, Oct. 5
It is very worrisome to read of HUPEG's plans to revitalize the downtown entertainment district as those plans, as published, do not consider the poor. Revitalization, decoded, is gentrification. Yet before the rich find yet more things to buy and places to dump their money, the poor need the rich's things to live and their places to live in.
Neither policing services nor HUPEG's private Downtown Safety and Security task force are able to address meaningfully the concerns of the poor: unaffordable, unavailable and undignified housing; lack of work, or of meaningful work, at a living wage; in a phrase, equal dignity. Moreover, it is concerning to read of private security task forces working in concert with policing services: neither, largely unaccountable, should be present in the city, their budgets redirected toward non-carceral, humane supports such as housing, mental-health supports, and a harm-reduction approach to drug use.
In short, how is any of what HUPEG imagines connected to "placemaking done by and for the community it serves," "a piece of land belonging to or affecting the whole community." Which community is served and how is this "commons" theirs? Whose streets are these; for whom is this place made; and to whom does this land belong? These questions should be answered by the poorest, first, as care for those in need is vital, long before an entertainment district.
Joshua Weresch, Hamilton
Bus ride provides ray of sunshine
Due to an unfortunate event my husband and I, both seniors, find ourselves using the bus in the Aldershot area. As we enter the bus, to our surprise, young people jump up and make the first few front seats available to us. Also, as people get off the bus, young and old, all shout out a "thank you" to the driver. How heart warming in today's chaotic world.
Isabelle Gainfort,Burlington
An 'inversion
of reality'
Graeme Mackay's Oct. 8 cartoon depicted Israel's prime minister fighting over a dove with Iran's ayatollah, standing over a mountain of bones. This caricature drew an obscene moral equivalence.
One year ago, Hamas (an Iranian-backed terrorist proxy), launched the biggest massacre of Jews since the Holocaust. The next day, Hezbollah, another Iranian proxy, began firing 10,000 rockets indiscriminately into Israel. Soon the Houthis in Yemen and other groups allied with Iran joined in. All of them are sworn to Israel's destruction.
And yet Israel - for refusing to lie down and die - is put in the same category as the genocidal maniacs who seek to destroy it, and whose followers have been rampaging on our streets for a year? This is akin to showing Hitler and Churchill fighting over a mountain of graves and is an absolute inversion of reality.
Robert Walker, assistant
director, Honest Reporting Canada
Common sense cuts welcome
Re: We need more than
a slogan, Oct. 5
I try to keep up to date on the political scene and what the leaders of the main parties are proposing and the only "cuts" I have heard the Conservative leader propose are cuts to wasteful government spending, cuts to the bloated CBC, cuts to the national debt, cuts to the number of serious criminals let out on bail and the elimination of the carbon tax, which to me make perfect "common sense." Any other so-called Conservative cuts are concocted in desperation by the Liberals and NDP.
Leslie Farrington, Ancaster
Change Trump coverage focus
Re: 'Uneven' debate made Walz more likable, Oct. 4
Again we were treated to an angst-ridden opinion piece about the evil Republican candidates for president and vice-president of the United States.
I understand The Spectator is publishing so many anti-Trump articles because the opinion page manager, or managers farther up the chain, believe that is what their readers want to read. And it may well be so. But it begs the question of why. Why would I, as an individual Canadian citizen residing in Canada, think that I should be presented with such drivel in an endless cycle? Regardless of the veracity of the facts on which the opinions are based, the underlying reality is that I am completely unable to have any impact on the American election.
If Canadians are worried about Donald Trump's possible victory in November, we ought to be focused on how we can react to and perhaps even benefit from it. And our media, such as The Spectator, ought to be leading that conversation instead of constantly stoking the angst which has obviously already paralyzed the minds of so many Canadians.
Dave de Jong, Burlington
The city can do better, right now
Today, I parked briefly in Summer's Lane by the entrance to the convention centre. It smelled of urine. In fact, the whole of Summer's Lane is one long, low, dreary cement tunnel - certainly not a welcoming place for the well-dressed people I saw on their way in.
I turned the corner, walking onto King, right beside the art gallery. The stretch up to the Ellen Fairclough building also smelled of urine. A couple of homeless people sat surrounded by garbage in one alcove. All of this made me both sad and angry. Why can't we organize more housing? Even temporarily at Sir John A. for this winter? Why can't we get that stretch cleaned every single day? Do we really want this to be the face of Hamilton for visitors? We can do better, right now.
Carol Town, Hamilton

National briefs: Ontario lowers mammogram self-referral age

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3114391683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Ontario+lowers+mammogram+self-referral+age&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Peter Nygard denied bail
Former fashion mogul Peter Nygard has been denied bail as he appeals his sexual assault convictions in Toronto and an 11-year prison sentence. An Ontario Court of Appeal judge dismissed the bail application, saying that Nygard's appeal appears to be "weak" and that he presents a flight risk as he faces charges in other jurisdictions. .
Ontario lowers mammogram self-referral age
Women in their 40s can book a mammogram in Ontario without needing a doctor's referral. Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced last year Ontario would lower the age for breast cancer screening self-referrals from 50 to 40, and she said Tuesday the change has taken effect. The government estimates that of the one million women between the ages of 40 and 49 who can now self-refer, about 305,000 will seek out screening.
Canada
Poilievre barred from speaking in House
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was not allowed to speak in the House of Commons Tuesday as his feud with Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly spilled into a second day. House Speaker Greg Fergus delivered the penalty a day after he asked the leader of the opposition to withdraw a remark he made Monday after he asked the Liberals to condemn what he called antisemitic and "genocidal chants from hateful mobs on our streets." Joly responded by naming the Canadians killed in the Hamas attack on Israel on Oct. 7, 2023, and saying the government stands with the Jewish people. Poilievre said she hadn't actually condemned antisemitism and accused her of refusing to do to in a bid to score political points for what he called her desire to run for the Liberal leadership. "She continues to pander to Hamas supporters and the Liberal party as part of her leadership campaign rather than doing her job," he said.
News services

Document 380 of 500

Conservatives are relying on Trudeau refusing to step down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/conservatives-are-relying-on-trudeau-refusing/docview/3114143427/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservatives+are+relying+on+Trudeau+refusing+to+step+down&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative members of Parliament must be ecstatic right now.
On Oct. 2 CTV news reported they have now opened a 21.1-point lead over Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals. And not only that, but the Liberals are also now in a statistical tie with Jagmeet Singh and the New Democratic Party.
For those who oppose the Conservatives, this is a nightmare. Think back to the federal elections of 1993, 1997 and 2000. In those elections the parties on the right (Reform and Progressive Conservative) split the conservative vote and allowed the Liberals to "come up the middle" and win huge majority victories. We may very well see the same, only with the Conservatives coming up the middle and winning a majority with the "progressive" vote split.
That all being said, there may be several factors that could still derail a potential Poilievre government.
First, polls show Canadians are exhausted with Trudeau. There is little to nothing that he can do to change this opinion among voters. But the possibility is there, that if the Liberals changed their leader, a new leader may close that gap in the polls. Poilievre and his party are in the lead due to fatigue and anger with Trudeau. He isn't in the lead due to voters embracing him. Remove Trudeau from this equation and we may very well see some changes in the polls.
Another factor involved here is Poilievre's way of operating. He is quite possibly the most vicious, opposition leader Canada has ever had. He attacks all the other parties relentlessly. That certainly gets him a lot of media coverage. However, it doesn't get him any goodwill in Parliament. He may very well be the most despised opposition leader in Canadian history.
Which brings me to my final point. If Trudeau steps down and is replaced by say Mark Carney or Chrystia Freeland, is it possible that the gap in the polling numbers becomes smaller? Usually, a new leader will give their party at least a short-term spike in the polls.
Let us imagine a scenario in which Carney becomes Liberal leader. And in the upcoming election the Conservatives are the victors, but are held to a minority.
The usual development in this case would be the Liberal government would then resign and the Conservatives would take power. But one must remember that in the Parliamentary system, the party that holds the confidence of the house, forms the government. Not necessarily the party with the most seats. Poilievre with his offensive, at times, juvenile pugnacity has probably burned all bridges with the other parties in Parliament. The odds of any of the other parties supporting a Poilievre-led Conservative minority government, are about the same as Donald Trump apologizing for his rude, coarse behaviour and becoming a polished, erudite gentleman.
In this case, I would suggest if the Liberals finish with the second-most seats, they will ensure the NDP and Bloc will support them, and the Liberal leader will inform the Governor General that they can form a government and have the confidence of the house. They will then form the government. That is totally appropriate in our system.
If the NDP comes second, the Liberal government will resign, and Poilievre's Conservatives will form a government. And at the very first opportunity, the opposition will defeat his government on a confidence vote. Then you would see the Liberals and Bloc support a Singh-led NDP government.
Poilievre is riding high in the polls today. But his path to forming and staying in government is contingent on winning a majority. He has chosen to be a nasty, at times offensive, "attack dog." That mode of operation will ensure he has no support in Parliament outside of the Conservative caucus.
CAPTION: Pierre Poilievre and his party are in the lead due to fatigue and anger with Trudeau, writesCraig Wallace. He isn't in the lead due to voters embracing him.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Subject: Conservatism; Members of Parliament; Voters; Parliaments

Full text: People will bike if they feel safe
Re: Turn bylaw on sidewalk cyclists, Oct. 3
I've pretty much had it up to here with the anti-bike-lane crowd. The latest letter bemoaned a possible increase in fines for parking in bike lanes, while observing "most (cyclists) ride illegally on the sidewalk."
As a 73-year-old cyclist, if my route does require a few blocks where the only other choice is risking life and limb on a four-lane road with no protected lane then damn right I'll choose the sidewalk, with due respect and courtesy to pedestrians. Otherwise, I stay in my lane ... if there is one.
I wish Doug Ford (given his latest idiotic musings on the subject) would pay a quick visit to European cities like Amsterdam, Copenhagen, Malmo, Helsinki and Hamburg - all major cities where cyclists can ride in safety and do so in very large numbers. Why? Because they can. And keep in mind, Malmo and Helsinki have similar or more severe winters than ours.
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Fully cover RSV vaccine
Re: As immunization rates dip, Haldimand-Norfolk's top doc takes on anti-vaxxers, Oct. 7
As far as taking on anti-vaxxers, maybe the medical officer of health can take on Health Canada and get the RSV vaccine covered. Last time I checked that vaccine costs anywhere from $175 to $350 and if you do not have private insurance to cover part of the cost, you are out of pocket. Anti-vaxxers can't take most of the blame here; most of the noncompliance lies with Health Canada. The flu shot and COVID-19 shots are 100 per cent covered, so the reason "we're losing this battle" with this vaccine is noncoverage.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Can't blame the Liberals for this one
Re: Blame Liberals for falling birth rate, Oct. 4
One can blame many things on the Liberals if they are so inclined. However, a decline in population growth has more to do with women having children later because of education and establishing a career. Indeed, many choose not to have children because of climate change and uncertainty in today's world.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
System keeps people in poverty
I was leaving work earlier this week and a homeless woman asked me who she should complain to. She had just bought her groceries with social assistance and returned to find her tent removed by the city without notice. I didn't think sending her to the police would provide any help, so I sent her to city hall.
We talk about helping the homeless, but there can be no getting out of poverty if we are giving to the poor with one hand and then later that day taking away everything again with the other hand. This is unconscionable.
David La Rush, Hamilton
Doing nothing is not the answer
Re: Sending hope and love, Oct. 3
Enid Aaron's description of how dangerous and frustrating driving is these days sounded so similar to mine that, at first, my husband thought I was the author. Drivers recklessly criss-cross lanes and cut off others, outrageously tailgate, speed and endlessly lane change with no signals. You name it, Enid's experience is mine. I drive in Hamilton and to Toronto once or twice a week and deal with these scary drivers all the time. None of them seem to have learned the rules of the road; many think this behaviour will get them to their destination faster. Instead, there are daily collisions (I refuse to use the word "accident" anymore) that slow us all down.
Of course, despite the huge volume of traffic, we could do something about it - such as highway cameras that photograph tailgating and speeding and mega-fines to act as a deterrent. Doing nothing is not the answer.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Self-checkouts sending me online
Re: Checking out from self-checkouts, Oct. 4
Lorraine Sommerfeld hit the nail on the head; we all hate the self-checkout lanes. I was fussing with one just trying to get out of the store and talking to the assistant, he hated the self checkout as much as I did. I am not trained in checkout and don't want to learn the four-digit codes for bananas and apples. If stores want to force me into self-checkout, I can stay home and order online where the process is a whole lot easier and I save gas.
Phil Davis, Burlington
The story behind the picture
"Seniors cause a stir": This headline in last Wednesday's Spectator and the photograph of the person on stilts depicted a captivating feature of the rally held in Dundas. Elders for Climate Sanity, a subgroup of Hamilton 350, was responsible for staging Hamilton's participation in a Canada-wide celebration of National Seniors Day organized by Seniors for Climate in Toronto. Seventy or more rallies of this kind were staged in cities and towns all across Canada.
While the goal of the Dundas rally was described as highlighting "frustration with government inaction on the climate emergency," the background of the photo depicting the Royal Bank of Canada (RBC) is also of considerable significance. Most of the signs worn by participants and posters drew attention to the fact, RBC is the second-largest bank in the world and loans billions of dollars of its customers money to the fossil fuel industry. The burning of fossil fuels is destroying the resource that provides life for all living creatures: the Earth.
Elders for Climate Sanity want RBC to face up to the truth. Stop investing in fossil fuels and invest in renewable energy instead. In advertising, stop "greenwashing" and using terms such as clean, green and sustainable. Stop violating Indigenous rights including the Wet'suwet'en in northern B.C., who struggle to protect their land and water from TC Energy's Coastal GasLink pipeline financed by RBC and stop funding weapons manufacturers. RBC owns millions of dollars of General Dynamics and Palantir shares. These companies are selling weapons and surveillance technology used in the Palestinian genocide.
Don Brown, Hamilton, on behalf of Seniors for Climate Sanity
Hometown needs drastic action
I live in Alberta but subscribe to the Spectator to keep in touch with Hamilton, the community I was born in and is my former home. As I read the Spectator every day, I'm shocked to learn how far Hamilton has gone downhill, including social chaos and crime. I wonder if Hamiltonians are like frogs in water that is reaching the boiling point and don't realize it. I fear only drastic action will return Hamilton to the safe multicultural community it used to be. Hopefully someone in the Hammer can step forward and lead the city back as it quickly approaches the point of no return. Time will tell if Hamilton can be saved, but the clock is ticking.
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
Vacant unit tax is wrong strategy
Toronto has had a vacant unit tax for almost one year and I would like to see proof that anyone has rented out their home because of this new tax. I doubt any sensible taxpaying citizen would think about renting their home considering how much could be lost if one gets an undesirable non-paying tenant in his home; the new landlord homeowner could lose hundreds of thousands of dollars.
It would be better if the Landlord and Tenant Board found a way to evict non-paying or bad tenants faster and if landlords could get back money lost through nonpayment and vandalism. Perhaps tenants could pay $1,000 into a fund that landlords could access if needed.
It seems socialist progressive councils are lazy and have a poor sense for business, trying to get more money by trying to make homeowners responsible for looking after renters, when it is their responsibility to find enough accommodation. The homeowner takes all the risk and the city sloughs off making rental units available to responsible taxpaying homeowners, who worked hard to purchase a home and not be able to use it the way they want to.
The vacant unit tax is going to cost way more than it is worth.
Steve Berenbaum, Waterdown
CAPTION: In all major cities where cyclists can ride in safety, they do so in large numbers. Why? Because they can, a reader argues.

Subject: Lessors; Respiratory syncytial virus; Vaccines; Native rights; Fossil fuels; Climate change; Cities; Homeowners

Document 382 of 500

Up to $89M annually lost if development charges frozen, Hamilton staff say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/up-89m-annually-lost-if-development-charges/docview/3114143091/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Up+to+%2489M+annually+lost+if+development+charges+frozen%2C+Hamilton+staff+say&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Finance staff have yet to see the fine print but fret Hamilton stands to lose tens of millions in revenue through a freeze in development charges as part of a new federal housing infrastructure program.
A condition of the Canada Housing Infrastructure Fund (CHIF) is a three-year freeze on the fees that municipalities charge developers to pay for services like sewage and water pipes to accompany growth.
That freeze could mean $44 million to $89 million a year in foregone revenue, depending on the pace of development, finance staff estimate in a recent report - but point out several details of the federal rollout still need to be clarified.
"Ideally, we would expect significantly more funding than that under this new program in order to actually advance objectives, beyond just being made whole," Kirk Weaver, acting director of financial planning, administration and policy, told councillors.
Municipalities depend on development charges to pay for infrastructure that's needed to service growth, such as sewer pipes, roads, recreation centres and firehalls.
If not through the charges to developers - or upper levels of government - the funds for such infrastructure has to come from somewhere, city finance staff point out.
But as of late, there's a growing focus on reducing local development fees, suggests Mike Zegarac, general manager of finance and corporate services.
"There seems to be an energy afoot for municipalities to assume these costs and pass them on to taxpayers and ratepayers," he told councillors last week.
The $6-billion CHIF program, which the federal Liberals announced in the 2024 budget, is meant to "accelerate the construction and upgrading of critical housing infrastructure" for new homes.
Of that total, $1 billion is for municipalities to address "urgent infrastructure needs" and $5 billion is for provinces to put toward "long-term priorities," the federal government says.
To receive their share, provincial governments must "commit to key actions that increase housing supply."
That includes the development charge freeze and other measures such as adopting a National Building Code and mandating municipal land-use policies that permit up to four units per lot "as of right."
"We are in a housing crisis and need everyone to work together to solve it," a spokesperson for federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser told The Spectator, calling development charges "a fee on new housing."
"They increase the cost of homes for everyone and limit potential supply while adding a financial burden on people, particularly those looking to buy or rent. During a housing crisis, we don't think you should raise taxes on housing," Sofia Ouslis said via email.
The CHIF "seeks to support critical housing infrastructure so that development charges are not relied upon," Ouslis said.
Hamilton city councillors, meanwhile, have expressed skepticism that mandated breaks or freezes on development charges translate into savings for homebuyers.
"The housing market is still the housing market," Coun. Brad Clark said in reaction to staff's CHIF report last week.
In the report, staff say the program "could alleviate financial barriers for developers," but it's still uncertain if the program will offer "sufficient compensation" to make up for lost development charge revenue.
The prospective freeze "feels like a bit of déjà vu," Zegarac told councillors.
After hearing from municipalities, he noted, Ontario's Conservative government "walked back" a mandatory phase-in of development charges under its Bill 23, the More Homes Built Faster Act.
Senior levels of government "could focus on their own tool box first" rather than look to diminish the municipal revenue source, Zegarac suggested.
Ontario's Tory government hasn't yet signed a deal with Ottawa for the CHIF. If one isn't signed by Jan. 1, the federal government says it will deal with municipalities directly.
A spokesperson for Housing Minister Paul Calandra didn't say if an agreement is on the horizon but told The Spectator via email the province is "working with municipalities ... to develop a Team Ontario approach that responds constructively to the federal government and results in an outcome that reflects the unique perspectives and roles that municipalities play in getting homes built."
"We continue to advocate to the federal government for fairness, predictability, flexibility and resources that are needed to protect, renew and expand infrastructure and housing across the province of Ontario," press secretary Bianca Meta wrote.
'Pointing fingers'
The debate over development charges' role in the housing crunch comes as the federal Liberal government teeters after the NDP ripped up the parties' supply-and-confidence agreement.
In Ontario, where there's also speculation about an early election, municipal leaders have called upon senior-level governments to play a greater role in tackling the housing crisis.
Hamilton, in particular, has emphasized it's outspending the two upper-level governments on the mammoth file as it contends with packed shelters and tents pitched in parks.
The city says it expects to spend $186 million on housing and homelessness through the 2024 budget and in-year council approvals, which is $39 million more than 2023.
Of that, $41 million is from provincial government and $20 million from Ottawa, with the city covering $125 million, or 67 per cent.
In a recent social-media post, Mike Moffatt, senior director of the Smart Prosperity Institute, noted municipalities in Ontario collected $4 billion in development charges in 2022 but spent less than $3 billion of that.
Together, they have more than $10 billion in "unspent" development charges, noted Moffatt, who couldn't be immediately be reached for comment.
In a op-ed this past August, Moffatt wrote the province had "allowed" municipalities to hike development charges "by tens of thousands of dollars per home, increasing costs to new renters and homebuyers, and causing some projects to be shelved altogether."
As of Dec. 31, Hamilton's development charge reserve balance sat at $548.7 million, an increase of $81.55 million from the previous year, a recent city staff report noted.
But if all approved capital funding were to be spent on projects and debt funding drawn from the reserves, the 2023 balance would be a deficit of $262.6 million.
Any council-approved exemptions to development charges, such as for non-profit organizations, must be recouped from other sources. The city's backlog of unfunded exemptions was nearly $70 million as of Dec. 31.
A variety of factors have jacked house prices, but development charges have been the "biggest single cost driver," says Mike Collins-Williams, CEO of the West End Home Builders' Association.
Rising costs - whether it's lumber, land or development charges - have contributed to a "price floor" that is beyond what the market is willing to pay, resulting in the current sluggish construction activity.
"We desperately need to lower the price floor," he said. "That includes development charges and a range of other things - some of which are on the industry and some of which are on the government."
All three levels of government must work together, including a "stronger role" from senior levels, to build infrastructure for growth, he said.
But "we're turning the corner," Collins-Williams added, pointing to the CHIF and other federal and provincial programs.
Coun. Alex Wilson, however, argues the private market "has failed" to solve the housing crisis as senior governments focus on "pointing fingers when no housing is being built."
A sounder approach, Wilson suggested, would be to flow CHIF's $6 billion directly to its 17 eligible cities (with 300,000-plus populations) for use by nonmarket providers, including CityHousing Hamilton.
With that amount divided equally, $300 million for CityHousing would make "such a big dent in actually building our way out of the housing crisis."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser speaks at Hamilton City Hall in July during a funding announcement as Mayor Andrea Horwath looks on.Coun. Alex Wilson says the private market "has failed" to solve the housing crisis as senior governments focus on "pointing fingers when no housing is being built."Senior levels of government "could focus on their own tool box first" rather than look to diminish the municipal revenue source, says Mike Zegarac, general manager of finance and corporate services.

Toronto's new Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton slams Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-new-nobel-laureate-geoffrey-hinton-slams/docview/3114142983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+new+Nobel+laureate+Geoffrey+Hinton+slams+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Geoffrey Hinton, Toronto's new Nobel laureate in physics, has some choice words for Ontario premier Doug Ford.
The University of Toronto professor emeritus and artificial intelligence pioneer appeared on CBC's "Metro Morning" on Tuesday, shortly after being awarded the prize, where he criticized Ford's handling of the Science Centre.
"Don't elect someone who's going to destroy the Science Centre."
That was Hinton's blunt answer when asked by host David Common what he has to say to young people interested in science, given what's happening to the centre.
The 76-year-old "godfather of AI" went on to encourage youth to "follow your curiosity," adding that "the best research comes out of being curious about how things work.
"Everybody's curious. And science is just being efficient about your curiosity so that you discover how things actually work... That's how you get the best research done."
Hinton and Princeton University researcher John Hopfield were awarded the Nobel Prize in physics on Tuesday morning for their "foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks," according to the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.
With files from the Canadian Press
CAPTION: British-Canadian cognitive psychologist and computer scientist Geoffrey Hinton, known as the "godfather of AI," speaks with Nick Thompson of The Atlantic (off frame) during the Collision Tech Conference at the Enercare Centre in Toronto on June 28, 2023.

Why I'm proud to be a 'tree hugger'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-im-proud-be-tree-hugger/docview/3114142933/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+I%27m+proud+to+be+a+%27tree+hugger%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, images of the destruction of the 800 or so trees at Ontario Place circulated online the morning after they were cut down, the action hidden behind a tall barrier, in the darkness of the night.
Like criminals doing a dirty deed.
As long as the trees were still there, a faint hope flickered there could be a chance for change. But not now. Not with the trees gone. All those years of life, giving to us without judgment, their shade from the sun, their life-sustaining oxygen, gone.
The story "The Giving Tree" by Shel Silverstein comes to mind, about a tree and a boy who becomes a man. The tree gives to the boy everything it has, shade to protect from the sun, branches to climb and play on, fruit to sustain and feed him. As he grows into a man, the demands shift to those of destruction. The tree is cut down to nothing but a stump the man can only sit on while he ponders his now very sad life. Pretty dark for a kid's book.
Premier Doug Ford will disparage those who are angry at this action as tree huggers, like it's a bad thing. Like he did when he used a grasshopper to mock people who protest the needless construction of Highway 413, or some unnecessary-to-human-life minnow that inhabits a creek that will be destroyed, damaging forever the natural watershed that moves water along creeks, streams and rivers and drains into the Great Lakes.
Extreme weather events are no longer on the horizon, they are here.
They've been here for a while, slowly creeping up, year after year. Right now, the southeastern U.S. is reeling from the horrific effects of Hurricane Helene. Its 600-mile (965-kilometre) path of destruction stretches from Florida through Georgia, into the Carolinas and Tennessee. Whole towns have been wiped out by flooding and landslides. Hundreds are dead and more missing.
This past July, a severe weather event led to flash flooding in the GTHA causing $940 million in insured damages according to the Insurance Bureau of Canada. Who knows what the real cost is when uninsured losses are considered?
Climate change is here, now, and extreme weather events will increase. It's not even a question of whether a person drives an electric car or gas-fuelled guzzler when nations are bombing the hell out of each other, spewing contaminants in the air and water.
With a nuclear threat never more present and people actually talking about a Third World War, what hope does the planet have, do we have, on surviving the next 100 years?
Yikes, that's pretty dark too.
People plant trees not for today but for tomorrow. It takes more than just digging a hole and planting a tree. Some of the ones I've planted that took are growing nicely, slowly, but steadily over the years. I marvel at the size of the tree in front of my house - a city tree, so free to me - as it towers over the roof. In the backyard, the bush that became a tree sustains all kinds of bird, animal and insect life. There's a woodpecker from time to time. Robins have nested and hatched babies. I swear they come back and visit now and then.
There were birds that made the trees in Ontario Place home. Countless birds have been distressed and displaced. How many have come back, flying over the remains, confused as to the events before them. How many died? What about the other wildlife? Where did it all go?
Cutting down trees is going in the wrong direction if we want a livable future. People need to be planting trees everywhere a tree can grow.
I'm happy to be a tree hugger. I recognize and respect the role that trees, many with a lifespan that far outpaces any human, has in sustaining life. Last Thursday was a sad day and not just for the birds.
People need to be planting trees everywhere a tree can grow
CAPTION: Cutting down trees is going in the wrong direction if we want a liveable future, Margaret Shkimba writes.

Subject: Trees; Creeks & streams; Birds

Title: Why I'm proud to be a 'tree hugger'
