Document 385 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3114090002/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: People will bike if they feel safe
Re: Turn bylaw on sidewalk cyclists, Oct. 3
I've pretty much had it up to here with the anti-bike-lane crowd. The latest letter bemoaned a possible increase in fines for parking in bike lanes, while observing "most (cyclists) ride illegally on the sidewalk."
As a 73-year-old cyclist, if my route does require a few blocks where the only other choice is risking life and limb on a four-lane road with no protected lane then damn right I'll choose the sidewalk, with due respect and courtesy to pedestrians. Otherwise, I stay in my lane ... if there is one.
I wish Doug Ford (given his latest idiotic musings on the subject) would pay a quick visit to European cities like Amsterdam, Copenhagen, Malmo, Helsinki and Hamburg - all major cities where cyclists can ride in safety and do so in very large numbers.
Why? Because they can.
And keep in mind, Malmo and Helsinki have similar or more severe winters than ours.
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Fully cover RSV vaccine
Re: As immunization rates dip, Haldimand-Norfolk's top doc takes on anti-vaxxers, Oct. 7
As far as taking on anti-vaxxers, maybe the medical officer of health can take on Health Canada and get the RSV vaccine covered.
Last time I checked that vaccine costs anywhere from $175 to $350 and if you do not have private insurance to cover part of the cost, you are out of pocket.
Anti-vaxxers can't take most of the blame here; most of the noncompliance lies with Health Canada. The flu shot and COVID-19 shots are 100 per cent covered, so the reason "we're losing this battle" with this vaccine is noncoverage.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Can't blame Liberals for this
Re: Blame Liberals for falling birth rate, Oct. 4
One can blame many things on the Liberals if they are so inclined. However, a decline in population growth has more to do with women having children later because of education and establishing a career.
Indeed, many choose not to have children because of climate change and uncertainty in today's world.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
System keeps people in poverty
I was leaving work earlier this week and a homeless woman asked me who she should complain to. She had just bought her groceries with social assistance and returned to find her tent removed by the city without notice.
I didn't think sending her to the police would provide any help, so I sent her to city hall.
We talk about helping the homeless, but there can be no getting out of poverty if we are giving to the poor with one hand and then later that day taking away everything again with the other hand. This is unconscionable.
David La Rush, Hamilton
Doing nothing is not the answer
Re: Sending hope and love, Oct. 3
Enid Aaron's description of how dangerous and frustrating driving is these days sounded so similar to mine that, at first, my husband thought I was the author.
Drivers recklessly criss-cross lanes and cut off others, outrageously tailgate, speed and endlessly lane change with no signals. You name it, Enid's experience is mine.
I drive in Hamilton and to Toronto once or twice a week and deal with these scary drivers all the time. None of them seem to have learned the rules of the road; many think this behaviour will get them to their destination faster. Instead, there are daily collisions (I refuse to use the word "accident" anymore) that slow us all down.
Of course, despite the huge volume of traffic, we could do something about it - such as highway cameras that photograph tailgating and speeding and mega-fines to act as a deterrent.
Doing nothing is not the answer.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Don't force self-checkouts
Re: Checking out from self-checkouts, Oct. 4
Lorraine Sommerfeld hit the nail on the head; we all hate the self-checkout lanes.
I was fussing with one just trying to get out of the store and talking to the assistant, he hated the self-checkout as much as I did.
I am not trained in checkout and don't want to learn the four-digit codes for bananas and apples.
If stores want to force me into self-checkout, I can stay home and order online where the process is a whole lot easier and I save gas.
Phil Davis, Burlington

Up to $89M annually lost if development charges frozen, Hamilton staffers say: 'Just being made whole' to cover losses of new federal housing infrastructure program not enough, according to finance team

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/up-89m-annually-lost-if-development-charges/docview/3114089772/se-2?accountid=15090

In a op-ed this past August, Moffatt wrote the province had "allowed" municipalities to hike development charges "by tens of thousands of dollars per home, increasing costs to new renters and homebuyers, and causing some projects to be shelved altogether."
A variety of factors have jacked house prices, but development charges have been the "biggest single cost driver," says Mike Collins-Williams, CEO of the West End Home Builders' Association.
Rising costs - whether it's lumber, land or development charges - have contributed to a "price floor" that is beyond what the market is willing to pay, resulting in the current sluggish construction activity.
"We desperately need to lower the price floor," he said. "That includes development charges and a range of other things - some of which are on the industry and some of which are on the government."
All three levels of government must work together, including a "stronger role" from senior levels, to build infrastructure for growth, he said. But "we're turning the corner," Collins-Williams added, pointing to the CHIF and other federal and provincial programs.
Coun. Alex Wilson, however, argues the private market "has failed" to solve the housing crisis as senior governments focus on "pointing fingers when no housing is being built."
A sounder approach, Wilson suggested, would be to flow CHIF's $6 billion directly to its 17 eligible cities (with 300,000-plus populations) for use by nonmarket providers, including CityHousing Hamilton.
With that amount divided equally, $300 million for CityHousing would make "such a big dent in actually building our way out of the housing crisis."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at
The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser speaks at Hamilton City Hall in July during a funding announcement as Mayor Andrea Horwath looks on.Senior levels of government "could focus on their own tool box first" rather than look to diminish the municipal revenue source, says Mike Zegarac, general manager of finance and corporate services.Coun. Alex Wilson says the private market "has failed" to solve the housing crisis as governments focus on "pointing fingers when no housing is being built."Spectator file photoSpectator file photo

Title: Up to $89M annually lost if development charges frozen, Hamilton staffers say:   'Just being made whole' to cover losses of new federal housing infrastructure program not enough, according to finance team

Canada's bail system is under fire, but how does it actually work?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-bail-system-is-under-fire-how-does/docview/3114088802/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+bail+system+is+under+fire%2C+but+how+does+it+actually+work%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's bail system is once again being questioned after the revelation that the man accused of shooting a police officer in Toronto last Wednesday was out on bail at the time.
In January 2023 and again this summer, Canada's premiers sent letters to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau asking for the bail system to be reformed and strengthened to prevent people who have been accused of a crime and released on bail from committing more offences.
Despite the government making changes to the system at the beginning of this year, reoffending still seems to be a prevalent issue.
How does the bail system work in Canada?
In Canada, some people who have been charged with a criminal offence are allowed to be released from police custody while they wait for their trial.
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms says that all people have a right to liberty and to be assumed innocent until proven guilty. Access to "reasonable bail" is also recognized as a constitutional right in the Charter, meaning people can't be denied bail without "just cause."
According to the Canadian Department of Justice's website, when determining that someone should not be granted bail, finding just cause requires considering whether the accused offender needs to be detained to ensure they go to their scheduled court date, to keep the public - including victims - safe or to maintain public trust in the justice system.
Decisions made in the Supreme Court of Canada (SCC) within the last decade have set the precedent that for most alleged crimes, the accused should be released on bail at the earliest opportunity with as few restrictions as possible
Who's responsible for what?
The federal, provincial and territorial governments share responsibility for Canada's criminal justice system.
According to information on the Department of Justice website, the federal government is responsible for enacting criminal law and procedures, including those relating to bail.
On the other hand, provincial and territorial governments are responsible for prosecuting most criminal offences that happen in their jurisdiction, conducting bail hearings related to those offences and enforcing conditions on those who have been released on bail.
The Government of Canada made reforms to the bail system in collaboration with the provinces, law enforcement, community organizations and Indigenous partners that came into effect in January.
Some of the changes address serious and violent repeat offences and the risks posed by intimate partner violence by making it the accused's responsibility to prove that they should be released while they await trial instead of assuming they should be.
When asked to comment on the state of the system, Minister of Justice Arif Virani's spokesperson said in an email to the Star that there just isn't enough court space to hold contested bail hearings, "so (the courts) are consenting to bail."
"Ontario must step up and ensure their courts and prosecutors are well resourced, that provincially appointed Justices of the Peace are applying the law, and that bail reviews are considered when there are concerns that bail was granted improperly," the spokesperson said. "It's time to stop deflecting and start enforcing the laws that we passed in collaboration with the provinces and territories."
To address concerns about crime, Premier Doug Ford recently appointed Brampton North MPP Graham McGregor to a new role in Ontario's Cabinet called the Associate Minister of Auto Theft and Bail Reform.
"The federal government is responsible for the Criminal Code and while Ontario appoints the judges, they write the instruction manual," McGregor's spokesperson said in an email to the Star when asked to comment on calls for reform and how he plans to address the issue provincially.
"Their lenient system created the circumstances that resulted in an Ontario police officer getting shot in the stomach at five in the afternoon," the statement said. "Enough is enough."
Is reform working?
Part of the challenge in reforming bail and addressing reoffenders is that there is "a lot of misinformation right now about the state of the bail system," said Shakir Rahim, director of criminal justice at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association (CCLA).
"One of the narratives I think that has been pushed by some in the public sphere is that bail is somehow easy to get," said Rahim. But a CCLA report finding that 70 per cent of people held in provincial and territorial courts in 2022 were there pre-trial shows that's not true, he explained.
According to Statistics Canada, that number rose to around 80 per cent in Ontario for the same period.
Rahim doesn't deny that there are important conversations to be had around public safety and how the bail system contributes to it, but "a lot of different levels of government, or police forces, want to reduce the problem to something that is based on a false conclusion," he said.
When it comes to reforming the bail system, Rahim explained "you have to have the data to study it, and you have to invest in solutions that we know are actually effective for ensuring compliance with bail conditions."
Potential solutions could look like bail beds for those who could be released but have nowhere to stay, reducing adjournments in bail hearings to speed up the process and increasing funds for legal aid services.
Efforts Rahim feels "are just not being taken up by any level of government in a meaningful way."
CAPTION: Canada's premiers have written to the Prime Minister twice asking for changes to be made to the bail system, but one expert says misinformation makes addressing the issue a challenge.

Document 388 of 500

Proud to be a tree hugger

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/proud-be-tree-hugger/docview/3114088695/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Proud+to+be+a+tree+hugger&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, images of the destruction of the 800 or so trees at Ontario Place circulated online the morning after they were cut down, the action hidden behind a tall barrier, in the darkness of the night.
Like criminals doing a dirty deed.
As long as the trees were still there, a faint hope flickered there could be a chance for change. But not now. Not with the trees gone. All those years of life, giving to us without judgment, their shade from the sun, their life-sustaining oxygen, gone.
The story "The Giving Tree" by Shel Silverstein comes to mind, about a tree and a boy who becomes a man. The tree gives to the boy everything it has, shade to protect from the sun, branches to climb and play on, fruit to sustain and feed him. As he grows into a man, the demands shift to those of destruction. The tree is cut down to nothing but a stump the man can only sit on while he ponders his now very sad life. Pretty dark for a kid's book.
Premier Doug Ford will disparage those who are angry at this action as tree huggers, like it's a bad thing. Like he did when he used a grasshopper to mock people who protest the needless construction of Highway 413, or some unnecessary-to-human-life minnow that inhabits a creek that will be destroyed, damaging forever the natural watershed that moves water along creeks, streams and rivers and drains into the Great Lakes.
Extreme weather events are no longer on the horizon, they are here.
They've been here for a while, slowly creeping up, year after year. Right now, the southeastern U.S. is reeling from the horrific effects of Hurricane Helene.
Its 965-kilometre path of destruction stretches from Florida through Georgia, into the Carolinas and Tennessee. Whole towns have been wiped out by flooding and landslides. Hundreds are dead and more missing.
This past July, a severe weather event led to flash flooding in the GTHA causing $940 million in insured damages according to the Insurance Bureau of Canada. Who knows what the real cost is when uninsured losses are considered?
Climate change is here, now, and extreme weather events will increase. It's not even a question of whether a person drives an electric car or gas-fuelled guzzler when nations are bombing the hell out of each other, spewing contaminants in the air and water.
With a nuclear threat never more present and people actually talking about a Third World War, what hope does the planet have, do we have, on surviving the next 100 years?
Yikes, that's pretty dark too.
People plant trees not for today but for tomorrow. It takes more than just digging a hole and planting a tree. Some of the ones I've planted that took are growing nicely, slowly, but steadily over the years. I marvel at the size of the tree in front of my house - a city tree, so free to me - as it towers over the roof. In the backyard, the bush that became a tree sustains all kinds of bird, animal and insect life. There's a woodpecker from time to time. Robins have nested and hatched babies. I swear they come back and visit now and then.
There were birds that made the trees in Ontario Place home. Countless birds have been distressed and displaced. How many have come back, flying over the remains, confused as to the events before them. How many died? What about the other wildlife? Where did it all go?
Cutting down trees is going in the wrong direction if we want a livable future. People need to be planting trees everywhere a tree can grow.
I'm happy to be a tree hugger. I recognize and respect the role that trees, many with a lifespan that far outpaces any human, has in sustaining life. Last Thursday was a sad day and not just for the birds.
People need to be planting trees everywhere a tree can grow
Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com.
You can follow her on X
(@menrvasofia) and "friend"
her on Facebook.
CAPTION: Cutting down trees is going in the wrong direction if we want a liveable future, Margaret Shkimba writes.Nick Lachance Toronto Star file photo

An old age security raise could pay for itself

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/old-age-security-raise-could-pay-itself/docview/3114088672/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+old+age+security+raise+could+pay+for+itself&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Most of the House of Commons supports raising old age security (OAS) payments for seniors under 75 years old by 10 per cent. However, the Liberal party opposes the increase.
No party in Canada has a majority of seats in Parliament, so the issue of retirement benefits could be the hill on which this federal government dies. Let's look at who benefits and who pays for the pension protection that could provoke the next election.
As many as 4.4 million seniors younger than 75 years old could benefit from a boost to OAS. The Parliamentary Budget Officer says boosting OAS payments for seniors under 75 years old will cost $16 billion over five years, or about 82 per cent of the amount that the proposed federal capital gains tax change will generate.
The need is real. While Statistics Canada reports there is less poverty overall in Canada, compared to before the pandemic, the number of persons in elderly families living in poverty, and elders not in families living in poverty, has soared more than 18 per cent.
There is regional variation in the distribution of seniors' poverty. Ontario saw the number of low-income elderly persons explode 25 per cent compared to 2019. In Toronto, there was almost a 37 per cent surge in seniors' poverty.
The cause of current distress among seniors is not rocket science.
COVID-19 is sweeping through the ranks of the elderly in a manner totally out of proportion with their share of the population.
More than 80 per cent of pandemic deaths are among seniors. Most COVID-19 hospitalizations are among the elderly.
The Public Health Agency of Canada notes poverty costs billions of dollars in added health-care costs every year.
That would suggest seniors' poverty alone is driving more than $1 billion - or more than 10 per cent - of the burden.
Exchanging poverty for better retirement should be a win-win.
Nonetheless, social policy should be about more than the money. It is really about the kind of future we want for ourselves, our children, our neighbours and community.
The stakes could not be higher. If we fail to forge a consensus on retirement benefits by the end of the month, we risk an election that may see a new government less inclined to support social policy, including child care, school lunches, pay equity, paid leave, dental and pharmacare.
The most recent census shows one in five people are aged 55 to 64 in Canada. That share of the population has never been higher and the pandemic has not slowed the impact of an aging population.
The window is closing to get ahead of the demographic curve and lay the foundation for healthy, generational change.
If we miss the opportunity now to adjust pensions to reflect a seniors population that is living longer, it could represent a self-inflicted wound on the body politic that injures all of us. Robust fiscal federalism offers a better path to prosperity.
Look at the impact of improving retirement benefits in the 1970s. By the 1990s, seniors' poverty plunged 74 per cent.
That change was mirrored by a 39 per cent fall in the general poverty rate during the same period. Income support programs work.
Let's not lose sight of the forest for the trees. What we desire for ourselves we want for all.
Say yes to progress, yes to retirement security and yes to economic security for all.
It is time to show that the old minority government bottle can still contain a new tonic for the good of all.
Tom Baker is a retired
union executive living
in Burlington.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Tom Baker;

Document 390 of 500

Has Poilievre burned his bridges?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/has-poilievre-burned-his-bridges/docview/3114088599/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Has+Poilievre+burned+his+bridges%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative members of Parliament must be ecstatic right now.
On Oct. 2 CTV news reported they have now opened a 21.1-point lead over Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals. And not only that, but the Liberals are also now in a statistical tie with Jagmeet Singh and the New Democratic Party.
For those who oppose the Conservatives, this is a nightmare. Think back to the federal elections of 1993, 1997 and 2000. In those elections the parties on the right (Reform and Progressive Conservative) split the conservative vote and allowed the Liberals to "come up the middle" and win huge majority victories.
We may very well see the same, only with the Conservatives coming up the middle and winning a majority with the "progressive" vote split.
That all being said, there may be several factors that could still derail a potential Poilievre government.
First, polls show Canadians are exhausted with Trudeau. There is little to nothing that he can do to change this opinion among voters. But the possibility is there, that if the Liberals changed their leader, a new leader may close that gap in the polls. Poilievre and his party are in the lead due to fatigue and anger with Trudeau. He isn't in the lead due to voters embracing him. Remove Trudeau from this equation and we may very well see some changes in the polls.
Another factor involved here is Poilievre's way of operating. He is quite possibly the most vicious, opposition leader Canada has ever had. He attacks all the other parties relentlessly. That certainly gets him a lot of media coverage. However, it doesn't get him any goodwill in Parliament. He may very well be the most despised opposition leader in Canadian history.
Which brings me to my final point. If Trudeau steps down and is replaced by say Mark Carney or Chrystia Freeland, is it possible that the gap in the polling numbers becomes smaller? Usually, a new leader will give their party at least a short-term spike in the polls.
Let us imagine a scenario in which Carney becomes Liberal leader. And in the upcoming election the Conservatives are the victors, but are held to a minority.
The usual development in this case would be the Liberal government would then resign and the Conservatives would take power. But one must remember that in the Parliamentary system, the party that holds the confidence of the house, forms the government. Not necessarily the party with the most seats.
Poilievre with his offensive, at times, juvenile pugnacity has probably burned all bridges with the other parties in Parliament.
The odds of any of the other parties supporting a Poilievre-led Conservative minority government, are about the same as Donald Trump apologizing for his rude, coarse behaviour and becoming a polished, erudite gentleman.
In this case, I would suggest if the Liberals finish with the second-most seats, they will ensure the NDP and Bloc will support them, and the Liberal leader will inform the Governor General that they can form a government and have the confidence of the house. They will then form the government.
That is totally appropriate in our system.
If the NDP comes second, the Liberal government will resign, and Poilievre's Conservatives will form a government. And at the very first opportunity, the opposition will defeat his government on a confidence vote. Then you would see the Liberals and Bloc support a Singh-led NDP government.
Poilievre is riding high in the polls today. But his path to forming and staying in government is contingent on winning a majority.
He has chosen to be a nasty, at times offensive, "attack dog." That mode of operation will ensure he has no support in Parliament outside of the Conservative caucus.
Craig Wallace
is a Hamilton resident
and author of five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace OPINION;

Document 391 of 500

Justin Trudeau condemns Hamas on anniversary of Oct. 7 attacks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-condemns-hamas-on-anniversary-oct/docview/3114242481/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+condemns+Hamas+on+anniversary+of+Oct.+7+attacks&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Canadian political leaders marked the first anniversary Monday of the Hamas attacks on Israel that launched war in the Middle East, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau saying Hamas "set the region down a path of war and violence" that has since killed tens of thousands, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre blaming Iran and its terrorist proxies.
Premier Doug Ford echoed the two federal leaders in reiterating solidarity with Israel, and with Jews in Canada facing antisemitism, and called for the release of all hostages.
The federal Liberal and Conservative leaders displayed different approaches to ascribing blame and marking the toll of events that followed the initial deadly assault by Hamas on Israel.
"We unequivocally condemn Hamas for the terror they wrought," Trudeau said in a written statement released by his office.
"We mourn the Canadians and all civilians killed in the year that followed. We condemn Hamas, Hezbollah and Iran, whose senseless attacks lead to more death and instability. The scale of civilian casualties since Oct. 7 is heartbreaking and unacceptable, and all actors must comply with international law."
The prime minister mourned the loss of seven Canadian victims and an eighth who had ties to Canada and lamented the "terrifying surge in antisemitism" in Canada.
"We've seen synagogues and schools attacked, people taunted for wearing a Star of David or a Kippah, blatant antisemitic slurs becoming common, demonstrators glorifying terrorists, and denial of the horrific events of October 7. This is all reprehensible and it must stop," Trudeau said as he reiterated his government's solidarity with Jewish people, and its call for the release of remaining hostages, as well as a ceasefire to set the stage for peace talks and a two-state solution to the intractable Israel-Palestine question.
"To Jewish people and Israelis in Canada and around the world: we stand with you on this painful anniversary, and we won't relent until we see every last hostage returned home safely. We will keep working relentlessly with our allies until there is a ceasefire in the Middle East, and we will always defend Israel's right to exist in peace and security," the prime minister said.
Poilievre's statement, a more full-throated condemnation of the violence of that day a year ago, outlined in graphic detail that "genocidal and sadistic terrorists carried out the worst attack on Jews since the Holocaust."
"They burned people alive, tortured children in front of their parents, mutilated dead civilians and posted these demonic cruelties online for the world to see. All while their cowardly bosses and benefactors watched from Tehran and other faraway safe-havens. 101 hostages are still being held. Let them go."
Poilievre's statement also took aim at the Liberal government and Trudeau for the fallout Jews in Canada have faced.
"Meanwhile, Jews in Canada face grotesque antisemitism in the streets and by (sic) the two-faced and weak leadership of their Prime Minister placating lawless mobs."
He hailed Israel and the Jewish people who "live on and fight on. Western democratic values depend on destroying Tehran's proxy armies of Hamas and Hezbollah."
Alluding to the slogan "Never again," widely understood as a post-Second World War vow by the United Nations community to prevent another Holocaust when it passed international laws against genocide, Poilievre said that fight is the present one, as he stated his solidarity with the Jewish Zionist cause.
"Canada's Conservatives have not and will not waver. We won't bow to the radical, woke, anti-Zionist Jew haters. Because 'Never Again' is now, tomorrow and every day after. We unapologetically stand with Israel through fire and water., and unite with Jews to say, 'Am Yisrael Chai!" reads Poilievre's statement.
Poilievre's statement did not allude to the ensuing Palestinian casualty toll in Gaza as a result of Israel's bombardment campaign.
Palestinian health authorities say Israel has killed more than 42,000 Palestinians in the past year. It does not distinguish between militant and civilian deaths.
Violence in the region is escalating, as Israel engages with Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon. Iran has fired a barrage of missiles at Israel. On Monday, Israel's ambassador to the UN vowed again that Israel would retaliate against Iran, as the United States and other global leaders are urging restraint.
Trudeau and Poilievre are expected to attend an event in solidarity with the Jewish community in Ottawa to mark the anniversary late Monday.
Ford was expected to attend a similar event in Toronto.
In his statement, Ford underscored the deaths of nearly 1,200 people in the original Hamas attack and the kidnapping of around 240 more, and expressed sympathy with the "many people in Ontario's Jewish community who maintain deep and close ties with family and loved ones in Israel."
Ford called for the "safe and immediate return of the remaining hostages being held captive by Hamas, which is the best way to bring about lasting peace in the region."
And the premier restated his government's "strong opposition to antisemitism and hate and reiterate the need to support our Jewish friends and neighbours. We will continue to stand up against hate wherever it is found and work to foster understanding, unity and respect in our province and communities."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau holds a news conference at the Francophonie Summit in Paris on Oct. 5, 2024.

Document 393 of 500

Pierre Poilievre has a plan to attract very specific voters. Here's how he is doing it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-has-plan-attract-very-specific/docview/3113527372/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+has+a+plan+to+attract+very+specific+voters.+Here%27s+how+he+is+doing+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-He's railed against mainstream media he says is "bought-and-paid-for" by the Trudeau government. He routinely bickers with a parliamentary press gallery he accuses of being biased.
But on a warm June evening at his Stornoway residence in Ottawa, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was on a media charm offensive.
"We're meeting here today so that we can honour this incredible group of independent media that tell the stories of our communities and hold governments accountable," Poilievre, joined by about a dozen MPs and his wife Ana, said to an audience. "I told my team that I wanted to bring together the independent voices in the media, not those that are controlled by the government or the big corporations, that are controlled by Bell or Shaw or Rogers or some other big conglomerate connected to the government, but the independent and diverse voices that tell the real story."
That audience - journalists from so-called "ethnic" or third-language media whose readers and viewers come from various cultural communities in Canada - could be a pathway to political gold for the Conservative leader.
It's one feature of Poilievre's strategy to reach diverse voters in Liberal dominated areas like the Greater Toronto Area and the Vancouver-Surrey region.
In that "informal" garden party gathering, Poilievre spent between 10 to 20 minutes speaking one-on-one with each invitee.
"I could see him absorbing a lot of information," said Darshan Maharaja, a political commentator who attended the event.
"Maybe other leaders have done this, but the mileage that I saw there was tremendous."
Since taking leadership of the Conservative Party in 2022, Poilievre has tried to make inroads in cultural communities by routinely speaking to ethnic media across Canada, in stark contrast with his frequently-stated disdain for the mainstream press.
As major news networks' audiences shrink and more than 500 local papers have shut down since 2008, there are still at least 1,200 ethnic media publishers across Canada, according to the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada, although many of them switched to digital-only over the pandemic. As immigrants become a growing proportion of Canada's population, the audiences of ethnic media represent a vital target for all political parties.
On his cross-country tour this summer, Poilievre did more than 50 interviews and roundtables with local and ethnic media, his office told the Star, although it did not differentiate between the two.
The Star watched five such ethnic media roundtables and two interviews by the Conservative leader in recent months, and spoke to some of the journalists who took part and former Conservative insiders to understand what they reveal about Poilievre and his strategy.
The interviews and press conferences vary in duration but can include dozens of journalists from a wide range of groups in Canada - Punjabis, Ukrainians, Portuguese and more. In those appearances, Poilievre repeated talking points on affordability and crime, but also gave detailed answers to questions related to specific communities in Canada, delving more deeply into his views on cross-cultural relations across the country and his visions for immigration and foreign policy.
He vigorously defended Canadian immigrants and international students who he said are feeling the brunt of a mishandled system under the Trudeau government, but said his immigration policy would shift greater focus on high-skilled workers and crack down on fraudulent asylum claims.
He received several questions about the Indian government's treatment of Sikhs and its alleged foreign interference, in particular at a Punjabi Press Club of B.C. event in Surrey in August, and declared in several roundtables he's the only political leader fighting for a direct flight to Amritsar, a city in Punjab, India - a commitment the prime minister made to Sikh Canadians this spring, according to one media report.
"I would encourage the community to tell us where and when you need us," Poilievre told a journalist who asked about his engagement with Black Canadians at a Mississauga roundtable earlier this month. "I will be there personally to show up for the events, the meetings and the engagements that matter most to you."
To another question on Hinduphobia in Canada, he said, "I just detest how the prime minister has divided our people."
"You know, we were getting along in this country. Now look - everybody's fighting," he said.
That Poilievre and the Conservatives are making this effort is not new. Jason Kenney, the minister for immigration and multiculturalism in Stephen Harper's government, was well-known for his success in courting newcomers - efforts that were widely seen, and credited by Harper himself, as vital to the Conservatives' 2011 majority victory.
But since Trudeau came to power in 2015, no Conservative leader had emulated that success, said Maharaja.
Poilievre, unlike past Conservative leaders, has taken it upon himself to lead that effort now, being present in multicultural communities - even before an election campaign - rather than delegating outreach to others around him.
That studies following the 2011 election later found that immigrant voters did not flock to the Conservatives in as large numbers as initially thought only goes to show how well Poilievre is doing. A Leger poll for OMNI news this week found the Tories are now the most popular political party among immigrants, with 44 per cent of respondents saying they would cast their ballot for Poilievre's Conservatives if an election were held today, compared to 26 per cent for the Liberals and 19 per cent for the NDP. The Conservatives and Liberals were, however, tied at 37 per cent apiece among newcomers who have been in Canada for less than six years.
Over the summer, the Tory leader appeared to spend more time in the GTA than both Trudeau and the NDP's Jagmeet Singh, visiting several cultural events like a Tamil festival in Scarborough and a centre for newcomers in North York. He is reaching out to a wider range of cultural groups than are the Liberals, according to a senior Liberal who is engaged in the governing party's efforts to reach the same communities.
Compared to past Tory leaders, "Pierre's style is a lot more intimate," Maharaja, an Indo-Canadian accountant who has written about politics for several years, told the Star.
The Tories have faced criticism in the past for targeted messaging and advertising in ethnic media that was controversial or misleading, or that spun a message that was different than that conveyed in mainstream media channels in English and French. In the 2015 election campaign, for example, the Tories dumped one GTA candidate after he wrote a homophobic editorial in a Punjabi newspaper.
Former Tory insiders who spoke to the Star said Poilievre's initiative in those settings shows message discipline and audience awareness that highlight his ability as a communicator and his desire to make inroads with those communities that have typically voted Liberal.
"He's building on what Conservative leaders have been trying to do for the last 20 years, which is to make many of these community members realize that they are actually small-c Conservatives," said Dimitri Soudas, a former director of communications for Harper. "Obviously, there's no absolute value to anything, but a good majority of those communities, regardless of where they come from in the world, believe in free enterprise, believe in freedom, low taxes and so on."
It also reflects a changing media landscape and a political environment that is becoming increasingly hostile to the Trudeau government.
Nitin Chopra, a journalist who has covered politics for a South Asian audience in Ontario since 2016, said Poilievre's message is resonating in many communities that have typically voted Liberal because it relates to issues people are concerned about, such as housing, immigration and crime.
Still, he wonders if this level of engagement will remain if Poilievre's Conservatives take power.
Some ethnic media journalists, many of whom run their own media channels where they are the reporter, the businessmen and the garbage men at the same time, can occasionally be explicit in their approval of Poilievre's message.
In one such press conference this summer, one journalist referred to a smiling Poilievre as the "future of Canada" and the "future prime minister."
"At the end of the day, this is part of the reality, and we as Conservatives, oftentimes feel, sometimes rightfully so ... that some traditional media are out to get us," Soudas said, referencing a recent CTV news story that altered Poilievre's remarks due to what it said was a "misunderstanding during the editing process." The Conservative leader vowed to blacklist CTV journalists when the editing error was revealed. Two journalists involved are no longer with the broadcaster, CTV said, and the broadcaster has apologized.
"I mean, you find me one politician that doesn't want ... the media outlet or the reporter to say, 'my goodness, you're the next prime minister,'" joked Soudas.
Independent media, which is crucial to the country's diversity and serves its smaller communities, often doesn't have the business scale, resources or audience size to hold powerful federal and provincial political leaders to account.
Back in 2021, Maharaja had sharply criticized the Conservatives, arguing in a blog piece that the party had failed to make inroads with many cultural communities because of insufficient outreach to ethnic media.
He contended that if the Conservatives "wish to improve their electoral prospects in the backdrop of an electorate that is increasingly foreign-born and not native speaker of English, they must improve their ethnic media outreach, otherwise their messaging would just amount to sounds of silence," which would be "a fatal flaw" in the Conservatives' communications strategy, he wrote.
Now, he said in an interview, it's clear the Conservative party, which leads all major polls under Poilievre, has taken that advice.
With files from Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Oct. 3, 2024.

Document 394 of 500

Charging drivers only way to fix highway congestion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/charging-drivers-only-way-fix-highway-congestion/docview/3113527363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Charging+drivers+only+way+to+fix+highway+congestion&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's tunnel vision has everyone focusing on his blind spots.
Too bad most motorists refuse to look at the congestion solution in plain sight.
For all the public mockery of the premier's plan to dig under Highway 401, which came out of nowhere recently, traffic jams are no joke. But the bitter truth is that almost everyone is guilty of closing their eyes to the economics of commuting and congestion.
The critics claim to have a cleverer, better way: Why not buy back Highway 407 (technically it's been leased, not sold) for an estimated $30 billion, remove its exorbitant tolls and take the pressure off the 401?
In fact, Ford says he hasn't ruled it out.
After all, he has cheerfully cancelled tolls in the past and could well do it again in future to win more votes.
In truth, removing those tolls would be a false economy - leaving us no further ahead in time or money spent. Just further behind fiscally and environmentally.
In reality, the only lasting solution would be the complete opposite:
Keep those existing tolls on the 407.
And go one step further by adding new tolls to the old 401 - not to mention all other major highways and parkways encircling and choking the GTA.
What? Tolls on the 401?
We all know that toll roads are the third rail of Ontario politics.
Yes, it's unthinkable. But try this thought experiment.
Forget the pocketbook politics for a moment. Never mind the populist impulses that everyone falls for.
Not just the premier but almost everyone - politicians, pundits and the public - has become avowedly, adamantly anti-tax and anti-toll.
Of course everyone instinctively dislikes tolls, but that distaste won't get us where we need to go.
There is no such thing as a free lunch. Or a free highway.
A toll-free highway still exacts a heavy price in lost time if you're stuck in traffic, because time is money.
The cost has been estimated in the billions of dollars annually in delayed deliveries and lost productivity, not to mention people cheated out of personal time - and family time.
In a free market, everything has a price - and it's never free. Highways cost big money to build, but road tolls (unlike bridge tolls) are no longer about cost recovery.
Now, road tolls (and congestion charges for crowded downtowns) are a price signal - a way of flagging for drivers that they must choose carefully whether or when to travel, not on a whim or caprice.
Economics is all about scarcity. Pricing is way of allocating or rationing - rationally - a commodity that others are also competing for, in this case highway space.
In the real world of economics, prices are typically set at levels that the market will bear, based on the fundamentals of supply and demand.
Building (or opening) more toll-free highways tends to encourage more cars, creating greater congestion until we end up right back where we started - in bumper-to-bumper equilibrium again.
Economists and planners call it "induced demand" - a consequence of increasing the supply of free freeways.
Raise prices (tolls) higher? Demand will decrease and traffic congestion will decline.
Reduce road tolls to zero, while building or tunnelling new roads? Demand from cars will increase as more motorists clog the roads - at an enormous price in lost time.
Digging a tunnel replicates the same dynamic: If you build it - or dig it - they will come.
People will drive on and under the 401 without limits as soon as you allow unlimited access. That's the inevitably of induced demand.
Ultimately, when the flow of traffic stalls, motorists will search for alternatives: rapid transit, remote work, off-peak commutes, or toll roads that represent value for money (savings in time).
To be clear, congestion is out of control.
The question is how to win the congestion battle, not an election battle.
The reality is that road tolls are time-savers, and time is money. The problem is that voters don't want to hear about it.
The premier's pie-in-the-sky proposal to tunnel across much of the GTA is not only a road map to ruin, it is a teachable moment. But the opposition's counterproposal for a free Highway 407, which would create a free-for-all, is merely a diversion on the road to induced demand.
Many are snickering at Ford's tunnel vision. But the joke is on us if we keep looking away from economic realities.
This thought experiment on road tolls might read like a political dead end.
But more of the same wishful thinking about free highways won't get us anywhere.
Martin Regg Cohn
covers provincial politics and international affairs for Torstar.
CAPTION: The realityis that road tolls are time-savers, and time is money. The problem is that voters don't want to hear about it, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Dreamstime photo

Document 395 of 500

Frank Stronach to face sex assault charges in Brampton court on Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/frank-stronach-face-sex-assault-charges-brampton/docview/3113527112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Frank+Stronach+to+face+sex+assault+charges+in+Brampton+court+on+Monday&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Frank Stronach will commence with his defence against the sexual assault charges that he is facing on Monday.
The billionaire businessman and founder of Magna International has been accused of sexually assaulting over a dozen women over the past 50 years and is scheduled to make his first appearance to face the charges in a Brampton Courthouse Monday morning. He may appear in court or just send a legal representative.
Stronach, 92, is facing allegations from 13 alleged victims, totalling 18 charges, many of them for historical sex assaults, that took place from 1977 to 2023.
Stronach has denied the allegations and none of the charges have been tested in court.
Stronach was initially charged in early June, by three accusers, that resulted in five charges, and authorities asked for more witnesses and more victims. Later that month, Peel Police added eight additional charges from seven more accusers. Last week, three additional victims and five more charges were laid against Stronach.
The charges include sexual assault, indecent assault, rape and forcible confinement. Because some are historical sexual assaults, they involve laws that no longer exist or have changed since the alleged incidents occurred.
"He was my abductor and rapist, and I feared for my life," is what one victim exclusively told The Star earlier this summer, of the alleged 1980 rape.
She claims he sexually assaulted her when she was employed as a worker at his Aurora farm, when she was 20 years old. The victim told the Star she reported the assault to the Toronto police in 2015, but little action was taken at the time.
"We have a lot of data which totally will prove those things are lies," Stronach said in a CBC Interview in August.
Stronach was born in Austria in 1932, and emigrated to Canada in 1954 with only a few hundred dollars. He arrived in Montreal before eventually settling in Ontario, and proceeding on his path to becoming one of Canada's richest men.
In 1958, he started Multimatic Investments, which eventually merged with Magna Electronics in 1969. The name was changed to Magna International in 1973, and by the 1990s, it grew into one of the largest auto parts manufacturers in the world. He was at the head of the company for decades and it led to him being considered a titan in the Canadian business world. Stronach was inducted the Canadian Business Hall of Fame in 1996. He also briefly wrote freelance opinion columns for the Star.
Stronach was bought out of his role in Magna at 2010, and, according to company spokespeople, has had no affiliation with the company since then. Despite that, Magna recently announced an internal probe into the company's conduct in the wake of the sexual assault charges.
His children and grandchildren have been involved in a long simmering dispute over the dispersal of assets of the family's fortune. This summer, his granddaughter, Selena Stronach, asked the court to compel The Stronach Group to disclose any documents relating to any settlements or payouts related to his sexual misconduct, but the court denied the request.
Throughout his life, Stronach has dabbled in different interests, like politics, where he ran as a candidate for the Liberals for the riding of York-Simcoe in 1988, but lost. He's also become more involved with business and politics in his native Austria, including starting a political party there in the 2010s. He also has been involved in thoroughbred horse racing.
CAPTION: Billionaire businessman Frank Stronach leaves Peel Regional Police 22 Division in Brampton, July 2, 2024.Frank Stronach is facing new sexual assault charges involving additional victims and is set to appear in Brampton Court on Oct. 7.

Document 396 of 500

A raise for seniors could pay for itself

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/raise-seniors-could-pay-itself/docview/3113116065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+raise+for+seniors+could+pay+for+itself&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Most of the House of Commons supports raising old age security (OAS) payments for seniors under 75 years old by 10 per cent. However, the Liberal party opposes the increase.
No party in Canada has a majority of seats in Parliament so the issue of retirement benefits could be the hill on which this federal government dies. Let's look at who benefits and who pays for the pension protection that could provoke the next election.
As many as 4.4 million seniors younger than 75 years old could benefit from a boost to OAS. The Parliamentary Budget Officer says boosting OAS payments for seniors under 75 years old will cost $16 billion over five years, or about 82 per cent of the amount that the proposed federal capital gains tax change will generate.
The need is real. While Statistics Canada reports there is less poverty overall in Canada, compared to before the pandemic, the number of persons in elderly families living in poverty, and elders not in families living in poverty, has soared more than 18 per cent.
There is regional variation in the distribution of seniors' poverty. Ontario saw the number of low-income elderly persons explode 25 per cent compared to 2019. In Toronto there was almost a 37-per-cent surge in seniors' poverty.
The cause of current distress among seniors is not rocket science. COVID-19 is sweeping through the ranks of the elderly in a manner totally out of proportion with their share of the population.
More than 80 per cent of pandemic deaths are among seniors. Most COVID-19 hospitalizations are among the elderly.
The Public Health Agency of Canada notes poverty costs billions of dollars in added health-care costs every year. That would suggest seniors' poverty alone is driving more than $1 billion - or more than 10 per cent - of the burden. Exchanging poverty for better retirement should be a win-win.
Nonetheless, social policy should be about more than the money. It is really about the kind of future we want for ourselves, our children, our neighbours and community.
The stakes could not be higher. If we fail to forge a consensus on retirement benefits by the end of the month, we risk an election that may see a new government less inclined to support social policy, including child care, school lunches, pay equity, paid leave, dental and pharmacare.
The most recent census shows one in five people are aged 55 to 64 in Canada. That share of the population has never been higher and the pandemic has not slowed the impact of an aging population. The window is closing to get ahead of the demographic curve and lay the foundation for healthy, generational, change.
If we miss the opportunity now to adjust pensions to reflect a seniors population that is living longer, it could represent a self-inflicted wound on the body politic that injures all of us. Robust fiscal federalism offers a better path to prosperity.
Look at the impact of improving retirement benefits in the 1970s. By the 1990s, seniors' poverty plunged 74 per cent. That change was mirrored by a 39-per-cent fall in the general poverty rate during the same period. Income support programs work.
Let's not lose sight of the forest for the trees. What we desire for ourselves we want for all.
Say yes to progress, yes to retirement security and yes to economic security for all. It is time to show that the old minority government bottle can still contain a new tonic for the good of all.
CAPTION: If we miss the opportunity now to adjust pensions to reflect a seniors population that is living longer, more active and healthier lives it could represent a self-inflicted wound on the body politic that injures all of us, Tom Baker writes.
CREDIT: Tom Baker

Document 397 of 500

Treat each stranger as if they are an angel

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/treat-each-stranger-as-if-they-are-angel/docview/3113116014/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Treat+each+stranger+as+if+they+are+an+angel&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares" The Bible, Hebrews 13.
This is a familiar quote, or at least a familiar sentiment, for many. I recently considered this quote when I had opportunity to show hospitality to a sex worker. We readily put street-level sex workers into the box of "stranger," but do we consider that they might possibly be among the "angels?"
The word that is translated as "hospitality" in English Bibles is the word "philoxenia" in Greek, which literally means "love of strangers." The quote suggests we should lead with love when engaging with people we don't know, because we don't know anything about them.
We don't know their stories - we don't know where they came from, why they are in the situation they have found themselves. When we meet a "stranger," the best thing to do is to show them love, without judgment. Love casts out fear and love of stranger may result in entertaining angels unawares.
A few weekends ago, I took my turn to staff "Project Hope," Helping Hands Street Mission's Saturday nighttime (1 to 6 a.m.) support space for women who are out and about on Barton Street at night. Basically, we open our doors and space for women to stop by for a coffee, a sandwich, do their nails together and receive some free makeup and personal safety supplies. There's not much to do at that time of night - no phone calls to take or email conversations to be had, so there's lots of time to chat and listen. That evening, listening involved hearing one particular woman's life story. She needed to talk and I had plenty of time to listen.
This woman, possibly an angel in disguise, currently lives in a tent. She has a son, a daughter and a grandson she loves deeply. She has an aging mother out east whom she would love to visit before it's too late. She has a litany of historical trauma that is holding her back from being able to live a healthy life.
This stranger is one of those people that Premier Doug Ford recently said should get off their "a-s-s" and get a job. I don't think he's offering her hospitality. He just judges her as a stranger - he doesn't know her story, and he doesn't know what's preventing her from getting a job. He doesn't even know that in her mind, she does have a job - just not one that she chose or prefers. And why does she work that job? Because, despite her lifetime of trauma and the daily difficulties it triggers for her (which they haven't taken the time to hear about), the Ontario government refuses to provide her with an adequate living wage or a level of social support that can actually cover the cost of housing and food.
After speaking to me for three hours while giving her broken feet a pedicure, she laughed and said to me "Oops, I guess I didn't work at all tonight yet!" and promptly left. About 15 minutes later she walked past our front door with a man, and 30 minutes after that she came back in alone for another sandwich.
I have no idea if this stranger to whom I showed hospitality was an angel I entertained unawares. But what I know is that if I treat each stranger as if they are an angel, then there is a chance they might see that in themselves - not someone to be judged as a stranger and cast aside, but someone to be loved, worthy of hospitality, just like the rest of us.
CAPTION: A sex trade worker is pictured in downtown Vancouver. A long conversation with a Hamilton sex worker one night had Alice Plug-Buist contemplating how we treat the strangers among us.
CREDIT: Alice Plug-Buist

Title: Treat each stranger as if they are an angel

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 5, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 398 of 500

Ford undermines system by bungling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-undermines-system-bungling/docview/3113046411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+undermines+system+by+bungling&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Figuring out how to undermine support for public health care isn't rocket science. All you have to do is destroy the public system and then people will be willing to accept private health care.
This appears to be Doug Ford's strategy for weakening the strong attachment Ontarians feel for public health care, thereby enabling his government to plow ahead with plans to further open up lucrative profit opportunities for private health-care businesses.
This would explain, for instance, Ford's bizarre handling of the nursing shortage. Despite nurses being underpaid, exhausted and demoralized by the pandemic, Ford kept a one per cent a year cap on their wage increases, even as inflation spiked to eight per cent. This angered nurses and prompted them to leave the public system in large numbers, generating chaos and longer wait times - and creating a situation where the province is now paying hundreds of millions of dollars extra per year as hospitals are forced to hire nurses from private agencies at much higher rates.
This bungled handling of the nursing shortage makes no sense, unless Ford was trying to undermine the public's confidence in our health care system - in which case he appears to have succeeded.
A poll released last week found Canadians feel access to medical care has worsened in the last few years and that wait-lists are now too long. As the Globe and Mail reported on its front page, the poll found that, "as a result, 73 per cent now say they're open to major health-care reform, including a larger role for the private sector."
This finding was no doubt sweet music for Premier Ford, since it suggests his plan to further privatize Ontario's health-care system - allowing private clinics to do knee and hip replacements, cataract surgery, colonoscopies, etc. - is in line with what people want. But on closer examination, the poll presents little evidence Canadians favour more private health care. Rather the poll, conducted by the public relations firm Navigator - which is, among other things, a registered lobbyist for the private health-care company Medcan - seems largely aimed at steering respondents away from solutions that would involve strengthening the public system.
Participants were asked which of two statements most closely resembles their view: "Canada's health-care system is one of the best in the world and we should keep it as it is." Or: "Canada's health-care system does not deliver on its promises and is in need of a major reform."
Given only these two choices, 73 per cent of respondents favoured major reform over doing nothing. But what we need to know is what kind of reform people want - do they, for instance, want more privatization, or do they want reform within the public system?
When asked what their concerns were with the public system, respondents clearly indicated their main concerns were staff shortages and long wait times. The poll asks them whether, given the long wait times for surgery, they would support some surgeries being done by the private sector? Respondents largely supported this, particularly if the private surgeries were publicly funded.
What does this tell us, however, other than that people are very concerned about long surgery wait-lists? And if they're only offered one option for solving the problem - private surgery clinics - they'll support that option. Why not give them another option such as would they support our public hospitals offering additional surgeries on weekends and evenings to reduce wait-lists?
Could it be the dissatisfaction with our health-care system may be best solved - not by introducing a lot of profit-making clinics - but simply by paying nurses good wages within the public system?
Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist and a freelance
contributing columnist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Linda McQuaig OPINION Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist and a freelance contributing columnist.;

Treat each stranger you meet as if they are an angel

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/treat-each-stranger-you-meet-as-if-they-are-angel/docview/3113045538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Treat+each+stranger+you+meet+as+if+they+are+an+angel&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

is the executive director
of Helping Hands Street
Mission.

Document 400 of 500

A tunnel will not improve transport or housing: Ontario's policies of more auto dependence are heading in the wrong direction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tunnel-will-not-improve-transport-housing/docview/3113045399/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+tunnel+will+not+improve+transport+or+housing%3A+Ontario%27s+policies+of+more+auto+dependence+are+heading+in+the+wrong+direction&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anyone who has driven in Toronto during rush hour can tell you how busy the roads can get.
In the face of traffic gridlock and rising commute times, Premier Doug Ford recently announced his government is exploring the idea of building a roughly 50-kilometre tunnel beneath the 401, North America's busiest highway.
That announcement came days after the Ford government said it is considering banning new cycling lanes if they result in reductions in car lanes.
Confronted with escalating housing costs and reduced affordability, the government proposed reforms to provincial planning legislation to speed up construction on Greenbelt sites in what many described as weakening local control over the quality of development and its potential environmental impacts.
If the provincial government continues with this agenda, the weight of evidence in planning research points to long-term decreases in the quality of life for Ontario residents. Here's why.
Issues with car-oriented planning
First, new highways bring more cars and eventually result in the same or even worse gridlock. They also contribute to air pollution and climate change.
Therefore, to accommodate new growth, governments need to consider alternative modes of transport.
Second, car-oriented development inherently produces more urban sprawl, making it difficult to accommodate walking, cycling and public transit that are known to have important environmental and health benefits.
When more highways are built, there is usually a desire to separate residential subdivisions from traffic, resulting in more dispersed amenities that are difficult to reach without a car.
That can be expensive. Given the rising cost of living, it is not unreasonable to expect that car ownership will become increasingly costly for more people.
This means that not only could Ontario residents face more urban sprawl, but many people will also find it difficult, and time-intensive, to get to where they need to go.
This is not an anti-car argument. There will still be many cars in the future and some of it will be from new growth, but progressive planning policies would help create alternatives.
Car-centred planning
Ford's emphasis on cars is reminiscent of another time, coincidentally often called the Fordist period by urban researchers - so named after the mass production of Ford automobile plants that became a symbol of the economy and suburban expansion of the times.
At its height between the 1950s and '70s, original Fordist urban planning focused on the automobile, separating land designated for workplaces, retail stores and homes, and developing large scale subdivisions at the suburban fringe. North America's heavy car reliance is an artifact of this development model.
Planning decisions have long-term consequences.
In part arising from the social activism of the 1960s, planning began to place more focus on environmental issues. In the '70s and '80s, this pertained to the protection of environmentally significant areas; the Niagara Escarpment plan was one important achievement of this movement.
In the 1990s, it became clear, however, that environmental protection would eventually be challenged, and fail, unless development itself was going to use less land and become more co-ordinated.
This was the height of the smart growth movement that emphasized the need for greenbelts to constrain suburban sprawl, and concentrate development into higher density neighbourhoods and more walkable growth centres connected by public transit.
Building better
Public health, the environment and growing pressures on municipal finances are all important reasons to build communities that are more walkable, bikeable and transit-oriented.
However, the Ontario government's recent announcements do not clearly indicate how prioritizing cars would address health, environmental and municipal fiscal issues.
Connecting communities with better rail service, for example, and putting some restrictions on automobile traffic would be an investment made in preparation for a future where gas prices are expected to rise.
Furthermore, there will likely be mounting pressures to reduce greenhouse gas emissions as governments face the rising costs of natural disasters and a changing climate.
The Ontario government's approach to infrastructure development hearkens back in time when urban planning did not incorporate urban planning ideas around environmental protection and smart growth.
It's also an approach without sufficient investments in critical community infrastructure, such as affordable housing.
Hallmarks of the original Fordist period were investments in community centres, schools, transit infrastructure as a social service, and yes, affordable housing.
Although the governments at the times focused on car-reliant urban development, there was still significant investment in social infrastructure.
That is not the case today. Governments are pushing for the kinds of policies used in the past that are the very reason why citizens are so reliant on cars today - but without the social infrastructure that was arguably a redeeming feature of original Fordist urban planning.
The Ontario government's approach is not consistent with the current evidence on best practice planning. Car use and low-density sprawl will continue and perhaps even accelerate because new and proposed policies make it attractive to do so.
The future would likely be more congested, more polluted and we would see more loss of agricultural and environmentally significant lands. Ontario's policies of more auto dependence are heading in the wrong direction, and would reduce the quality of life of Ontario residents in the future.
Markus Moos is a professor
in the School of Planning
at the University of Waterloo.
This was originally published
on The Conversation.
CAPTION: The Ontario government's recent announcements do not clearly indicate how prioritizingcars would address health, environmental and municipal fiscal issues, Markus Moos writes.Lars HagbergThe Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Markus Moos Markus Moos is a professor in the School of Planning at the University of Waterloo. This was originally published on The Conversation.;

Subject: Quality of life; Public transportation; Infrastructure; Urban planning; Traffic; Roads & highways; Suburban areas; Automobiles; Smart growth; Outdoor air quality; Environmental protection; Affordable housing; Urbanization; Government

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure Affordable housing

Location: North America

Title: A tunnel will not improve transport or housing:   Ontario's policies of more auto dependence are heading in the wrong direction
