Ford government draws public criticism for destruction of Ontario Place tree grove

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-draws-public-criticism/docview/3113045424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+draws+public+criticism+for+destruction+of+Ontario+Place+tree+grove&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government's quick destruction of more than 800 trees on the West Island of Ontario Place is "shameful", says a prominent landscape architect who quit the controversial spa-focused redevelopment project.
"It is shocking to me that we are destroying our own unique, sensitive habitat that was home to coyotes, fox, raccoons and birds," Walter Kehm, known for designing the Trillium and Tommy Thompson parks, told the Star in an interview Friday.
"We've lost our cathedral grove that was overlooking the lake, where you could see the sunset through the trees - it was magical."
Crews with chainsaws and backhoes apparently started the removal operation, with no public notice, on Wednesday evening, shocking many people who woke up Thursday to see much of the mini-forest reduced to stumps and brush. By Friday afternoon it was a waste removal operation.
A video posted on social media Thursday showed birds circling above a tangle of felled 20-metre Norway spruce trees, as well as oak, green ash and birch, while a backhoe dumped debris into a dumpster.
The thicket was cleared to make way for a towering spa resort and waterpark to be built near the water's edge by Austrian company Therme, a centrepiece of the Premier Doug Ford government's remake of the site that was home to a provincial theme park that closed in 2012.
On Thursday, taking reporters' questions while making public the province's 95-year lease deal with Therme, Infrastructure Ontario chief executive Michael Lindsay defended the deforestation, saying it was timed to avoid bird and bat nesting times. The lost trees will be replaced by many more, including by a six-to-one ratio for the mature ones, he said.
Kehm resigned in 2023 as a senior principal at Toronto-based LANDinc, one of two firms under contract to help design and construct the new Ontario Place's "public realm", citing opposition to the deforestation plan.
He is accusing the Ford government of making the lease public on Thursday to divert media and public attention away from the clear-cut, calling it a cynical move that is typical of the government's approach to the waterfront revamp that will also feature an expanded, year-round Budweiser Stage and a relocated Ontario Science Centre.
Ian McConachie, an Infrastructure Ontario spokesperson, said Friday that site preparation had been underway for a year and that replacement trees will increase biodiversity.
"The province is rebuilding Ontario Place into a world-class, year-round destination with expanded parks and public spaces, increased waterfront access, a new public marina and family-friendly entertainment," he said in an email. "The redeveloped Ontario Place will create thousands of new jobs and attract an estimated four to six million visitors each year."
Advocacy group Ontario Place Protectors filed paperwork for an injunction to stop the tree destruction but, given that the trees are all down, the group "is currently trying to determine if that is still something that should proceed," said lawyer Eric Gillespie.
Ontario Place Protectors has been granted leave to appeal a July court decision that upheld the province's right to redevelop the site without doing a full environmental assessment, with a court date yet to be set, Gillespie said.
The lease details reveal that the Ford government is under pressure to create a huge number of new parking spots within 650 metres of the spa entrance by the time the spa opens or 2030, whichever comes first - or pay significant financial penalties to Therme.
The lease requires creation of spots for 1,800 vehicles but the province says it plans to build 2,500 spaces to accommodate visitors to other site attractions including the Science Centre and parkland.
Ford originally said his government would build an underground lot at Ontario Place. During negotiations with city hall on a new fiscal deal for Toronto he agreed to consider building it, below ground or above, at Exhibition Place.
The city and province will only say that negotiations continue between them but the Star has learned of one option on the table.
Some officials would like to see a three-storey underground lot built on the site of the Better Living Centre, which would be razed and replaced with a 4.5-acre "festival plaza" that could be made appealing for events and the walk to the west bridge to Ontario Place, according to a city hall source not authorized to discuss details of the discussions.
Ford, however, would have to be convinced to spend more to bury the garage rather than building a cheaper above-ground option. The Exhibition Place board has said it will not support an above-ground parkade on the city-owned site.
CAPTION: Crews began removing trees at Ontario Place's West Island on Wednesday.

Subject: Court decisions; Deforestation

Company / organization: Name: Infrastructure Ontario; NAICS: 926110, 926120

Document 402 of 500

Stop the nonsense and talk about real solutions for real issues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stop-nonsense-talk-about-real-solutions-issues/docview/3112846073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stop+the+nonsense+and+talk+about+real+solutions+for+real+issues&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The political television ad begins with a voice-over narration, "Everything feels broken in Canada."
Is it, though?
The ad is set in what appears to be a very nice home, presumably that of Pierre Poilievre. His toddler is helping him build a jigsaw puzzle of a map of Canada. It's safe to say that a toddler would not be a great help with such a task. Perhaps the ad is an allegory showing Poilievre knows how to fix things while we, the people, are the toddler in need of being shown how it's done.
This seems like a Canadian version of, "I alone can fix it, eh."
The last thing Canadians need is the sort of absurdity that goes on south of us.
Exaggerations, outright lies and make-believe are the order of the day in the politics of our neighbours. The assumption seems to be that people are uninformed nitwits quite content in their echo-chambers of inanities.
As Canadians, we are better than that. Or should be.
It may be tough at times, we may have to tighten our belts on some things, but most Canadians live comfortably. Not all of us by any means, but most of us have homes and jobs and don't want for much except lower grocery prices. Most of us are doing OK. Most of us are in a position to provide some measure of help for those who need it.
The country is not broken.
Absolutely, there are problems that need to be addressed, such as the need for affordable housing, health care, climate change, lack of potable water in many First Nations communities, mental health and substance abuse. These are serious issues requiring solutions from serious people.
Most of the issues mentioned above are not uniquely Canadian challenges, so we should all stop blaming individual Canadians for what are international issues needing international fixes.
No one is perfect, so to expect it from our politicians is unfair. But it is not too much to expect honesty and a plan.
Governing is hard work and the issues can be complicated, requiring complex solutions. It's OK to talk to us like we're six when necessary to make it understandable, but treat us like adults. We should be treated like flowers that with some care blossom and grow in the sunlight, not like mushrooms kept in the dark with manure shovelled at us.
There are sometimes politicians whose political stances we disagreed with, but we admire them for the work they did and the co-operative way in which they did it. None were perfect, but they surely tried.
Now we have silly, demeaning nicknames and legislatures reminiscent of schoolyard squabbles. Parliament should have a corner where ill-behaved members are made to stand.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau seems like a guest who can't find his coat, but hangs around because he is too embarrassed to ask.
Premier Doug Ford, somehow oblivious to the existing world, wants to build more highways instead of maintaining and improving the highways we have. He insists on the fanciful notion that he will build the longest highway tunnel in the world at an astronomical cost, and wants to build on public land a spa that most of us will never think of using.
This, in the face of more immediate real world challenges.
Like storm clouds peeking over the horizon, there are elections looming. Stop the nonsense and talk about real solutions for real issues. That's not too much to ask of the people we elect to represent our interests.
We should not step into the voting booth to choose the least objectionable candidate. We should be there to choose the best candidate.
CAPTION: It may be tough at times, we may have to tighten our belts on some things, but most Canadians live comfortably, Michael McDonnell writes.
CREDIT: Michael McDonnell

Subject: Canadians

Location: Canada

Title: Stop the nonsense and talk about real solutions for real issues

Oct. 4: Why are dangerous dogs not euthanized, stop enabling drug users and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-4-why-are-dangerous-dogs-not-euthanized-stop/docview/3112845736/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+4%3A+Why+are+dangerous+dogs+not+euthanized%2C+stop+enabling+drug+users+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why are dangerous dogs not euthanized?
I would like to know what happened to the days when we put down aggressive dogs. I am reading about all the fines given to a clearly negligent dog owner whose dogs have repeatedly attacked and hurt humans in parks and on bike trails. Why are these dogs still alive?
A month ago, my co-worker's daughter was attacked in Burford while walking her dog, and she lost half of her finger. That dog continues to live. My sister-in-law works with a woman whose daughter was killed by huskies two years ago near Strathroy. Thankfully, those dogs were put down.
So does a dog have to actually kill someone before being deemed dangerous enough to be put down? Why do dogs that repeatedly attack humans continue to live? They are like ticking time bombs. Please help me understand.
Christa Vander Horst, Hamilton
Trump's hold on Americans amazing
Re: Why is Trump lashing out at potential voters? Oct. 2
Kudos to Donald Abelson for his commentary on Donald Trump - it was bang on. It never ceases to amaze me how Trump's loyal followers believe every word he says (true or false) and will continue to vote for him unconditionally.
The truth, however, is without a rude awakening of our American neighbours, Trump may still win. Now should he lose, he will claim the election was rigged, maybe this time by people who eat cats and dogs. Oh, and by the way, a second loss especially to a woman will make storming the Capitol look like a quiet little tea party.
Joe Collina, Ancaster
Canadian politics worthy of attention
Almost daily, I read opinion pieces by academics who are so wound up by U.S. politics one has to wonder if they have lost sight of the fact that they're talking about another country. Focusing on the parliamentary shenanigans in Ottawa may not be as colourful, but at least the protagonists are honest, sane and most assuredly deserved of our attention.
Phil Beard, Dundas
City should stop enabling drug users
Re: Hamilton councillors debate plan for harm-reduction vending machines, Oct. 2
I cannot understand why the province and city refuse to address the issues that these addicts have, instead of handing out free drug paraphernalia to perpetuate their addictions. What is to stop people from emptying these vending machines? I'm sure they will be free.
People throw garbage everywhere now, so we will have to deal with more dangerous litter thanks to these machines. Spend the money on rehabilitation services and not free supplies to help do drugs.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Blame Liberals for falling birth rate
Statistics Canada's sad announcement that Canada's birth rate has fallen to the lowest level in recorded history is an indictment of Justin Trudeau and the Liberal government. Poverty and economic uncertainty has Canadians, more so than ever before, deciding they can't even afford to have a family in Canada. Trudeau's horrific government has stolen more than prosperity from Canadians, it has literally stolen our children!
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
Cutting sales tax, not carbon tax, is the best strategy
I understand the frustration of many Canadians who are struggling to pay mortgages and feed themselves.
Opposition leader Pierre Poilievre has convinced a lot of Canadians that the problem is the carbon tax.
As has been said and written many times, the vast majority of Canadians get more in rebates than what they pay out in carbon tax for fuels. The only ones who pay more than what they get back are the big consumers of fuels, such as industries. A carbon tax, while not the only means of combating pollution, has proven to be effective in many countries of the world in reducing consumption of fossil fuels, and thus has helped lower pollution.
If a federal politician really wanted to help Canadians lower their tax bill, how about lowering or eliminating sales taxes? The average Canadian family pays thousands of dollars in sales taxes a year, added at the till to almost everything they buy, except grocery-store food and children's clothing. This system taxes everyone the same, regardless of ability to pay. Aren't we supposed to have progressive taxation in this country, taxing people based on their income levels?
While "Axe the tax" is a catchy phrase, and taps into the feelings of many Canadians that they pay too much tax, eliminating a carbon tax isn't the way to make life here more affordable.
Alan Murray, New Dundee
A 1950s vision
Re: Should Doug Ford buy the 407 instead of digging a tunnel to ease gridlock? Sept. 27
With his typical penchant for tunnel vision, Premier Doug Ford has hatched a preposterous scheme to dig us out of some of Ontario's transportation woes. He calls his antique 1950s approach to problem-solving visionary - delusional and embarrassing are more apt descriptors.
Douglas Melville, St. Catharines
Better the devil you know ...
As I contemplate the next federal election, I find I have no choice but to vote for Justin Trudeau. Better the devil you know than the one you don't.
I don't like Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre with his nasty, aggressive manner, and while he brags that he will axe the tax, fix the budget, build the homes and fight crime, I've seen no details. How would he propose to deal with climate change once the carbon "tax" is gone? What unique measures would he take to ensure millions of affordable homes are built nationwide? What services and programs would be cut to fix the budget? Is crime so high that it requires electing him to fight it?
While I like NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, I don't see him and his party as strong enough to lead the country. Poilievre does not have the personality to represent me in the nation's highest office.
Bob Coupland, Oakville
CAPTION: A rottweiler is removed from a Kitchener home in 2012 after it, and another dog, attacked a person. Does a dog have to actually kill someone before being deemed dangerous enough to put down, a reader asks.

National briefs: RCMP followup on complaints lax, inquiry told

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3112756411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+RCMP+followup+on+complaints+lax%2C+inquiry+told&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Tories want probe into appointment
The Conservatives have asked Canada's lobbying commissioner to investigate whether it violates ethics rules for the prime minister to make Mark Carney his own personal adviser. The Liberals announced at their recent caucus retreat that the former Bank of Canada governor had been appointed chair of a task force on economic growth and adviser to the Liberal leader. Tory ethics critic Michael Barrett says in a letter to the commissioner of lobbying that Carney is not registered to lobby federally, but his corporate positions put him in potential conflicts of interest.
RCMP followup on complaints lax, inquiry told
A senior Mountie says the RCMP is working to address concerns of diaspora community members who say police often neglect to follow up with them about complaints of foreign interference. RCMP Deputy Commissioner Mark Flynn told a federal inquiry Thursday the national police force plans to have internal discussions with key units on how to improve. Katherine Leung, a policy adviser with Hong Kong Watch, told the inquiry that police duly take notes when someone reports being threatened or harmed by a person acting on behalf of a foreign regime. Leung said that's where the story ends with some.
Ontario
Stronach faces new charges
Court documents show billionaire businessperson Frank Stronach is facing more sexual assault charges involving three additional complainants whose allegations date back to the 1980s and 1990s. The latest charges bring the total number of accusers in his case to 13. The 92-year-old Stronach was first charged in early June with five sex-related offences involving three complainants.
News services

Document 406 of 500

Foreign student strategy needed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/foreign-student-strategy-needed/docview/3112756261/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Foreign+student+strategy+needed&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians love a good pasta restaurant, but of all the workforce challenges we face as a nation, Kitchener's Conestoga College should not be wasting limited international student visas on an "Italian Culinary Arts" course in September 2025.
Canada desperately needs more post-secondary graduates in applied biotechnology, computer science, construction engineering, early childhood education, machining and practical nursing, among other key skills.
Given the lack of affordable housing, and a local unemployment rate of more than seven per cent, why ever would Conestoga recruit students - primarily from India and China - for a one or two-year course in Charcuterie and Artisanal Foods, Interior Design, Public Relations or Social Media Marketing?
On the heels of broad-based pressure, effectively encapsulated by Ontario Conservative MP Arpan Khanna, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government is rushing to reduce the number of student visas being granted. But broad-based cuts don't take into account Canada's needs - that requires a scalpel, not a machete.
Given their focus on messaging, I'm not surprised the Liberals put social media courses on the same pedestal as construction engineering, but the downside is obvious. Ontario's Metrolinx, for example, requires a future pool of trained young professionals to deliver a 20-year pipeline of multi-faceted construction projects on behalf of Premier Doug Ford's government.
Ottawa didn't prioritize the skills that will help bring these generational infrastructure projects to fruition.
Western University president Alan Shepard put it succinctly: "The post-secondary system is bulging with offerings to entice students from around the world. But not all offerings are equal, and the students they attract are not supported equally when they arrive."
That's my alma mater boss politely reminding politicians that when Western or the University of Toronto offer a first-year student a spot, they're guaranteed a residence room. Other institutions have no such concern, as Brampton's bylaw department discovered when it found 25 students living in an illegal basement apartment.
I'd have thought that Ontario's newly appointed Minister of Colleges and Universities, Nolan Quinn, could bring order to this chaos. That the minister might roll back the excesses, including the new $495,000 salary that Conestoga's Board of Governors recently awarded President John Tibbits (a figure that's higher than the presidential salaries at McMaster or Queen's, for example).
Not likely, according to one of Quinn's predecessors.
"(Post secondary institutions) are ungovernable," according to Glen Murray, who held the Training, Colleges and Universities cabinet portfolio under former Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty. They may be our tax dollars, but Ontario's funding formula gives each institution wide latitude to run their own show.
Colleges and Institutes Canada president Pari Johnston claims Trudeau's visa cuts will cause "significant harm ... to local businesses that rely on the schools to supply graduates to the labour force."
I asked her team for the underlying analysis, but was told "no one data set" supported their assertion.
The last time Gallup checked, 74 million people wanted to immigrate to Canada. That luxury means we can "craft a careful and co-ordinated strategy that makes the best use of top global talent," as Shepard argues.
One former college instructor told me she quit in disgust when she realized most of her international students "had zero interest in the class. It was all about getting permanent residency."
Murray and I are in violent agreement when he says "our national interest is to have the most highly educated, skilled and civil society in the world. To reinforce our democracy and strengthen our economy and productivity."
That requires choices: "Who do we want to come here to be educated? Who do we want to come and be educated and go back to their country? Who do we want to retain here?"
These essential questions are neither partisan nor anti-immigrant and should guide an international student strategy that reflects our national interest.
Mark McQueen worked on Bay Street for more than 30 years. He is the former board chair of two federal agencies and was an adviser in the Brian Mulroney PMO.

Document 407 of 500

It's not too much to expect some honesty and a plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/not-too-much-expect-some-honesty-plan/docview/3112756236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+not+too+much+to+expect+some+honesty+and+a+plan&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The political television ad begins with a voice-over narration, "Everything feels broken in Canada."
Is it, though?
The ad is set in what appears to be a very nice home, presumably that of Pierre Poilievre. His toddler is helping him build a jigsaw puzzle of a map of Canada. It's safe to say that a toddler would not be a great help with such a task.
Perhaps the ad is an allegory showing Poilievre knows how to fix things while we, the people, are the toddler in need of being shown how it's done.
The last thing Canadians need is the sort of absurdity that goes on south of us.
Exaggerations, outright lies and make-believe are the order of the day in the politics of our neighbours. The assumption seems to be that people are uninformed nitwits quite content in their echo-chambers of inanities.
As Canadians, we are better than that. Or should be.
It may be tough at times, we may have to tighten our belts on some things, but most Canadians live comfortably. Not all of us by any means, but most of us have homes and jobs and don't want for much except lower grocery prices. Most of us are doing OK. Most of us are in a position to provide some measure of help for those who need it.
The country is not broken.
Absolutely, there are problems that need to be addressed, such as the need for affordable housing, health care, climate change, lack of potable water in many First Nations communities, mental health and substance abuse. These are serious issues requiring solutions from serious people.
Most of the issues mentioned above are not uniquely Canadian challenges, so we should all stop blaming individual Canadians for what are international issues needing international fixes.
No one is perfect, so to expect it from our politicians is unfair. But it is not too much to expect honesty and a plan.
Governing is hard work and the issues can be complicated, requiring complex solutions. It's OK to talk to us like we're six when necessary to make it understandable, but treat us like adults.
We should be treated like flowers that with some care blossom and grow in the sunlight, not like mushrooms kept in the dark with manure shovelled at us.
There are sometimes politicians whose political stances we disagreed with, but we admire them for the work they did and the co-operative way in which they did it. None were perfect, but they surely tried.
Now we have silly, demeaning nicknames and legislatures reminiscent of schoolyard squabbles. Parliament should have a corner where ill-behaved members are made to stand.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau seems like a guest who can't find his coat, but hangs around because he is too embarrassed to ask.
Premier Doug Ford, somehow oblivious to the existing world, wants to build more highways instead of maintaining and improving the highways we have. He insists on the fanciful notion that he will build the longest highway tunnel in the world at an astronomical cost, and wants to build on public land a spa that most of us will never think of using.
This, in the face of more immediate real world challenges.
Like storm clouds peeking over the horizon, there are elections looming. Stop the nonsense and talk about real solutions for real issues. That's not too much to ask of the people we elect to represent our interests.
We should not step into the voting booth to choose the least objectionable candidate. We should be there to choose the best candidate.
Michael McDonnell
of Waterford is an
occasional commentator
on current affairs.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Michael McDonnell Michael McDonnell of Waterford is an occasional commentator on current affairs.;

Canadians three times more positive about Kamala Harris than Donald Trump, survey says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-three-times-more-positive-about-kamala/docview/3112920871/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+three+times+more+positive+about+Kamala+Harris+than+Donald+Trump%2C+survey+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are three times more positive about U.S. presidential candidate Kamala Harris than they are about Donald Trump, a new Pollara poll suggests.
"If Canada was the 51st state, it would be a state that no one would bother to campaign in because it would be a landslide for the Democrats," said Dan Arnold, chief strategy officer for Pollara Strategic Insights.
Indeed, 62 per cent of respondents had positive feelings about Harris with only 18 per cent having negative feelings toward the current Democratic vice-president.
In comparison, 66 per cent had negative feelings about Trump with 20 per cent having positive feelings toward the former Republican president.
"There's still apprehension that Trump might win - people are much more positive about the Democrats."
As part of Pollara's "Rage Index" polling that examines the mood of Canadians, the firm surveyed 1,500 people on their thoughts about the Nov. 5 U.S. election.
The poll was conducted between Sept. 11 - the day after the Harris-Trump debate - and Sept. 19 using online panels. While such opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Arnold said Canadians appear slightly more optimistic about the Democrats' chances since President Joe Biden stepped aside on July 21 following his disastrous June 27 debate performance against Trump.
In June, six per cent said they were "happy" about the U.S. election while 53 per cent were "angry." Last month, 10 per cent were happy and 46 per cent were angry.
But Harris's strong showing in the Sept. 10 debate against Trump has helped her standing among voters of all three major Canadian political parties.
Seven out of eight Liberal supporters - 87 per cent - felt positively toward her with only seven per cent feeling negative. Similarly, 83 per cent of Liberals were negative toward Trump with 10 per cent positive.
Almost half of Conservative voters - 45 per cent - had positive feelings about Harris with 37 per cent negative. That's greater than the 39 per cent of Tories who held a positive view toward Trump. Nearly half - 46 per cent - had a negative sentiment about him.
Among New Democratic voters, 83 per cent were positive about Harris with six per cent negative, while 88 per cent of NDP supporters had a negative view of Trump with six per cent positive.
Pollara also tested the views Canadians have toward the current president and about the two running mates.
Only one-third - 34 per cent - were positive about Biden with 36 per cent negative.
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz - Harris's vice-presidential pick and a favourite of Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who has worked closely with him on trade issues - was viewed positively by 32 per cent of respondents and negatively by 10 per cent.
Ohio Sen. JD Vance, Trump's running mate, fared worse than Harris, Trump, Walz or Biden in terms of the "positive" metric.
Just 12 per cent of those surveyed viewed Vance positively, while 36 per cent had negative feelings about him.
The survey was completed before Tuesday's vice-presidential debate.
Arnold noted awareness of the vice-presidential candidates is high among Canadians - 63 per cent knew who Vance was while 57 per cent knew who Walz was.
That's greater than those who could identify Canada's longest-serving premier - Saskatchewan's Scott Moe at 35 per cent - or federal Health Minister Mark Holland at 31 per cent.
"It's been more exciting than a typical Canadian election. People here are following it very closely."
Harris has a strong connection to Canada, where she spent most of her teenage years when her mother worked as a researcher at the Jewish General Hospital and taught at McGill University. She graduated from Montreal's Westmount High School in 1981 then returned stateside for university.
"Canadians always like 'Canadians' who make it big in the U.S.," said Arnold, noting that is despite the fact the vice-president seldom publicly mentions her time in Montreal.
"She doesn't talk about it because it doesn't help her politically," he added.
But 77 per cent of Quebecers polled viewed her positively with just eight per cent having negative feelings.
"So the 'hometown' effect might be a bit in play," said Arnold.
CAPTION: Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump giving a speech on Aug. 20, 2024, about immigration and crime i in Howell, Michigan.

Ontario Place's Therme megaspa lease details released

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-places-therme-megaspa-lease-details/docview/3112920776/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Place%27s+Therme+megaspa+lease+details+released&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario taxpayers will fund 2,500 parking spots for the spa and other attractions at a redeveloped Ontario Place - 700 spots more than required under the province's newly revealed 95-year lease with Therme.
Premier Doug Ford's government released details of the lease on Thursday after years of public concern, secrecy and controversy over plans to replace trees and other natural features with a towering indoor spa and water park on the shuttered theme park's West Island, along with an expanded Budweiser Stage and relocated Science Centre.
Background materials released before a media briefing state that "the lease requires a minimum of 1,800 parking spaces. The government is proposing 2,500 parking spaces to meet demand for all users and tenants.
"Of the 2,500 proposed spaces, the lease stipulates a requirement of 1,600 dedicated spaces for Therme Canada to meet user demand."
The province says a "portion" of Therme's allocated spots will be shared with LiveNation during shows at Budweiser Stage.
Materials accompanying the lease do not say how much provincial taxpayers will foot for the new parking spaces, estimated to be in the hundreds of millions of dollars. Instead, the materials focus on predicted tourism and economic benefits for Ontarians and the fact that the government isn't paying any costs toward the spa building itself.
Benefits "include, at a minimum, nearly $2 billion in estimated revenue contributions from Therme Canada to the province over the duration of the lease and $700 million in upfront capital investments from Therme Canada," according to a news release.
"Of these upfront capital investments, $500 million will go to build the Therme Canada facility and $200 million will support approximately 16 acres of freely accessible public space ...
"Under the terms of the lease, the province will maintain ownership of the lands and has not provided or committed to any operational subsidy or capital contributions to the Therme Canada facility."
Therme's facility at Ontario Place cannot, under lease terms, be a casino, shopping mall or condominium. The lease starts in 2025 and runs for 75 years with a 20-year extension clause.
Originally, the province said the parking garage would be built underground at Ontario Place. Later, under terms of the province's new fiscal deal with Toronto, Ford said the new parking would likely be built across the street at city-owned Exhibition Place rather than on the Ontario Place waterfront.
In July, the Star revealed that Ford and his officials had decided that a deep underground garage on either side of Lake Shore Boulevard West would be too expensive and that the province would instead build an above-ground parking structure, either at Exhibition Place or Ontario Place.
The province is in negotiations on parking options at Exhibition Place, with officials saying no agreement has yet been reached.
The lease says the province has the right to early termination of the lease with five years' notice after the tenth year of operations. That could trigger provincial damages to the spa operator. If Therme doesn't achieve key lease obligations, however, the company could be required to pay damages to the province.
More later
CAPTION: The release of the Therma spa lease on Thursday by Doug Ford's government follows years of public concern over the controversial plans to redevelop the now-shuttered waterfront park.

Revving up the speed: Here is what's behind Ontario Premier Doug Ford's push for higher highway speed limits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/revving-up-speed-here-is-whats-behind-ontario/docview/3112920675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Revving+up+the+speed%3A+Here+is+what%27s+behind+Ontario+Premier+Doug+Ford%27s+push+for+higher+highway+speed+limits&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is geared up to push the limits in Ontario.
"You look at the 400 series highways - we've increased (speed limits) in quite a few parts of the province," said Ford at a news conference in East York on Wednesday. "We want to finish it off."
His desire to up speed limits to 110 km/h on the 400 series highways is based on the successful two-year pilot program in 2019 which paved the way for the first rollout of the increased speed in 2022 and more this summer.
"I want to emphasize to the (Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria), only where it is safe to do so," he said.
The government says the sections of highway that have seen 110-kilometre-per-hour speed limits since 2019 have comparable speeds and collision trends to similar highway sections with limits of 100 km/h.
"Everything has come back very positive," said Minister Sarkaria of the feedback from previous speed changes. He noted that engineers continue working to ensure road safety for all.
"It's about making sure that those people that are on the roads respecting the laws can get to where they need to go in a faster, easier way," he said.
The minister reaffirmed the government's commitment to road safety, citing the province's strict laws on careless drivers, particularly those charged with impaired and stunt driving, as a strong deterrent.
Ontario is one of seven provinces to have speed limits over 100 km/h on some highways.

Document 411 of 500

Toronto to Montreal in 3 hours? Canada might be finally ready to build a high speed rail line - but how fast it will be remains an open question

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-montreal-3-hours-canada-might-be-finally/docview/3112756386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+to+Montreal+in+3+hours%3F+Canada+might+be+finally+ready+to+build+a+high+speed+rail+line+-+but+how+fast+it+will+be+remains+an+open+question&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A key stop on the track to better passenger rail service that the Liberal government has promised is just around the bend - and with it comes a multibillion-dollar question that's still without an answer:
If it really gets built, how fast will the trains go?
In the coming weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's cabinet is expected to choose which of three groups of private companies will get to help design and build a new passenger rail project that proponents bill as a game-changer for travel through the most populated part of the country. Running 1,000 kilometres from Toronto to Quebec City, the promised rail corridor is meant to slash journey times between major cities and could bring Canada - many say belatedly - into the modern era of high speed rail travel.
"We are looking at all options," said Transport Minister Anita Anand, when asked whether the project will bring a dedicated line of trains that travel faster than 200 km/h, the standard definition of high speed rail (HSR).
Vance Badawey, a Liberal MP who is Anand's parliamentary secretary, said the government is studying which areas might be best served by faster trains, and which could still rely on slower - but more frequent - rail service. But, like Anand, he said the government has not yet decided what to do.
At an office at the federal Transport Department in downtown Ottawa this week, Martin Imbleau expressed high hopes for the project. As chief executive officer of the Crown corporation created to advance the project, Imbleau shared a vision for rail travel from Toronto to Québec that rivals European countries like France.
That country's Trains à Grande Vitesse (TGV) system has successfully reduced travel times between cities over four decades of operation, Imbleau said, even though routes like the one from Paris to Amsterdam include stretches where trains run slower than 200 km/h.
He suggested the new rail project could look something like that: a corridor with trains that slow down when necessary, like when entering an urban centre, and speed up beyond 200 km/h along other parts of the route. The idea now is to have a single new corridor with stops in Toronto, Peterborough, Ottawa, Laval, Montreal, Trois-Rivières and Quebec City.
But the final shape depends on which private group the government chooses, and how that group works with Imbleau's Crown corporation to design and build the project, he said, adding that he expects the decision in the coming weeks. The three groups include more than 20 companies, with the likes of Air Canada, national rail companies from France, Spain and Germany, and British infrastructure investment firm John Laing.
Each group was asked to make two proposals to the government: one for trains that travel less than 200 km/h, and one for trains that travel faster. And regardless of who wins the project, the designers will be allowed to pull from all the proposals to "mix and match" and meet their goals of faster, more frequent and reliable passenger service, Imbleau said.
Depending on what happens, he predicted train travel between Montreal and Toronto could be reduced to little more than three hours, compared to the current reality of more than five.
"We're really looking at reducing the journey time with those three factors," Imbleau said. "How fast remains to be seen."
The Liberals first floated the project more than five years ago as "high frequency rail." The idea was to build new tracks dedicated exclusively to electrified passenger trains that would run more frequently. That way, passenger service wouldn't run on tracks owned by Canadian National (CN) and Canadian Pacific (CPKC), whose freight traffic is prioritized over Via Rail trains carrying people, resulting in delays that drag out the time it takes to travel by rail.
But as one government source explained, the vision for the project shifted toward high speed rail, despite the "conventional wisdom" at Via and within the public service "just to do it at regular speed."
Part of that was a perception of public appetite, the source said. A public opinion survey completed in August for Imbleau's Crown corporation found a majority of respondents - 58 per cent of those from Quebec and 57 per cent from Ontario - said they preferred a faster but more expensive train over a slower, less costly option.
"Everybody wants high speed," said the source, who agreed to speak on condition of anonymity.
Of course, HSR is more expensive to build, in part because it requires special infrastructure to make it safe to travel at high speeds, like grade separation. In 2021, then-transport minister Omar Alghabra floated a rough cost estimate for the project at between $6 billion and $12 billion. But the real cost might actually be more than double that, the government source said, depending on how much of the project is built for high speed, where the tracks end up getting built, and because of factors like inflation that have driven up construction costs in recent years.
A 2017 assessment of a possible "ultra-high speed" rail project from Portland, Oregon to Vancouver, B.C. estimated such a project - with up to 800 kilometres of track - could cost between $32 billion and $57 billion.
"The truth of the matter is the cost is going to go up," the source said.
Imbleau told the Star it's too early to talk about costs, since they still don't know exactly what they're going to build. But he stressed that the rail project would ultimately bring in revenue from ticket sales.
"If it costs more but it brings in more value, maybe there's a business case there," he said, adding that he sees the project as an "investment" to help Canada catch up with other countries that have had rapid rail service for decades. And, although he noted there are still many uncertainties, he predicted a first section of the rail line could be finished within about 12 years.
"I will let the government make their decision, and I will respect that," Imbleau said. "What we're developing is a project that is required, and it's an investment for future generations."
Paul Langan, a long-time advocate for HSR in Canada, said the project appears to be veering towards high speed. "Just how high is the question," he said.
His big concern is, given the political situation that finds the governing Liberals in a minority Parliament and trailing badly in the polls, what will happen to the project if a new party - namely the Conservatives - takes power after the next election.
In an emailed statement this week, Conservative MP and transport critic Philip Lawrence did not say what his party would do with the rail project if it came to power. Instead, he said the public should doubt whether the Liberals will actually get it built.
"Like every photo op and every big promise from Justin Trudeau, Canadians can't trust that he will deliver on anything," the statement said.
According to the government source, the proposals from the private groups are in the "final stages" of assessment by the public service, and cabinet could decide by November which one is going to take part in the project.
"It was a big mistake we didn't do this 30 years ago," the source said. "These types of project are, in my opinion, non-negotiable."
CAPTION: A train travels on France's high-speed rail network, in Connerre, western France, on Aug. 29, 2024.

Subject: Construction costs; Design; High speed; Public opinion surveys; Cost estimates

Business indexing term: Subject: Construction costs Public opinion surveys Cost estimates

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 412 of 500

Doug Ford's tunnel won't fix Highway 401. Charging drivers is the only way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tunnel-wont-fix-highway-401-charging/docview/3112756124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+tunnel+won%27t+fix+Highway+401.+Charging+drivers+is+the+only+way&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's tunnel vision has everyone focusing on his blind spots.
Too bad most motorists refuse to look at the congestion solution in plain sight.
For all the public mockery of the premier's plan to dig under Hwy. 401, which came out of nowhere last week, traffic jams are no joke. But the bitter truth is that almost everyone is guilty of closing their eyes to the economics of commuting and congestion.
The critics claim to have a cleverer, better way: Why not buy back Hwy. 407 (technically it's been leased, not sold) for an estimated $30 billion, remove its exorbitant tolls and take the pressure off the 401?
In fact, Ford says he hasn't ruled it out. After all, he has cheerfully cancelled tolls in the past and could well do it again in future to win more votes.
In truth, removing those tolls would be a false economy - leaving us no further ahead in time or money spent. Just further behind fiscally and environmentally.
In reality, the only lasting solution would be the complete opposite:
Keep those existing tolls on the 407.
And go one step further by adding new tolls to the old 401 - not to mention all other major highways and parkways encircling and choking the GTA.
What? Tolls on the 401?
We all know that toll roads are the third rail of Ontario politics.
Yes, it's unthinkable. But try this thought experiment.
Forget the pocketbook politics for a moment. Never mind the populist impulses that everyone falls for.
Not just the premier but almost everyone - politicians, pundits and the public - has become avowedly, adamantly anti-tax and anti-toll. Of course everyone instinctively dislikes tolls, but that distaste won't get us where we need to go.
There is no such thing as a free lunch. Or a free highway.
A toll-free highway still exacts a heavy price in lost time if you're stuck in traffic, because time is money. The cost has been estimated in the billions of dollars annually in delayed deliveries and lost productivity, not to mention people cheated out of personal time - and family time.
In a free market, everything has a price - and it's never free. Highways cost big money to build, but road tolls (unlike bridge tolls) are no longer about cost recovery.
Now, road tolls (and congestion charges for crowded downtowns) are a price signal - a way of flagging for drivers that they must choose carefully whether or when to travel, not on a whim or caprice.
Economics is all about scarcity. Pricing is way of allocating or rationing - rationally - a commodity that others are also competing for, in this case highway space.
In the real world of economics, prices are typically set at levels that the market will bear, based on the fundamentals of supply and demand.
Building (or opening) more toll-free highways tends to encourage more cars, creating greater congestion until we end up right back where we started - in bumper-to-bumper equilibrium again. Economists and planners call it "induced demand" - a consequence of increasing the supply of free freeways.
Raise prices (tolls) higher? Demand will decrease and traffic congestion will decline.
Reduce road tolls to zero, while building or tunnelling new roads? Demand from cars will increase as more motorists clog the roads - at an enormous price in lost time.
Digging a tunnel replicates the same dynamic: If you build it - or dig it - they will come.
People will drive on and under the 401 without limits as soon as you allow unlimited access. That's the inevitably of induced demand.
Ultimately, when the flow of traffic stalls, motorists will search for alternatives: rapid transit, remote work, off-peak commutes, or toll roads that represent value for money (savings in time).
To be clear, congestion is out of control. The question is how to win the congestion battle, not an election battle.
The reality is that road tolls are time-savers, and time is money. The problem is that voters don't want to hear about it.
The premier's pie in the sky proposal to tunnel across much of the GTA is not only a road map to ruin, it is a teachable moment. But the opposition's counterproposal for a free Hwy. 407, which would create a free-for-all, is merely a diversion on the road to induced demand.
Many are snickering at Ford's tunnel vision. But the joke is on us if we keep looking away from economic realities.
This thought experiment on road tolls might read like a political dead end. But more of the same wishful thinking about free highways won't get us anywhere.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has people snickering with his suggestion we build a highway tunnel across GTA, but the joke is on us, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Commuting; Toll roads; Automobile drivers; Cost recovery; Cost control; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Economics

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost recovery Cost control Economics

Title: Doug Ford's tunnel won't fix Highway 401. Charging drivers is the only way

'Do not use': Margarine sold at Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Zehrs, Wholesale Club, Shoppers Drug Mart stores recalled

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/do-not-use-margarine-sold-at-real-canadian/docview/3112504464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Do+not+use%27%3A+Margarine+sold+at+Real+Canadian+Superstore%2C+No+Frills%2C+Zehrs%2C+Wholesale+Club%2C+Shoppers+Drug+Mart+stores+recalled&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health Canada recalled several items over the past week due to a number of health and safety concerns.
PC Blue Menu brand Original Margarine and Margarine with Olive Oil
Reason for recall: PC Blue Menu brand Original Margarine and Margarine with Olive Oil recalled due to possible mould. Health Canada said the microbial contamination is classified as non harmful. The recalling firm is Loblaw Companies Limited.
What to do: Do not use, sell, serve or distribute the affected products.
What products are impacted: Some Original Margarine and Margarine with Olive Oil. Loblaw confirmed the PC Blue Menu brand Original Margarine and Margarine with Olive Oil recall impacted Loblaw grocery (i.e. Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Zehrs, Wholesale Club and Shoppers Drug Mart stores) nationally. Loblaw says these products have been removed from shelves.
"Food safety and quality assurance are fundamental to our business," the company said in a statement.
Various Ballistol Cleaning Oils and Gun Maintenance Products
Reason for recall: The recalled products do not have the proper consumer chemical labelling as required by the Consumer Chemicals and Containers Regulations, 2001, under the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act. The lack of appropriate labelling information could result in unintentional exposure to the products and lead to serious illness or injury. As of Sept. 20, the company had received no reports of incidents or injuries in Canada.
What you should do: Immediately stop using the recalled products and contact North Sylva Sports for a replacement label.
What products are impacted: This recall involves various Ballistol cleaning oils and gun-maintenance products. All UPC and EAN codes are included in this recall for the period sold between June 2018 and September 2024. The company reported that 33,607 units of the affected products were sold in Canada.
Ballistol - Guncer Ceramic Gun Oil - 200 mlBallistol - Gunex Special Gun Oil - 200 mlBallistol - Universal Oil - 400 mlBallistol - Universal Oil - 200 mlBallistol - Universal Oil - 500 mlBallistol - Universal Oil - 100 mlBallistol - Universal Oil - 25 mlBallistol Honing Grinding Oil - 65 mlBallistol Robla Black Powder Solvent - 100 mlBallistol-Set - Quick Browning (various container sizes included)Brunier-Set (various sizes)Gun Maintenance Kit / Ballistol-Set Gun Care (various sizes)Quick Bluing/Kit Bronzage (various sizes)
Mint-Risperidon tablet
Reason for recall: Product quality.
What you should do: Consult your health-care professional if you have any health concerns. Verify if your product is affected. Consult your health-care provider prior to discontinuing use of the affected product(s), or for any health concerns. Contact the recalling firm if you have any questions about the recall. Report any health product-related side effects to Health Canada. Report any other health product safety complaints to Health Canada.
What product is impacted: Mint-Risperidon 0.25 mg tablet
Tetsujin brand Breaded Oysters
Reason for recall: Undeclared milk.
What you should do: Do not use, sell, serve or distribute the affected products.
What products are impacted: Tetsujin brand Breaded Oysters distributed in Alberta, B.C., Nova Scotia, Ontario and Quebec.
Accu-Chek Guide
Reason for recall: The issue arises when a blood glucose measurement is taken and the result is displayed in the wrong measuring unit (i.e., mg/dl instead of mmol/l). If it goes unnoticed, the incorrectly displayed blood glucose value may result in inappropriate therapy decisions, including the administration of insulin, and could potentially result in severe consequences. Such possible consequences range from no clinical impact to adverse health events such as hypoglycemia.
What to do: Contact the manufacturer if you require additional information.
What products are impacted: Accu-Chek Guide, Lot 406670, Lot 406711, SN 93040301566, SN 93040300107, SN 93040304746, SN 93040305146, Model or catalogue number: 07398832004.
More unauthorized 'sexual enhancement' products recalled
Reason for recall: Health Canada has recalled more unauthorized sexual enhancement products that may pose serious health risks. The products are labelled to contain or have been tested and found to contain dangerous ingredients such as levodopa, prasterone, sildenafil, tadalafil and nortadalafil.
What to do: Stop using the products listed in the table listed on Health Canada's website. Consult your health-care professional if you have used these products and have health concerns. Read product labels to verify that health products have been authorized for sale by Health Canada. Authorized health products have an eight-digit Drug Identification Number (DIN), Natural Product Number (NPN) or Homeopathic Drug Number (DIN-HM). You can also check if products have been authorized for sale by searching Health Canada's Drug Product Database and Licensed Natural Health Product Database.
What products are impacted: Health Canada keeps and updated list of recalled products. To view impacted products click here.

Oct. 3: A dark legacy we must face; turn bylaw officers on sidewalk cyclists and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-3-dark-legacy-we-must-face-turn-bylaw/docview/3112504462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+3%3A+A+dark+legacy+we+must+face%3B+turn+bylaw+officers+on+sidewalk+cyclists+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A legacy we must face and rectify
As a longtime advocate of social justice and ally of the Indigenous community, I attended Hamilton's commemoration to mark National Day for Truth and Reconciliation on Monday. I was encouraged to see the photos of the sombre yet celebratory event on the front page of The Spec.
The atrocities of residential schools and the generational trauma that followed is a dark legacy that Canadians must face and rectify. Through education and open dialogue with our Indigenous communities we must make amends for the suffering that was inflicted by our government and the church.
By continuing to listen and learn rather than speak, we can ensure that "truth and reconciliation" is not just a catchphrase to which we pay lip service. It is only by doing so that the mistakes of the past can be prevented from occurring in the future.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Holiday would weaken day's importance
Re: Truth and reconciliation: Hamilton to explore city holiday, Sept. 28
Truth and Reconciliation Day ought to be treated like Remembrance Day when children and adolescents are in school and participate in various activities designed to remind them of the significance of Nov. 11. If we make it a provincial holiday most people, and their children, would simply treat it as "just another day off" and give no mind to its intended purpose i.e. to consider the historical mistreatment of our First Nations and share the value of Indigenous history and culture. In the long-term, the success of Truth and Reconciliation lies with the education of our youngest citizens.
David Fawcett, Hamilton
Turn bylaw on sidewalk cyclists
Re: Council to consider increasing one-third of all parking-related fines, Sept. 30
The bike lane advocates are at it again. This time not about increasing the abundance of the underused lanes, but rather increasing the fine to $200 for a car that momentarily stops in one.
My observation is that the bike lanes are hardly used by cyclists, as most of them ride illegally on the sidewalk. That being so, maybe our money-making "parking enforcement officers" should be issuing $200 tickets to these sidewalk cyclists. And while so engaged, they could add the electric scooter riders to their quota!
Terry Butlin, Hamilton
Musicians are working people, too
Re: After dropping journalism, music, Mohawk College shifts focus to health care, Oct. 1
Mohawk College president Paul Armstrong's emphasis on jobs is correct, but he seems not to understand that musicians are also working people who contribute to our economy. The college strays from its mandate as a college of applied "arts" and technology by cancelling its music program. Mohawk has a reputation for music excellence and Hamilton has always strongly supported the arts, styling itself as "Music City." Not every aspiring musician needs a university degree and cancelling its music program is forcing Hamilton's young people seeking post-secondary music education to leave the community. Armstrong is less than three months on the job as president and is already talking about his "legacy." His legacy will include damaging the reputation of both Mohawk College and Hamilton for excellence in music and the arts.
Greg Crone, Caledonia
Sending hope and love
I'm sending hope and love to the drivers who cut me off today as I exited the Red Hill Valley Parkway to King Street while another driver criss-crossed the other way, after realizing he was at the wrong exit. And the 80 km/h speed limit is a joke on the RHVP, as are most speed limits. The constant tailgating, speeding and lack of signaling are the norm. But I save the best for last. When I pulled along side the driver who cut me off to exit, only to arrive at the red light the same time I did, he exclaimed, "You're too old!" Well, for your information, I'll most likely outlast you if you don't smarten up and drive safely. Thanks for making my day!
Enid S. Aaron, Hamilton
Hezbollah poses a real threat
Re: Will Netanyahu invade Lebanon? Of course he will, Oct. 1
Gwynne Dyer, in his predictably anti-Israel screed, blames Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for the problems at Israel's northern border. He claims Israel will invade Lebanon simply because Netanyahu wants to stay in power, and dismisses the rocket attacks by Hezbollah as "mostly for show." Nowhere does he mention the tunnels built by that terrorist organization to further its aim of invading Israel and kidnapping Israelis, as they have in the past. Nowhere does he mention that 60,000 people have had to leave their homes because of incessant and unprovoked rocket and mortar fire. No country should be subjected to these threats to their citizens. Why is it that only Israel is expected to? Haven't we had enough of Dyer and his constant anti-Israel nonsense?
David Streiner, Hamilton
Ontario is in a sorry state
Our waits at hospitals are terrible. Our roads are a mess and we have many homeless in the province. The LRT is on the back burner. The premier is telling the homeless to get off their ass and get a job. How can they do that when they don't have an address? Maybe Doug Ford will be nice and let them use his address. Not likely. Now his new idea is building a tunnel. I am wondering what his next bird-brain idea is going to be.
Sandra Martin, Hamilton
Ford needs to rethink cycling stance
Re: Will controversial Tory congestion law throw a wrench into Hamilton's bike lane build-out? Sept. 30
My questions for Premier Doug Ford are: How does he explain the constant congestion on our highways? These roads have restrictive access which prohibits bikes from using them.
Didn't Ford say he felt safer when there were bike lanes available during his Toronto cycling trip with NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh on an episode of "Political Blind Date" a few years ago?
Since cyclists tend to move slower than drivers of motor vehicles, would it not be wise to actually separate the two by building protected bike lanes? If they are wide enough they could also double as emergency vehicle access when the roads are stuffed full of cars. Then the vehicle drivers won't need to complain about being stuck behind a cyclist. Keeping in mind of course that cyclists also deserve to be able to take the most direct route possible to their destination as many secondary roads are windy, with a lot of stops thus slowing down a cyclists commute. Why are cars the only mode of transit Ford thinks should have direct door to door infrastructure?
I drive and I cycle to work. My commute door to door by car is about 30 minutes on average. My bike commute? Thirty-five minutes. I feel calmer, more relaxed and in a better mood when I bike to work. Although not perfect, I am thankful for the decent cycling infrastructure we have in Hamilton. I have seen a huge influx of cyclists this year, and have chatted with those who commute as far as Oakville by bike.
I hope Ford rethinks this policy because it is a populist decision meant to garner more votes and nothing more. Bikes are not the cause of traffic congestion. Motor vehicles are.
Erin Shacklette, Hamilton
Infrastructure fixes are a priority
Hamilton became a city in 1846. A little over 10 years later a pumping station was built to supply the city with water. That's a big undertaking to lay all those water lines. Fast forward 160 odd years later and I'm sure, even though some of those lines have been replaced, the pipes are going to need some serious attention. Now to the crux of the issue. Our infrastructure needs fixing. Roads and water mains are just a couple of examples. Why are our provincial, federal and even city governments wasting billions of dollars on a LRT? That money should be earmarked to fix this city! There should be no question, that's how it should be spent, and I'm sure I'm not the only one in this city that thinks so.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Bring MPP Jama back into the fold
Re: NDP hasn't ruled out bringing Sarah Jama back into the party for next election, Sept. 25
This move on the part of the NDP is long overdue. Sarah Jama is out in the neighbourhoods she represents every day, knocking on doors and asking constituents how she can help, or being part of community events, labour advocacy for workers, you name it. Even her most controversial move was to call for ceasefire in Gaza a week before everyone else did. Doug Ford saw an opening to distract from his massive Greenbelt scandal at the time and he took it. Ford and others divide - Sarah Jama unites.
Pat Antila, Hamilton
CAPTION: About 150 people attended a ceremony to mark the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation at the new James Street Plaza at the foot of James Street North on Hamilton Harbour. Residential school survivor Darlene LaForme spoke and Vision Spirit an Indigenous drum circle from Hamilton played during the ceremony.

Subject: Automobiles; College presidents; Music; Infrastructure; Musicians & conductors; Education; Roads & highways; Reconciliation; Vehicles; Bicycling

Here's how much Prime members can save on Lego, Mattel, Ray-Ban, Levi's, Ninja, Shark, LG, Google and other brands during Amazon's Prime Big Deal Days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-how-much-prime-members-can-save-on-lego/docview/3112504370/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+how+much+Prime+members+can+save+on+Lego%2C+Mattel%2C+Ray-Ban%2C+Levi%27s%2C+Ninja%2C+Shark%2C+LG%2C+Google+and+other+brands+during+Amazon%27s+Prime+Big+Deal+Days&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime members, these deals are just for you.
Amazon will be holding what it calls Prime Big Deal Days on Oct. 8 and 9, offering deals on major-brand products, but only Amazon Prime members get the deals.
"For 48 hours, Amazon will be offering Prime members thousands of deals worldwide from categories including electronics, home, toys, beauty, apparel, and much more," the company wrote. "Prime members will enjoy some of Amazon's lowest prices of the year on select products from top brands like Ninja, Shark, LG, JBL, HP, Levi's L'Oréal Paris, Murad, Mattel, and Lego."
So how much can shoppers actually save on these two days?
Amazon shared with us a sneak peak of the offers that will be available. Take a look below.
