Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3112401103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Musicians are workers too
Re: After dropping journalism, music, Mohawk College shifts focus to health care, Oct. 1
Mohawk College president Paul Armstrong's emphasis on jobs is correct, but he seems not to understand that musicians are also working people who contribute to our economy. The college strays from its mandate as a college of applied "arts" and technology by cancelling its music program. Mohawk has a reputation for music excellence and Hamilton has always strongly supported the arts, styling itself as "Music City." Not every aspiring musician needs a university degree and cancelling its music program is forcing Hamilton's young people seeking post-secondary music education to leave the community. Armstrong is less than three months on the job as president and is already talking about his "legacy." His legacy will include damaging the reputation of both Mohawk College and Hamilton for excellence in music and the arts.
Greg Crone, Caledonia
Turn bylaw on sidewalk cyclists
Re: Council to consider increasing one-third of all parking-related fines, Sept. 30
The bike lane advocates are at it again. This time not about increasing the abundance of the underused lanes, but rather increasing the fine to $200 for a car that momentarily stops in one. My observation is that the bike lanes are hardly used by cyclists, as most of them ride illegally on the sidewalk. That being so, maybe our money-making "parking enforcement officers" should be issuing $200 tickets to these sidewalk cyclists. And while so engaged, they could add the electric scooter riders to their quota!
Terry Butlin, Hamilton
Don't weaken day's importance
Re: Truth and reconciliation: Hamilton to explore city holiday, Sept. 28
Truth and Reconciliation Day ought to be treated like Remembrance Day when children and adolescents are in school and participate in various activities designed to remind them of the significance of Nov. 11. If we make it a provincial holiday most people, and their children, would simply treat it as "just another day off" and give no mind to its intended purpose i.e. to consider the historical mistreatment of our First Nations and share the value of Indigenous history and culture. In the long term, the success of Truth and Reconciliation lies with the education of our youngest citizens.
David Fawcett, Hamilton
A legacy we must face and rectify
As a longtime advocate of social justice and ally of the Indigenous community, I attended Hamilton's commemoration to mark National Day for Truth and Reconciliation on Monday. I was encouraged to see the photos of the sombre yet celebratory event on the front page of The Spec.
The atrocities of residential schools and the generational trauma that followed is a dark legacy that Canadians must face and rectify. Through education and open dialogue with our Indigenous communities we must make amends for the suffering that was inflicted by our government and the church.By continuing to listen and learn rather than speak, we can ensure that "truth and reconciliation" is not just a catchphrase to which we pay lip service. It is only by doing so that the mistakes of the past can be prevented from occurring in the future.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Infrastructure
fixes a priority
Hamilton became a city in 1846. A little over 10 years later a pumping station was built to supply the city with water. That's a big undertaking to lay all those water lines. Fast forward 160 odd years later and I'm sure, even though some of those lines have been replaced, the pipes are going to need some serious attention. Now to the crux of the issue. Our infrastructure needs fixing. Roads and water mains are just a couple of examples. Why are our provincial, federal and even city governments wasting billions of dollars on a LRT? That money should be earmarked to fix this city! There should be no question, that's how it should be spent, and I'm sure I'm not the only one in this city that thinks so.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Ontario is in
a sorry state
Our waits at hospitals are terrible. Our roads are a mess and we have many homeless in the province. The LRT is on the back burner. The premier is telling the homeless to get off their ass and get a job. How can they do that when they don't have an address? Maybe Doug Ford will be nice and let them use his address. Not likely. Now his new idea is building a tunnel. I am wondering what his next bird-brain idea is going to be.
Sandra Martin, Hamilton
Bring Jama back into the NDP fold
Re: NDP hasn't ruled out
bringing Sarah Jama back into the party for next election, Sept. 25
This move on the part of the NDP is long overdue. Sarah Jama is out in the neighbourhoods she represents every day, knocking on doors and asking constituents how she can help, or being part of community events, labour advocacy for workers, you name it. Even her most controversial move was to call for ceasefire in Gaza a week before everyone else did. Doug Ford saw an opening to distract from his massive Greenbelt scandal at the time and he took it. Ford and others divide - Sarah Jama unites.
Pat Antila, Hamilton
Hezbollah poses
a real threat
Re: Will Netanyahu invade Lebanon? Of course he will, Oct. 1
Gwynne Dyer, in his predictably anti-Israel screed, blames Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for the problems at Israel's northern border. He claims Israel will invade Lebanon simply because Netanyahu wants to stay in power, and dismisses the rocket attacks by Hezbollah as "mostly for show." Nowhere does he mention the tunnels built by that terrorist organization to further its aim of invading Israel and kidnapping Israelis, as they have in the past. Nowhere does he mention that 60,000 people have had to leave their homes because of incessant and unprovoked rocket and mortar fire. No country should be subjected to these threats to their citizens. Why is it that only Israel is expected to? Haven't we had enough of Dyer and his constant anti-Israel nonsense?
David Streiner, Hamilton
Sending hope
and love
I'm sending hope and love to the drivers who cut me off today as I exited the Red Hill Valley Parkway to King Street while another driver criss-crossed the other way, after realizing he was at the wrong exit. And the 80 km/h speed limit is a joke on the RHVP, as are most speed limits. The constant tailgating, speeding and lack of signalling are the norm. But I save the best for last. When I pulled along side the driver who cut me off to exit, only to arrive at the red light the same time I did, he exclaimed, "You're too old!" Well, for your information, I'll most likely outlast you if you don't smarten up and drive safely. Thanks for making my day!
Enid S. Aaron, Hamilton

national briefs: Speed limit rise on 400-seriesin the works

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3112398055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=national+briefs%3A+Speed+limit+rise+on+400-seriesin+the+works&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Toronto officer shot in midtown
Toronto police say an officer has been rushed to hospital after being shot in the city's midtown area and they are searching for a suspect. Police say it happened around 5:30 p.m. Wednesday after officers stopped a vehicle while conducting an investigation in the Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue area. They say an officer discharged a firearm at the scene. Police say one person was arrested at the scene and another is still at large. They say the province's police watchdog is investigating. The shooting led to rush-hour chaos in what is a busy part of the city, with sirens blaring and traffic gridlock. There was no word on the extent of the officer's injuries.
Speed limit rise on 400-series
in the works
Ontario Premier Doug Ford says the province is planning to increase speed limits on 400-series highways across the province. The government increased speed limits from 100 kilometres per hour to 110 km/h on six sections of provincial highways in 2022 after several successful pilot programs that first began in 2019. Earlier this year, the province raised the speed limit on 10 more sections of highways across the province. Ford says Wednesday that he has directed Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria to raise the speed limit to 110 kilometres on all 400-series highways "where it is safe to do so." Ford says he is leaving no stone unturned in his goal to help fight gridlock. The move comes as Ford has also directed officials to explore building a tunnel under Highway 401 across the Greater Toronto Area.
News services

Document 418 of 500

Buying back Hwy. 407 'on the table,' Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/buying-back-hwy-407-on-table-ford-says/docview/3112397729/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Buying+back+Hwy.+407+%27on+the+table%2C%27+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TORONTO Ontario is considering buying back the tolled Highway 407, possibly in addition to building a massive tunnel under Highway 401 in a bid to ease congestion, Premier Doug Ford said Wednesday.
The Mike Harris Progressive Conservative government sold Highway 407 in 1999 for $3.1 billion dollars to a consortium that included SNC Lavalin, Quebec's provincial pension fund and Spanish company Ferrovial.
The Canada Pension Plan Investment Board now owns 50.01 per cent of 407 ETR, which runs the highway that goes across the Greater Toronto Area.
Ford has already tasked transportation officials with conducting a feasibility study on digging a tunnel under Highway 401 for traffic and transit going across the GTA, saying gridlock costs the economy $11 billion a year.
The premier has repeatedly said he will forge ahead with the tunnel regardless of the study's outcome, framing it as looking at how - not if - it can be done.
When asked Wednesday at a news conference whether Ontario could instead buy back Highway 407 to get more cars off Highway 401 at a lower price, Ford did not rule it out.
"All options are on the table - maybe both options," he said. "We'll do the feasibility study. We'll look at the 407 and see exactly which way are we going."
The province still owns a 22-kilometre stretch on Highway 407's eastern flank and tolls drivers at a significantly cheaper rate than the private portion of the highway.
Ford on Wednesday lamented the high cost for drivers using Highway 407. While his government has removed tolls on highways 412 and 418, he has not so far indicated he would remove tolls on the provincially owned section of Highway 407 East.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 419 of 500

Prison time for woman who forced Brantford teen into sex trade: Three co-conspirators were convicted in a previous trial; another person was acquitted

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/prison-time-woman-who-forced-brantford-teen-into/docview/3112397424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prison+time+for+woman+who+forced+Brantford+teen+into+sex+trade%3A+Three+co-conspirators+were+convicted+in+a+previous+trial%3B+another+person+was+acquitted&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The last of five Caledonia residents accused of forcing a young woman from Brantford into the sex trade is headed to prison.
In court on Tuesday in Cayuga, Carly Creor was sentenced to two years - after subtracting 63 days for time served in pretrial custody - after pleading guilty to procuring and advertising sexual services.
Justice Paul Sweeny ruled Creor, now 30, was a key player in a scheme to traffic an 18-year-old woman - whose name is protected by a publication ban - over the course of three and a half months in late 2019 and early 2020.
Three co-conspirators were convicted of human trafficking and other offences in a previous trial. A fifth accused was acquitted. Creor pleaded guilty in January, but her sentencing was delayed several times due to scheduling issues and delays gathering medical records.
Court heard the victim was already performing "sexual favours" for her drug dealer and future boyfriend, Daniel Campbell, when Campbell introduced her to Creor and Creor's partner at the time, Dragisa Lucic, at a southwestern Ontario hotel in December 2019.
The victim thought she was taking her first steps toward a modelling career by posing for photos in lingerie. But the façade fell away when Creor "explained what would be expected of (her) in the sex trade," Sweeny said while reading a summation of the facts.
The victim was given a new name and hair colour and "worked seven days a week," Sweeny said, providing sexual services for up to 10 clients a day.
Creor placed online advertisements for high-priced oral, vaginal and anal sex acts. She also handled negotiations with clients, booked the hotel rooms, and "in part" determined when the victim was allowed to eat and take breaks, Sweeny said.
Creor got grief from Lucic and Campbell when the victim was late or missed appointments, and she in turn berated the victim and made her feel "worthless," the judge said.
Court heard Creor gave the victim "uppers" to stay awake for 16 to 20 hours at a stretch, and green Xanax pills - known as "hulks" - to calm down afterwards.
Creor also arranged "party and play services," procuring cocaine for the victim to take with clients.
The victim saw none of the money and was forced to perform sex acts she was uncomfortable with. On one occasion, Sweeny said the victim told Creor and her other handlers she "had a bad feeling" about a client who wanted to meet her for a "car call."
"But they made her see him anyway," Sweeny said of the call that saw the victim beaten up and robbed.
When searching Creor's cellphone, police found texts sent to Campbell complaining about the victim oversleeping and passing up "money that is literally knocking at her door."
"She was a drug addict, vulnerable and homeless," Sweeny said, noting Creor took advantage of the victim's precarious housing and lack of family support while exercising a "tremendous amount of control" over her movements and finances.
Prosecutor Heather Palin from the Ontario Human Trafficking Prosecution Team sought a sentence of three and a half years, while the defence pushed for a conditional sentence of two years less a day to be served under house arrest, plus three years probation.
In an interview, Creor's lawyer Scott Reid called the two-year sentence "unfortunate," arguing his client was "victimized" by the scheme's actual masterminds, Campbell and Lucic, who Reid said "exerted their control" over Creor.
Creor later told a cousin Lucic abused her, a claim supported by her phone's internet search history indicating she looked up information about self-harm and intimate partner violence, court heard.
Sweeny acknowledged Creor's vulnerabilities, finding she gained no financial benefit from the scheme. But the victim's sex work paid for the Caledonia house where Creor and her co-conspirators lived, and Sweeny noted Creor "continued in her role with the group" despite her own alleged experience of abuse.
The judge acknowledged Creor is a first-time offender with "little risk of reoffending," and her guilty plea avoided the need for a trial. But he said those mitigating factors did not justify a conditional sentence.
The victim did not address the court or submit an impact statement, but Sweeny said what happened to her must have had "a profound negative impact" on her life.
"Ms. Creor must know this," the judge said.
Noting Creor started psychological counselling while under house arrest, Reid said going to prison "is certainly going to interrupt that," arguing a conditional sentence "would have conveyed the same amount of deterrence and denunciation" as time in custody.
"I think incarceration should be an absolute last resort," Reid told reporters. "I don't think it was necessary in this case, and it's regrettable that the judge felt otherwise."
Creor will be on probation for two years after her release and must not contact the victim.
Three other charges - including trafficking in persons and receiving material benefit from sexual services - were withdrawn at the request of the Crown.
J.P. Antonacci's reporting
is funded by the Canadian
government through its
Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com

Document 420 of 500

Doug Ford pledges 'complete audit' of Ontario children's aid 'nightmare stories' in wake of Star investigation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pledges-complete-audit-ontario/docview/3112599782/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pledges+%27complete+audit%27+of+Ontario+children%27s+aid+%27nightmare+stories%27+in+wake+of+Star+investigation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has pledged his government will do a "complete audit" of Ontario children's aid societies, alleging that he's heard "nightmare stories" about the misuse of taxpayer dollars by the agencies.
His comments, made Wednesday, came in response to Star reporting that child welfare leaders are sounding the alarm about kids and teens with complex health needs being housed in hotels, Airbnbs and office buildings because they are unable to get the medical help and specialized treatments they need.
Ford said his government has increased funding to children's aid societies, but alleged some are abusing taxpayer money rather than properly spending those funds on kids.
"We're hearing nightmare stories about the abuse of taxpayers' money - I've heard stories of some of these agencies working in Taj Mahals. They're paying rent - $100,000 for rent," Ford said when asked about the issue at an unrelated announcement Wednesday morning in East York.
And any managers "giving yourselves a bonus - not worrying about the kids - I'm coming for you," the premier said.
A Star investigation has revealed that hundreds of Ontario children with complex health needs have been living in hotels, Airbnbs, rental apartments, offices and even camping trailers because the province has no live-in treatment options for them. New figures from the organization that represents Ontario's children's aid societies show the number of kids and teens in the care of child welfare agencies and living in unlicensed settings has tripled since 2021.
Children with complex needs often have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, or substance abuse and self-harm, and their families typically struggle to get them timely and adequate medical care and supports.
Child welfare leaders across the province are reporting an increasing number of desperate families relinquishing care of their kids because they've been unable to get help in the community. But because CAS does not have faster access to scarce treatment beds or intensive services, the agencies are forced to house young people with complex mental health and developmental needs in unlicensed settings, which in turn leads to high costs for agencies.
It's unclear how much is being spent across the province on housing kids in makeshift settings.
In the year ending in March 2024, there were 354 children whose care had each cost child welfare agencies more than $200,000 per year; the Star has learned of at least four children and youth for whom the annual cost of housing and supervision is roughly $1 million each.
In the wake of Star reporting, child advocates and opposition MPPs are calling on the Ford government to launch an emergency response to address the high numbers of children with complex needs ending up in the care of children's aid and say the province can no longer "look away" from the crisis.
On Wednesday, Ford said the province has boosted funding over the last decade and will now do "a complete audit" on children's aid societies, adding: "If we see funds not being spent properly on the kids, guess what? You're looking for another job. Simple as that."
The total number of children in the care of child welfare agencies has gone down by about a third over the last decade. Yet the agencies are still "running non-stop deficits," he added. "Not all, but of a lot of them, and we've increased (funding) ... It's just unacceptable."
Ford said he has asked Michael Parsa, Minister of Children, Community and Social Services, to start audits immediately "across the board."
"Where there's smoke, there's fire, and there's definitely an abuse of taxpayers money throughout the whole system there, but we'll get down to the bottom (if it)," Ford said.
Child welfare leaders previously told the Star that deficits stem from creating emergency placements for children with complex needs. The number of children and youth needing high-cost care has doubled in the past three years, according to data provided to the Star by the Ontario Association of Children's Aid Societies.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, seen here in a July 10, 2024, file photo, has pledged a "complete audit" of Ontario children's aid societies.

Document 421 of 500

Doug Ford signals Ontario will take action on homeless tent encampments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-signals-ontario-will-take-action-on/docview/3112599675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+signals+Ontario+will+take+action+on+homeless+tent+encampments&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is signalling the province will take action to deal with homeless tent encampments in Toronto parks and other communities across Ontario.
"It's a big problem," said Ford, whose office at the Ontario legislature is about 400 metres south of an encampment at city-run Queen's Park.
"We've got to find homes for these folks as well, and that's our goal - all three levels of government. But I'll tell you, communities across the province, they're just beside themselves," the premier said Wednesday at an unrelated announcement in East York.
"I got a call yesterday. There's needles in the park right beside a place (with) encampments all over the place. It's unacceptable, and we have to all work together to find them shelter and get them out of the public spaces. You just can't be doing it."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who was also at the announcement, said the housing crisis is compounded by the city's increasing population, and "it's hard to find affordable housing for everyone, which is why you see all our shelters are full. We now have 12,000 people in our shelters; half of them are refugees claimants. So the situation is really difficult."
She acknowledged concerns raised by area residents about the encampments, saying "in big parks, small parks - especially in small parks ... (the tents) are right beside playgrounds, and it's just really difficult for families and the kids, because then they have no room for the kids to play. So, we're working hard."
She said a Canada-Ontario rent supplement, a program that will resume this fall, helps moves families out of shelters "which means that we can then make room for people that are living in tents, as the winter arrives in a few months - we can now get them from the encampments into shelters so that they're indoors and don't have to endure the outdoor environment."
Chow noted that shelter spots and housing were found for people living in tents in Grange Park, and many at Allan Gardens.
"People don't want to be camping, they don't want to be outside," the mayor also said. " ... And some of the neighbourhoods are being impacted tremendously ... It's in the best interest for us to deal with this crisis."
Last week, Ford said homeless people in encampments need to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else," though was criticized for not considering how the cost of food or housing impacts people, especially those in low-wage jobs or on social assistance.
The premier has said he's heard from a number of Ontarians and municipal officials who are "rightly frustrated" with encampments.
Encampments are an issue jurisdictions across North America are struggling with post-pandemic as homelessness, mental health issues and substance abuse are on the rise.
In 2021, Toronto began enforcing the no-camping rules in some parks, clearing out homeless campers, but has since backed off that approach.
Hamilton is now allowing encampments with small cabins, making social supports available, until housing is found for them.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford.

Document 422 of 500

Prison time for Caledonia woman who forced 18-year-old into sex trade

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/prison-time-caledonia-woman-who-forced-18-year/docview/3112401559/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prison+time+for+Caledonia+woman+who+forced+18-year-old+into+sex+trade&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The last of five Caledonia residents accused of forcing a young woman from Brantford into the sex trade is headed to prison.
In court on Tuesday in Cayuga, Carly Creor was sentenced to two years - after subtracting 63 days for time served in pretrial custody - after pleading guilty to procuring and advertising sexual services.
Justice Paul Sweeny ruled Creor, now 30, was a key player in a scheme to traffic an 18-year-old woman - whose name is protected by a publication ban - over the course of three and a half months in late 2019 and early 2020.
Three co-conspirators were convicted of human trafficking and other offences in a previous trial. A fifth accused was acquitted.
Creor pleaded guilty in January, but her sentencing was delayed several times due to scheduling issues and delays gathering medical records.
Court heard the victim was already performing "sexual favours" for her drug dealer and future boyfriend, Daniel Campbell, when Campbell introduced her to Creor and Creor's partner at the time, Dragisa Lucic, at a southwestern Ontario hotel in December 2019.
The victim thought she was taking her first steps toward a modelling career by posing for photos in lingerie. But the façade fell away when Creor "explained what would be expected of (her) in the sex trade," Sweeny said while reading a summation of the facts.
The victim was given a new name and hair colour and "worked seven days a week," Sweeny said, providing sexual services for up to 10 clients a day,
Creor placed online advertisements for high-priced oral, vaginal and anal sex acts. She also handled negotiations with clients, booked the hotel rooms, and "in part" determined when the victim was allowed to eat and take breaks, Sweeny said.
Creor got grief from Lucic and Campbell when the victim was late or missed appointments, and she in turn berated the victim and made her feel "worthless," the judge said.
Court heard Creor gave the victim "uppers" to stay awake for 16 to 20 hours at a stretch, and green Xanax pills - known as "hulks" - to calm down afterwards.
Creor also arranged "party and play services," procuring cocaine for the victim to take with clients.
The victim saw none of the money and was forced to perform sex acts she was uncomfortable with. On one occasion, Sweeny said the victim told Creor and her other handlers she "had a bad feeling" about a client who wanted to meet her for a "car call."
"But they made her see him anyway," Sweeny said of the call that saw the victim beaten up and robbed.
When searching Creor's cellphone, police found texts sent to Campbell complaining about the victim oversleeping and passing up "money that is literally knocking at her door."
"She was a drug addict, vulnerable and homeless," Sweeny said, noting Creor took advantage of the victim's precarious housing and lack of family support while exercising a "tremendous amount of control" over her movements and finances.
Prosecutor Heather Palin from the Ontario Human Trafficking Prosecution Team sought a sentence of three and a half years, while the defence pushed for a conditional sentence of two years less a day to be served under house arrest, plus three years probation.
In an interview, Creor's lawyer Scott Reid called the two-year sentence "unfortunate," arguing his client was "victimized" by the scheme's actual masterminds, Campbell and Lucic, who Reid said "exerted their control" over Creor.
Creor later told a cousin Lucic abused her, a claim supported by her phone's internet search history indicating she looked up information about self-harm and intimate partner violence, court heard.
Sweeny acknowledged Creor's vulnerabilities, finding she gained no financial benefit from the scheme. But the victim's sex work paid for the Caledonia house where Creor and her co-conspirators lived, and Sweeny noted Creor "continued in her role with the group" despite her own alleged experience of abuse.
The judge acknowledged Creor is a first-time offender with "little risk of reoffending," and her guilty plea avoided the need for a trial. But he said those mitigating factors did not justify a conditional sentence.
The victim did not address the court or submit an impact statement, but Sweeny said what happened to her must have had "a profound negative impact" on her life.
"Ms. Creor must know this," the judge said.
Noting Creor started psychological counselling while under house arrest, Reid said going to prison "is certainly going to interrupt that," arguing a conditional sentence "would have conveyed the same amount of deterrence and denunciation" as time in custody.
"I think incarceration should be an absolute last resort," Reid told reporters. "I don't think it was necessary in this case, and it's regrettable that the judge felt otherwise."
Creor will be on probation for two years after her release and must not contact the victim.
Three other charges - including trafficking in persons and receiving material benefit from sexual services - were withdrawn at the request of the Crown.
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com
CAPTION: A Caledonia woman who pleaded guilty in connection with a sex-trafficking scheme was sentenced to two years on Tuesday.

Document 423 of 500

Justin Trudeau dishes with Liberal MP on where he went wrong, what he did right and why he's the one to beat Pierre Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-dishes-with-liberal-mp-on-where-he/docview/3112397987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+dishes+with+Liberal+MP+on+where+he+went+wrong%2C+what+he+did+right+and+why+he%27s+the+one+to+beat+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Under siege in the polls but fighting for a shot at a fourth term, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took to a backbench MP's podcast to riff on why he thinks he deserves it.
In a nearly hour-long interview recorded Friday and published Tuesday, Trudeau gave more revealing answers to fellow political traveller Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith than he has to any journalist in months.
The prime minister conceded a few mistakes. But only a few. And he expressed little regret.
Trudeau conceded: the Liberal party has given Pierre Poilievre two years to build his political brand instead of immediately running ads against the Conservative leader; that as prime minister he failed to deliver on his promise of electoral reform; that the Liberal party bears some responsibility for two bad byelection losses in Toronto and Montreal strongholds; and that the federal government should have acted sooner on housing.
Yet his abstract responses to Erskine-Smith's challenge on other questions - on what policies he wants to see through in a fourth term, on why he's running despite anonymous backbench naysayers and bad polls, or on how he thinks Liberals can win - underscored a bigger issue.
Can the Liberal leader, who has been on the Canadian and American podcast circuit in an attempt to broaden the reach of his message, get through to those he needs to persuade - not least, other Liberal MPs, party volunteers and those voters he's lost since 2015 that he hopes to bring back onside.
First, the mistakes:
 Trudeau defended the decision not to run political ads against Poilievre as a tactical consideration, that years before an election was too soon and that he'd rebuffed a similar attempt by Conservatives to frame him too early, and more importantly, that he felt it would look petty to voters if the Liberals took their eye off the bigger economic and financial woes then facing Canadians. When Erskine-Smith begged to differ, Trudeau insisted there is still time to paint Poilievre as the risk to the environment, equality rights and social programs that the Liberals believe he is.
 On the broken promise of electoral reform, Trudeau wished he'd had a do-over. He said he should have immediately shut down talk about proportional representation as a viable option. He repeated his oft-expressed view it would have been too divisive and spliced the Canadian electorate into ever smaller pies, but confessed Liberals were deliberately vague in order to appeal to Fair Vote Canada advocates. Trudeau said he wished he'd used his majority to bring in the ranked ballot model he preferred, saying it would not have changed ridings or even the ballot that much - suggesting instead of a check, voters would rank their preferences in order - and would force parties to appeal beyond their bases. But Trudeau said he did not feel there was a broad level of support in the House for what he called "an irreversible change," so he pulled the plug. Erskine-Smith said the broken promise was his most difficult day in nine years in Trudeau's caucus. Trudeau said it was "a gut-wrenching day for me."
 On the byelection losses in Toronto-St. Paul's and LaSalle-Emard-Verdun, Trudeau said there were "a lot of factors" in each but the Liberal candidates did not have enough time on the ground to campaign ahead of the byelection dates, noting he'd had a year to campaign before winning Papineau. However Trudeau's answer did not reflect the fact that Leslie Church had been campaigning for nearly a year in the riding before she was appointed, and it did not address complaints that the appointments of her and Laura Palestini as candidate in the Montreal-area riding prevented competitive nomination races and angered some in those ridings. Nor did Trudeau directly address Erskine-Smith's question about what those losses told him about the bigger changes that are needed.
 On housing and immigration, Trudeau acknowledged that the massive post-pandemic influx of temporary residents - foreign workers and international students - had an impact on housing shortages. But he said his government should have "leaned harder" on provinces and territories to address "NIMBYism" (not-in-my-backyard pushback) in order to build more housing.
But it was the prime minister's responses to Erskine-Smith's probing about why he is still running and how he thinks he can reverse the Liberals' fortunes that underscored the big challenge.
"Obviously your brain didn't melt on national television the way that Biden's did," Erskine-Smith said, but Kamala Harris has given that party a better shot at winning. He asked Trudeau to explain why he thinks he's the best person to lead the Liberals' next fight.
Trudeau's answer was, basically, that he is the one who knows better than anyone "exactly how hard this fight is going to be," that it is "fundamental to how Canadians come out of or come through" the past few difficult years, and it is "exactly why I got into politics."
He touted progressive policies that delivered for people and will last over the long term: the Canada Child Benefit, standing up a national child care program, fighting climate change including with carbon pricing, and efforts to tackle housing shortages he said will pay off years from now.
Trudeau pivoted to note, "Nobody is asking Poilievre what ... he is fighting for ... he hasn't even begun to articulate what he's fighting for ... other than himself and his desire to be in power."
However, Trudeau effectively said that there is no new vision for the coming campaign, rather that Liberals are fighting for the same vision he fought for in 2015, to protect the environment and the economy, to lift up ordinary Canadians and improve people's resilience for the future.
"Yeah, it's going to be a change election," he said. "Everything has changed, not just climate change, but the way we work, the way AI works, the way geopolitics happens, the pressures on everything. The world is in a massive pivot moment right now, and we don't know what the biggest issue is going to be."
To confront that kind of change, Trudeau insisted that Canadians understand what his "values" are, and what he brings to a crisis, pointing out that the crises he confronted, from the COVID-19 pandemic to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, were not foreseen in any campaign.
Values, he said, are "ultimately the only thing."
Except in Poilievre, the Liberals are facing a political rival who is promising practical benefits for individual households, with slogan-based promises to "axe the tax, build the homes," and "stop the crime" and is laying claim to represent "common sense" Canadian values.
Erskine-Smith rightly called Trudeau out on all the high-level talk, and said Poilievre is a better communicator than any Conservative leader in the past nine years. He urged Trudeau to be better at telling the Liberals' story.
On Wednesday, after it aired, Erskine-Smith said his personal decision not to run in the next election is because he needs to spend more time with his young children, but he still supports Trudeau, at least for now.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau rises during question period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, onOct. 2, 2024.

Subject: Electoral reform; Canadians; Voters; Climate change; Prime ministers; Political advertising; Pandemics; Elections

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Justin Trudeau dishes with Liberal MP on where he went wrong, what he did right and why he's the one to beat Pierre Poilievre

Document 424 of 500

Doug Ford raising speed limit to 110 km/h on all 400-series highways 'where it is safe to do so'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-raising-speed-limit-110-km-h-on-all-400/docview/3112109564/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+raising+speed+limit+to+110+km%2Fh+on+all+400-series+highways+%27where+it+is+safe+to+do+so%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford feels the need for speed.
Against the backdrop of a potential spring provincial election, Ford on Wednesday said the speed limit on all 400-series highways will be raised from 100 km/h to 110 km/h "where it is safe to do so."
Speaking to reporters at a campaign-style announcement in East York to tout construction on the Ontario Line subway, the premier said he was hitting the gas pedal to keep motorists moving.
"Earlier this year, we raised the speed limit on 10 additional sections of 400-series highways and today I've directed the minister to raise the speed limit to 110 kilometres on all 400 series highways where it is safe to do so," he said.
"I'm going to repeat that: where it is safe to do. So we're leaving no stone unturned to help fight gridlock on our roads, reduce travel times and allow people to spend less time commuting and more time at home with their families, and that includes the Ontario Line, which is going to be a game changer for Toronto and the entire region."
Ford, who last week promised a feasibility study into a potential 60-kilometre tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga and Brampton to Scarborough that could cost tens of billions of dollars, stressed "everything's on the table right now" to reduce gridlock.
The Toronto Region Board of Trade has estimated that traffic snarls cost the provincial economy $11 billion annually in lost productivity.
Asked about proposals from Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles to eliminate tolls on the privately owned Highway 407 so more cars and trucks could use it, Ford conceded such a move could be in the works.
"We'll look at the 407 and see exactly which way are we going. We own about 22 kilometres on the far (eastern) end of the 407 and there's other opportunities along the 407," he said, noting motorists pay about $1.4 billion in tolls annually.
"All options are on the table, maybe both options. Let's see. But we know one thing is costing our economy $11 billion a year. This is costing people over 100 hours of sitting in traffic on the 401," he said.
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener-Centre) said the speed limit gambit proves "the Ford government doesn't have a serious plan to make it easier for Ontarians to get around."
"The Eglinton LRT looks like it won't open until at least 2025, the Finch West LRT is now delayed too, and gridlock on the 401 has become so bad that it's almost unbearable," said Clancy.
"If the premier thinks increasing the speed limit on some sections of some highways is going to fix the problem, he just doesn't get it," she said, urging improved all-day GO train and bus service, dedicated transit lanes and "more bike-share programs and e-bike infrastructure."
While NDP premier Bob Rae built the 407, his Progressive Conservative successor Mike Harris sold a 99-year lease to operate it to a private consortium for $3.1 billion.
Ford has repeatedly criticized that sell-off - even though his father, Doug Ford, Sr., was an MPP in that Tory government and Harris's son, Mike Harris, Jr., is a cabinet minister in the premier's government.
"I would have never sold it," he said during his successful 2022 re-election campaign.
Wednesday's changes come as the premier is considering an election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford takes part in the parade as he attends the International Ploughing Match in Lindsay, Ont., on Oct. 1, 2024.

Subject: Speed limits; Automobile drivers; Roads & highways

Title: Doug Ford raising speed limit to 110 km/h on all 400-series highways 'where it is safe to do so'

Document 425 of 500

Oct. 2: Don't give these monsters any attention, action better than empty words and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-2-dont-give-these-monsters-any-attention/docview/3112109555/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+2%3A+Don%27t+give+these+monsters+any+attention%2C+action+better+than+empty+words+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't put killers in spotlight
Re: Peacock series about serial killer John Wayne Gacy films at Dundas legion, Sept. 27
I was saddened to see the article in the Spectator describing the filming of a series about John Wayne Gacy. I can't help but think when it comes out, those close to his victims will be victimized all over again. Because make no mistake - the families and friends of the murdered are just as much victims as the people tortured and murdered by this monster. And really, what do we gain as a society by glorifying and celebrating a real-life monster?
I can't help but contrast this story with a recent experience. When I was privileged enough to visit family in Norway, they took us to see the beautiful and incredibly moving memorial to 77 people murdered there in a 2011 terror attack. What is most striking is all the dead are memorialized forever - but the killer is not named. Not in the media and not on the memorial. Norway decided to deny him the notoriety he craved - and to recognize his victims alone. He will likely die in prison, anonymous and forgotten. It's much more fitting than being turned into a vile hero in some sick recounting of the story for transitory titillation.
Wayne Stansfield, Hamilton
Let's show some compassion
I belong to several neighbourhood groups on social media and it's heartbreaking to read the judgmental rants about people with substance disorders and their paraphernalia littering our streets and parks. I pick up thousands of beer, liquor and power drink bottles from ditches. I see people throwing cigarette butts out of car windows and on sidewalks. Every diabetic I know uses their insulin so they can have something sweet. A huge percentage of our population is obese and often eat for comfort. And yet I rarely hear the nasty rhetoric toward those people that I hear toward people who use drugs.
These people may have lost their job, home or family and turn to drugs as a way to cope, just as we turn to cigarettes, alcohol, caffeine, sugar and social media. Let's have a little more compassion and look at our own addictions before we judge others.
Martha Howatt, Hamilton
Action better than empty words
Re: Truth and reconciliation: Hamilton to explore city holiday, Sept. 28
Will there ever be an end to pointless virtue signalling? The city has an escalating housing crisis and many of the people experiencing homelessness are, no doubt, Indigenous.
Onto the front lines in this fight charges Mayor Andrea Horwath, who suggests making Truth and Reconciliation Day a municipal holiday, which - it has obviously not occurred to her - would shut many doors to services that those people need.
In addition to the endless land acknowledgments we have to suffer through at various meetings and sports events, I received a letter from a bank that began with a pious "acknowledgment" of the land the bank is built on.
Whether it's Horwath, Justin Trudeau or others, none of this sanctimony is making a jot of difference. My plea then is, instead of empty words that achieve nothing - do something to improve the lot of the people you supposedly advocate for. How long has Grassy Narrows not had potable water?
Neville Newman, Hamilton
We've lost a country music icon
Re: Kris Kristofferson, singer-songwriter and actor, dies at 88, Sept. 29
I am heartbroken to learn about the loss of country music icon Kris Kristofferson. The beloved artist, songwriter, actor and Country Music Hall of Fame member has forever left a mark on music's history. My sincere condolences go out to his family and loved ones during this difficult time.
Paul Bacon, Hallandale Beach, Fla.
Who can afford this "help?"
Re: Ottawa to allow 30-year mortgage loans for all first-time and new-build homebuyers, Sept. 16
Looking into this pre-election carrot we received from the Liberal party, it seems you can now borrow up to $1.5 million with as low as only 10 per cent down. So, first off you need a $150,000 deposit, add about $57,000 to the mortgage amount for insurance with monthly payments of about $7,700 per month. You also need an income of just north of $300,000 per year and as a bonus you get to pay for it for a longer period. I really don't know what else to add to that. Maybe they're just trolling us.
Tony Fallucco, Hamilton
Seek out tiny home sponsors
The concept of tiny homes and more temporary shelter is great. However, guaranteeing an apartment is questionable. I would like to see people who show responsibility and a desire to improve their lives become housed. I have spoken to city housing employees with horror stories of damaged apartments. Indwell does it right by combining housing skills with responsibility. I for one am thankful for Indwell.
Secondly, let's encourage sponsorship of the actual tiny homes. At about $15,000 each, they could be funded by companies, charitable groups and even individuals themselves to help with our crisis. Food banks presently receive this type of sponsorship. Put their names on the homes.
There will always be mental health, drug addictions and in a knowledge-based economy, unemployment. The homeless problem needs long-term solutions.
David McIntyre, Hamilton
Premier's comments are heartless
I agree 100 per cent with Janet MacNeil's letter that Premier Doug Ford should be ashamed of himself for his nasty and heartless remarks: "get off your asses and get a job." He is totally uncaring about anybody and everybody except his buddies.
Now he talks of building tunnels. Time for him to go but while he's here, he needs to help with some of these issues and get off his ass and help people.
Janet Remias, Ancaster
CAPTION: The Dundas Legion recently plays a Moose Lodge in Illinois for the Peacock TV series "Devil in Disguise: John Wayne Gacy." We shouldn't give "real-life monsters" this kind of attention, a reader argues.

Document 426 of 500

Farmers raise fear of dwindling farmland at annual rural expo: Secrecy behind Waterloo region project called 'flat-out undemocratic'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/farmers-raise-fear-dwindling-farmland-at-annual/docview/3111857866/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Farmers+raise+fear+of+dwindling+farmland+at+annual+rural+expo%3A+Secrecy+behind+Waterloo+region+project+called+%27flat-out+undemocratic%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several Ontario farmers raised concerns on Tuesday about dwindling farmland across the province as politicians of all stripes gather at an annual rural and agricultural expo.
Ontario politicians descended upon Lindsay, Ont., as part of their yearly pilgrimage to the International Plowing Match.
Several farmers from the Waterloo region said they are concerned with a massive land assembly of prime farmland in the area that is cloaked in non-disclosure agreements and threats of expropriation.
The Region of Waterloo has said it is trying to assemble a supersite in an effort to lure a massive manufacturing plant. The province is funding the land assembly, but has said it does not have any companies lined up for the site.
Alfred Lowrick, who lives next to the target site, said the entire process has shown a lack of respect to farmers, in addition to the threat of losing 770 acres of prime farmland in southwestern Ontario.
"We don't understand why all of a sudden it came out of the blue," said Lowrick, a spokesperson for the grassroots group, Fight for Farmland.
Lowrick's mother-in-law lives inside the area the region is targeting and is being pressured to sell, he said.
About two dozen farmers from Wilmot, within the Waterloo region, drove three hours to display their displeasure and silently held up protest signs when Premier Doug Ford spoke at the opening ceremonies.
"Fight for Farmland, say no to expropriation," the signs read.
Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli has said the province is against expropriation.
Ford said he loves farmers and his party won "every single farming community" in the province in the last election.
"I just want the region to treat them fairly," Ford said of the Wilmot farmers.
Kevin Ferguson came down to the event from his farm south of Ottawa. He believes Ford's Progressive Conservative government is doing a good job, generally.
"There's a lot of common sense moves that we like," he said.
But Ferguson believes the government has one big problem.
"The biggest concern coming from a farm background is losing good farmland to build when they could have other choices," he said. "It needs to stop."
Catherine Fife, a New Democrat who represents the riding of Waterloo, said the province can show it cares about farmers by not funding the land assembly in her region.
"There has been not one public meeting, not one fiscal report to to the general public, and now there's just a finger pointing exercise between the regional government and the PC government, whereby they're blaming each other," Fife said. "But at the end of the day, it is Doug Ford who is funding it, and so he is driving this whole process."
She said the secrecy behind the project is "flat-out undemocratic."
Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie said she opposes the land assembly in Wilmot.
"There are other options," she said of where a large industrial site should go.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A protester opposed to development of farmland in Wilmot County holds a sign as he attends the International Plowing Match in Lindsay, Ont., on Tuesday.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 427 of 500

Tunnel fantasy gives politics a bad name

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tunnel-fantasy-gives-politics-bad-name/docview/3111856780/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tunnel+fantasy+gives+politics+a+bad+name&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's fantasy tunnel will never be built. Let's start there.
There will never be a 50-kilometre tunnel connecting Markham to Brampton. There will be no shorter version linking Scarborough to Etobicoke. Not with roads. Not with trains. Not at all.
Put aside the cost. (North of $60 billion according to some estimates.) Put aside the logistics. The fact is that even if the tunnel was built it wouldn't do much to solve the GTA's very real and very pressing traffic woes. It would induce more traffic, bring in more cars, cause even more gridlock.
Perhaps for Ford that's neither here nor there. His tunnel gambit, after all, was politically effective, goading his opponents into shooting down a plan, however far-fetched, to address the congestion crisis that is top of mind for so many in Toronto.
Never mind that this kind of politicking encourages people to be even more cynical about politics than they already are. Never mind that it is not a realistic proposal to ease the GTA's traffic nightmares, a far cry from congestion pricing, road tolls, major new transit investments or any number of other ideas pushed by policy wonks.
Instead, the premier is choosing easy politics rather than doing the hard work of dealing with the actual problems this province faces.
Just think of all the things Ford wasn't talking about last week when he was talking about digging a tunnel about as long as the distance from the CN Tower to St. Catharines.
He wasn't talking about long-term care, where, the Toronto Star reported last week, seniors in one Scarborough home are being warehoused next to a lobby all day.
He wasn't talking about children in government custody being shipped to hotels, Airbnbs and even office buildings because the province can't care for them properly.
He wasn't talking about parents giving up their own children with complex needs because they simply can't get the supports they require. He wasn't talking about the plan to deal with that crisis his own government commissioned three years ago and then shelved.
He wasn't talking about kids being strip-searched in provincial jails.
He wasn't talking about school test scores.
He wasn't talking about the crisis in primary care, about hallway medicine, about housing costs, or about the giant fiscal cliff colleges and universities are facing in this province as international student numbers decline.
He wasn't even really talking about improving traffic. Not in a serious way. Which is a shame because traffic requires our serious attention.
Many of us know all too well the profound frustrations of endless commutes that corrode our quality of life, harm the environment and hurt our city's competitiveness.
Of course, there are no panaceas, in the sky or underground. The real solutions are politically challenging, logistically complicated and often dull.
Few politicians want to talk about working with cities on moving to 24-hour construction scheduling, which would require municipalities and their construction partners to co-ordinate in ways that would be new to both.
Few want to talk about moving to a world with fewer cars on the road. That stuff is boring, complicated and hard work compared to a giant tunnel.
At his best, Ford is an empathetic and flexible leader in touch with the concerns of many in this province. We often disagree with him on policy. But we believe he shares the Torstar's commitment to a stronger, better and even faster Ontario.
That's why this kind of naked politicking is so disappointing.
After more than six years in power, we expect better - and we deserve better, too.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

National Briefs: Hoggard denies he raped woman

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3111855900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+Briefs%3A+Hoggard+denies+he+raped+woman&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Teen sentenced
to probation
The second teen to be sentenced in the death of a Toronto homeless man will not serve any more time in custody, but will spend close to two years under probation. The girl pleaded guilty to manslaughter earlier this year in the 2022 death of Kenneth Lee, a 59-year-old man who was living in the city's shelter system. In a hearing Tuesday, the girl - who was 13 at the time of the incident - was given credit for 15 months of pre-sentence custody and ordered to serve 21 months of probation under an Intensive Support and Supervision Program.
Hoggard denies he raped woman
Jacob Hoggard took the stand in his sexual assault trial on Tuesday, denying he raped his accuser and painting a dramatically different picture of their encounter. Hoggard told a jury that he and the complainant in the case had a consensual one-night stand in Kirkland Lake, Ont., after his then-band Hedley performed a show.
Canada
Bloc to push for pension pact
The Bloc Québécois put the squeeze on the Liberals Tuesday by calling for a debate in the House of Commons about increasing old age security payments for all seniors. Bloc Leader Yves-François Blanchet has given the government until Oct. 29 to green-light his party's pension bill, which is estimated to cost about $16 billion over five years.
News services

Document 429 of 500

Protesting farmers greet Doug Ford at International Plowing Match

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/protesting-farmers-greet-doug-ford-at/docview/3112184888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protesting+farmers+greet+Doug+Ford+at+International+Plowing+Match&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LINDSAY, ONT. - A group of farmers came to the plowing match here hoping to get Premier Doug Ford to start paying a little less attention to tunnelling under Highway 401 and a little more to what's going on above ground.
With about 770 acres of farmland in the Waterloo area under threat of expropriation for unknown reasons, the farmers wanted to gain the support of other farmers as well as politicians on Tuesday at the annual event.
"We've come from Wilmot to put our voices forward, to help people understand what the issues are with the land acquisition that's proposed," said Alfred Lowrick, who lives beside the properties the region wants to obtain, and whose mother-in-law received an expropriation notice.
While the region is the expropriating authority, there's confusion and secrecy around the province's role and what it is funding, he added, "so there's a bit of a quandary of who we should be talking to and what we should be addressing."
The region has acquired one of seven properties identified for future, unspecified industrial use "and the rest of the farmers don't want to sell. They're not interested in doing this, and the community isn't," he added, referring to support for a local lawn-sign campaign.
The Wilmot farmers were handing out stickers in support of their cause to those who attended opening day of the 105th annual International Plowing Match and Rural Expo, held this year at the Lindsay Fairgrounds.
Every fall, it is a major weeklong event for rural Ontario - one that draws cabinet ministers, MPPs and the party leaders, who take part in a plowing contest.
Asked about the Wilmot issue by reporters, Ford said, "I just want the region to treat them fairly."
But Waterloo MPP Catherine Fife, a New Democrat, said "there has not been one public meeting, not one fiscal report to the general public and now there's just a finger-pointing exercise between the regional government and the PC government - they're just blaming each other."
Fife charged that the provincial government is "driving the whole process," and said the public deserves answers.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, taking part in her first plowing match as head of the provincial party, said the Wilmot farmers "know that we've been with them."
Crombie said farmers also have the same concerns as the rest of Ontario's residents - such as trying to find a family doctor or keeping the emergency room open at their local hospital.
Ford also told reporters that while there are a lot of opinions about tunnelling under Highway 401, a proposal he announced last week, "we're just doing a feasibility study. Anyone that's a driver on the 401 on a daily basis knows we're just jammed."
The congestion "takes $11 billion a year out of our economy - not to mention all the time you can either spend at home with your family, have some fun in the sun or whatever you want to do."
Lowrick said that while tunnelling would cost billions of dollars for "a nice little hole they want to drill" to address heavy traffic in the Greater Toronto Area, the Ford government is "missing the boat" by not also addressing the concerns of farmers.
CAPTION: Ontario politicians descended upon Lindsay, Ont., as part of their annual pilgrimage to the International Plowing Match. Several of them raised concerns about the land assembly in Waterloo region, which is buying up prime farmland and threatening expropriation in order to build a supersite for a potential massive manufacturing plant. (Oct. 1, 2024 / The Canadian Press)A protester opposed to development of farmland in Wilmot County holds a sign as Premier Doug Ford takes part in the parade at the International Plowing Match in Lindsay, Ont., on Oct. 1, 2024.

Document 430 of 500

Casino competition: Are more operators of gambling facilities coming to Niagara Falls?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/casino-competition-are-more-operators-gambling/docview/3112184778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Casino+competition%3A+Are+more+operators+of+gambling+facilities+coming+to+Niagara+Falls%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It appears Niagara Falls' call to open the city's casino market up to competition has caught the attention of Queen's Park.
"We've been talking about this through multiple governments, multiple ministers," said Mayor Jim Diodati. "The good news is, finally, someone's listening.
"We've got 10 studies over 15 years requesting that we open up gaming and make it a competitive environment - not a monopoly. We even sued the OLG (Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corp.) a number of years ago over the last decision to not open it up. The outcome, we ended up getting a $130-million theatre. We were upset because monopolies don't serve the public - you need competition, it keeps everybody sharp."
Currently, Mohegan operates as the service provider for Niagara Fallsview Casino Resort and Casino Niagara on OLG's behalf.
That deal, signed six years ago, expires in 2040. Prior to that, the two casinos were operated by OLG, which manages lotteries and casinos in Ontario.
Since the mid-1990s, OLG has provided more than $200 million to the City of Niagara Falls for hosting two casinos.
An article last week, by online news publication The Trillium focused on the latest developments at Queen's Park, reported Premier Doug Ford's government is trying to change the deal dictating how Niagara Falls casinos are run and potentially encourage more casinos to open in the city.
Based on government records and a well-placed source, The Trillium reported one of the province's intentions is to help attract more tourists to Niagara Falls and the wider region.
The publication said the source was promised anonymity because the individual wasn't permitted to discuss what, until now, have been behind-the-scenes negotiations.
Information from the source is supported by various government records received in response to freedom of information requests by The Trillium.
Calendars of several senior Ford officials indicate the existing Niagara Falls casino-operations deal has been a topic of interest within the premier's office for more than the past year and a half, the publication reported.
In May, three separate "negotiations update(s)" about a "Niagara Destination Strategy" were prepared for the deputy finance minister's office, the ministry disclosed in response to a freedom of information request by The Trillium.
A spokesperson in the office of the premier did not respond to questions emailed by the Niagara Falls Review.
Anthony Vukojevic, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Tourism, Culture and Gaming and Ministry of Sport, said Niagara's $1.8-billion tourism industry plays a "key role" in creating jobs and supporting the local economy.
"Our government is considering several initiatives under a larger destination strategy aimed at unlocking the full potential of the Niagara region," he said in an emailed statement to the Review, which didn't address specific questions.
"Niagara is a major destination for global travellers, and this strategy will elevate it to the next level, strengthening Ontario's global reputation as a top-tier tourist destination."
Niagara Casinos referred questions to OLG.
OLG spokesperson Tony Bitonti said Niagara Falls' casinos are important to the success of OLG's land-based casino business.
"To respect commercial confidentiality, OLG does not comment on any discussions with any of its casino service providers," he said in an emailed statement.
But Diodati said he's happy the province is looking at the current model in Niagara Falls.
"We've got multiple, unanimous resolutions over several successive councils requesting that we be the gaming capital of Canada and to utilize gaming to leverage tourism and to bring in competition," he said.
"We're saying this model doesn't work. Gaming is dying in Ontario. It's getting old, it's getting stale and it's going to become irrelevant.
"We've got some good opportunities if we do this right. We've got great insight from some gaming consultants from around the world who have made suggestions on there's ways to do it better and that's what we're suggesting to the government."
And it all starts with a competitive environment, said Diodati.
"We've got two casinos, but they're operated by the same group, so it's not competition. Even in the past, we said when you re-tender the casino operation, have the two casinos separated and competing with two different operators. Finally, after all these years of multiple resolutions, multiple discussions, we've finally got a government that's willing to listen to what we're asking for and to investigate it."
He said his comments are "no slight" to Mohegan officials.
"They're wonderful people and they're nice operators and we have a great relationship with them. This pre-dates Mohegan, this goes long before they ever came to the table," said Diodati.
Asked if he supports more casinos in Niagara Falls, Diodati said that would depend on what market studies suggest.
"The most important thing above all is competition," he said, adding the gaming world has changed since Casino Niagara and Niagara Fallsview Casino Resort opened in 1996 and 2004, respectively.
"There's a lot more competition out there than in the beginning. Fallsview Casino was the most successful casino in North America at the time. Casino revenues are significantly declining in Ontario. Online gaming is another factor that wasn't present when they first contemplated gaming."
Diodati said there should be a "completely new model and new regulations."
"With competition, that incentivizes the casino operator to reinvest in the product, because if you don't the product gets old. Our gem, the jewel in our gaming crown, is Fallsview Casino and it's almost 20 years old already. Casino Niagara is going on 30 years," he said.
"Yes, you can paint it and fix it up, but when you go to Las Vegas, you see what they do and it's a significantly different model."
Diodati said he has spoken with "some of the major operations" in Las Vegas.
"They've made it quite clear that you guys need a compete model change if you want to attract the major brands of the world into your community," he said.
"Along with that comes boutique hotels where they'll build five-star, along with that will come more entertainment options. Back in the day you went to Vegas to gamble - today gambling is not the top reason why you go. You go for the entertainment, you go for the great culinary experiences, you go for the sporting events."
While the city has seen hotel applications come before council, Diodati said Niagara Falls hasn't had a new hotel built in 17 years.
"Our inventory is getting stagnant and it's getting stale, so we could use some upgrades and some additions," he said, pointing to how opening gaming up to competition has benefited other jurisdictions around the world.
"Nashville has built more hotels in the last 10 years than we have ever. We've got the falls, one of the great natural wonders of the world - no excuse for our tourism to stagnate."
Diodati said Niagara Falls is missing the "nicer, high-end boutique" hotel market.
"Those kinds of people end up staying in Toronto and day trip to Niagara Falls. We want them to stay here and then day trip to Toronto."
Diodati said more competition will help grow the market, so the "high tide raises all boats."
"The pizza guy will make better money, the chambermaids will get more money, the T-shirt people will make more money, the operators, the hotels, the attractions - everybody benefits," he said.
"We need more offerings on that buffet of fun and excitement. We need competition and we'll bring the traffic from farther away, we'll get more investment in the area, create more jobs and better jobs."
With files from John Law
CAPTION: Casino Niagara opened in 1996.Niagara Fallsview Casino Resort was the most successful casino in North America when it first opened in 2004, says Mayor Jim Diodati. But since then, casino revenues are "significantly" declining in Ontario with more competition, including online gaming, being a factor, he adds.
