Document 431 of 500

Ford's 'get a job' comment achieves nothing in solving the homelessness crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-get-job-comment-achieves-nothing-solving/docview/3111594751/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+%27get+a+job%27+comment+achieves+nothing+in+solving+the+homelessness+crisis&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thanksgiving seems to have come early this year.
By telling homeless people to get off their backsides and find a job, Doug Ford has been impersonating the crazy right-wing uncle that seems to appear at every holiday gathering. All right, Ford used a less polite term than "backside," but you get the point - this is a "G-rated" column.
I was Ontario's minister of children, community and social services, and Ford's comments are familiar. As the minister in charge of social assistance, I often heard from Ontarians advocating a "get-a-job" solution to homelessness.
I never dismissed them out of hand. It was their tax dollars funding social assistance, and you would be naïve to believe that nobody ever games the system. But I also noticed that these armchair critics refused to recognize that many of those living in homelessness have mental health and addiction issues, are fleeing abuse or are victims of trauma. Others simply can't find an affordable place to live.
There are homeless people who are working but don't have enough to pay rent. For others, the distance between being job-ready and staying in a shelter, living in a tent or sleeping rough is often too far. Telling them to "get a job" achieves nothing.
Ford must know this. After years in government, he understands that major problems don't have simple solutions - which is why we tend to ignore our crazy uncles.
Nevertheless, there is one point that Ford and I probably agree on: the current system isn't working.
Asking people to exhaust their savings and sign up for a monthly stipend so small that most of us couldn't survive on it for a week - let alone a month - is not a winning formula. Despite the booming economy, the number of Ontarians on social assistance has increased, homelessness is up and the number of homeless encampments across the province is skyrocketing.
We need to rethink the entire system.
I was associated with a government that did that. It piloted a basic income program where a group living in poverty were given an allowance large enough to lead more stable, healthier lives and pursue further education or employment.
Although I had left politics at that point, I supported the pilot, not because I was necessarily convinced that basic income was a magic solution, but because it represented a new approach that was going to be tested and studied.
Despite promising not to touch the pilot, Ford's government cancelled it, and now we await his solution.
Maybe the premier's most recent comments contain the seeds of one. During one rant, he promised that he would take care of those with mental health and addiction issues or those with disabilities. He also praised Hamilton's "tiny homes" initiative that provides supportive housing for the homeless.
So Premier, if it is all about finding a job, then live up to these comments. Invest heavily in mental health and addiction services and create thousands of supportive housing units, all with the intention of stabilizing the lives of the homeless. Work with potential employers and develop comprehensive training programs that lead to jobs. Add some tough love if you want - it's your program.
It will be costly and take time, but it would represent something better than a status quo that isn't working. However, until he's ready to take this step, I would advise the premier to stop the crazy uncle routine.
CAPTION: Despite the booming economy, the number of Ontarians on social assistance has increased, homelessness is up and the number of homeless encampments across the province is skyrocketing. We need to rethink the entire system, John Milloy writes.
CREDIT: John Milloy

Subject: Homeless encampments; Mental health; Addictions; Homeless people; Basic income

Business indexing term: Subject: Basic income

Document 432 of 500

Oct. 1: Urban green space underutilized, say no to looming disaster and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oct-1-urban-green-space-underutilized-say-no/docview/3111594723/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oct.+1%3A+Urban+green+space+underutilized%2C+say+no+to+looming+disaster+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Urban green space underutilized
Re: Hamilton golf courses busier than ever - but should city make room for nongolfers? Sept. 27
Converting at least one of Hamilton's three golf courses to other uses should be a no-brainer.
Consider the numbers - last year, 90,000 rounds of golf were played, 30,000 per course. Assuming there were 200 playing days in the year, that's about 150 golfers a day per course. Each course occupies nearly 150 acres, so on average, roughly an acre of publicly-owned green space is being reserved for a single golfer's use. Is that what we want?
Harry Shannon, Dundas
Is the choice golf or tents?
Perhaps we should consider converting all parks to golf courses. A few could be nine- or 18-hole full-size courses, but par threes and mini putt could be accommodated. The choice seems to be golf or tents. Which would the area property owners prefer?
There was a time when proximity to a park was considered a plus in real estate. That time is gone! Could a future house sales advertisement state "cannot see a park from this property."
I have not played golf in many years and probably never will again, so do not have a side in this.
Craig Thompson, Hamilton
City's golf courses aren't broken
Apparently Toronto and Burlington councils see the value of preserving public golf courses in their communities. The Hamilton inner city courses are green spaces available to the public as well. One city council member wishes to transform our courses into more natural spaces. The courses have a 100-year history of welcoming deer, geese, foxes, eagles, turkey vultures, dog walkers, trail walkers and stair climbers.
My fear is that the land will fall into disrepair and become another tent city with accompanying neighbourhood impacts. The golf courses are not broken, so please don't try to fix them, city hall.
Pat Lynch, Stoney Creek
Leave golf courses alone
This article is a further case of city hall ineptitude. Why would they consider destroying the golf courses which are enjoyed by many? Is it room for more tents they seek? Clean up the existing parks so they can be used as intended. Concentrate your energies on solving the homeless issues instead.
Garry Young, Canfield
Curtail spending before hiking taxes
Re: Hamilton's tax-hike shell game: Here we go again, Sept. 26
As city council prepares us for a new round of tax increases, let's look at the wasteful spending this council has enacted over their first two years.
Mayor Andrea Horwath set the tone with her lavish $38,000 inauguration when the previous council did the same for $11,000; $20,000 to hire our own city poet; $597,000 for a safety bollard for the forecourt of city hall that did nothing to prevent tenters from their recent protest; $1.6 million split among 15 councillors to hire more staff; $750,000 for e-bikes that will have absolutely no benefit to our air quality; $60 million for more bike lanes when both of our east-west (Cannon and Stinson) routes are already underused and they are putting another one on Main Street; after five years and three different sponsors the previous council had to bail out the bike share program with a $200,000 grant; and $16 million to convert Main Street to two-way, which will turn it into a pollution-spewing parking lot.
We have always been the envy of Torontonians on how easily we could transverse our city and now we are needlessly about to experience their traffic congestion. Council can do better.
Tom Winning, Hamilton
Say no to looming disaster
I see, with no small feeling of dismay, that my property taxes are going to increase yet again. As upset as I am, however, regarding the increase, I am terrified more by the spectre of the not-needed and unwanted LRT and how it will add a crushing, unsustainable cost to us all.
The anticipated cost has already tripled, and the actual construction start date is yet to be determined. A year from now? Really!
I read a Spectator article some time ago that the anticipated operating costs to the city, we taxpayers, will be $15 million to $20 million annually. In that the original construction costs have tripled, it is a sure bet that the operating costs will do the same. Do the powers that be want to add another $40 million to our annual tax bill? Surely not!
Please, I beg of our politicians, have none of you the moral courage to say a resounding no to this looming disaster!
John D. Wilson, Stoney Creek
Move security guards' shifts
Re: Security guards now patrol Hamilton escarpment stairs, Sept. 12
Patrolling the escarpment stairs to keep people feeling safer is a great idea. What I don't understand is the hours that the security guards are stationed there: 5 p.m. to 1 a.m. Eight hours. Instead of that, how about two four-hour shifts, 5 to 9 a.m. and 5 to 9 p.m. Those times pretty much cover the "before work" and "after work" exercisers and anyone using the stairs as a way to get to the mountain or down to the lower city.
It is these times when assaults are more likely to happen. With the cutback of vegetation, improved lighting and cameras, there isn't much more that can be done.
Deb Whalen, Simcoe
Feeling let down by network
Re: CTV says staffers 'no longer' work for news team after manipulated Pierre Poilievre clip, Sept. 27
I have been a loyal CTV News viewer for decades. I know what an agenda-driven and slanted view we are subjected to with CBC. I always trusted CTV to give balanced and mostly impartial reporting until that manipulated sound bite of Pierre Poilievre. Can I trust CTV ever again?
Mike Davy, Hamilton
Ford, Jones living in fantasy world
Re: Arbitrator awards Ontario doctors 10% increase in 1st year of new deal, Sept. 13
An arbitrator has awarded Ontario doctors a 10-per-cent compensation increase for the first year of their new agreement. The government suggested three per cent, which the arbitrator thought was completely unrealistic.
The Ministry of Health, in its argument, wrote that recruitment and retention of doctors was "not a major concern" and there was no concern of a diminished supply of physicians. Doug Ford and Sylvia Jones, if you do not realize 500,000 to two million people in Ontario (from your own documents) do not have any access to a family doctor, you are living in a fantasy world.
Relatives in Peterborough say the majority of people there are unable to access a family doctor, even though they have severe medical problems requiring regular followups. This causes constant stress to the families, but total disinterest from our minister of health. When is our Conservative government going to prioritize health care for all instead of funding highways, beer sales, etc. instead of doctors and hospitals? None of these government ventures will improve population health, but definitely worsen it for the future.
Shirley Jean Smith, Hamilton
Government ads are unnecessary
As a regular radio-listener, I have the privilege of hearing advertisements with "paid for by the Government of Ontario" repeatedly. Every time I hear one of these promotions, extolling the Ontario government, I can't help but think "is this the best use of our taxpayer dollars?"
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Local Canadian Tire will be missed
Re: Canadian Tire pitching redevelopment of its downtown Hamilton store, Sept. 17
As someone who used to live close to this Canadian Tire, it is sad to see the city once again considering prioritizing unaffordable housing over the needs of residents.
It would be better if the store was kept and some affordable housing built above it. That way, residents who frequent the store could continue to go and we'd get much-needed housing for the many people who currently lack it. Plus, those residents could frequent the store. It is a convenient place to get batteries, your keys cut and some food. For many who don't drive, it's close and convenient when you just need one thing, rather than going a few more blocks to No Frills. Keeping the store, and building affordable housing as well, is the way to go.
Aha Blume, Hamilton
Don't deny Canadians their rights
Re: Are 'busloads' of homeless travelling to Hamilton? Sept. 15
I would like to congratulate Joseph Mancinelli of the Labourers' International Union of North America for his hard work over many years to get decent wages for his members. I would also like to congratulate him for investing his members' pension money on much-needed housing. Mr. Mancinelli, you have achieved your goals. You can now use your power and influence to pick on the less fortunate of society: the poor and homeless.
Your workers are mostly now qualified members of the "not-in-my-backyard" middle class who can comfortably ignore the problems of homelessness and poverty.
By the way, as far as I know, all citizens of Canada have complete freedom to move or travel to whichever city they chose. This is not Communist China, where everyone must have a household registry card and thousands of construction labourers must live illegally in appalling dormitories while building modern cities.
Good luck in keeping poor people away from your privileged members.
John Roy, Hamilton
Ford comment on homeless was out of touch
Re: 'Get off your a-s-s:' Doug Ford scolded for telling homeless people to get jobs, Sept. 24
Premier Doug Ford's recent comments about people experiencing homelessness are disgraceful. He is implying that those who are not employed do not have jobs due to lack of effort.
How sad that our premier adds to the stigma surrounding homelessness. Encampments are not a new phenomenon and are the result of a system that has failed to take care of some of our most vulnerable persons.
Housing is a fundamental determinant of health and a long recognized human right enshrined in Canadian law. The housing crisis significantly undermines individual health and well-being. It also places a huge burden on our health system. People who are unhoused often experience barriers to health care, forcing them to rely on emergency services for care and even for shelter, which causes unnecessary strain on the system.
Rather than looking down on people experiencing homelessness, Ford should be providing more affordable and accessible housing options as well as social services to help them find work and live dignified lives in safe and healthy environments. Ford should make it his duty to take care of all Ontarians, including those living in encampments.
Doris Grinspun, CEO of Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO)
Move writer out of 'women's section'
Re: Let's Hope Susan B. Anthony Doesn't Turn Over in Her Grave on Tuesday, Nov. 5, Sept. 28
Anne Bokma has written another thoughtful, discerning, and illuminating piece, this time sharing the history of Susan B. Anthony's role in achieving the vote for American women and its continued relevance and resonance. Bokma's Saturday essays frequently speak to the undervaluing of women's contributions, and their historic marginalization in a variety of political, economic, and social sectors and settings. Her reflections and analyses provide valuable insights into women's varied experiences and the importance of their place within the world.
How ironic, then, that the Spectator continues to relegate Bokma to the arts and life section, once called the "women's section." Her pieces are implicitly political, and should be featured on the opinion page, not banished to a section that focuses on entertainment, food and fashion.
Dayna Firth, Hamilton
Canada needs to own its history
Re: Six Nations says Crown could owe trillions over mismanagement of Grand River lands, Sept. 27
I'm a proud second-generation Canadian of British/Ukrainian descent. I read about the drawn-out Six Nations lawsuit with provincial and federal governments over disputed land claims. This needs to be investigated and resolved if we want to believe that Canada is a fair and equitable country. If guilty, who is responsible is an open question, because most of this happened before Confederation. Perhaps Great Britain? Perhaps this goes to the international court in The Hague? One way or another, and like so many things in respect to the treatment of First Nations, this one shouldn't get swept under the rug.
I also read about the fight over the difficulty and frustration clouding the finding of the truth with respect to the history and suspected abuses with the performance of residential schools. Although not uniform, Canada and the provinces started requiring death certificates in the 1880s. Regarding the suspected unknown graves of children at these schools, especially deaths under suspicious circumstances, where are these documents? That question needs to be answered; apologies won't cut it. If these turn out to be the unmarked graves of children in the care of residential schools, the same process of investigation and if proved, justified punishment should be applied, just as in the land claims disputes. Stop hiding our history and start to own it, whether it is black or white. This comment is about truth and reconciliation; both are necessary.
George Nauylor, Caledonia
CAPTION: The city has security guards patrolling Hamilton's escarpment staircases to improve safety. But one reader wonders if the timing of their shifts should be modified.A debate is coming over whether to repurpose parts of city-owned golf courses like Chedoke for new uses, like passive parkland.

Subject: Construction costs; Guards; Tents; Parks & recreation areas; Councils; Affordable housing; Homeless people; Operating costs; Tax increases

Document 433 of 500

American voters set for a 'weird' debate: VP hopefuls face off in razor-thin race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/american-voters-set-weird-debate/docview/3111421868/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=American+voters+set+for+a+%27weird%27+debate%3A+VP+hopefuls+face+off+in+razor-thin+race&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tim Walz and JD Vance are set to face off Tuesday in the only debate for the vice-presidential hopefuls during the razor-thin race to November's election.
Walz, the Democratic governor of Minnesota, and Vance, the Republican senator from Ohio, are expected to play the political attack dog.
Todd Graham, a professor of debate at Southern Illinois University, said Walz has one main job: to keep saying Vance is too weird for the White House.
"Vance is going to say, basically, 'I'm not weird and I can be trusted to be president,' " he said.
That may seem like a strange prediction for the focus of a vice-presidential debate, but Walz made it to national prominence by labelling rival Republicans as "weird."
That has become the most successful attack for Democrats after a tumultuous summer that began with a disastrous debate between Donald Trump and Joe Biden and the president taking himself out of the race.
It was also a contributing factor for Vice-President Kamala Harris to bring Walz onto the reinvigorated Democratic ticket in August.
The attack has particularly focused on Vance for his comments on abortion, on "childless cat ladies" and his previous suggestions that political leaders who didn't have biological children "don't really have a direct stake" in the country.
Online memes and videos have targeted those remarks, as well as Vance's interactions with voters, including a viral encounter at a doughnut shop.
But he has also shown he can circumvent criticism and has doubled down on controversial comments.
He stood by a false claim that Haitian immigrants in Ohio were eating pets, something that Trump repeated during the presidential debate earlier this month.
He could prove to be a fierce opponent for Walz, said Aaron Kall, the director of debate for the University of Michigan.
"They can both deliver sharp barbs and give exchanges, but they can do it with a smile," Kall said.
The 40-year-old Vance rose to fame with the 2016 publication of his memoir, "Hillbilly Elegy." Formerly a Trump critic, he was elected to the U.S. Senate in 2022 after becoming one of the former president's loudest supporters.
He was chosen to connect to white working-class voters who are important for Trump's chances in Wisconsin, Pennsylvania and Michigan.
Those states swung Republican when Trump won in 2016, and in 2020 they helped put Biden in the White House.
Democrats are hoping that 60-year-old Walz's "Minnesota nice" demeanour, his history as a football coach and plain-spoken delivery will connect with those same crucial voters.
Graham said both will have three tasks in the debate: defend the top of their ticket, attack the top of the other ticket and prove they are capable of being the president if they had to step in.
While Trump was victorious in June's debate against Biden, political experts have said Harris dominated when the two squared off.
Both vice-presidential picks have a Canadian connection. Walz's state shares an 885-kilometre border with Ontario and Manitoba. Not too long ago he tossed around a football with Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
Meanwhile, Vance went to university with Jamil Jivani, the Conservative MP for Durham. Jivani has called Vance his best friend from Yale and performed a Bible reading at the American politician's wedding.
But both parties have pitched policies experts have called protectionist. Trump has repeated plans for a 10 per cent tariff on imports and Vance is a vocal opponent of U.S. military aid to Ukraine to fend off Russia's invasion.
Last week, Harris highlighted that she was one of 10 U.S. senators to vote against the Canada-United States-Mexico agreement under Trump, saying it wasn't sufficient to protect American workers. If she becomes president, Harris said, she will push the trade pact's review in 2026.
Matthew Lebo, a specialist in U.S. politics at Western University in London, Ont., said Canadians watching Tuesday's debate are unlikely to glean much detail about those trade policies. Walz and Vance will talk about personal economic issues and inflation to connect with American voters.
"In an election that is so close, the VP can reflect on the judgment of the presidential pick," Lebo said.
With files from The Associated Press
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The entrance to the CBS Broadcast Center undergoes repairs the day before the television network will host the vice-presidential debate in New York City.Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, left, and Ohio Sen. J.D. Vance. Both vice- presidential picks have a Canadian connection.Chip Somodevilla Getty Images

Document 434 of 500

Inaction is harming Ontario's vulnerable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inaction-is-harming-ontarios-vulnerable/docview/3111421796/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inaction+is+harming+Ontario%27s+vulnerable&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Oct. 1 could have been a celebratory day for Ontarians concerned with the struggles of low-income residents. After all, it's the day the minimum wage will nudge up - following behind inflation, sure - but up nonetheless.
But if you're living on Ontario Works, or what used to be called welfare, the date will carry a more bitter significance. It will have been a full six years since any increase in the amounts for basic needs and shelter.
In that time, inflation has worked its ugly powers and the price of everything has gone up. There has been a 20 per cent hike in the cost of living for the typical Ontario family, hitting the basics like food and housing especially hard.
If you happen to be surviving on an Ontario Works (OW) income, you've felt that sting deeper than most. The cost of living at the provincial poverty line has gone up 22 per cent. This isn't just inflation doing its usual damage. No, the source of the pain runs deeper, rooted in a 1995 decision when Mike Harris slashed welfare by 22 per cent. Now Doug Ford has done the same, just more slowly and quietly. No need for a flashy cut when the grind of inaction does the job for you.
By freezing Ontario Works at its 2018 levels, Ford's government has delivered a stealthier, yet no less painful blow. A single recipient in Toronto is now $435 further below the poverty line than they were six years ago.
You don't need to squint to see the impact: Food insecurity has shot up and food bank usage is skyrocketing. Homelessness and pressures on an overburdened shelter system continue to mount. Since 2018, social assistance indexation - increasing OW benefits to increase along with price inflation - would have meant a combined basic needs and shelter allowance of $882 a month. This would be $149 more than the current amounts - money that would surely make a big difference in the lives of many of the poorest people in the province.
The real kicker is that this didn't need to happen. Before Ford, the Liberal governments never found the resolve to restore the original cuts to OW, but they did tie increases to inflation, the kind of basic economic policy that, like progressive income taxation, everyone agrees on.
The province, however, has ignored Ontario Works, while continuing to index many other things such as child benefits, income taxes and the minimum wage, for example. It also increased Ontario Disability Support Program amounts in 2022 and commenced indexing in 2023. The Guaranteed Annual Income System - a program aimed at low-income seniors - started indexing in 2023. These are positive moves that ensure that some of the poorest didn't slide backwards with every price hike.
Yet here we are with OW recipients suffering under a rising cost of living while the government watches from a distance.
As if that weren't enough, the rules around working while on Ontario Works haven't changed either. You can earn up to $200 a month before the benefits start getting clawed back. But that $200 has been frozen since 2013. Back then, that threshold got you nearly 20 hours of work in a month at minimum wage. After Oct. 1, one can only work 12 hours before exceeding the limit. If that amount had risen with the times, it would be $332 now - a change that would let people work more and live better without losing benefits. But again, nothing moves, nothing adjusts, and the so-called incentives to work become more inadequate every year.
What we're left with is a situation in which life is harder for the poorest citizens as the province cuts their safety net inch by inch.
The Ford government can still change course. The province has recently come around to indexation for low-income seniors and people with disabilities. It is time to extend this good policy to Ontario Works recipients and give those struggling Ontarians a fighting chance.
Brian Lewis is a Senior Fellow with the CD Howe Institute
and Munk School for Global Affairs and Public Policy.
He is the former provincial chief economist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Brian Lewis Brian Lewis is a Senior Fellow with the CD Howe Institute and Munk School for Global Affairs and Public Policy. He is the former provincial chief economist.;

Document 435 of 500

Ford's 'get a job' comment achieves nothing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-get-job-comment-achieves-nothing/docview/3111421774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+%27get+a+job%27+comment+achieves+nothing&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thanksgiving seems to have come early this year.
By telling homeless people to get off their backsides and find a job, Doug Ford has been impersonating the crazy right-wing uncle that seems to appear at every holiday gathering. All right, Ford used a less polite term than "backside," but you get the point - this is a "G-rated" column.
I was Ontario's minister of children, community and social services, and Ford's comments are familiar. As the minister in charge of social assistance, I often heard from Ontarians advocating a "get-a-job" solution to homelessness.
I never dismissed them out of hand. It was their tax dollars funding social assistance, and you would be naïve to believe that nobody ever games the system. But I also noticed that these armchair critics refused to recognize that many of those living in homelessness have mental health and addiction issues, are fleeing abuse or are victims of trauma. Others simply can't find an affordable place to live.
There are homeless people who are working but don't have enough to pay rent. For others, the distance between being job-ready and staying in a shelter, living in a tent or sleeping rough is often too far. Telling them to "get a job" achieves nothing.
Ford must know this. After years in government, he understands that major problems don't have simple solutions - which is why we tend to ignore our crazy uncles.
Nevertheless, there is one point that Ford and I probably agree on: the current system isn't working.
Asking people to exhaust their savings and sign up for a monthly stipend so small that most of us couldn't survive on it for a week - let alone a month - is not a winning formula. Despite the booming economy, the number of Ontarians on social assistance has increased, homelessness is up and the number of homeless encampments across the province is skyrocketing.
We need to rethink the entire system.
I was associated with a government that did that. It piloted a basic income program where a group living in poverty were given an allowance large enough to lead more stable, healthier lives and pursue further education or employment.
Although I had left politics at that point, I supported the pilot, not because I was necessarily convinced that basic income was a magic solution, but because it represented a new approach that was going to be tested and studied.
Despite promising not to touch the pilot, Ford's government cancelled it, and now we await his solution.
Maybe the premier's most recent comments contain the seeds of one. During one rant, he promised that he would take care of those with mental health and addiction issues or those with disabilities. He also praised Hamilton's "tiny homes" initiative that provides supportive housing for the homeless.
So Premier, if it is all about finding a job, then live up to these comments. Invest heavily in mental health and addiction services and create thousands of supportive housing units, all with the intention of stabilizing the lives of the homeless. Work with potential employers and develop comprehensive training programs that lead to jobs. Add some tough love if you want - it's your program.
It will be costly and take time, but it would represent something better than a status quo that isn't working. However, until he's ready to take this step, I would advise the premier to stop the crazy uncle routine.
John Milloy, a former Liberal MPP and cabinet minister, is the director of the Centre for Public Ethics at Martin Luther University College.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: John Milloy John Milloy, a former Liberal MPP and cabinet minister, is the director of the Centre for Public Ethics at Martin Luther University College.;

Subject: Mental health; Addictions; Homeless people; Basic income

Business indexing term: Subject: Basic income

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3111421770/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Green space
underutilized
Hamilton golf courses busier than ever - but should city make room for nongolfers? Sept. 27
Converting at least one of Hamilton's three golf courses to other uses should be a no-brainer
Consider the numbers - last year, 90,000 rounds of golf were played, 30,000 per course. Assuming there were 200 playing days in the year, that's about 150 golfers a day per course. Each course occupies nearly 150 acres, so on average, roughly an acre of publicly owned green space is being reserved for a single golfer's use. Is that what we want?
Harry Shannon, Dundas
Is the choice
golf or tents?
Perhaps we should consider converting all parks to golf courses. A few could be nine- or 18-hole full-size courses, but par threes and mini putt could be accommodated. The choice seems to be golf or tents. Which would the area property owners prefer?
There was a time when proximity to a park was considered a plus in real estate. That time is gone! Could a future house sales advertisement state "cannot see a park from this property."
I have not played golf in many years and probably never will again, so I do not have a side in this.
Craig Thompson, Hamilton
City's golf courses aren't broken
Apparently Toronto and Burlington councils see the value of preserving public golf courses in their communities.
The Hamilton inner city courses are green spaces available to the public as well. One city council member wishes to transform our courses into more natural spaces. The courses have a 100-year history of welcoming deer, geese, foxes, eagles, turkey vultures, dog walkers, trail walkers and stair climbers.
My fear is that the land will fall into disrepair and become another tent city with accompanying neighbourhood impacts. The golf courses are not broken, so please don't try to fix them, city hall.
Pat Lynch, Stoney Creek
Leave our golf courses alone
This article is a further case of city hall ineptitude. Why would they consider destroying the golf courses which are enjoyed by many? Is it room for more tents they seek? Clean up the existing parks so they can be used as intended. Concentrate your energies on solving the homeless issues instead.
Garry Young, Canfield
Don't hike taxes, curtail spending
Re: Hamilton's tax-hike
shell game: Here we go again, Sept. 26
As city council prepares us for a new round of tax increases, let's look at the wasteful spending this council has enacted over its first two years.
Mayor Andrea Horwath set the tone with her lavish $38,000 inauguration when the previous council did the same for $11,000; $20,000 to hire our own city poet; $597,000 for a safety bollard for the forecourt of city hall that did nothing to prevent tenters from their recent protest; $1.6 million split among 15 councillors to hire more staff; $750,000 for e-bikes that will have absolutely no benefit to our air quality; $60 million for more bike lanes when both of our east-west (Cannon and Stinson) routes are already underused and they are putting another one on Main Street; after five years and three different sponsors the previous council had to bail out the bike share program with a $200,000 grant; and $16 million to convert Main Street to two-way, which will turn it into a pollution-spewing parking lot.
We have always been the envy of Torontonians on how easily we could transverse our city and now we are needlessly about to experience their traffic congestion. Council can do better.
Tom Winning, Hamilton
Say no to this looming disaster
I see, with no small feeling of dismay, that my property taxes are going to increase yet again. As upset as I am, however, regarding the increase, I am terrified more by the spectre of the not-needed and unwanted LRT and how it will add a crushing, unsustainable cost to us all.
The anticipated cost has already tripled, and the actual construction start date is yet to be determined. A year from now? Really!
I read a Spectator article some time ago that the anticipated operating costs to the city, we taxpayers, will be $15 million to $20 million annually. In that the original construction costs have tripled, it is a sure bet that the operating costs will do the same. Do the powers that be want to add another $40 million to our annual tax bill? Surely not!
Please, I beg of our politicians, have none of you the moral courage to say a resounding no to this looming disaster!
John D. Wilson, Stoney Creek
Move security guards' shifts
Re: Security guards now patrol Hamilton escarpment stairs, Sept. 12
Patrolling the escarpment stairs to keep people feeling safer is a great idea. What I don't understand is the hours that the security guards are stationed there: 5 p.m. to 1 a.m. Eight hours. Instead of that, how about two four-hour shifts, 5 to 9 a.m. and 5 to 9 p.m. Those times pretty much cover the "before work" and "after work" exercisers and anyone using the stairs as a way to get to the mountain or down to the lower city.
It is these times when assaults are more likely to happen. With the cutback of vegetation, improved lighting and cameras, there isn't much more that can be done.
Deb Whalen, Simcoe
Feeling let down by network
Re: CTV says staffers 'no longer' work for news team after manipulated Pierre Poilievre clip, Sept. 27
I have been a loyal CTV News viewer for decades. I know what an agenda-driven and slanted view we are subjected to with CBC.
I always trusted CTV to give balanced and mostly impartial reporting until that manipulated sound bite of Pierre Poilievre. Can I trust CTV ever again?
Mike Davy, Hamilton
Government ads are unnecessary
As a regular radio-listener, I have the privilege of hearing advertisements with "paid for by the Government of Ontario" repeatedly. Every time I hear one of these promotions, extolling the Ontario government, I can't help but think "is this the best use of our taxpayer dollars?"
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Canadian Tire will be missed
Re: Canadian Tire pitching
redevelopment of its downtown Hamilton store, Sept. 17
As someone who used to live close to this Canadian Tire, it is sad to see the city once again considering prioritizing unaffordable housing over the needs of residents.
It would be better if the store was kept and some affordable housing built above it. That way, residents who frequent the store could continue to go and we'd get much-needed housing for the many people who currently lack it.
Plus, those residents could frequent the store. It is a convenient place to get batteries, your keys cut and some food. For many who don't drive, it's close and convenient when you just need one thing, rather than going a few more blocks to No Frills. Keeping the store, and building affordable housing as well, is the way to go.
Aha Blume, Hamilton
Don't deny rights of Canadians
Re: Are 'busloads' of homeless travelling to Hamilton?
Sept. 15
I would like to congratulate Joseph Mancinelli of the Labourers' International Union of North America for his hard work over many years to get decent wages for his members.
I would also like to congratulate him for investing his members' pension money on much-needed housing. Mr. Mancinelli, you have achieved your goals. You can now use your power and influence to pick on the less fortunate of society: the poor and homeless.
Your workers are mostly now qualified members of the "not-in-my-backyard" middle class who can comfortably ignore the problems of homelessness and poverty.
By the way, as far as I know, all citizens of Canada have complete freedom to move or travel to whichever city they chose. This is not Communist China, where everyone must have a household registry card and thousands of construction labourers must live illegally in appalling dormitories while building modern cities.
Good luck in keeping poor people away from your privileged members.
John Roy, Hamilton
Ford, Jones living in fantasy world
Re: Arbitrator awards Ontario doctors 10% increase in 1st year of new deal, Sept. 13
An arbitrator has awarded Ontario doctors a 10 per cent compensation increase for the first year of their new agreement. The government suggested three per cent, which the arbitrator thought was completely unrealistic.
The Ministry of Health, in its argument, wrote that recruitment and retention of doctors was "not a major concern" and there was no concern of a diminished supply of physicians. Doug Ford and Sylvia Jones, if you do not realize 500,000 to two million people in Ontario (from your own documents) do not have any access to a family doctor, you are living in a fantasy world.
Relatives in Peterborough say the majority of people there are unable to access a family doctor, even though they have severe medical problems requiring regular followups. This causes constant stress to the families, but total disinterest from our minister of health.
When is our Progressive Conservative government going to prioritize health care for all instead of funding highways, beer sales, etc. instead of doctors and hospitals? None of these government ventures will improve population health, but definitely worsen it for the future.
Shirley Jean Smith, Hamilton
Ford comment
was out of touch
Re: 'Get off your a-s-s:'
Doug Ford scolded for telling homeless people to get jobs, Sept. 24
Premier Doug Ford's recent comments about people experiencing homelessness are disgraceful. He is implying that those who are not employed do not have jobs due to lack of effort.
How sad that our premier adds to the stigma surrounding homelessness. Encampments are not a new phenomenon and are the result of a system that has failed to take care of some of our most vulnerable persons.
Housing is a fundamental determinant of health and a long recognized human right enshrined in Canadian law. The housing crisis significantly undermines individual health and well-being. It also places a huge burden on our health system.
People who are unhoused often experience barriers to health care, forcing them to rely on emergency services for care and even for shelter, which causes unnecessary strain on the system.
Rather than looking down on people experiencing homelessness, Ford should be providing more affordable and accessible housing options as well as social services to help them find work and live dignified lives in safe and healthy environments.
Ford should make it his duty to take care of all Ontarians, including those living in encampments.
Doris Grinspun, CEO of Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO)
Move writer into opinion section
Re: Let's Hope Susan B. Anthony Doesn't Turn Over in Her Grave on Tuesday, Nov. 5, Sept. 28
Anne Bokma has written another thoughtful, discerning, and illuminating piece, this time sharing the history of Susan B. Anthony's role in achieving the vote for American women and its continued relevance and resonance.
Bokma's Saturday essays frequently speak to the undervaluing of women's contributions, and their historic marginalization in a variety of political, economic, and social sectors and settings. Her reflections and analyses provide valuable insights into women's varied experiences and the importance of their place within the world.
How ironic, then, that the Spectator continues to relegate Bokma to the arts and life section, once called the "women's section."
Her pieces are implicitly political, and should be featured on the opinion page, not banished to a section that focuses on entertainment, food and fashion.
Dayna Firth, Hamilton
CAPTION: A debate is coming over whether to repurpose parts of city-owned golf courses like Chedoke for new uses, like passive parkland.Barry Gray Hamilton Spectator file photo

Will controversial Tory congestion law throw a wrench into Hamilton's bike lane build-out?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-controversial-tory-congestion-law-throw/docview/3111188667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+controversial+Tory+congestion+law+throw+a+wrench+into+Hamilton%27s+bike+lane+build-out%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's Tory government is preparing to limit where bike lanes can be built - just months after Hamilton's council voted to double the pace of bicycle-safety construction in the city.
The planned anti-congestion legislation - first reported by the CBC but not yet officially announced by the province - would ban new bike lanes on roads where a car-traffic lane would be lost as a result.
Details on the expected legislation remain scarce, but Premier Doug Ford effectively confirmed the basics in responding to reporter questions about the issue at a recent Cobourg announcement.
"We just want to get traffic moving, that's what we want to do," he told reporters. "That's what it comes down to, making sure you aren't putting bike lanes in the middle of some of the busiest streets in the country. Put them on the secondary roads."
The apparent provincial plan has elicited support from some car drivers frustrated by slow commutes, but also outrage from cycling and road safety advocates, thousands of whom have so far signed a petition set up by Cycle Toronto.
"It does worry me," said Mark Anderson, a Hamilton biking advocate and board member with Cycle Hamilton. "Protected bike lanes make roads safer for cyclists, but also for people who use mobility devices, pedestrians walking on the sidewalk because they benefit from having that buffer ...
"If Doug Ford wants to improve congestion, he should spend more on bike lanes and transit to get cars off the road."
But will the contentious legislation actually thwart Hamilton's newly approved plan to "accelerate" construction of 119 kilometres of new or improved bike infrastructure?
It's a big question for a council that prioritized creation of "complete streets" and committed tens of millions of dollars to expand multimodal transportation options for residents while trying to reduce fatal collisions through Vision Zero road safety improvements.
Transportation planning director Brian Hollingworth said the city won't comment directly on unannounced, "draft" legislation. But he noted "many" of the bike lane projects in Hamilton's $60-million, fast-tracked bike network build-out "do not require the replacement of traffic lanes."
There are a few notable exceptions.
 Planned bike lanes on part of Grays Road call for a "road diet" taking four car lanes down to three;
 A curb lane on Mountain Brow Boulevard will be repurposed to help connect a popular multi-use path along the urban Mountain escarpment edge. Worth noting: axing that car lane is also a strategic move meant to calm traffic on a section of road Coun. Tom Jackson has said some motorists use as a "speedway;"
 Technically, the equivalent of a car lane on busy Main Street will be repurposed for cycling between Sherman and Victoria avenues (and maybe someday into Westdale) as part of a larger two-way traffic conversion through the core.
Those planned protected bike lanes will take up former car-traffic space on what was once one of the widest one-way arterial roads in Canada. But the city has already strategically blocked vehicle access to one of the original lanes of traffic via bump-outs and parking along much of the collision-prone street.
The pending legislation looks like another example of provincial politicians "undermining" local decision-making without any effort to understand local issues, suggested Anderson, who pointed to the Tory government's now-infamous flip-flop on Hamilton's Greenbelt lands and urban boundaries.
Coun. Maureen Wilson also called on Ford to "stay in your lane."
"Let me say this to the premier ... stop playing wedge politics," she said via email. "Instead, support cities in their efforts to be healthy, sustainable, resilient and economically prosperous."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Hamilton plans to double the amount of construction annually to add new or improved bike lanes and multi-use pathways. That includes more than 80 kilometres of pathways separated from traffic (dotted lines).Hamilton has vowed to add more protected bike lanes on busy streets - if proposed provincial rule changes don't throw a wrench in its plans.

Subject: Traffic flow; Legislation; Roads & highways; Traffic control; Bicycling

Title: Will controversial Tory congestion law throw a wrench into Hamilton's bike lane build-out?

Document 438 of 500

After 40 years at the Star, it's finally time to say goodbye

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/after-40-years-at-star-finally-time-say-goodbye/docview/3111069351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+40+years+at+the+Star%2C+it%27s+finally+time+to+say+goodbye&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I remember when I was lying in bed as a child, listening to the radio and hearing the moving song, "Thanks for the Memories," sung by the late comedian Bob Hope.
Today, I'm feeling much deeper nostalgia for the remarkable 40 years of my lifetime career working for the Toronto Star.
Now, I'm retiring.
It's been fulsome and fantastic years of engaging with Toronto Star readers. It became a mutual relationship when I wrote my advice column for the last 23 of those years. Oddly enough, it was because the famous American-based advice columnist, Ann Landers, had died.
I'd read every column of hers in the Star since I was an early teen, fascinated by her readers' personal stories and Landers' insights.
After she passed on, I called the Star's then-publisher, John Honderich, and asked him, "What about a made-in-Canada relationship adviser?" He said "Lunch!" And the deal was done. I called it, simply, "Ellie." It was syndicated across Canada.
Honderich immediately launched a $1-million campaign to promote my new column. Billboards blasted my face on the sides and back of every bus in Toronto (like past billboard ads for the American TV Series "Sex in the City.")
I became the Star's relationship advice columnist, answering thousands of letters and emails over the years. I'm grateful for the loyalty and trust of every reader who sought my advice, even those who sometimes disagreed. I was learning all sides of people's personal situations.
So how did I land in the field of professional journalism?
Back when I was a young mother with two school-age children, I wrote freelance articles at home, sending them to magazines and newspapers. To my surprise, all were published.
Next came an unexpected phone call from the Star's then-managing editor, Mark Harrison, who invited me to join the newspaper staff as a reporter. It was the chance of a lifetime. I became a staff reporter writing daily news. Next, the Family Section editors put me in charge of the Food, Fashion and Home sections.
With the fashion season in full bloom, I was sent to cover the Paris "showings." While interviewing Karl Lagerfeld, the creative director for Chanel, about his latest fashion coup presenting "the maxi skirt," he called over an employee to deliver my skirt size.
I carefully said, "It's lovely." He said, "It's yours." I said, "I can't take it." He tried once more to give me the skirt, but I refused. That's when Lagerfeld, smiling, said, "The New York Times doesn't take freebies either."
It was a proud moment for me.
But Paris' excess was in full mode at the following after-party. The smoke-filled room boasted fashion models and celebrities alike. Suddenly, a woman pulled me to her lap. It was Iman, the Somalian-born beauty, married to the late singer-songwriter David Bowie for 24 years. We chatted briefly and I left.
During that same time, the Star boasted some fearless female reporters including the late, sadly missed Christie Blatchford, plus the remarkable Rosie DiManno, whose hard-hitting truths are still regularly delivered by her on page two, with the intellectual force of a wakeup punch!
Then I proudly became the Star's first female Sunday Editor. It was a big title for a very popular weekend paper. I remained as the editor throughout the astonishing baseball World Series of both 1992 and 1993!
And those were only the early years of my working for the Toronto Star.
I was next assigned to the Star's investigative unit, writing feature stories ... and everything changed.
I consider my biggest, most significant story was about the Dionne quintuplets and how I became involved with one of Canada's most fascinating yet shocking tales.
They were the first five identical newborn girls, hailed as worldwide wonders ever to survive beyond a few days, especially as they were so tiny, and then exploited for years. Ontario's then-Liberal government recognized the babies' enormous political and economic value. They were placed under guardianship of four provincial appointees with three acts granting control until the quintuplets were age 21.
All other power grabs and exploitations soon became highly possible, including a specially built "tourist attraction" named Quintland, where the very young girls were displayed like caged animals in a zoo. Money from countless visiting American and world-travelling tourists poured into the government's "guardians," but not to the Quintuplets themselves.
Years later, I followed closely the pursuit by the surviving three adult quintuplets (Cecile, Annette and Yvonne, after the earlier deaths of Emilie and Marie), for fair compensation from their trust funds.
Extravagant expenses and dubious billing practices had bled the women's trusts by half. Finally, the three sisters, urged on by Cecile's son Bertrand, connected with a Montreal public relations adviser, who told me, "I was meeting a part of Canadian history."
I felt the same way.
I wrote daily Toronto Star accounts about the women's determined efforts to receive fair compensation from the province. An avalanche of 6,000 faxes and letters of support arrived at my newsroom desk.
Readers expressed their great empathy for the women and outrage over years of their being exploited.
I packed boxes with the many letters delivered to me, got help carrying them to Premier Mike Harris' office and handed them over to him.
Finally, after negotiations between two high-powered Toronto lawyers, the women received a total of $4 million in compensation. It was a joyous highlight of my time at the Star.
Soon after, some remarkably big investigative stories followed with journalists pursuing them, across the globe.
Luckily, I'd found access through a Toronto woman to the story of Raoul Wallenberg, a Swedish diplomat based in Budapest, Hungary, during the Second World War amid Nazi terror. At the neutral Swedish legation, he was implored for help by Jewish residents living in Hungary and hiding for their lives. Wallenberg designed a self-styled "protection passport" providing Swedish immigration permits which he handed out.
He personally sheltered people for safety in his own apartment, including a baby born there (maternity hospitals were closed to Jews). He'd protected the delivering mother by remaining outside his own door, holding a gun.
He named the baby Yvonne after one of his relatives and considered her his goddaughter. She grew up to attend a ceremony years later in 1997, along with Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of England. I accompanied Yvonne Singer to the unveiling of the Wallenberg Monument by the Queen, at Western Marble Arch Synagogue. It honoured Wallenberg's selfless bravery.
Meanwhile, my Toronto Star work-related connections were expanding. A Canadian-based TV show called "Outlaw In-Laws," started in 2007, hired me to be "the talent," guiding warring in-laws towards some mutual understanding.
It was long gruelling work, travelling across Ontario in the middle of winter with a TV crew. But it was rewarding, fun and a new adventure for me. And throughout, those TV scripts were a basis for helping families heal their differences.
Several years ago, the #MeToo period began, with people of all ages and backgrounds speaking out about having experienced sexual abuse. I asked readers to send me, anonymously, their own stories. Most were heartbreaking.
Older women who'd been raped when young, said they'd never fully recovered from it. One man disclosed when, as a boy, he'd been tied to a tree and abused by older boys. Their letters committed me firmly to my work.
Now, years later and having reached retirement, I'm delighted that my daughter Lisi has also chosen to do the important work of offering relationship advice ... in the tradition of the daughters of Ann Landers and her twin sister, Dear Abby.
Thanks to all my readers for sharing these memories with me. I loved the varied jobs and learned enormously from special colleagues who'd accepted a "newbie."
Thanks especially for the past 26 years since I met and married a colleague there and, while loving the newspaper, I've loved him too.
To work at something deeply meaningful is a gift.
I hope all who've read this, have also achieved personal satisfaction in life. And that you still find and maintain it, whenever possible.
CAPTION: The Dionne siblings,five identical newborn girls, were the first quintuplet to survive beyond a few days. Hailed as worldwide wonders, the children were exploitedfor years.Ellie Tesher holds some of the hundreds of letters that arrived at the Star in support of the Dionne quintuplets, a story she worked on and considers the biggest of her career. Ellie's daughter Lisi has chosen to continue the advice column.EI Scan photo by Fred Davis from Helen DafoeSteve RussellToronto star file photo

Document 439 of 500

Ontario had a plan to solve the growing kids crisis. It sat on the shelf for more than two years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-had-plan-solve-growing-kids-crisis-sat-on/docview/3111069210/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+had+a+plan+to+solve+the+growing+kids+crisis.+It+sat+on+the+shelf+for+more+than+two+years&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be the plan that finally made a difference.
At least, that's what Karen Wilson believed when she agreed to join a group of experts, child welfare leaders and parents Doug Ford's government asked to find ways to improve treatment and care for children with complex mental health needs.
This was in 2021, when healthcare leaders were sounding the alarm about a youth mental health crisis coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic.
To Wilson, a parent and child advocate, it seemed like the provincial government was finally ready to take action - to transform a health and social services system that had long left Ontario's most vulnerable children in the shadows.
"This wasn't going to be another report that sat collecting dust on a shelf," Wilson said. "That's what I thought."
Two-and-a-half years after the roundtable completed its plan to address a systemwide crisis, the province has not acted - and the situation has gotten worse.
Kids are facing years-long waitlists for treatment. Exhausted families are being told their child is "too high needs" for specialized care. Scores of vulnerable children with complex needs are living out of hotels and office buildings while under the care of Ontario child welfare agencies. And hundreds of Ontario parents have been forced to relinquish care of their kids to children's aid societies because they've been unable to get help.
The Star has obtained a copy of the roundtable report, which had not previously been made public, and spoke to six former table members who confirmed the province has not been in touch for follow-up discussions or to take the lead on any of the 32 recommendations meant to help pull families out of crisis and bring cohesion to a fragmented system.
Meanwhile, several members of the roundtable who spoke to the Star say the group has received little more than a 'thank you' for their work.
In March 2022, the roundtable submitted a report that included 32 recommendations to help pull families out of crisis. In a letter introducing their findings, the leaders made it clear they expected a response.
"To remove any doubt regarding our future expectations, we, along with all collaboration table members, declare - unequivocally - that we await with great interest the response from (the ministries)," they wrote, noting they were committed to working together on the solutions.
Wilson, 55, was hopeful. When the roundtable began, she had gone to some of the struggling families she worked with in the Windsor area and urged them to share their stories.
This is our chance, she told them. "We're going to make a difference."
She herself had raised a son with mental health and developmental needs and had then become a child and youth advocate, guiding desperate families through the same challenges she'd navigated as the mother of a boy with what the province calls "complex needs." Children like her son typically have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, and are united by the fact that Ontario's health and social services struggle to meet their needs.
The roundtable met more than a dozen times over 12 months. It felt like a good sign that representatives from two Ontario ministries - the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) - attended the meetings. They heard parents speak about living in crisis.
"I asked these families to step up and share their stories, to bare their hearts," said Wilson. "And then literally nothing came of it."
In response to questions from the Star, the two ministries sent a statement that said the report provided them "with insight and recommendations to improve the lives of children and youth."
The statement did not respond directly to questions about which recommendations, if any, have been acted upon, but cited several recent investments in child and youth health, including a $97 million pilot program for kids with complex special needs at three children's hospitals, funding to "expand access to community-based intensive services" and 10 new complex care beds.
A plan for a growing crisisThe roundtable recognized a host of long-standing challenges.
Its final report proposed recommendations that are "balanced, achievable, and essential for meaningful change," co-chairs Patrick Dion, the former executive director of a mental health services agency, and Mark Kartusch, a child welfare leader, told the Star in a statement.
"We remain ready to support the implementation of these recommendations and are confident that our colleagues share this commitment to progress."
Some members of the roundtable who viewed the ministries' statement to the Star said that while investments are appreciated and will help children and families in some regions, the funding announcements noted by the government don't address the group's recommendations, which were meant to improve the system's overall effectiveness.
A boy on a futon in an office. Teens in budget hotels. How Ontario is failing kids who are 'too complex' for care
Derrick Drouillard, executive director of the Windsor-Essex Children's Aid Society and member of the 2021 roundtable, said it's notable that the report's first recommendation - to create a standing committee at the deputy ministerial level between the MCCSS and the ministries of Health and Education - has not yet been acted upon.
"Never have we been able to sit and say, at the highest ministry levels, let's sit together and unpack these recommendations," said Drouillard.
In an interview, Kartusch, now a director at an Indigenous child welfare agency near Peterborough, acknowledged the report "has sat" and said its status is "something you'd have to ask the ministry about." He added that he remains "hopeful" the report will move forward, noting the province needs "a broad system solution, otherwise, we're just band-aiding it."
Among the recommendations: create and appropriately fund a new "Escalated Service" for young people in crisis; implement an "accelerated response mechanism" to overcome structural barriers for Indigenous and young people who face inequities; build capacity to ensure families and children get early intervention, minimizing their need for a crisis response; and boost the availability of live-in treatment options and access to respite services.
Drouillard said an "Escalated Service" - essentially a crisis response team - for each region is critical, noting that child welfare staff need to have confidence they can call a children's mental health provider or community service and quickly get a residential treatment spot for the child or teen who has come through their doors.
"We don't have that right now," he said. "We'll have a meeting and we'll hear that it's going to take three months or six months and we are just waiting and waiting and waiting."
'I'm desensitized to nothing happening'Panel member Tanya Stack was raising a 14-year-old son with autism, anxiety and other complex needs when she agreed to join the roundtable to share her experience. Stack, an Oshawa bookkeeper with two younger children, said she was disappointed but not surprised the government hasn't taken action. Parents of children with complex needs are used to wasting their time pushing for change that never comes, she said. "That's our life."
"This is just another one of the things that I put my heart and soul into, all that effort, and it goes to the back burner," she said. "I'm desensitized to nothing happening."
Stack and her spouse have endured the cycle familiar to parents of children with high needs: traumatic police calls and emergency room trips that ended with their son waiting for many hours and then being released; getting turned down for services because of his dual diagnosis or high-functioning autism; unpredictable access to respite services.
"We're doing everything that we can do to try to survive day to day," Stack said.
'The hardest thing' for a parent: This family tried for years to get their son help but may have to give him up
One year after the report was submitted to the government, Karen Wilson wrote to the ministry staff members who'd sat with her on the panel, saying she felt "sick" knowing that nothing had come of it.
"Can it really be true that not one of the recommendations has been acted upon to better the lives of families and youth in our communities ... even though we gave them the information they needed to begin the necessary changes to make things better?" she wrote.
Wilson, who works for a family support network in Windsor, said she continues to hear from parents who see no help coming and feel like they are "drowning."
"The family members are becoming physically ill, marriages are falling apart, and young people ... are continuing to spiral, taking their parents and siblings with them."

Things have gotten much worse for Ontario families since Wilson was raising her own son, Robert, who is now 28, she said. Wilson considers herself fortunate: she managed to get residential care for her child beginning at age 13 and educational supports when he was in school. "Although it was extremely challenging parenting a complex child with disabilities, we didn't face the same horrible journey that the families coming up behind us are."
In 2005, Wilson was told that the only way to get appropriate treatment for her then-nine-year-old child was to give him up to CAS under a temporary care agreement. Appalled, she phoned the office of the provincial minister. Soon after, the government, then led by Dalton McGuinty's Liberal Party, made a public promise to stop putting families in that position.
"No family in this province, in this day and age, should have to consider giving up their children to get the help" they need, then-minister Sandra Pupatello, said in the legislature.
But that's exactly what families are now faced with, Wilson said.
"The support these kids need is not available. It doesn't exist today," Wilson said. "Or if it does, it's hidden."
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and mogilvie@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Karen Wilson holds a photo of her son, Robert, at age two. Wilson says she was thrilled to sit on a roundtable aimed to address the growing crisis facing Ontario families of kids with complex needs.

Will Doug Ford's congestion law thwart Hamilton bike lanes?: City hopes to build or improve 119 kilometres of bike infrastructure over next five years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-doug-fords-congestion-law-thwart-hamilton/docview/3111068967/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Doug+Ford%27s+congestion+law+thwart+Hamilton+bike+lanes%3F%3A+City+hopes+to+build+or+improve+119+kilometres+of+bike+infrastructure+over+next+five+years&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's Tory government is preparing to limit where bike lanes can be built - just months after Hamilton's council voted to double the pace of bicycle-safety construction in the city.
The planned anti-congestion legislation - first reported by the CBC but not yet officially announced by the province - would ban new bike lanes on roads where a car-traffic lane would be lost as a result.
Details on the expected legislation remain scarce, but Premier Doug Ford effectively confirmed the basics in responding to reporter questions about the issue at a recent Cobourg announcement.
"We just want to get traffic moving, that's what we want to do," he told reporters. "That's what it comes down to, making sure you aren't putting bike lanes in the middle of some of the busiest streets in the country. Put them on the secondary roads."
The apparent provincial plan has elicited support from some car drivers frustrated by slow commutes, but also outrage from cycling and road safety advocates, thousands of whom have so far signed a petition set up by Cycle Toronto.
"It does worry me," said Mark Anderson, a Hamilton biking advocate and board member with Cycle Hamilton. "Protected bike lanes make roads safer for cyclists, but also for people who use mobility devices, pedestrians walking on the sidewalk because they benefit from having that buffer ...
"If Doug Ford wants to improve congestion, he should spend more on bike lanes and transit to get cars off the road."
But will the contentious legislation actually thwart Hamilton's newly approved plan to "accelerate" construction of 119 kilometres of new or improved bike infrastructure?
It's a big question for a council that prioritized creation of "complete streets" and committed tens of millions of dollars to expand multimodal transportation options for residents while trying to reduce fatal collisions through Vision Zero road safety improvements.
Transportation planning director Brian Hollingworth said the city won't comment directly on unannounced, "draft" legislation. But he noted "many" of the bike lane projects in Hamilton's $60-million, fast-tracked bike network build-out "do not require the replacement of traffic lanes."
There are a few notable exceptions.
Planned bike lanes on part of Grays Road call for a "road diet" taking four car lanes down to three;
A curb lane on Mountain Brow Boulevard will be repurposed to help connect a popular multi-use path along the urban Mountain escarpment edge. Worth noting: axing that car lane is also a strategic move meant to calm traffic on a section of road Coun. Tom Jackson has said some motorists use as a "speedway;"
Technically, the equivalent of a car lane on busy Main Street will be repurposed for cycling between Sherman and Victoria avenues (and maybe someday into Westdale) as part of a larger two-way traffic conversion through the core.
Those planned protected bike lanes will take up former car-traffic space on what was once one of the widest one-way arterial roads in Canada. But the city has already strategically blocked vehicle access to one of the original lanes of traffic via bump-outs and parking along much of the collision-prone street.
The pending legislation looks like another example of provincial politicians "undermining" local decision-making without any effort to understand local issues, suggested Anderson, who pointed to the Tory government's now-infamous flip-flop on Hamilton's Greenbelt lands and urban boundaries.
Coun. Maureen Wilson also called on Ford to "stay in your lane."
"Let me say this to the premier ... stop playing wedge politics," she said via email. "Instead, support cities in their efforts to be healthy, sustainable, resilient and economically prosperous."
Matthew Van Dongen i
s a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Hamilton has vowed to add more protected bike lanes on busy streets, providing proposed provincial rule changes don't throw a wrench in its plans.hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter Matthew Van Dongen i s a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com

Hamilton-area Today: Did Ottawa and Ontario defraud Six Nations of money and territory? + What about our bike lanes?
