Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton-area+Today%3A+Did+Ottawa+and+Ontario+defraud+Six+Nations+of+money+and+territory%3F+%2B+What+about+our+bike+lanes%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning! It's Sept. 30, here are the top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
The mild weather continues into the first week of October. Environment Canada is forecasting a high of 21 C at the airport weather station. There are no alerts.
It is 17 C and slightly misty at 8 a.m.
Sunrise 7:17 a.m. and sun sets tonight at 7:01 p.m.
TRAFFIC AND TRANSIT UPDATES:
BURLINGTON: QEW Toronto bound at Walker's Line, collision blocking left lane. 8:55 a.m.
Traffic continues to move surprisingly well in the Hamilton area for a Monday morning.
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
- #HSRAlert, Sept. 30 at 9 a.m. to Nov. 1 at 6 p.m., detour on routes 1 KING and 20 A-LINE due to James Street construction.
- #HSRAlert, Sept. 30 - Oct. 7. Detours on Routes 22, 23, 24, 25, 26, 27 when travelling southbound. Routes 21, 33 & 35 will be on detour travelling northbound. Because of construction at John Street South and Arkledun Avenue.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.
Top stories on The Spec:'We're not asking to break Canada'
According to Six Nations of the Grand River, it was one of the biggest frauds in Canadian history - the swindling of almost a million acres of land, leaving the Haudenosaunee people bankrupt. Now a lawsuit launched 40 years ago, alleging that the Crown breached treaty obligations in the Haldimand Tract, inches toward a court date.
Massive amounts of time saved
In its first six months, a pilot project involving eight full-time special constables diverted approximately 2,172 hours of police work, freeing up officers to respond to other urgent calls. Now, members of the police services board have approved a request to hire 16 more full-time special constables to help expand the work.
What about our bike lanes?
Doug Ford's Tory government is preparing to limit where bike lanes can be built - just months after Hamilton's council voted to double the pace of bicycle-safety construction in the city.
Gun violence 'must stop'
A man was arrested and a firearm seized after he allegedly pointed a gun at a group of people on King Street earlier this month. The firearm was one of 301 that police have taken off Hamilton streets this year.
Also, starting Monday, the city is closing the stretch of James Street to traffic for a month.
Subscribe to our newsletters for the latest local content.
CAPTION: Traffic on Hamilton area highways Monday morning.When firefighters arrived, heavy smoke and fire could be seen coming from the first floor of the two-storey home.

Document 442 of 500

Third NDP MPP hopes to swap Queen's Park for Ottawa in the next federal election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/third-ndp-mpp-hopes-swap-queens-park-ottawa-next/docview/3110897113/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Third+NDP+MPP+hopes+to+swap+Queen%27s+Park+for+Ottawa+in+the+next+federal+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Jagmeet Singh's NDP could be adding a third member of the Ontario legislature to its roster of candidates, with Parkdale-High Park MPP Bhutila Karpoche announcing her intention to seek the party's federal nomination in that riding, the Star has learned. "Our country is at a crossroads and what we decide to do next will shape all our lives for a long time. It is not the time to simply accept things the way they are or cross our fingers that things will get better. It is a time to stand up and make the changes we want to see happen," Karpoche wrote in a statement to the Star."We must refuse the politics of cynicism and division. We must care for each other, not lose our sense of humanity. We must build a Canada where everyone, especially young people, can feel that our future is bright and secure." Karpoche becomes the third member of Ontario New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles's caucus who hopes to run under the NDP's federal banner when the next election is called - which could happen at any time, now that Singh has ended his parliamentary alliance that propped up the minority Liberal government.Hamilton Mountain MPP Monique Taylor and Ottawa Centre MPP Joel Harden have also mounted bids to secure the federal nominations in their ridings, both of which are currently held by Justin Trudeau's Liberals but have previously been won by the NDP. The same is true of Toronto's Parkdale-High Park, a riding that was previously won twice by the NDP's Peggy Nash, but has been home to Liberal MP - and now federal justice minister - Arif Virani since 2015. Despite Virani's current grip on the riding, Parkdale-High Park has produced close races between the Liberals and New Democrats in the past.The three MPPs are not yet official candidates for the federal New Democrats, who have already nominated 59 candidates but have yet to schedule nomination contests in their ridings. And while all three can remain in Stiles's caucus until a federal campaign is triggered, their potential departures would represent a blow for the Ontario NDP leader, who has ejected two MPPs - Sarah Jama and Mike Mantha - from her caucus, both of whom now sit as Independents. A loss of numerous incumbents could put the Ontario NDP on the back foot at a time when Premier Doug Ford has not ruled out calling an early provincial election.But should Karpoche win the nomination, her candidacy would be key to the federal NDP's hopes of wresting a number of Toronto ridings away from the Liberals amid waning support for the Trudeau government. The federal New Democrats have not held a riding in Toronto since 2015, when they lost their eight Toronto-area seats to the Liberals as Trudeau won a majority government. The party believes that the ascension of Olivia Chow - who was an NDP MP in the former riding of Trinity-Spadina from 2006 to 2014 - to Toronto's mayoralty last summer has given it an opening to reclaim long-lost turf. Karpoche, who also considered running in the mayoral race last year, has represented Parkdale-High Park at Queen's Park since 2018, and sailed easily to re-election in 2022 with 54 per cent of the vote. She is currently the Ontario NDP's affordability and GTA issues critic, and was the first Tibetan-Canadian elected to public office in North America.
CAPTION: Parkdale-High Park MPP Bhutila Karpochespeaks at a rally in Toronto protesting the closure of the Ontario Science Centre on July 14, 2024.

Subject: Provincial elections; Nominations; Local elections

Sept. 28: LRT will bring infrastructure upgrades, Ford should be ashamed of comments and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-28-lrt-will-bring-infrastructure-upgrades/docview/3110721365/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+28%3A+LRT+will+bring+infrastructure+upgrades%2C+Ford+should+be+ashamed+of+comments+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LRT brings infrastructure upgrades
I have to wonder if the people who are against LRT have actually taken the time to see what's included in the project. Years ago I saw a presentation on this and my recollection is that besides the benefits of reducing a reliance on vehicles, which, having been to many European cities, I believe in, it includes the replacement of our city's most significant core infrastructure along the entire route from McMaster to Eastgate Square, i.e. all of the aging main (and critical) sewer and water lines - failures that have recently (in Calgary and Montreal) cost Canadian cities and their individual taxpayers a fortune in the past year alone.
In addition, the project includes the installation of new infrastructure to support fibre optic and other technologies that will help bring this city into the future for decades to come. The fact that the cost will not be borne entirely by the taxpayers of Hamilton makes this an absolute no-brainer to me.
Please, stop the delay and get this going before we lose the opportunity of having more than $3 billion of needed improvements done to this city of ours.
Dave Hachey, Dundas
LRT will die after election
Re: Hamilton LRT delays force city to consider road fixes that will only be 'torn up again' later, Sept. 24
Without sounding like "Captain Obvious" here, the answer is yes, go right ahead and do your road repairs as you see fit.
This LRT is never going to be built. The mayor can't get a straight answer from anyone as to what is happening with this supposed project.
There is no champion or driving force moving this forward. This can only mean one thing, and that is it's being left on the vine to die a natural death. If there was any enthusiasm for this project we would have a clear vision by now and would see positive movement daily.
My guess is Doug Ford is waiting for the spring election to take place, after which he pulls the plug again. This was a Liberal promise to the city of Hamilton which Ford inherited; obviously it's not a pet project of his and he sees little upside to its completion. The memo should come through shortly after the spring election.
My prediction is there will be a tunnel underneath the 401 before you see LRT in Hamilton.
Frank Jalsevac, Hamilton
Ford's tunnel plans all wet
Premier Doug Ford wants to build a tunnel under Highway 401. I wonder how long it will take and how many of us will still be driving when it is completed. Would it not be easier and cheaper to build an elevated roadway above the highway? How many sunken roadways in Toronto flood when we get serious rain, and what is the likelihood that this tunnel roadway will become unusable in bad weather? Let us think carefully before we pull the trigger on this.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
Do not open door for Jama
Re: NDP hasn't ruled out bringing Sarah Jama back into the party for next election, Sept. 25
The interest in reinstating Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama to the NDP caucus is deeply troubling. Her divisive rhetoric and toxic leadership style undermined the unity we desperately need in Ontario. If the NDP aspires to govern our province, it must stand for inclusivity, not reward behaviours that fuel division and distrust. Ontario deserves leaders who build bridges. Allowing Jama back into the fold sends the wrong message about the kind of province we want to be.
J. Solomon Williams, St. Catharines
Ford should be ashamed of comments
A few days ago I watched with growing dismay as our premier responded to a question about homeless encampments when he was in Cobourg. Never have I seen such a lack of compassion and I was astounded at Mr. Ford's ignorance concerning the people who live in such encampments. He so "eloquently" said they should get off their asses and find a job. Unbelievable! A lot of the folks who are living in the encampments, including seniors, have mental health issues, physical health issues, have been unable to live on minimum wages or have been kicked out of their apartments because their landlords are renovating them or going to use the units for themselves.
Ford pointed to the construction equipment behind him in Cobourg and said they should get a job doing that, as if someone with no heavy equipment training could get a job there. Our premier then said that if they had health problems that he would take care of them for life. An empty promise as those in Ontario who are living on provincial disability payments can hardly pay their rent, let alone pay for food, clothing, transportation to appointments or medicines. Premier Ford should be ashamed of himself.
Janet L. MacNeil, Caledonia
Encampment plans are troubling
Re: Council's new encampment plan raises questions, Sept. 19
Something that really bothers me is the mayor and councillors have a fiduciary responsibility to Hamilton taxpayers. They're spending all these millions for an issue that even the mayor admits is a provincial and federal government responsibility, not Hamilton taxpayers.
In addition, are they going to single-source spend $18 million? How on earth will we ever know if that is value per dollars spent? Would they do this if they were operating a private business or if the $18 million was coming from mayor and councillors' private bank accounts? Of course not.
Bob Graham, Waterdown
Complacency is the enemy
Re: Americans can't afford to stay on the sidelines this November, Sept. 25
Donald Abelson's column basically outlined the inherent necessity for voting in this upcoming November election in the U.S. I believe this message is relevant and can transcend all geopolitical borders. We must be reminded that we need to take initiative and stand up for our beautiful country. In my opinion, the power of the vote cannot be undermined, especially in these very unstable times. Complacency and apathy are the enemies and very dangerous for our country. We must step up to the plate and use our vote as a way of expressing how we want to be governed. Indifference must be circumvented so that we may continue to exercise our democracy. Otherwise the alternative could possibly erode it.
Joanne Venzon, Hamilton
Offer kindness to strangers
Recently, I witnessed three people, including an older woman, with their dog slowly packing up and clearing a tent encampment they seemed to have been living in in our abandoned neighbourhood school property. They took the warnings of local police seriously. I went over in the afternoon to ask where they were headed and was greeted with friendliness, but not consoled by their lack of direction. I worked with the homeless for almost 10 years in St. Catharines and find it so hard to accept this kind of "lostness" in our community. I remember when being baffled in the 1980s by what then seemed to be a kind of novel social stigma that helped keep the issue invisible. Well, it's 2024, and the world appears to be shaking with political strife and economic instability. Maybe the least we can do as individuals is earnestly practice a kindness to strangers. The systemic barriers and gaps in services are another thing that can also benefit from practices of basic human compassion. Let us each do our part in these mean times.
Julia Blushak, St. Catharines
Visitor grateful for help from locals
May I please use your letters column to thank those Hamilton residents who came to my help last Friday night?
I flew from Florida to Buffalo and drove a rented a car to Hamilton to attend a family party. On my plane, I realized I was still wearing my sunglasses, having left my clear glasses in my car, so I drove as fast as possible to reach Hamilton before nightfall.
Sadly, I didn't make it. I found myself in a suburb I didn't know the name of; able to drive but not navigate as I could not make out street names at intersections. So I stopped outside a house where the occupants were still watering their garden. Alex was very helpful, and having established that I could drive he phoned the guest house where I was to stay and arranged with Tim, the co-owner, that after leading me to a midway location he would - so to speak - turn over the reins to Tim, who would lead me to the guest house. The plan worked and I had a wonderful night's sleep there, free from the anxiety that leaving my glasses behind had caused.
Alex also gave me a pair of his old glasses, which saw me through the rest of the weekend until I got back home to Florida. Thanks to Alex and Tim I had a lovely weekend in Hamilton as well as a wonderful family party.
Martin Wilson, Fernandina Beach, Fla.
CAPTION: "Never have I seen such a lack of compassion and I was astounded at (Premier Doug) Ford's ignorance concerning the people who live in such encampments," a reader writes.A street in downtown Kitchener is rebuilt during LRT construction in 2016. Light rail will bring widespread - and much needed - infrastructure improvements to Hamilton, a reader argues.

Document 444 of 500

Truth and reconciliation: Hamilton to explore city holiday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/truth-reconciliation-hamilton-explore-city/docview/3110721353/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Truth+and+reconciliation%3A+Hamilton+to+explore+city+holiday&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton will explore making National Day for Truth and Reconciliation a statutory municipal holiday next year.
Mayor Andrea Horwath received council's support for a staff report on a possible city statutory holiday starting with Sept. 30, 2025.
The federal government declared the national day of observance in 2021 and some provinces have followed suit, but not Ontario.
Likewise, several municipalities have declared statutory holidays, including Burlington and Oakville, where staff receive a paid holiday and some services shut down.
Council can consider whether the federal day should be "reinforced here in Hamilton as a day for reflection and participation in events for everyone," Horwath said.
In 2015, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) report made 94 "calls to action" relating to the lasting effects of Canada's residential schools.
"We have commitments around truth and reconciliation at this city," Horwath said Wednesday, pointing to Hamilton's "Urban Indigenous Strategy."
Council has asked staff report back with recommendations on the process to recognize a city holiday, including potential legal, financial and human resource issues.
City politicians also agreed to send a letter to Premier Doug Ford urging him to declare a provincial holiday.
Coun. John-Paul Danko said he supported a staff report but noted the "complexity of inserting another statutory holiday" between Labour Day and Thanksgiving.
The argument against making Remembrance Day a statutory holiday, Danko added, is that it's "beneficial" to have people, whether at school or work, together to commemorate Nov. 11.
That could also apply to National Truth and Reconciliation Day, he suggested.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Council has backed Mayor Andrea Horwath's motion calling for a staff report into a potential city statutory holiday to mark the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation starting with Sept. 30, 2025.

Subject: Councils; Reconciliation

People: Horwath, Andrea

Build homes
not tunnels
So Premier Ford wants to build a tunnel that could stretch from Mississauga to Markham. What a feat! What an accomplishment! That would be an epic legacy for him to be remembered by for generations to come! Meh. I have a better idea. Instead of spending billions on that, spend it on providing truly affordable homes for young families, increasing the number of affordable rental units and provide adequate funding for social services that help and house the vulnerable in communities. This is not the time for a "Tunnel of Babel." Fix the housing crisis first, Mr Ford!
Brenda Bianchi, Hamilton

Document 447 of 500

Why is everything we do now wrong?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-is-everything-we-do-now-wrong/docview/3110652390/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+everything+we+do+now+wrong%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Please be aware: whatever you're doing, you're doing it wrong.
I was researching drones this summer and was struck by the number of expert posts about how doing this or that was wrong. You're using the RC all wrong. You're changing the blades all wrong. Takeoff is wrong. Landing is wrong.
It freaked me out. So now I'm sticking with paper planes.
Cutting an avocado? Congratulations, you're doing it wrong. Vacuuming? You're doing it wrong. Laundry? You're doing it wrong. Taking supplements? You're doing it wrong. Arts and crafts? Wrong and wrong. Negotiating a raise? You're doing it wrong. Charging your phone? You're doing it wrong. Scrubbing grout? Your technique is so hilariously wrong.
Building a tunnel under the 401? You're definitely doing it wrong. Look, I'm all for easing traffic congestion. Our commuting times couldn't be longer if the streets were coated with molasses. But if a city needs six weeks to fill a pothole, eight months to fix a bridge and 13 years (and counting) to construct an LRT line, excavating a subterranean motorway from Brampton to Scarborough will take about 900 years.
Premier, put down the imaginary shovel. If you can't fortify the Science Centre, the world's longest tunnel is the world's biggest pipe-dream. Supersonic flying buses driven by robot monkeys are more feasible. Teleportation will exist long before that tunnel opens.
Doug Ford, you're doing it wrong. Not that you should take offence.
According to the fastest growing segment of service journalism, it seems we are all doing everything wrong all the time. Culture has turned efficiency and perfectionism into twin virtues and now the internet is chockablock with glib scolds who are keen to point out why your pillow fluffing is all wrong.
I assume you're breathing right now? You're doing it wrong. Use your nose and engage your diaphragm. Inhale. Exhale. Pull out your hair.
Last year, WebMD noted, "10 Ways You're Drinking Water Wrong." Too cold. Gulping too fast. Drinking out of a stream (?). When my daughters were young, they loved when I made them "spa water." All I did was chuck a few lemon and cucumber slices into the pitcher and stir dramatically. Their eyes got big. It was like I was doing a magic trick.
Unfortunately, I was also increasing their exposure to salmonella and E. coli.
Now I know why Drake wrote a song called, "Doing It Wrong."
A story this week noted how we are pouring champagne wrong. You shouldn't be using a flute. A universal wine glass is a better bubbly vessel. Luckily, I work at a newspaper and can't afford champagne. But I was horrified by another emphatic headline: "How To Make a Dirty Martini - You're Doing It Wrong All Wrong."
You know what else I've doing wrong? Tying my shoes. Yeah. My parents taught me to make two loops, tuck one under the other and pull. Wrong. They didn't teach me any Lifehacker methods, including "Bunny Ears," "Big Loops," "Cheerio" and "Ian knot." Once at Home Depot, my laces came undone and I suddenly lurched toward a circular saw display.
Mom? Dad?Doing it wrongcould have slashed my throat.
The Doing It Wrong Movement is now criss-crossing the globe. In India, they are cleaning air conditioners wrong. In Sweden, they are making coffee wrong. In Japan, they are making eye contact wrong. In Singapore, the men are having sex wrong. In Ireland, they are getting into the back of cabs wrong.
And you don't want to know how wrong you are while peeling a banana.
So today I raise an anti-wrong fist and shout, "Enough!"
I am taking a stand against the Do Righters. I don't have time to worry about optimized posture. Or the correct way to squeegee a windshield. I'm scraping off the bird poop and beating rush hour. Related, I try to stay clear of the scatological, but we are also wiping wrong?
What's that, Popular Science? I'm mowing my lawn wrong? I should be following the "One-Third Rule" - whatever that is - and be aware if the grass variant is Bermuda, Zoysia or Fescue? Listen to me. You lunatics are now engaged in unpopular science.
You want to help me out? Send over an intern to mow my lawn.
You do you, Do Righters. Leave the rest of us alone. I don't care if there is a more efficient way to pack a suitcase when I'm running late. Enough with the shaming. Enough with the annoying advice. If it works, it's fine. Am I brushing my teeth too aggressively and risking gum damage? Possibly. But my mouth feels minty fresh, so go suck a lemon out of the spa water and think about what might happen when your engine conks out in that 401 tunnel.
But you're showering wrong! You're stretching wrong! You're enunciating wrong!
Fine, how's this: Shut. Up. Already.
CAPTION: Building a tunnel under the 401? You're definitely doing it wrong. If a city needs six weeks to fill a pothole, excavating a subterranean motorway from Brampton to Scarborough will take about 900 years, writes Vinay Menon.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Vinay Menon OPINION

Document 448 of 500

Should Doug Ford buy the 407 instead of digging a tunnel to ease gridlock?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/should-doug-ford-buy-407-instead-digging-tunnel/docview/3110652997/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Should+Doug+Ford+buy+the+407+instead+of+digging+a+tunnel+to+ease+gridlock%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's political opponents say a more prudent means for the province to relieve congestion on the 401 is to buy back the underused 407.
Leaders of the three other major political parties in Ontario agree it would be far cheaper, quicker and simpler to purchase the private toll road than what Ford proposed - digging what could be the world's largest tunnel beneath the world's busiest highway.
While the 18-lane behemoth that is the 401 is perpetually packed, the 407, which runs parallel to it, resorted to a promotional campaign earlier this year to entice drivers to take it.
Despite this, the Ford administration never considered buying the 407 before announcing the 401 tunnel proposal, a senior government source at Queen's Park told the Star.
The reason being that if population growth trends in Ontario hold, all of the 400-series highways in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area will hit or exceed capacity within the decade, according to the province's traffic modelling.
"The 407 will be at or over capacity in 10 years anyway," said the source, speaking privately on background to discuss internal deliberations.
Ford said as much on Wednesday in unveiling the tunnel plan.
"We can't just sit by and let this happen," he told reporters, noting Ontario's population increased by 800,000 last year.
The premier has made no secret that he is not a fan of the tolls motorists pay on 407, and has says former Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris made a wrong turn in 1999 when he decided to privatize the highway to raise money to lower his government's deficit.
But tolls can be tools to fight congestion, said Matti Siemiatycki, professor of geography and planning and director of the infrastructure institute at the University of Toronto.
"Tolls encourage people to use other modes of transportation," he said. "Or to carpool or to go during times when tolls are lower. That's what we've seen in other regions."
Had the 407 not been privatized in a 99-year lease for $3.1 billion, the initial cost of building the highway would have long ago been paid off and tolls eliminated.
More recent estimates of 407's value have come in at around $30 billion or more.
Arguments in favour of buying the 407 over building the tunnel hinge on the former seeming like it would be significantly cheaper. But the province has not released a cost estimate for the dig and experts say they don't have enough information yet to make their own calculations.
Siemiatycki's best guess, at this stage, is "mega billions."
"We don't know the length, we don't know how many lanes we're talking about here, this is really early days," he said. "The premier also talked about building transit under there, so that's another huge cost."
The Star reached out to 407 ETR, the company that operates the highway, to ask whether it would sell to the province, and if so, for how much. It declined to answer.
"407 ETR is a private road operator and we're not involved in provincial transportation policy discussions beyond our responsibilities under the concession agreement," said Christina Basil, 407 ETR's vice-president of communications and government relations. "Shareholder transactions are commercially sensitive, and as such, we are unable to comment on valuations."
Either way, tunnel or 407 acquisition, you can't defeat congestion for good with more lanes, transit experts say. This is explained by the concept of induced demand. More space for cars encourages more drivers. If you build it, they will come.
University of Toronto professor Steven Farber, a transportation geographer and spatial analyst, has a more sophisticated solution to gridlock than either proposal.
Instead, Ontario needs to develop a "holistic strategy" for tolling the 400-series highways, he said.
Single-occupancy vehicles are the largest contributor to congestion, he said. A "modest investment" in high-occupancy vehicle lanes could reduce their concentration.
"I would also restrict truck use of the 401 during peak periods to encourage logistics companies to pay for 407 use out of pocket," said Farber.
Ontarians should give up on the idea that roadways will become less congested, he added. Instead, efforts should go toward making other forms of transportation more accessible.
"The best we can do is provide more people opportunities to use other modes so that they don't have to be part of the congestion," he said. "But that's easy for me. I live about 5 km from my work and there are good transit options. We need to create those conditions for more people in more parts of the city."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is touting a tunnel under Highway 401, possibly from Mississauga to Scarborough, to carry more traffic and a public transit line as GTHA roads become increasingly crowded.

Subject: Transportation planning; Tolls; Roads & highways; Cost estimates

Document 449 of 500

Sept. 27: Hamilton must examine its needs vs. wants, taxpayers can't shoulder more and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-27-hamilton-must-examine-needs-vs-wants/docview/3110386335/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+27%3A+Hamilton+must+examine+its+needs+vs.+wants%2C+taxpayers+can%27t+shoulder+more+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Council must examine its needs vs. wants
Re: Hamilton's tax-hike shell game: Here we go again, Sept. 26
In Scott Radley's column about upcoming property tax increases, there were interesting comments by councillors Jeff Beattie ("We can't do it all"), Mike Spadafora (a possible hiring freeze) and Brad Clark ("Council has to start looking for efficiencies").
That led me to this year's sunshine list. And the question: what are the needs required to operate the city as opposed to the wants? I managed to go through the first 10 pages of a 130-page list. There are many police officers, firefighters and paramedics on that list, all of whom are integral to keeping the city functioning. But there are also some positions, in a very small sampling, that one wonders about. To be clear, this is not a reflection on the people doing those jobs; rather, are the jobs "needed" as opposed to "nice to haves?" And these also may be vital jobs. It's just hard to see how they directly result in an efficiently functioning city: Manager of early years - $130,000; Supervisor Strategy Architecture - $129,000; Program Lead Cultural Heritage - $112,000; Instructor - $121,000 (what is this person "instructing?"); and Manager Planning Competency Development - $129,000.
From their job titles, most of these positions would also have staff reporting to them, adding to costs.
Have any of our councillors gone through the list and asked that question? Has the mayor? Or do they simply shrug their shoulders and pass on cost increases to property owners who are already at or near the top of the tax pyramid in the province?
Thank you to the three councillors mentioned above for starting to ask the right questions.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Taxpayers can't shoulder more
This city council had better look hard at its budget. The taxpayers of this city cannot afford to shoulder anymore costs for its programs. If we cannot afford the mayor's sanctioned encampment then we don't get it. Has she any idea how many homeless will be drawn to the city to take advantage of this? We need more people putting into the city coffers not taking out. My CPP and OAS doesn't increase by these percentage. I'm beginning to wonder how I'm going to afford my house in this city.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Residents oppose apartment building
Thank you to the 678 residents in the Corman neighbourhood who signed a petition opposing the construction of an eight-storey apartment building at the former Glen Echo school property as being out of character for the neighbourhood and unsustainable within existing infrastructure. The petition has been submitted to the Ontario Land Tribunal in support of the City of Hamilton's defence of its decision to deny the developer's application at the upcoming case management hearing on Oct. 2. We appreciate your support.
Rick Estok, Hamilton
Do we need to legislate house prices?
Re: Liberals missed memo on housing, Sept. 26
Carolyn Whitzman makes a number of interesting points about the housing crisis. However, without legislation to curb the rise of house prices, we are not going to solve the affordable housing crisis. Government units might help, but then we have the Jamesville fiasco to consider. More relevant, I think, would be a legislated curb on how much the value of a house can increase year by year - something related to the "rent control" idea. A neighbour told me recently that her bank had increased the value of her house by $300,000 from one year to the next. How can we let that happen? Yes, we all like "more" and that is why we need legislation to save us from this very human tendency to "always want more."
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
Trump comes off as desperate
I am so sad and disgusted in what Donald Trump has done to the Haitian legal residents in Springfield, Ohio. What a desperate despot. I cant believe anyone would vote for him. I'm dumbfounded he has any supporters.
Jann Hanson, Hamilton
Carbon tax is moral posturing
Re: Poilievre and Singh don't like the Liberal climate plan. So what's theirs? Sept. 21
The question the benighted Spectator dare not allow to be asked on its pages is: why bother? The fact that the Spectator dare not allow to be stated on its pages is: that Canada accounts for only 1.5 per cent of world CO2 emissions.
Asia, which includes India and China, is responsible for more than 60 per cent of global CO2 emissions, and India and China aren't playing the CO2 reduction game. Canada's contribution is insignificant; we're not the problem and aren't the solution.
The U.S., China, India, Germany and Australia are a few trading partners that have no carbon tax. By having one, we disadvantage our producers and exporters. Canada's carbon tax represents nothing but a stage for moral posturing; it has no effect whatsoever on weather, climate, or the growth of CO2 in the atmosphere. Canada's carbon tax can, with the safety of the world in mind, be scrapped without replacement.
Vincent Curtis, Calgary
Put tax dollars into health care
The first step in less for them (read Ford's developer friends) and more for you (the taxpayers of this province) is to cancel the unneeded and unwanted Highway 413, and spend the billions on health care. Doug Ford needs to go.
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
CAPTION: City councillors will soon begin considering early numbers for next year's budget.

Subject: Councils; Carbon; Housing prices; Cultural heritage; Tax increases; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing prices Tax increases Environmental tax

Location: China; Canada; India

Document 450 of 500

NDP hasn't ruled out getting Jama back: MPP kicked out for insubordination, legislature censure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-hasnt-ruled-out-getting-jama-back/docview/3110234467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+hasn%27t+ruled+out+getting+Jama+back%3A+MPP+kicked+out+for+insubordination%2C+legislature+censure&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: She was kicked out of the New Democrat caucus at Queen's Park for insubordination last fall, but the party has left the door open for Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama to be its candidate in next provincial election.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles confirmed Wednesday that the party's executive is preparing an analysis on "electoral implications" involving Jama, who has been sitting as an Independent and has stated publicly she plans to seek re-election as one.
"We'll discuss it when we get it," Stiles said of the report, which would consider factors such as campaign finances and support.
The exercise is "not really anything out of the ordinary," Stiles added. "We're looking at what the potential is for different electoral outcomes."
When asked specifically, she did not rule out the possibility that Jama could be the party's candidate in the riding previously held by former NDP leader Andrea Horwath, now mayor of Hamilton.
"We will be running an NDP candidate in every seat, as is our responsibility to the people of Ontario and to our members," Stiles said.
She later noted the prospect of Jama rejoining the New Democrat caucus now is "not something that we're discussing at this time."
An NDP source, speaking on background to discuss internal deliberations, told the Star that it is up to the Hamilton Centre riding association and its members to hold a nomination meeting and vote on a candidate, but added that Jama is "not under consideration now to be the NDP candidate." No date has been set for a nomination meeting, at which "the outcome could be anything," the source said.
Jama did not reply Wednesday to messages from the Star asking whether she would consider returning to the NDP after Stiles ejected her from its caucus for "acting unilaterally too many times" and "undermining our work."
At issue was a statement Jama made about the Israel-Hamas war that prompted the legislature to censure her, meaning she is not recognized to speak in the chamber until she apologizes - which she has refused to do. Jama's statement focused on the plight of Palestinians living under Israeli occupation.
The NDP has two MPPs in Hamilton, but one of them, Monique Taylor (Hamilton Mountain), has signalled her intention to seek the party's nomination for the next federal election, potentially leaving that riding vulnerable.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama has stated publicly she plans to seek re-election.Christopher Katsarov The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 452 of 500

Wedge politics trump planning

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wedge-politics-trump-planning/docview/3110234400/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wedge+politics+trump+planning&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When you hear Doug Ford talking up a tunnel under the 401, keep your eyes on the road.
When you see the premier declaring war on bike paths, look where he's going.
Ford is cheerfully driving a wedge on the campaign trail ahead.
Pining for an early election, the premier is pouncing on the most divisive issues of the day. Not just to rally the Tory base, but to reel in suburbia.
As Ford goes fishing for votes, many in the media are taking the bait. His most righteous critics underestimate his right-wing crusade at their peril.
Apart from battling the so-called war on the car, Ford is targeting homeless and jobless people in encampments. He is also mounting a pincer movement against addicts in needle exchanges. But beware the premier's populist assault on that most polarizing of debates, the urban cycling path. Ford knows a winning wedge when he sees one.
He's made a career of making the car king, first as a Toronto city hall councillor who helped bend rapid transit lines out of shape (and over budget) to protect those precious road lanes. Now, as an omnipotent premier, he wants to bar municipal bike lanes from riding roughshod over car lanes.
Next month, he will use his majority muscle to legislate against any democratically elected city council from prioritizing bike lanes over car congestion. It's a breathtaking U-turn for a premier who insisted until recently that he dare not impose his will upon cities when it comes to increased housing density.
It's "not up to the province to dictate to the other municipalities - no one knows their communities better than municipalities," Ford argued just the other day. Never mind. Inconsistency and hypocrisy never stopped politicians from telling voters what they think they want to hear.
This is about provincial politics, not urban planning.
Bike lanes are Rorschach blotter tests. Where some people see safe spaces for cyclists, others see only road rage from motorists.
Pedallers, politicians and (some) pundits will tell you breathlessly that bike lanes are things of beauty and safety, a way to get people out of cars just like in Amsterdam. If you build it, they will come cycle. Critics, commuters and (some) columnists will counter that seasonal bike lanes are nonsensical in winter, that not everyone can afford to live downtown close to their workplace.
One can support the goodness of bike lanes while still acknowledging that you can have too much of a good thing, with diminishing returns. Bike lanes are not always in a good place. For example, Ford singled out those oversized bike lanes that flank the grand thoroughfare that is University Avenue, clogging traffic and slowing ambulances in front of all those hospitals.
Those extra-wide lanes are often empty, while other north-south bike routes cover much the same ground - which makes for peculiar urban planning. My point isn't that Ford is right - it's still a wrong-headed wedge. But there's lots that's wrong with the way municipalities lay out those lanes.
By trash-talking bike paths and talking up underground tunnels, Ford is carefully laying the groundwork for the campaign trail to come. But with so much political mileage under his belt, he has learned how to play the game better than ever.
What makes this a winning wedge is that the more Ford's pedal power critics mock him, the more they play into his hands. That's politics.
Where once upon a time he crowned the car as undisputed king, he now purports to share the stage - or tunnel - with rapid transit. Proclaiming his blue sky idea to dig under the 401 this week, the premier promised to save space for subway cars alongside regular cars. The problem with Ford's tunnel vision is that it's both unfathomable and unfunded.
Like previous multibillion-dollar highways to political heaven, proffered by the premier in the last election, this latest proposal is wholly unaccounted for in the budget.
Not so much a work in progress as words for politics. In other words, wedges.
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.;

Document 453 of 500

Conservative MP denies accusations of homophobia after heckling Justin Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/conservative-mp-denies-accusations-homophobia/docview/3110234700/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservative+MP+denies+accusations+of+homophobia+after+heckling+Justin+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A Conservative MP is rejecting allegations that he made a homophobic statement in the House of Commons, claiming he "wasn't thinking about sex at all" when he shouted at the prime minister about a Canadian diplomat holding meetings in a luxury bathtub.
The explanation from Alberta's Garnett Genuis came during yet another extraordinary scene in a minority Parliament swarming with animosity and insults that saw several MPs challenge the chamber's already-embattled Speaker, Liberal MP Greg Fergus.
Rising for the first time to address the allegations on Thursday, Genuis said he is facing "false and defamatory" claims over a comment he heckled at Prime Minister Justin Trudeau during question period one day earlier, provoking Trudeau to declare he's "used to casual homophobic comments from the other side of the House."
The exchange occurred after Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre criticized the government's purchase of a $9-million condominium for Canada's consul general in New York, which the Tories allege is an extravagant waste of money. In making that case, Poilievre read a description of the condo that included a reference to a copper bathtub. When Trudeau responded by talking about "engaging with international leaders" on climate change and other issues, Genuis - reading Thursday from the official record of the proceedings - said that he shouted, "Does he engage with them in the bathtub?"
With other MPs yelling over Genuis, Fergus interrupted him three times and urged other parliamentarians to let him continue.
"We've heard a few words said in this house about bullying. I'm trying to provide an explanation with context and to answer false allegations that have been made against me, and I will persist in doing that," Genuis said.
"The point of that comment is to illustrate that, of course, meetings don't take place in a bathtub ... A luxury bathtub has nothing to do with meetings," he added.
"It had nothing to do with sex. I wasn't thinking about sex at all."
NDP MP Charlie Angus, whose party has asked Fergus to review the exchange , immediately questioned why Genuis wasn't apologizing. He also questioned why Fergus, as the arbiter of debate and decorum in the House, gave the Conservative MP so much time to defend himself.
"I just want to understand that the Speaker's saying that a Conservative member can make a homosexual slur against the prime minister of the country and it's OK, and he can defend himself for a good 10 minutes," Angus said.
"We would like to know that that's the standard that this Speaker is bringing, because it very much clarifies where we go from here."
Rob Oliphant, a Liberal MP from Toronto who is gay, then stood up to criticize Genuis's explanation.
"It is a homophobic slur," Oliphant said, adding that he views another comment made during the condo exchange - when an unidentified MP shouted about whether the consul general gets the "top bunk" - in the same way.
"They were both absolutely personal, and if the consul general in New York were a woman, if she was treated that way in this House, this House would be outraged," he said. "Every member of this House should be outraged."
Fergus then addressed the House to say he would consider the issue, but that the situation shows MPs shouldn't "speak out of turn," which would help avoid instances "where there are different interpretations as to what is said."
Moments before the hubbub over Genuis , Fergus ruled that Poilievre had not followed the Speaker's instruction to withdraw disparaging comments the Conservative leader made about NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh during a tense exchange last week.
In a ruling released Thursday, Fergus said Poilievre's words - he called Singh "a fake, a phoney and a fraud" - were "excessively scornful and personal." Fergus said it is also "unacceptable for a member to leave their seat and move towards another member," as Singh did during the exchange, sparking questions this week about whether he was willing to fight the Conservative leader.
Fergus's ruling said Singh, responding to his request to "make amends," informed the Speaker that "he will act differently in the future." But Poilievre did not do the same, the ruling said, and then did not formally withdraw the insults he had levelled at Singh.
Fergus then deducted three questions from Poilievre during the question period on Thursday, a decision that Liberal MP Kevin Lamoureux challenged as insufficient.
It was not the first time Fergus has been criticized for his actions as Speaker. In the year since he was elected, Fergus has faced rounds of criticism from opposition parties over what they characterized as partisan activity. Last year, they called for his resignation after he appeared in a video, dressed in his Speaker's robes, to praise the outgoing interim leader of the Ontario Liberal Party. Fergus later apologized for the video and repaid the parliamentary resources used to make it.
In May, Fergus faced renewed calls to step down from the Conservatives and Bloc Québécois, who said they lost confidence in his impartiality over how he publicized an event for his Liberal riding association with partisan language that attacked Poilievre.
Fergus's office said the language was used by mistake.
Meanwhile, the broader parliamentary debate Thursday was again dominated by Conservative attempts to declare non-confidence in the Liberal minority government and trigger an election. After the Bloc and NDP voted to save the government in a confidence vote Wednesday, Poilievre immediately tabled another motion to set up a second make-or-break vote for Trudeau's Liberals in the coming days.
The next federal election is scheduled for the fall of 2025, unless the government falls before then.
CAPTION: Conservative MP Garnett Genuis speaks in the House of Commons on Sept. 26, 2024.

Document 454 of 500

Sept. 26: Premier's victim blaming is shameful, Hamilton's LRT project is doomed and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-26-premiers-victim-blaming-is-shameful/docview/3109935320/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+26%3A+Premier%27s+victim+blaming+is+shameful%2C+Hamilton%27s+LRT+project+is+doomed+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Victim blaming is shameful
Re: Doug Ford scolded for telling homeless people to get jobs, Sept. 24
I should not be surprised anymore by what comes put of the premier's mouth, but his latest remarks to homeless people are truly horrible. A job would definitely help, but supports to help homeless people get treatment for addiction and mental health would be much better. Washroom and laundry facilities for homeless people to clean up and be presentable for interviews would be much better. Minimum-wage jobs will not cover rent, food and utilities. Being fed so you can concentrate for an interview would be a big help. Affordable housing - as not everyone was born into a family of wealth - would solve the problem. Victim blaming is shameful.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton
LRT is a doomed project
Re: LRT delays force city to consider road fixes that will only be 'torn up again' later, Sept. 24
Today's article about delayed road repairs and development projects along the proposed LRT route is yet another in a series of omens indicating this ridiculous project must be cancelled. Clearly the provincial government knows it will likely cost at least a billion dollars more than the $3.4 billion pledged in 2021 and are not prepared to pony up any additional funds. Metrolinx seems to have been advised to stay mum on providing any construction updates, and we all know the citizens of Hamilton have no appetite or budget to invest anything. If it has not been painfully obvious already that this is a doomed project, it should be now.
David Shupe, Dundas
Follow Alberta's lead on LRT
The city of Calgary just shut down construction of the proposed Green Line LRT. The cost to date is $2.2 billion and no track laid. The budget for a shortened 10-km line escalated to over $6 billion. The project was deemed not viable if Calgary taxpayers had to pay for it. The provincial and federal government promised $3.4 billion to complete a 14-km Hamilton LRT over three years ago. The province of Alberta pulled the plug on Calgary's project; will upper levels of government do the same to the ill-conceived Hamilton LRT? The Calgary line was last budgeted at $6.2 billion for 10 kilometres. The Hamilton budget likely should be $8.5 billion. That's just a budget; wait until the real numbers come in.
Hamilton taxpayers cannot afford this project. Millions have already been spent by the city and Metrolinx, and the spending continues. This needs to stop now.
Gary Aikema, Flamborough
A regressive, dangerous policy
Re: Doug Ford's war on the bike continues, Sept. 24
Premier Doug Ford has adopted the American practice of attempting to disguise and "sell" legislation through simplistic, inaccurate and misleading naming. The most recent example is the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," which restricts the construction of bike lanes where motor vehicle lanes would be reduced. Ford's legislation will have the opposite effect. Strategies that facilitate cycling and pedestrian traffic, as well as public transit, divert drivers to means of transportation that reduce the number of cars that would otherwise occupy space on roadways.
As Margaret Shkimba notes, this "war" on cycling is regressive and dangerous, evidenced by the number of cyclist fatalities in encounters with motor vehicles. Portraying bike lanes and cycling as a cause of gridlock stokes the growing antipathy toward cyclists. I am a responsible, cautious and rule-abiding rider who uses a bike as an alternative to my car, whose safety has been enhanced by the measures implemented to separate cyclists from drivers. Nonetheless, I have been the subject of unprovoked profanity from drivers who yell at me as they speed past, angered, it appears, by my mere presence. Misrepresentation and misinformation contribute to an unnecessarily adversarial, and potentially perilous, relationship between drivers and cyclists.
Dayna Firth, Hamilton
Not a fan of politicians
Re: Poilievre makes case for taking down the government to restore 'promise of Canada,' Sept. 24
I've been around on this Earth for seven decades-plus. When are people going to realize that politicians lie? In the infamous words of Harry Truman, "I'd rather have a one-party system with politicians that aren't corrupt than a democracy with rich, corrupt ones." They all lie. It doesn't matter which party you vote for, the outcome is the same. We live in an imperfect society. Please think about your negative comments regarding Canada's politicians, it just doesn't help the situation. Let's get back to talking about the weather!
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
CAPTION: "I should not be surprised anymore by what comes put of the premier's mouth, but his latest remarks to homeless people are truly horrible," a reader writes.

Document 455 of 500

The story behind a massive deal that will transform the west Mountain brow lands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/story-behind-massive-deal-that-will-transform/docview/3109935226/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+story+behind+a+massive+deal+that+will+transform+the+west+Mountain+brow+lands&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mohawk College orchestrated the massive deal for Hamilton's brow lands that will see the school get back part of the property it lost when the province backed out of an agreement to sell it for campus expansion.
"The ability to access that land to give us future capacity is pretty important in our longer-term planning," Mohawk College president Paul Armstrong told The Spectator. "I think what I'm most proud of is the role we played in actually bringing together the group of people that are going to enable the entire project."
As reported exclusively by The Spectator, the former surplus provincial hospital land at 290 Fennell Ave. W. has been sold in a multi-party deal involving Mohawk College, long-term care operator Schlegel Villages, non-profit social housing group Indwell and a yet-to-be chosen housing developer.
The deal has not yet been publicly announced, but land registry documents obtained by The Spectator show a lands transfer to Schlegel Villages for about $24.3 million on Aug. 29. The total value of the development project is not stated - but a $350-million charge was registered on the property with the province at the same time as the sale.
Both Schlegel Villages and Mohawk say it was the college that was the main driver behind the deal for the redevelopment of the land that used to house Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital.
The school - needing to ensure it got at least part of the property for campus expansion - approached the industry giant when it was clear that the Progressive Conservative government wanted a large long-term care home built at the site.
"When they raised the prospect of this site and working together on it, it was an easy yes," said company president and CEO James Schlegel. "There was a very good, nice relationship with the college that made it very easy to have the next conversation about how we could work together."
It is the second time Mohawk College has put together a deal for the 28.8-acre property across from its campus at Fennell Avenue West and West Fifth Street.
The former Liberal government approved an agreement with the city and the college just one month before being ousted in the June 2018 provincial election. That deal would have seen the college buy the land for expansion and, in return, Mohawk would restore historic-but-deteriorating Century Manor. The sale proceeds of about $9.5 million would have been used to build affordable housing at a planned 20-storey development at 191 York Blvd. that never happened.
However, Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government backed out of the deal in October 2018 to make way for a long-term care home and residential development on the brow lands. A controversial minister's zoning order (MZO) was used, allowing Ontario's minister of municipal affairs and housing to override local zoning.
When the province put the brow lands up for sale in 2022, Mohawk leaders knew the college had to be part of any new plan for the site.
"I need to think about what is Mohawk going to need in five, 10, 15 or 20 years," Armstrong said. "It's long-term planning for Mohawk."
The college already worked with Schlegel Villages, including campuses at the Village of Wentworth Heights in Hamilton and the Village of Tansley Woods in Burlington, where a number of different health professions train at the company's long-term care homes and retirement neighbourhoods.
"We had very strong relationships already," Armstrong said. "I went to Schlegel and said, 'Is this something we could work with you on?'"
The request to partner with Mohawk on the brow lands came as Schlegel Villages was seeking a new home for Hamilton Continuing Care because its aging building at 125 Wentworth St. S. was not feasible to continue using as a long-term care home in the future.
"We were looking for a site in Hamilton to redevelop the long-term care beds that we have at Hamilton Continuing Care in the core of the city," Schlegel said. "Our partners at Mohawk had an interest in those lands to expand their main campus ... so it was a fairly natural next step in the conversation."
In addition, Schlegel saw benefit to locating a seniors' village next to a hospital, which is why the company has chosen the east side of the site next to the West Fifth Campus of St. Joseph's Healthcare to build two long-term care homes with a combined total of 512 beds, a retirement home, independent apartments and amenities like stores, a hair salon, a library or a community centre.
Mohawk College will take the west part of the property, including the Beckfield building. Land registry documents show a transaction in late August that transfers some of the land for $7.3 million from Schlegel to Mohawk.
Around the centre of the site, Indwell will restore Century Manor that was part of the original Hamilton Asylum built in 1875. The deteriorating Victorian Gothic manor that has been closed to the public since 1995 will become affordable housing.
"We're very, very excited about the opportunity to find a creative reuse of the Century Manor building," Schlegel said. "We know how important that building has been to the local community and seeing it preserved, recognizing the heritage value of that building and connecting to the past."
In addition, Schlegel will donate the Hamilton Continuing Care building to Indwell for affordable housing once the long-term care home's 64 beds have been moved to the brow lands. Indwell will also get a second downtown property for affordable housing that is currently owned by Mohawk.
Under the deal, the city gets control over the escarpment itself for a $2 fee.
Lastly, a yet-to-be-found developer will build multistorey housing that could be as high as 18 storeys on the north-end of the property at the Mountain brow.
"At the end of the day, I think we architected an opportunity for Mohawk for our future expansion," Armstrong said. "But I think we've created a really great opportunity for this community. It's going to meet needs around general housing. It's going to create one of the biggest long-term care homes in Hamilton ... Indwell is going to build affordable housing within Century Manor. Century Manor is going to be preserved and celebrated."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: James Schlegel, CEO of Schlegel Villages, said "it was an easy yes," when Mohawk approached the industry giant about partnering on the brow lands.Mohawk College president Paul Armstrong is most proud of "the role we played in actually bringing together the group of people that are going to enable the entire project."Mohawk College was the main driver behind the deal for the redevelopment of the surplus provincial land that used to house Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital.

Title: The story behind a massive deal that will transform the west Mountain brow lands

Document 456 of 500

letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3109646270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Hamilton taxpayers cannot afford this project. Millions have already been spent by the city and Metrolinx, and the spending continues. This needs to stop now.
Gary Aikema, Flamborough
A regressive, dangerous policy
Re: Doug Ford's war on the bike continues, Sept. 24
Premier Doug Ford has adopted the American practice of attempting to disguise and "sell" legislation through simplistic, inaccurate and misleading naming. The most recent example is the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," which restricts the construction of bike lanes where motor vehicle lanes would be reduced. Ford's legislation will have the opposite effect. Strategies that facilitate cycling and pedestrian traffic, as well as public transit, divert drivers to means of transportation that reduce the number of cars that would otherwise occupy space on roadways.
As Margaret Shkimba notes, this "war" on cycling is regressive and dangerous, evidenced by the number of cyclist fatalities in encounters with motor vehicles. Portraying bike lanes and cycling as a cause of gridlock stokes the growing antipathy toward cyclists. I am a responsible, cautious and rule-abiding rider who uses a bike as an alternative to my car, whose safety has been enhanced by the measures implemented to separate cyclists from drivers. Nonetheless, I have been the subject of unprovoked profanity from drivers who yell at me as they speed past, angered, it appears, by my mere presence.
Misrepresentation and misinformation contribute to an unnecessarily adversarial, and potentially perilous, relationship between drivers and cyclists.
Dayna Firth, Hamilton
Not a fan of politicians
Re: Poilievre makes case for taking down the government to restore 'promise of Canada,' Sept. 24
I've been around on this Earth for seven decades-plus. When are people going to realize that politicians lie? In the infamous words of Harry Truman, "I'd rather have a one-party system with politicians that aren't corrupt than a democracy with rich, corrupt ones." They all lie. It doesn't matter which party you vote for, the outcome is the same. We live in an imperfect society. Please think about your negative comments regarding Canada's politicians, it just doesn't help the situation. Let's get back to talking about the weather!
Catherine Castellan, Hannon

Document 457 of 500

The story behind a massive deal that will transform the west Mountain brow lands: Mohawk College worked behind the scenes to make sure it got the land it needed for campus expansion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/story-behind-massive-deal-that-will-transform/docview/3109645792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+story+behind+a+massive+deal+that+will+transform+the+west+Mountain+brow+lands%3A+Mohawk+College+worked+behind+the+scenes+to+make+sure+it+got+the+land+it+needed+for+campus+expansion&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mohawk College orchestrated the massive deal for Hamilton's brow lands that will see the school get back part of the property it lost when the province backed out of an agreement to sell it for campus expansion.
"The ability to access that land to give us future capacity is pretty important in our longer-term planning," Mohawk College president Paul Armstrong told The Spectator. "I think what I'm most proud of is the role we played in actually bringing together the group of people that are going to enable the entire project."
As reported exclusively by The Spectator, the former surplus provincial hospital land at 290 Fennell Ave. W. has been sold in a multi-party deal involving Mohawk College, long-term-care operator Schlegel Villages, non-profit social housing group Indwell and a yet-to-be chosen housing developer.
The deal has not yet been publicly announced, but land registry documents obtained by The Spectator show a lands transfer to Schlegel Villages for about $24.3 million on Aug. 29. The total value of the development project is not stated - but a $350-million charge was registered on the property with the province at the same time as the sale.
Both Schlegel Villages and Mohawk say it was the college that was the main driver behind the deal for the redevelopment of the land that used to house Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital.
The school - needing to ensure it got at least part of the property for campus expansion - approached the industry giant when it was clear that the Progressive Conservative government wanted a large long-term care home built at the site.
"When they raised the prospect of this site and working together on it, it was an easy yes," said company president and CEO James Schlegel. "There was a very good, nice relationship with the college that made it very easy to have the next conversation about how we could work together."
It is the second time Mohawk College has put together a deal for the 28.8-acre property across from its campus at Fennell Avenue West and West Fifth Street.
The former Liberal government approved an agreement with the city and the college just one month before being ousted in the June 2018 provincial election. That deal would have seen the college buy the land for expansion and, in return, Mohawk would restore historic-but-deteriorating Century Manor. The sale proceeds of about $9.5 million would have been used to build affordable housing at a planned 20-storey development at 191 York Blvd. that never happened.
However, Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government backed out of the deal in October 2018 to make way for a long-term care home and residential development on the brow lands. A controversial minister's zoning order (MZO) was used, allowing Ontario's minister of municipal affairs and housing to override local zoning.
When the province put the brow lands up for sale in 2022, Mohawk leaders knew the college had to be part of any new plan for the site.
"I need to think about what is Mohawk going to need in five, 10, 15 or 20 years," Armstrong said. "It's long-term planning for Mohawk."
The college already worked with Schlegel Villages, including campuses at the Village of Wentworth Heights in Hamilton and the Village of Tansley Woods in Burlington, where a number of different health professions train at the company's long-term care homes and retirement neighbourhoods.
"We had very strong relationships already," Armstrong said. "I went to Schlegel and said, 'Is this something we could work with you on?'"
The request to partner with Mohawk on the brow lands came as Schlegel Villages was seeking a new home for Hamilton Continuing Care because its aging building at 125 Wentworth St. S. was not feasible to continue using as a long-term care home in the future.
"We were looking for a site in Hamilton to redevelop the long-term care beds that we have at Hamilton Continuing Care in the core of the city," Schlegel said. "Our partners at Mohawk had an interest in those lands to expand their main campus ... so it was a fairly natural next step in the conversation."
In addition, Schlegel saw benefit to locating a seniors' village next to a hospital, which is why the company has chosen the east side of the site next to the West Fifth Campus of St. Joseph's Healthcare to build two long-term care homes with a combined total of 512 beds, a retirement home, independent apartments and amenities like stores, a hair salon, a library or a community centre.
Mohawk College will take the west part of the property, including the Beckfield building. Land registry documents show a transaction in late August that transfers some of the land for $7.3 million from Schlegel to Mohawk.
Around the centre of the site, Indwell will restore Century Manor that was part of the original Hamilton Asylum built in 1875. The deteriorating Victorian Gothic manor that has been closed to the public since 1995 will become affordable housing.
"We're very, very excited about the opportunity to find a creative reuse of the Century Manor building," Schlegel said. "We know how important that building has been to the local community and seeing it preserved, recognizing the heritage value of that building and connecting to the past."
In addition, Schlegel will donate the Hamilton Continuing Care building to Indwell for affordable housing once the long-term-care home's 64 beds have been moved to the brow lands. Indwell will also get a second downtown property for affordable housing that is currently owned by Mohawk.
Under the deal, the city gets control over the escarpment itself for a $2 fee.
Lastly, a yet-to-be-found developer will build multistorey housing that could be as high as 18 storeys on the north end of the property at the Mountain brow.
"At the end of the day, I think we architected an opportunity for Mohawk for our future expansion," Armstrong said. "But I think we've created a really great opportunity for this community. It's going to meet needs around general housing. It's going to create one of the biggest long-term-care homes in Hamilton ... Indwell is going to build affordable housing within Century Manor. Century Manor is going to be preserved and celebrated."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mohawk College was the main driver behind the deal for the redevelopment of the surplus provincial land that was once home to the Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital.Paul Armstrong, Mohawk College president James Schlegel, CEO of Schlegel VillagesHamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
