Ford wants tunnel under Highway 401

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-wants-tunnel-under-highway-401/docview/3109645741/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+wants+tunnel+under+Highway+401&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is exploring the idea of digging a massive tunnel under Highway 401 to move traffic and transit across the Toronto area, with Premier Doug Ford pledging to build it regardless of what a feasibility study says.
A beaming Ford had high hopes for his vision as he announced the plan Wednesday.
"It will be one of the world's longest tunnels," he said at a news conference in Toronto alongside Highway 401.
Ford provided no cost estimates or timeline for the project.
"The reason we're having a feasibility study is it's going to determine the length," he said.
"If they're telling me, 30 kilometres is x, 40 kilometres is y, and 70 kilometres or 60 kilometres is another cost, let's take a look at it. That will determine the length of this tunnel and that's why we're doing the feasibility study. But we're going to get the job done, mark my words."
Ford said the project could stretch from Mississauga in the west to Markham in the east.
"It's something that you have to be a visionary," he said. "You have to look at the cost of $11 billion a year it's costing our economy, not to mention how do you put time on people's lives that are stuck in traffic?"
The closest Ford came to providing a cost estimate Wednesday was that he doesn't think the project would cost "hundreds of billions of dollars."
The closest he came to putting a timeline on the project was that it will be in place by the time he's long gone.
"Well after we're gone, well after, you know, when I'm pushing up tulips somewhere, that road is going to be around for 75 years, 100 years, the tunnel," he said. "So just put a tulip for me on there somewhere."
The opposition pounced on the tunnel proposal.
"The premier cannot dig himself out of the mess that he has created in our province, not even with a fantasy tunnel," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"This is not a serious proposal. This is not a serious premier. It is a silly thought from a government that has run out of ideas."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner did not mince words.
"The idea that this would ever get built under this (government), let alone be good for traffic at all, is a complete joke," he said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 459 of 500

Homeless are working hard at surviving

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/homeless-are-working-hard-at-surviving/docview/3109645739/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Homeless+are+working+hard+at+surviving&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford, if you are homeless in Ontario, you are always working - working to survive.
People walk dozens of kilometres daily to find water and enough food to eat. They spend hours each day looking for somewhere to relieve themselves, bathe and secure clean, weather-appropriate clothing. They scout endlessly for a safe space to rest out of the elements, although they never get more than a couple of hours of sleep at a time.
People's lives are fully spent meeting their basic needs, and that is the hardest, most relentless work there is.
And yet, even under these exhausting circumstances, many people do have formal employment. As a front-line worker, I've had clients working full-time at Dollarama and No Frills. I've had clients who were lawyers and real estate agents.
I've personally employed dozens of unhoused people at my agencies - including in full-time, living-wage and unionized positions - as eviction prevention and outreach workers in their own communities. Construction site foremen often go to shelters and drop-ins to pick up homeless people as under-the-table labourers, sometimes to build condominiums that they could never afford.
But even understanding that surviving homelessness is work in-itself and that people without homes can also have formal employment, the issue isn't about the canniness of each individual to lift themselves up out of homelessness. The enduring and unchallenged stigma surrounding homelessness means that many employers would never hire you. Many landlords will also refuse to rent to you.
But this also isn't about the attitudes of individual employers or landlords - although anti-homeless bigotry is a serious and under-addressed systemic problem.
Just how is the Ontario government working for homeless people?
If this is about jobs, what jobs?
Ontario's unemployment rate is climbing: at 7.1 per cent, we've surpassed the national average. Moreover, since Ford became premier in 2018, Ontario has experienced a net increase of just 26,500 jobs, despite our population growing by over 1.5 million people.
Last week in Windsor, Premier Ford framed the solution to local encampments as "getting a good-paying job." What he failed to mention is that Windsor's unemployment rate is 9.2 per cent, or that jobs, let alone decent ones, are scarce across the province.
If this is about housing, what housing?
Ford's plan to build 1.5 million homes by 2031 requires 150,000 new housing starts every year. Yet, in June 2023, there were just 10,114 new homes started in Ontario - less than 10 per cent of what the government itself has mandated. This year, the number of provincial housing starts dropped to just 5,681. And this doesn't even address what kind of housing is being built and who can afford it, let alone the fact someone must live in a completed home - not a housing start.
While the Ford government fails on both employment and housing, it also fails in its homelessness response. There is no provincial homelessness strategy. Provincial funding for homelessness is based on outdated statistics and historical funding levels, not on actual population need.
In some cases, Ford has even delayed the release of crucial funding for homelessness programs without any reason or recourse. And, of course, the province is also failing to reform the laws surrounding rent increases, evictions, the rates of social assistance - in fact, 59,100 homeless Ontarians are on Ontario Works and the Ontario Disability Support Program - and a slew of other policy factors that impact whether people can access, and keep, housing.
In combination, these are egregious political choices, given that the ministry knows 234,000 Ontarians are actively or at high risk of homelessness. They can't all pick themselves up by the bootstraps, as Ford would have it. Again, if this were really about jobs, it should be abundantly clear who is flagrantly failing to do theirs: Doug Ford and his government.
Diana Chan McNally is a front line worker supporting
homeless people.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Diana Chan-McNally Diana Chan McNally is a front line worker supporting homeless people.;

'He's been wasting his time:' Liberal MPs slam Doug Ford's stand on bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hes-been-wasting-his-time-liberal-mps-slam-doug/docview/3110033348/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27He%27s+been+wasting+his+time%3A%27+Liberal+MPs+slam+Doug+Ford%27s+stand+on+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Premier Doug Ford's push to limit the construction of bike lanes in Ontario municipalities is facing criticism from some Liberal cabinet ministers and MPs.
Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi, a former Ontario attorney general, told the Star on Wednesday the controversial plan was an "unacceptable" distraction from a premier who "doesn't want to do his job."
"Instead of doing his job and making sure that we deliver health care in a way that actually serve all Ontarians, he's been wasting his time in banning bike lanes," said Naqvi, who ran unsuccessfully for the provincial Liberal leadership last year.
"That's going to jeopardize further safety of a lot of bike users in our province," he said.
It's the latest criticism levied against the upcoming legislation that if passed would block construction of new bike lanes if they result in the loss of lanes used by cars, buses, trucks and other traffic. It's one of several initiatives the Ontario government is looking at in an effort to ease gridlock. The plan, which requires overriding municipal powers, was immediately slammed in Toronto, where six cyclists have been killed so far this year.
Federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, who has previously butted heads with Ford over provincial plans to build Highway 413, said the decision is "difficult to understand."
"As we've seen in many cities around the world, (bike lanes) don't impede the traffic, they help it," he told reporters in Ottawa. "I mean, if you have more people using bicycles, it uses less space on our roads, you can move more people, good for the environment, good for our health."
Pressed about concerns over the plan earlier this week, Ford defended his government's decision, saying cars and trucks should have full priority on major roads like Toronto's Bloor Street and University Avenue.
"We just want to get traffic moving ... that's what it comes down to, making sure you aren't putting bike lanes in the middle of some of the busiest streets in the country," Ford told reporters Monday.
But the province should not be in the business of telling cities where they can put bike lanes, federal International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen said.
"We have to leave those issues to the municipal government," the Toronto MP said.
With files from Rob Ferguson.
CAPTION: The Ford provincial government is moving to limit municipal powers when it comes to building bike lanes in Ontario's towns and cities.

Subject: Trucks

Document 461 of 500

NDP hasn't ruled out bringing Sarah Jama back into the party for next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-hasnt-ruled-out-bringing-sarah-jama-back-into/docview/3109645780/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+hasn%27t+ruled+out+bringing+Sarah+Jama+back+into+the+party+for+next+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: She was kicked out of the New Democrat caucus at Queen's Park for insubordination last fall, but the party has left the door open for Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama to be its candidate in next provincial election.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles confirmed Wednesday that the party's executive is preparing an analysis on "electoral implications" involving Jama, who has been sitting as an Independent and has stated publicly she plans to seek re-election as one.
"We'll discuss it when we get it," Stiles said of the report, which would consider factors such as campaign finances and support levels.
The exercise is "not really anything out of the ordinary," Stiles added. "We're looking at what the potential is for different electoral outcomes."
When asked specifically, she did not rule out the possibility that Jama could be the party's candidate in the riding previously held by former NDP leader Andrea Horwath, now mayor of Hamilton.
"We will be running an NDP candidate in every seat, as is our responsibility to the people of Ontario and to our members," Stiles said.
She later noted the prospect of Jama re-joining the New Democrat caucus now is "not something that we're discussing at this time."
An NDP source, speaking on background to discuss internal deliberations, told the Star that it is up to the Hamilton Centre riding association and its members to hold a nomination meeting and vote on a candidate, but added that Jama is "not under consideration now to be the NDP candidate."
No date has been set for a nomination meeting, at which "the outcome could be anything," the source said.
Jama did not reply Wednesday to messages from the Star asking whether she would consider returning to the NDP after Stiles ejected her from its caucus for "acting unilaterally too many times" and "undermining our work."
At issue was a statement Jama made about the Israel-Hamas war that prompted the legislature to censure her, meaning she is not recognized to speak in the chamber until she apologizes - which she has refused to do.
Jama's statement on her MPP letterhead did not condemn the Oct. 7 surprise attack on Israel by Hamas or the more than 1,200 lives lost, but focused on the plight of Palestinians living under Israeli occupation. It was never retracted as Stiles originally requested. Since then, more than 40,000 Palestinians have been killed in the conflict.
The political calculus involving Jama is tricky for the New Democrats, said Peter Graefe, an associate professor of political science at McMaster University.
To leave Jama as an Independent could further alienate staunch supporters in the riding who were NDP stalwarts until her ejection, costing the party a local volunteer and donor base that may in turn make it difficult to find a candidate to run against her.
"That's probably part of what was pushing the party leadership to engage in this reflection," said Graefe.
But bringing her back into the NDP fold would risk worsening fissures that developed with some of the party's MPPs last fall, when several felt it took Stiles too long to boot Jama from caucus and others were furious at the move.
If the NDP were to run a candidate against Jama, that could split the progressive vote, allowing a Liberal or Progressive Conservative to win the riding, he noted.
The NDP has two MPPs in the Steel City, but one of them, Monique Taylor (Hamilton Mountain), has signalled her intention to seek the party's nomination for the next federal election, potentially leaving that riding vulnerable.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on May 6, 2024.

Subject: Provincial elections; Nominations; Leadership; Palestinian people

Location: Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: NDP hasn't ruled out bringing Sarah Jama back into the party for next election

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 462 of 500

Doug Ford's tunnel talk and war on bike lanes are about wedge politics, not urban planning

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tunnel-talk-war-on-bike-lanes-are/docview/3109645735/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+tunnel+talk+and+war+on+bike+lanes+are+about+wedge+politics%2C+not+urban+planning&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When you hear Doug Ford talking up a tunnel under the 401, keep your eyes on the road.
When you see the premier declaring war on bike paths, look where he's going.
Ford is cheerfully driving a wedge on the campaign trail ahead.
Pining for an early election, the premier is pouncing on the most divisive issues of the day. Not just to rally the Tory base, but to reel in suburbia.
As Ford goes fishing for votes, many in the media are taking the bait. His most righteous critics underestimate his right-wing crusade at their peril.
Let us count his wedges of the week(s):
Apart from battling the so-called war on the car, Ford is targeting homeless and jobless people in encampments. He is also mounting a pincer movement against addicts in needle exchanges.
But beware the premier's populist assault on that most polarizing of debates, the urban cycling path. Ford knows a winning wedge when he sees one.
He's made a career of making the car king, first as a city hall councillor who helped bend rapid transit lines out of shape (and over budget) to protect those precious road lanes. Now, as an omnipotent premier, he wants to bar municipal bike lanes from riding roughshod over car lanes.
Next month, he will use his majority muscle to legislate against any democratically elected city council from prioritizing bike lanes over car congestion. It's a breathtaking U-turn for a premier who insisted until recently that he dare not impose his will upon cities when it comes to increased housing density.
It's "not up to the province to dictate to the other municipalities - no one knows their communities better than municipalities," Ford argued just the other day.
Never mind. Inconsistency and hypocrisy never stopped politicians from telling voters what they think they want to hear.
This is about provincial politics, not urban planning. The premier isn't a world class planner, but his track record suggests he knows a thing or two about voter psychology.
Bike lanes are Rorschach blotter tests. Where some people see safe spaces for cyclists, others see only road rage from motorists.
It's like the fight over off-leash dog parks. Everyone thinks they're an expert on bike lane appropriation or doggy do.
Pedallers, politicians and (some) pundits will tell you breathlessly that bike lanes are things of beauty and safety, a way to get people out of cars just like in Amsterdam. If you build it, they will come cycle.
Critics, commuters and (some) columnists will counter that seasonal bike lanes are nonsensical in winter, that not everyone can afford to live downtown close to their workplace.
Full disclosure - I'm a (fair weather) commuting cyclist and columnist. I love bike paths.
But that doesn't blind me to their shortcomings. For it should be possible for a columnist to hold two opposing views (even if not all readers want to hear them).
One can support the goodness of bike lanes while still acknowledging that you can have too much of a good thing, with diminishing returns. Bike lanes are not always in a good place.
For example, Ford singled out those oversized bike lanes that flank the grand thoroughfare that is University Avenue, clogging traffic and slowing ambulances in front of all those hospitals. Those extra-wide lanes are often empty, while other north-south bike routes cover much the same ground - which makes for peculiar urban planning.
My point isn't that Ford is right - it's still a wrong-headed wedge. But there's lots that's wrong with the way municipalities lay out those lanes, often without any serious afterthoughts from the same people who are piling on Ford now.
For all the rhetoric about making safety a priority, why can't the city grapple with the perils of overpowered e-bikes, outfitted with pretend pedals that are never used, screaming past regular cyclists? Why do supposedly progressive politicians and city planners persist in protecting the on-street parking spots that flank bike lanes, taking up precious space while imperiling people?
Can't they pick a lane?
As for Ford, by trash-talking bike paths and talking up underground tunnels, he is carefully laying the groundwork for the campaign trail to come. But with so much political mileage under his belt, he has learned how to play the game better than ever.
What makes this a winning wedge is that the more Ford's pedal power critics mock him, the more they play into his hands. That's politics.
Where once upon a time he crowned the car as undisputed king, he now purports to share the stage - or tunnel - with rapid transit. Proclaiming his blue sky idea to dig under the 401 this week, the premier promised to save space for subway cars alongside regular cars.
The problem with Ford's tunnel vision is that it's both unfathomable and unfunded. Like previous multibillion-dollar highways to political heaven, proffered by the premier in the last election, this latest proposal is wholly unaccounted for in the budget.
Not so much a work in progress as words for politics. In other words, wedges.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks in Port Colborne on Sept. 24, 2024.

Subject: Automobiles; Commuting; Local elections; Urban planning; Politics; Politicians; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Document 463 of 500

Trudeau government survives Tory non-confidence vote as Bloc issues new ultimatum

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-survives-tory-non-confidence/docview/3109386309/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+survives+Tory+non-confidence+vote+as+Bloc+issues+new+ultimatum&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau survived a confidence vote but faced a new ultimatum to preserve his minority government on Wednesday, when opposition parties tried to show the Liberals have little scope to feel secure about their grip on power.
Even before the Conservative attempt to trigger an immediate election was defeated, jeers and accusations flew in an at-times chaotic debate in the House of Commons. And Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, gunning hard for a campaign his party fully expects to win, was already brandishing two more proposed non-confidence motions and goading the Bloc Québécois and New Democratic Party to join him in voting down the government.
At the same time, the Bloc came out with a new set of demands for the government to help enact two of the party's proposed laws - including one that could cost billions of dollars - before Oct. 29. Failure to do so means the separatist Bloc would work with the other opposition parties to topple the Trudeau Liberals and plunge Canada into an election this fall, said Bloc Leader Yves-François Blanchet.
The brinksmanship was on display as the Liberal government faced its first non-confidence vote since the NDP pulled out of a parliamentary deal in which it was propping up the Trudeau regime in exchange for action on progressive policies.
With the Bloc, NDP and Greens voting with the Liberal government, the Conservative attempt to declare non-confidence was defeated 211 to 120.
Grinning on the floor of the House of Commons before the vote, Poilievre demanded an immediate "carbon tax election" so his party can get elected to scrap the federal government's consumer carbon pricing regime.
Trudeau shot back that the impending vote, which he ended up winning, would show the House "does not have confidence in the Conservative leader."
Poilievre responded, "He's bragging that he has more politicians on his side. Isn't it the case that in Canada the people decide?"
Moments later, animosity erupted in the House when Trudeau accused an unnamed Conservative MP of making a homophobic statement during a debate over a controversial government-paid residence for Canada's consul general in New York, Tom Clark. After Poilievre referenced an expensive copper tub at the residence, Trudeau alleged another MP heckled him about "sharing a bathtub with Tom Clark." Trudeau said the parliamentarian - whom he did not identify - should take responsibility for the statement.
A Conservative spokesperson denied a Tory MP made that comment. Poilievre's press secretary, Sebastian Skamski, said by email that one of the party's MPs had "rightly questioned" how the tub was necessary for diplomatic meetings.
"We're used to casual homophobic comments from the other side of the House," Trudeau charged.
Shouts erupted from the opposition benches after Trudeau initially refused to withdraw the comment, as instructed by Speaker Greg Fergus. Trudeau said that "standing up to bullies requires us to call them out on their crap."
Fergus intervened again, saying that he heard a comment from the opposition benches but didn't know who said it, and asked Trudeau to withdraw his own statement and be the "better person."
Trudeau then said he withdrew "the comment about defecating" and said he wasn't accusing the entire Conservative caucus of being homophobic. Fergus accepted that and debate continued.
Earlier in the day, the Bloc upped the pressure on the government by declaring it would work to topple the Liberals from power if two of the Bloc's proposed laws aren't passed into law by Oct. 29.
Speaking to reporters, Blanchet said his party is trying to "profit, literally" from the new-found influence it has gained now that the NDP has pulled out of its parliamentary alliance.
One of the Bloc bills, C-319, aims to increase pensions for seniors aged 65 to 74. The other, C-282, would protect the supply management system for dairy farmers from getting diluted in future trade negotiations.
"It's the entire demand in an irrevocable and non-negotiable way," Blanchet said in French, when asked if he would accept only one of those bills becoming law by his deadline.
Blanchet said he chose Oct. 29 since that would leave time to hold an election before the Christmas season, and that it left a reasonable amount of time to adopt both pieces of legislation. The Bloc leader also suggested his party could work with other opposition parties to trigger an election sooner if the Liberal government isn't helping them try to get their two bills passed according to their timeline.
"Between now and Oct. 29, we could come to the conclusion that they have not used the time being offered them to successfully go forward," he said, stating that there has so far been "very little" discussion with the government on the Bloc's priorities.
The Bloc, with 33 seats in the House of Commons, couldn't topple the government alone. It would require the other main opposition parties - the Conservatives and the NDP - to vote together to declare non-confidence in the government.
The Conservatives are adamant that an election should be called now, arguing repeatedly that the Liberal government has fuelled struggles with the cost of living through policies like carbon pricing. But the NDP has suggested it is in no rush to trigger an election and wants to push the government on New Democrats' preferred priorities, including a proposal to cap prices for grocery staples, among other things.
On Wednesday afternoon, Liberal House Leader Karina Gould said she wouldn't negotiate in public, but she is committed to negotiating with other parties to avoid an election.
She also accused the Conservatives of standing alone in their drive to topple the government.
"They are playing games, and I find it actually quite lame that just moments after they have lost that motion (of non-confidence) in the House of Commons, they're putting forward another one," Gould said, referring to Poilievre's plan to stage another confidence vote as early as next week.
Other parties have supported the Bloc bill to boost seniors payouts, C-319. That legislation has passed third reading in the House of Commons, but it requires a "royal recommendation" to advance to become law. The bill also hasn't been yet considered in the Senate.
Last year, the independent Parliamentary Budget Officer estimated that the increased seniors payments would cost more than $16 billion between 2023-24 and 2027-28.
The other law Blanchet wants enacted, C-282, also passed third reading in the House earlier this year with support from all parties. It is now being considered by a Senate committee, having passed two readings in the red chamber.
CAPTION: Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet speaks to reporters on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Sept. 9, 2024.

Document 464 of 500

Sept. 25: COVID tests should be easily accessible, keep students off social media and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-25-covid-tests-should-be-easily-accessible/docview/3109386252/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+25%3A+COVID+tests+should+be+easily+accessible%2C+keep+students+off+social+media+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: COVID tests should be easily accessible
Yesterday, I sent a letter outlining my difficulty in finding valid COVID-19 test kits in Hamilton. Last evening I found an Ontario government website, updated as of Sept. 20, that listed pharmacies with COVID kits accessible from Hamilton. I've now called two of them. Neither had kits. Both had to throw out kits because they had expired. Why? The last ones they had received were distributed by the Ontario government in June 2023. They agreed that kits at any other nearby pharmacies would have surely expired by now. Given the likelihood of new COVID variants spreading rapidly, this is a situation that needs immediate government attention.
Roy Adams, Hamilton
Keep social media out of schools
Re: Public school block on social-media platforms set to roll out soon, Sept. 16
I am very pleased with the decision to ban social media platforms in schools. As a mother of four, I have struggled to keep my children focused and free from distractions like social media. Schools should provide an environment where students receive a full, focused education.
As president of the Ahmadiyya Women Association (Hamilton Mountain), our community strives to engage youth in activities that reduce screen time. If students spend their free time at school on devices, our efforts are undermined.
We should encourage kids to return to meaningful activities like writing, drawing and reading. Many children are surprised at the idea of life without social media, but this is a valuable opportunity for them to rediscover hands-on learning. Thank you, HWDSB, for this important step.
Khalida Iffat, Hamilton
A flaw in the child care system
While there's been a lot of talk, and some action, regarding $10-a-day daycare, no one seems to be addressing the reality of the work day and the school day being different lengths. Parents who must piece together and pay for before-school and after-school care know how stressful this can be.
Marg Heidebrecht, Dundas
There's more to the LRT story
Re: Lets follow Calgary's LRT lead, Sept. 21
Don Hull in his letter urging Hamilton to take Calgary's lead and cancel its LRT project does not provide the whole picture. He fails to mention that Calgary already has an LRT that has been up and running for over 40 years and is considered to be one of the busiest and most successful light rail systems in North America. It consists of two lines, a Red Line and a Blue Line, and the City of Calgary reluctantly pressed pause on construction of a third LRT line known as the Green Line only after the Conservative government in Alberta pulled its funding. In contrast, Ontario's Conservative government is building Hamilton's LRT for the city under the auspices of Metrolinx. Also, Calgary's paused Green Line is not truly dead since the Alberta government recently said it could possibly go ahead with renewed funding after following design modifications. It is surprising that Ross, a former City of Hamilton transportation director, would urge the cancellation of Hamilton's LRT based upon, at best, partial information.
Greg Crone, Caledonia
Transit moving in wrong direction
Mr. Hull, I couldn't agree with you more. Give us bus rapid transit and drop the LRT idea - a potential total debacle. So here we are, years later, still entertaining this useless idea. So much money to be spent on so few. Most of the citizens of Hamilton will not be using the LRT because they don't live anywhere near the proposed routes. Getting rid of lanes of traffic on Main Street and King Street will remove two routes that are the most used in traversing the city. Talk about gridlock! The city better begin buying up as much industrial strength Senokot that they can get their hands on. They will need it in order to relieve the inevitable constipation this city is going to suffer due to the LRT.
Elena Trkulja, Hamilton
Better to spend on more buses
With all that is happening with homeless and infrastructure decline, it's time to cancel this silly LRT that is going to be way over budget! There is nothing this LRT will do that some extra buses can't do!
Arthur Cattafi, Hamilton
Singh right to challenge hecklers
Re: The scary truth behind this viral video of Jagmeet Singh, Sept. 23
Kudos to Luke Lebrun for an excellent article. And kudos to Jagmeet Singh for having the courage to confront the hecklers. The only way to fight such cowards is to vigorously confront their bad manners. The cretins of the far-right have no notion about how to go about instituting change and that protesting is merely one aspect of any social movement. In the immortal words of Saul D. Alinsky: These guys couldn't organize a luncheon let alone a revolution.
Dan Riga, Burlington
Aerial photos tell a story
Re: Hamilton on high: Photographer David Gruggen captures the city from above, Sept. 23
Nice "birds eye view" photo of the city on the front page of Monday's Spec. Unfortunately, all I see are highrise apartments and condos. Where is the downtown core photo? Take that same photo five years from now and all you'll see are highrises and tents.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
We shouldn't need food banks
Re: Red bag food drive invites Dundas to help tackle hunger, Sept. 14
Kudos to the Purolator workers for their food drive. But I can't help thinking that it shouldn't be necessary. Doug Ford has kept minimum wage far too low. Even working full time, too many still have to rely on food banks.
I wonder what would happen if food banks announced that in a few months they will close their operations. Would it force governments to finally do the right thing and mandate a living wage? Maybe they'd also bring in a wealth tax so important services aren't constantly underfunded.
It's long past time that we stopped listening to those who oppose these measures. Governments have done that for decades and look where it's got us.
Harry Shannon, Dundas
Run the city like a business
Re: Auditor-general probe finds 'eye-popping' $1.2 million in arrears and rental losses unlikely to be recovered by city, Sept. 19
The bottom line is the same as "Sewergate": people not doing their job on all levels; managers not managing. The buck stops with the city manager and ultimately with the mayor; all on the dime of the taxpayers.
We need a business person as our mayor, not a politician. We need a person who can balance a budget because in business you don't have taxpayers to bail you out.
Kathi Howes-Jones, Hamilton
Ticats' Katsantonis a class act
After the coin toss at the Hamilton Ti-Cats and Toronto Argos game on Friday night in Toronto, Hamilton Tiger Cat Stavros Katsantonis was the only player to stay behind and shake hands with members of our Canadian Forces. A class act! You were raised well Stavros. Respect. Proud of you.
Rob Baboth, Ancaster
CAPTION: It shouldn't be this difficult to get your hands on a COVID-19 test, a reader says.

Document 465 of 500

Doug Ford is right that we're all fed up with traffic. But his solution to it is just plain offensive

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-right-that-were-all-fed-up-with/docview/3109386248/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+right+that+we%27re+all+fed+up+with+traffic.+But+his+solution+to+it+is+just+plain+offensive&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has rarely met a wedge issue he didn't seem to love or encountered a jurisdictional line he wasn't happy to breach.
In the latest skirmish of the long-running culture war between cars and bicycles, the premier has clearly found both.
In Ford's ideal society, four wheels always trump two pedals - the better to appeal to suburban and hinterland voters on whom his Progressive Conservative government's political success has been built.
His latest initiative on behalf of motorists is set to be launched once the Ontario legislature resumes Oct. 21, with introduction of legislation that would restrict new bike lanes from being built, and override municipal authority in order to do so.
The Ford government says the bill - which would block construction of new bike lanes if it would result in the loss of lanes to cars and trucks - will help reduce the gridlock that has motorists across Greater Toronto grinding their molars.
That frustration is entirely understandable. A recent study put Toronto traffic at the third worst in the world for congestion, behind only London and Dublin.
This sort of world-class congestion costs drivers in fuel costs (as well as stress levels) while increasing carbon-dioxide emissions.
But Ford's solution is both ill-judged and offensive.
It won't solve the problem it complains of. Indeed, it's bound to make it worse. And along the way, Ford is once again trespassing on the municipal realm.
Admittedly, it likely makes for good politics in what might be the run-up to an early election Ford is reportedly musing on for next year. But it is bad policy.
Research from around the world has shown that bike lanes separated from cars are good for the safety of cyclists, pedestrians and motorists as well as helping the environment.
An American study published last November in the Journal of Transport and Health studied 13 years of traffic data and found that protected bike lanes make roads significantly safer for cyclists and other users.
Cities with dedicated bike lanes had 44 per cent fewer deaths and 50 per cent fewer serious accidents than other American cities.
Around the world, especially after the pandemic lockdown, cities have been deciding that providing cycling infrastructure gets more people moving in ways that benefit personal health and the environment.
Moreover, it isn't cyclists and bike lanes that are the main culprits in creating gridlock. It is construction, road closures and traffic returning to pre-pandemic levels. Yes, the installation of bike lanes in some cities has contributed slightly to slowed traffic. But done right, as in Paris, London and other metropolises, these lanes have often had the opposite effect, leading to more cyclists and fewer cars on the road.
Unhappily, what cyclists have been doing is dying on Toronto roads with horrifying frequency.
Six cyclists have been killed in Toronto this year and, as Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner has pointed out, the Ford government's plan is going to put cyclists in yet more danger and could well lead to more deaths.
Dianne Saxe, the councillor for University-Rosedale, where two cyclists have been killed this year, said the province is "gambling with people's lives."
Jurisdictionally speaking, the premier seems constitutionally incapable of sticking to his own knitting. He is proposing to directly attack municipalities, chiefly Toronto, and their capacity to make decisions based on local needs and knowledge.
As New Democrat MPP Joel Harden said, "this is putting wedge politics over real solutions."
It's as insulting as it is misguided. But, when it comes to wedge issues, the insult may well be part of the point.
When Ford spoke to reporters Monday there was little nuance, or sense of complexity, or citation of relevant research, or even much of a nod to the death and injury suffered by cyclists.
There was, instead, the overwrought language, the emoting, the type of black-and-white sensibility that divides citizens.
Research on cycling experiences in Canada and around the world was dismissed as "a bunch of hogwash."
Cars and trucks were backed up "from here to Timbuktu."
"It's an absolute disaster!" he said. "It's a nightmare!"
Whatever its electoral purposes, any policy justified by such histrionics - especially when it poaches on municipal affairs - should have Ontarians taking a good look under the hood.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives plan to ban bike lanes that require removal of a lane of vehicle traffic next month.

Subject: Automobile drivers; Politics; Pandemics; Trucks; Bicycling

Title: Doug Ford is right that we're all fed up with traffic. But his solution to it is just plain offensive

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Document 466 of 500

Doug Ford announces proposal for tunnel under Highway 401 to carry more traffic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-announces-proposal-tunnel-under-highway/docview/3109386210/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+announces+proposal+for+tunnel+under+Highway+401+to+carry+more+traffic&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it Doug's big dig. Or as skeptics call it, Doug's big distraction.
Premier Doug Ford is touting a tunnel under Highway 401, possibly from Mississauga to Scarborough, to carry more traffic and a public transit line as GTHA roads become increasingly crowded.
"Ontario is growing too fast and we need to do more," he said Wednesday at Islington and the 401 with heavy rush-hour traffic moving slowly on Canada's busiest multi-lane highway behind him.
Ford said the province will launch a feasibility study to determine more details and costs, but insisted "we're getting the tunnel built" because gridlock in the region costs the economy $11 billion annually.
No timelines were provided. Experts said it could take many months or longer to determine a path forward and years to build.
"This is not happening soon," said Matti Siemiatycki, professor of geography and planning and director of the infrastructure institute at the University of Toronto.
"This felt like the starting gun to do a study on the concept of a plan."
Acknowledging that critics will dismiss the bold proposal, Ford said, "I know this is an ambitious idea and some people will say it can't be done."
Opposition parties were skeptical, given that Ford has been hinting at calling an early election next spring and it is unlikely a full feasibility study could be completed before then.
"This is a fantasy tunnel when we need real, solid solutions for a serious issue," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, who called it a reminder of Ford's previous plans on Toronto city council for a monorail and a Ferris wheel.
Ford also mused about extending subways to Pickering and Markham after he was elected premier in 2018.
Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Ford could quickly ease traffic congestion on Highway 401 by allowing trucks to bypass Toronto with free access to toll Highway 407.
Schreiner warned a 401 tunnel project "will cost taxpayers billions, make your commute more expensive and only make gridlock worse" because of years of complicated construction.
They both called the tunnel idea is a "distraction" from the province's growing homelessness and encampment problem, a shortage of family doctors and long wait times in hospital emergency rooms.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie slammed the tunnel as a "pipe dream" that would cost "hundreds of billions of dollars" and urged the feasibility study examine the costs of buying the privately owned Highway 407, which is underused most of the day.
But the Toronto Region Board of Trade applauded the proposal.
"We need big and bold ideas, and a transit line under the 401 is exactly the kind of visionary thinking governments should pursue," the organization said in a statement.
"By integrating both car and transit solutions, this plan tackles gridlock at its core, offering a transformative path forward."
Ford's tunnel plan is reminiscent of Boston's controversial "Big Dig" that replaced above-ground highways through downtown with tunnels but ran years over schedule, caused major disruptions and was billions over budget. It was also a much smaller project of just a few kilometres. A tunnel across the Greater Toronto Area could be 60 kilometres long, depending on the route chosen.
Siemiatycki cautioned that a 401 tunnel "is not going to solve the problem" of traffic because of the concept of "induced demand," which draws cars to new highways that encourage more auto-oriented housing and industrial development.
That would create bottlenecks on feeder roads and at interchanges, the professor predicted, saying the feasibility study should look at road tolls and more public transit to ease the traffic burden.
"If we're at the stage where we're throwing out big, bold ideas, let's do a study on road tolls."
The premier would not estimate a cost for the 401 tunnel - which, if built, would be one of the largest public infrastructure projects in the world - or put a price tag on the feasibility study.
He has yet to reveal a cost for building the planned Highway 413, with construction slated to begin next year, from Highway 401 at Milton northeast to Highway 400 in Vaughan. Critics said that does not bode well for transparency on the cost of a 401 tunnel.
The tunnel feasibility study will consider the number of lanes needed, the length of the route and number of interchanges, and involve preliminary soil testing to determine how the geology of the route would impact design and construction.
It's not clear how many lanes could be included in a tunnel under the 401, raising questions about how much additional traffic capacity it would provide in terms of a cost-benefit analysis.
According to the province's traffic modelling, all of the 400-series highways in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area "will be at or exceed capacity within the next decade."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford at an announcement earlier this week.

Document 467 of 500

Justin Trudeau is fighting to keep his minority government alive. Here's what that might cost him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-is-fighting-keep-his-minority/docview/3109025476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+is+fighting+to+keep+his+minority+government+alive.+Here%27s+what+that+might+cost+him&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-What is the price of parliamentary peace? Is any price too high for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau now that the NDP-Liberal co-operation deal is dead?
Liberals have begun talks with the New Democrats and the Bloc Québécois, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said Tuesday. The goal is to find common political ground with the opposition for the months ahead.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says he is out to topple the government "at the earliest opportunity."
The Liberals appear in no hurry to cut new deals, but the parliamentary math for the minority Liberals is bloodless.
In the current 338-seat House of Commons, a majority of 170 votes is needed in order for the Trudeau government to pass laws, approve spending of public funds, or survive a non-confidence motion.
Trudeau has the most MPs - 153 - but he's 17 shy of the majority needed. So he needs a lifeline from either the Bloc which has 33 MPs, or the NDP with 25, to retain the "confidence" of the House that is required to govern.
Poilievre can count on his MPs' 119 votes. But the Conservative leader needs the other two main opposition parties to join him in a vote to defeat the government.
(The rest don't make a difference. The Green Party has two MPs. There are four Independents and two vacant seats where byelections have not yet been called.)
Here's what we know about some of the Bloc's and NDP's demands and priorities, and where they could try to extract gains during the Liberal government's final months.
Pharmacare and dental care
The NDP has suggested its support for the Liberals will last as long as it takes to get the pharmacare bill to provide free contraceptives and diabetes drugs and devices through the Senate and into law, and to expand eligibility for dental care beyond children and seniors. The Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO) estimated the cost of pharmacare at about $1.9 billion over five years, assuming provincial and private plans continue covering medications under their current terms.
Senate leaders have agreed to finish study of the pharmacare bill by Oct. 10. Royal assent could soon follow.
Dental care is now available to eligible seniors, children under 18 and disabled Canadians. The Liberal government says about 750,000 Canadians have already received care under the plan. Online registration for all remaining eligible Canadian residents between the ages of 18 and 64 is expected to be available in 2025. The PBO pegs the cost at $10 billion over five years.
The expansion, in theory, could be expedited, allowing both the Liberals and the NDP to claim credit.
More money for seniors
The Bloc Québécois wants more money for seniors aged 65-74, which would align with changes the Liberals have already made for those aged over 75. The PBO analyzed a Bloc MP's private member's bill that underpins the Bloc demand.
It would increase the amount of the full pension to which all pensioners aged 65 or older are entitled by 10 per cent, according to the PBO, and raise the exemption for employment income or self-employment earnings to $6,500 from $5,000 when it comes to determining the amount of the Guaranteed Income Supplement (GIS).
The PBO analysis of the bill said the changes would cost $2.9 billion in the first year, rising to $3.5 billion a year in the fifth year, for a total of $16 billion over the first five years.
Freeland on Tuesday touted what the Liberals have done for aging Canadians to date. The Liberal government already reversed a past Conservative government policy and restored the age of eligibility for OAS and GIS to 65 from 67, and increased the GIS for single seniors by up to $947 annually.
Should there be more changes? Freeland, who is finance minister, declined to answer , saying only that the Liberals are having "fruitful conversations" with the NDP and Bloc.
One Liberal source said Liberal MPs have discussed whether seniors should be allowed to earn a higher level of income before seeing the GIS clawed back.
Speaking in New York Tuesday, Trudeau told reporters the Bloc didn't support the government on dental care, which helps seniors, but said he is "going to continue to work with any progressives in this House that want to deliver for Canadians in concrete ways. I'm always ready to talk about what more we can do for vulnerable seniors."
Chalk this one up to maybe.
Grocery price cap
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh says big grocery chains and suppliers should be forced to lower the price of essential food like bread and baby formula, or face a price cap. In July, he set a six-month timeline for the price drop he wanted to see.
But other than beefing up the Competition Bureau's ability to investigate price-fixing or halt mergers that limit competition - which the Liberals have done - it is not clear how the NDP thinks prices should be capped. The Liberal government has gotten all major retailers to sign onto a voluntary grocery code of conduct.
Yet when it comes to price caps, such as those enacted by a handful of European countries, the Liberals have said no.
The Commons agriculture committee, chaired by a Liberal, in its report last year hedged big time, urging a windfall profits tax instead.
A Liberal source says a price-cap mechanism is unlikely to find favour with a Liberal government hard-pressed to tell farmers why they can't raise prices as their own costs increase, or as commodities worldwide rise.
So could the government tweak tax measures for big profit-making corporations or grocery chains? After her spring budget , Freeland directly avoided answering questions about the NDP's call for a windfall profits tax. Another Liberal insider said the government believes the cost of such measures get passed on to consumers.
Chalk this one up to highly doubtful.
More powers for Quebec on temporary immigration
The Bloc Québécois wants what Quebec Premier François Legault has demanded: more power for Quebec to set limits on the number of asylum seekers and temporary foreign workers allowed into that province.
Last week, Immigration Minister Marc Miller was characteristically blunt when asked whether he would give into the Bloc demand to give Quebec more control over immigration, especially refugee claimants, in exchange for their support.
"Not a chance," he told reporters.
Chalk this one up to unlikely.
Protections for agricultural sectors
In Canada, several agriculture sectors - milk, chicken and egg producers - operate under a system that regulates production, pricing mechanisms and imports.
The Bloc says it wants more protection for dairy farmers, and a Bloc private member's bill now in the Senate seeks to exclude supply-managed sectors from any future international trade agreements negotiated by the international trade minister.
The Liberals have tweaked, but largely protected, supply management in trade agreements signed or renegotiated under Trudeau, and had to pay about $4 billion in compensation to dairy farmers after NAFTA was renegotiated. The prime minister said then that dairy would be protected in the future, and the Liberals voted for the Bloc bill, which awaits final passage in the Senate.
Chalk this one up to an easy area for the government to look co-operative.
Carbon pricing
Would the Liberals agree to dial back a signature policy like the consumer carbon fuel charge - say, lower the rate at which it increases until 2030 when it hits $170 a tonne - now that the federal NDP (and provincial Liberals in Ontario and Newfoundland and Labrador, and some federal Liberals MPs) have gotten cold feet because of the high cost of living?
That seemed impossible last spring. Until it wasn't. Battered in Atlantic polls, the Liberals put a three-year pause on applying the carbon price to home heating oil, largely because of the financial hit to Atlantic Canada households where there are a lack of clean energy alternatives.
The NDP's Singh said he no longer believes there should be a carbon pricing burden on "working people" and will introduce a new plan "in the coming months" to make "big polluters" pay "their fair share," suggesting an increased price should be paid by industrial emitters.
On Tuesday, the Star asked Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault if there was any world in which the Liberals would again dial back the federal fuel charge, and he said flatly "No."
That is also the prime minister's view, said a government official.
Chalk this one up to highly doubtful.
Other opposition asks
Online harms bill
NDP and Bloc Québécois MPs have called for the Liberals to split their massive Online Harms bill C-63 that seeks to protect children from harmful online conduct, to ban hate propaganda, and to create a new digital regulator to investigate complaints against tech platforms.
Bloc Leader Yves-François Blanchet wants the removal of an exemption for religious speech which he said can be used by those who would "come and literally propagate hate against women, sexual minorities, and the people of Israel."
Justice Minister Arif Virani is reportedly open to changes.
Chalk this one up to a possible area of co-operation.
Medical assistance in dying
The Bloc wants Ottawa to allow Quebec the freedom to broaden its own regime of medical assistance in dying (or MAID). The federal government has used its criminal-lawmaking power to regulate MAID across the country, but last winter the Liberals (with the support of the Conservatives and NDP) delayed by three years the expansion of MAID to people suffering solely from a mental disorder. That change would now only take effect March 17, 2027.
The Liberals have rejected past demands for a special carve-out from the criminal law for Quebec, saying MAID cannot be regulated on a province-by-province basis.
Unlikely to be reopened.
With files from Mark Ramzy.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau rises during question period in the House of Commons on Sept. 18, 2024.

Document 468 of 500

letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3109020049/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Make COVID tests accessible
Yesterday, I sent a letter outlining my difficulty in finding valid COVID-19 test kits in Hamilton. Last evening, I found an Ontario government website, updated as of Sept. 20, that listed pharmacies with COVID kits accessible from Hamilton. I've now called two of them. Neither had kits. Both had to throw out kits because they had expired. Why? The last ones they had received were distributed by the Ontario government in June 2023. They agreed that kits at any other nearby pharmacies would have surely expired by now. Given the likelihood of new COVID variants spreading rapidly, this is a situation that needs immediate government attention.
Roy Adams, Hamilton
Keep social media out of schools
Re: Public school block on
social-media platforms set
to roll out soon, Sept. 16
I am very pleased with the decision to ban social media platforms in schools. As a mother of four, I have struggled to keep my children focused and free from distractions like social media. Schools should provide an environment where students receive a full, focused education.
As president of the Ahmadiyya Women Association (Hamilton Mountain), our community strives to engage youth in activities that reduce screen time. If students spend their free time at school on devices, our efforts are undermined.
We should encourage kids to return to meaningful activities like writing, drawing and reading. Many children are surprised at the idea of life without social media, but this is a valuable opportunity for them to rediscover hands-on learning. Thank you, HWDSB, for this important step.
Khalida Iffat, Hamilton
A flaw in the
child-care system
While there's been a lot of talk, and some action, regarding $10-a-day daycare, no one seems to be addressing the reality of the work day and the school day being different lengths. Parents who must piece together and pay for before-school and after-school care know how stressful this can be.
Marg Heidebrecht, Dundas
There's more
to the LRT story
Re: Lets follow Calgary's LRT lead, Sept. 21
Don Hull in his letter urging Hamilton to take Calgary's lead and cancel its LRT project does not provide the whole picture. He fails to mention that Calgary already has an LRT that has been up and running for over 40 years and is considered to be one of the busiest and most successful light rail systems in North America. It consists of two lines, a Red Line and a Blue Line, and the City of Calgary reluctantly pressed pause on construction of a third LRT line known as the Green Line only after the Conservative government in Alberta pulled its funding. In contrast, Ontario's Conservative government is building Hamilton's LRT for the city under the auspices of Metrolinx. Also, Calgary's paused Green Line is not truly dead since the Alberta government recently said it could possibly go ahead with renewed funding after following design modifications. It is surprising that Ross, a former City of Hamilton transportation director, would urge the cancellation of Hamilton's LRT based upon, at best, partial information.
Greg Crone, Caledonia
Transit moving in wrong direction
Mr. Hull, I couldn't agree with you more. Give us bus rapid transit and drop the LRT idea - a potential total debacle. So here we are, years later, still entertaining this useless idea. So much money to be spent on so few. Most of the citizens of Hamilton will not be using the LRT because they don't live anywhere near the proposed routes. Getting rid of lanes of traffic on Main Street and King Street will remove two routes that are the most used in traversing the city. Talk about gridlock! The city better begin buying up as much industrial strength Senokot that they can get their hands on. They will need it in order to relieve the inevitable constipation this city is going to suffer due to the LRT.
Elena Trkulja, Hamilton
Better to spend
on more buses
With all that is happening with homeless and infrastructure decline, it's time to cancel this silly LRT that is going to be way over budget! There is nothing this LRT will do that some extra buses can't do!
Arthur Cattafi, Hamilton
Singh right to
face hecklers
Re: The scary truth behind this viral video of Jagmeet Singh, Sept. 23
Kudos to Luke Lebrun for an excellent article. And kudos to Jagmeet Singh for having the courage to confront the hecklers. The only way to fight such cowards is to vigorously confront their bad manners. The cretins of the far-right have no notion about how to go about instituting change and that protesting is merely one aspect of any social movement. In the immortal words of Saul D. Alinsky: These guys couldn't organize a luncheon let alone a revolution.
Dan Riga, Burlington
We shouldn't need food banks
Re: Red bag food drive invites Dundas to help tackle hunger, Sept. 14
Kudos to the Purolator workers for their food drive. But I can't help thinking that it shouldn't be necessary. Doug Ford has kept minimum wage far too low. Even working full time, too many still have to rely on food banks.
I wonder what would happen if food banks announced that in a few months they will close their operations. Would it force governments to finally do the right thing and mandate a living wage? Maybe they'd also bring in a wealth tax so important services aren't constantly underfunded. It's long past time that we stopped listening to those who oppose these measures. Governments have done that for decades and look where it's got us.
Harry Shannon, Dundas
Run the city
like a business
Re: Auditor-general probe finds 'eye-popping' $1.2 million in arrears and rental losses, Sept. 19
The bottom line is the same as "Sewergate": people not doing their job on all levels; managers not managing. The buck stops with the city manager and ultimately with the mayor; all on the dime of the taxpayers.
We need a business person as our mayor, not a politician. We need a person who can balance a budget because in business you don't have taxpayers to bail you out.
Kathi Howes-Jones,
Hamilton
Ticats player
is a class act
After the coin toss at the Hamilton Tiger-Cats and Toronto Argos game on Friday night in Toronto, Hamilton Tiger-Cat Stavros Katsantonis was the only player to stay behind and shake hands with members of our Canadian Forces. A class act! You were raised well Stavros. Respect. Proud of you.
Rob Baboth, Ancaster

Document 469 of 500

New Crombie sounds like the old Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-crombie-sounds-like-old-ford/docview/3109020007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Crombie+sounds+like+the+old+Ford&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals had billed it as a weekend policy conference to brainstorm for a few good ideas on good governance. But when Bonnie Crombie finally took the podium, she skipped the (policy) platform. Pondering her path to power, Crombie chose a shortcut.
No policy bible, no fine print, no road map to reviving and revivifying the moribund Liberals. While it's still early days, the party leader detoured from any details for the 1,000 party delegates meeting in London.
Henceforth, her provincial Liberals will build their campaign all around Doug Ford. And downplay their ties to Justin Trudeau's unpopular federal Liberals. To be clear, she won't just focus on Ford's faults. Crombie's Liberals are also stealing the premier's proven playbook - battle-tested in two winning elections - and turning it against his governing Tories with a vengeance.
Remember Ford's winning campaign in 2018, when he badmouthed Kathleen Wynne's Liberals as corrupt parasites allegedly enriching their "insider" friends? Remember the attacks against "hallway health care" and the cheesy slogans promising "buck a beer?"
Now, Crombie wants to flip Ford's script, by turning his own words against him. If you can't beat them, borrow their sloganeering. Which is why the new Crombie sounds increasingly like the old Ford, circa 2018. Policy light, rhetoric heavy.
At her final pep rally Saturday evening, the culmination of all those hours of deep thought by the party faithful, there was barely a peep about policy. Just chapter headings on health care and education, with a promise of more to come - hopefully before Ford springs an early election on the opposition parties next spring.
"So how will we do all of this?" she asked rhetorically, before retreating from her own challenge.
"We start by doing less for the rich insiders - the people who are getting ahead while everyone else is falling behind. Less for Doug's buddies. Less for the people who are under criminal investigation."
She complained about "hospital hallways" that are crammed, echoing Ford's old complaints. And then Crombie delivered her carefully prepared punch line, that she hopes will shake up Ontario politics:
"We are going to do less for them, so we can do more for you."
In case you missed it, that phrase, first heard at London's convention centre, will resound and redound across the province if the Liberals have their way. On cue, as Crombie led the chant, her followers shouted it out:
"Less for them. More for you."
As slogans go, it's not merely clunky but cheesy. It encapsulates both the politics of envy and the pocketbook politics of our era. It frames politics as entirely transactional, not transformational. It is anti-tax and all about affordability. Will it sell?
To be sure, people are feeling besieged by economic disparities. But the solutions are not as simple as politicians pretend.
Affordability and tax-cutting won't pay the bills. Sustainability and efficiency are part of the equation.
Without sustainable tax revenues, you can't sustain the education and health services that people count on, and that public sector workers bargain on. If you can't drive efficiencies in an $85-billion health-care budget (without reflexively invoking privatization sloganeering and scaremongering), you won't be able to fix overflowing hospital hallways.
Meanwhile, the Tories are spending big money on ads that frame and defame Crombie as a posh and pampered politician who is out of touch with ordinary Ontarians. Now, the Liberal leader is recasting Ford as the real rich kid who grew up with a silver spoon in his father's Etobicoke "mansion," heir to a family firm and political dynasty, while she is the hard scrabble survivor who grew up in a "rooming house" raised by a single mother.
Will this battle of personal narratives speak to voters?
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

Document 470 of 500

Bike-lane ban will make traffic worse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bike-lane-ban-will-make-traffic-worse/docview/3109019923/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bike-lane+ban+will+make+traffic+worse&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Doug Ford government is reportedly planning to introduce legislation that would ban municipalities from building new bicycle lanes that reduce the space for motor vehicles.
If there is one north star that has guided the premier in his policy-making, it is an absolute devotion to the automobile. Road tolls removed. Licence-plate fees waived. Highways widened. Four wheels good, two wheels bad.
And yet, even amid Ford's relentlessly pro-car agenda, this is something else. Leaving aside the heavy-handedness of Queen's Park dictating to municipalities what they are allowed to do with their own road infrastructure, as though the Premier's Office knows better than the elected officials of towns and cities that each have unique travel and commuting patterns, a ban on new bike lanes will literally have the opposite of the desired effect.
Bike lanes do not increase traffic congestion, they reduce it. Full stop.
It's not as though there isn't real-word evidence of this. Major cities that have committed to bike-lane expansion - Paris, London, Madrid, Seoul - have reduced vehicular traffic over time, for the dead simple reason that more humans can fit on a bike lane than in a lane of traffic while in their cars. Some studies estimate that 10 times as many people can commute on a protected bike lane as compared to a typical lane of motor-vehicle traffic.
That's not to say there won't be complaints, as it takes time for commuting behaviours to change after bike lanes are implemented. Motorists grumble about the reduced space for their unnecessarily large vehicles, bringing negative headlines. This seems to be basically what the premier is responding to: congestion in Toronto is bad, and also some bike lanes were implemented, so therefore bike lanes must be to blame.
But just because a certain segment of the Tory base thinks bikes are too woke doesn't mean they are the problem. This is government by vibes.
Ford could look, for example, to the east, where Montreal has been expanding bike lanes for years in an effort to reduce vehicular traffic on the island. It has worked as designed. In one example, the installation of protected bike lanes along historic Saint-Denis Street has helped transform it from a thoroughfare for commuting motorists into something closer to its original purpose as a commercial centre. Vehicular traffic is down, bike trips have sky rocketed, pedestrian traffic is up and the commercial occupancy rate has steadily increased.
And again, this isn't some kind of weird one-off. Studies around the world have shown that cyclists and pedestrians are more likely to stop and shop than just-passing-through drivers. This seems like a pretty good outcome for Premier Open for Business.
Achieving those kinds of results, by transforming cities away from car dependency, of course takes time and patience. It's much easier to grouse about bikes taking away space from those precious cars, and politically expedient because more people drive than cycle.
But you know what encourages people to switch from car to bike? Better bicycle lanes, especially those that are protected from vehicular traffic. Having to hug the shoulder of a road or threading the space between moving vehicles on one side and parked cars on the other, can be uncomfortable and dangerous. Separating riders from cars has had a transformative effect wherever it is tried: it's much safer, it moves people out of cars and on to bikes, which is better for public health and the environment.
This feels like one of those Ford initiatives that is impervious to facts. He's heard people complain about bike lanes, so he has concerns about bike lanes. Passing a law to prohibit them, after all, won't cost a thing. Except in the long run.
Scott Stinson is a journalist in suburban Toronto.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Scott Stinson Scott Stinson is a journalist in suburban Toronto.;

Subject: Automobiles; Bans; Commuting; Bicycles; Automobile drivers; Traffic; Roads & highways; Bicycling
