'Get off your a-s-s:' Doug Ford scolded for telling homeless people to get jobs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/get-off-your-s-doug-ford-scolded-telling-homeless/docview/3109495058/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Get+off+your+a-s-s%3A%27+Doug+Ford+scolded+for+telling+homeless+people+to+get+jobs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is being scolded for telling homeless people in encampments to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else."
Experts in the field said Ford has made the mistake of assuming that people living in tents in parks don't want to work when their precarious status often makes it difficult to find and keep employment.
"It's hard to have a job when you have no shower, you have no food and you have nowhere to keep your clothes and you don't have a phone because it's being stolen out of your tent," said Kimberly Curry, executive director of the Toronto-based Seeds of Hope Foundation.
"Yes, there are people in encampments - or there can be - who are taking advantage, like in all areas of society. But 90 per cent do not want to be there."
Critics at Queen's Park said Ford overlooked the role that skyrocketing rents and the cost of living are having on people, particularly those struggling to survive on Ontario Works, the Ontario Disability Support Plan or low-wage jobs. A recent report found more than one million Ontarians visited a food bank last year.
Shelters are also overcrowded and turning people away, unable to keep up with rising demand for beds. On average, 233 people nightly were turned away from Toronto shelters in August.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles called Ford's remarks "heartless."
"Housing is a human right, not a luxury ... instead, the person in charge of our province is too busy using his soapbox to belittle vulnerable people."
"Doug Ford continues to blame everyone but himself for the housing crisis," added Green Leader Mike Schreiner, calling for more affordable housing and community supports.
Ford made his controversial comments Monday in Cobourg, east of Toronto, where a local radio reporter asked him about more than 1,000 people on a waiting list for affordable housing in the area.
The premier summed up government supports for homeless people before he offered this advice at the announcement of provincial funding for water and sewer services in a new housing development:
"Do you know what's the best way to get people to be able to get out of the encampments, get out of homelessness? Get an application and drop it off at one of these companies, and start working," he said, referring to construction firms working on the development.
"You need to start working if you're healthy. Bottom line, if you're unhealthy, I'll take care of you ... but if you're healthy, get off your a-s-s and start working," Ford added, spelling out the word.
"You can't have people setting up encampments in beautiful neighbourhoods, they just can't do that. It destroys the neighbourhoods."
A spokeswoman for Ford later told the Star that the Progressive Conservative government is looking at "all options available to the province" to deal with encampments.
"We're hearing from municipalities and residents who are rightly frustrated with the encampments in their communities."
On Tuesday in Port Colborne, the premier reiterated his push for homeless people to find jobs and said he applauds the City of Hamilton's plan to create sanctioned encampments with small cabins for homeless people with supports to get them out of parks and residential areas.
"Sure, if we can give them a shelter, give them an opportunity, get them back on their feet, have an address, they can go out and apply for new opportunities."
Curry said housing is a critical first step toward finding a job, which is why the Seeds of Hope Foundation has paid for temporary lodgings in bare-bones rooming houses to help clients stabilize their lives.
There is a long list of cracks in Ontario's social safety net, such as the uphill battle to get help for mental illness and addictions, but the lack of affordable housing is the biggest factor, said Sarah Ovens, a case manager with All Saints Community Centre in Moss Park.
She noted the wait for publicly subsidized affordable housing in Toronto is a decade long with nearly 90,000 on the list at the end of June.
"People need housing before they can try to do anything else, stabilize anything else, whether it's employment or mental health treatment or substance abuse issues," Ovens added.
"To look at an encampment and say that these people should just get jobs, that's not a real policy position."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford made his controversial comments Monday in Cobourg, east of Toronto, where a local radio reporter asked him about more than 1,000 people on a waiting list for affordable housing in the area.

Subject: Parks & recreation areas; Affordable housing; Homeless people

Pierre Poilievre vows he would balance the federal budget 'as soon as possible' - but doesn't give details about cuts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-vows-he-would-balance-federal/docview/3109025443/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+vows+he+would+balance+the+federal+budget+%27as+soon+as+possible%27+-+but+doesn%27t+give+details+about+cuts&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - In a campaign-style speech in the House of Commons, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre made his case to topple the Liberal government in a vote to be held Wednesday, vowing that once he wins power, he will balance the federal budget "as soon as possible."
Poilievre, who has said he would also cut income taxes, pledged Tuesday that he will bring in "lower, fairer, simpler taxes" across the board, adding, "We will cap government spending with a dollar-for-dollar law that requires we find one dollar of savings for every new dollar of spending. We will cut bureaucracy, waste and consulting contracts, so that we can get the budget close or, hopefully, on balance as soon as possible to bring down interest rates, inflation and debt."
While Poilievre has long claimed he would "fix the budget," he has not set out a timeline for balancing it.
Ottawa is currently projecting a deficit of nearly $40 billion this year, dropping to $20 billion five years out. Poilievre did not specify where he would cut government costs during a sometimes heated debate in the Commons.
His political rivals piled on the Conservative leader, who is leading by a wide margin in public opinion polls, warning he plans to make drastic cuts to programs like health care, pharmacare and dental care.
But Poilievre urged the Bloc Québécois and New Democratic Party to support the Conservative motion of non-confidence in the Trudeau Liberals, saying there is an "existential choice" to be made for the Canadian economy.
"This will be a carbon-tax referendum and a carbon-tax election," said Poilievre, who doubled down on his claim that scheduled increases in the consumer carbon fuel charge will cause "nuclear winter" to the Canadian economy.
Without reference to the corresponding rise in carbon rebate payments, Poilievre said the carbon price scheme will "grind our economy to a halt, that would force our truckers to leave to the U.S. where there is no carbon tax, leaving nobody to bring goods to our grocery store, parts to our factories or jobs to our people. It will be a nuclear winter if this happens."
Yet the New Democrats and Bloc Québécois said their MPs will not support the Conservatives' bid to trigger an immediate election. The BQ argued it will trade its support for "gains" for Quebec and is not yet willing to swap Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for Poilievre, whose plan for program cuts are unclear.
There will be plenty of other chances to bring down the Liberals in the next three months if they don't choose to work with the Bloc, said BQ House leader Alain Therrien. The BQ, NDP and the Conservatives will have several other opportunities to set the parliamentary agenda and present non-confidence motions, he noted.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh attacked Poilievre as a threat to universal health care, accusing the Conservatives of supporting a bigger role for the private sector in health-care delivery because of corporate ties. He asked Poilievre directly if he would cut dental care, and Poilievre turned the tables on the NDP leader, saying his desire for a "single-payer" pharmacare plan puts at risk the private insurance plans of ordinary workers that were hard-won by unions.
While Poilievre was presenting his motion in the House, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson staged a news conference outside to excoriate the Conservative leader over his opposition to the Liberals' signature climate policy.
Guilbeault said gutting the federal carbon pricing requirement would benefit wealthy fossil fuel companies while removing the rebates for households where the made-in-Ottawa consumer system applies. This year, a family of four in Ontario is set to receive more than $1,100 in rebates under that plan.
Wilkinson said eliminating those rebates along with the federal consumer carbon price would be akin to taking money away from lower-income Canadians. The independent Parliamentary Budget Office has calculated that lower-income households see a larger fiscal benefit from the system than wealthier ones.
The ministers also zeroed in on Poilievre's recent statements that increasing the federal carbon price to $170 per tonne of emissions by 2030, as the Liberals plan to do, would be akin to a "nuclear winter" for the economy.
Experts on the science of nuclear war have projected that a real nuclear winter would kill billions of people around the world, and possibly cause almost the entire Canadian population to starve to death.
"I would say some of the more recent colourful language that he has been using around things like nuclear winter are simply ridiculous, and not becoming of a leader in a major G7 country," Wilkinson said.
"They are stupid, and he should know that."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises to speak in the House of Commons on Sept.24, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Rebates; Federal budget; Dental care; Nuclear winter; Carbon; No confidence motions & votes; Tax elections

Document 473 of 500

Things are tough for Justin Trudeau at home, but the world stage is a different story

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/things-are-tough-justin-trudeau-at-home-world/docview/3109019920/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Things+are+tough+for+Justin+Trudeau+at+home%2C+but+the+world+stage+is+a+different+story&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a confident Justin Trudeau, sitting on the set of the The Late Show with Stephen Colbert in New York on Monday night, who asserted that he was "pretty good" at his job.
Back in Ottawa, Pierre Poilievre begged to differ, kicking off a debate on Tuesday in the Commons, and asking it to declare no confidence in this prime minister or his government.
The promise of Canada is "broken," Poilievre proclaimed.
The juxtaposition is almost jarring. There was Trudeau in New York, the toast of the talk-show circuit, while here in Canada, Poilievre just wants him to be toast.
On Wednesday, Trudeau is expected to survive the Conservatives' effort to end his time in office - not because the opposition parties agree that he's pretty good at his job, but because the time is not yet right for an election.
The Colbert appearance was more a lucky coincidence than grand strategy, but it played into what Trudeau and his team seem determined to do this fall, and that's a more concerted pushback against the relentless Conservative attacks on the Liberals' competence - and confidence.
All day Tuesday, as the debate over the non-confidence motion droned on inside the chamber, Liberals were fanned out at news conferences asserting what they were doing for Canadians, on everything from the economy to the environment to hate speech and crime.
Trudeau used his Colbert appearance to play up what he and his Liberals believe is his strength - the prime minister's comfort on the world stage. This isn't new. From the moment in the fall of 2015 when Trudeau was sworn in, he was doing interviews with high-profile U.S. media and rubbing shoulders with other world leaders at international summits.
He has made more of an effort over the past year to speak with U.S. interviewers, who tend to be friendlier and talk more about big-picture politics than the day-to-day back and forth of domestic Canadian fare.
The difference between the early days of 2015 and now is that this celebrity is well-worn with a lot of Canadians, and Trudeau has rivals who are all too happy to present his world travels as a evidence of a prime minister out of touch with ordinary Canadians' concerns.
Trudeau took every opportunity to talk about those issues, however, when he sat down with Colbert, acknowledging he was weathering the unpopularity that tough times bring to incumbent leaders.
"People are frustrated," Trudeau said. "The idea that maybe they want an election now is something that my opponents are trying to bank on, because people are taking a lot out on me for understandable reasons. I've been here, and I've been steering us through all these things, and people are sometimes looking at change."
He didn't need to present Poilievre as Canada's version of Donald Trump - Colbert did that for him. "Your opponent there has been called Canada's Trump, and I'm sorry about that," Colbert joked.
Colbert didn't ask whether Trudeau was Canada's Joe Biden, willing to consider stepping aside for the greater good of the country and progressive politics. That question came on Tuesday when the prime minister faced reporters at the United Nations, where he has spent time this week as part of its general assembly meetings.
Trudeau ducked the straight comparison, saying it was Poilievre who seems more interested in power than the greater good of the country.
"Every politician has a choice about why they serve, what they're trying to do, how they're trying to help," he said. "My focus is very much, and always has been, on how to serve Canadians and build a better future. That's not what we're seeing from the Conservative party."
If Trudeau's government survives the flurry of confidence votes expected this fall session, Canadians are going to get a chance to see more of this contrast between a prime minister abroad and a more imperilled one at home.
November is always a month filled with international summits and it will be no surprise to see Trudeau using these occasions to telegraph to Canadians that he's the steady symbol of continuity in an increasingly unstable world. Whether that's important to voters here, consumed with issues of affordability closer to home, is an open question.
It's also unclear how much Canadians are tuned into the daily fray of the House of Commons, where Poilievre has always seemed to be more at home than Trudeau. (That has been true since Trudeau came to the Commons 16 years ago.)
On Wednesday, Trudeau will be back from New York, facing the confidence vote in the Commons. He probably won't assert that he's doing a pretty good job - at least not in so many words - knowing that the reception will be slightly less friendly than the one he received from Colbert.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, flanked by entourage and security, waves as he arrives to the CBS studios for the filming of "The Late Show with Stephen Colbert," in New York on Sept. 23, 2024.

Document 474 of 500

Doug Ford's war on the bike continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-war-on-bike-continues/docview/3108751940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+war+on+the+bike+continues&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, internal documents leaked to the public show that the provincial Conservatives are considering legislation that would make it illegal to establish bike lanes in areas where car lanes would be reduced.
That would be almost everywhere already built, with bike lanes available only for new communities, I guess.
Unless car lanes would be reduced in these new communities, too. Make it make sense.
This is "deeply regressive" according to Tim Gray, executive director of Environmental Defence. I could add few choice words to that statement, but my editor would cut them.
This comes after a high-profile death in Toronto in August, where a 26-year old woman was killed after being forced out of a bike lane blocked by a vehicle. She was the sixth cyclist to die in Toronto this year.
But let's reduce bike lanes and forbid further establishment?
Let's try to make it make sense. If the bike lane weren't there, the vehicle wouldn't have had to park in it. The cyclist wouldn't have had to detour into traffic. But most importantly, maybe the cyclist wouldn't even try to cycle. Those damn bike lanes, putting everyone at risk.
I live on a street with a bike lane. Where it was once a busy three-lane road, now it's two lanes, with a bike lane on each side.
The most noticeable difference it made was to make the sidewalk more safe by creating a four-foot buffer zone between the sidewalk and the road. Most Hamilton sidewalks are not wide enough to provide any feeling of safety for pedestrians.
When the protected York Boulevard bike lane was operational, the sense of safety walking down York, with its wide sidewalk, was unlike any road in the city. I surely appreciated the extra space and distance from busy traffic when walking my dogs. I don't take that route anymore.
But pedestrian safety is a byproduct of bike lanes, which are after all, for cyclists, although they are used by motorized vehicles as well, people riding scooters and electric bikes. The bike lanes are used by such a variety of vehicles, calling it a bike lane is a misnomer.
Society is moving toward alternative forms of transportation. SoBi bikes and Bird scooters are everywhere in my downtown neighbourhood. Electric bikes are growing in popularity. Improvements in public transit are always on the horizon, never quick enough, but consistently moving in the direction of more access.
Decades ago, I was on a committee to establish a network of bike lanes throughout the city.
I was an avid cyclist, I didn't drive and cycled everywhere; to work, to play, to visit family and friends. Planning was at the beginning stages and I didn't stay involved, but the results of those who carried on are enjoyed by cyclists all over Hamilton.
It's impossible to say how many lives have been saved by this network, how many accidents avoided. Although as I write this I'm reminded of images I saw recently of a twisted up bike and shaken up cyclist who was riding in the bike lane and met up with a car.
Nothing is perfect, which is sorry consolation to the cyclist, but we should be moving in that direction and toward improving the network, not diminishing it or starving it from growing.
This idea of no more bike lanes on existing roads, which is what this essentially means, is beyond backwards. It's dangerous. It will lead to more deaths as people move with the times and toward alternative forms of movement and seek their space on the sidewalks or roads.
Doug Ford loves drivers. There is nothing he wouldn't do for them. Rob the treasury of vehicle registration fees, rape the environment for new roads, invest billions in EV technology and pump up production of electric vehicles.
This is just another salvo in his war for the car and against anyone riding in the bike lane who are in his way. Beep. Beep.
CAPTION: The Advocacy for Respect for Cyclists group organized a memorial ride in Toronto in July to advocate for safer roads for cyclists. On July 25, a 24-year-old woman was killed by the driver of a large truck while trying to manoeuvre around a blocked bicycle lane at Bloor Street, east of Avenue Road.Limiting bicycle lanes will lead to more deaths as people move with the times and toward alternative forms of movement and seek their space on sidewalks or roads, Margaret Shkimba argues.

Subject: Bicycles; Walkways; Traffic accidents & safety; Roads & highways; Vehicles; Bicycling

Doug Ford's bike lane ban will make life worse for everyone - including drivers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-bike-lane-ban-will-make-life-worse/docview/3108751928/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+bike+lane+ban+will+make+life+worse+for+everyone+-+including+drivers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Doug Ford government is reportedly planning to introduce legislation that would ban municipalities from building new bicycle lanes that reduce the space for motor vehicles.
To which one must say: Sigh. It's a shame the Premier doesn't have some developer friends who engaged in a bit of bike lane speculation.
In truth, even that probably wouldn't have mattered. If there is one north star that has guided the Premier in his policy-making, it is an absolute devotion to the automobile. Road tolls removed. Licence-plate fees waived. Highways widened.
Four wheels good, two wheels bad.
And yet, even amid Ford's relentlessly pro-car agenda, this is something else. Leaving aside the heavy-handedness of Queen's Park dictating to municipalities what they are allowed to do with their own road infrastructure, as though the Premier's Office knows better than the elected officials of towns and cities that each have unique travel and commuting patterns, a ban on new bike lanes will literally have the opposite of the desired effect.
Bike lanes do not increase traffic congestion, they reduce it. Full stop. That is, and it feels kind of obvious to say it, the point.
It's not as though there isn't real-word evidence of this. Major cities that have committed to bike-lane expansion - Paris, London, Madrid, Seoul - have reduced vehicular traffic over time, for the dead simple reason that more humans can fit on a bike lane than in a lane of traffic while in their cars. Some studies estimate that 10 times as many people can commute on a protected bike lane as compared to a typical lane of motor-vehicle traffic, since the majority of those vehicles will have a single occupant - the least efficient mode of travel in terms of sharing the road.
That's not to say there won't be complaints, as it takes time for commuting behaviours to change after bike lanes are implemented. Motorists grumble about the reduced space for their unnecessarily large vehicles, bringing negative headlines. This seems to be basically what the Premier is responding to: traffic congestion in Toronto is bad, and also some bike lanes were implemented, so therefore bike lanes must be to blame.
But just because a certain segment of the Tory base thinks bikes are too woke doesn't mean they are the problem. This is government by vibes.
Ford could look, for example, to the east, where Montreal has been expanding dedicated bike lanes for years now in an effort to reduce vehicular traffic on the island. It has worked as designed. In one example, the installation of protected bike lanes along historic Saint-Denis Street has helped transform it from a thoroughfare for commuting motorists into something closer to its original purpose as a commercial centre. Vehicular traffic is down, bike trips have skyrocketed, pedestrian traffic is up and the commercial occupancy rate has steadily increased.
And again, this isn't some kind of weird one-off. Studies around the world have shown that cyclists and pedestrians are more likely to stop and shop than just-passing-through drivers. This seems like a pretty good outcome for Premier Open for Business.
Achieving those kinds of results, by transforming cities away from car dependency, of course takes time and patience. It's much easier to grouse about bikes taking away space from those precious cars, and politically expedient because more people drive than cycle.
But you know what encourages people to switch from car to bike? Better bicycle lanes, especially those that are protected from vehicular traffic. Having to hug the shoulder of a road or threading the space between moving vehicles on one side and parked cars on the other, can be uncomfortable and dangerous. Separating riders from cars has had a transformative effect wherever it is tried: it's much safer, it moves people out of cars and on to bikes, which is better for public health and the environment.
Despite all that, this feels like one of those Ford initiatives that is impervious to facts. He's heard people complain about bike lanes, so he has concerns about bike lanes. The motorists are unhappy? Can't have that. And so the blame for congestion will fall on the bike lanes that haven't even been built yet.
Passing a law to prohibit them, after all, won't cost a thing. Except in the long run.
CAPTION: The Ford government wants to stop construction of city bike lanes that take space away from cars.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson, Contributor

Subject: Automobiles; Bans; Commuting; Bicycles; Automobile drivers; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Document 476 of 500

Electoral ballots aren't a joke. Pranking makes our democracy less credible, not more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/electoral-ballots-arent-joke-pranking-makes-our/docview/3108751917/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Electoral+ballots+aren%27t+a+joke.+Pranking+makes+our+democracy+less+credible%2C+not+more&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When did election ballots become a staging ground for political stunts?In two of the three recent federal by-elections - in Toronto, Winnipeg and Montreal - self-styled electoral system reformers figured out how to load up the actual ballot with dozens of names of unaffiliated candidates to produce outsized and difficult-to-use ballots. These need to be laboriously hand-counted, meaning that results come in extraordinarily late by Canadian election standards.Not to be confused with the joke parties of the past, this tactic depends on large numbers of people registering as independent candidates, with neither the desire nor the chance to win, in order to make a point about why Canada's election system needs an overhaul.The organizers, who call themselves the Longest Ballot Committee and have not revealed their identities publicly, declared victory after the second such race: "We can say without a doubt our campaign was a success because we had so much fun and sparked important conversations about our shared democracy," they said in a statement reported by the CBC.Their campaign is about pressuring the federal government to enact the ranked ballot reforms promised and then dismissed by Justin Trudeau's Liberals in 2015. But finding a way to make the actual ballot cumbersome and confusing risks infecting Canada's electoral system with the cynicism that has disabled its U.S. counterpart. Indeed, one has to ask why is it a good idea to confuse voters in the ballot box, a place that should be literally beyond the reach of politics?There's a strand of populist ideology that elections should be as open as possible. But it doesn't follow that election administration should be vulnerable to an activity that makes a mockery of the voting process and renders the task of determining a winner exceedingly difficult. I don't buy the argument that Canada's first-past-the-post system is hopelessly tainted because the winners don't necessarily represent the majority of votes cast. The entire history of post-war Canada, which is what we're talking about, can't simply be dismissed as an eight-decade-long disaster. What's more, no electoral system is ideal; even those that seem to be the most democratic - e.g., proportional representation - are vulnerable to abuse. Just look at Israel's fatally-fractured parliament, where tiny extremist parties control the balance of power. It's also important to acknowledge that different Canadian jurisdictions have very different rules about how individuals can enlist as candidates.At the federal level, a prospective candidate must collect 100 signatures of electors, which can now be done very easily using electronic forms. The refundable deposit used to be $1,000, but Alberta's court of appeal struck down that requirement in 2017, deeming it unconstitutional.Deposits, however, still exist in provincial and municipal elections.In Ontario, candidates for the legislature, as well as municipal councils and school boards in jurisdictions with over 4,000 residents, have to find 25 signatures for their nomination papers and pay a $200 deposit. B.C. requires 75 signatures and a $250 deposit. Alberta, in turn, wants 25 signatures and a $500 deposit.Which of these represents the most optimally democratic approach? The one with the least required names? The one with no deposit? There is no right answer, because these rules are obviously arbitrary.What we do now know is that it's become easy to punk the federal races and there's every reason to think these tactics will spread. After the past week's by-elections, Canada's chief electoral officer Stéphane Perrault said he'll look at tightening the rules, citing the difficulties that voters with accessibility issues experienced when presented with metre-long ballots containing dozens of names.I'm glad Perrault is advocating on behalf of a marginalized group that came face-to-face with the potential disenfranchisement posed by Longest Ballot's stunt. But I'd argue that Elections Canada can broaden its scope of concern to everyone who wants to cast a vote without worrying that some anonymous gadfly group is having fun at their expense.If the Longest Balloteers think there's appetite for reform, let them seek a space within the parties and then expend some energy making their case. But leave the election process alone. After all, we know by now what happens when people start to sow doubt about a system that demands air-tight credibility to remain legitimate.
CAPTION: Voters arrive to cast their ballot in the federal byelection for the riding of Lasalle-Emard-Verdun, in Montreal, Monday, Sept. 16, 2024.
CREDIT: John Lorinc, Contributor

Subject: Election results; Local elections; Democracy; Candidates; Voters; Special elections

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

National briefs: Controversial law to be tested

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3108515870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Controversial+law+to+be+tested&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Hoggard enters not-guilty plea
Musician Jacob Hoggard pleaded not guilty Monday to sexual assault in a northeastern Ontario court. Jury selection has begun for the trial that's taking place in Haileybury. The former Hedley frontman is facing a sexual assault charge for an incident alleged to have happened on June 25, 2016, in nearby Kirkland Lake.
Controversial law to be tested
A Charter challenge that got underway on Monday will test the constitutionality of a controversial Ontario law that allows hospitals to place discharged patients into long-term care homes not of their choosing or face a $400-per-day charge if they refuse. The Advocacy Centre for the Elderly and the Ontario Health Coalition argues the law violates the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Canada
MPs asked to behave better
House of Commons Speaker Greg Fergus gave MPs a stern warning on Monday about improving their behaviour as they prepare for a fractious debate about whether the Liberals should continue to govern. News last week that NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and his caucus would not support a no-confidence motion led to a heated exchange that one MP thought might come to physical blows. "There were words which fall outside of what's acceptable," Fergus said before question period on Monday.

Document 478 of 500

The war on the bicycle continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/war-on-bicycle-continues/docview/3108515505/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+war+on+the+bicycle+continues&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, internal documents leaked to the public show that the provincial Conservatives are considering legislation that would make it illegal to establish bike lanes in areas where car lanes would be reduced.
That would be almost everywhere already built, with bike lanes available only for new communities, I guess.
Unless car lanes would be reduced in these new communities, too. Make it make sense.
This is "deeply regressive" according to Tim Gray, executive director of Environmental Defence. I could add few choice words to that statement, but my editor would cut them.
This comes after a high-profile death in Toronto in August, where a 26-year old woman was killed after being forced out of a bike lane blocked by a vehicle. She was the sixth cyclist to die in Toronto this year.
But let's reduce bike lanes and forbid further establishment?
Let's try to make it make sense. If the bike lane weren't there, the vehicle wouldn't have had to park in it.
The cyclist wouldn't have had to detour into traffic. But most importantly, maybe the cyclist wouldn't even try to cycle. Those damn bike lanes, putting everyone at risk.
I live on a street with a bike lane. Where it was once a busy three-lane road, now it's two lanes, with a bike lane on each side.
The most noticeable difference it made was to make the sidewalk more safe by creating a four-foot buffer zone between the sidewalk and the road. Most Hamilton sidewalks are not wide enough to provide any feeling of safety for pedestrians.
When the protected York Boulevard bike lane was operational, the sense of safety walking down York, with its wide sidewalk, was unlike any road in the city. I surely appreciated the extra space and distance from busy traffic when walking my dogs. I don't take that route anymore.
But pedestrian safety is a byproduct of bike lanes, which are after all, for cyclists, although they are used by motorized vehicles as well, people riding scooters and electric bikes. The bike lanes are used by such a variety of vehicles, calling it a bike lane is a misnomer.
Society is moving toward alternative forms of transportation. SoBi bikes and Bird scooters are everywhere in my downtown neighbourhood. Electric bikes are growing in popularity. Improvements in public transit are always on the horizon, never quick enough, but consistently moving in the direction of more access.
Decades ago, I was on a committee to establish a network of bike lanes throughout the city.
I was an avid cyclist, I didn't drive and cycled everywhere; to work, to play, to visit family and friends. Planning was at the beginning stages and I didn't stay involved, but the results of those who carried on are enjoyed by cyclists all over Hamilton.
It's impossible to say how many lives have been saved by this network, how many accidents avoided. Although as I write this I'm reminded of images I saw recently of a twisted up bike and shaken up cyclist who was riding in the bike lane and met up with a car.
Nothing is perfect, which is sorry consolation to the cyclist, but we should be moving in that direction and toward improving the network, not diminishing it or starving it from growing.
This idea of no more bike lanes on existing roads, which is what this essentially means, is beyond backwards. It's dangerous. It will lead to more deaths as people move with the times and toward alternative forms of movement and seek their space on the sidewalks or roads.
Doug Ford loves drivers. There is nothing he wouldn't do for them. Rob the treasury of vehicle registration fees, rape the environment for new roads, invest billions in EV technology and pump up production of electric vehicles.
This is just another salvo in his war for the car and against anyone riding in the bike lane who are in his way. Beep. Beep.
Margaret Shkimba
is a Hamilton writer.
For more, visit margaret-shkimba.Substack.com.
You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) and
friend her on Facebook.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Margaret Shkimba OPINION Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more, visit margaret-shkimba.Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) and friend her on Facebook.;

Document 479 of 500

'Put them on secondary roads:' Doug Ford shoots down criticism of controversial plan to limit bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/put-them-on-secondary-roads-doug-ford-shoots-down/docview/3108515902/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Put+them+on+secondary+roads%3A%27+Doug+Ford+shoots+down+criticism+of+controversial+plan+to+limit+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is defending his government's push to limit bike lanes in Ontario municipalities, saying cars and trucks should have full priority on major roads like Bloor Street and University Avenue.
"We just want to get traffic moving ... that's what it comes down to, making sure you aren't putting bike lanes in the middle of some of the busiest streets in the country," Ford told reporters Monday.
"Put them on secondary roads."
The controversial plan - which government sources have confirmed is in upcoming legislation titled the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act" - would block construction of new bike lanes if they result in the loss of other lanes to cars, buses, trucks and other traffic.
It quickly sparked concern when details leaked last week. Six cyclists have been killed on Toronto streets this year.
"This is politics that plays with people's lives," said New Democrat MPP Joel Harden (Ottawa Centre), his party's transportation critic.
"This is putting wedge politics over real solutions."
University of Toronto geography and planning professor Matti Siemiatycki called the plan to limit bike lanes "ineffective and dangerous" as people increasingly take to bicycles to get around.
Toronto city council recently voted in favour of adding 100 kilometres of bike lanes to major routes within three years to reduce vehicle emissions and combat climate change.
But Ford was not backing down during a water and sewer funding announcement for a new housing development in Cobourg, an hour's drive east of Toronto.
He bristled at notions that removing motor vehicle lanes in favour of bike lanes doesn't increase congestion.
"That's a bunch of hogwash," Ford said, insisting the reduction of lanes on University Avenue's "hospital row" south of College Street to make way for bike lanes has made that stretch "jammed like crazy."
He also cited Bloor Street, claiming bike lanes have "disrupted all the businesses there."
"We need to focus on transportation routes. They get people ... from point A to point B."
Ford evaded a question about why he repeatedly says municipalities "know best" where housing is needed as the province battles a shortage of affordable new homes, but now wants to override municipal powers on where bike lanes should be located.
Critics said there is an inconsistency there as the premier apparently looks to court support from motorists and suburbanites as he mulls whether to call an early election next spring instead of waiting until the next slated provincewide vote in June 2026.
"Nobody asked for the government to dictate people's transportation choices or get in the way of local communities doing what works best for them," said Harden.
Ford was criticized for meddling in municipal affairs shortly after he was elected premier in 2018 and passed legislation cutting the size of Toronto city council nearly in half during the city's election campaign.
An Etobicoke resident, he has frequently slammed the expansion of bike lanes in Toronto. City staff have found bike ridership increases when new protected bike lanes are installed.
The premier also sounded off Monday about environmental assessments for Highway 413, which would link Highway 401 at Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan.
"Let's build the damn highway. There's hundreds of thousands of people stuck in our cars backed up from here to Timbuktu, and you're worried about a grasshopper jumping across the highway."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces a $25-million investment in water infrastructure to help enable the construction of new homes in Cobourg on Sept. 23, 2024.

Document 480 of 500

Here's why the new Bonnie Crombie sounds like the old Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-why-new-bonnie-crombie-sounds-like-old-doug/docview/3108515479/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+why+the+new+Bonnie+Crombie+sounds+like+the+old+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals had billed it as a weekend policy conference to brainstorm for a few good ideas on good governance.
But when Bonnie Crombie finally took the podium, she skipped the (policy) platform. Pondering her path to power, Crombie chose a shortcut.
No policy bible, no fine print, no road map to reviving and revivifying the moribund Liberals. While it's still early days, the party leader detoured from any details for the 1,000 party delegates meeting in London.
Henceforth, her provincial Liberals will build their campaign all around Doug Ford. And downplay their ties to Justin Trudeau's unpopular federal Liberals.
To be clear, she won't just focus on Ford's faults. Crombie's Liberals are also stealing the premier's proven playbook - battle-tested in two winning elections - and turning it against his governing Tories with a vengeance.
Remember Ford's winning campaign in 2018, when he badmouthed Kathleen Wynne's Liberals as corrupt parasites allegedly enriching their "insider" friends? Remember the attacks against "hallway health care" and the cheesy slogans promising "buck a beer?"
Now, Crombie wants to flip Ford's script, by turning his own words against him. If you can't beat them, borrow their sloganeering.
Which is why the new Crombie sounds increasingly like the old Ford, circa 2018. Policy light, rhetoric heavy.
At her final pep rally Saturday evening, the culmination of all those hours of deep thought by the party faithful, there was barely a peep about policy. Just chapter headings on health care and education, with a promise of more to come - hopefully before Ford springs an early election on the opposition parties next spring.
"So how will we do all of this?" she asked rhetorically, before retreating from her own challenge.
"We start by doing less for the rich insiders - the people who are getting ahead while everyone else is falling behind. Less for Doug's buddies. Less for the people who are under criminal investigation."
She complained about "hospital hallways" that are crammed, echoing Ford's old complaints. And then Crombie delivered her carefully prepared punch line, that she hopes will shake up Ontario politics:
"We are going to do less for them, so we can do more for you."
In case you missed it, that phrase, first heard at London's convention centre, will resound and redound across the province if the Liberals have their way. On cue, as Crombie led the chant, her followers shouted it out:
"Less for them. More for you."
As slogans go, it's not merely clunky but cheesy. It encapsulates both the politics of envy and the pocketbook politics of our era.
It frames politics as entirely transactional, not transformational. It is anti-tax and all about affordability.
Will it sell?
To be sure, people are feeling besieged by economic disparities. But the solutions are not as simple as politicians pretend.
Affordability and tax-cutting won't pay the bills. Sustainability and efficiency are part of the equation.
Without sustainable tax revenues, you can't sustain the education and health services that people count on, and that public sector workers bargain on. If you can't drive efficiencies in an $85-billion health-care budget (without reflexively invoking privatization sloganeering and scaremongering), you won't be able to fix overflowing hospital hallways.
On the environment, Crombie says her provincial Liberals want nothing to do with any carbon tax. But she hasn't even hinted at her own alternative, beyond saying she wants to "protect our planet."
For his part, Ford never presented any serious policies in his last two campaigns, other than his perennial "axe the tax" attack on any federal carbon levy. Nor has NDP Leader Marit Stiles come forth with serious new policies since taking over her party.
Meanwhile, the Tories are spending big money on ads that frame and defame Crombie as a posh and pampered politician who is out of touch with ordinary Ontarians. Now, the Liberal leader is recasting Ford as the real rich kid who grew up with a silver spoon in his father's Etobicoke "mansion," heir to a family firm and political dynasty, while she is the hard scrabble survivor who grew up in a "rooming house" raised by a single mother.
Will this battle of personal narratives speak to voters? Is it a substitute for the detailed policy wars of the past, when all three major parties offered substantive platforms instead of placeholders for personal brands?
Voters hungry for substance will have to content themselves with a content-free pitch from Crombie (and Stiles), each insisting that corruption has insinuated itself into every sinew of Ford's being. The challenge is that Ford remains far ahead in the polls and keeps winning byelections, most recently by promising to "Get it done!"
Can Crombie outdo Ford in slogans? That depends on how - or whether - she ever answers her own question in her weekend speech:
"How will we do all of this?"
Good question.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at the party's gathering in London, Ont. on Sept. 21, 2024.

Subject: Voters

Title: Here's why the new Bonnie Crombie sounds like the old Doug Ford

Document 481 of 500

The scary truth behind this viral video of Jagmeet Singh

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/scary-truth-behind-this-viral-video-jagmeet-singh/docview/3108249871/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+scary+truth+behind+this+viral+video+of+Jagmeet+Singh&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's something cathartic about watching bullies get put in their place.
That's likely a big reason why a video showing NDP leader Jagmeet Singh confronting and schooling a far-right heckler is resonating with so many people after going viral last week.
The video starts with Singh being followed around Parliament Hill by a group of hecklers filming him on their phones. The hecklers are part of a spin-off group that has been gathering daily outside Parliament since the end of the Freedom Convoy two years ago - a number of them say they are banned from entering the House of Commons for security reasons.
After one man affiliated with the group cursed at Singh, the NDP leader stopped, turned around and asked him: "You got something to say?"
"It wasn't me," the man stuttered. "It was the gentleman behind me, I guess." He twirled around but there was no such gentleman to be seen.
"You're a coward," Singh told the man, standing chest to chest. "You're not going to say it to my face."
Most who saw the video took delight in watching a man behaving like an Internet troll melt faster than a snowflake on a sunny day when confronted in real life. Others seemed relieved to see a political leader finally stand up to the obnoxious convoy crowd.
Only a few hours earlier that day, members of that same group disrupted a ceremony honouring Terry Fox, shouting "traitors," "liars" and "f-k Trudeau."
Other members of the group have been following MPs around downtown Ottawa screaming "treason" and "criminals." They claim these confrontations are justified because they are holding elected officials "accountable."
In our democracy, the public has a right to question elected politicians. In fact, the public has a democratic duty to hold them accountable. But this group is confusing public accountability with personal abuse.
Watching their videos, it's hard not to wonder how any normal person would react to a group of men following them down the street screaming profanities while broadcasting their abusive behaviour live on social media.
Singh himself has spoken openly about his experiences with childhood bullying. His brother Gurratan, a former Ontario NDP MPP, reacted to the video by pointing out his older brother has "stood up to bullies his whole life and we all know bullies fold when someone stands up to them."
This is undeniably true. And yet this is Parliament Hill, not a schoolyard. Why should elected officials be subjected to behaviour considered dangerously inappropriate for seven-year-olds?
This exact kind of confrontation could easily escalate and spiral out of control, dragging innocent bystanders into a dangerous mess. It's only a matter of time before an MP, a random pedestrian or a tourist gets hurt.
Still there is something about this viral moment that felt inevitable.
Elected officials have been dealing with these volatile confrontations more and more since the end of the pandemic. Last year, Chrystia Freeland was aggressively ambushed by a local conspiracy theorist in Grande Prairie, Alberta. Jagmeet Singh and a Radio-Canada journalist were harassed by a man who tried to place them under "citizen's arrest."
We know national security agencies have been monitoring threats targeting cabinet ministers and MPs, particularly women, from convoy-adjacent extremists.
Make no mistake, this is happening across the political spectrum. People affiliated with these groups have been charged for criminal harassment outside the home of Newfoundland and Labrador's Liberal premier, Andrew Fury. Others have staged protests outside the homes of Ontario Premier Doug Ford and former Progressive Conservative Health Minister Christine Elliot. Liberal, Conservative or NDP, they don't seem to care.
It's happening at every level of government too. In Pickering, Ontario, local city councillors say they are being overwhelmed by out-of-town far-right conspiracy groups.
It's too simplistic to describe this phenomenon as a decline in decorum and civility. The far-right is engaging in tactics targeted at Canada's democratic institutions.
These groups will claim their abusive and harassing behaviours are justified in the name of patriotism, accountability or even free speech. But unless we see concrete steps to address this scourge and create safe workplaces for public officials, more and more Canadians will simply opt-out of public life.
Whatever you think of his politics, Singh was right about one thing. We can't let he bullies win. Our democracy is at stake.
CAPTION: Protestors linked to the so-called Freedom Convoy have been accused of harassing and even abusing politicians across Canada.Luke LeBrun is the editor PressProgress.
CREDIT: Luke LeBrun

Subject: Extremism; Conspiracy; Democracy; Public officials; Accountability

Location: Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Document 483 of 500

Liberal leader Crombie takes aim at Ford in campaign-style speech at AGM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-leader-crombie-takes-aim-at-ford-campaign/docview/3107938459/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+leader+Crombie+takes+aim+at+Ford+in+campaign-style+speech+at+AGM&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's new Liberal leader took aim at the premier over the weekend as she tested out a new slogan in a campaign-style speech at her party's annual general meeting.
Bonnie Crombie's address to members came on Saturday while the party gathered in London, Ont., to set policies amid the threat of an early election call.
"It's time for a government that does less for them and more for you," Crombie said.
She used the "more for you" line a number of times, saying it's what Ontarians deserve.
It was Crombie's first speech at the annual meeting since winning the party's leadership race late last year.
She used much of her speech to blast Premier Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative government, refering to the party nearly two dozen times throughout her remarks.
"Right now people aren't thriving, they're barely surviving," Crombie said.
"And you know what? Doug Ford's to blame."
The former mayor of Mississauga, Ont., pledged to do more on health care, education and housing, but did not offer details about how she would do so.
Ontario, like the rest of Canada, continues to grapple with an affordability crisis, especially in housing.
Ford has promised to build 1.5 million homes by 2031, but a difficult market with high interest rates and several missteps has kept his government well off the pace to achieve that goal.
The RCMP is currently investigating Ford's Greenbelt fiasco, which saw the province open up 15 parcels of protected land to build 50,000 homes. Two provincial investigative bodies have said the province favoured certain developers over others during that process.
Ford eventually returned those lands to the Greenbelt and his government has tried a number of different approaches to spur housing development.
Health care organizations across the province continue to deal with staffing shortages among doctors, nurses and a variety of other support workers.
While Crombie spoke at length about Ford, she did not mention her federal Liberal counterparts, namely embattled Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 484 of 500

Teacher unions, Ontario Liberals mend fences at party's weekend meeting: 'We have to have alliances'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/teacher-unions-ontario-liberals-mend-fences-at/docview/3107938470/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teacher+unions%2C+Ontario+Liberals+mend+fences+at+party%27s+weekend+meeting%3A+%27We+have+to+have+alliances%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LONDON, ONT. - After a decade-long rift, the Ontario Liberals and the province's teacher unions are starting to play nice in the sandbox.
The party's annual meeting here on the weekend featured a 90-minute education policy session led by the presidents of all four major teacher unions - something that has not happened in recent memory - who received an enthusiastic standing ovation at the end.
"It will be a co-operative collaboration ... There's no payoff when there's friction, only the kids suffer," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie told the Star in an interview, acknowledging the party learned the hard way when a decade of strong ties abruptly ended in 2012 after the then-McGuinty government imposed contracts on teachers as part of an austerity push, sparking 10-plus years of strained relations.
"We've all acknowledged that we need to invest in our educators," Crombie said. "We (are) serious about fixing education and training our kids for success."
On Saturday, Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario president Karen Brown told the Star she believes the PC government has failed educators and students "and we're looking at parties that have platforms that support public education. They're seeing us as key stakeholders, and they want to dialogue with us on that, and we're here to give our expertise."
The Liberals "want to listen, and we're hoping to see some of that expertise embedded in their policy," she said after the panel discussion.
But, she added, it won't be easy, as "there are some things that our members are going to have a difficult time still with reconciling," including their long-standing resentment of the McGuinty government's Bill 115, legislation that was ultimately ruled unconstitutional by the courts.
"But we need to look forward and move ahead," Brown said. "So this is part of building on our commonalities and repairing those relationships."
In 2018, ETFO - the country's largest such union - took the unusual step of endorsing the NDP in the provincial election.
Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, said "we don't have the kind of relations we'd want to have with the Conservatives, and we've been on-again, off-again with the Liberals. They are at least saying the right things right now" and consulting with the unions.
"The trust level still remains to be rebuilt with our members because I participate in an event like this, and I will hear from people who will be very angry, but we can't disagree with everyone," said Littlewood. "At some point in time, we have to have alliances ... what we need are parties that are going to listen to what's happening in education and are actually going to do something about it."
Experts say both sides benefit from improved relations.
The unions "recognize that if there is going to be a change in government, it's going to be Liberal," said Nelson Wiseman, a longtime political expert and professor emeritus at the University of Toronto. "They've already decided that the Ford government, Conservative governments generally, are not sympathetic to them. The Liberals and the teachers' unions are, in effect, courting each other."
Wiseman said he expects the unions to also be a part of NDP policy development given that their leadership tends to be more in line with the New Democrats.
Professor Jonathan Rose, who heads the political studies department at Queen's University, said "it's a competition on the left to get union support and both parties - the Liberals and the NDP - are trying to do that."
Littlewood said 2012 "was a bad year for (the Liberals) - it was a bad move. But I have to give (recent interim leader) John Fraser credit, because I can't tell you the number of times I've heard him apologize in the legislature to us for that."
Fraser has even reminded the Ford government of the Liberals' mistake when the Tories imposed wage-capping legislation on public sector workers - which was also later ruled unconstitutional by the courts, Littlewood noted.
(The Liberals' Bill 115 ultimately cost taxpayers almost $200 million in payments to teachers, and so far the Ford government has paid out $6.7 billion in retroactive pay raises to the broader public sector.)
Since the Liberal party has just nine MPPs, and lacks party status in the legislature, "they're going to say the right things, they want our support," Littlewood added. "But we approach everything with caution, to make sure that they are consulting with us and making decisions that will be beneficial to our members."
Some members do have lingering resentment over Bill 115 - and sometimes raise the unpopular "Rae Days" imposed by the NDP government in the early 1990s, she added.
"I hear it at meetings, but I also hear people say that we've got to do something. We're trying to make sure that when we develop a platform, we're sharing it with the parties, and that the parties are reflecting that in their platforms too."
At some point, Littlewood added, "you have to move on. You can't hate everybody forever."
René Jansen in de Wal, president of the Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association, said "I've always been willing to talk to whoever can help us move to the most important things we all agree on - so I've always been open to talking to all parties."
After Saturday's education panel, he said it's significant that all four teacher union presidents were asked, and accepted, the Liberals' invitation.
"We're on the same stage together," he said. "That's pretty unusual - I don't know if it's ever happened before, but it's symbolic."
CAPTION: Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario president Karen Brown, seen in 2023, told the Star that her members give the governing PCs a failing grade and 'we're looking at parties that have platforms that support public education.'Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombieat the Liberals' convention in London, Ont. on Saturday.

Title: Teacher unions, Ontario Liberals mend fences at party's weekend meeting: 'We have to have alliances'

Document 485 of 500

Why the stakes are the highest they've ever been for Jagmeet Singh: 'The moment that makes or breaks his political career'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-stakes-are-highest-theyve-ever-been-jagmeet/docview/3107696417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+the+stakes+are+the+highest+they%27ve+ever+been+for+Jagmeet+Singh%3A+%27The+moment+that+makes+or+breaks+his+political+career%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Inside the world of Jagmeet Singh, things are looking pretty swell.
The NDP leader prematurely withdrew from his increasingly perilous alliance with Justin Trudeau's minority Liberals - after extracting almost everything he wanted from him.
He posted a byelection result he insists proves the New Democrats are more competitive than any other party.
He confronted a heckler on Parliament Hill, and achieved the impossible by earning the internet's respect.
That was just in the last two weeks.
But the worst thing Singh could do now is be complacent.
The stakes are the highest they've ever been for the NDP leader, who is approaching his eighth year - and third election - at the party's helm.
For several years, the New Democrats have been stagnant in the polls, and over the past two federal contests, the party's seat count has only grown by one.
"It's a big inflection point," said Christo Aivalis, a historian of Canadian politics and labour issues.
"This is going to be the moment that makes or breaks his political career."
Many in New Democratic circles are acutely aware of this moment: support for the Liberals is the lowest it has ever been under Singh's reign, even if it seems like Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are the immediate beneficiaries of that decline.
"This is our opportunity. We have a unique window, and windows like this don't often open up for the New Democrats, given the old school politics of Canada," veteran NDP MP Charlie Angus told the Star.
"There's a whole huge swath of middle voters who are going to be testing us. They're going to be checking us out. So this is our opportunity to reach out to them."
Singh must show Canadians that he can make the transition from an opposition leader to someone with the potential to be a national one, the outgoing MP said, just as former NDP leader Jack Layton did in 2011.
"That really, to me, was a transformative moment. And I think this is the moment where Jagmeet will need to step up and show that he is prime ministerial material," said Angus, of Singh's need to harness his likability, but also demonstrate strength in areas like economic policy.
"That's going to come down to putting an offer on the table that Canadians come to believe in, particularly given the very polarized conditions we're in," Angus said.
For others, Singh's most urgent challenge is defending his early exit from his supply-and-confidence agreement with the Liberals and inching out from beneath Justin Trudeau's shadow.
"The prime minister is deeply unpopular and I think it's: how do you position yourself as the alternative for people who don't want to vote for the Conservatives and don't support Pierre Poilievre?" said Matthew Dubé, the vice-president of Proof Strategies and a former NDP MP who served under the party's last three permanent leaders.
"What remains a challenge is that despite the free fall of Liberal support, it hasn't yet translated into support for the NDP."
At doorsteps, Singh can safely point to the policy goals around health care and workers' rights that he pushed for and secured under the deal.
But the Liberals can also take credit for those achievements. And in the first days after he left the governing pact, Singh struggled to explain to the public why, despite his belief that Canadians want Trudeau out and his insistence that the NDP is ready to mount an election campaign, he wouldn't commit to triggering a national contest at the earliest opportunity.
(Singh has since said he's looking for extra commitments on issues like food affordability, and that he wouldn't support Poilievre's immediate efforts to take down the Trudeau government.)
Angus said he was initially "worried" about the political repercussions of leaving the deal, but said Singh needs to cut through the drama of election speculation and instead "speak directly to Canadians."
The difficulty lies now in taking on a Conservative party that has thrived on using charged rhetoric to undermine Canada's left, said former New Democratic Party president Dhananjai Kohli.
"I think it is going to be a challenge to break through that and be able to communicate with folks," Kohli said.
"I think the biggest challenge for Jagmeet is going to be how he's going to be able to cut through that noise and be able to cut through the persona that Poilievre has falsely created of him, and be able to connect with people in his true self."
That "true self" is one of the most important things in Singh's arsenal, said Elias Assefa, the riding association president for Edmonton-Centre.
It has surfaced in the past when Singh has responded to protesters with "love and courage," and again this week when he channelled that intensity in a blunt confrontation with a heckler in Ottawa.
"I love how he connects to people. I love how he always brings it back to material realities. It's not just about esoteric ideas of freedom. It's about how much groceries can you afford? How are you going to move into a house or afford rent?" Assefa told the Star.
Even so, Assefa acknowledged that Poilievre's message appears to be resonating: something that is cause for concern in a riding like Edmonton-Centre, which is poised for a showdown between the NDP and Conservatives to unseat the Liberals.
"People are frustrated and they're reaching out to anybody that's willing to tell them what they want to hear," he said.
One of the best things Singh can do now is be seen where it counts, said Davenport riding association president Corrigan Hammond.
Hammond's Toronto riding is a red-orange split: the New Democrats lost to the Liberals there in 2021 by a margin of fewer than 100 votes.
"We're pretty formidable in Davenport and I think we could win it. I just want to see more of him," Hammond said.
The NDP's local ground game is one of the biggest areas where the party could stand to improve, said Miles Krauter, a longtime organizer for the Ontario and federal NDP.
While the NDP can credibly say it did well in a pair of byelections held last week - the party held on to their seat in Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona, and significantly grew their share of the vote in Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun - there are lessons to be learned.
The Conservatives led a compelling challenge in Winnipeg, closing the gap between both parties. And in Montreal's Liberal stronghold, the NDP landed in third place after a tight, three-way race that saw the party roll out one of its strongest ever byelection campaigns.
"I think we failed to win that election for the same reason a lot of NDP campaigns have failed. And I hesitate to say that. I know folks are still raw from the loss and are not going to want to hear it," said Krauter, who briefly volunteered on the Montreal campaign and said the party needs to improve its voter outreach strategy and provide more guidance to local campaigns.
The grassroots organizer said that not all of that work is up to Singh, but that he thought the NDP leader - who has "pleasantly surprised" Krauter - might usher in a new era of ground-level strategizing.
"I was happily surprised by the hustle that (Singh's) leadership campaign showed. And I was really hopeful that we would see more of that translate into the actual operations of the party," Krauter said, of Singh's 2017 first-ballot win.
"He really demonstrated that he was willing to do the work and do that relationship building and that outreach on a massive scale. It was very impressive. But where is that now?"
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh walks on Parliament Hill, in Ottawa Sept. 16, 2024, as members of Parliament return from summer break.

Subject: Presidents; Canadians; Political leadership; Political parties

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin
