Ontario's controversial nursing home transfer law is being challenged in court. Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-controversial-nursing-home-transfer-law/docview/3107696347/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+controversial+nursing+home+transfer+law+is+being+challenged+in+court.+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A controversial Ontario law that can force hospital patients to choose between moving into a long-term-care home they didn't pick or face fines will be challenged in court this week.
Beginning on Monday, lawyers for the Ontario Health Coalition and Advocacy Centre for the Elderly will argue against the Ford government's More Beds, Better Care Act, introduced in 2022 as a way to deal with a shortage of acute care beds in hospitals.
The two organizations argue that the legislation violates the Charter rights of hospital patients by targeting a specific group of "older, ill and very vulnerable patients" and removing their right to informed consent about where they will live and the care they receive.
Under the Act, patients designated "alternate level of care" (ALC) - believed to no longer need hospital care - who are waiting for space in long-term care can be required to move to a different long-term care facility not of their choosing or pay $400 each day they remain in hospital.
However, there are no clear standards for how to determine who is ALC, leading to inconsistencies, including some patients being labelled ALC who still need hospital treatment, say the organizations challenging the law.
As a result, the law violates the patients' right to life, liberty and security of the person, is contrary to fundamental justice and denies patients' right to equality, the groups say in a summary of their submission.
"When you hear from the patients who've gone through this and their families ... it's torture," said Natalie Mehra, executive director of the health coalition.
The government denies that the law violates the Charter, maintaining that it is necessary in light of the shortage of public hospital beds and that there is no evidence that it targets patients of a particular age or ability.
"The patient waiting in the emergency room has the same constitutional right to 'medical autonomy' as the ALC patient waiting for an LTC home in a hospital bed that they no longer need," reads the province's submission.
The law says hospitals must still make "reasonable efforts" to get the consent of the patient or their decision-maker. Before the law, hospitals could charge much higher amounts to ALC patients.
The problem with the law, the critics said, is that it strips patients of their choices and can mean patients are moved into facilities which don't offer culture- or religion-specific services. Patients can also be moved far from family and friends - up to 150 kilometres away in northern Ontario, or 70 kilometres away in southern Ontario.
"I think people are being forced into situations that are not good for the patients and not good for the families," said Gail Herrington, 67, who said a hospital told her to change her care home preferences for her late husband to prioritize ones with shorter wait-lists.
Chris Glover, NDP MPP Spadina-Fort York, called the law "the thin edge of the wedge for privatizing our health-care system in Ontario."
Though patients and their families may be told to add different long-term care homes to their choices for temporary stay until a bed in another home becomes available, the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly wrote in an online document about the law, once a hospital patient is admitted to a home, they are no longer considered a priority for another home.
Under the law, an ALC patient can decline any medical treatment - including a physical exam to assess eligibility for long-term care - and decline moving into LTC. The Act does not allow ALC patients to be physically transferred to LTC without their consent, the submission adds.
"There is no Charter right to publicly funded health care," the province argued. "It would therefore be anomalous if a patient had a ... right to choose to remain in a public hospital free of charge even after they no longer require the intensity of resources or services provided in the hospital care setting."
The Canadian Press reported that 293 ALC patients were moved from hospital to long-term care homes they didn't choose from September 2022 to January 2024. As of April 2024, seven people had been fined under the Act.
CAPTION: TheFord government's More Beds, Better Care Act was introduced in 2022 as a way to deal with a shortage of acute care beds in hospitals.

Subject: Patients; Long term health care; Hospitalization; Consent

'More for you' to be Ontario Liberals' new message as Bonnie Crombie tries to kick-start party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-you-be-ontario-liberals-new-message-as/docview/3107645644/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27More+for+you%27+to+be+Ontario+Liberals%27+new+message+as+Bonnie+Crombie+tries+to+kick-start+party&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LONDON, ONT. - "More for you" is the new message as Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie looks to kick-start the party's fortunes and get ready for a provincial election that could be called sooner than expected.
In an exclusive, sit-down interview with the Star, Crombie also said a new ad - one that she's just wrapped up filming - will begin airing when the legislature resumes in late October so Ontarians can get to know her and what direction the party is going in.
But experts say Crombie faces a tough road ahead, with the party running a distant second in most polls and feeling the weight of the federal Liberals' woes as it tries to rebuild after back-to-back dismal showings in the last two provincial elections.
"We truly believe the only people getting ahead in Ontario today are the wealthy friends, rich insiders. We've seen that time and time again," Crombie said, referring to a number of controversies including the Ford government's recent decision to speed up beer and cooler sales in corner stores at a cost it has pegged at $225 million, but which critics have estimated to be much higher.
"It's the lobbyists, it's the well-connected, rich insiders, friends of the premier," said Crombie, adding the Liberals will focus on the theme "it's going to be less for them, more for you."
In her spirited speech Saturday to the more-than 1,000 party faithful gathered here for their annual meeting and policy convention, Crombie said the top priority of a Liberal government would be fixing Ontario's health system and ensuring everyone has a family doctor.
"Our hospital hallways are crammed with patients who can't get care - 2,000 people, every single day, here in Ontario lay on stretchers in break rooms or crammed in corridors while they wait for hospital care," she said.
"Instead of fixing health care, Doug spends his time on a billion-dollar scheme to put beer in the 7-Eleven a bit sooner. It's time for a government in Ontario that's focused on you."
Crombie, who spoke of her humble roots as the child of single mom who grew up in her family's rooming house, said "too many people are struggling to pay the bills in the province ... that's not who we are. That's not how things should be. People deserve more - and we're going to get it for them."
Some insiders are frustrated about why the Liberal leader, who was elected nine months ago, has not been more visible, saying Crombie needs to boost her public profile - especially if an election is called earlier than 2026.
But Jonathan Rose, head of the political studies department at Queen's University, said "if she's keeping a low profile because she is working with party leaders and party presidents across the province, and candidates, and if she's keeping a low profile because she's ensuring that she is election-ready in terms of fundraising ... that's a great thing at this stage."
But the danger, he added, is that "she runs the risk of being defined by the government ... and if there's no alternative, competing messaging, that messaging will stick."
Since becoming leader, Crombie said she's spent time with her caucus, been at Queen's Park and travelled "to the key ridings that were important for us, (and is) continuing to get to know stakeholders and their issues." She said she's spoken to people across the province, and attended dozens of events over the summer.
She told the Star she's also spent time in rural areas, listening to voters there "to see how we can better speak to those communities."
Crombie also said the PC's efforts, using attack ads to paint her as someone who is out of touch and who would raise taxes, haven't worked.
The Liberals, which now have confirmed or soon-to-be confirmed candidates in 19 ridings, also unveiled a new logo at the weekend convention - held in a city that was a one-time Liberal stronghold where all three city ridings have gone to the NDP in the last decade, with the PCs holding Elgin-Middlesex-London.
Crombie said she feels the party had momentum coming into the weekend convention after Thursday's byelection in the Bay of Quinte riding, where Liberal candidate Sean Kelly came in second but boosted the party's performance well beyond its results in the riding in the 2022 general election.
"I'm delighted," she said. "I mean, obviously you always want to win, but I think it demonstrates now, a second or third time, that we really are the party that people are looking for if they're not going to vote for Doug Ford, and they want to see a change of government."
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, at an event in Peterborough this past May, says a new ad that she's just wrapped up filming will begin airing when the legislature resumes in late October so Ontarians can get to know her and the party's direction.

Document 488 of 500

Mounting byelection losses increase pressure on Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mounting-byelection-losses-increase-pressure-on/docview/3107472621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mounting+byelection+losses+increase+pressure+on+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The loss in Quebec's LaSalle-Emard-Verdun byelection appears to have had no effect on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's determination to continue, despite the latest Ipsos poll which shows his further drop in overall voter approval to 26 per cent, versus Pierre Poilievre's rating of 45 per cent.
This gap is widening despite Poilievre's dangerous proposals, which include elimination of the carbon tax; a cap on immigration limits pending substantial housing increases; cancellation of CBC funding; and a crackdown on transgender rights.
There is as well his previous support of the 2022 "Freedom Convoy's" destructive Ottawa behaviour, and more recently his Trump-like "wacko" jargon both in Parliament and in his increasing public appearances.
Do the Liberals have an effective response to their declining public popularity?
The Liberal Party has no applicable leadership review mechanism built into its constitution. There is a provision for a leadership endorsement ballot, which allows them to vote on whether they still support their leader. But this vote only can occur after the party loses a general election.
The party would have to amend its constitution to force a leadership review which could only take place at its next annual general meeting. That is not expected before 2025. With a year left in its minority government, subject to defeat at any time, an earlier AGM is virtually impossible.
Within hours of the byelection loss, Trudeau predictably repeated he has no intention of resigning and he will be the leader in the next election. Given the statements so far made by his cabinet colleagues, it appears there is no prospect they will request him to step down.
The Bloc Québécois' byelection victory poses a new danger. Its leader, Yves-François Blanchet, questioned about the prospect of his party renewing the sovereignty movement, refused to say yes or no. Clearly this is a very troublesome possibility, and one which should emphasize the need for strong party leadership to uphold Trudeau's father's successful preservation of Canada.
Despite these troublesome concerns, and assuming the unlikely possibility that the Liberal party takes timely action to achieve a new leader, who are the best prospects?
Mark Carney is frequently mentioned, but given his recent appointment by Trudeau as chair of the "Taskforce on Economic Growth," he is an unlikely candidate. Granted, his training and performance as an economist and banker is exceptional, followed by his career as both governor of the Bank of Canada and governor of the Bank of England, and more recently head of impact investing at Brookfield Asset Management and chair of Bloomberg Inc.
However, he lacks parliamentary experience, a crucial requirement in facing the debating skills of Poilievre.
Another possibility is Dominic LeBlanc, an able federal parliamentarian, now holding the position of Minister of Public Safety, Democratic Institutions and Intergovernmental Affairs, following several previous important posts in the Trudeau cabinet. However, he has regrettably suffered severe health problems since 2017. Although thankfully recovered, he has indicated repetitively that he does not wish to replace Trudeau.
This, in my view, leaves one extremely able and strong alternative - Bob Rae, Canada's Ambassador to the UN. In addition to that top diplomatic position, he was recently internally elected as president of the UNs Economic and Social Council for a one-year term.
However, I strongly believe that he would be the best replacement. I have written previously about his many positive accomplishments as Ontario's premier in the 1990s, including successful promotion of the Jobs Ontario Program; supporting action on numerous Indigenous issues; increased investment on child care; training incentives for employers hiring people on welfare and workers whose employment insurance had run out, all resulting in the creation of over 50,000 new jobs.
By the end of the Rae government's term, Ontario led the way in economic growth among the provinces and had one of the strongest economies in the G7. After he left the NDP, he joined the Liberal party and served as an elected member for five years - two of which as its interim Liberal leader.
All options depend of course on Trudeau's early departure from his position. A hopeless expectation? Let's hope not!
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks at the riding office of Laura Palestini, Liberal candidate for LaSalle-Emard-Verdun in Montreal ahead of the recent byelection. The Bloc Québécois took the longtime Liberal riding.
CREDIT: Tim Armstrong

Document 489 of 500

Poilievre loves to attack Chow and Trudeau on housing, but Doug Ford is Ontario's chief NIMBY

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievre-loves-attack-chow-trudeau-on-housing/docview/3107472609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+loves+to+attack+Chow+and+Trudeau+on+housing%2C+but+Doug+Ford+is+Ontario%27s+chief+NIMBY&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this month, Scott Aitchison, Conservative MP for Parry Sound-Muskoka, posted a video he filmed at 91 Barton Ave. in Toronto's Seaton Village neighbourhood. The address is notable because a proposal to build a three-storey, eight-unit multiplex apartment building plus two laneway units here was rejected twice by Toronto's Committee of Adjustment.
The committee decides if applications that don't conform to existing regulations are allowed or not. The Barton project required some minor variances but was denied in the spring. The developer revised plans but it was rejected again in the summer.
Never mind that three blocks away on Barton, far larger apartment buildings exist, happily part of the neighbourhood for decades, or that Seaton Village, like many parts of Toronto, has gone down in population over the last 70 years, or that there are two subway stations nearby, an ideal location for more density.
All of this instigated a rightful fury in housing and civic circles. Over at Spacing, columnist John Lorinc argued we should burn down the Committee of Adjustment and create something that isn't a tool used by NIMBYs to block reasonable and needed projects during a housing crisis.
For his part, Aitchison strutted down the alleyway next to 91 and railed against Trudeau, Toronto City Hall gatekeepers and its NIMBY residents too. Perhaps he would do the same if residents in his own riding - in towns like Huntsville, Bracebridge, Gravenhurst or Muskoka - objected to a similar development.
One hopes, but he strutted in Toronto and it's hard to argue with some of his aspersions. The Barton situation is another grievously embarrassing Toronto episode, instigated by both the committee and residents. Mayor Chow could and should be leading some of the civic castigation as it undermines her own housing goals, but some of her progressive allies on council can be quite NIMBY themselves when it comes to adding density.
It's dismal: few have the courage to stand up to powerful and selfish homeowners.
Still, Toronto aside, it's extremely conspicuous that Aitchison chose to go after just Trudeau and Toronto, leapfrogging over the great big legislative and political elephant in the middle: Ontario. The province has incredible power over cities, not the least of which is the Ontario Planning Act. As Torontonians have experienced first-hand, Premier Ford has also not hesitated to meddle in city affairs, particularly when cutting the number of councillors in half during the 2018 election. Ford is willing to intervene when he fancies.
If Ford wanted to, he could abolish Toronto's Committee of Adjustment. He could clear out every gate and its keeper at City Hall. He could allow 10-unit apartment buildings to be built anywhere in the city (not a bad idea, actually, but Ford even backtracked on expanding permissions for smaller fourplexes last winter). He could allow skyscrapers on Barton if he and his party wanted to.
He could do whatever he wants to facilitate housing but he isn't, and Aitchison and his boss Pierre Poilievre aren't talking about it. They also aren't talking about Premier Ford's failure to deliver on housing and deliberate undermining of his own pro-housing bluster. Just look at the considerable evidence.
As Noor Javed reported in the Star last week, the Ford government fast-tracked the development of a block of land in Vaughan using a Ministerial Zoning Order (MZO), overriding the usual process, yet nothing has been built. Then the Star's David Rider reported on an affordable housing project in Conservative MPP Stan Cho's ward where the government has refused to issue an MZO for this desperately needed modular housing project that has some NIMBY constituents upset. So the housing rots away in a warehouse.
Ford has largely ignored the strong prescriptions set out by his own housing task force, and last week Global News reported that the "massive density" Ford proposed around transit stations is gone because Ontario PCs gutted their own housing bill. Ford, a resident of Toronto himself, doesn't sound like much of a housing champion - in fact, he sounds exactly like the kind of gatekeepers and local NIMBYs Aitchison was lambasting.
Poilievre has gotten a lot of traction with the gatekeeper rants, but Ford has proven he and his party are Ontario's most powerful gatekeepers. This is a problem for the federal conservatives because everyone who has watched Ford has heard the bluster about housing and they have seen how Ford can run roughshod over a city when he wants to, but they've also seen his government actively gut their own housing bill, backtracked on pro-housing positions and even prevented and failed to get actual housing built.
Toronto doesn't get off the hook, but the silence from Poilievre and company on Ford is conspicuous. How do we know they're not the same kind of conservatives who talk tough but don't actually follow through?
CAPTION: Toronto's Committee of Adjustment denied an application for a small multiplex apartment building here, at 91 Barton Ave. In August of 2024, over 40 artists transformed the soon-to-be-demolished into an immersive art installation.The silence from Pierre Poilievre and company on Doug Ford's role in Toronto's housing situation is conspicuous.

Subject: Committees; Affordable housing

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Poilievre loves to attack Chow and Trudeau on housing, but Doug Ford is Ontario's chief NIMBY

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 490 of 500

Pierre Poilievre returns to a shaky Parliament with more flop than flex

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-returns-shaky-parliament-with/docview/3107472597/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+returns+to+a+shaky+Parliament+with+more+flop+than+flex&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Parliament is back with a bang and things are not going Pierre Poilievre's way.
It started with a stinging byelection loss for the Conservatives in Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona, where they spent considerable resources trying to win a seat from the NDP. Conservative signs urged voters to reject NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh for crafting a deal with the Grits - "Sellout Singh" as he was branded on posters standing next to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and for supporting the Liberals' carbon levy.
It's too early to tell how many voters were swayed by the message - Singh certainly tried to distance himself from the Grits by pulling out of the supply and confidence agreement and opened the possibility he'll walk back support for a consumer price on carbon. But what is clear is that progressive voters, faced with a binary choice, flocked to the NDP. Despite a 16 per cent increase in Conservative support, the NDP vote held steady, likely because Liberal voters shifted to its side.
The result? Conservatives were unable to project themselves as a party with momentum, riding a wave of support after their byelection success in Toronto-St. Paul's in June, and demonstrating to voters that Canadians were rejecting the Liberal/NDP agenda.
It wasn't the only Conservative setback. Welcoming parliamentarians back to Ottawa was a small crowd of "F-k Trudeau" hecklers, who accused the prime minister of being "Satan," of sexually grooming children, and of treason. If Canadians needed a reminder of whose support Poilievre has been courting, it was right there for all to see. It didn't help that Conservative MP Michael Cooper was photographed with the protesters and thanked on social media for meeting with them. In a statement, Cooper said he was "in no way associated with these individuals" and had just been in a restaurant where he was recognized by them. He accused the Liberals of spreading lies about him.
Wednesday should have been a great day for the Tories. The Liberals had offered them an opposition day in the House next week, and Poilievre announced he would use it to put forward a simple motion of non-confidence in the government. It was a win-win: he would either get to show voters that Singh and Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet are hypocrites who claim not to support Trudeau but prop up his government, or he would get a federal election.
But Blanchet stole Poilievre's thunder when he said the Bloc would vote against the motion. Blanchet didn't have confidence in the Trudeau government but, he said, he was "reasonably confident" he could get increased pension payments and protection for supply management - and, if the Liberals didn't meet his demands, he was "reasonably confident" he'd get a chance to defeat them.
What was more telling, however, was his take on Poilievre. "I know the Conservatives' values and they are not Quebecers' values," he said. There was nothing in the non-confidence motion, Blanchet said, but Poilievre's "personal and disproportionate ambition" and his "impatience" to rule - which he didn't give "a damn" about.
The next day, it was the NDP's turn. Despite Singh having said the Liberals "can expect no help and no support from us," the New Democrats wisely tried to unpaint themselves out of that corner. With cover from the Bloc, the NDP tried to safeguard any influence it could have in obtaining policy gains and setting the timing of the next election. Singh set his sights on Poilievre as well, accusing him of wanting to cut pharmacare, health care, pensions and dental care. "Our plan is to stop him."
Though the Bloc and the NDP have their beefs with the Liberals, their leaders acknowledged this week that the greatest threat to the policies they champion are the Conservatives.
Even when Quebec Premier François Legault called on the Bloc Thursday to side with the Conservatives and defeat the Trudeau government, it was Poilievre who suffered from the endorsement. Quebec's newspapers called Legault to task for suggesting the province would be better off with a prime minister who wants to build a controversial natural gas project, opposes construction of a tramway in Quebec City, and has offered no commitments on health care or dental care.
Meanwhile in the House of Commons, Poilievre was asking the Bloc why it was abandoning Quebec's sovereigntists. Yes, that's right, the leader of the Conservative party - a federalist party - accused the Bloc of not defending the interests of separatists.
Liberal House Leader Karina Gould was all smiles that day. Assured the government would survive, she scheduled another Conservative opposition day for next Thursday, minimizing the Grits' chances of defeat as swiftly as possible.
The Liberals appeared revived in Parliament.
The Conservatives sounded like trained seals. Over and over, they stood up to repeat the same line - "after nine years of the NDP-Liberal government, taxes are up, prices are up, crime is up and time is up" - and to demand a "carbon-tax election" that no other party wants.
And that leaves Poilievre with other problems on his hands. He tightly controls his ship, but it has unhappy sailors. Conservatives MPs are mostly banned from official travel with other parliamentarians. Whether that is, as some suggest, to prevent them from befriending other MPs, or so that Poilievre can accuse the other parties of spending taxpayers' money unnecessarily, the result is discouraged members who want to be more than partisan parrots.
Another problem for the Conservatives is that encouraging economic data is starting to challenge Poilievre's talking points. Inflation is now down to two per cent, the lowest rate since February 2021. Gas prices are down. Interest rates are coming down. Even Canada's greenhouse gas emissions are down (although still far from the 2030 target).
If Canadians start feeling like life isn't all that bad, what will Poilievre offer then?
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period in the House of Commons on Sept. 18, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Confidence; Dental care; Voters; Carbon; Prime ministers; No confidence motions & votes; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax elections

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Document 491 of 500

Sept. 21: Please, Doug Ford, drop the propaganda, also don't ignore sepsis data and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-21-please-doug-ford-drop-propaganda-also/docview/3107472571/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+21%3A+Please%2C+Doug+Ford%2C+drop+the+propaganda%2C+also+don%27t+ignore+sepsis+data+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Drop the propaganda, please
Ontario needs more doctors, shorter wait times in hospitals, affordable housing and other ways of spending tax dollars that benefit the people of Ontario. We do not need government propaganda on TV in print or any other form. Cut the waste and help the people.
Ian Martin, Hamilton
Who's getting all these jobs?
I saw the ad in the Hamilton Spectator paid for by the Government of Ontario recently regarding the 800,000 jobs it has created and bigger paycheques than ever before. So where are those jobs? Certainly not hospital workers, including doctors, nurses and support staff. Not when you have hallway medicine at disgustingly high levels! Not in education either, with every aspect of improving our students level of learning down the tubes, starting with the lack of school bus drivers and no incentive from this government to make sure we have enough. So, Doug, other than making sure your builder buddies are taken care of, what are you doing to help the average Joe in this great province of ours? Stripping our health care and educational systems of the much-needed funds they deserve absolutely stinks.
R.J. Kovljenic, Ancaster
Visionary leadership desperately needed
Re: Crisis of priorities: Why governments are failing the climate test and us, Sept. 16
Kudos to Robert Hicks for pointedly highlighting government inaction and complicity with fossil fuel interests when addressing by far the greatest existential crisis ever faced by humankind. Climate change is the enormous and potentially highly destructive elephant in the room. We ignore it at our peril. We desperately need strong and visionary leadership in order to get off fossil fuels. We have here and now viable alternatives. We must resist the short-sighted and incredibly selfish greed of oil companies, banks and their political cronies.
Grant Linney, Dundas
We can't ignore the data
Re: Which Hamilton neighbourhoods have double the risk of developing a deadly response to infection? Sept. 18
I am disturbed by the significant differences in the risk of developing sepsis and access to health care between communities in Hamilton.
Despite the racial and socio-economic inequities that surfaced during the pandemic, Black, Indigenous and other racialized people continue to face disproportionate health challenges in certain neighbourhoods across our city. As a result, it is extremely important to collect disaggregated data.
By ignoring income level, gender, race and ethnicity in its data collection, Hamilton Health Sciences continues to perpetuate this existing disparity. It is only by adopting an "equity, diversity and inclusion lens" that Hamilton's health-care system can move toward being accessible and effective for all Hamiltonians.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Can't we find a different site?
Coun. Matt Francis is working diligently to support the majority of residents in protecting the much-used Stoney Creek parking lot. Councillors voted to reject that particular location for affordable housing.
Mayor Andrea Horwath then used her "strong-mayor" powers to veto this decision.
Francis explained by retaining the parking lot it would be protecting jobs, small businesses and giving parking options for visitors to a nearby medical centre and veterans who frequent the Royal Canadian Legion.
He then suggested a site near the Dominic Agostino Riverdale Community Centre as a replacement.
A question for Mayor Horwath: Is your decision written in stone? Is there absolutely no other location to replace the parking lot within the whole of the Stoney Creek area that could be used to build affordable housing?
Jacquie Reid, Hamilton
Enforce truck route rules
Re: Big trucks aren't a big problem, Sept. 18
Whether you believe big trucks are a problem or not, they are breaking the law.
John Laudonio, Flamborough
Trump deserves the criticism
On Thursday, the Spectator printed two letters defending Donald Trump against bullying by the Canadian media - apparently completely without irony!
Let's take a look at some of the statements he's made:
"Can't you just shoot them? Just shoot them in the legs or something?" about people protesting the murder of George Floyd.
"I'd like to kick their asses ..." about people protesting at his rallies.
"If you don't fight you won't have a country."
"Any guy that can do a body slam, he is my type!" About a Montana representative who assaulted a reporter.
"Stand back and stand by." About the far-right Proud Boys.
"There were very decent people ..." describing the group of torch-carriers chanting "Jews will not replace us!"
"I will be your retribution."
"I will only be a dictator on the first day."
"They're eating the cats. They're eating the dogs." A lie demonizing legal Haitian immigrants - similar to the way the Nazis demonized Jews.
And worthy of an honourable mention - paraphrasing: "She was Indian and now she's Black. I don't get it. Which is she?"
There's only one leader using violent rhetoric and there's only one who's a bully and it's the one you're defending.
With respect to those "many in the power structure" who will do "almost anything to stop him from becoming president again," geez, I just can't understand why. That was irony, by the way.
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Why carbon tax focus?
I am really struggling with understanding Pierre Poilievre's fixation with the carbon tax and making its repeal the centre of the Conservative Party's platform. The last increase in the carbon tax on gasoline amounted to three cents a litre. Which is significantly less than the 10 cents a litre swing in gas prices that we see every day from morning to night by the oil companies. Four times a year, Canadians receive a rebate of about 90 per cent of the money that they have paid in that tax. Eighty per cent of Canadian households receive more back than they paid in tax.
Environmentalists and economists agree the carbon tax is an effective mechanism for reducing greenhouse gas emissions across the board. So, why should Canadians care about "axing" a tax that has been in effect for five years and has had little to no real effect on inflation?
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
City streets need attention now
The city needs to do something about our roads. I drove down Aberdeen yesterday and almost lost the front end of my car! It's a disgrace. Aberdeen, Dundurn, Main Street, Upper Paradise are almost all untravellable they are so bad.
Let's give some priority to this situation.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Billionaire's comments feel empty
Re: SpaceX Polaris Dawn crew including billionaire entrepreneur completes first commercial space walk, Sept. 12
During his space walk, tech billionaire Jared Isaacman pontificated, "Back at home, we still have a lot of work to do. But from here it still looks like a perfect world." Maybe Jared could start that work by figuring out how to clean up the 76,000 metric tons of carbon dioxide Elon Musk's SpaceX rocket puked into Earth's atmosphere propelling him into orbit.
Phil Beard, Dundas
Lets follow Calgary's LRT lead
The City of Calgary is cancelling a planned light rail transit (LRT) expansion being termed a "multimillion-dollar boondoggle" due to unaffordability, quoting similar numbers to Hamilton at an initial cost estimate of about $700 million exploding to over $6.2 billion. The City of Hamilton needs to do the same, not the least of which due to unaffordability at all three levels of government.
Unlike Calgary, having already wasted millions of dollars at the taxpayers' expense, the city of Hamilton can turn its millions of dollars already spent into a major win for the entire city. Bus rapid transit (BRT) will cost exponentially less than LRT and will achieve superior performance provided by HSR employees who excel in the delivery of public transit.
A team of transit professionals, whose only stake in the game was to do what was in the best interests of the City of Hamilton, unanimously proposed BRT as the predecessor to LRT. Contact your elected representative on council and voice your opinion as to whether you support unprecedented tax increases for an unwarranted political legacy LRT project or a growth plan for Hamilton promoting BRT that was unanimously endorsed by council of the day in a standing recorded vote.
Don Hull, retired city of Hamilton director of transportation
Transit needs to advance
The American Public Transportation Association (APTA) has found that for every dollar invested in public transit there is a $4 return. Admittedly, the APTA is a U.S. entity; however, our transportation needs aren't that much different from theirs. This return on investment is multifaceted. As is the return on taxpayer-funded infrastructure for traffic signals, firefighting, police protection, sidewalks, roads, snow removal, etc. This is a no-brainer. An urban need met.
The holdup for this advancement is, as with many progressive changes, as usual, the fear of change, promoted sometimes by people who have a financial interest in the status quo. For instance, the insurance industry that fought universally available single-payer government regulated health care. Remember that one, if you are old enough?
We Homo sapiens have been in constant change mode since the cognitive revolution, about 70,000 years ago. Imagine an archaic hunter/gatherer who has just woken up, "cognitively," and he/she pops up at the McNab Transportation Centre in Hamilton, Ont. There are two buses loading for the same route. For one bus, all the people must enter only by the front door and wait, while all passengers pay for the trip. At the other bus, he/she can enter front or rear door and no one has to wait for people to pay and off goes the bus. Which bus do you think she/he gets onto? Or maybe, she/he pops up in Burlington and there is no choice; the bus there has already progressed beyond neanderthal.
Bill Brush, Hamilton
U.S. politicians are fooling themselves
With gun violence in "super-epidemic levels," the stale comments from U.S. politicos is nauseating. Time and again, U.S. politicians have stated, "This is not who we are." Wrong! It is exactly who you are. The U.S.A. was founded upon violence and is central to its DNA. Americans adore guns. I believe nowhere else on earth (outside of wars zones) is gun violence more prevalent among the people. Travel advisory to U.S.A. should be implemented.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
PSA test should be covered
I wholeheartedly believe prostate-specific antigen (PSA) tests should be covered by OHIP. If PSA testing was covered and mammograms were not, I am sure there would be protests in front of the Ontario legislature. Men have for years sat quietly and paid for the tests, but now, with the economy where it is, many cannot afford the fee for the test. Come on, Premier Ford, have the PSA test covered by OHIP and save lives.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
Don't fall for the AI hype
Re: Will AI make us food illiterate? Sept. 14
Sylvain Charlebois writes, "The internet is a tool, but AI can act on its own, making decisions for us."
Mr. Charlebois' column is in serious need of an editor because this statement is outright false. AI is nowhere close to acting on its own; it cannot make decisions for us and will likely never be able to do so. It seems Mr. Charlebois had fallen hard for the hype. AI, as hyped this decade, refers to large language models (LLMs). The LLMs being hyped are given nearly all text that had been digitized as input (without permission) and used to create a statistical model that, in essence, predicts the next word based on previous words. Its output is frequently inaccurate, despite its appearance of legitimacy.
LLMs are not sentient. They do not understand things. And they do not have agency. They look like they do because they appear to carry on a conversation, but when you push them even a little bit, they fall over. Simple research into the field will show this. The problem is the proponents of it, as is typical, do not care about facts because they are looking for sales. Mr. Charlebois and the editorial staff would do well to heed this.
Geoff Wozniak, Burlington
CANUSA lost its sparkle
Remember the good old days of the CANUSA Games between Hamilton, Canada and Flint, U.S.A.? Our father was a longtime coach, and I participated as an athlete in the games during the '90s when they truly meant something. I decided to have our daughter involved this past summer, but it's just not the same. The idea of having our children stay with other families made me nervous in today's society.
Luckily, we were able to afford to stay in hotel; a very expensive trip that many were not able to go on.
We struggled to find enough players to fill our soccer team, and when we finally did, it was not very organized and the skill level was very low. I've heard from coaches and co-ordinators that the event is now more of a social event for them.
The city should reconsider its support for this program, which poses a significant liability. Billeting young vulnerable athletes is far too risky nowadays.
I no longer recommend these games to anyone. The competition level has diminished, and the CANUSA name no longer carries the same prestige.
Christine Hill, Hamilton
Radio needs station to hear
Kudos to the Hamilton fire department for its emergency preparedness campaign. Bug out/emergency kit: First aid supplies, batteries, non perishable food, water, etc.
Further inquiries online led me to an important item: a crank radio not dependent on power. This would be crucial in finding out the disaster situation in the Hamilton area. I almost bought one until I remembered, we don't have a "hometown news radio" station anymore!
Wray Brown, Dundas

Document 492 of 500

How developers will benefit from the Yonge subway route change - and why it's raising the spectre of the Ford government's Greenbelt scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-developers-will-benefit-yonge-subway-route/docview/3107472558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+developers+will+benefit+from+the+Yonge+subway+route+change+-+and+why+it%27s+raising+the+spectre+of+the+Ford+government%27s+Greenbelt+scandal&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Premier Doug Ford's administration unveiled its plan in 2021 to extend a Toronto subway line to Richmond Hill, it looked different from what the previous government intended to build.
Instead of going straight north, a portion of the Yonge north subway would swerve eastward off Yonge Street, then run under a residential neighbourhood, a public school and a tributary of the East Don River before emerging above ground for its final stretch.
It would also include two new stations in the middle of land owned by some of Ontario's most prominent developers.
The route change, selected by Metrolinx and endorsed by the Ford government, paves the way for a profitable boon for the developers, who plan to build and sell more than 40,000 new condo units in a thicket of skyscrapers surrounding the two stations.
With Ontario facing a housing shortage and a growing population, a spokesperson for the premier's office said it's important that transit lines be accessible to new communities - and called the new route "the best option."
But it has also resurrected some of the same concerns that surrounded the Greenbelt scandal, where decisions disproportionately favouring certain developers were sprung on local communities, leaving them questioning whose needs were being prioritized.
Ontario government inked secret deal with developers
The new route, according to an internal assessment of three options, offered the poorest performance for commuters, with the fewest expected riders and lowest travel time savings.
Meanwhile, the government has made a series of moves to make it easier for the developers' work to proceed, overriding opposition from local governments who said they don't have the infrastructure to support the proposed 64 new condo towers.
The province made new rules that will spare the developers from having to pay billions of dollars to one of the impacted cities.
Around the same time, the Ford government struck deals with the developers, under which it says the builders will pay money to offset the costs of an extra subway station and other public infrastructure.
The terms of those deals, however, are secret - and are even being withheld from the Markham and Richmond Hill municipal governments most affected by the decisions.
"Our focus remains squarely on building the transit, homes and infrastructure that Ontario needs to support the province's growth," the premier's office's spokesperson said in a written response, adding that previous plans along the original route for the Yonge subway extension "did not meet the needs of York Region's rapidly growing population."
The new route, which is the most cost-efficient option to build the $5.6-billion subway extension, allows "expansion of the line and more stations to better connect people across the region, while addressing concerns raised by the municipality."
New route was most affordable - but also poorest performing option
Local and provincial officials have for decades wanted a Yonge subway extension.
In 2009, the environment minister of the Liberal provincial government of the day greenlit a plan for a fully underground, six-station route.
The project remained unfunded and dormant until 2019 when the Ford government introduced legislation to upload the delivery of Toronto's future rapid transit projects, including the Yonge subway extension, to the provincial agency Metrolinx.
At that time, the cost for the originally planned six-station route was estimated to be $9.3 billion, nearly doubling what the Ford government had budgeted. They could only afford to build three new stations, Metrolinx said at that time.
The agency had quietly considered three alignments.
The chosen option, referred to in Metrolinx's initial business case as Option 3, was the cheapest. It was also the poorest performer from a transit perspective. It offers the fewest expected daily riders, fewest daily travel minutes saved and fewest "net new transit users" projected.
"It's building an inferior, suboptimal alignment. And you see it in their own numbers if you go into this business case," said Eric Miller, a professor of civil engineering at the University of Toronto.
The agency also compared the travel time of different trips between York Region and Toronto and concluded Option 3 gave the least total time savings. It's also expected to draw nearly 16,000 fewer daily boardings than the original route.
Metrolinx's analysis suggests the chosen route could also result in less ridership revenue, more greenhouse gas, and more traffic on the road in the area than the other two options.
Route change choice based on Metrolinx's 'engineering expertise,' premier's office says
The premier's office said the route choice was "based on the engineering expertise and maximized community benefits." The route change also requires less excavation and is less disruptive to the community.
While Option 3 was estimated to cost less than the original route to maintain and operate, tight curves would make it more complex to build, and could also lead to higher maintenance bills in the long run, transit experts told the Star - costs that would end up being the responsibility of the Toronto Transit Commission.
"It's going to be an ongoing maintenance cost because the tracks will wear out faster, and it'll be a slightly slower trip," Toronto transit expert Steve Munro said.
The new route will create a "transit hub" at Bridge Station, Metrolinx and the premier's office said, connecting the Yonge subway line with GO Transit and local buses. It will also make it easier to extend the subway north along the existing rail line, something that wasn't possible under the other options.
Option 3 is also at least $1 billion cheaper than a three-station version of the original route in capital costs, according to Metrolinx's analysis.
The new route will include five stations.
The two northernmost stations - "Bridge" and "High Tech" - are both in the middle of 42 hectares of land owned by the same group of developers.
Developers have been amassing land where new stations will be
Land records show that since the mid-2000s, that group of development companies had been assembling lands that would become the Bridge station area. By 2021, the firms already owned more than 60 per cent of the land in a 25-hectare area.
These companies all list Angelo De Gasperis as a director. Angelo, alongside his brothers Tony and Fred De Gasperis, founded Condrain in 1954 as a concrete and drainage company which has now developed into a major construction empire based in Concord, Ont.
To the north of Highway 7, on the Richmond Hill side of what will become the new route, four De Gasperis-controlled companies own much of the land, which they started acquiring in the late 1990s. Some of these properties also abut the terminus station on the original route.
Three of the corporations list Angelo as a director and the other is headed by Angelo's nephew Jim De Gasperis and their business partner Marc Muzzo. Developer Sam Balsamo is also a director of two of the companies.
None of them responded to repeated requests for comment.
The De Gasperis-controlled companies Condor and Metrus hired former Progressive Conservative MPP Frank Klees to lobby on their behalf (Klees did not respond to the Star's questions.). A week before the new route was announced in March 2021, Klees updated his information on Ontario's lobbyist registry to include a new lobbying goal: to "facilitate and assist" in negotiations with the province to develop a "proposed transit oriented community" on their land.
Transit-oriented communities, or TOCs, are part of a provincial program introduced in July 2020 to build new transit. Under the program led by Infrastructure Ontario, developers are allowed to build higher-density buildings near transit, which are more profitable for them, and they offset the public cost of building public transportation. The goal is to make vibrant, high-density communities connected to a public transit station.
All TOC negotiations with developers are handled by "non-partisan, arms-length agency officials" inside Infrastructure Ontario, the premier's office said.
'They're with the government, they got the power'
After the Ford government announced the new subway route, the De Gasperis companies started acquiring more land.
One company purchased a 2.55-hectare plot of land near the proposed Bridge station from the provincial government for $175 million. The land was considered surplus, the spokesperson for the premier's office said, and was "identified as needed for a TOC priority project." The land was sold to the De Gasperis company at fair market value.
Another company called 10 Cedar Avenue - headed by Angelo De Gasperis - also bought two parcels of land for $30 million from a landscape supply company in early 2022. The lots are located just east of the CN rail lines where the developers are planning to erect a 65-storey condo tower.
"I didn't want to sell the land," said Tony Pacitto, president of the landscape company.
Pacitto said he felt compelled to sell when a representative from Condor approached and told him the province "only wanted" to partner with them, not an individual landowner like him.
"When these guys come and kind of push you and they're with the government, they've got the power to do it. Sometimes you've got to take what you can and move on," he said.
Permission to build thousands more condo units
In September 2021, the Ford government graced the De Gasperis group's lands surrounding the planned Bridge and High Tech stations with TOC status.
With the TOC designation, the developers proposed to build about 18,000 additional condo units than they would've been able to before the province stepped in.
The planned development was met with immediate pushback from Richmond Hill and Markham councils. The developers' proposal would see a total of 64 condo towers, many as high as 80 storeys, built between the two sites over several decades. They are expected to bring in roughly 80,000 residents.
Since 2008, Markham officials had eyed the area around what will now be Bridge station as a vibrant community. Building plans envisioned walkable streets, public transit and open space - a little bit of everything for locals so that the area could more seamlessly integrate with neighbouring communities. In Richmond Hill, the land around what will become High Tech station had been expected to transform into "a new downtown" with a diversity of architecture and building types.
The new plans, and the dense grouping of towers they would bring, did not adequately consider the additional schools, libraries, and community centres that would be required with the expected influx of future residents, the region cautioned the province.
York Region's chief planner wrote four letters appealing the Ford government to revise the plans. One of his reports warned of "unanticipated consequences" if they went ahead.
In April 2022, the province sealed the fate of the two TOCs by wielding another of its developer-friendly tools.
Former Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing Steve Clark issued two enhanced minister's zoning orders (MZOs) to override the local authorities and greenlight the large-scale development plan.
MZOs bypass municipal planning rules and can be used to fast-track developments. The MZOs cannot be appealed.
'Overlap' between route change decisions and the Greenbelt landswap
"There's a lot of overlap between this development and the Greenbelt deal. They all follow the same kind of pattern," said Graham Churchill, a former director of the Federation of Urban Neighbourhoods.
"Let me do a deal with the developer but give away some value so that we can get some money to get something done, but without thinking about the broader impact of how does this solve the problem of the overall region."
The government's zealous use of MZOs came under scrutiny amid the Greenbelt scandal, which put a spotlight on Premier Ford's decision to open up the protected area for development and how a flawed process favoured well-connected developers. (The branch of the De Gasperis family behind the companies set to benefit from the Yonge subway extension did not own land included in the Greenbelt land swap.)
Following damning reports by the Auditor General and Integrity Commissioner, Ford reversed his government's plans to open up the Greenbelt and apologized to Ontarians. Clark, the housing minister, resigned. His successor Paul Calandra began reviewing the minister's zoning orders issued during Clark's time.
The two enhanced MZOs for the TOCs in York Region are considered "provincial priorities" and not part of the review, the government said.
Developers' off the hook for paying billions to municipality
The province's actions along the planned subway extension don't just pave the way for possible big profits for the developers - they may also offer them great savings.
Previously, Markham had the power to require developers to contribute land to the city for parks or pay cash-in-lieu for the impacted TOC land. That cash amount was to be calculated based on the numbers of proposed units and land value and enforced through a city bylaw.
Markham was expected to rake in $2.2 billion. That is about four times the city's entire budget in 2024.
But new legislation would change that. The More Homes for Everyone Act altered the parkland requirements for transit-oriented communities. In Markham, it means the developers in the Bridge TOC will no longer have to pay any cash-in-lieu to the city.
This "will have a lasting impact on the city's ability to provide for adequate parkland to support complete communities," Pody Lui, Markham's spokesperson said.
The premier's office said the changes for parkland dedication on these sites "will increase cost certainty and the speed with which subways and increased housing will be built."
The developers in the TOCs will still pay development charges and community benefit charges to Richmond Hill and Markham. Both cities said they cannot yet calculate how much money the developers are expected to contribute as the development plans are not finalized.
The province will also collect money from the developers, which it says will offset the cost to build Royal Orchard Station in Markham, to be located just south of where the subway line will veer off Yonge Street.
As part of the province's agreement with building partners at these TOCs, a portion of revenues realized by the province will go toward the building of libraries, community centres and affordable housing, according to the premier's office.
The premier's office would not say how much money the province will receive from the developers in the deal.
Metrolinx says it has refined route based on community feedback
A knock on the door was all the heads-up Dwight Richardson got before he and his family found out just how much the Yonge north subway reroute was going to impact their home.
It was December 2021 when the homeowners heard from the provincial agency for the first time that the subway extension will tunnel directly under their two-storey house, Richardson said.
This was more than a year after Metrolinx had already started eyeing the new route and six months after it had announced it had chosen it over other options.
"The whole problem with this ... is the silence," Richardson said. Frustrated with the amount of information Metrolinx shared in open houses and community meetings, the homeowner took it upon himself to find out more. He filed a freedom of information request seeking records that included the detailed drawings and the concept design for the subway extension. They were denied.
He said the secrecy just foments more questions and suspicion among residents.
"If these are all such good plans, then tell us about it."
The provincial transit agency says it has listened to the public and continues to refine plans for a route that could save billions while delivering more transit benefits for the people of York Region and Toronto.
The latest version of Metrolinx's plan will tunnel under 20 homes and 15 other properties, updated from its earlier design that would have run under 63 properties. The tunnel will also run deeper to lessen its impact.
"We are determined to make the project the best possible fit for the communities it will serve, and there will be many more opportunities to share feedback," said Metrolinx spokesperson Andrea Ernesaks.
When it comes to the deals made on the transit-oriented communities, even York Region and the city governments of Markham and Richmond Hill were kept out of the loop.
In April 2022, the provincial government entered into confidential commercial agreements with Metrus and Condor for the developments of the TOC lands.
York Region is not privy to the details of the deals between the province and the De Gasperis companies.
CAPTION: The Premier's office said the new route, chosen by Metrolinx, is "the best option" to serve the needs of York Region's growing population.Eric Miller,a professor of civil engineering at the University of Toronto.Premier Doug Ford, seen in 2019 with a map that featured the original planned route for the Yonge subway extension.Representatives from Condor Properties and politicians celebrate the August 2020 groundbreaking of the new Walmart distribution centre. Among the attendees are developers' Marc Muzzo (left) and Angelo De Gasperis (second from the left).The new Yonge subway extension route,selected by Metrolinx and endorsed by the Ford government.

Full text: Quebec
Three injured in mosque attack
Three men have been injured after a 24-year-old man entered a mosque southwest of Montreal armed with a knife. Police in Châteauguay, Que., say the three men were in their fifties and suffered only minor injuries. The suspect entered the Muslim cultural centre in Châteauguay Friday afternoon. Police wouldn't confirm whether the knife was used to cause any of the injuries.
Ontario
PCs hold seat
in byelection
The Progressive Conservatives won a provincial byelection in eastern Ontario Thursday, retaining a seat, though the result was much closer than the riding has seen in the past two elections. Tyler Allsopp, a municipal councillor in Belleville, will now represent the Bay of Quinte.
Four dead in house fire: OPP
Four people are dead after a house fire in the northern Ontario city of Temiskaming Shores, police said Friday, with the mayor describing what happened as a tragedy that has shaken the small community. OPP said officers and firefighters responded to the blaze around 5:30 p.m. on Thursday.
Michael Ford steps away
Ontario's minister of citizenship and multiculturalism, who is also Ontario Premier Doug Ford's nephew, says he is temporarily stepping away from his cabinet duties. In a statement Friday, Michael Ford says he is taking a leave of absence from cabinet, effective immediately. Ford says he needs to prioritize his health and well-being over the next couple of months.

Full text: Please drop
the propaganda
Ontario needs more doctors, shorter wait times in hospitals, affordable housing and other ways of spending tax dollars that benefit the people of Ontario. We do not need government propaganda on TV in print or any other form. Cut the waste and help the people.
Ian Martin, Hamilton
Who's getting
all these jobs?
I saw the ad in the Hamilton Spectator paid for by the Government of Ontario recently regarding the 800,000 jobs it has created and bigger paycheques than ever before. So where are those jobs? Certainly not hospital workers, including doctors, nurses and support staff. Not when you have hallway medicine at disgustingly high levels! Not in education either, with every aspect of improving our students level of learning down the tubes, starting with the lack of school bus drivers and no incentive from this government to make sure we have enough. So, Doug, other than making sure your builder buddies are taken care of, what are you doing to help the average Joe in this great province of ours? Stripping our health care and educational systems of the much-needed funds they deserve absolutely stinks.
R.J. Kovljenic, Ancaster
Visionary
leadership needed
Re: Crisis of priorities: Why governments are failing the climate test and us, Sept. 16
Kudos to Robert Hicks for pointedly highlighting government inaction and complicity with fossil fuel interests when addressing by far the greatest existential crisis ever faced by humankind. Climate change is the enormous and potentially highly destructive elephant in the room. We ignore it at our peril. We desperately need strong and visionary leadership in order to get off fossil fuels. We have here and now viable alternatives. We must resist the short-sighted and incredibly selfish greed of oil companies, banks and their political cronies.
Grant Linney, Dundas
We can't ignore the data
Re: Which Hamilton
neighbourhoods have double the risk of developing a deadly response to infection? Sept. 18
I am disturbed by the significant differences in the risk of developing sepsis and access to health care between communities in Hamilton.
Despite the racial and socio-economic inequities that surfaced during the pandemic, Black, Indigenous and other racialized people continue to face disproportionate health challenges in certain neighbourhoods across our city. As a result, it is extremely important to collect disaggregated data.
By ignoring income level, gender, race and ethnicity in its data collection, Hamilton Health Sciences continues to perpetuate this existing disparity. It is only by adopting an "equity, diversity and inclusion lens" that our health-care system can move toward being accessible and effective for all Hamiltonians.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Can't we find a different site?
Coun. Matt Francis is working diligently to support the majority of residents in protecting the much-used Stoney Creek parking lot. Councillors voted to reject that particular location for affordable housing. Mayor Andrea Horwath then used her "strong-mayor" powers to veto this decision.
Francis explained by retaining the parking lot it would be protecting jobs, small businesses and giving parking options for visitors to a nearby medical centre and veterans who frequent the Royal Canadian Legion.
He then suggested a site near the Dominic Agostino Riverdale Community Centre as a replacement.
A question for Mayor Horwath: Is your decision written in stone? Is there absolutely no other location to replace the parking lot within the whole of the Stoney Creek area that could be used to build affordable housing?
Jacquie Reid, Hamilton
Enforce truck route rules
Re: Big trucks aren't a big problem, Sept. 18
Whether you believe big trucks are a problem or not, they are breaking the law.
John Laudonio, Flamborough
Trump deserves the criticism
On Thursday, the Spectator printed two letters defending Donald Trump against bullying by the Canadian media - apparently completely without irony!
Let's take a look at some of the statements he's made:
"Can't you just shoot them? Just shoot them in the legs or something?" about people protesting the murder of George Floyd.
"I'd like to kick their asses ..." about people protesting at his rallies.
"If you don't fight you won't have a country."
"Any guy that can do a body slam, he is my type!" About a Montana representative who assaulted a reporter.
"Stand back and stand by." About the far-right Proud Boys.
"There were very decent people ..." describing the group of torch-carriers chanting "Jews will not replace us!"
"I will be your retribution."
"I will only be a dictator on the first day."
"They're eating the cats. They're eating the dogs." A lie demonizing legal Haitian immigrants - similar to the way the Nazis demonized Jews.
And worthy of an honourable mention - paraphrasing: "She was Indian and now she's Black. I don't get it. Which is she?"
There's only one leader using violent rhetoric and there's only one who's a bully and it's the one you're defending. With respect to those "many in the power structure" who will do "almost anything to stop him from becoming president again," geez, I just can't understand why. That was irony, by the way.
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Carbon pricing isn't that bad
I am really struggling with understanding Pierre Poilievre's fixation with the carbon tax and making its repeal the centre of the Conservative Party's platform.
The last increase in the carbon tax on gasoline amounted to three cents a litre. Which is significantly less than the 10 cents a litre swing in gas prices that we see every day from morning to night by the oil companies. Four times a year, Canadians receive a rebate of about 90 per cent of the money that they have paid in that tax.
Eighty per cent of Canadian households receive more back than they paid in tax.
Environmentalists and economists agree the carbon tax is an effective mechanism for reducing greenhouse gas emissions across the board. So, why should Canadians care about "axing" a tax that has been in effect for five years and has had little to no real effect on inflation?
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
City streets need attention now
The city needs to do something about our roads. I drove down Aberdeen yesterday and almost lost the front end of my car!
It's a disgrace. Aberdeen, Dundurn, Main Street, Upper Paradise are almost all untravellable they are so bad.
Let's give some priority to this situation.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
A billionaire's
empty comments
Re: SpaceX Polaris Dawn
crew including billionaire
entrepreneur completes
first commercial space
walk, Sept. 12
During his space walk, tech billionaire Jared Isaacman pontificated, "Back at home, we still have a lot of work to do. But from here it still looks like a perfect world."
Maybe Jared could start that work by figuring out how to clean up the 76,000 metric tons of carbon dioxide Elon Musk's SpaceX rocket puked into Earth's atmosphere propelling him into orbit.
Phil Beard, Dundas
Follow Calgary's lead on LRT
The City of Calgary is cancelling a planned light rail transit (LRT) expansion being termed a "multimillion-dollar boondoggle" due to unaffordability, quoting similar numbers to Hamilton at an initial cost estimate of about $700 million exploding to over $6.2 billion.
The City of Hamilton needs to do the same, not the least of which due to unaffordability at all three levels of government.
Unlike Calgary, having already wasted millions of dollars at the taxpayers' expense, the city of Hamilton can turn its millions of dollars already spent into a major win for the entire city. Bus rapid transit (BRT) will cost exponentially less than LRT and will achieve superior performance provided by HSR employees who excel in the delivery of public transit.
A team of transit professionals, whose only stake in the game was to do what was in the best interests of the City of Hamilton, unanimously proposed BRT as the predecessor to LRT.
Contact your elected representative on council and voice your opinion as to whether you support unprecedented tax increases for an unwarranted political legacy LRT project or a growth plan for Hamilton promoting BRT that was unanimously endorsed by council of the day in a standing recorded vote.
Don Hull, retired city
of Hamilton director
of transportation
Transit needs
to advance
The American Public Transportation Association (APTA) has found that for every dollar invested in public transit there is a $4 return. Admittedly, the APTA is a U.S. entity; however, our transportation needs aren't that much different from theirs. This return on investment is multifaceted. As is the return on taxpayer-funded infrastructure for traffic signals, firefighting, police protection, sidewalks, roads, snow removal, etc. This is a no-brainer. An urban need met.
The holdup for this advancement is, as with many progressive changes, as usual, the fear of change, promoted sometimes by people who have a financial interest in the status quo. For instance, the insurance industry that fought universally available single-payer government regulated health care. Remember that one, if you are old enough?
We Homo sapiens have been in constant change mode since the cognitive revolution, about 70,000 years ago. Imagine an archaic hunter/gatherer who has just woken up, "cognitively," and he/she pops up at the McNab Transportation Centre in Hamilton, Ont. There are two buses loading for the same route. For one bus, all the people must enter only by the front door and wait, while all passengers pay for the trip. At the other bus, he/she can enter front or rear door and no one has to wait for people to pay and off goes the bus.
Which bus do you think she/he gets onto? Or maybe, she/he pops up in Burlington and there is no choice; the bus there has already progressed beyond neanderthal.
Bill Brush, Hamilton
Americans fooling themselves
With gun violence in "super-epidemic levels," the stale comments from U.S. politicos is nauseating. Time and again, U.S. politicians have stated, "This is not who we are." Wrong! It is exactly who you are.
The U.S.A. was founded upon violence and is central to its DNA. Americans adore guns. I believe nowhere else on earth (outside of wars zones) is gun violence more prevalent among the people. Travel advisory to U.S.A. should be implemented.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
PSA test should be covered
I wholeheartedly believe prostate-specific antigen (PSA) tests should be covered by OHIP. If PSA testing was covered and mammograms were not, I am sure there would be protests in front of the Ontario legislature.
Men have for years sat quietly and paid for the tests, but now, with the economy where it is, many cannot afford the fee for the test. Come on, Premier Ford, have the PSA test covered by OHIP and save lives.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
